topics.title: Classification of Metal Forming Processes According to Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Metal Forming Processes According to Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Metal Forming** is defined as a manufacturing process group wherein the desired geometric shape and mechanical properties of a metallic workpiece are achieved through **plastic deformation** without the addition or removal of intrinsic mass (i.e., no chips are generated, unlike machining). The classification "**According to Stresses**" segregates these processes based on the **dominant state of stress** developed within the deformation zone of the workpiece.

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly mandates that students understand the four primary stress states governing the classification:

1. **Tensile (T)** — Uniaxial or multiaxial pulling stresses.
2. **Compressive (C)** — Squeezing / pushing stresses.
3. **Shear (S)** — Tangential sliding stresses.
4. **Combined / Compound (Mixed)** — A simultaneous superposition of two or more of the above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (PCPET602 - Module 1):** Students must be able to *map at least 3 industrial processes* to each of the four stress states and *justify* the stress state using a free-body diagram (FBD) approach.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **piece of soft clay (plasticine)** in your hand. You can:

- **Pull it apart** from both ends → that is **TENSION** (e.g., wire drawing, stretch forming).
- **Squash it** between your palms → that is **COMPRESSION** (e.g., forging, rolling, extrusion).
- **Slide one part of it sideways over the other** with a knife edge → that is **SHEAR** (e.g., guillotine shearing, blanking).
- **Bend it over a pencil**, which simultaneously stretches one side and compresses the other → that is a **COMBINED stress state** (e.g., bending, deep drawing).

The dominant stress dictates the *flow pattern of metal grains* and ultimately the *tool design geometry*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$ or $S_y$):** Measured in **MPa (N/mm²)**. Typical value for mild steel: **$\sigma_y \approx 250 \text{ MPa}$**.
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):** $\sigma_u$ in **MPa**.
- **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Dimensionless ratio, often expressed in **mm/mm**.
- **Flow Stress ($\bar{\sigma}$):** Instantaneous yield stress during plastic deformation, in **MPa**.
- **Von Mises Yield Criterion constant:** $k = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}$ for pure shear derivation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification Logic

The classification tree follows a strict hierarchical logic based on the *stress tensor components* ($\sigma_{ij}$) acting on the principal axes:

- **$\sigma_1 > 0, \sigma_2 = 0, \sigma_3 = 0$** → **Tension Dominant**
- **$\sigma_1 = 0, \sigma_2 = 0, \sigma_3 < 0$** → **Compression Dominant**
- **$\sigma_1 > 0, \sigma_2 = 0, \sigma_3 < 0$** → **Tension–Compression (Combined)**
- **$\sigma_1 > 0, \sigma_2 < 0, \sigma_3 = 0$ (or pure shear $\tau$)** → **Shear / Plane Strain / Drawing**

## 2.2 Process-by-Process Mapping

### A. Tensile-Stress Dominant Processes
The workpiece is subjected to a *pulling* load. Material is drawn or stretched.
- **Wire Drawing**
- **Tube Drawing**
- **Stretch Forming**
- **Tensile Testing (in process terms, used for characterization)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why tension?** Because the material is pulled *into* the die or over a form block. The cross-sectional area reduces (Poisson effect). The flow is *uniaxial* along the pulling direction.

### B. Compressive-Stress Dominant Processes
The dominant stress is *squeezing* in nature. Material is squeezed or pushed into a confined die cavity.
- **Forging (Open & Closed Die)**
- **Rolling**
- **Extrusion (Direct, Indirect, Hydrostatic)**
- **Coining**
- **Compressive Heading / Upsetting**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why compression?** Because the tool *pushes* the material. Hydrostatic compressive stress improves ductility (a key advantage: metals can deform far more in compression than in tension without fracture).

### C. Shear-Stress Dominant Processes
Material is severed or deformed by *tangential sliding* of atomic planes.
- **Blanking**
- **Piercing**
- **Slitting**
- **Guillotine Shearing**
- **Trimming**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In *shearing*, the dominant mechanism is **$\tau_{xy} \neq 0$** while normal stresses are minimal. The fracture is *ductile-to-brittle* depending on the clearance.

### D. Combined-Stress Dominant Processes
A *superposition* of stresses occurs simultaneously. Real-world forming rarely uses a single stress.
- **Bending** (Outer fibers: tension; Inner fibers: compression)
- **Deep Drawing** (Flange: compression; Wall: tension; Die radius: bending)
- **Spinning** (Combination of compression, tension, and shear)
- **Hydroforming** (Multi-axial state)
- **Roll Bending / Stretch Bending**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations** for solving classification-related numerical problems in KTU ESE.

| **Equation** | **Description** | **Application / Process** | **Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$ | **Von Mises Effective Stress** | All metal forming | **MPa** |
| $\bar{\sigma} = \sigma_y = \sqrt{3}\,k$ | Yield criterion (Von Mises) | All metal forming | **MPa** |
| $k = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Shear yield stress | Shear dominant processes | **MPa** |
| $P = \bar{\sigma}\,A$ | Approximate deformation load | Forging, extrusion | **N** |
| $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$ | Hollomon flow curve | Tensile / drawing | **MPa** |
| $\sigma_d = \bar{\sigma}\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu\,L}{2h}\right)$ | Drawing stress (slab analysis) | Wire / tube drawing | **MPa** |
| $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n \dot{\varepsilon}^m$ | Norton-Hoff (strain-rate sensitive) | Hot working | **MPa** |
| $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = 2\ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_f}\right)$ | True strain (compression / drawing) | Drawing, extrusion | **dimensionless** |
| $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\sigma_y}$ | Strain hardening ratio | Sheet metal forming | **dimensionless** |
| $LDR = \sqrt{e^{\,\varepsilon_{\max}}}$ | Limiting Drawing Ratio | Deep drawing | **dimensionless** |

> **Note:** Above, the symbol $\vert x \vert$ has been rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$ using LaTeX escape sequences to preserve table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding stress classification is **not academic** — it is the foundation of:

1. **Tool Design**: Compressive-stress tools (forging dies) require *harder materials* (H13, H11 tool steels) than shear tools because of the cyclic impact.
2. **Process Selection**: A *fracture-sensitive* alloy (e.g., magnesium) is preferably formed in **compression-dominant** processes like forging rather than tension-dominant ones like drawing.
3. **FEM (Finite Element Method) Software (e.g., DEFORM, ABAQUS)**: Engineers specify the **stress state** as a boundary condition; this classification maps directly to FEA input decks.
4. **Defect Diagnosis**: Cracks in drawn wires are *cup-cone* (tensile), while cracks in forged parts are often *laps* (tensile residual). Knowing the dominant stress aids in root-cause analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mapping the Stress State to a Process (Slab Analysis)

**Problem:** A cylindrical billet of initial diameter $D_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$ and height $H_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$ is reduced to $D_f = 35 \text{ mm}$ in a **compressive-stress dominant** upset-forging operation. Compute the true strain and verify that the operation is *compression-dominant* using the sign of the principal stresses.

### Step 1: Identify the Process and the Stress State
The tool (a flat die) pushes the billet *axially downward*. Therefore:
- The **axial stress** ($\sigma_z$) is **negative (compressive)**: $\sigma_3 = \sigma_z < 0$.
- The **radial stress** ($\sigma_r$) and **hoop stress** ($\sigma_\theta$) develop *tensile* reaction stresses due to the Poisson effect (since axial compression forces material to bulge outward).
- However, the **dominant** (largest magnitude) stress is $\vert \sigma_3 \vert \gg \vert \sigma_1 \vert$.

### Step 2: Volume Conservation
For an *incompressible* plastic deformation (volume is conserved since no porosity is generated):
$$V_0 = V_f$$
$$\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 H_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 H_f$$

### Step 3: Solve for Final Height
$$H_f = \dfrac{D_0^2 \, H_0}{D_f^2}$$
$$H_f = \dfrac{(50)^2 (100)}{(35)^2}$$
$$H_f = \dfrac{250{,}000}{1{,}225} = 204.08 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Compute the True Strain (Axial Direction)
The true strain in the axial (loading) direction is:
$$\varepsilon_z = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{H_f}{H_0}\right)$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{204.08}{100}\right) = \ln(2.0408) = 0.7133$$

### Step 5: Compute the True Strain (Radial Direction)
By volume constancy:
$$\varepsilon_z + 2\varepsilon_r = 0$$
$$\varepsilon_r = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_z}{2} = -\dfrac{0.7133}{2} = -0.3567$$

### Step 6: Verify Dominance Using Von Mises Stress
Assume yield begins at $\sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$ for mild steel. For *frictionless upset forging*:
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_r = 0 \text{ (free surface, no radial constraint initially)}$$
$$\sigma_3 = \sigma_z = -250 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$
$$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(0-0)^2 + (0-(-250))^2 + (-250-0)^2}{2}}$$
$$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0 + 62{,}500 + 62{,}500}{2}} = \sqrt{62{,}500} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Conclusion:** $\bar{\sigma} = \sigma_y$, confirming yielding has initiated. Since $\sigma_3 < 0$ is the largest magnitude stress, the process is **COMPRESSION-DOMINANT** ✓.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Verifying Tension-Dominance in Wire Drawing

**Problem:** A copper wire is drawn from $D_0 = 10 \text{ mm}$ to $D_f = 7 \text{ mm}$. The average flow stress is $\bar{\sigma} = 220 \text{ MPa}$, the die semi-angle is $\alpha = 8^\circ$, and the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.08$. Estimate the drawing stress and confirm the process is **tension-dominant**.

### Step 1: True Strain
$$\varepsilon = 2 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_f}\right) = 2\ln\!\left(\dfrac{10}{7}\right) = 2 \times 0.3567 = 0.7133$$

### Step 2: Area Reduction Ratio
$$r = 1 - \left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_0}\right)^2 = 1 - \left(\dfrac{7}{10}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.49 = 0.51$$
$$\Delta A = A_0 - A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(10^2 - 7^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(100 - 49) = 40.06 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 3: Drawing Stress (Slab Method)
The standard slab-analysis formula for the drawing stress $\sigma_d$ is:
$$\sigma_d = \bar{\sigma}\left[\dfrac{1 + B}{B}\right]\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_f}{A_0}\right)^{B}\right]$$
where
$$B = \dfrac{\mu}{\tan\alpha}$$
$$B = \dfrac{0.08}{\tan(8^\circ)} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.1405} = 0.5693$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Evaluate
$$\sigma_d = 220 \left[\dfrac{1 + 0.5693}{0.5693}\right]\left[1 - (0.49)^{0.5693}\right]$$
$$\sigma_d = 220 \times (2.7565) \times \left[1 - 0.6615\right]$$
$$\sigma_d = 220 \times 2.7565 \times 0.3385$$
$$\sigma_d \approx 205.3 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5: Verify Dominance
- $\sigma_d$ is the **axial tensile stress** pulling the wire through the die.
- $\sigma_1 = \sigma_d = +205.3 \text{ MPa}$ (tensile, along drawing axis).
- $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 \approx 0$ (radial and hoop, free surfaces).

Since $\sigma_1 > 0$ and is the *largest* principal stress, the process is **TENSION-DOMINANT** ✓.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shear-Dominance in a Blanking Operation

**Problem:** A 5 mm thick aluminum sheet is blanked using a punch with a punch-die clearance of $c = 0.10 \text{ mm}$. Estimate the punching force and confirm the operation is **shear-dominant**.

### Step 1: Identify the Stress State
In blanking, the punch pushes down on the sheet confined between the die opening. The deformation zone is a *narrow band* of width $c$ at the die edge, where the **shear stress** $\tau$ is dominant.
$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{P}{A_{shear}}$$

### Step 2: Shear Strength
For aluminum, the ultimate shear strength $\tau_u \approx 0.75 \times \sigma_{UTS}$.
Assume $\sigma_{UTS} = 180 \text{ MPa}$. Then:
$$\tau_u = 0.75 \times 180 = 135 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3: Perimeter of Cut
Let the blank be a rectangle $100 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm}$.
$$L = 2(100 + 60) = 320 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Shear Area
$$A_{shear} = L \times t = 320 \times 5 = 1{,}600 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 5: Punching Force
$$P = \tau_u \times A_{shear} = 135 \times 1{,}600 = 216{,}000 \text{ N} = 216 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 6: Dominance Check
- The punch is loaded in axial compression (compressive) only to *initiate* failure.
- The *fracture* propagates along the **shear plane** defined by the clearance $c$.
- Maximum shear stress theory: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$.

Since the fracture surface is *shear-dominated*, the classification is **SHEAR-DOMINANT** ✓.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| **Process** | **$\sigma_1$ (MPa)** | **$\sigma_2$ (MPa)** | **$\sigma_3$ (MPa)** | **Dominant Stress** | **Classification** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Wire Drawing | $+205.3$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\sigma_1$ (+) | **Tension** |
| Upset Forging | $0$ | $0$ | $-250$ | $\vert\sigma_3\vert$ | **Compression** |
| Blanking (in shear zone) | $+90$ | $0$ | $-90$ | $\tau_{xy}$ | **Shear** |
| Deep Drawing (wall) | $+350$ | $-100$ | $0$ | Mixed | **Combined (T + C)** |
| Bending (outer fiber) | $+200$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\sigma_1$ (+) | **Combined (T outer, C inner)** |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Master Classification of Metal Forming Processes


## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Stress Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Workpiece Input Geometry"] --> B["Boundary Condition Analysis"]
    B --> C{"Principal Stress Identification"}
    C --> D1["sigma1 greater than 0 Dominant"]
    C --> D2["sigma3 less than 0 Dominant"]
    C --> D3["tau max Dominant"]
    C --> D4["Mixed Multi Axial State"]

    D1 --> E1["TENSION CLASS"]
    D2 --> E2["COMPRESSION CLASS"]
    D3 --> E3["SHEAR CLASS"]
    D4 --> E4["COMBINED CLASS"]

    E1 --> F1["Tool Design: Carbide Drawing Dies"]
    E2 --> F2["Tool Design: Hot Die Steel H13"]
    E3 --> F3["Tool Design: D2 High Carbon Die Steel"]
    E4 --> F4["Tool Design: Multi Stage Compound Die"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Illustration — Free-Body Diagrams (Descriptive)

Since Mermaid cannot render true engineering FBDs, the following is a **structured functional description** of the stress vector arrangement per process:

```
┌──────────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────────┐
│   TENSION-DOMINANT (FBD) │    │  COMPRESSION-DOMINANT    │
│                          │    │                          │
│  F →  [WORKPIECE]  ← F   │    │  ↓ F                     │
│  (uniaxial pull)         │    │  [WORKPIECE]             │
│  σ₁ > 0, σ₂=σ₃=0         │    │  ↑ F                     │
│  e.g. Wire Drawing       │    │  σ₃ < 0, σ₁=σ₂=0         │
└──────────────────────────┘    │  e.g. Forging/Extrusion  │
                                 └──────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────────┐
│   SHEAR-DOMINANT (FBD)   │    │  COMBINED-STRESS (FBD)   │
│                          │    │                          │
│  ↓ P         ↑ Reaction  │    │  Outer: ←─────F (Tension)│
│  ────► τ     ◄───        │    │  [  BEND  ]              │
│  σ₁=+τ, σ₃=-τ           │    │  Inner: F────→(Compress.)│
│  e.g. Blanking/Shear     │    │  σ₁ > 0 AND σ₃ < 0      │
└──────────────────────────┘    │  e.g. Bending/Deep Draw  │
                                 └──────────────────────────┘
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Classify metal forming processes based on the type of stress developed. Give two examples for each category.**

**Model Answer:**

Metal forming processes are classified into four categories based on the **dominant state of stress** in the deformation zone:

1. **Tensile-Stress Dominant:** Wire drawing, tube drawing, stretch forming.
2. **Compressive-Stress Dominant:** Forging, rolling, extrusion, coining.
3. **Shear-Stress Dominant:** Blanking, piercing, slitting, shearing.
4. **Combined-Stress Dominant:** Bending, deep drawing, spinning, hydroforming.

*(2 marks for classification + 1 mark for examples — full 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State Von Mises yield criterion. Why is it important in classifying metal forming processes?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Von Mises yield criterion** states that yielding begins when the second deviatoric stress invariant reaches a critical value:

$$\left(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2\right)^2 + \left(\sigma_2 - \sigma_3\right)^2 + \left(\sigma_3 - \sigma_1\right)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2$$

**Importance:**
- It allows **multi-axial stress states** (which exist in all real forming processes) to be reduced to a **single effective stress** $\bar{\sigma}$.
- It provides a **unified mathematical basis** to compare the severity of tension, compression, shear, and combined stress states on the same scale.
- It is the foundation of FEM-based process simulation in software like DEFORM and ABAQUS.

*(2 marks for equation + 1 mark for importance — full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**a)** A cylindrical billet of initial diameter $D_0 = 60 \text{ mm}$ and height $H_0 = 80 \text{ mm}$ is upset-forged in a **compressive-stress dominant** process to a final diameter of $D_f = 45 \text{ mm}$. The material is mild steel with $\sigma_y = 280 \text{ MPa}$. Compute:
   (i) The final height of the billet. (4 marks)
   (ii) The true strain in the axial direction. (2 marks)
   (iii) The Von Mises effective stress at the onset of yielding. (4 marks)

**b)** Justify with a free-body diagram why upset forging is classified as a **compression-dominant** process and not a tension-dominant one. (4 marks)

---

#### **Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1: Volume conservation assumption** (incompressible plastic flow, valid for most metals at room temperature):
$$\dfrac{\pi}{4}D_0^2 H_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D_f^2 H_f$$

**Step 2: Final height calculation**
$$H_f = \dfrac{D_0^2 \, H_0}{D_f^2} = \dfrac{(60)^2(80)}{(45)^2} = \dfrac{3600 \times 80}{2025} = \dfrac{288{,}000}{2025} = 142.22 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: True strain in axial direction**
$$\varepsilon_z = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{H_f}{H_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{142.22}{80}\right) = \ln(1.7778) = 0.5754$$

**Step 4: Principal stresses at yielding (frictionless assumption)**
- $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = 0$ (free lateral surface)
- $\sigma_3 = -\sigma_y = -280 \text{ MPa}$ (axial compression)

**Step 5: Von Mises effective stress**
$$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$
$$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0 + (0-(-280))^2 + (-280-0)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{78{,}400 + 78{,}400}{2}}$$
$$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{78{,}400} = 280 \text{ MPa} = \sigma_y \;\;\checkmark$$

### Part (b) — FBD Justification

In upset forging, the punch applies a **downward axial force $F$** while the anvil provides an **upward reaction force $F$**. Both are *compressive*. The radial expansion of the billet causes *tensile* secondary stresses $\sigma_r = \sigma_\theta$ in the hoop and radial directions, but these are *passive* (Poisson-induced) and are *much smaller* in magnitude than the *active* compressive axial stress.

Therefore:
$$\vert \sigma_3 \vert_{\text{axial}} \gg \vert \sigma_1 \vert_{\text{radial}}$$
$$\therefore \text{Dominant stress} = \text{COMPRESSION}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023] *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** A copper wire is drawn from $D_0 = 12 \text{ mm}$ to $D_f = 8 \text{ mm}$ through a conical die with semi-angle $\alpha = 6^\circ$. The average flow stress is $\bar{\sigma} = 250 \text{ MPa}$ and the friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.05$. Calculate:
   (i) The drawing stress using the slab method. (5 marks)
   (ii) The Von Mises effective stress. (3 marks)
   (iii) Confirm that the process is **tension-dominant**. (3 marks)

**b)** Compare **wire drawing** (tension) and **wire flattening** (compression) in terms of dominant stress, tool material, and product application. (3 marks)

---

#### **Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1: True strain in drawing**
$$\varepsilon = 2 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_f}\right) = 2\ln\!\left(\dfrac{12}{8}\right) = 2\ln(1.5) = 2 \times 0.4055 = 0.8109$$

**Step 2: Compute the parameter $B$**
$$B = \dfrac{\mu}{\tan\alpha} = \dfrac{0.05}{\tan(6^\circ)} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.1051} = 0.4757$$

**Step 3: Compute the area ratio**
$$\dfrac{A_f}{A_0} = \left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_0}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{8}{12}\right)^2 = (0.6667)^2 = 0.4444$$

**Step 4: Drawing stress using slab method**
$$\sigma_d = \bar{\sigma}\!\left[\dfrac{1 + B}{B}\right]\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_f}{A_0}\right)^{B}\right]$$
$$\sigma_d = 250 \times \left[\dfrac{1 + 0.4757}{0.4757}\right] \times \left[1 - (0.4444)^{0.4757}\right]$$
$$\sigma_d = 250 \times (3.1024) \times \left[1 - 0.6926\right]$$
$$\sigma_d = 250 \times 3.1024 \times 0.3074$$
$$\sigma_d \approx 238.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Von Mises effective stress**
For uniaxial tension $\sigma_1 = \sigma_d$ and $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$:
$$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1 - 0)^2 + (0 - 0)^2 + (0 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2} = \vert\sigma_d\vert = 238.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Dominance check**
- $\sigma_1 = +238.4 \text{ MPa}$ (tensile, axial)
- $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$
- Since $\sigma_1 > 0$ and is the *largest* principal stress magnitude, the process is **TENSION-DOMINANT** ✓

### Part (b) — Comparison Table

| **Aspect** | **Wire Drawing (Tension)** | **Wire Flattening (Compression)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dominant Stress** | $\sigma_1$ (axial, tensile) | $\sigma_3$ (axial, compressive) |
| **Tool Material** | Carbide / Diamond dies | D2 / H13 hardened steel rolls |
| **Product Application** | Electrical conductors, cables | Rectangular sections, spring flats |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps (Examiner-Insider View):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up wire drawing and extrusion**: Many students wrongly classify **wire drawing** as a *compression* process because the material is "squeezed through a die." In reality, the **drawing force is tensile** — the material is *pulled*, not pushed. The die provides a *reaction* but the *driver* is a tensile pull at the exit. **Lose 2 marks if this is wrong.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting volume conservation in upset forging**: Numerical questions on compression-dominant forging will *not* explicitly state that volume is conserved. You must *state this assumption* in Step 1 of your solution. **Lose 1 mark** if not mentioned.
>
> 3. **Confusing deep drawing with wire drawing**: Both are tension-dominant in the *wall*, but **deep drawing has a compression-dominant flange**. KTU expects you to call it a **combined-stress** process, not pure tension. **Lose 2 marks** for misclassification.
>
> 4. **Sign convention error**: In principal stress notation, tensile is **positive (+)** and compressive is **negative (−)**. Reversing these signs gives a *completely wrong* Von Mises value. Always write the sign explicitly in your solution.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers**: The final answer in a Part B numerical *must* have units (MPa, mm, etc.). KTU examiners follow a strict "no unit = 0.5 mark deduction" rule.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Metal forming is a **chip-less** manufacturing process based on **plastic deformation**.
- ✅ The four dominant stress states are: **Tension (T), Compression (C), Shear (S), Combined (Mixed)**.
- ✅ **Tension-dominant processes**: Wire drawing, tube drawing, stretch forming.
- ✅ **Compression-dominant processes**: Forging, rolling, extrusion, coining, upsetting.
- ✅ **Shear-dominant processes**: Blanking, piercing, slitting, shearing, trimming.
- ✅ **Combined-stress processes**: Bending, deep drawing, spinning, hydroforming, roll forming.
- ✅ The **Von Mises yield criterion** is the *unified mathematical basis* for comparing all stress states.
- ✅ **Volume conservation** is assumed in *all* bulk deformation processes: $V_0 = V_f$.
- ✅ **True strain** in compression/drawing: $\varepsilon = \ln(H_f/H_0) = 2\ln(D_0/D_f)$.
- ✅ **Slab analysis** is the primary analytical tool for *tension* (drawing) and *compression* (forging) processes.
- ✅ Sign convention: **Tensile (+), Compressive (−)**, $\tau$ may be positive or negative.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic compressive stress** improves ductility → compression-dominant processes are *safer* for brittle materials.
- ✅ **Tensile residual stresses** at the surface cause *fatigue failure* — a reason why drawn wires are often stress-relieved.
- ✅ Always state the **assumptions** in numerical problems: *frictionless*, *incompressible*, *uniform deformation*, *isothermal*.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: **(1) State assumption → (2) Diagram → (3) Equation → (4) Substitution → (5) Answer with units → (6) Justification / FBD**.
- ✅ Internal choice (Q-A or Q-B) is mandatory in KTU ESE — *practice both variants*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Plastic Deformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Theory of Plastic Deformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plastic Deformation** is defined as the permanent, non-recoverable change in the shape or dimensions of a solid material body when subjected to an external load that exceeds its **elastic limit (yield strength)**. In the context of metal forming, plastic deformation is the fundamental mechanism by which bulk metal is reshaped into useful engineering components through processes such as forging, rolling, extrusion, and drawing.

In mathematical terms, when the applied stress $\sigma$ exceeds the material's **yield stress $\sigma_y$** (or **Y**), the strain $\varepsilon$ no longer remains recoverable, and the deformation becomes permanent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Theory of Plastic Deformation forms the foundation block for all metal forming analyses. Mastery of stress-strain relationships, yield criteria, and work calculations is **mandatory** for deriving forming loads, die pressures, and energy requirements in subsequent modules.

The classical strain decomposition under any loading path can be written as:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{elastic} + \varepsilon_{plastic}$$

The elastic portion is recovered upon unloading; the plastic portion remains permanently imprinted in the workpiece geometry.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a soft ball of **kneaded dough (wheat flour + water)** resting on a kitchen counter:

- **Gentle pressing** with your thumb → The dough flattens slightly, but springs back when you remove your finger. This is **elastic deformation**.
- **Forceful pressing** with the heel of your palm → The dough flattens and **stays flat** even after you remove the load. This is **plastic deformation**.

The same dough, when pressed unevenly (one side thicker than the other), shows **inhomogeneous deformation** — the central region flows more than the edges because the edges are constrained by friction against the counter.

In metals, the "dough" consists of a crystalline lattice structure (grains) in which **dislocations (line defects)** glide past one another on crystallographic slip planes when the shear stress exceeds the **critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Yield Strength | $k$ or $\tau_y$ | **165 – 250** | MPa |
| Tensile Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ or $Y$ | **250 – 400** | MPa |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **200 – 210** | GPa |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.28 – 0.33** | dimensionless |
| Strength Coefficient | $K$ | **500 – 1500** | MPa |
| Strain Hardening Exponent | $n$ | **0.10 – 0.25** | dimensionless |
| Melting Temperature | $T_m$ | **1500 – 1530** | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Relation between $\sigma_y$ and $k$:** For a perfectly plastic material obeying the **Tresca yield criterion**, $\sigma_y = 2k$. For materials obeying the **von Mises criterion**, $\sigma_y = \sqrt{3} \, k \approx 1.732 \, k$. This distinction is critical in KTU problems on forging and extrusion load estimation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stress–Strain Curve: Engineering vs. True

When a cylindrical tensile specimen is loaded, the relationship between load $F$ and instantaneous cross-sectional area $A$ gives rise to two distinct stress measures.

### Engineering Stress and Engineering Strain
These are based on **original dimensions** $A_0$ and $L_0$:

$$\sigma_0 = \frac{F}{A_0}, \qquad e = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

### True Stress and True Strain
These are based on **instantaneous dimensions** $A$ and $L$:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}, \qquad \varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Engineering stress-strain is what is **directly measured** in a tensile test. True stress-strain is what is **theoretically required** for accurate forming analysis. KTU problems on rolling and forging **always use true stress** unless explicitly told otherwise.

### Conversion Relationships

$$\sigma = \sigma_0 \, (1 + e)$$

$$\varepsilon = \ln(1 + e)$$

## 2.2 Plastic Deformation Mechanisms

Metals deform plastically through the following atomic-level mechanisms:

1. **Slip** — Dislocations glide along close-packed crystallographic planes (e.g., $\{111\}\langle 110\rangle$ in FCC metals). This is the **dominant** mechanism.
2. **Twinning** — A portion of the crystal reorients to form a mirror image across a twin plane. Common in HCP metals (e.g., Mg, Zn) at low temperatures.
3. **Grain Boundary Sliding** — Significant at **hot working** temperatures (above $0.6 \, T_m$).
4. **Diffusion Creep** — Important at very high temperatures and low strain rates (Coble creep, Nabarro-Herring creep).

## 2.3 Yield Criteria (Most KTU-Favorite Topic)

### Tresca Criterion (Maximum Shear Stress Theory)
Yields when the **maximum shear stress** reaches a critical value:

$$\max \left( \tfrac{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert}{2}, \tfrac{\vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert}{2}, \tfrac{\vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert}{2} \right) = k$$

Or equivalently:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2k = \sigma_y$$

### Von Mises Criterion (Distortion Energy Theory)
Yields when the **distortion strain energy** reaches a critical value:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2 \sigma_y^{\,2} = 6 k^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> Von Mises is more **physically accurate** and matches experimental data for ductile metals better than Tresca. However, Tresca is **mathematically simpler** and is preferred for hand calculations in forging and extrusion.

## 2.4 Flow Curve and Strain Hardening

As plastic deformation proceeds, most metals exhibit **strain hardening** (work hardening) — the flow stress increases with strain. The empirical **Hollomon power law** captures this:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_f = K \, \varepsilon^{\,n}\;}$$

where:
- $K$ = **strength coefficient (MPa)**
- $n$ = **strain hardening exponent** (0 for perfectly plastic, ~1 for perfectly elastic)

## 2.5 Homogeneous vs. Inhomogeneous Deformation

- **Homogeneous deformation**: Every point in the workpiece deforms by the same amount. Example: **idealized compression of a frictionless cylinder** (slab analysis).
- **Inhomogeneous deformation**: Different regions deform by different amounts. Example: **barrel-shaped compression** of a cylinder due to interface friction.

The departure from homogeneity is quantified by the **inhomogeneity index**:

$$\eta = \frac{\varepsilon_{max} - \varepsilon_{min}}{\varepsilon_{mean}}$$

## 2.6 Temperature Regimes in Metal Forming

| Regime | Temperature Range | Material State |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold Working** | $T < 0.3 \, T_m$ | Strain hardened, higher strength, poor ductility |
| **Warm Working** | $0.3 \, T_m \le T < 0.6 \, T_m$ | Partial strain hardening, moderate forces |
| **Hot Working** | $T \ge 0.6 \, T_m$ | Fully recrystallized, lowest strength, best ductility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a problem mentions "annealed aluminum" or "hot forging of steel at 1200 °C," always verify which temperature regime applies and use the corresponding flow stress value.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F / A$ | True stress definition | Universal forming |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \ln(L / L_0)$ | True strain definition | Tensile/Compression |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = 2 \ln(D_0 / D)$ | True strain in diameter reduction | Drawing, Extrusion |
| 4 | $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$ | Hollomon flow curve | Strain hardening |
| 5 | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2k$ | Tresca yield criterion | Forging load estimation |
| 6 | $6k^2 = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + \cdots$ | Von Mises yield criterion | Plane strain (rolling) |
| 7 | $\sigma_y = \sqrt{3} \, k$ | Von Mises–Tresca conversion | Ductile metal forming |
| 8 | $\sigma_y = 2k$ | Tresca–uniaxial conversion | Forging, Extrusion |
| 9 | $\bar{p} = \sigma_f (1 + \mu d / 3h)$ | Avg. pressure in compression w/ friction | Slab analysis |
| 10 | $F = \bar{p} \cdot A$ | Forming load | All bulk processes |
| 11 | $W_i = V \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Ideal work | Energy balance |
| 12 | $\eta_{eff} = W_i / W_a$ | Process efficiency (0 < $\eta < 1$) | Energy studies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention (KTU-Standard):** In the above table, all formulas use **true stress** $\sigma$ and **true strain** $\varepsilon$. Do **not** confuse with engineering quantities $\sigma_0$ and $e$, which are used only for raw tensile-test data conversion.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Theory of Plastic Deformation underpins every metal forming operation in the manufacturing industry:

- **Automotive forging**: Predicting die pressures for crankshaft forging.
- **Steel rolling mills**: Calculating roll separating forces in plate rolling.
- **Aluminum extrusion**: Estimating ram loads in profile extrusion presses.
- **Wire drawing**: Determining drawing force and allowable reduction per pass.
- **Sheet metal forming (stamping)**: Predicting failure (necking) using forming limit diagrams (FLD).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between True and Engineering Stress

**Given:** Engineering stress $\sigma_0 = F / A_0$ and engineering strain $e = (L - L_0) / L_0$.

**To Prove:** $\sigma = \sigma_0 (1 + e)$.

**Assumption:** Plastic deformation occurs at **constant volume** (incompressibility) — a cornerstone of metal forming theory.

$$V_0 = A_0 L_0 = A L \quad \text{(constant volume assumption)}$$

**Step 1.** Solve for instantaneous area $A$ in terms of initial area $A_0$ and strains:

$$A = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L} = \frac{A_0}{L / L_0} = \frac{A_0}{1 + e}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute into the true stress definition:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{A_0 / (1 + e)} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot (1 + e)$$

**Step 3.** Recognize that $F / A_0 = \sigma_0$:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma = \sigma_0 \, (1 + e)\;}$$

This shows that for tensile loading, true stress is always **greater than or equal to** engineering stress.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between True and Engineering Strain

**Given:** $e = (L - L_0)/L_0$, hence $L = L_0(1+e)$.

**Step 1.** Substitute into the true strain integral definition:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left( \frac{L}{L_0} \right)$$

**Step 2.** Replace $L$:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left( \frac{L_0(1+e)}{L_0} \right) = \ln(1+e)$$

**Step 3.** Final boxed result:

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon = \ln(1 + e)\;}$$

For small strains ($e < 0.1$), $\varepsilon \approx e$ to within **5%** accuracy.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Von Mises Yield Criterion (Plane Strain Case)

Consider a 2D plane-strain deformation (typical in **rolling**), where $\varepsilon_z = 0$, so:

$$\sigma_z = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$$

Substituting into the general von Mises expression:

$$(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (\sigma_y - \sigma_z)^2 + (\sigma_z - \sigma_x)^2 = 2 \sigma_y^{\,2}$$

**Step 1.** Substitute $\sigma_z = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/2$:

$$\sigma_y - \sigma_z = \sigma_y - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_x}{2}$$

$$\sigma_z - \sigma_x = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sigma_x = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_x}{2}$$

**Step 2.** The first term is unchanged:

$$(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2$$

**Step 3.** The other two terms become equal:

$$\left( \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_x}{2} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_x}{2} \right)^2 = 2 \cdot \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{4} = \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{2}$$

**Step 4.** Add them:

$$(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{2} = \frac{3}{2}(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2$$

**Step 5.** Set equal to $2 \sigma_y^{\,2}$:

$$\frac{3}{2}(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 = 2 \sigma_y^{\,2}$$

$$(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \sigma_y^{\,2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_x - \sigma_y = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \, \sigma_y = 2k\;}$$

This is the famous result: **in plane strain, the von Mises criterion reduces to a Tresca-like form** with $k = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$, and this is widely used in rolling-mill calculations.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Average Pressure in Frictional Compression (Slab Method)

**Setup:** A cylindrical workpiece of diameter $d$ and height $h$ is compressed between two rough platens. Coefficient of friction is $\mu$.

**Step 1.** Take a thin horizontal slab of thickness $dh$ at height $y$ from the bottom. Consider equilibrium in the horizontal direction.

**Step 2.** By slab analysis (force balance on element), the axial stress $\sigma_x$ varies with position due to interface friction:

$$\frac{d\sigma_x}{dx} = \frac{2 \mu \sigma_x}{h}$$

where $\sigma_x$ is the pressure (axial stress) and $h$ is the current workpiece height.

**Step 3.** Solve the differential equation:

$$\frac{d\sigma_x}{\sigma_x} = \frac{2 \mu \, dx}{h}$$

$$\ln \sigma_x = \frac{2 \mu x}{h} + C$$

**Step 4.** Apply symmetry boundary condition: at the center $x = 0$, the pressure equals the **flow stress** $\sigma_f$ (since there is no friction at the centerline).

$$\sigma_x(0) = \sigma_f \implies C = \ln \sigma_f$$

$$\sigma_x(x) = \sigma_f \exp\!\left( \frac{2 \mu x}{h} \right)$$

**Step 5.** Average pressure across the contact radius $R = d/2$:

$$\bar{p} = \frac{1}{R} \int_0^{R} \sigma_f \exp\!\left( \frac{2 \mu x}{h} \right) dx$$

**Step 6.** Evaluate the integral:

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\sigma_f h}{2 \mu R} \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{2 \mu R}{h} \right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 7.** For small values of $\mu R / h$ (typical in forging), expand the exponential as a Taylor series $e^x \approx 1 + x + x^2/2$ and retain first-order terms:

$$\bar{p} \approx \frac{\sigma_f h}{2 \mu R} \cdot \frac{2 \mu R}{h} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{\mu R}{h} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\bar{p} = \sigma_f \left( 1 + \frac{\mu d}{3 h} \right)\;}$$

This is the famous **Siebel's formula** for average forging pressure under frictional conditions.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Ideal Work of Deformation (Homogeneous Compression)

**Step 1.** For homogeneous compression of a cylinder from height $h_0$ to $h_f$, the true strain is:

$$\varepsilon = \ln(h_0 / h_f)$$

**Step 2.** The instantaneous force is $F = \sigma_f \cdot A$. Under constant volume, $A = A_0 h_0 / h$.

$$F(h) = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_0 h_0}{h}$$

**Step 3.** The incremental work is $dW = F \, dh$:

$$dW = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_0 h_0}{h} \, dh$$

**Step 4.** Integrate from $h_0$ to $h_f$:

$$W = \int_{h_0}^{h_f} \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_0 h_0}{h} \, dh = \sigma_f A_0 h_0 \int_{h_0}^{h_f} \frac{dh}{h}$$

$$\boxed{\;W_i = \sigma_f V \ln\!\left( \frac{h_0}{h_f} \right) = \sigma_f V \varepsilon\;}$$

where $V = A_0 h_0$ is the workpiece volume. This is the **ideal work** — the minimum energy required for the deformation ignoring friction and redundant work.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress–Strain Curve (Functional Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Origin O] --> A1[Linear Elastic Region]
    A1 -->|Hookes Law slope E| A2[Yield Point Y]
    A2 --> A3[Strain Hardening Region]
    A3 --> A4[Ultimate Tensile Strength UTS]
    A4 --> A5[Necking Begins]
    A5 --> A6[Fracture Point]
    
    A1 -.->|Recoverable| A7[Unload along slope E]
    A7 -.-> A0
    A6 -.->|Permanent set| A8[Residual Strain]
    A8 -.-> A0
```

## 4.2 Plastic Deformation Mechanism Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Plastic Deformation Mechanism] --> B1[Slip Dominant]
    B0 --> B2[Twinning]
    B0 --> B3[Grain Boundary Sliding]
    B0 --> B4[Diffusion Creep]
    
    B1 --> B11[FCC Metals at Low T]
    B1 --> B12[BCC Metals Moderate T]
    B1 --> B13[Dislocation Glide on Close Packed Planes]
    
    B2 --> B21[HCP Metals Mg Zn]
    B2 --> B22[Low Temperature Impact Loading]
    
    B3 --> B31[T greater than point six Tm]
    B3 --> B32[Superplastic Forming]
    
    B4 --> B41[Coble Creep]
    B4 --> B42[Nabarro Herring Creep]
    B4 --> B43[Power Law Creep]
```

## 4.3 Yield Criteria Comparison (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Stress State Sigma1 Sigma2 Sigma3] --> C1{Which Theory}
    C1 -->|Max Shear| C2[Tresca Hexagonal Yield Surface]
    C1 -->|Distortion Energy| C3[Von Mises Cylindrical Yield Surface]
    
    C2 --> C4[Result sigma1 minus sigma3 equals 2k]
    C3 --> C5[Result sum of squared differences equals 6 k squared]
    
    C4 --> C6[Mathematically Simpler]
    C4 --> C7[More Conservative in Uniaxial Tension]
    
    C5 --> C8[Physically More Accurate]
    C5 --> C9[Better Fit to Experimental Data]
```

## 4.4 Forming Process Selection Based on Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Workpiece at Temperature T] --> D1{T compared to 0 point 3 Tm}
    D1 -->|Less than 0.3 Tm| D2[Cold Working]
    D1 -->|0.3 to 0.6 Tm| D3[Warm Working]
    D1 -->|Greater than 0.6 Tm| D4[Hot Working]
    
    D2 --> D21[Strain Hardening Significant]
    D2 --> D22[High Forming Loads]
    D2 --> D23[Good Surface Finish]
    D2 --> D24[Examples Cold Rolling Wire Drawing]
    
    D3 --> D31[Partial Recovery]
    D3 --> D32[Moderate Loads]
    D3 --> D33[Examples Warm Forging]
    
    D4 --> D41[Dynamic Recrystallization]
    D4 --> D42[Lowest Flow Stress]
    D4 --> D43[Risk of Oxidation and Grain Growth]
    D4 --> D44[Examples Hot Rolling Hot Forging]
```

## 4.5 Force and Pressure Flow in Slab Analysis (Compression)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E0[Workpiece Top Surface p avg] --> E1[Slab Element dx wide]
    E1 --> E2[Axial Stress sigma x acts downward]
    E1 --> E3[Friction mu sigma x acts radially]
    E3 --> E1
    E1 --> E4[Equilibrium d sigma x over dx equals 2 mu sigma x over h]
    E4 --> E5[Solution sigma x equals sigma f exp of 2 mu x over h]
    E5 --> E6[Integrate to get p avg equals sigma f times bracket 1 plus mu d over 3 h close bracket]
```

## 4.6 Strain Hardening Behavior (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Plastic Strain epsilon increases] --> F1[Dislocation Density increases]
    F1 --> F2[Dislocations interact and entangle]
    F2 --> F3[Further slip becomes harder]
    F3 --> F4[Flow Stress sigma f increases]
    F4 --> F5[Hollomon Law sigma f equals K epsilon to the power n]
    
    F5 --> F6{Process}
    F6 -->|Hot Work T greater than 0.6 Tm| F7[Recrystallization Removes Hardening]
    F6 -->|Cold Work T less than 0.3 Tm| F8[Hardening Accumulates]
    F6 -->|Annealing at Moderate T| F9[Recovery then Recrystallization]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms:**
(i) True stress  
(ii) True strain  
(iii) Flow stress

**Model Answer:**

**(i) True Stress ($\sigma$):** The ratio of the instantaneous applied load $F$ to the instantaneous cross-sectional area $A$ of the workpiece.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

**(ii) True Strain ($\varepsilon$):** The natural logarithmic ratio of the instantaneous dimension to the original dimension. For a tensile specimen:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left( \frac{L}{L_0} \right) = \ln\!\left( \frac{A_0}{A} \right)$$

**(iii) Flow Stress ($\sigma_f$):** The instantaneous value of true stress required to continue plastic deformation at a given strain, strain rate, and temperature. Mathematically:

$$\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n \quad \text{(Hollomon equation for cold working)}$$

**[Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1]**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain Tresca's yield criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Tresca's yield criterion states that yielding in a ductile material begins when the **maximum shear stress** in the material reaches a critical value equal to the shear yield strength $k$ in a uniaxial tension test.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$\max \left( \tfrac{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert}{2}, \, \tfrac{\vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert}{2}, \, \tfrac{\vert \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 \vert}{2} \right) = k$$

For ordered principal stresses $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$, this simplifies to:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2k = \sigma_y$$

**Explanation:** The criterion is based on the experimental observation that ductile fracture in metals occurs primarily by shear on the plane of maximum shear stress. It is mathematically simpler than the von Mises criterion but more conservative (predicts yielding earlier).

**[Marks Distribution: 1.5 + 1.5]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between true stress and engineering stress. A steel specimen with initial diameter $12 \, \text{mm}$ and gauge length $50 \, \text{mm}$ is subjected to a tensile load. If the load at yielding is $38 \, \text{kN}$ and the final diameter after fracture is $8 \, \text{mm}$, calculate:
(i) Engineering stress at yielding
(ii) True stress at yielding
(iii) True strain at fracture

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the differences between **homogeneous deformation** and **inhomogeneous deformation** in metal forming. How does friction at the tool-workpiece interface cause inhomogeneity? Derive the expression for average pressure in compression with friction using slab analysis.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:

**(a) Solution:**

**Derivation (already covered in Section 3.1):**

From constant volume $A_0 L_0 = A L$, we obtain $\sigma = \sigma_0(1+e)$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- Initial diameter $d_0 = 12 \, \text{mm}$, so $A_0 = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(12)^2 = 113.10 \, \text{mm}^2$
- Initial gauge length $L_0 = 50 \, \text{mm}$
- Yield load $F_y = 38 \, \text{kN} = 38000 \, \text{N}$

**(i) Engineering Stress at Yielding:**

$$\sigma_0 = \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{38000}{113.10} = 336.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

**(ii) True Stress at Yielding:**

At the onset of yielding, the strain is still zero (idealized). So $A \approx A_0$ and:

$$\sigma = \sigma_0(1 + e_y) \approx \sigma_0 = 336.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

> If the elastic strain is included, $e_y = \sigma_y / E = 336/210000 \approx 0.0016$, giving $\sigma \approx 336.5 \, \text{MPa}$ (negligible difference).

**(iii) True Strain at Fracture:**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(8)^2 = 50.27 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$\varepsilon_f = \ln\!\left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) = \ln\!\left( \frac{113.10}{50.27} \right) = \ln(2.250) = 0.8109$$

**[Marks Distribution: Derivation 2 + Numerical 5]**

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Homogeneous vs. Inhomogeneous Deformation:**

| Aspect | Homogeneous | Inhomogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| Strain distribution | Uniform throughout | Varies with position |
| Cylinder shape after compression | Stays flat | Becomes barrel-shaped |
| Friction condition | Frictionless platens | Frictional contact |
| Strain rate | Same everywhere | Different in different zones |

**Sketch Description:**

- *Homogeneous*: Cylinder height reduces uniformly; diameter increases uniformly; cross-section remains rectangular.
- *Inhomogeneous*: Edges bulge outward (barrel shape); central region is constrained vertically; strain is maximum at equator and minimum at tool-workpiece interface.

**Effect of Friction:** Friction at the platen interface **restricts radial flow** of material near the top and bottom surfaces. The central region is unconstrained radially and flows outward, creating the **barrel shape**. Hence, friction is the primary cause of inhomogeneity.

**Derivation of Average Pressure (already covered in Section 3.4):**

$$\bar{p} = \sigma_f \left( 1 + \frac{\mu d}{3 h} \right)$$

> The bracketed term is the **friction hill factor**. It is always $\ge 1$ and increases with $\mu$ and $d/h$ ratio.

**[Marks Distribution: Hom vs Inhom 2 + Friction effect 2 + Derivation 3]**

---

### QUESTION B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and derive the **von Mises yield criterion** in terms of principal stresses. A thin steel sheet is subjected to biaxial stresses $\sigma_1 = 250 \, \text{MPa}$ (tensile) and $\sigma_2 = 150 \, \text{MPa}$ (compressive) in the plane of the sheet. If the uniaxial yield strength is $400 \, \text{MPa}$, determine whether yielding will occur according to **both Tresca and von Mises criteria**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Hollomon power law** for strain hardening. A copper alloy exhibits the following true stress-strain data:
- At $\varepsilon = 0.20$, $\sigma = 350 \, \text{MPa}$
- At $\varepsilon = 0.50$, $\sigma = 500 \, \text{MPa}$

Calculate:
(i) The strength coefficient $K$ and strain hardening exponent $n$
(ii) The flow stress at $\varepsilon = 0.30$

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:

**(a) Solution:**

**Statement of von Mises Criterion:**

The von Mises criterion states that yielding occurs when the **second invariant of the deviatoric stress tensor** reaches a critical value. In principal stress form:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^{\,2}$$

**Derivation (sketch, full derivation already in Section 3.3):**

It can be derived from the principle that yielding occurs when the **distortion strain energy** $U_d$ equals the distortion strain energy at yielding in a uniaxial test. The relationship between deviatoric stresses and strain energy density gives the boxed equation above.

**Numerical Application:**

Given: $\sigma_1 = +250 \, \text{MPa}$, $\sigma_2 = -150 \, \text{MPa}$, $\sigma_3 = 0$ (plane stress), $\sigma_y = 400 \, \text{MPa}$.

**Tresca Check:**
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 250 - (-150) = 400 \, \text{MPa}$$

Since $400 = \sigma_y = 2k$, **yielding occurs exactly at the Tresca limit.**

> Note: $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 250 - (-150) = 400$ is the maximum.

**Von Mises Check:**

$$(250 - (-150))^2 + ((-150) - 0)^2 + (0 - 250)^2$$

$$= (400)^2 + (-150)^2 + (-250)^2$$

$$= 160000 + 22500 + 62500 = 245000 \, \text{MPa}^2$$

RHS: $2 \sigma_y^{\,2} = 2 \times 400^2 = 2 \times 160000 = 320000 \, \text{MPa}^2$

Since $245000 < 320000$, **yielding does NOT occur according to von Mises.**

> This illustrates that Tresca is more conservative: it predicts yielding here, while von Mises does not.

**[Marks Distribution: Statement 2 + Derivation outline 2 + Numerical 3]**

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Hollomon Power Law:**

$$\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$$

where $K$ = strength coefficient and $n$ = strain hardening exponent. This empirical relation fits true stress vs. plastic strain for many metals in the cold-working regime.

**Numerical Solution:**

Take logarithms of both data points:

$$\ln \sigma = \ln K + n \ln \varepsilon$$

**Point 1:** $\ln(350) = \ln K + n \ln(0.20)$ → $5.858 = \ln K - 1.609 \, n$
**Point 2:** $\ln(500) = \ln K + n \ln(0.50)$ → $6.215 = \ln K - 0.693 \, n$

**Subtract (Point 2 − Point 1):**

$$0.357 = n (1.609 - 0.693) = 0.916 \, n$$

$$\boxed{\;n = 0.390\;}$$

**Solve for $\ln K$:**

$$\ln K = 5.858 + 1.609(0.390) = 5.858 + 0.628 = 6.486$$

$$\boxed{\;K = e^{6.486} \approx 654.7 \, \text{MPa}\;}$$

**(ii) Flow Stress at $\varepsilon = 0.30$:**

$$\sigma_f = 654.7 \times (0.30)^{0.390} = 654.7 \times 0.622 = 407.3 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_f \approx 407 \, \text{MPa}\;}$$

**[Marks Distribution: Hollomon 2 + Setup 2 + Calculation 3]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 
> **1. Mixing true and engineering quantities.** Engineering stress uses **original** area $A_0$; true stress uses **instantaneous** area $A$. Confusing them is the **#1 reason** for numerical errors in forming load problems.
> 
> **2. Forgetting constant volume assumption.** $A_0 L_0 = A L$ is the foundation of nearly every metal forming derivation. Forgetting this yields wrong answers in **Section 3.1** and **3.5** derivations.
> 
> **3. Confusing Tresca and von Mises conversion factors.** $\sigma_y = 2k$ for Tresca; $\sigma_y = \sqrt{3} k$ for von Mises. Using the wrong factor in forging load calculations gives errors of **15–25%**.
> 
> **4. Sign convention for principal stresses.** Tensile stresses are **positive**, compressive are **negative**. Mixing signs in $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ yields wrong Tresca calculations.
> 
> **5. SI Unit Consistency.** Always express load in Newtons, dimensions in meters (or convert to mm with MPa consistently). Mixed units (kgf with MPa) is a **frequent 1-mark penalty** in KTU valuation.
> 
> **6. Skipping units in final answer.** KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 marks if the unit (MPa, N, mm) is missing.
> 
> **7. Incorrect Hollomon law application.** Students sometimes write $\sigma_f = K + n\varepsilon$ (linear, wrong) instead of $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$ (power law, correct).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **True stress** $\sigma = F / A$ uses instantaneous area; **engineering stress** $\sigma_0 = F / A_0$ uses original area. Relationship: $\sigma = \sigma_0(1+e)$.
- **True strain** $\varepsilon = \ln(L/L_0)$ accounts for continuous deformation; **engineering strain** $e = (L-L_0)/L_0$ is for small deformations only.
- **Constant volume assumption** ($A_0 L_0 = A L$) is the bedrock assumption in bulk metal forming.
- **Tresca criterion**: $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2k = \sigma_y$. Mathematically simpler, more conservative.
- **Von Mises criterion**: $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2 \sigma_y^{\,2} = 6k^2$. Physically accurate for ductile metals.
- **Conversion**: $\sigma_y = \sqrt{3} \, k$ for von Mises; $\sigma_y = 2k$ for Tresca.
- **Hollomon law**: $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$, valid for cold working. $K$ in MPa; $n$ dimensionless.
- **Slab analysis** in compression gives $\bar{p} = \sigma_f (1 + \mu d / 3h)$, accounting for friction hill effect.
- **Ideal work of deformation**: $W_i = \sigma_f V \varepsilon$, valid only for homogeneous, frictionless deformation.
- **Temperature regimes**: Cold ($< 0.3 T_m$), Warm ($0.3 - 0.6 T_m$), Hot ($> 0.6 T_m$).
- **Plastic deformation mechanisms** in order of dominance: Slip > Twinning > Grain boundary sliding > Diffusion creep.
- **Forming load** in any process: $F = \bar{p} \cdot A$.
- **Plane strain** (rolling): Von Mises reduces to Tresca form: $\sigma_x - \sigma_y = 2k$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Hardening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strain Hardening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Hardening (Work Hardening / Cold Working):**
> *Strain hardening is the phenomenon in which a ductile metal exhibits a progressive increase in true stress (resistance to plastic deformation) as it is subjected to increasing plastic strain at a temperature below its recrystallization temperature.* This occurs because the microstructural mechanism of plastic flow — primarily the movement and multiplication of dislocations — is progressively impeded as dislocation density rises from ~$10^6$ to ~$10^{12}$ lines/$m^2$.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, strain hardening is treated as the **single most exploited strengthening mechanism in cold forming operations** such as cold rolling, deep drawing, wire drawing, extrusion, and sheet metal forming. The mathematical law that governs it within the plastic regime is the **Hollomon Equation** (1945), the cornerstone of KTU Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Crumpled Paper Analogy (Think Like This!):**
> Take a fresh A4 sheet and try to tear it — easy. Now fold it 10–15 times, crushing the fibers in on themselves. Try to tear it again — it is now extremely difficult, almost cardboard-like. The paper has become *stronger and harder* purely from accumulated mechanical deformation, with **no heat treatment and no change in chemical composition**. This is exactly what happens inside a metal at the microstructural level: dislocations (line defects) multiply, tangle, and lock each other, requiring a progressively higher load to push them past one another.

A second intuitive picture is a **traffic jam on a narrow bridge**: as more cars (dislocations) try to cross a single-lane bottleneck (a grain boundary or a tangle), the flow rate (strain rate) drops and the resistance (stress) climbs.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Symbols

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Frequency Symbols You Must Memorize:**
> - **$n$** — Strain Hardening Exponent (dimensionless, typically **$0.0 \le n \le 0.5$**)
> - **$K$** — Strength Coefficient (units of MPa, represents true stress at $\varepsilon = 1.0$)
> - **$\sigma_0$** — True Stress at the onset of plastic deformation (yield stress)
> - **$\sigma$** — Instantaneous true stress
> - **$\varepsilon$** — True (logarithmic) plastic strain
> - **$m$** — Strain rate sensitivity exponent (NOT to be confused with $n$ — do not interchange in KTU exams)

| Behavior | $n$ Value | Material Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Perfectly Plastic | $0.0$ | Annealed mild steel (idealized) |
| Low Hardening | $0.05 - 0.15$ | Mild steel, brass |
| Moderate Hardening | $0.20 - 0.30$ | Stainless steel, aluminium alloys |
| High Hardening | $0.30 - 0.50$ | Austenitic stainless steel, copper |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Mechanism — The "WHY" Behind Strain Hardening

The plastic deformation in a metal occurs through the glide of **dislocations** along specific slip planes. As deformation proceeds:

1. **Dislocation Multiplication:** Sources such as **Frank–Read sources** generate new dislocations. The total dislocation density $\rho$ increases from $10^6$ to $10^{12}$ lines/m².
2. **Dislocation Interaction:** Dislocations begin to obstruct one another. Two dislocations of opposite sign annihilate, while same-sign dislocations form **dipoles, jogs, and sessile locks (Lomer–Cottrell locks in FCC metals)**.
3. **Forest Dislocation Concept:** Imagine a moving dislocation trying to cut through a "forest" of perpendicular dislocations. Each intersection creates a new jog, demanding extra energy.
4. **Tangling & Cell Formation:** Dislocations arrange into dense **tangled cell walls** with relatively dislocation-free interiors. The mean free path for further slip shortens.
5. **Macroscopic Manifestation:** Because the metal can no longer deform easily, the **true stress required to continue plastic flow rises** — this rise is strain hardening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for a KTU student?**
> The value of $n$ directly controls **formability, uniform elongation, and the limiting drawing ratio (LDR)** in deep drawing. High-$n$ metals resist localized necking, which is why the tensile test, the bulge test, and the Swift cup-drawing test are all used in KTU lab modules to indirectly measure $n$.

## 2.2 Governing Equations — KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every equation you are expected to derive, recall, or apply** for KTU Module 1 questions on strain hardening.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Name / Use | Boundary / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sigma = K \varepsilon^{\,n}$ | **Hollomon Equation** (1945) | $\varepsilon \ge \varepsilon_0$, $0 \le n \le 0.5$ |
| 2 | $\sigma = \sigma_0 + K \varepsilon^{\,n}$ | **Ludwik Equation** | Includes initial yield offset $\sigma_0$ |
| 3 | $\sigma = K(\varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon)^{\,n}$ | **Swift Equation** | Used for total strain (elastic + plastic) |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_i}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{l_i}{l_0}\right)$ | True Strain from area / length | Volume constancy assumed |
| 5 | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{F}{A_i} = \sigma_E (1+\varepsilon_E)$ | True Stress from Engineering data | Valid up to necking |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{d(\ln \sigma)}{d(\ln \varepsilon)}$ | Definition of $n$ | Slope on log-log plot |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_u = n$ | Uniform elongation (true strain) | Considère's criterion |
| 8 | $\sigma_f = K \left(\dfrac{n}{\varepsilon}\right)^{\,n}$ | Flow stress at a given strain | Mid-radius average for forming |
| 9 | $\bar{\sigma} = \dfrac{K \varepsilon^{\,n}}{1+n}$ | Mean flow stress | Used for forging force |
| 10 | $\rho \propto \varepsilon$ | Dislocation density $\propto$ strain | Empirical |
| 11 | $\tau \propto \sqrt{\rho}$ | Taylor relation: shear stress vs. $\rho$ | $G$, $b$ implicit in $K$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **K-T-U Pitfall Trap:** In the formula table, equation (8) gives flow stress at strain $\varepsilon$ — make sure you read the *correct* KTU question. Marks have been lost when students confuse *flow stress at a given strain* (Eq. 8) with *mean flow stress* (Eq. 9).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why It Matters Beyond the Exam Hall

- **Process Design:** A higher $n$ allows **larger uniform deformation** before necking, enabling deeper cups in deep-drawing (limiting drawing ratio LDR $\approx 2.0 - 2.5$ for low-$n$ steels, $2.6 - 2.9$ for high-$n$ austenitic stainless).
- **Material Selection:** Crash-relevant automotive panels are specified as **"EDDQ" (Extra Deep Drawing Quality)** with $n \ge 0.22$ and total elongation $\ge 40\%$.
- **Process Control:** During a multi-pass rolling schedule, the rolls must apply progressively higher torque because of strain hardening — essential for **load prediction and motor sizing** in KTU design problems.
- **Post-Form Heat Treatment:** If a heavily cold-worked part is later annealed, recovery and recrystallization (covered later in Module 3) will *undo* the strain hardening — so the KTU formability calculation must consider whether the part is used as-formed or annealed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hollomon Equation from the Log-Log Slope

**Statement to Prove:**
For a metal obeying power-law hardening, the true stress–true strain relationship in the plastic region is:
$$\sigma = K \varepsilon^{\,n}$$

**Derivation (each line is a logically complete step):**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides of the assumed power law:
$$\ln \sigma = \ln K + n \ln \varepsilon$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $\ln \varepsilon$ (treating $K$ and $n$ as constants):
$$\frac{d(\ln \sigma)}{d(\ln \varepsilon)} = n$$

Therefore, when the experimental true-stress / true-strain data are plotted on a log–log scale, the slope of the straight-line fit gives the strain hardening exponent $n$, and the $y$-intercept at $\ln \varepsilon = 0$ (i.e. $\varepsilon = 1.0$) gives $\ln K$. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Derivation of Mean Flow Stress $\bar{\sigma}$

**Use Case in KTU:** Average pressure in forging, drawing, and rolling.

Start with the definition of mean flow stress as the average of $\sigma$ over a strain interval $[0,\,\varepsilon]$:
$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon - 0}\int_0^{\varepsilon} \sigma \, d\varepsilon^{\prime}$$

Substitute the Hollomon relation:
$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\int_0^{\varepsilon} K (\varepsilon^{\prime})^{\,n}\, d\varepsilon^{\prime}$$

Evaluate the integral:
$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{K}{\varepsilon}\left[\frac{(\varepsilon^{\prime})^{\,n+1}}{n+1}\right]_0^{\varepsilon}$$

$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{K}{\varepsilon}\cdot \frac{\varepsilon^{\,n+1}}{n+1}$$

$$\boxed{\;\bar{\sigma} = \frac{K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}}{1+n}\;}$$

**Engineering Use:** Forging force $F = \bar{\sigma}\cdot A$ when a workpiece is upset from height $h_0$ to $h_f$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Considère's Criterion for Uniform Elongation

**Statement:** Necking begins when $\dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} = \sigma$.

**Derivation:**

Engineering stress in terms of true values (volume constancy):
$$S = \frac{\sigma}{1+\varepsilon}$$

Differentiate with respect to true strain $\varepsilon$ and set $dS/d\varepsilon = 0$ at the onset of necking:
$$\frac{dS}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{(1+\varepsilon)\dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} - \sigma}{(1+\varepsilon)^2} = 0$$

The numerator must vanish:
$$(1+\varepsilon)\frac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} = \sigma$$

For a Hollomon material, $\dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} = K n \varepsilon^{\,n-1} = \dfrac{n\sigma}{\varepsilon}$:

$$(1+\varepsilon)\frac{n\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \sigma \quad\Rightarrow\quad n = \varepsilon_u$$

Therefore, **the strain hardening exponent numerically equals the maximum uniform true strain** the material can sustain. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]:**
> A cylindrical brass specimen of initial diameter $d_0 = 20\,\text{mm}$ and gauge length $l_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$ is loaded in tension. At a particular instant the load is $F = 88\,\text{kN}$ and the diameter has reduced uniformly to $d_i = 19\,\text{mm}$. Given the Hollomon constants $K = 600\,\text{MPa}$ and $n = 0.30$, verify consistency and calculate the mean flow stress up to this instant.

**Solution Step 1 — Compute instantaneous true strain:**

Volume constancy gives:
$$A_0\,l_0 = A_i\,l_i$$

$$\frac{A_0}{A_i} = \frac{d_0^{\,2}}{d_i^{\,2}} = \frac{20^2}{19^2} = \frac{400}{361} = 1.1080$$

$$\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_i}\right) = \ln(1.1080) = 0.1026$$

**Solution Step 2 — Compute instantaneous true stress:**

$$A_i = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_i^{\,2} = \frac{\pi}{4}(19)^2 = 283.53\,\text{mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{88\,000}{283.53} = 310.4\,\text{MPa}$$

**Solution Step 3 — Check consistency with Hollomon's law:**

$$\sigma_{\text{predicted}} = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n} = 600 \times (0.1026)^{0.30}$$

Compute the power term:
$$(0.1026)^{0.30} = e^{0.30\,\ln(0.1026)} = e^{0.30 \times (-2.276)} = e^{-0.6828} = 0.5053$$

$$\sigma_{\text{predicted}} = 600 \times 0.5053 = 303.2\,\text{MPa}$$

The experimental value (310.4 MPa) is within ~2.4 % of the predicted value (303.2 MPa), so the material **conforms to power-law hardening** in this range.

**Solution Step 4 — Compute mean flow stress:**

$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}}{1+n} = \frac{303.2}{1 + 0.30} = \frac{303.2}{1.30} = 233.2\,\text{MPa}$$

**Final Answers:** $\varepsilon = 0.1026$, $\sigma_T = 310.4\,\text{MPa}$, $\bar{\sigma} = 233.2\,\text{MPa}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — From Dislocation to Hardening

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **cause-and-effect chain** that a KTU answer on strain hardening mechanism must reproduce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied External Load F] --> B[Elastic Strain Region]
    B --> C[Yield Point sigma_y Reached]
    C --> D[Dislocation Sources Activate Frank Read Mechanism]
    D --> E[Dislocation Density Rises from 10^6 to 10^12 per m^2]
    E --> F[Dislocations Interact and Form Tangles and Cell Walls]
    F --> G[Mean Free Path for Slip Decreases]
    G --> H[True Stress sigma required for further flow Rises]
    H --> I[Macroscopic Strain Hardening Observed]
    I --> J{Strain Hardening Exponent n}
    J --> K[High n greater than 0.25 - Good Formability]
    J --> L[Low n less than 0.15 - Poor Formability]
    K --> M[Uniform Elongation epsilon_u equals n]
    L --> N[Early Necking and Failure]
```

## 4.2 True Stress – True Strain Schematic (Block Functional Architecture)

Because Mermaid cannot faithfully render a continuous log-log plot, the following block diagram maps the **function-level architecture of the true stress–true strain curve** and the role of $K$ and $n$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin O at zero zero] --> B[Elastic Linear Region slope equals E Young Modulus]
    B --> C[Yield Point sigma_0 epsilon_y]
    C --> D[Plastic Region governed by sigma equals K epsilon to power n]
    D --> E[Uniform Plastic Deformation up to point UTS]
    E --> F[Diffuse Necking Begins at Considere Criterion]
    F --> G[Fracture Point epsilon_f]
    D --> H[Slope of Log Log Plot equals n]
    D --> I[Intercept at epsilon equals 1 gives log K]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Explanation of the Two Key Parameters

- **$n$ (slope on log-log plot)** controls the *shape* — how fast the material hardens. A flat line ($n \to 0$) is perfectly plastic; a steep line ($n \to 0.5$) is highly hardening.
- **$K$ (intercept at $\varepsilon = 1$)** controls the *magnitude* — the absolute level of stress. Two materials with the same $n$ but different $K$ harden in the same proportion but at very different absolute loads.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)**
> *Define strain hardening. Mention its significance in metal forming.*

**Model Answer:**
Strain hardening (or work hardening) is the phenomenon in which a metal's resistance to plastic deformation (true flow stress) increases progressively with increasing plastic strain at temperatures below the recrystallization temperature. It occurs due to multiplication and interaction of dislocations during plastic deformation.
**Significance in metal forming:** (i) It allows uniform distribution of strain and delays localized necking, thereby enhancing formability. (ii) It raises the load requirements in subsequent forming stages, which must be accounted for in press and roll design. (iii) It is the principal strengthening mechanism used in cold-rolled sheets, drawn wires, and deep-drawn cups. (2–3 lines, 3 marks.)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] (3 Marks)**
> *What is the strain hardening exponent $n$? State its typical range.*

**Model Answer:**
The strain hardening exponent $n$ is the slope of the $\log \sigma$ versus $\log \varepsilon$ plot in the plastic region and quantifies the rate at which a metal hardens with plastic strain. For most engineering metals, $n$ lies between **$0.0$ and $0.5$**. A value of $n = 0$ corresponds to a perfectly plastic material, while $n = 1$ corresponds to a perfectly elastic (linearly hardening) solid. Sheet-metal forming grades (e.g. EDDQ steel) have $n \ge 0.22$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Part (a)** Define the Hollomon equation. With a neat log–log sketch, explain how the strain hardening exponent $n$ and strength coefficient $K$ are determined experimentally. Discuss the physical meaning of $n = 0$, $n = 0.5$, and $n = 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Hollomon equation is the empirical constitutive law for strain hardening in the plastic region:
$$\sigma = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}$$

**Experimental Determination (Sketch Description):** Convert load–elongation data to true stress $\sigma$ and true strain $\varepsilon$. Plot $\log \sigma$ on the $y$-axis and $\log \varepsilon$ on the $x$-axis. A straight line of best fit is drawn. The **slope** of the line gives $n$, and the **anti-log of the $y$-intercept** at $\log \varepsilon = 0$ (i.e. $\varepsilon = 1.0$) gives $K$.

**Physical Meaning:**

- **$n = 0$:** $\sigma = K$ (constant). The flow stress does not increase with strain — *perfectly plastic* behaviour (idealized annealed mild steel, or rigid-plastic assumption in simple analysis).
- **$n = 0.5$:** A high hardening case. Most ductile metals never reach this, but it represents an upper limit of useful work hardening (e.g. heavily cold-rolled austenitic stainless steel approaches $n \approx 0.45$).
- **$n = 1$:** $\sigma = K\varepsilon$ (linear hardening with strain). This is the *maximum possible* hardening before elastic and plastic strain rates become equal — physically unrealizable for most metals but a useful mathematical limit.

The uniform true strain at necking is $\varepsilon_u = n$ (Considère's criterion), confirming that higher $n$ means greater formability. (7 marks distributed: 2 for definition, 2 for sketch/experimental, 3 for physical meaning.)

---

**Part (b)** A copper specimen is tested in tension. The Hollomon constants are $K = 530\,\text{MPa}$ and $n = 0.34$. Find: (i) the true stress at a true strain of $0.20$, (ii) the mean flow stress up to that strain, and (iii) the uniform elongation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) True stress at $\varepsilon = 0.20$:**
$$\sigma = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n} = 530 \times (0.20)^{0.34}$$

Compute the power term:
$$(0.20)^{0.34} = e^{0.34\,\ln(0.20)} = e^{0.34 \times (-1.6094)} = e^{-0.5472} = 0.5787$$

$$\sigma = 530 \times 0.5787 = 306.7\,\text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Mean flow stress up to $\varepsilon = 0.20$:**
$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}}{1+n} = \frac{306.7}{1 + 0.34} = \frac{306.7}{1.34} = 228.9\,\text{MPa}$$

**(iii) Uniform elongation (Considère's criterion):**
$$\varepsilon_u = n = 0.34$$

Hence, the maximum true strain before necking is $0.34$ (or $34\,\%$).

**Final Answers:** $\sigma = 306.7\,\text{MPa}$, $\bar{\sigma} = 228.9\,\text{MPa}$, $\varepsilon_u = 0.34$. (7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Part (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the dislocation mechanism of strain hardening in a metal subjected to cold working. Discuss the role of Frank–Read sources, forest dislocations, and Lomer–Cottrell locks. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch (to be drawn):** Initial dislocation line pinned at two nodes. As shear stress increases, the line bows out into a semicircle, wraps around, and emits a closed dislocation loop while a new segment regenerates at the pinning points — this is the **Frank–Read source**.

**Mechanism (4 key steps):**

1. **Frank–Read Multiplication:** A pinned dislocation segment bows under applied shear stress. The critical stress to activate it is $\tau_c \approx Gb/L$, where $L$ is the segment length. Once activated, it spirals outward, producing a continuous stream of new dislocations.
2. **Dislocation Density Build-up:** As deformation continues, the total dislocation density $\rho$ rises from $\sim 10^6$ to $\sim 10^{12}\,\text{lines/m}^2$. This exponential rise is the structural origin of hardening.
3. **Forest Dislocations:** A gliding dislocation intersects a perpendicular "forest" of dislocations on a different slip system. Each intersection creates a **jog** that must drag along, requiring extra energy and raising the flow stress. The Taylor relation captures this: $\tau \propto G b \sqrt{\rho}$.
4. **Lomer–Cottrell Locks:** In FCC metals, two dislocations from different slip systems can react to form a **sessile (immobile) dislocation** at the intersection — a Lomer–Cottrell lock. It blocks further slip on both original systems, forcing the material to activate higher-order slip systems and contributing strongly to hardening.

**Conclusion:** The progressive accumulation and interaction of dislocations — by multiplication, forest intersection, and lock formation — reduce the mean free path for slip and increase the macroscopic flow stress, manifesting as strain hardening. (7 marks: 2 sketch + 5 mechanism.)

---

**Part (b)** A cylindrical aluminium specimen of initial diameter $d_0 = 16\,\text{mm}$ and gauge length $l_0 = 40\,\text{mm}$ is pulled in tension. At a load of $56\,\text{kN}$ the diameter is found to be $15.4\,\text{mm}$. Given $K = 420\,\text{MPa}$, determine the strain hardening exponent $n$ and the mean flow stress at this instant. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial and instantaneous cross-sectional areas:**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(16)^2 = 201.06\,\text{mm}^2$$

$$A_i = \frac{\pi}{4}(15.4)^2 = 186.27\,\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — True strain (using volume constancy):**
$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_i}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{201.06}{186.27}\right) = \ln(1.0793) = 0.0763$$

**Step 3 — True stress:**
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{56\,000}{186.27} = 300.6\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hollomon's law to find $n$:**
$$\sigma = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 300.6 = 420 \times (0.0763)^n$$

$$\frac{300.6}{420} = (0.0763)^n \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.7157 = (0.0763)^n$$

Take natural log of both sides:
$$\ln(0.7157) = n\,\ln(0.0763)$$

$$-0.3345 = n \times (-2.5735)$$

$$n = 0.130$$

**Step 5 — Mean flow stress at this instant:**
$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}}{1+n} = \frac{300.6}{1 + 0.130} = \frac{300.6}{1.130} = 266.0\,\text{MPa}$$

**Final Answers:** $n = 0.130$ and $\bar{\sigma} = 266.0\,\text{MPa}$. (7 marks: 2 area + 1 strain + 1 stress + 2 finding $n$ + 1 mean flow.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not interchange true and engineering stress/strain.** Engineering values underestimate true stress and overestimate true strain after $\sim 1\%$ strain. KTU examiners award zero credit for using $S = F/A_0$ in a question that asks for *true* stress.
> 2. **Do not confuse $n$ (strain hardening) with $m$ (strain-rate sensitivity).** They appear in similar power-law form ($\sigma = K\varepsilon^{\,n}$ vs. $\sigma = C\dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m}$), but $m$ relates to deformation speed and is covered later.
> 3. **Units of $K$ are MPa**, not dimensionless. Always carry units through to the final answer.
