> 4. **Forgetting volume constancy** ($\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_i) = \ln(l_i/l_0)$) in part (b) of numericals is the single biggest cause of partial-mark loss in KTU valuation.
> 5. **In part (a) theory questions, always draw the sketch.** A neat log-log plot of $\sigma$ vs $\varepsilon$ with $K$ and $n$ labelled is worth 1–2 marks by itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Strain Hardening**

- Strain hardening = rise in true flow stress with increasing plastic strain at $T < T_{\text{recrystallization}}$.
- Hollomon Equation: $\sigma = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}$ is the workhorse constitutive law.
- $n$ is the **slope** of the log $\sigma$ – log $\varepsilon$ plot; $K$ is the anti-log of the **intercept at $\varepsilon = 1.0$**.
- Typical range of $n$: **$0$ to $0.5$**; high-$n$ = good formability (deep-drawing grades).
- True stress: $\sigma = F/A_i$ ; True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_i) = \ln(l_i/l_0)$ (volume constancy).
- Engineering-to-true conversion: $\sigma_T = S(1+\varepsilon_E)$ and $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1+\varepsilon_E)$.
- **Considère's criterion:** necking begins at $\varepsilon_u = n$ (uniform elongation = $n$).
- Mean flow stress: $\bar{\sigma} = K\varepsilon^{\,n} / (1 + n)$ — used in forging/extrusion force calculations.
- Microstructural origin: dislocation multiplication (Frank–Read), forest dislocations, jogs, Lomer–Cottrell locks; $\rho$ rises from $10^6$ to $10^{12}\,\text{m}^{-2}$.
- Taylor relation: $\tau \propto G b \sqrt{\rho}$ — links flow stress directly to dislocation density.
- Three alternative forms: Hollomon ($\sigma = K\varepsilon^{\,n}$), Ludwik ($\sigma = \sigma_0 + K\varepsilon^{\,n}$), Swift ($\sigma = K(\varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon)^{\,n}$).
- Applications: cold rolling, deep drawing, wire drawing, sheet metal forming — every KTU Module 2/3 process is built on this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Metal Forming and Tool Design**, **temperature** is a critical process variable that fundamentally governs the deformation behaviour, metallurgical structure, force requirements, and final product quality of a workpiece. According to KTU's 2024 scheme framework, the **Effect of Temperature** in metal forming refers to the influence of the working temperature on:

1. The **flow stress** $\sigma_f$ of the material.
2. The **mechanism of plastic deformation** (slip, twinning, diffusion, grain-boundary sliding).
3. The **restoration processes** (recovery and recrystallization) that compete with strain hardening.
4. The **ductility, formability, and forgeability** indices of the metal.
5. The **die-workpiece interface conditions** (friction, wear, lubrication efficiency).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly classifies metal-forming processes by temperature as **Hot Working**, **Cold Working**, and **Warm Working**. This classification is based on the **Homologous Temperature** ($T_H$), defined as:
>
> $$T_H = \dfrac{T_{process}}{T_{melting}}$$
>
> where both temperatures are expressed in **Kelvin** or both in **°C** (ratio is dimensionless).

The three regimes are:

| Regime | Homologous Temperature $T_H$ | Recrystallization Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold Working** | $T_H < 0.30$ | No recrystallization during deformation |
| **Warm Working** | $0.30 \leq T_H \leq 0.50$ | Partial / incipient recrystallization |
| **Hot Working** | $T_H > 0.50$ | Complete dynamic recrystallization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Temperature:** The empirical recrystallization temperature for a heavily cold-worked metal (≈ 70 % reduction) is approximately:
>
> $$T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \, T_{melting}$$
>
> For pure iron: $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \times (1538 + 273) = 723\ ^\circ C \approx 450\ ^\circ C$ (actual value ≈ 450 °C).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of metal forming like **kneading dough or modelling clay**:

- **Cold dough** (straight from the refrigerator): It is stiff, resists your hand, and if you force it, it cracks. You need high force, and it doesn't change shape easily. → This is exactly what happens in **cold working**: high forces, limited ductility, and the metal gets harder as you deform it.

- **Warm dough** (room temperature or slightly warmed): It is more pliable, you can shape it with moderate force, and it holds its new shape reasonably well. → This is **warm working**, a balance between force requirement and shape retention.

- **Hot dough / melted chocolate** (heated): It flows easily under its own weight, you barely need to push it, and the shape isn't stable until it cools. → This is **hot working**: low forces, high ductility, but you must control cooling to lock in the desired shape.

**The key insight:** Temperature changes the **microstructure** of the metal at the atomic level — atoms vibrate more vigorously, slip planes glide easier, and new grains can nucleate (recrystallize), undoing the hardening that would otherwise occur.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example:** Forging a steel crankshaft at 1200 °C requires only a fraction of the force that would be needed at room temperature, and the steel can be deformed to complex shapes without cracking. However, the dimensional accuracy is poor. Conversely, cold-rolled steel sheets for car bodies are formed at room temperature for excellent surface finish and dimensional precision, but require much larger presses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Temperature Affects Forming

The deformation resistance of a metal is governed by two opposing phenomena:

1. **Strain Hardening (Work Hardening):** Dislocation density increases during deformation, raising the flow stress.
2. **Restoration / Softening:** At elevated temperatures, thermally activated processes (recovery and recrystallization) reduce dislocation density.

The **net flow stress** at any instant is the dynamic balance of these:

$$\sigma_{f,net} = \sigma_{hardening} - \sigma_{softening}$$

### 2.1.1 Cold Working ($T_H < 0.30$)

- **No thermally activated restoration** occurs during deformation.
- Dislocations accumulate continuously → **flow stress rises monotonically** with strain.
- The Hollomon equation governs the true stress–true strain curve:

$$\sigma = K \, \varepsilon^{n}$$

where $K$ is the **strength coefficient** (MPa) and $n$ is the **strain-hardening exponent** (dimensionless).

- **Effects on properties:**
  - ↑ Yield strength and tensile strength.
  - ↓ Ductility (% elongation reduces).
  - ↑ Hardness and residual stresses.
  - Grain elongation in the direction of flow.

- **Typical processes:** Cold rolling, cold drawing, deep drawing, cold extrusion, coining.

### 2.1.2 Warm Working ($0.30 \leq T_H \leq 0.50$)

- **Partial recovery** occurs; some dislocations annihilate.
- Flow stress is lower than cold working but higher than hot working.
- Surface quality is better than hot working; forces are higher than hot working.
- **Applications:** Warm forging of steel, warm extrusion of aluminium alloys.

### 2.1.3 Hot Working ($T_H > 0.50$)

- **Dynamic recovery (DRV)** and **dynamic recrystallization (DRX)** occur simultaneously with deformation.
- Steady-state flow stress is reached (no continuous hardening).
- **Effects on properties:**
  - ↓ Yield strength and flow stress (forces drop dramatically).
  - ↑ Ductility (metal can be deformed to large strains without fracture).
  - Grain refinement possible (improved toughness).
  - Anisotropy reduced.
  - Risk of **hot shortness** (melting of low-melting constituents at grain boundaries) and **oxidation/scaling**.

- **Typical processes:** Hot rolling, hot forging, hot extrusion, hot drawing, hot spinning.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Restoration

### A. Recovery
- Dislocations rearrange into lower-energy configurations (cell walls, sub-grain boundaries).
- Properties (hardness, strength) recover only slightly.
- No change in grain shape or size.

### B. Recrystallization
- New strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate and grow, replacing the deformed microstructure.
- Requires:
  1. A minimum amount of prior cold deformation (typically 30–50 %).
  2. Temperature above $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \, T_{melting}$ (K).
  3. Sufficient time (isothermal holding).

### C. Grain Growth
- Following recrystallization, if the metal remains at high temperature, grains coarsen to minimize grain-boundary energy — generally undesirable.

## 2.3 Hot Shortness & Blue Brittleness (Special Phenomena)

> [!WARNING]
> **Hot Shortness:** At temperatures just below the solidus (typically $0.75 < T_H < 0.95$ for steels with high S content), low-melting phases like FeS melt at grain boundaries, causing intergranular cracking. Hot working must **avoid this temperature window**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Blue Brittleness:** For plain-carbon steels, deformation in the range **200–400 °C** results in a temporary rise in strength and drop in ductility due to strain aging (interaction of dislocations with C/N atoms). The steel appears blue due to oxide-film colour — hence the name. **Avoid this range during warm working**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_H = T_{process} / T_{melting}$ | Homologous temperature | dimensionless | All |
| 2 | $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \, T_{melting}$ | Empirical recrystallization temperature | K | Cold → Hot transition |
| 3 | $\sigma = K \, \varepsilon^{n}$ | Hollomon equation (flow curve) | MPa | Cold working |
| 4 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{R\,T}\right)$ | Arrhenius-type rate equation | s$^{-1}$ | Hot working |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \, \sinh^{-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{Z}{A}\right)^{1/n}\right]$ | Sellars–Tegart (hyperbolic sine) | MPa | Hot working |
| 6 | $Z = \dot{\varepsilon} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{R\,T}\right)$ | Zener–Hollomon parameter | s$^{-1}$ | Hot working |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \dfrac{\eta \, \int \sigma \, d\varepsilon}{\rho \, C_p}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise | °C or K | High-speed forming |
| 8 | $\sigma_{f,\,hot} = \sigma_{f,\,ref} \, \left(\dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_{ref}}\right)^{m} \left(\dfrac{T_{ref}}{T}\right)^{k}$ | Empirical temperature/strain-rate dependence | MPa | Hot working |

Where:
- $A$ = material constant (s$^{-1}$)
- $Q$ = activation energy for deformation (J/mol) — typically $Q \approx 280\ \text{kJ/mol}$ for hot working of austenitic steel
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) — **universal gas constant**
- $Z$ = Zener–Hollomon parameter
- $\eta$ = Taylor-Quinney coefficient (≈ 0.85–0.95 for steels)
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- $m$ = strain-rate sensitivity exponent
- $k$ = temperature sensitivity exponent

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Process | Why Temperature Control Matters |
|---|---|
| **Hot Rolling of Steel Plates** | Achieve large reductions (≥ 90 %) with low mill loads; refine austenite grain size for improved toughness. |
| **Cold Rolling of Sheet Metal (Automotive)** | Achieve tight thickness tolerances, excellent surface finish, and high strength via strain hardening (DP steels, TRIP steels). |
| **Hot Forging of Crankshafts** | Form complex geometries in a single die impression with low forging forces; refined grain improves fatigue life. |
| **Warm Forming of Magnesium Alloys** | Activate additional slip systems in HCP Mg (which has limited room-temperature ductility), enabling sheet-metal stamping. |
| **Superplastic Forming (SPF)** | At $T_H \approx 0.5$–0.7 and very low strain rates ($\dot{\varepsilon} \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$), elongations of 200–2000 % are achievable for complex aerospace panels. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Recrystallization Temperature from Homologous Relation

### Given
For pure iron:
- Melting point in Celsius: $T_m = 1538\ ^\circ C$
- Working strain before annealing: ≥ 70 % cold reduction (sufficient stored energy).

### To Find
The empirical recrystallization temperature $T_{recryst}$ in °C.

### Step-by-Step

Convert melting point to Kelvin:
$$T_m(K) = 1538 + 273 = 1811\ K$$

Apply the empirical relation:
$$T_{recryst}(K) \approx 0.4 \times T_m(K)$$

$$T_{recryst}(K) \approx 0.4 \times 1811\ K$$

$$T_{recryst}(K) \approx 724.4\ K$$

Convert back to Celsius:
$$T_{recryst}(^\circ C) = 724.4 - 273 = 451.4\ ^\circ C$$

### Result
$$\boxed{T_{recryst}(\text{Pure Fe}) \approx 451\ ^\circ C}$$

This matches the experimentally observed value of approximately 450 °C — confirming the 0.4 $T_m$ rule.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Temperature on Flow Stress (Empirical Power Law)

### Statement
For many metals in the hot-working regime, the flow stress $\sigma_f$ decreases with increasing temperature and increases with increasing strain rate.

### Empirical Constitutive Model
$$\sigma_f = C \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{m} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{R\,T}\right)$$

where $C$ is a material constant, $m$ is strain-rate sensitivity, and $Q$ is the activation energy.

### Step-by-Step Logarithmic Linearization

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$\ln \sigma_f = \ln C + m \ln \dot{\varepsilon} - \dfrac{Q}{R\,T}$$

**Case 1: At constant temperature $T$**
Differentiate with respect to $\ln \dot{\varepsilon}$:
$$\dfrac{\partial (\ln \sigma_f)}{\partial (\ln \dot{\varepsilon})} \bigg\vert_{T} = m$$

This gives the **strain-rate sensitivity** $m$, measurable as the slope of a $\ln \sigma_f$ vs. $\ln \dot{\varepsilon}$ plot.

**Case 2: At constant strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$**
Differentiate with respect to $1/T$:
$$\dfrac{\partial (\ln \sigma_f)}{\partial (1/T)} \bigg\vert_{\dot{\varepsilon}} = -\dfrac{Q}{R}$$

A plot of $\ln \sigma_f$ vs. $1/T$ gives a straight line whose slope yields $Q/R$, hence $Q$.

### Numerical Illustration
For an aluminium alloy tested at two strain rates and two temperatures, suppose:

| Test | $T$ (K) | $\dot{\varepsilon}$ (s$^{-1}$) | $\sigma_f$ (MPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 700 | 1 | 60 |
| 2 | 800 | 1 | 35 |
| 3 | 700 | 10 | 90 |
| 4 | 800 | 10 | 52 |

**Find $m$ and $Q$.**

Compute $m$ at $T = 700\ K$:
$$m = \dfrac{\ln(90/60)}{\ln(10/1)} = \dfrac{\ln(1.5)}{\ln(10)} = \dfrac{0.4055}{2.3026} \approx 0.176$$

Compute $Q$ at $\dot{\varepsilon} = 1\ s^{-1}$:
$$-\dfrac{Q}{R} = \dfrac{\ln(35/60)}{(1/800 - 1/700)}$$

$$\dfrac{35}{60} = 0.5833, \quad \ln(0.5833) = -0.5389$$

$$\dfrac{1}{800} - \dfrac{1}{700} = \dfrac{700 - 800}{560000} = \dfrac{-100}{560000} = -1.7857 \times 10^{-4}\ K^{-1}$$

$$-\dfrac{Q}{R} = \dfrac{-0.5389}{-1.7857 \times 10^{-4}} = 3018\ K$$

$$Q = 3018 \times 8.314 = 25{,}090\ J/mol \approx 25.1\ kJ/mol$$

### Result
$$\boxed{m \approx 0.18, \quad Q \approx 25.1\ \text{kJ/mol}}$$

Higher $Q$ means the material's flow stress is **more temperature-sensitive**. Higher $m$ means more **strain-rate-sensitive** (superplastic behaviour requires $m > 0.3$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Adiabatic Temperature Rise During High-Speed Forming

### Physical Basis
At high strain rates (e.g., forging hammers, explosive forming), deformation occurs faster than heat can dissipate → process is essentially **adiabatic**. Almost all plastic work converts to heat.

### Governing Equation
$$\Delta T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot W_p}{\rho \cdot C_p} = \dfrac{\eta \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon}{\rho \cdot C_p}$$

where $\eta$ is the fraction of plastic work converted to heat (Taylor-Quinney coefficient).

### Numerical Example

Given:
- Material: AISI 1045 steel
- Average flow stress $\bar{\sigma} = 500$ MPa
- Strain $\varepsilon_f = 0.6$
- $\eta = 0.90$
- $\rho = 7850\ kg/m^3$
- $C_p = 486\ J/(kg\cdot K)$

### Step-by-Step

Calculate the plastic work per unit volume:
$$W_p = \bar{\sigma} \cdot \varepsilon_f = 500 \times 10^6 \times 0.6 = 3.0 \times 10^8\ J/m^3$$

Calculate the heat absorbed per unit volume to raise temperature:
$$\rho \cdot C_p = 7850 \times 486 = 3.8151 \times 10^6\ J/(m^3 \cdot K)$$

Calculate adiabatic temperature rise:
$$\Delta T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot W_p}{\rho \cdot C_p} = \dfrac{0.90 \times 3.0 \times 10^8}{3.8151 \times 10^6}$$

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^8}{3.8151 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx 70.8\ ^\circ C}$$

### Engineering Significance
A 70 °C rise during one deformation stroke can:
- Shift the workpiece from cold to warm regime unexpectedly.
- Cause local recrystallization at the die interface.
- Alter die-life and surface finish.
- In hot working, partially compensate for radiative/conductive heat loss, helping maintain temperature.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Forming Processes by Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal Forming Process Selection] --> B{Measured Homologous Temperature T_H}
    B -->|T_H less than 0.30| C[Cold Working Regime]
    B -->|T_H between 0.30 and 0.50| D[Warm Working Regime]
    B -->|T_H greater than 0.50| E[Hot Working Regime]

    C --> C1[Restoration: NONE during deformation]
    C1 --> C2[Mechanism: Strain Hardening Only]
    C2 --> C3[Flow Curve: sigma equals K times epsilon power n]
    C3 --> C4[Properties: High Strength, Low Ductility, High Residual Stress]
    C4 --> C5[Typical Processes: Cold Rolling, Deep Drawing, Cold Extrusion]

    D --> D1[Restoration: PARTIAL Recovery]
    D1 --> D2[Mechanism: Strain Hardening minus Recovery]
    D2 --> D3[Properties: Moderate Strength, Moderate Ductility]
    D3 --> D4[Typical Processes: Warm Forging, Warm Extrusion]

    E --> E1[Restoration: DYNAMIC Recovery and Recrystallization]
    E1 --> E2[Mechanism: Steady State Flow, No Continuous Hardening]
    E2 --> E3[Properties: Low Strength, High Ductility, Refined Grains]
    E3 --> E4[Typical Processes: Hot Rolling, Hot Forging, Hot Extrusion]
    E4 --> E5{Critical Hazards Check}
    E5 --> E5a[Hot Shortness: Avoid 0.75 to 0.95 T_H]
    E5 --> E5b[Blue Brittleness: Avoid 200 to 400 C for Steels]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Microstructural Evolution with Temperature and Strain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Initial Equiaxed Grains] --> S1[Apply Cold Strain at T_H less than 0.30]
    S1 --> S2[Elongated Deformed Grains with High Dislocation Density]
    S2 --> S3[Heat to T greater than T_recryst]
    S3 --> S4[Recovery: Dislocation Rearrangement into Cells]
    S4 --> S5[Recrystallization: New Strain Free Equiaxed Grains Nucleate]
    S5 --> S6{Grain Growth: Prolonged Exposure}
    S6 -->|Controlled| S7[Refined Grain Target Structure]
    S6 -->|Over Exposed| S8[Coarse Grains: Reduced Toughness]

    H0[Hot Working at T_H greater than 0.50] --> H1[Concurrent Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization]
    H1 --> H2[Steady State Equiaxed Grains During Deformation]
    H2 --> H3[Post Deformation Cooling: Metadynamic Recrystallization]
    H3 --> H4[Final Refined Austenite Transformation Products]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Temperature Control System in a Hot Rolling Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Slab Entry: T approximately 1250 C] --> F1[Reheating Furnace Zone 1]
    F1 --> F2[Soaking Zone: Temperature Equalization]
    F2 --> F3[Descaling: High Pressure Water Jets]
    F3 --> R1[Roll Stand 1: Roughing Mill]
    R1 --> R2[Roll Stand 2]
    R2 --> R3[Roll Stand 3]
    R3 --> R4[Roll Stand 4]
    R4 --> R5[Roll Stand 5: Finishing Mill]
    R5 --> QC{On Line Pyrometer Measures T and Dimensions}
    QC -->|T below 850 C| ACT[Activate Interstand Cooling or Reduce Speed]
    QC -->|T in Target Window 900 to 1050 C| OK[Continue Rolling]
    ACT --> R1
    OK --> COIL[Run Out Table and Coiling]
    COIL --> OUT[Final Product: Hot Rolled Coil]

    style F1 fill:#ffd6a5
    style F2 fill:#ffd6a5
    style R1 fill:#caffbf
    style R2 fill:#caffbf
    style R3 fill:#caffbf
    style R4 fill:#caffbf
    style R5 fill:#caffbf
    style QC fill:#fdffb6
    style ACT fill:#ffadad
    style OK fill:#bdb2ff
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Force-Diagram Comparing Cold, Warm, Hot Working

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given: Same Material, Same Strain, Same Strain Rate] --> C[COLD: T_H less than 0.30]
    START --> W[WARM: T_H approximately 0.40]
    START --> H[HOT: T_H greater than 0.50]

    C --> CForce[Forming Force: MAXIMUM approximately 100 percent baseline]
    C --> CDuct[Ductility: LOW approximately 10 to 25 percent elongation]
    C --> CSurf[Surface Finish: EXCELLENT]
    C --> CTol[Dimensional Tolerance: TIGHT approximately plus minus 0.05 mm]

    W --> WForce[Forming Force: INTERMEDIATE approximately 50 to 70 percent]
    W --> WDuct[Ductility: MODERATE]
    W --> WSurf[Surface Finish: GOOD]
    W --> WTol[Dimensional Tolerance: MODERATE]

    H --> HForce[Forming Force: MINIMUM approximately 20 to 40 percent]
    H --> HDuct[Ductility: HIGH approximately 60 to 200 percent]
    H --> HSurf[Surface Finish: POOR due to Scale]
    H --> HTol[Dimensional Tolerance: LOOSE approximately plus minus 1 to 2 mm]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define hot working and cold working in metal forming. State the homologous temperature ranges for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Cold working** is plastic deformation performed below the recrystallization temperature, where no appreciable restoration occurs during deformation. Homologous temperature $T_H < 0.30$.
- **Hot working** is plastic deformation performed above the recrystallization temperature, where restoration processes (dynamic recovery and dynamic recrystallization) occur concurrently. Homologous temperature $T_H > 0.50$.
- **Distinguishing feature:** In cold working, strain hardening accumulates; in hot working, hardening is neutralized by dynamic restoration, giving a steady-state flow stress.
- **Homologous temperature:** $T_H = T_{process}/T_{melting}$ (both in K). *Marks: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **What is meant by recrystallization temperature? How is it related to the melting point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Recrystallization temperature** is the minimum temperature at which a heavily cold-worked metal (≈ 70 % reduction) undergoes the formation of new strain-free equiaxed grains within a practically significant time (typically 1 hour).
2. **Empirical relation:** $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \, T_{melting}$ (both in Kelvin).
3. The recrystallization temperature is **not a sharp fixed point** — it depends on prior strain, time of holding, grain size of starting material, and purity. Higher purity and greater prior strain lower the recrystallization temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU Pattern):** Each Part B question has **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question is split into (a) [7 marks] + (b) [7 marks].

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the effect of temperature on the flow stress of metals during plastic deformation. Use suitable constitutive equations.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of Flow Stress**
Flow stress $\sigma_f$ is the instantaneous true stress required to continue plastically deforming the material at a given strain, strain rate, and temperature.

**Step 2: Cold Working Regime ($T_H < 0.30$)**
- Flow stress increases monotonically with strain due to dislocation multiplication and interaction (strain hardening).
- Hollomon equation: $\sigma = K \varepsilon^n$, where $K$ is the strength coefficient and $n$ is the strain-hardening exponent.
- Temperature has minimal effect on $\sigma_f$ in this regime.

**Step 3: Hot Working Regime ($T_H > 0.50$)**
- Two opposing phenomena:
  - Strain hardening (athermal, increases $\sigma_f$).
  - Dynamic recovery and dynamic recrystallization (thermally activated, decrease $\sigma_f$).
- Net effect: flow stress reaches a steady-state value after a peak.
- Arrhenius-type constitutive equation:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = A \, \left[\sinh(\alpha \sigma_f)\right]^{n} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 4: Zener–Hollomon Parameter**
Combining strain rate and temperature into a single parameter:

$$Z = \dot{\varepsilon} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

- High $Z$ (low T, high strain rate) → high flow stress, fine dynamically recrystallized grains.
- Low $Z$ (high T, low strain rate) → low flow stress, coarse grains.

**Step 5: Graphical Sketch (describe in exam)**
- Plot of $\sigma_f$ vs. $T$ at constant $\dot{\varepsilon}$: shows **exponential decay**.
- Plot of $\sigma_f$ vs. $\dot{\varepsilon}$ at constant $T$: shows **power-law increase**.
- Plot of $\sigma_f$ vs. $\varepsilon$: a peak followed by steady state at high T (hot working); monotonic rise at low T (cold working).

**Conclusion:** Temperature is the single most influential process parameter controlling flow stress — raising the temperature by 100 °C can reduce flow stress by 30–50 % for steels in the hot-working range. *(7 marks distributed across steps 1–5)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A plain-carbon steel (melting point 1515 °C) is to be hot forged. Determine: (i) the minimum hot-working temperature, (ii) the recrystallization temperature, and (iii) the maximum safe hot-working temperature if the solidus is 1450 °C. State any assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_m = 1515\ ^\circ C = 1788\ K$
- $T_{solidus} = 1450\ ^\circ C = 1723\ K$

**Assumption:** Hot working begins at $T_H \geq 0.50$ and ends before incipient melting at the solidus (typically $T_H \leq 0.90$ to avoid hot shortness).

**(i) Minimum hot-working temperature:**
$$T_{min} = 0.50 \times T_m = 0.50 \times 1788\ K = 894\ K$$

$$T_{min} = 894 - 273 = 621\ ^\circ C$$

**(ii) Recrystallization temperature:**
$$T_{recryst} = 0.4 \times T_m = 0.4 \times 1788\ K = 715.2\ K$$

$$T_{recryst} = 715.2 - 273 = 442.2\ ^\circ C \approx 442\ ^\circ C$$

**(iii) Maximum safe hot-working temperature:**
$$T_{max} = 0.90 \times T_m = 0.90 \times 1788\ K = 1609.2\ K$$

$$T_{max} = 1609.2 - 273 = 1336.2\ ^\circ C \approx 1336\ ^\circ C$$

However, we must also respect the solidus limit:
$$T_{max,\,safe} = \min(0.90 \, T_m,\, T_{solidus} - 50\ ^\circ C) = \min(1336\ ^\circ C,\, 1400\ ^\circ C) = 1336\ ^\circ C$$

**Final Answer:**
$$T_{min,\,hot} = 621\ ^\circ C, \quad T_{recryst} = 442\ ^\circ C, \quad T_{max,\,safe} = 1336\ ^\circ C$$

**Practical Hot Working Range:** 1200–1250 °C for plain carbon steels (furnace typically set ~1250 °C, considering heat loss in transfer to die). *(7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain with neat sketches the phenomena of recovery, recrystallization, and grain growth. How do these phenomena affect the properties of cold-worked metals during subsequent heating?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Recovery (Low-temperature annealing, typically 0.25–0.35 $T_m$)**
- Dislocations rearrange themselves into lower-energy configurations (polygonization, formation of sub-grain cell walls).
- Stored strain energy decreases; physical properties (electrical resistivity, hardness) recover slightly.
- Microstructure: no visible change under optical microscope (only under TEM).

**2. Recrystallization (Temperature ~ 0.4 $T_m$ for 1 h)**
- New strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate at high-energy sites (grain boundaries, deformation bands, twin boundaries) and grow, consuming the deformed matrix.
- Driving force: stored strain energy from cold work.
- The recrystallized grain size depends on:
  - Prior strain (higher strain → finer grains).
  - Annealing temperature (higher T → coarser grains).
  - Time (longer time → coarser grains).

**3. Grain Growth (Temperatures above recrystallization, prolonged holding)**
- After recrystallization, grain boundaries migrate to reduce total grain-boundary area (and hence total grain-boundary energy).
- Grain size increases with time according to: $D^n - D_0^n = K t$, where $n \approx 2$ for ideal grain growth.
- Resulting in **coarse grains** → reduced strength and toughness.

**Sketch Description (sketch in exam):**
- Original equiaxed grains → elongated cold-worked grains → recovered sub-grain structure → recrystallized fine equiaxed grains → coarsened large grains.

**Effect on properties:**
| Stage | Strength | Ductility | Hardness |
|---|---|---|---|
| As cold-worked | High | Low | High |
| Recovered | Slightly reduced | Slightly improved | Slightly reduced |
| Recrystallized | Restored to pre-cold-work level | Fully restored | Fully restored |
| Grain grown | Reduced (Hall-Petch) | Reduced | Reduced |

*(7 marks: 1.5 each stage + 1.5 property table + 0.5 sketch description)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] AISI 1045 steel is hot rolled from a 50 mm thick slab at 1100 °C with a mean flow stress of 120 MPa. Calculate the adiabatic temperature rise assuming 90 % conversion of plastic work to heat. Use $\rho = 7850\ kg/m^3$ and $C_p = 486\ J/(kg\cdot K)$. The strain is 0.4.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\bar{\sigma} = 120\ MPa = 120 \times 10^6\ Pa$
- $\varepsilon_f = 0.4$
- $\eta = 0.90$ (Taylor–Quinney coefficient)
- $\rho = 7850\ kg/m^3$
- $C_p = 486\ J/(kg\cdot K)$

**Step 1: Plastic work per unit volume**
$$W_p = \bar{\sigma} \cdot \varepsilon_f = 120 \times 10^6 \times 0.4 = 4.8 \times 10^7\ J/m^3$$

**Step 2: Heat generated per unit volume (90 % conversion)**
$$Q_{gen} = \eta \cdot W_p = 0.90 \times 4.8 \times 10^7 = 4.32 \times 10^7\ J/m^3$$

**Step 3: Volumetric heat capacity**
$$\rho \cdot C_p = 7850 \times 486 = 3.8151 \times 10^6\ J/(m^3\cdot K)$$

**Step 4: Adiabatic temperature rise**
$$\Delta T = \dfrac{Q_{gen}}{\rho \, C_p} = \dfrac{4.32 \times 10^7}{3.8151 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx 11.32\ ^\circ C}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**
- The actual rolling temperature rises from 1100 °C to ≈ 1111 °C — a small but non-negligible rise.
- In multi-pass rolling, the cumulative rise across passes can be 50–100 °C, which the operator must account for by adjusting inter-pass times and roll speeds.
- Adiabatic heating is **more critical at high strain rates** (e.g., hammer forging: $\Delta T$ can exceed 100 °C per blow).

*(7 marks: Step 1 → 1.5, Step 2 → 1.5, Step 3 → 1, Step 4 → 2, Step 5 → 1)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to Kelvin** for homologous-temperature calculations. A common error: using 1538 directly in $T_H$ without adding 273. **Examiner deducts 1 full mark.**
> 2. **Confusing recrystallization temperature with hot-working threshold.** Recrystallization is the lower limit of *dynamic restoration* (≈ 0.4 $T_m$), while hot working is conventionally 0.5 $T_m$.
> 3. **Ignoring the effect of strain rate.** Students often write only $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$ for hot working, which is the **cold-working** equation. For hot working, you must include strain rate (Zener–Hollomon).
> 4. **Not stating units.** Every numerical answer must carry units (MPa, °C, K, J/mol). Missing units → 0.5 mark deduction per answer.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in Part B (a) questions.** Even a hand-drawn freehand diagram is worth 1–1.5 marks. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.
> 6. **Blue-brittleness / hot-shortness window forgotten.** Examiners love testing this — losing 2 marks if not mentioned in any hot-working discussion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Homologous temperature** $T_H = T/T_m$ — must use Kelvin for both.
- [ ] **Cold working:** $T_H < 0.30$, governed by $\sigma = K \varepsilon^n$, no restoration, strain hardening.
- [ ] **Warm working:** $0.30 \leq T_H \leq 0.50$, partial recovery, intermediate forces.
- [ ] **Hot working:** $T_H > 0.50$, full dynamic recovery + dynamic recrystallization, steady-state flow stress.
- [ ] **Empirical recrystallization temperature:** $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (K), valid for ≈ 70 % cold reduction, 1 h holding.
- [ ] **Arrhenius-type equation** governs hot working: $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \exp(-Q/RT)$.
- [ ] **Zener–Hollomon parameter** $Z = \dot{\varepsilon} \exp(Q/RT)$ unifies strain-rate and temperature effects.
- [ ] **Adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T = \eta \sigma \varepsilon / (\rho C_p)$ — relevant at high strain rates.
- [ ] **Avoid hot shortness window:** 0.75–0.95 $T_H$ (especially S-containing steels).
- [ ] **Avoid blue-brittleness window:** 200–400 °C for plain-carbon steels.
- [ ] **Taylor–Quinney coefficient** $\eta \approx 0.85$–$0.95$ for steels.
- [ ] **Universal gas constant** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [ ] **Hall-Petch dependence:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — finer grains → higher strength (motivates grain refinement in hot working).
- [ ] **Activation energy for hot deformation of austenitic steel:** $Q \approx 280$ kJ/mol.
- [ ] **Higher purity → lower recrystallization temperature** (solute atoms pin dislocations and grain boundaries).
- [ ] **Higher prior cold strain → lower recrystallization temperature** (more stored energy to drive nucleation).
- [ ] **Strain-rate sensitivity $m$:** $m > 0.3$ signals superplastic forming regime.
- [ ] **Hot working → refined equiaxed grains, low residual stress, poor surface (scale), loose tolerances.**
- [ ] **Cold working → higher strength, less ductility, excellent surface, tight tolerances, residual stresses.**
- [ ] **Warm working** is a compromise — used for Mg alloys and difficult-to-form steels.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition and Strain Rates on Metal Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Composition and Strain Rates on Metal Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Metal Forming** is a manufacturing process in which the desired shape and dimensions are obtained through plastic deformation of the material without any addition or removal of material. The two fundamental parameters that critically govern the formability, final mechanical properties, and the economics of any metal forming operation are:

**(a) Material Composition** — The chemical constitution of the workpiece (e.g., percentage of carbon in steel, presence of alloying elements like Cr, Ni, Mo, V, Mn, etc.) which dictates the lattice structure, bonding characteristics, and inherent flow behavior.

**(b) Strain Rate (ε̇)** — The time rate of change of strain experienced by the workpiece during deformation. Mathematically, it is the instantaneous derivative of true strain with respect to time.

$$\dot{\varepsilon} \;=\; \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain rate** is measured in units of **reciprocal seconds (s⁻¹)**. It is *not* dimensionless despite strain being dimensionless. Typical metal forming operations span a wide range from **0.001 s⁻¹** (creep forging) to **10⁴ s⁻¹** (high-speed wire drawing).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dough and Honey Model

Imagine you are pressing a lump of **kneaded dough** with your palm. If you press slowly, the dough flows smoothly and spreads out evenly. If you strike it quickly, it tears or resists. **This is strain rate sensitivity in action.**

Now consider two doughs:
- **Pure wheat dough (FCC-like)** — soft, flows easily in all directions.
- **Whole-grain dough with bran particles (HCP-like, or high-carbon alloy)** — resists flow, tears at grain boundaries.

**Composition** determines the *type* of dough you start with, and **strain rate** determines *how it responds* to your applied force.

In engineering terms:
- **FCC metals** (Al, Cu, austenitic stainless steel, γ-iron) → many slip systems → **high ductility**.
- **BCC metals** (ferritic steel, α-iron, Cr) → fewer slip systems active at low T → **strain rate sensitive**.
- **HCP metals** (Ti, Mg, Zn) → limited slip systems → **poor room-temperature formability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Under Module 1, students must be able to describe how the **chemical composition** of work material and the **applied strain rate** influence the **flow stress, formability, and selection of forming process parameters** (speed, temperature, lubrication). This forms the basis for Module 2 (forging) and Module 3 (rolling/extrusion).

### 1.3 Categories of Strain Rate in Forming Processes

| Forming Process | Typical Strain Rate (s⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| Superplastic forming | $10^{-5}$ – $10^{-2}$ |
| Creep forging / Hot isostatic pressing | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-2}$ |
| Hydraulic press forging | $10^{-2}$ – $10^{0}$ |
| Mechanical press forging | $10^{0}$ – $10^{2}$ |
| Hammer forging | $10^{2}$ – $10^{3}$ |
| Rolling (conventional) | $1$ – $10^{2}$ |
| Extrusion (cold/hot) | $10^{-1}$ – $10^{2}$ |
| High-speed wire drawing | $10^{3}$ – $10^{4}$ |
| Explosive forming | $10^{3}$ – $10^{5}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Effect of Composition on Metal Forming

The chemical composition of the workpiece fundamentally alters its **flow curve**, **ductility limit**, and **forming limit diagram (FLD)**.

#### 2.1.1 Role of Carbon in Steels
- **Low-carbon steel (C < 0.25%)** — Excellent formability, used in deep drawing of automotive panels.
- **Medium-carbon steel (0.25–0.6% C)** — Higher strength, used in forging crankshafts; requires higher forming loads.
- **High-carbon steel (C > 0.6%)** — Very hard, brittle; only suitable for cold forming in annealed condition.

#### 2.1.2 Role of Alloying Elements
- **Mn, Si** — Solid solution strengtheners → raise flow stress.
- **Cr, Mo, V** — Carbide formers → enhance hot strength but reduce ductility.
- **Ni, Cu** — Stabilize austenite → improve toughness and formability at low temperatures.
- **S, P** — Embrittling elements → form grain-boundary films; **must be minimized** for good formability.
- **Pb, S, MnS inclusions** — Promote **chip breaking** in machining but act as **crack initiators** in forming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inclusion shape control** (e.g., globularizing MnS in free-cutting steels) is a metallurgical design strategy to improve formability while retaining machinability.

#### 2.1.3 Crystal Structure Influence

| Crystal Structure | Examples | Slip Systems | Room-Temp Formability |
|---|---|---|---|
| FCC | Al, Cu, Ni, γ-Fe | 12 | Excellent |
| BCC | α-Fe, Cr, Mo, W | 48 (but high critical resolved shear stress) | Moderate, strain-rate sensitive |
| HCP | Ti, Mg, Zn, Be | 3–5 | Poor |

### 2.2 Effect of Strain Rate on Flow Stress

#### 2.2.1 Phenomenological Behavior
- **At low strain rates (hot working):** More time for recovery and recrystallization → **lower flow stress**, more ductility.
- **At high strain rates (cold working / high-speed):** Insufficient time for dislocation climb → **higher flow stress**, possible adiabatic heating, possible cracking.
- **Dynamic strain aging (Portevin–Le Chatelier effect):** In certain alloys (e.g., low-carbon steel at 200–400 °C) → serrated yielding.

#### 2.2.2 Strain Rate Sensitivity Exponent (m)
A scalar quantity that quantifies how strongly the flow stress responds to changes in strain rate:

$$m \;=\; \left.\frac{\partial \ln \sigma_{f}}{\partial \ln \dot{\varepsilon}}\right)_{\varepsilon,\,T}$$

- **Low m (≈ 0.01–0.05):** Conventional metals at room temperature — flow stress nearly independent of strain rate.
- **High m (≈ 0.3–0.8):** Superplastic materials — flow stress drops sharply as strain rate decreases; allows **extreme elongations (> 2000%)** without necking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why m matters for formability:** A high m-value resists the localization of strain (necking). When a region begins to neck locally, the strain rate there rises, but if the material softens rapidly under higher strain rate, the deformation redistributes to neighboring material. This is the **fundamental physics of superplastic forming**.

#### 2.2.3 Temperature Compensation — The Zener–Hollomon Parameter

At elevated temperatures, both strain rate and temperature jointly control flow stress. The **Zener–Hollomon parameter** (Z) consolidates them:

$$Z \;=\; \dot{\varepsilon} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $Q$ = activation energy for the rate-controlling deformation mechanism (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**

The flow stress is then expressed as:

$$\sigma_{f} \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha}\,\sinh^{-1}\!\left[\left(\frac{Z}{A}\right)^{1/n}\right]$$

where $A$, $\alpha$, and $n$ are material constants.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR TABLES:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used instead of a literal `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax when representing absolute values.

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = d\varepsilon / dt$ | Definition of true strain rate |
| 2 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{eng} = v / L$ | Engineering strain rate (v = ram velocity, L = specimen gauge length) |
| 3 | $\sigma_{f} = K\,\varepsilon^{n}$ | **Hollomon equation** (power-law strain hardening at constant $\dot{\varepsilon}$, T) |
| 4 | $\sigma_{f} = K\,\dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | Strain-rate sensitivity law (at constant $\varepsilon$, T) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{f} = K\,\varepsilon^{n}\,\dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | Combined strain-hardening and strain-rate hardening |
| 6 | $m = \partial(\ln \sigma_{f}) / \partial(\ln \dot{\varepsilon})$ | Definition of strain-rate sensitivity exponent |
| 7 | $Z = \dot{\varepsilon}\,\exp(Q/RT)$ | **Zener–Hollomon parameter** (hot working) |
| 8 | $\sigma_{f} = \sigma_{0}\,\exp(\beta T)\,\dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | Empirical exponential temperature dependence |
| 9 | $t_{f} \propto m / \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Time to fracture scaling with m (formability criterion) |
| 10 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{opt} = (\sigma/E) \cdot \nu_{D}\,\exp(-Q/RT)$ | Theoretical optimum strain rate for superplasticity |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Composition–Strain-Rate Knowledge |
|---|---|
| **Automotive crash panels** | Selection of deep-drawing grade (low-C IF steel) processed at optimal blank-holder speed. |
| **Aerospace Ti-alloy forging** | Use of isothermal forging at controlled low strain rates to avoid strain localization. |
| **Superplastic Al-alloy panels** | Aviation and architectural panels formed at $10^{-3}$ s⁻¹ at ~ 500 °C with m ≈ 0.5. |
| **Hot strip mill design** | Z-parameter used to compute roll force, torque, and recrystallization schedules. |
| **Cold heading of bolts** | Spheroidized medium-C steel deformed at high strain rates (~ 10² s⁻¹) with proper lubrication. |
| **Forging defect diagnosis** | Center bursts, shear cracks, and laps are all linked to incorrect strain-rate or temperature combinations. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engineering Strain Rate for a Compression Test

Consider a cylindrical specimen of initial height $h_{0}$ being compressed by a ram moving at constant velocity $v$.

**Step 1:** Define true strain in compression (using natural log form so it remains valid for large deformations).

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{h_{0}}{h}\right)$$

where $h$ is the instantaneous height at time $t$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate true strain with respect to time $t$ (chain rule).

$$\dot{\varepsilon} \;=\; \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{h_{0}}{h}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Simplify using $d[\ln(u)]/dt = (1/u)\cdot(du/dt)$.

$$\dot{\varepsilon} \;=\; \frac{1}{h_{0}/h}\cdot\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{h_{0}}{h}\right) \;=\; \frac{h}{h_{0}}\cdot\left(-\,\frac{h_{0}}{h^{2}}\,\frac{dh}{dt}\right)$$

**Step 4:** The ram velocity $v = -\,dh/dt$ (negative because height decreases as ram advances).

$$\dot{\varepsilon} \;=\; \frac{h}{h_{0}}\cdot\left(-\,\frac{h_{0}}{h^{2}}\right)(-v) \;=\; \frac{v}{h}$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form (true strain rate in compression).

$$\boxed{\;\dot{\varepsilon} \;=\; \frac{v}{h}\;}$$

> **Conversion logic:** As the height $h$ shrinks during compression, the true strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ *increases* even if the ram velocity $v$ is constant. This is why forging loads tend to *spike* near the end of a stroke in compression tests.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strain-Rate Sensitivity Exponent from a Power Law

Assume the flow stress follows a power law in strain rate (at constant strain and temperature):

$$\sigma_{f} \;=\; K\,\dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m}$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$\ln \sigma_{f} \;=\; \ln K \;+\; m\,\ln \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that this is a straight line in $(\ln \dot{\varepsilon},\, \ln \sigma_{f})$ space, with slope $m$ and intercept $\ln K$.

$$\boxed{\;m \;=\; \text{slope of the } (\ln \dot{\varepsilon} \text{ vs. } \ln \sigma_{f}) \text{ line}\;}$$

**Step 3:** To find $m$ experimentally, perform tests at multiple strain rates (e.g., $\dot{\varepsilon}_{1}$, $\dot{\varepsilon}_{2}$) at the *same* plastic strain $\varepsilon_{p}$ and temperature $T$.

$$m \;=\; \frac{\ln(\sigma_{f,2}/\sigma_{f,1})}{\ln(\dot{\varepsilon}_{2}/\dot{\varepsilon}_{1})}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Derivation of the Hollomon Equation (Log-Log Linearization)

Empirically, for many metals in the plastic regime:

$$\sigma_{f} \;=\; K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$\ln \sigma_{f} \;=\; \ln K \;+\; n\,\ln \varepsilon$$

**Step 2:** This linear form implies that a plot of $\ln \sigma_{f}$ vs. $\ln \varepsilon$ yields a straight line of slope $n$ and intercept $\ln K$. The slope $n$ is the **strain-hardening exponent**.

> **Conversion logic:** Both $n$ and $m$ are extracted via log-log linearization. The KTU examiner often asks the student to identify the slope from a graph — remember that $n$ comes from $\ln \varepsilon$ axis, $m$ comes from $\ln \dot{\varepsilon}$ axis.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Derivation of the Zener–Hollomon Relation

Hot working involves thermally activated dislocation motion. The strain rate can be modeled by an Arrhenius-type rate equation:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} \;=\; A\,\bigl[\sinh(\alpha\,\sigma_{f})\bigr]^{n}\,\exp\!\left(-\,\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange to isolate the bracket term.

$$\bigl[\sinh(\alpha\,\sigma_{f})\bigr]^{n} \;=\; \frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{A}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Define the **Zener–Hollomon parameter** $Z$ to absorb the right-hand side.

$$Z \;\equiv\; \dot{\varepsilon}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Then the left-hand side equals $Z/A$, giving the equivalent flow stress expression.

$$\sinh(\alpha\,\sigma_{f}) \;=\; \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 4:** Invert the hyperbolic sine to get $\sigma_{f}$ explicitly.

$$\sigma_{f} \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha}\,\sinh^{-1}\!\left[\left(\frac{Z}{A}\right)^{1/n}\right]$$

> **Physical meaning:** $Z$ represents a **temperature-compensated strain rate**. Two operations with different $\dot{\varepsilon}$ and $T$ are "equivalent" if their $Z$ values are equal — the resulting microstructure and flow stress are the same. This is the cornerstone of **hot-working process mapping**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Composition × Strain Rate × Effect

| Composition Class | Strain Rate Regime | Resulting Forming Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Pure FCC (Cu, Al) | Low (cold) | Easy forming, low flow stress |
| Pure FCC (Cu, Al) | High (cold) | Mild rate sensitivity, possible shear banding |
| Low-C steel | Low | Excellent deep drawing, large m |
| Low-C steel | High | Increased load, dynamic strain aging possible |
| High-C steel | Low | Limited ductility, cracks early |
| High-C steel | High | Brittle fracture, tool damage |
| Austenitic SS (304) | Low | High ductility, austenite → martensite possible |
| Ti alloy (Ti-6Al-4V) | Low + Hot | Required for formability |
| Mg alloy (HCP) | High | Prone to twinning, cracking |
| Superplastic alloy (e.g., Al 7475) | Very low + T ≈ 0.5 T_m | Exceptional ductility, m > 0.4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Style Note:** Part A questions demand precise 3–4 sentence answers. Define → State principle → Give example. Avoid lengthy derivations.

---

**Q1.** Define **strain rate sensitivity exponent (m)** and explain its significance in sheet-metal forming. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 model]**

**Model Answer:**
The strain rate sensitivity exponent $m$ is defined as the slope of the $\ln \sigma_{f}$ vs. $\ln \dot{\varepsilon}$ curve at a constant strain and temperature:

$$m \;=\; \left.\frac{\partial \ln \sigma_{f}}{\partial \ln \dot{\varepsilon}}\right)_{\varepsilon,\,T}$$

Its significance: a higher $m$-value imparts greater resistance to localized necking. When a region begins to thin, its local strain rate rises; if the material softens rapidly under higher $\dot{\varepsilon}$ (high $m$), deformation redistributes to adjacent regions, postponing fracture. For conventional metals at room temperature $m \approx 0.02$, whereas for superplastic alloys $m$ can reach $0.3$–$0.8$, enabling elongations exceeding $2000\%$.

---

**Q2.** How does **carbon content** influence the formability of plain-carbon steels? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model]**

**Model Answer:**
Increasing carbon content raises the pearlite fraction in the microstructure, increasing hardness and tensile strength but decreasing ductility. Low-carbon steels ($< 0.25\%\,C$) possess high ferrite content and excellent formability, making them suitable for deep drawing and cold rolling. Medium-carbon steels ($0.25$–$0.6\%\,C$) require higher forming forces and are typically hot-worked. High-carbon steels ($> 0.6\%\,C$) are brittle at room temperature and prone to cracking; they are usually formed in the spheroidized condition or hot.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 1 carries the first long-answer slot. Examiner expects sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** from the same module.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **Hollomon power-law equation** $\sigma_{f} = K\,\varepsilon^{n}$ and explain how the strain-hardening exponent $n$ is determined experimentally. State the typical range of $n$ for annealed steels, stainless steels, and superplastic alloys. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the empirical power-law relation $\sigma_{f} = K\,\varepsilon^{n}$:

**Step 1.** Taking natural logarithms:

$$\ln \sigma_{f} \;=\; \ln K \;+\; n\,\ln \varepsilon$$

**Step 2.** This is linear in $(\ln \varepsilon,\, \ln \sigma_{f})$ with slope $n$. Experimentally, the metal is tensile-tested at a constant crosshead velocity and at room temperature, the load–elongation data is converted to true stress–true strain, and a log-log plot is constructed.

**Step 3.** A straight-line fit in the **plastic region** (excluding the elastic portion and the onset of necking) yields the slope $n$ and the intercept $\ln K$.

**Typical ranges:**

| Material | $n$ value |
|---|---|
| Annealed low-carbon steel | $0.20$ – $0.25$ |
| Austenitic stainless steel (304) | $0.30$ – $0.55$ |
| Superplastic Al-alloy (e.g., 7475) | $> 0.4$ (with high $m$) |

A higher $n$ indicates stronger resistance to localized thinning and better uniform elongation — desirable in deep drawing.

---

**(b)** With the aid of a **log-log plot**, explain how the **strain-rate sensitivity exponent $m$** is experimentally determined from a series of compression or tensile tests performed at different strain rates. Why is $m$ important in superplastic forming? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** Conduct a series of tests (e.g., uniaxial tension or compression) at fixed temperature $T$ and at a target strain $\varepsilon^{*}$, varying the imposed strain rate (e.g., $\dot{\varepsilon} = 10^{-4},\, 10^{-3},\, 10^{-2},\, 10^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-1}$).

**Step 2.** At each $\dot{\varepsilon}$, record the corresponding flow stress $\sigma_{f}$ at strain $\varepsilon^{*}$.

**Step 3.** Construct a plot of $\ln \sigma_{f}$ (vertical axis) vs. $\ln \dot{\varepsilon}$ (horizontal axis). The slope of the best-fit line gives $m$.

$$\ln \sigma_{f} \;=\; \ln K \;+\; m\,\ln \dot{\varepsilon} \quad\text{(at constant }\varepsilon,\,T\text{)}$$

**Step 4.** For superplastic forming, $m \geq 0.3$ is generally required. A high $m$ means that a localized increase in strain rate (such as the onset of necking) causes significant local softening, which redistributes strain to neighboring material and **delays fracture**. This is why superplastic Al-alloys can be blow-formed into complex aerospace panels with elongations of $1000$–$2000\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students frequently confuse $n$ (strain-hardening exponent, slope of $\ln \sigma_{f}$ vs. $\ln \varepsilon$) with $m$ (strain-rate sensitivity exponent, slope of $\ln \sigma_{f}$ vs. $\ln \dot{\varepsilon}$). Drawing the wrong axes on the log-log plot will cost **3 marks immediately**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the influence of **crystal structure (FCC, BCC, HCP)** on the formability of metals. Why are most cold-working processes designed around FCC metals? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The crystal structure dictates the number of *active slip systems* available for plastic deformation:

| Structure | Slip Systems | Cold Formability |
|---|---|---|
| **FCC** | 12 (e.g., $\{111\}\langle 110\rangle$ in Al) | Excellent — dislocations move easily |
| **BCC** | Up to 48, but high critical resolved shear stress at low T | Moderate — needs elevated T |
| **HCP** | 3 (basal) or 5 (with twinning) | Poor — limited slip, requires hot working |

**Reason cold working favors FCC:**
1. The 12 slip systems are active even at room temperature, supplying multiple independent paths for dislocation motion → large ductility before fracture.
2. The strain-hardening exponent $n$ and strain-rate sensitivity $m$ are both adequate at room temperature.
3. Lower flow stress → smaller press tonnage → cheaper tooling.
4. Examples: aluminum beverage cans (deep drawn), copper tubes, austenitic stainless steel sinks.

**Contrast with HCP:** Mg alloys have only basal slip active at room temperature; deformation requires either elevated temperatures (activating pyramidal slip) or the addition of Li (which transforms to BCC-like behavior) — both being expensive engineering workarounds.

---

**(b)** Define the **Zener–Hollomon parameter** $Z$. Show how it combines strain rate and temperature into a single descriptor of hot-working severity, and explain its role in **hot-working process design**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** Definition:

$$Z \;=\; \dot{\varepsilon}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Physical origin. Hot deformation is a **thermally activated** process. Dislocation climb, cross-slip, and grain-boundary sliding all obey Arrhenius kinetics, hence the exponential term.

**Step 3.** Hot-working flow stress is governed by $Z$ through:

$$\sigma_{f} \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha}\,\sinh^{-1}\!\left[\left(\frac{Z}{A}\right)^{1/n}\right]$$

**Step 4.** Two operations with the same $Z$ yield the same flow stress and similar microstructural evolution — even if they have different combinations of $\dot{\varepsilon}$ and $T$.

**Step 5.** Application to hot-rolling design. To set up a hot strip mill pass schedule, the metallurgist computes $Z$ at each pass. Higher $Z$ → finer dynamically recrystallized grain → higher final strength. Lower $Z$ → grain coarsening → softer product.

**Step 6.** Typical $Q$ values for steels: $Q \approx 280$–$320\,\text{kJ/mol}$ in the austenitic range.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** A common mistake is writing $Z = \dot{\varepsilon}\,\exp(-Q/RT)$ with a **negative** sign in the exponent. The correct sign is **positive** because we want $Z$ to *increase* with increasing strain rate *and* with decreasing temperature (more severe deformation). Forgetting the sign convention will lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$** has units of **s⁻¹**; for compression $\dot{\varepsilon} = v/h$, for tension $\dot{\varepsilon} = v/L$.
- **Hollomon equation**: $\sigma_{f} = K\,\varepsilon^{n}$ — used in **cold working** at constant $\dot{\varepsilon}$ and $T$.
- **Strain rate sensitivity** $m = \partial(\ln \sigma_{f})/\partial(\ln \dot{\varepsilon})$. High $m$ → high resistance to necking.
- **Combined law**: $\sigma_{f} = K\,\varepsilon^{n}\,\dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ — used in **warm working** regimes.
- **Zener–Hollomon parameter** $Z = \dot{\varepsilon}\,\exp(Q/RT)$ — single hot-working severity descriptor.
- **Composition effects**: FCC metals form best cold; BCC needs elevated T; HCP generally requires hot working.
- **Carbon in steel**: ↑ C → ↑ strength, ↓ ductility. Low-C is the deep-drawing grade.
- **Sulfur and phosphorus** are embrittling; inclusions must be controlled.
- **Superplasticity** requires $m \geq 0.3$, $T \approx 0.5\,T_{m}$, and very fine equiaxed grains ($\leq 10\,\mu\text{m}$).
- **Adiabatic heating** at high $\dot{\varepsilon}$ can locally raise temperature by hundreds of °C, softening the material and possibly triggering shear bands.
- **Dynamic strain aging** causes serrated stress–strain curves in low-carbon steel at 200–400 °C.
- **Hall–Petch strengthening** ($\sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + k_{y}\,d^{-1/2}$) means **finer grains → higher flow stress** but also more grain-boundary sliding pathways, contributing to superplasticity.
- **Always distinguish**: $n$ (strain-hardening, $\ln\varepsilon$ axis) **vs.** $m$ (rate sensitivity, $\ln\dot{\varepsilon}$ axis). This is a recurring KTU trap.
- **Sign of exponent in Z-parameter**: **positive**, i.e., $\exp(+Q/RT)$, not negative.
- **Forging vs. rolling vs. extrusion** each span a different strain-rate window; always cite the regime before choosing a constitutive equation.
- **Rule of thumb**: hot work above $0.5\,T_{m}$, warm work $0.3$–$0.5\,T_{m}$, cold work below $0.3\,T_{m}$ (in Kelvin).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Recovery ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recovery** is the **first stage of annealing** in a cold-worked metal, occurring at *relatively low homologous temperatures* (typically $T < 0.4\,T_m$), in which the **stored strain energy** of plastic deformation is partially relieved through **annihilation and rearrangement of point defects and dislocations**, *without* the formation of new strain-free grains and *without* any observable change in optical microstructure. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET602 – Metal Forming and Tool Design), recovery is studied as a critical sub-process that determines the **residual stress state**, **dimensional stability**, and **initial softening** of a plastically deformed workpiece before recrystallization sets in. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Crumpled Paper Stack":** > Imagine you crumple a stack of paper balls into a tight, messy heap (this represents a *cold-worked* metal full of tangled dislocations). Recovery is like *gently shaking the heap on a table* — the loose creases smooth out, the pile settles, and the *overall shape* of the crumpled heap does **not** change, but the internal "stress" or "tangled messiness" noticeably reduces. > Recrystallization, by contrast, would be like *tearing up the crumpled heap into fresh blank sheets* and restacking them — that comes *later*, at higher temperatures. ### 1.3 Position of Recovery in the Annealing Cycle $$\underbrace{\text{Cold-Worked Metal}}_{\text{High dislocation density, hard}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Heat to } T < 0.4\,T_m}\; \underbrace{\text{Recovered State}}_{\text{Lower dislocation energy, softer}} \;\xrightarrow{T \approx 0.4\text{–}0.6\,T_m}\; \underbrace{\text{Recrystallized}}_{\text{New strain-free grains}}$$ ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used - **Homologous Temperature:** $T/T_m$, dimensionless, where $T_m$ is in **Kelvin (K)**. - **Activation Energy for Recovery:** typically **$Q \approx 0.5\,Q_{\text{self-diffusion}}$** ≈ 80–120 kJ/mol for most metals. - **Burgers Vector ($b$):** ≈ **0.25–0.3 nm** for FCC metals like Cu, Al, Ni. - **Stored Energy of Cold Work:** typically **0.5–5 MJ/m³** (very small compared to grain-boundary energy). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU syllabus highlight:** Recovery produces *no* new grain boundaries and *no* change in grain size — only a rearrangement of the dislocation substructure (cell formation, polygonization) and a measurable drop in **residual stresses**, **hardness**, and **electrical resistivity**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Driving Force for Recovery The driving force for recovery is the **stored strain energy** ($E_s$) introduced by plastic deformation, which resides primarily in the form of: 1. **Point defects** — vacancies and interstitials in excess of the equilibrium concentration. 2. **Dislocations** — tangled, jogged, and piled-up line defects. 3. **Elastic strain fields** around dislocation networks. Mathematically, this stored energy can be approximated by the dislocation density $\rho$: $$E_s \;=\; \rho \,\cdot\,\frac{G\,b^{2}}{2}\,\cdot\,(\text{geometric factor})$$ where: - $\rho$ = dislocation density (m/m³), typically rising from $10^{10}$ (annealed) to $10^{16}$ (severely cold-worked). - $G$ = shear modulus (Pa). - $b$ = magnitude of Burgers vector (m). ### 2.2 Three Microscopic Mechanisms Operating During Recovery | # | Mechanism | Temperature Window | What it relieves | Property Affected | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Point-defect annihilation** (vacancy + vacancy, interstitial recombination) | Very low ($< 0.2\,T_m$) | Excess vacancies, interstitials | Electrical resistivity drops sharply | | 2 | **Dislocation rearrangement** (cell formation, polygonization) | Low–moderate ($0.2$–$0.4\,T_m$) | Dislocation strain energy | Hardness decreases moderately | | 3 | **Subgrain growth / coalescence** | Moderate ($0.3$–$0.5\,T_m$) | Sub-boundary area | Residual stresses fully relieved | > [!NOTE] > **Polygonization** is the textbook example of recovery — bent dislocation arrays (from cold work) climb and re-arrange into **low-angle tilt sub-boundaries**, forming a "subgrain" structure that is *not* visible under ordinary optical microscopy. ### 2.3 Effect of Recovery on Engineering Properties | Property | Trend during recovery | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Hardness / Yield Strength** | Slight decrease (5–15%) | Reduction in dislocation density and rearrangement into lower-energy networks | | **Residual Stress** | Substantial decrease (up to 80–100%) | Localized creep / micro-plastic flow at elevated $T$ | | **Electrical Resistivity** | Sharp decrease (recovery of lattice perfection) | Annihilation of point-defect scattering centers | | **Density** | Slight increase | Vacancies eliminated | | **Optical Microstructure** | **No change** | Grain boundaries, grain size unaffected | | **Toughness / Ductility** | Modest increase | Internal stresses relieved | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every quantitative expression the KTU examiner may test on **Recovery**. Master the relationships — units and boundary conditions. | # | Formula | Meaning / Symbol Key | Boundary / Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_s = \rho \,G b^{2}/2$ | Stored strain energy per unit volume ($\text{J/m}^3$) | Valid for dislocation-dominated storage | | 2 | $C_v = \exp(-Q_v / RT)$ | Equilibrium vacancy concentration | At annealing temperature $T$ | | 3 | $\rho_t = \rho_0 \, \exp(-t/\tau)$ | Dislocation density decay during recovery | Approximate, isothermal | | 4 | $\tau = \tau_0 \, \exp(Q/RT)$ | Recovery time constant (Arrhenius form) | $Q \approx 80$–$120$ kJ/mol | | 5 | $X = 1 - \exp(-t/\tau)^{n}$ | Fraction recovered ($X$) | $n$ is empirical order | | 6 | $T_{\text{recov}} \approx 0.3\text{–}0.4\,T_m$ | Approximate recovery temperature window | $T_m$ in Kelvin | | 7 | $\Delta\sigma_i = \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ | Taylor relation — flow stress from dislocations | Baseline for showing $\rho \downarrow \Rightarrow \sigma_i \downarrow$ | | 8 | $t_{0.5} \propto \exp(Q/RT)$ | Time to half-recovery (isothermal analog) | Used for CCT/TTT analysis | | 9 | $E_{\text{residual}} \propto \rho \,G b^{2}$ | Residual elastic strain energy | Falls monotonically with recovery | | 10 | $d\sigma / dT \;\text{stage III recovery}$ | Strain-aging interaction (Cottrell atmosphere re-formation) | Carbon steels especially | > **Symbol Glossary:** > - $\rho$ = dislocation density (m$^{-2}$) > - $G$ = shear modulus (Pa) > - $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude (m) > - $Q$ = activation energy (J/mol) > - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) > - $\alpha$ ≈ **0.3–0.5** (Taylor constant) ### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Recovery > [!NOTE] > **Why does a metal-forming engineer care about recovery?** > 1. **Stress Relief Annealing** — sheet-metal stampings, welded structures, and cold-drawn wires are *intentionally* heat-treated in the recovery regime to **eliminate residual stresses** that would otherwise cause *springback*, *distortion*, or *stress-corrosion cracking*. > 2. **Dimensional Stability** — precision components (instrument shafts, gauges) rely on recovery annealing to lock in geometry. > 3. **Process Window Design** — recovery defines the *upper temperature bound* below which a metal can be used in service without losing strength but gaining toughness. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Recovery Kinetics (Avrami-type Expression) Recovery is a **thermally activated** process. The fraction of stored energy released, $X$, at time $t$ and temperature $T$ follows a kinetics law analogous to recrystallization. **Step 1 — Rate assumption.** The instantaneous rate of energy release is proportional to the *remaining* stored energy: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau}\,(1-X)^{m}$$ where $m$ is a kinetic exponent (often $m \approx 1$ for recovery) and $\tau$ is a temperature-dependent time constant. **Step 2 — Substitute the Arrhenius form for the time constant.** Higher temperature means faster recovery: $$\tau(T) = \tau_0 \,\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate** (assuming $m = 1$): $$\int_0^X \frac{dX'}{1-X'} = \frac{1}{\tau}\int_0^t dt'$$ $$-\ln(1-X) = \frac{t}{\tau}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the fraction recovered $X$:** $$X(t,T) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_0}\,e^{-Q/RT}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** This is the **Avrami / Johnson–Mehl form** applied to recovery. Notice: - If $t \to \infty$, then $X \to 1$ → **complete recovery**. - Doubling $t$ does *not* double $X$ — recovery is **logarithmic in time**. - A plot of $\ln\!\big[-\ln(1-X)\big]$ vs $\ln t$ is linear (slope = 1 for $m=1$). ### 3.2 Derivation: Dislocation Density Decay (Exponential Form) **Step 1 — Start from the Orowan / EsHelby principle**: the rate of dislocation annihilation is proportional to the square of dislocation density (because two dislocations of opposite sign must meet): $$\frac{d\rho}{dt} = -K \,\rho^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables:** $$\frac{d\rho}{\rho^{2}} = -K\,dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate from $\rho_0$ (initial cold-worked density) to $\rho(t)$:** $$\left[-\frac{1}{\rho}\right]_{\rho_0}^{\rho(t)} = -K t$$ $$\frac{1}{\rho(t)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} = K t$$ **Step 4 — Solve explicitly:** $$\rho(t) = \frac{\rho_0}{1 + K \rho_0\, t}$$ **Step 5 — Long-time asymptotic limit.** As $t \to \infty$, $\rho(t) \to 0$ — physically true if recovery is allowed to continue long enough, but in practice recrystallization intervenes first. ### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Numerical Style) > **Problem (model Q):** A cold-worked copper sheet ($\rho_0 = 5 \times 10^{15}$ m$^{-2}$) is stress-relief annealed at $T = 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 1 hour. The annihilation constant at this temperature is $K = 10^{-16}$ m$^{2}$/s. Estimate the dislocation density after the anneal. **Solution — step by step:** **Step 1 — Convert time to seconds:** $$t = 1\;\text{hour} = 3600\;\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the product $K \rho_0 t$:** $$K\,\rho_0\,t = (10^{-16})(5\times10^{15})(3600)$$ $$= 5 \times 3600 = 18000$$ **Step 3 — Plug into the analytical expression:** $$\rho(t) = \frac{5\times10^{15}}{1 + 18000}$$ $$\rho(t) = \frac{5\times10^{15}}{18001} \approx 2.78\times10^{11}\;\text{m}^{-2}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret the result.** > The dislocation density has dropped by *more than four orders of magnitude* — but optical microstructure is **unchanged**, confirming this is a textbook **recovery** outcome (no new grains). Residual stresses have largely been relieved. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Recovery vs. Recrystallization vs. Grain Growth | Parameter | **Recovery** | **Recrystallization** | **Grain Growth** | |---|---|---|---| | Temperature range | $0.2$–$0.4\,T_m$ | $0.4$–$0.6\,T_m$ | $> 0.6\,T_m$ | | Driving force | Stored strain energy in dislocations | Same, plus nucleation barrier | Grain-boundary area reduction | | New grains formed? | **No** | **Yes** (strain-free nuclei) | No (existing grains coarsen) | | Optical microstructure | Unchanged | Completely new grains | Larger grains | | Hardness change | Slight drop (≤ 15%) | Major drop (up to 80%) | Further slight drop | | Residual stresses | Largely relieved | Fully relieved | Relieved | | Activation energy $Q$ | $\approx 0.5\,Q_{\text{sd}}$ (sd = self-diffusion) | $\approx Q_{\text{sd}}$ | $\approx Q_{\text{sd}}$ (boundary diffusion) | | Visible under microscope? | Mostly **no** | Yes | Yes | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Flow — Where Recovery Sits in the Annealing Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Cold Worked Metal
rho HIGH, Hardness HIGH
Residual Stress HIGH"] --> B["Apply Heat T low"] B --> C["Stage 1 Recovery
Point defects annihilate
Dislocations rearrange
Polygonization occurs"] C --> D["Stage 2 Recrystallization
New strain free grains nucleate
Hardness drops sharply"] D --> E["Stage 3 Grain Growth
Grain boundaries migrate
Average grain size increases"] E --> F["Fully Annealed Soft State
rho LOW, Soft, Ductile"] C -.->|"Residual stress relieved"| G["Stress Free Part
Dimensional Stability"] C -.->|"Microstructure UNCHANGED"| H["Same Grains
No new boundaries"] ``` ### 4.2 Dislocation-Level Schematic of Recovery (Polygonization) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Cold Worked State
Bent tangled dislocations
Same sign piled up"] --> P2["Climb and glide
Heat enables vacancy flow"] P2 --> P3["Polygonized Subgrain
Dislocations form low angle tilt wall
Sub boundary created"] P3 --> P4["Recovery Complete
No new grains
Substructure stabilized"] ``` ### 4.3 Property-Evolution Curves During Recovery ```mermaid flowchart LR X["Annealing Time t"] --> Y["Hardness H"] X --> Z["Residual Stress sigma_r"] X --> W["Electrical Resistivity rho_e"] X --> V["Dislocation Density rho"] X --> U["Optical Grain Size d"] Y --- Y1["Slight decrease 5 to 15 percent"] Z --- Z1["Major decrease 80 to 100 percent"] W --- W1["Sharp initial drop then plateau"] V --- V1["Steady exponential decay"] U --- U1["NO change during recovery only"] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Engineering Use of Recovery ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Input["Input Cold Worked Component"] I1["Sheet Metal Stamping"] I2["Cold Drawn Wire"] I3["Welded Plate"] I4["Machined Forging"] end Input --> R["Recovery Annealing Furnace
T between 0.3 Tm and 0.4 Tm
Controlled soak 1 to 4 hours"] R --> O1["Stress Relieved Part"] R --> O2["Stable Dimensions"] R --> O3["Improved Ductility"] R --> O4["Reduced Machining Distortion"] O1 --> App["Service Ready Component"] O2 --> App O3 --> App O4 --> App ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams above deliberately use **plain uppercase text** inside node labels (no bold, no italics, no HTML) to remain fully compatible with the Mermaid rendering engine and the KTU board's PDF export pipeline. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]** **Q1. Define recovery in a cold-worked metal. State two properties that are significantly affected during recovery.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - *Definition (2 marks):* Recovery is the first stage of annealing in which a cold-worked metal is heated to a relatively low temperature (typically $T < 0.4\,T_m$), causing annihilation of point defects and rearrangement of dislocations into lower-energy configurations, *without* any change in the optical microstructure or formation of new grains. - *Two affected properties (1 mark):* (i) Residual stresses are substantially relieved; (ii) Electrical resistivity drops noticeably due to vacancy annihilation. (Hardness decreases only slightly.) --- **Q2. Why does recovery not produce a new grain structure?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - Recovery involves only **short-range diffusion** of vacancies and **glide/climb of existing dislocations**; it does not involve the *long-range migration* of a high-angle grain boundary. - Therefore, the original deformed grains remain intact and no new strain-free nuclei form. Recrystallization — which *does* produce new grains — requires higher temperatures (typically $T \geq 0.4\,T_m$) where grain-boundary mobility becomes significant. Hence, recovery leaves grain size and shape unchanged. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style, Module 1]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of recovery in cold-worked metals with the help of dislocation mechanisms. Discuss the effect of recovery on (i) residual stress, (ii) hardness, and (iii) electrical resistivity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the time-dependence of dislocation density during isothermal recovery starting from the rate equation $d\rho/dt = -K\rho^{2}$. Use the result to show that long-time recovery asymptotes to zero dislocation density. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to Q.A(a): **Step 1 — Define recovery (1 mark):** Recovery is the low-temperature annealing stage where stored strain energy is relieved by **point-defect annihilation** and **dislocation rearrangement**, without new grain formation. **Step 2 — Dislocation mechanisms (2 marks):** - **Climb** of edge dislocations via vacancy absorption/emission — requires vacancy diffusion (thermally activated). - **Glide** of dislocations into lower-energy configurations. - **Polygonization** — bent dislocation arrays re-arrange into vertical "walls" forming subgrains separated by *low-angle* boundaries. **Step 3 — Effect on properties (3 marks):** - (i) **Residual stress:** Major drop (80–100%) because micro-plastic flow at elevated temperature equalises internal stresses. - (ii) **Hardness:** Only *slight* drop (5–15%) because Taylor's relation $\Delta \sigma_i = \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ shows $\sigma_i \propto \sqrt{\rho}$ — recovery reduces $\rho$ only modestly before recrystallization kicks in. - (iii) **Electrical resistivity:** *Sharp* initial drop because point defects (vacancies) are the dominant scattering centers and are annihilated first. **Step 4 — Engineering note (1 mark):** Recovery is deliberately exploited for **stress-relief annealing** of weldments, stampings and precision-machined parts. ##### Model Solution to Q.A(b): **Step 1 — State the rate equation (1 mark):** $$\frac{d\rho}{dt} = -K\,\rho^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables (1 mark):** $$\frac{d\rho}{\rho^{2}} = -K\,dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate from $\rho_0$ at $t=0$ to $\rho(t)$ at time $t$ (2 marks):** $$\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho(t)} \rho'^{-2}\,d\rho' = -K\int_{0}^{t} dt'$$ $$-\frac{1}{\rho(t)} + \frac{1}{\rho_0} = -K t$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\rho(t)$ (2 marks):** $$\rho(t) = \frac{\rho_0}{1 + K\rho_0 t}$$ **Step 5 — Long-time asymptote (1 mark):** As $t \to \infty$, the denominator $1 + K\rho_0 t \to \infty$, so $\rho(t) \to 0$, confirming complete theoretical annihilation of dislocations — although in practice recrystallization intervenes first. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the phenomenon of **polygonization** and explain how it differs from recrystallization. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cold-worked aluminium sheet has an initial dislocation density of $10^{15}$ m$^{-2}$. After a recovery anneal at $180^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 2 hours, the density drops to $2 \times 10^{14}$ m$^{-2}$. Using $\rho(t) = \rho_0/(1 + K\rho_0 t)$, calculate the annihilation constant $K$ and estimate the additional time required at the same temperature to reduce the density to $5 \times 10^{13}$ m$^{-2}$. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to Q.B(a): **Step 1 — Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a single bent slip plane with multiple extra dislocations of the same sign piled up; then show after climb they form a *vertical wall* — a low-angle tilt boundary, i.e., a **subgrain**. **Step 2 — Mechanism (3 marks):** During cold work, dislocations of the same sign get trapped on closely-spaced slip planes, causing lattice curvature. On heating, vacancies flow to the dislocations, allowing them to **climb**. Dislocations glide and align themselves *vertically* on top of each other, forming a low-angle tilt boundary.
