This subgrain wall reduces the long-range elastic strain energy of the original random array. **Step 3 — Difference from recrystallization (2 marks):** - Polygonization = **recovery** feature; produces *low-angle* sub-boundaries, *no* new high-angle grain boundaries, optical microstructure unchanged. - Recrystallization = produces *new high-angle grain boundaries* via nucleation and growth of strain-free grains; the optical microstructure is visibly altered. ##### Model Solution to Q.B(b): **Step 1 — Use the formula directly (1 mark):** $$\rho(t) = \frac{\rho_0}{1 + K\rho_0 t}$$ $$2\times 10^{14} = \frac{10^{15}}{1 + K(10^{15})(7200)}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $K$ (3 marks):** $$1 + 7.2\times 10^{18}\,K = \frac{10^{15}}{2\times 10^{14}} = 5$$ $$7.2\times 10^{18}\,K = 4$$ $$K = \frac{4}{7.2\times 10^{18}} = 5.56\times 10^{-19}\;\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Time to reach $\rho = 5\times 10^{13}$ m$^{-2}$ (3 marks):** $$\rho(t) = \frac{\rho_0}{1 + K\rho_0\,t} \;\Rightarrow\; t = \frac{1}{K}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)$$ $$t = \frac{1}{5.56\times 10^{-19}}\!\left(\frac{1}{5\times 10^{13}} - \frac{1}{10^{15}}\right)$$ $$t = 1.8\times 10^{18}\,(2\times 10^{-14} - 1\times 10^{-15})$$ $$t = 1.8\times 10^{18}\,(1.9\times 10^{-14})$$ $$t \approx 3.42\times 10^{4}\;\text{s} \approx 9.5\;\text{hours}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret (extra clarity):** The recovery process becomes **progressively slower** as $\rho$ decreases — this is the hyperbolic behaviour of $1/\rho$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students LOSE marks on Recovery questions:** > 1. **Stating that recovery produces new grains** — this is *wrong*. Recovery = no new grains. (–2 marks typical) > 2. **Confusing polygonization with recrystallization** — polygonization = subgrain (low-angle) boundary; recrystallization = new high-angle grain boundary. (–2 marks) > 3. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — write $K$ in **m²/s**, $\rho$ in **m$^{-2}$**, $t$ in **seconds**. (–1 mark) > 4. **Writing $T < T_m$** without normalising — KTU examiners expect **homologous temperature** $T/T_m$ for any "low temperature" remark. (–1 mark) > 5. **Skipping the $\sqrt{\rho}$ dependence** of flow stress — when explaining "why hardness only drops slightly," always cite **Taylor's relation** $\sigma_i = \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$. (–1 mark) --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Recovery** - ☐ Recovery = **first stage of annealing**, $T \approx 0.3\,T_m$, **no new grains**, **no microstructure change under optical microscope**. - ☐ Driving force = **stored strain energy** from cold work, mostly in **dislocations** ($E_s \propto \rho G b^{2}/2$). - ☐ Three sub-mechanisms: (1) **vacancy annihilation**, (2) **dislocation rearrangement / cell formation**, (3) **polygonization / sub-boundary formation**. - ☐ Property effects: **residual stress ↓↓ (major)**, **electrical resistivity ↓↓ (major)**, **hardness ↓ (minor, ≤ 15%)**, **optical grain size = unchanged**. - ☐ Activation energy: $Q_{\text{recov}} \approx 0.5\,Q_{\text{sd}}$ (lower than recrystallization). - ☐ Kinetics law: $\rho(t) = \dfrac{\rho_0}{1 + K\rho_0 t}$ — hyperbolic decay, long-time limit $\rho \to 0$. - ☐ Arrhenius temperature dependence: $t_{0.5} \propto \exp(Q/RT)$ — doubling $T$ speeds up recovery enormously. - ☐ Engineering use: **stress-relief annealing** of weldments, stampings, drawn wires, and machined forgings. - ☐ **Polygonization** = textbook microscopic signature of recovery; forms **low-angle tilt sub-boundaries**. - ☐ Recovery **precedes** recrystallization — at higher $T$, recrystallization takes over and *consumes* the recovered substructure. - ☐ Always express recovery temperatures in **homologous form** $T/T_m$ (Kelvin scale) in KTU answers. - ☐ Hardness drop during recovery alone is **NOT enough** to qualify as recrystallization — the optical micrograph is the **deciding evidence**. ---

topics.title: Recrystallization and Grain Growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Recrystallization and Grain Growth # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Recrystallization** is a thermally activated, diffusion-controlled solid-state process in which a new set of **strain-free, equiaxed grains** nucleate and grow within a plastically deformed (cold-worked) metal matrix, replacing the deformed microstructure. The driving force for this transformation is the **stored strain energy** (defect energy) accumulated in the lattice during prior plastic deformation. **Grain Growth** is the subsequent microstructural phenomenon in which the newly formed recrystallized grains increase in average size at the expense of smaller neighboring grains, driven solely by the **reduction in total grain boundary energy** of the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Both processes occur **without any phase change**. The crystal structure remains identical; only the grain morphology, size, and internal defect density evolve. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you have a tightly **crumpled sheet of aluminum foil** (representing a heavily cold-worked metal with tangled dislocations and high internal stress). - **Recovery** is like gently smoothing the foil by hand — the major creases relax, internal tensions drop, but the sheet is still visibly wrinkled. - **Recrystallization** is like placing the foil into a warm oven — small, smooth, new "baby crystals" (grains) appear at the most crumpled regions and slowly consume the wrinkled areas, leaving a smooth, stress-free sheet. - **Grain Growth** is what happens if you leave the foil in the oven too long — the small smooth regions begin merging with each other, with the larger ones swallowing the smaller ones (to minimize total edge length, analogous to soap bubbles merging). The foil never melts or changes its chemical identity — it merely **reorganizes its internal architecture** to minimize its stored energy. > [!NOTE] > **Stored Strain Energy** in a heavily cold-worked metal typically ranges between **0.5 to 2 cal/gram (≈ 2 to 8 J/g)**. This minute energy is the entire engine that drives recrystallization. --- ## 1.3 Why These Processes Matter in Metal Forming In metal forming operations like **rolling, forging, extrusion, and deep drawing**, controlling the final microstructure is paramount because it directly dictates: - **Yield Strength** (via Hall-Petch strengthening) - **Ductility and Toughness** - **Formability** for subsequent operations - **Anisotropy** and earing behavior in deep drawn cups Without recrystallization, every cold-formed component would retain dangerous residual stresses and be excessively brittle. --- ## 1.4 Pre-Requisite: What Happens During Cold Working? Before recrystallization can occur, the metal must first be cold worked. The consequences of cold working are: - **Dislocation density** ($\rho$) increases from $\sim 10^6 \text{ lines/cm}^2$ (annealed) to $\sim 10^{12} \text{ lines/cm}^2$ (severely deformed). - **Strength and hardness** increase (strain hardening / work hardening). - **Ductility** decreases. - **Residual stresses** build up. - Grains become **elongated** along the deformation direction. This stored defect energy is the *thermodynamic fuel* that makes recrystallization possible. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Three Sequential Stages During Annealing When a cold-worked metal is heated, three overlapping microstructural stages occur: ### Stage 1: Recovery - Occurs at **low homologous temperatures** ($T < 0.3 T_m$). - **Mechanism:** Annihilation of point defects (vacancies) and rearrangement of dislocations into lower-energy configurations (cell walls, low-angle grain boundaries). - **No observable change** in grain shape or grain size under optical microscopy. - **Mechanical Effect:** Significant drop in electrical resistivity; slight recovery of yield strength. - **No nucleation of new grains.** ### Stage 2: Recrystallization - Occurs at temperatures typically between **$0.3 T_m$ to $0.5 T_m$**. - **Mechanism:** Nucleation of strain-free grains at high-energy sites (grain boundaries, deformation bands, twin boundaries) followed by their growth. - **Microstructural Hallmark:** New equiaxed grains completely replace the deformed matrix. - **Mechanical Effect:** Sharp drop in hardness/strength, full restoration of ductility. ### Stage 3: Grain Growth - Occurs **after** recrystallization is complete, at higher temperatures or longer times. - **Mechanism:** Grain boundary migration driven by reduction in total grain boundary area. - **Microstructural Hallmark:** Progressive increase in average grain diameter; no further change in dislocation density. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Overlap Zone:** In practice, recovery, recrystallization, and grain growth can overlap in time, especially in highly cold-worked metals. --- ## 2.2 Thermodynamics: The Driving Force The **driving force per unit volume** for recrystallization is the difference in stored energy between the deformed matrix and the strain-free nucleus: $$\Delta G_v = \rho \cdot \mu \cdot b^2 / 2$$ where: - $\rho$ = dislocation density - $\mu$ = shear modulus - $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude The driving force for **grain growth** is the **grain boundary energy per unit area** ($\gamma_{gb}$), typically **0.5 to 1.0 J/m²** for most metals. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the **complete set of equations** required for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments on this topic. | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Variables & Meaning | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Avrami (Johnson-Mehl) Kinetics | $X = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$ | $X$ = fraction recrystallized, $t$ = time, $k, n$ = constants | Predicts fraction transformed at any time during isothermal annealing | | 2 | Avrami Rate Constant (Arrhenius form) | $k = k_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | $Q$ = activation energy, $R$ = gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature | Determines temperature sensitivity of recrystallization | | 3 | Hall-Petch Strengthening | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield strength, $d$ = grain diameter, $\sigma_0, k_y$ = constants | Relates grain size to mechanical strength | | 4 | Grain Growth Law | $D^n - D_0^n = K t$ | $D$ = grain diameter at time $t$, $D_0$ = initial diameter, $K$ = rate constant, $n \approx 2$ (ideal) | Predicts grain coarsening during prolonged exposure | | 5 | Grain Growth Rate Constant | $K = K_0 \exp(-Q_{gg}/RT)$ | $Q_{gg}$ = activation energy for grain boundary diffusion | Temperature dependence of grain growth | | 6 | Recrystallization Temperature | $T_R \approx (0.3 \text{ to } 0.5) T_m$ | $T_m$ = melting point in Kelvin | Rule-of-thumb for designing annealing cycles | | 7 | Stored Strain Energy | $E_s = \rho \mu b^2 / 2$ | $\rho$ = dislocation density, $\mu$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector | Quantifies driving force for recrystallization | | 8 | Activation Energy for Recrystallization | $Q_R \approx Q_{SD} + Q_N$ | $Q_{SD}$ = self-diffusion, $Q_N$ = nucleation barrier | Confirms diffusion-controlled nature of process | --- ## 2.4 Variables Affecting Recrystallization Temperature The **recrystallization temperature** is **not a fixed physical constant**; it depends on: 1. **Degree of Prior Cold Work** — Greater deformation → lower $T_R$. 2. **Purity of the Metal** — Solute atoms and impurities pin grain boundaries → raise $T_R$. 3. **Annealing Time** — Longer times → lower apparent $T_R$. 4. **Initial Grain Size** — Fine starting grains → slightly lower $T_R$. 5. **Presence of Second-Phase Particles** — Pin boundaries, raise $T_R$ dramatically. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common exam trap is asking for "the recrystallization temperature of a metal." The correct answer is *not* a single number but a *range* that depends on the variables above. --- ## 2.5 Variables Affecting Recrystallized Grain Size 1. **Amount of Deformation** — Higher strain → finer recrystallized grains (more nucleation sites). 2. **Annealing Temperature** — Higher T → larger grains. 3. **Annealing Time** — Longer time → larger grains. 4. **Purity / Solute Atoms** → Finer grains (boundary pinning). 5. **Original Grain Size** — Finer prior grains → finer recrystallized grains. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Application | Relevance | |---|---| | **Sheet Metal Forming (Automotive)** | Controlled annealing produces uniform deep-drawing quality steel (IF steels, DQSK grade) | | **Aluminum Can Manufacturing** | Recrystallization controls earing and texture in 3004-H19 alloy | | **Electrical Steel Laminations** | Large-grained grain-oriented silicon steel reduces transformer core losses | | **Titanium Aerospace Forgings** | $\beta$-annealing controls $\alpha$-grain size for fatigue life | | **Copper Wire Drawing** | Intermediate annealing restores ductility between drawing passes | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: The Avrami (Johnson-Mehl) Equation The **Avrami equation** is the cornerstone of recrystallization kinetics. We derive it step by step. ### Step 1: Define the Extended Volume Fraction Assume nucleation occurs **randomly** in space and time at a steady nucleation rate $\dot{N}$ (nuclei per unit volume per second). The grains grow isotropically at a constant linear velocity $G$. For a time interval between $\tau$ and $\tau + d\tau$, the number of nuclei formed per unit volume is: $$dN = \dot{N} \, d\tau$$ Each such nucleus grows into a sphere of radius $r = G(t - \tau)$ at a later time $t$. The volume of this sphere is: $$V_{\text{grain}}(\tau, t) = \frac{4}{3}\pi G^3 (t - \tau)^3$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Extended (Imaginary) Volume Summing the volumes of all grains that *would* exist if no impingement occurred: $$dV_{\text{ext}} = V_{\text{grain}} \cdot dN = \frac{4}{3}\pi \dot{N} G^3 (t-\tau)^3 \, d\tau$$ Integrating from $\tau = 0$ to $\tau = t$: $$V_{\text{ext}}(t) = \int_0^t \frac{4}{3}\pi \dot{N} G^3 (t-\tau)^3 \, d\tau$$ ### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral Let $u = t - \tau$, so $du = -d\tau$. When $\tau = 0$, $u = t$; when $\tau = t$, $u = 0$. $$V_{\text{ext}}(t) = \frac{4}{3}\pi \dot{N} G^3 \int_0^t u^3 \, du = \frac{4}{3}\pi \dot{N} G^3 \cdot \frac{t^4}{4}$$ $$\boxed{V_{\text{ext}}(t) = \frac{\pi}{3} \dot{N} G^3 t^4}$$ ### Step 4: Convert to Fraction Transformed Define the extended fraction $X_{\text{ext}} = V_{\text{ext}} / V_{\text{total}}$. Because real grains cannot overlap, the actual fraction is: $$dV_{\text{real}} = (1 - X) \, dV_{\text{ext}}$$ Separating variables: $$\frac{dX}{1 - X} = dX_{\text{ext}}$$ Integrating with initial condition $X = 0$ at $X_{\text{ext}} = 0$: $$-\ln(1 - X) = X_{\text{ext}}$$ $$\boxed{X = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)}$$ where $k = \frac{\pi}{3} \dot{N} G^3$ and $n = 4$ for constant nucleation and growth rate. > [!NOTE] > In real recrystallization, $n$ typically lies between **2 and 4**, depending on whether nucleation is site-saturated ($n = 3$) or constant ($n = 4$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Hall-Petch Equation (Yield Strength vs Grain Size) The **Hall-Petch relationship** is fundamental to connecting grain size with mechanical strength. ### Step 1: Conceptual Basis Dislocations pile up at grain boundaries. The stress concentration at the tip of a pile-up of $n$ dislocations against a boundary is $n\tau$. By fracture mechanics of a pile-up, the number of dislocations in a pile-up is: $$n = \frac{\pi d (\tau - \tau_0)}{G b}$$ where $\tau$ is applied shear stress, $\tau_0$ is friction stress, $d$ is grain diameter. ### Step 2: Yield Criterion Yielding occurs when the stress at the next grain reaches a critical value $\tau_c$: $$\tau_c = n \tau^*$$ where $\tau^*$ is the critical stress to operate a source in the adjacent grain. Substituting: $$\tau_c = \frac{\pi d (\tau - \tau_0)}{G b} \cdot \tau^*$$ Solving for $\tau$: $$\tau = \tau_0 + \frac{G b \tau_c}{\pi \tau^*} \cdot d^{-1}$$ Converting shear to normal stress ($\sigma = 2\tau$) and combining constants: $$\boxed{\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}}$$ This is the celebrated **Hall-Petch equation**, demonstrating that **finer grains give higher strength**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example > **Problem:** A cold-worked copper specimen (melting point $T_m = 1085°\text{C}$) is annealed at $300°\text{C}$ for 60 minutes. The fraction recrystallized follows $X = 1 - \exp(-k t^4)$ with $k = 1.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}^{-4}$. Calculate the fraction recrystallized. **Step 1:** Convert time to seconds: $t = 60 \times 60 = 3600 \text{ s}$ **Step 2:** Compute $t^4$: $$t^4 = (3600)^4 = 1.6796 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^4$$ **Step 3:** Compute $k t^4$: $$k t^4 = 1.2 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.6796 \times 10^{14} = 2015.5$$ **Step 4:** Apply the Avrami equation: $$X = 1 - \exp(-2015.5) \approx 1 - 0 = 1.0$$ **Result:** Recrystallization is **100% complete** within the first hour at this temperature. The high $k$ value reflects very rapid transformation. --- ## 3.4 Grain Growth Analytical Treatment For normal grain growth, the average grain diameter evolves as: $$D^n - D_0^n = K t$$ Taking the time derivative: $$\frac{dD}{dt} = \frac{K}{n D^{n-1}}$$ For ideal pure metals, $n = 2$ (parabolic growth). The driving pressure from a curved grain boundary is: $$P = \frac{2 \gamma_{gb}}{r}$$ where $r$ is the local radius of curvature, inversely related to grain size $D$: $$r \propto D \quad \Rightarrow \quad P \propto \frac{1}{D}$$ Since the growth rate $dD/dt \propto P$: $$\frac{dD}{dt} \propto \frac{1}{D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D^2 \propto t$$ confirming parabolic (n = 2) kinetics for ideal grain growth. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Stages of Annealing of Cold-Worked Metal ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Cold Worked Metal
High dislocation density
Elongated grains
High stored energy"] --> B["Stage 1 RECOVERY
T less than 0.3 Tm
Dislocation rearrangement
No grain shape change"] B --> C["Stage 2 RECRYSTALLIZATION
T equals 0.3 to 0.5 Tm
Nucleation of new grains
Strain-free equiaxed grains"] C --> D["Stage 3 GRAIN GROWTH
Higher T or longer t
Large grains consume small grains
Grain boundary energy minimized"] D --> E["Final Annealed Microstructure
Coarse equiaxed grains
Low dislocation density
Restored ductility"] F["Factors Influencing Process"] -.-> B F -.-> C F -.-> D G["Time and Temperature Control"] -.-> C G -.-> D ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Recrystallization Kinetics Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Start of Annealing
t equals 0
X equals 0 percent"] -->|Induction Period| B["Nucleation Onset
X less than 5 percent"] B -->|Rapid Growth| C["Maximum Rate
X equals 50 percent
t equals t half"] C -->|Impingement| D["Saturation
X greater than 95 percent"] D --> E["Complete Recrystallization
X equals 100 percent"] E --> F["Grain Growth Stage
Diameter increases with time"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Variables Controlling Recrystallized Grain Size ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INPUT["INDEPENDENT VARIABLES"] P1["Amount of Cold Work
Higher strain finer grain"] P2["Annealing Temperature
Higher T coarser grain"] P3["Annealing Time
Longer t coarser grain"] P4["Purity of Metal
Impurities finer grain"] P5["Initial Grain Size
Fine prior grain finer product"] end subgraph MECHANISM["PHYSICAL MECHANISM"] Q1["Nucleation Rate increases
with strain and grain boundaries"] Q2["Grain Boundary Mobility
controlled by T and solute drag"] end subgraph OUTPUT["MICROSTRUCTURAL OUTPUT"] R1["Recrystallized Grain Diameter D"] R2["Grain Size Distribution"] R3["Mechanical Properties
Strength Ductility"] end P1 --> Q1 P2 --> Q2 P3 --> Q2 P4 --> Q2 P5 --> Q1 Q1 --> R1 Q2 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R1 --> R3 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Description: Microstructural Evolution If we could "freeze" the microstructure at various time points during isothermal annealing, the progression would be: | Time | Microstructural Appearance | Dominant Process | |------|---------------------------|----------------| | t = 0 | Highly elongated, internally fragmented grains, dense dislocation tangles | Cold-worked state | | t = t₁ (small) | Elongated grains, cell structure formed inside, low-angle boundaries visible | Recovery | | t = t₂ (medium) | Small, new strain-free grains appearing at old grain boundaries | Recrystallization onset | | t = t₃ = t₁/₂ | About 50% of volume is new grains | Mid-recrystallization | | t = t₄ (large) | Completely new equiaxed grain structure | Recrystallization complete | | t > t₄ | Grain boundaries migrating, large grains eating small ones | Grain growth | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define recrystallization temperature. Mention any two factors that affect it.** **Model Answer:** **Recrystallization Temperature** is defined as the minimum temperature at which a cold-worked metal, when held for a specified time (conventionally one hour), undergoes complete recrystallization (i.e., 100% replacement of the deformed microstructure by new strain-free grains). **Two factors affecting recrystallization temperature:** 1. **Degree of Prior Cold Work:** Greater the amount of cold deformation, the lower the recrystallization temperature. This is because higher stored strain energy provides a larger thermodynamic driving force for nucleation. 2. **Purity of the Metal:** The presence of solute atoms and impurity particles raises the recrystallization temperature. Solute atoms exert a drag force (solute drag) on migrating grain boundaries, impeding their motion. *(Mention of additional valid factors like annealing time and initial grain size earns full 3 marks.)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Distinguish between recovery and recrystallization.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Recovery | Recrystallization | |---------|----------|-------------------| | Temperature range | Low ($T < 0.3 T_m$) | Moderate ($0.3 T_m$ to $0.5 T_m$) | | Optical microstructure | No visible change | New strain-free grains appear | | Dislocation density | Slight decrease | Drastic decrease | | Driving force | Stored strain energy (point defects) | Stored strain energy (dislocations) | | Activation energy | Lower (vacancy migration) | Higher (self-diffusion + nucleation) | | Property change | Minor recovery of strength; resistivity drops | Full restoration of ductility | --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **(a)** Explain the mechanism of recrystallization with neat sketches. Discuss the variables that affect the recrystallized grain size. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cold-worked brass strip is annealed at 600°C. The recrystallization follows the Avrami equation with $n = 3$ and $k = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-3}$. Determine the time required for 50% recrystallization. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Mechanism of Recrystallization:** Recrystallization proceeds in two fundamental steps: **1. Nucleation of new strain-free grains:** - Occurs preferentially at **high-energy heterogeneous sites** such as: - Prior grain boundaries (especially triple junctions) - Deformation bands and twin boundaries - Inclusions and second-phase particle interfaces - Zones of high lattice curvature - The nucleus is a small, dislocation-free region bounded by a high-angle grain boundary. - For a stable nucleus, the radius must exceed the **critical radius**: $$r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{gb}}{\Delta G_v}$$ **2. Growth of nuclei:** - Once stable nuclei form, their boundaries migrate into the surrounding deformed matrix. - The boundary migration velocity $v$ is given by: $$v = M \cdot P = M \cdot \frac{2\gamma_{gb}}{r}$$ where $M$ is grain boundary mobility. **Sketch Description:** - Left panel: Cold-worked microstructure with elongated grains and dense dislocation tangles. - Middle panel: Small new strain-free grains nucleating at old grain boundaries. - Right panel: Fully recrystallized, equiaxed, strain-free grain structure. **Variables affecting recrystallized grain size:** 1. **Amount of prior deformation** — Higher strain → finer grains (more nucleation sites). 2. **Annealing temperature** — Higher T → coarser grains (faster boundary migration). 3. **Annealing time** — Longer time → coarser grains. 4. **Purity / solute content** — Higher purity → coarser grains (no boundary pinning). 5. **Initial grain size** — Finer prior grains → finer recrystallized grains. 6. **Presence of dispersed particles** — Pin boundaries → finer grains. *(Diagram + 5 variables + mechanism explanation awards full 7 marks.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $n = 3$, $k = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-3}$, $X = 0.5$ **Avrami equation:** $$X = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$$ Substitute $X = 0.5$: $$0.5 = 1 - \exp(-k t^3)$$ $$\exp(-k t^3) = 0.5$$ Taking natural logarithm: $$-k t^3 = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$ $$t^3 = \frac{0.693}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1386 \text{ s}^3$$ $$t = (1386)^{1/3}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 11.15 \text{ seconds}}$$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a)** Discuss the phenomenon of grain growth. Explain normal and abnormal grain growth with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mild steel specimen has a yield strength of 200 MPa with an average grain size of 50 μm. Calculate the yield strength if the grain size is reduced to 10 μm. Given $\sigma_0 = 50$ MPa and $k_y = 0.55$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Grain Growth:** Grain growth is the process by which the average grain size of a fully recrystallized metal increases with continued exposure to elevated temperature. It occurs **after** recrystallization is complete. **Driving Force:** Reduction in total grain boundary area (and hence total grain boundary energy). Curved boundaries migrate toward their center of curvature. **Normal Grain Growth:** - Continuous and uniform increase in average grain size. - Grain size distribution remains approximately self-similar (log-normal). - Kinetics follow: $$D^2 - D_0^2 = K t$$ - Smaller grains shrink and disappear; larger grains grow. **Abnormal Grain Growth (Secondary Recrystallization):** - A few grains grow extremely rapidly, consuming neighboring normal grains. - Results in a **bimodal** microstructure: very large grains embedded in a matrix of small grains. - **Conditions favoring abnormal grain growth:** - Presence of second-phase particles that pin most boundaries but are overwhelmed at certain grains. - Strong crystallographic texture. - Critical grain size distribution. - **Engineering example:** Production of grain-oriented electrical steel for transformer cores. *(Neat sketch showing normal growth with uniform enlargement, plus abnormal growth showing one large grain consuming neighbors, awards full marks.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** - $d_1 = 50 \, \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, m$ - $\sigma_{y1} = 200 \, MPa$ - $d_2 = 10 \, \mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6} \, m$ - $\sigma_0 = 50 \, MPa$ - $k_y = 0.55 \, MPa \cdot m^{1/2}$ **Hall-Petch equation:** $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$$ **Step 1:** For the new grain size $d_2$: $$d_2^{-1/2} = (10 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = (10^{-5})^{-1/2} = 10^{2.5}$$ $$d_2^{-1/2} = 316.23 \, m^{-1/2}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate new yield strength: $$\sigma_{y2} = 50 + 0.55 \times 316.23$$ $$\sigma_{y2} = 50 + 173.93$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{y2} \approx 223.93 \, MPa}$$ **Interpretation:** Reducing the grain size from 50 μm to 10 μm increases the yield strength by approximately **24 MPa** (~12% improvement), validating the Hall-Petch strengthening principle. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing recovery with recrystallization** — Students often claim new grains form during recovery. They do not; recovery only rearranges existing dislocations. > > 2. **Quoting recrystallization temperature as a single fixed value** — Always state it as a *range* (0.3 to 0.5 Tm) and mention the *one-hour convention*. > > 3. **Forgetting to convert units in Hall-Petch problems** — Grain diameter MUST be in **meters** (not μm) when using $k_y$ values in standard SI form. > > 4. **Mixing up normal and abnormal grain growth** — Abnormal growth requires a *bimodal* grain distribution; do not confuse it with simple coarsening. > > 5. **Omitting diagrams in 7-mark theory questions** — A neat sketch is **mandatory** for full marks in KTU valuation. > > 6. **Calculation errors in Avrami problems** — Remember to take natural log of both sides and raise to fractional power $1/n$. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before Your Exam:** - Recrystallization is a **diffusion-controlled, solid-state process** that produces **new strain-free equiaxed grains**. - It is **not a phase transformation**; the crystal structure does not change. - The driving force is **stored strain energy** from prior cold work (not a chemical free energy difference). - **Three stages of annealing:** Recovery → Recrystallization → Grain Growth. - Recrystallization temperature is approximately **$0.3$ to $0.5 \, T_m$** (one-hour convention). - Higher cold work → lower recrystallization temperature and finer recrystallized grains. - Higher purity → lower recrystallization temperature but coarser recrystallized grains. - **Avrami equation:** $X = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$ with $n$ between 2 and 4. - **Hall-Petch equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — finer grains = higher strength. - **Grain growth law:** $D^2 - D_0^2 = K t$ for ideal normal growth (parabolic). - **Abnormal grain growth** produces bimodal microstructures; exploited in grain-oriented electrical steels. - Grain boundaries act as dislocation barriers — this is the physical basis of Hall-Petch strengthening. - Solute atoms and second-phase particles **pin** grain boundaries, raising $T_R$ and refining grain size. - The activation energy for recrystallization is approximately equal to the activation energy for **self-diffusion**.

topics.title: Characteristics and Applications of Hot Working and Cold Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Applications of Hot Working and Cold Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Metal Forming — The Foundation

**Metal Forming** is a manufacturing process group in which the desired shape of a solid metallic workpiece is obtained through *plastic deformation* — the permanent rearrangement of its internal crystal structure by applying external mechanical forces, without any removal of material (i.e., no chips are produced).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastic Deformation vs Elastic Deformation:**
> - *Elastic*: Temporary, atoms return to original positions when load is removed (Hooke's Law region).
> - *Plastic*: Permanent, atoms slide along crystallographic planes (slip/twinning) and do not return.

The metal forming processes are broadly classified by **temperature** at which deformation occurs:

| Process Type | Temperature Range | Recrystallization Status |
|---|---|---|
| Cold Working | $T < 0.3\,T_m$ | Below recrystallization temperature |
| Warm Working | $0.3\,T_m < T < 0.5\,T_m$ | Partial recovery |
| Hot Working | $T > 0.5\,T_m$ | Above recrystallization temperature |

where **$T_m$ is the absolute melting temperature (in Kelvin)**.

---

### 1.2 Hot Working — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hot Working** is defined as the plastic deformation of metals carried out at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature**, such that the rate of recrystallization is at least equal to the rate of strain hardening caused by deformation.

This simultaneous action of strain hardening (during deformation) and recrystallization (between deformation intervals or during it) keeps the material *soft and ductile* throughout the process.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine kneading hot wax with your hands. The wax is soft because heat keeps its internal "structure" regenerating itself, allowing you to reshape it endlessly without it becoming hard or cracking. Cold wax, in contrast, becomes brittle if you keep bending it. Metals behave similarly.

---

### 1.3 Cold Working — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Working** is defined as the plastic deformation of metals carried out at a temperature **below the recrystallization temperature** (typically at or near room temperature), where strain hardening is retained and no recrystallization takes place during or immediately after deformation.

Since atoms cannot rearrange into new strain-free grains, the dislocations pile up, making the metal **harder and stronger** but **less ductile**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of repeatedly bending a paperclip (steel wire) at room temperature. The first bend is easy, the second harder, and soon the wire snaps because it has become progressively harder and brittle — exactly the strain-hardening behavior seen in cold working.

---

### 1.4 The Recrystallization Temperature ($T_{rex}$)

The **recrystallization temperature** is the minimum temperature at which a heavily cold-worked metal forms a completely new set of strain-free equiaxed grains within a specified time (conventionally taken as 1 hour).

A practical rule used in industry:
$$T_{rex} \approx 0.4 \, T_m \quad \text{(Kelvin)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> For some high-melting-point alloys and refractory metals, $T_{rex}$ may deviate significantly from the 0.4 rule. Always refer to the alloy-specific stress-relief diagrams in design.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hot Working — Characteristics (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **High deformation temperature** ($T > 0.5\,T_m$) → atoms vibrate strongly → slip planes activate easily.
2. **Dynamic recovery and dynamic recrystallization** continuously replace work-hardened grains with new, strain-free grains.
3. **Yield stress is lowered** (typically to $1/10$ to $1/20$ of cold-working values), so smaller forces and lower power are needed.
4. **Ductility is very high** → heavy reductions (up to $90\%+$ in single passes for rolling) are possible without fracture.
5. **Grain refinement** is achievable because new grains nucleate uniformly throughout the deformed volume.
6. **Surface scaling/oxidation** occurs (mill scale, $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$) because oxygen reacts with hot surfaces.
7. **Dimensional accuracy is poor** because of thermal contraction upon cooling and surface scaling.
8. **Strength of finished part is lower** compared to cold-worked equivalents (grain refinement compensates only partially).
9. **Anisotropy** (directional properties) in the finished product is reduced due to grain randomization.
10. **No residual stresses** (or very low) because recrystallization relieves internal stresses.

---

### 2.2 Cold Working — Characteristics (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Deformation at or near room temperature** ($T < 0.3\,T_m$) → no atomic rearrangement by diffusion.
2. **Dislocation density increases** with strain → they entangle and impede each other's motion.
3. **Strain hardening (work hardening)** raises yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and hardness.
4. **Ductility decreases** → only limited deformation is possible before cracking.
5. **Surface finish is excellent** (no oxide scales, smooth die surfaces transfer their finish).
6. **Dimensional tolerances are tight** (± 0.025 mm typical in cold rolling).
7. **Strength-to-weight ratio improves** — useful for aerospace and automotive structural parts.
8. **Anisotropy develops** — properties vary with rolling/extrusion direction.
9. **Residual stresses** are induced (tensile in some layers, compressive in others).
10. **Intermediate annealing** may be required for large total reductions.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — frequently asked in KTU University Exams. Memorize units carefully.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon^{n}$ | **Hollomon Equation** — true stress–true strain in plastic region | Cold working, strain-hardened zone |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{K \cdot \varepsilon^{n}}{1+n}$ | Average flow stress with work hardening | Forge load calculations |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\sigma_f}{\sigma_{\text{avg}}} = 1+n$ | Ratio of final flow stress to average | Work-hardening check |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | **True (logarithmic) strain** in compression | Rolling, forging, extrusion |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_T = 2\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_0}{R_f}\right)$ | True strain in wire/tube drawing | Drawing |
| 6 | $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + e)$ | True stress from engineering stress | Tensile testing analysis |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + e)$ | True strain from engineering strain | Tensile testing analysis |
| 8 | $Y_{avg} = \dfrac{Y_f + Y_0}{2}$ | Average flow stress **without** strain hardening | Hot working approximation |
| 9 | $Y_f = K \varepsilon^{n}$ | Final yield (flow) stress after strain | Cold working |
| 10 | $T_{rex} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | Recrystallization temperature (K) | Classify hot/cold working |
| 11 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{F}{A_f}$ | True flow stress | Force–area relation |
| 12 | $F = Y_f \cdot A_f$ | Forging load (no friction) | Die design |
| 13 | $\sigma_V = \sqrt{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2} + \sigma_3^{2} - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1}$ | **Von Mises yield criterion** | Multi-axial yielding |
| 14 | $\sigma_V = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | **Tresca yield criterion** | Conservative yielding check |
| 15 | $\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0}\times 100$ | Percent cold work | Cold rolling/drawing |
| 16 | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{\bar{\sigma}\,\varepsilon}{\rho \, C_p}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise | Hot working efficiency |
| 17 | $\bar{\sigma}_{fr} = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\!\int_{0}^{\varepsilon}\!\! K\varepsilon^{n}\,d\varepsilon$ | Average flow stress (integral form) | Cold forging load |

Where:
- $K$ = **strength coefficient** (MPa)
- $n$ = **strain-hardening exponent** (dimensionless, $0 < n < 1$)
- $\sigma_f$ = final flow stress
- $\bar{\sigma}$ or $\sigma_{\text{avg}}$ = average flow stress
- $\sigma_E, e$ = engineering stress and strain
- $\sigma_T, \varepsilon_T$ = true stress and true strain
- $Y_f$ = yield stress at end of deformation
- $Y_{avg}$ = average yield stress
- $\rho$ = density of metal
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity
- $T_m$ = absolute melting temperature

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hot Working**: Used in primary metal industries (steel mills, aluminum billet production, large forging of turbine shafts, ship propellers).
- **Cold Working**: Used for precision components like sheet metal panels in automobiles, beverage cans (deep drawing of aluminum), electrical wires (copper drawing), cold-headed fasteners.
- **Hybrid**: Many real-world processes combine both — e.g., hot-rolled steel strip is subsequently cold-rolled to improve surface finish and mechanical properties.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Flow Stress with Strain Hardening

Starting from the **Hollomon Equation**:
$$\sigma = K\,\varepsilon^{n}$$

The average flow stress $\sigma_{\text{avg}}$ is defined as the mean value of the instantaneous flow stress over the strain interval $[0, \varepsilon_f]$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f - 0}\int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f}\sigma\, d\varepsilon
$$

Substituting $\sigma = K\varepsilon^{n}$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f}\int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f}K\varepsilon^{n}\,d\varepsilon
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_f}\left[\frac{\varepsilon^{n+1}}{n+1}\right]_{0}^{\varepsilon_f}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_f}\cdot\frac{\varepsilon_f^{n+1}}{n+1}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{K\,\varepsilon_f^{n}}{n+1}
$$

Since the final flow stress is $\sigma_f = K\,\varepsilon_f^{n}$, we obtain:

$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sigma_f}{1+n}
$$

Equivalently, the **ratio** of final-to-average flow stress is:
$$
\frac{\sigma_f}{\sigma_{\text{avg}}} = 1 + n
$$

> **Engineering insight:** A larger $n$ (more strain-hardenable metal like austenitic stainless steel) means a bigger gap between $\sigma_f$ and $\sigma_{\text{avg}}$. This is why cold forging force calculations must always use the *average* flow stress, not the *final* flow stress, otherwise the press would be vastly oversized.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — True Strain in Cold Rolling

For a strip of initial thickness $h_0$ rolled to final thickness $h_f$ (assuming width change is negligible and volume constancy holds):

Volume constancy: $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$ (where $A$ is cross-sectional area, $h$ is thickness for sheet)

Differential strain at any instant:
$$
d\varepsilon = -\frac{dh}{h}
$$

Integrating from $h_0$ to $h_f$:

$$
\varepsilon_T = \int_{h_0}^{h_f}\!\left(-\frac{dh}{h}\right) = -\left[\ln h\right]_{h_0}^{h_f}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)
$$

This is the **true (logarithmic) strain** in cold rolling. The percent cold work follows directly:

$$
\%CW = \frac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0}\times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{h_f}{h_0}\right)\times 100
$$

> **Numerical illustration:** For an aluminum sheet going from $h_0 = 5.0\,\text{mm}$ to $h_f = 1.5\,\text{mm}$:
> - True strain: $\varepsilon_T = \ln(5.0/1.5) = \ln(3.333) = 1.204$
> - Percent cold work: $\%CW = (1 - 1.5/5.0)\times 100 = 70\%$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature Criterion (Classification Rule)

The recrystallization temperature for a metal is given by the empirical rule:
$$
T_{rex} = 0.4\,T_m \quad \text{(in Kelvin)}
$$

Therefore the operating temperature rule becomes:
$$
\text{Hot Working:} \quad T_{work} \geq 0.5\,T_m
$$
$$
\text{Warm Working:} \quad 0.3\,T_m \leq T_{work} < 0.5\,T_m
$$
$$
\text{Cold Working:} \quad T_{work} < 0.3\,T_m
$$

> **Worked Example — Low-carbon Steel:**
> Melting point: $T_m \approx 1500\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 1773\,\text{K}$
> - $T_{rex} \approx 0.4 \times 1773 = 709\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - Hot working upper limit: $\geq 900\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - Warm working range: $530 - 900\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - Cold working: below $530\,^{\circ}\text{C}$

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Hot Working vs Cold Working

| Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | $T > 0.5\,T_m$ | $T < 0.3\,T_m$ (often RT) |
| Recrystallization | Continuous (dynamic) | Absent |
| Yield stress | Low (easy to deform) | High (forceful deformation) |
| Ductility | Very high | Limited |
| Max reduction/pass | Up to 90 % | ~ 5–25 % |
| Strain hardening | Negligible | Significant |
| Surface finish | Rough, oxidized | Smooth, bright |
| Dimensional tolerance | Poor | Excellent (± 0.025 mm) |
| Mechanical strength | Lower | Higher |
| Residual stress | Low | High |
| Grain structure | Refined, equiaxed | Elongated, textured |
| Equipment power | Lower | Higher |
| Tool life | Short (wear from heat/scale) | Long |
| Energy requirement | High (reheating) | Low |
| Typical processes | Hot rolling, hot forging, hot extrusion, hot drawing | Cold rolling, cold forging, cold extrusion, deep drawing, wire drawing, sheet metal operations |

---

### 3.5 Engineering Applications Matrix

| Industry Sector | Preferred Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Steel structural beams (I-sections) | Hot rolling | Large cross-section, ductility |
| Beverage cans (Aluminum) | Cold deep drawing + ironing | Thin walls, tight tolerances |
| Copper electrical wire | Cold drawing | High conductivity, precise diameter |
| Gas turbine disc | Hot forging (isothermal) | Grain flow + strength |
| Aircraft skin panels (Al) | Cold rolling | Smoothness, fatigue strength |
| Railway wheels | Hot forging + rolling | Large size, high ductility |
| Bolts and screws | Cold heading | High volume, tight tolerance |
| Steel pipes for plumbing | Cold drawing | Bright surface, exact ID/OD |
| Crankshafts (auto) | Hot forging | Grain flow under fatigue |
| Bicycle frames (Ti) | Cold tube bending + drawing | Surface finish |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Metal Forming by Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal Forming Process] --> B{Working Temperature?}
    B -->|T less than 0.3 Tm| C[Cold Working]
    B -->|T between 0.3 and 0.5 Tm| D[Warm Working]
    B -->|T greater than 0.5 Tm| E[Hot Working]
    C --> F1[Strain Hardening Active]
    C --> F2[No Recrystallization]
    C --> F3[High Strength and Accuracy]
    D --> G1[Partial Recovery]
    D --> G2[Reduced Force than Cold]
    D --> G3[Better Surface than Hot]
    E --> H1[Dynamic Recrystallization]
    E --> H2[Low Yield Stress]
    E --> H3[Large Reduction Possible]
    F1 --> F4[Cold Rolling]
    F1 --> F5[Deep Drawing]
    F1 --> F6[Cold Heading]
    F1 --> F7[Wire Drawing]
    H1 --> H4[Hot Rolling]
    H1 --> H5[Hot Forging]
    H1 --> H6[Hot Extrusion]
    H1 --> H7[Hot Piercing]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Hot Rolling Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Reheating Furnace 1100 to 1250 C] --> R2[Descaler High Pressure Water]
    R2 --> R3[Roll Stand 1 Roughing]
    R3 --> R4[Roll Stand 2 Roughing]
    R4 --> R5[Intermediate Descaling]
    R5 --> R6[Finishing Stand Group 5 to 7 Stands]
    R6 --> R7[Run Out Table with Laminar Cooling]
    R7 --> R8[Coiler or Shear]
    R8 --> R9[Cooling Bed and Inspection]
    A1[Load Cell Force Feedback] --> R3
    A1 --> R4
    A1 --> R6
    A2[Pyrometer Temperature Loop] --> R1
    A2 --> R7
    A3[Automatic Gauge Control AGC] --> R3
    A3 --> R4
    A3 --> R6
```

> **Reading guide:** Each rectangle represents a major functional block. Arrows show the material flow direction. Auxiliary inputs (A1–A3) feed control signals back to the deforming stands — these are *not* material flows but **feedback control loops** in modern mills.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Interaction Between Strain Hardening and Recovery / Recrystallization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start of Deformation] --> S1[Plastic Strain Increases]
    S1 --> S2{Is T greater than T rex?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Dynamic Recovery Occurs]
    S2 -->|Yes| S4[Dynamic Recrystallization Nucleates New Grains]
    S3 --> S5[Dislocation Density Falls]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Flow Stress Drops to Steady State]
    S2 -->|No| S7[Strain Hardening Only]
    S7 --> S8[Dislocations Multiply and Entangle]
    S8 --> S9[Flow Stress Rises Continuously]
    S9 --> S10[Possible Cracking or Fracture if Continued]
    S6 --> S11[Hot Working Continues Easily]
    S10 --> S12[Annealing Required Before Further Cold Working]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Cold Working Process Examples (Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Input Stock Sheet or Wire or Billet] --> P1{Pick Process}
    P1 --> P2a[Cold Rolling]
    P1 --> P2b[Wire Drawing]
    P1 --> P2c[Deep Drawing]
    P1 --> P2d[Cold Extrusion]
    P2a --> P3a[Reduced Thickness and Increased Length]
    P2b --> P3b[Reduced Diameter and Increased Length]
    P2c --> P3c[Sheet into Cup Shape]
    P2d --> P3d[Slug into Profile Shape]
    P3a --> P4[Intermediate Anneal if Needed]
    P3b --> P4
    P3c --> P4
    P3d --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Further Cold Reduction Pass]
    P5 --> P6[Final Anneal if Softness Required]
    P5 --> P7[Finished Part with High Strength and Finish]
```

> **Interpretation:** This block layout shows how a single cold-worked product may go through multiple deformation + annealing cycles, depending on the total deformation required. Each cycle of cold reduction increases strength, each anneal restores ductility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]**

#### Question 1. Define Hot Working. State any four characteristics of hot working.
**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Hot working is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation is carried out at a temperature above the recrystallization temperature of the metal, so that the work-piece remains soft and ductile throughout deformation.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Low flow stress:** Yield strength drops sharply above $T_{rex}$, allowing large deformations with low forces.
2. **High ductility:** Reductions up to $90\%$ are possible in a single rolling pass.
3. **Surface oxidation / scaling:** Hot metal reacts with atmospheric oxygen forming mill scale ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$, $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4}$).
4. **Grain refinement:** Dynamic recrystallization produces fine equiaxed grains, improving toughness.

*(Any four characteristics from: low yield stress, dynamic recrystallization, large reductions, poor surface finish, no residual stress, no work hardening, requires reheating, refined grains.)*

---

#### Question 2. List four advantages of Cold Working over Hot Working.
**Model Answer:**

1. **Better surface finish:** Absence of scale gives a bright, smooth surface suitable for visible parts.
2. **Tighter dimensional tolerances:** Dimensional accuracy of $\pm 0.025$ mm is routinely achieved.
3. **Higher strength and hardness:** Strain hardening raises yield and ultimate tensile strength.
4. **Improved directional properties (anisotropy):** Can be tailored for specific load directions.
5. **No heating required** — energy efficient for small parts.
6. **Longer tool life** — cold dies do not suffer from thermal fatigue.

*(Any four accepted.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern: Choose ONE out of TWO.]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare the characteristics of hot working and cold working in detail. Discuss the criteria used to classify a metal forming process as hot or cold working, with reference to recrystallization temperature. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A low-carbon steel strip is cold-rolled from an initial thickness of $4.0\,\text{mm}$ to a final thickness of $1.6\,\text{mm}$. If the strength coefficient $K = 530\,\text{MPa}$ and the strain-hardening exponent $n = 0.26$, compute:
   (i) True strain
   (ii) Final flow stress
   (iii) Average flow stress during deformation
   (iv) Percent cold work

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison and Classification:**
- *Classification criterion:* The deciding factor is whether the working temperature $T_w$ is above or below the **recrystallization temperature** $T_{rex}$ of the metal. Empirically, $T_{rex} \approx 0.4\,T_m$.
- *Hot Working ($T_w > 0.5\,T_m$):* Strain hardening and recrystallization occur simultaneously → no net hardening; large reductions; poor surface; refined grains; lower forces.
- *Cold Working ($T_w < 0.3\,T_m$):* No recrystallization → strain hardening accumulates; limited ductility; better surface and tolerance; higher forces; improved strength.
- *Warm Working (intermediate):* Partial recovery only.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $h_0 = 4.0\,\text{mm}$, $h_f = 1.6\,\text{mm}$, $K = 530\,\text{MPa}$, $n = 0.26$.

**(i) True strain:**
$$
\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{4.0}{1.6}\right) = \ln(2.5) = 0.9163
$$

**(ii) Final flow stress:**
$$
\sigma_f = K\varepsilon_T^{n} = 530 \times (0.9163)^{0.26}
$$

Compute $(0.9163)^{0.26}$:
- $\ln(0.9163) = -0.0875$
- $0.26 \times (-0.0875) = -0.0228$
- $e^{-0.0228} = 0.9774$

$$
\sigma_f = 530 \times 0.9774 \approx 518\,\text{MPa}
$$

**(iii) Average flow stress:**
$$
\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sigma_f}{1+n} = \frac{518}{1.26} \approx 411\,\text{MPa}
$$

**(iv) Percent cold work:**
$$
\%CW = \frac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0}\times 100 = \frac{4.0 - 1.6}{4.0}\times 100 = 60\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - Students frequently use **engineering strain** $(h_0 - h_f)/h_0 = 0.6$ instead of **true strain** $\ln(h_0/h_f) = 0.916$ in the Hollomon equation. This gives wrong $\sigma_f$ — always use *true* strain in $K\varepsilon^n$.
> - When asked for average flow stress, do NOT use the arithmetic mean of $\sigma_0$ and $\sigma_f$; use $\sigma_f/(1+n)$.
> - Don't forget the **percent cold work** formula uses engineering reduction, not true strain.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches (block diagrams acceptable) the dynamic recovery and dynamic recrystallization mechanisms that occur during hot working of metals. Discuss their effect on flow stress. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A cylindrical billet of copper is hot-extruded at $750\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. The extrusion ratio (initial area / final area) is $9$. The average flow stress at this temperature is $Y_{avg} = 70\,\text{MPa}$. Calculate:
   (i) True strain in the deformation
   (ii) Final flow stress if $n = 0.15$ (assumed Hollomon behavior)
   (iii) Required extrusion force (ignore friction) for a final diameter of $20\,\text{mm}$

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dynamic Recovery and Dynamic Recrystallization:**

- *Dynamic Recovery:* During hot deformation, vacancies and dislocations move under stress and temperature. Dislocations annihilate and rearrange into sub-grain boundaries, lowering stored strain energy. The flow stress reaches a steady plateau.
- *Dynamic Recrystallization:* When strain exceeds a critical value $\varepsilon_c$, new strain-free grains nucleate at grain boundaries (especially at triple junctions) and grow. The flow stress shows an initial peak followed by oscillations to a steady state.
- *Effect on flow stress:* Both mechanisms counteract strain hardening. The flow stress–strain curve in hot working rises briefly and then plateaus or oscillates, unlike cold working where stress rises monotonically until fracture.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: Extrusion ratio $r = A_0/A_f = 9$, $Y_{avg} = 70\,\text{MPa}$, $n = 0.15$, $D_f = 20\,\text{mm}$.

**(i) True strain (constant volume, area ratio = extrusion ratio for solid billet):**
$$
\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln(9) = 2.197
$$

**(ii) Final flow stress:**
$$
\sigma_f = \sigma_{avg} \cdot (1+n) = 70 \times 1.15 = 80.5\,\text{MPa}
$$

(Alternatively via Hollomon: $\sigma_f = K\varepsilon_f^{n}$; solving for $K$ via $\sigma_{avg} = \sigma_f/(1+n)$ gives same result.)

**(iii) Extrusion force (ideal, no friction):**

Final cross-sectional area:
$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\,D_f^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\times (20)^{2} = 314.16\,\text{mm}^{2}
$$

Ignoring friction and redundant work:
$$
F = \sigma_f \cdot A_f = 80.5 \times 314.16 \approx 25{,}290\,\text{N} \approx 25.3\,\text{kN}
$$

(In real extrusion, friction adds $30-60\%$ to this value.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - Confusing **extrusion ratio** $(A_0/A_f)$ with **reduction in area** $((A_0-A_f)/A_0)$ — both look similar but the formula used in $\varepsilon_T = \ln(A_0/A_f)$ is the *ratio*, not the *fraction*.
> - In force calculation, **always use $\sigma_f$ (final stress), not $Y_{avg}$**, when computing $F = \sigma_f \cdot A_f$. Using $Y_{avg}$ would underestimate the force needed at the die exit.
> - Failing to convert mm² to m² when SI units of MPa and kN are required: $\text{MPa} \times \text{mm}^{2} = \text{N}$ (numerically convenient), but explicitly state the units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — for last-hour KTU exam prep:**

- ✅ Hot working = $T > 0.5\,T_m$; Cold working = $T < 0.3\,T_m$; Recrystallization temperature $T_{rex} \approx 0.4\,T_m$.
- ✅ **Hollomon Equation:** $\sigma = K\varepsilon^n$ — applies to **cold working only**.
- ✅ **Average flow stress (cold):** $\sigma_{avg} = K\varepsilon^{n}/(1+n)$.
- ✅ **Average flow stress (hot):** $Y_{avg} \approx (Y_0 + Y_f)/2$ (no work hardening assumption).
- ✅ **True strain** = $\ln(A_0/A_f)$ = $\ln(h_0/h_f)$ = $\ln(L_f/L_0)$.
- ✅ **Percent cold work** is an *engineering* quantity based on area or thickness reduction.
- ✅ Hot working → dynamic recrystallization, no strain hardening, large reductions, scale, poor tolerance.
- ✅ Cold working → strain hardening, limited ductility, fine surface, tight tolerance, residual stresses.
- ✅ **Von Mises** is the more accurate yield criterion; **Tresca** is more conservative (smaller predicted safe load).
- ✅ **Adiabatic heating** during high-speed cold working can locally raise temperature, sometimes triggering *dynamic recovery* — this is why machining often shows warm chips.
- ✅ Real applications: hot-rolled I-sections (steel), cold-drawn copper wire, deep-drawn aluminum cans, hot-forged crankshafts, cold-headed bolts.
- ✅ KTU students should remember that **internal choice in Part B is mandatory** — practice both Question A and Question B styles.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressive Forming: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compressive Forming: Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Compressive Forming** is a category of bulk metal forming processes in which plastic deformation of the workpiece is achieved primarily by the application of **compressive (normal) stresses** between two or more tool surfaces. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCPET602, Module 1), compressive forming encompasses processes such as **forging, extrusion, rolling, deep drawing, coining, heading, and upsetting**, in which the dominant component of the stress tensor is negative (compressive), forcing the material to flow plastically into the desired geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Compressive forming is a class of metal forming operations in which plastic flow of the material is induced by the application of predominantly compressive forces, leading to a reduction in cross-sectional area or a change in shape without intentional removal of material."*

### 1.2 Mathematical Statement of Stress State

In compressive forming, the principal stresses acting on the workpiece are predominantly compressive (negative). The general triaxial stress state is given by:

$$\sigma_1 < 0, \quad \sigma_2 \le 0, \quad \sigma_3 < 0$$

At least **two of the three principal stresses** must be compressive for the process to be classified under compressive forming (as opposed to tensile forming).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Clay Press**
> Imagine taking a piece of modelling clay and squeezing it firmly between the palms of your hands. The clay does not crack or break — instead, it bulges outwards sideways, taking the shape of the cavity formed by your fingers. This is exactly what happens in **compressive forming**: the metal "wants" to flow in the direction of least resistance (perpendicular to the applied load), and because we confine it with shaped dies, it is forced to take the geometry of the die cavity. Just as the clay deforms without losing mass, the metal workpiece in compressive forming retains its volume (constant volume law) and merely redistributes its material.

### 1.4 Classification of Compressive Forming Processes

| Process | Tooling Type | Material Flow Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Forging** | Two opposing dies (hammer/anvil) | Lateral (perpendicular to force) |
| **Extrusion** | Die with orifice | Through die orifice |
| **Rolling** | Two counter-rotating rolls | Through roll gap |
| **Drawing** | Die with pulling force | Through die (with tension assist) |
| **Coining** | Closed matched dies | Flashless filling |
| **Heading** | Punch and die | Axial upsetting |

### 1.5 Why "Compressive" — The Physics Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compressive forming exploits the fact that most metals exhibit **higher ductility under compressive stress** than under tensile stress. This is because:
> - Hydrostatic compressive components **suppress void nucleation and growth**
> - Micro-cracks cannot open under negative pressure
> - Ductile fracture (cup-and-cone type) is avoided
> - Higher strains are achievable without failure
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Working Principle — Structured Logic Breakdown

The working principle of compressive forming can be broken down into **six fundamental stages** that occur during every compressive forming operation:

**Step 1 — Application of Compressive Load**
The punch, die, or roll applies a force $F$ on the workpiece surface. This generates a contact (normal) stress $\sigma_n$ on the interface:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{F}{A_c}$$

where $A_c$ is the instantaneous contact area.

**Step 2 — Generation of Internal Stress State**
The applied load propagates through the workpiece as a triaxial compressive stress field. The constraint from surrounding tool surfaces adds **hydrostatic pressure** to the deviatoric (shear) component of stress.

**Step 3 — Yielding Initiation (Yield Criterion Triggered)**
Once the equivalent von Mises stress reaches the material's yield strength $Y$ (or flow stress $\sigma_f$ in plastic region), plastic flow begins:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2} \ge Y$$

**Step 4 — Material Redistribution (Constant Volume Law)**
Plastic flow obeys **incompressibility**:

$$\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$$

This means volume is conserved; material displaced in one direction must reappear in another.

**Step 5 — Die Cavity Filling**
Under continued loading, the material flows into the die cavity, conforming to the tool geometry. In closed-die forging, complete filling produces a flash; in flashless processes (coining), precise load control is essential.

**Step 6 — Springback and Unloading**
After load removal, elastic recovery (springback) occurs. The amount of springback depends on the elastic modulus and the elastic strain present at the moment of unloading.

### 2.2 The Hydrostatic Pressure Component — The "Why" Behind Compressive Forming

The **mean stress** (hydrostatic component) is:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$$

In compressive forming, $\sigma_m$ is **highly negative**. This negative mean stress has a critical engineering consequence: it **inhibits fracture** by keeping micro-voids closed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The larger the negative magnitude of $\sigma_m$, the **higher the ductility** achievable in forming. This is why processes like hydrostatic extrusion (with $\sigma_m$ approaching $-1000\,\text{MPa}$) can deform even brittle materials like tungsten.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{eq} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress | MPa | Yield criterion check |
| 2 | $\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3}{3}$ | Hydrostatic / mean stress | MPa | Ductility prediction |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_3 = 0$ | Incompressibility (constant volume) | dimensionless | Strain analysis |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_3$ | Volumetric strain | dimensionless | Volume conservation check |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$ | Hollomon flow curve | MPa | Stress-strain relation |
| 6 | $\sigma_f = K \cdot (\varepsilon + \varepsilon_0)^n \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^m$ | Strain-rate sensitive flow stress | MPa | Dynamic forming |
| 7 | $\frac{p}{\sigma_f} = 1 + \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{\mu L}{h}$ (with friction factor $m$) | Friction hill equation (for upsetting) | dimensionless | Average forging pressure |
| 8 | $p_{avg} = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu d}{3h}\right)$ | Average pressure with Coulomb friction | MPa | Friction affected forging |
| 9 | $F = p_{avg} \cdot A_c$ | Total forging/extrusion force | N | Load calculation |
| 10 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{v}{h}$ (forging) or $\frac{v \tan \alpha}{r}$ (rolling) | Strain rate | s$^{-1}$ | Process speed analysis |
| 11 | $\sigma_f = \sigma_0 \left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_0}\right)^m$ | Power-law strain rate sensitivity | MPa | High-speed forming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on LaTeX in Tables:** Notice that all absolute value / modulus expressions use $\vert$ instead of the vertical pipe character $\vert$ inside the markdown table — this prevents the markdown parser from breaking the table structure.

### 2.4 The Friction Hill — Why Load is Not Uniform

In upsetting/compression with friction at the die-workpiece interface, the pressure is **not uniform**. Maximum pressure occurs at the centre and falls off towards the periphery. This pressure distribution is called the **friction hill**:

$$p(x) = \sigma_f \cdot e^{\frac{2\mu}{h}(L/2 - x)}$$

where:
- $p(x)$ = pressure at distance $x$ from the centre
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction
- $h$ = instantaneous height of workpiece
- $L$ = instantaneous width/diameter

The **friction hill** phenomenon is critical in forging die design because it dictates:
- The maximum tonnage required on the press
- The likelihood of die cavity corner filling
- The onset of barrelling defects
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Pressure in Upsetting (Friction Hill Average)

Consider a cylindrical workpiece of instantaneous height $h$ and radius $R$ being upset between two flat dies with Coulomb friction coefficient $\mu$. The workpiece is in a state of plastic flow, so by von Mises yield criterion and slab method analysis:

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium on an elemental disc**

Take an elemental ring at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$ and height $h$. The forces acting on it are:
- Pressure $p$ pushing from left, $p + dp$ pushing from right (with $dp < 0$ as $r$ increases)
- Friction stress $\tau = \mu p$ acting on top and bottom surfaces (opposing outward flow)

$$p \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr - (p + dp) \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr - 2 \cdot \mu p \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr = 0$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the equilibrium**

$$-dp \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr - 2\mu p \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr = 0$$

Divide by $2\pi r \cdot dr$:

$$-dp = 2\mu p \cdot \frac{dr}{h}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables**

$$\frac{dp}{p} = -\frac{2\mu}{h} dr$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $r = 0$ (centre) to $r = R$ (free surface)**

$$\int_{p_{max}}^{0} \frac{dp}{p} = -\frac{2\mu}{h} \int_0^R dr$$

$$\ln \frac{0}{p_{max}} = -\frac{2\mu R}{h}$$

But since pressure at $r = R$ (free edge) equals the flow stress in the absence of constraint (deforms freely), the boundary condition is $p(R) = \sigma_f$:

$$\ln \frac{\sigma_f}{p_{max}} = -\frac{2\mu R}{h}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for maximum pressure**

$$\frac{\sigma_f}{p_{max}} = e^{-\frac{2\mu R}{h}}$$

$$\boxed{p_{max} = \sigma_f \cdot e^{\frac{2\mu R}{h}}}$$

**Step 6 — Average pressure across the disc**

$$p_{avg} = \frac{\int_0^R p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr}{\pi R^2}$$

Substituting $p(r) = p_{max} \cdot e^{-\frac{2\mu r}{h}}$ and integrating:

$$\boxed{p_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_f}{\frac{2\mu R}{h}} \left(e^{\frac{2\mu R}{h}} - 1\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As friction $\mu \to 0$, the expression reduces to $p_{avg} = \sigma_f$ (homogeneous deformation). As $\mu R/h$ becomes large (high friction, squat workpiece), $p_{avg}$ grows exponentially — this is the **friction hill**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Constant Volume Law in Upsetting

Consider a cylinder of initial height $h_0$, radius $R_0$, being upset to final height $h_f$, final radius $R_f$.

**Step 1 — Initial volume**

$$V_0 = \pi R_0^2 h_0$$

**Step 2 — Final volume**

$$V_f = \pi R_f^2 h_f$$

**Step 3 — Equate (plastic deformation conserves volume)**

$$\pi R_0^2 h_0 = \pi R_f^2 h_f$$

**Step 4 — True strains**

$$\varepsilon_h = \ln \frac{h_f}{h_0} \quad \text{(axial strain, negative for upsetting)}$$

$$\varepsilon_r = \ln \frac{R_f}{R_0} \quad \text{(radial strain, positive)}$$

**Step 5 — Show incompressibility**

$$\varepsilon_h + 2\varepsilon_r = \ln \frac{h_f}{h_0} + 2\ln \frac{R_f}{R_0} = \ln \left(\frac{h_f}{h_0} \cdot \frac{R_f^2}{R_0^2}\right) = \ln \frac{V_f}{V_0} = 0$$

$$\therefore \quad \varepsilon_h + 2\varepsilon_r = 0$$

This is the **fundamental constant volume equation** for axisymmetric upsetting.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Strain Rate in Upsetting

The strain rate during upsetting under a closing velocity $v$ of the upper die:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{d\varepsilon_h}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\ln \frac{h}{h_0}\right) = \frac{1}{h}\frac{dh}{dt}$$

Since $h$ decreases at rate $v$:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = -\frac{v}{h}$$

The negative sign indicates compressive strain rate. The **magnitude** is:

$$\boxed{\vert\dot{\varepsilon}\vert = \frac{v}{h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $h \to 0$ (workpiece becomes very thin), strain rate becomes very large. This is why final stages of forging are susceptible to **adiabatic heating** and possible **cracking**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Working Principle Across Different Processes

| Process | Stress State (Typical) | Dominant Mechanism | Typical Strain Rate (s$^{-1}$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open-die forging** | $\sigma_z < 0,\ \sigma_r = \sigma_\theta = 0$ (uniaxial) | Lateral spreading | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{1}$ |
| **Closed-die forging** | Triaxial compression $\sigma_r, \sigma_\theta, \sigma_z < 0$ | Cavity filling | $10^{0}$ to $10^{2}$ |
| **Direct extrusion** | $\sigma_z < 0,\ \sigma_r = \sigma_\theta < 0$ | Squeezing through die | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{2}$ |
| **Rolling** | $\sigma_y < 0,\ \sigma_x < 0$ (biaxial) | Compressive bite | $10^{0}$ to $10^{3}$ |
| **Drawing** | $\sigma_z < 0$ (mostly), $\sigma_r < 0$ | Drawing through die | $10^{1}$ to $10^{3}$ |
| **Coining** | Near-hydrostatic | Flashless filling | $10^{2}$ to $10^{3}$ |
| **Heading** | Uniaxial then triaxial | Material accumulation | $10^{0}$ to $10^{2}$ |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A cylindrical steel billet of diameter $D_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$ and height $h_0 = 100\,\text{mm}$ is upset in a forging operation to a final height $h_f = 60\,\text{mm}$. The flow stress of the material is $\sigma_f = 400\,\text{MPa}$ and the coefficient of friction at the die-workpiece interface is $\mu = 0.2$. Calculate:
> (a) The final diameter after forging (assuming no barrelling)
> (b) The maximum forging pressure
> (c) The average forging pressure

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Final diameter using constant volume:**

$$D_f = D_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 50 \sqrt{\frac{100}{60}}$$

$$\boxed{D_f = 50 \times 1.291 = 64.55\,\text{mm}}$$

**Part (b) — Maximum pressure using friction hill equation:**

The instantaneous aspect ratio at the start is $R/h = 25/100 = 0.25$:

$$p_{max} = \sigma_f \cdot e^{\frac{2\mu R}{h}} = 400 \cdot e^{2 \times 0.2 \times 0.25}$$

$$p_{max} = 400 \cdot e^{0.1} = 400 \times 1.1052$$

$$\boxed{p_{max} \approx 442.1\,\text{MPa}}$$

**Part (c) — Average pressure:**

$$p_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_f}{\frac{2\mu R}{h}} \left(e^{\frac{2\mu R}{h}} - 1\right) = \frac{400}{0.1}(1.1052 - 1)$$

$$p_{avg} = 4000 \times 0.1052$$

$$\boxed{p_{avg} \approx 420.8\,\text{MPa}}$$

This example demonstrates the standard KTU-style numerical on compressive forming with friction hill analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Working Principle Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Workpiece placed between tooling surfaces"] --> B["Compressive Load F Applied"]
    B --> C["Contact Stress sigma_n Generated"]
    C --> D["Triaxial Compressive Stress Field Propagates"]
    D --> E{"Von Mises Yield Criterion Satisfied?"}
    E -- "No, sigma_eq < Y" --> F["Elastic Deformation Only"]
    F --> B
    E -- "Yes, sigma_eq >= Y" --> G["Plastic Flow Initiates"]
    G --> H["Material Flows Perpendicular to Load Direction"]
    H --> I["Die Cavity Begins Filling"]
    I --> J{"Cavity Completely Filled?"}
    J -- "No" --> K["Continue Load Application"]
    K --> I
    J -- "Yes" --> L["Load Held for Material Consolidation"]
    L --> M["Tool Retraction"]
    M --> N["Elastic Springback Occurs"]
    N --> O["Final Forged Component"]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style O fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
```

### 4.2 Stress State Classification in Compressive Forming

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Compressive Forming Processes"] --> B["Uniaxial Compression"]
    A --> C["Biaxial Compression"]
    A --> D["Triaxial Compression"]
    A --> E["Near-Hydrostatic Compression"]
    B --> B1["Open-die Forging"]
    B --> B2["Heading / Upsetting"]
    C --> C1["Rolling"]
    C --> C2["Drawing"]
    D --> D1["Closed-die Forging"]
    D --> D2["Direct Extrusion"]
    E --> E1["Hydrostatic Extrusion"]
    E --> E2["Coining"]
    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Compressive Forming Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        I1["Raw Billet / Slab / Bloom"]
        I2["Material Properties: Y, n, mu"]
        I3["Temperature Profile"]
    end
    subgraph TOOLING["TOOLING SYSTEM"]
        T1["Upper Die / Punch"]
        T2["Lower Die / Anvil"]
        T3["Lubrication System"]
    end
    subgraph DEFORMATION["DEFORMATION ZONE"]
        D1["Primary Shear Zone"]
        D2["Friction-affected Boundary Layer"]
        D3["Plastic Flow Lines"]
        D4["Dead Metal Zones"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        O1["Formed Component Geometry"]
        O2["Residual Stresses"]
        O3["Springback Effect"]
        O4["Surface Finish Quality"]
    end

    I2 --> D1
    I3 --> D1

    T2 --> D1
    T3 --> D2

    D2 --> O4

    style O1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```
