<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024]**
**Define compressive forming. List any four processes that fall under this category.**

**Model Answer:**

Compressive forming is a group of bulk metal forming processes in which plastic deformation of the workpiece is accomplished predominantly by the application of **compressive stresses** between tool surfaces, leading to a change in shape without removal of material.

Four processes under compressive forming are:
1. Forging
2. Extrusion
3. Rolling
4. Drawing (Coining is also acceptable)

[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing with brief identification]

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the constant volume law as applicable to plastic deformation in compressive forming.**

**Model Answer:**

The constant volume law (incompressibility condition) states that during plastic deformation, the **volume of the workpiece remains constant**. Mathematically:

$$\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$$

For axisymmetric upsetting of a cylinder:

$$\varepsilon_h + 2\varepsilon_r = 0$$

This arises from the fact that plastic deformation occurs without change in density, so material displaced in the axial direction must appear as radial expansion.

[3 Marks: Statement + equation + brief interpretation]

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — INTERNAL CHOICE (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of compressive forming with a neat sketch. Discuss the triaxial stress state that develops during the process and explain why compressive forming enables higher ductility than tensile forming.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical aluminium billet of initial diameter $50\,\text{mm}$ and height $75\,\text{mm}$ is upset in a forging press to a final height of $40\,\text{mm}$. The flow stress is $\sigma_f = 250\,\text{MPa}$ and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.15$. Calculate:
(i) Final diameter of the billet
(ii) Maximum forging pressure
(iii) Average forging pressure

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Working Principle of Compressive Forming:**

**Working Principle:** Compressive forming operates on the principle that when a metal workpiece is subjected to **predominantly compressive forces** between tool surfaces (dies, punches, or rolls), it undergoes plastic deformation and flows in the direction of least resistance to fill the die cavity or pass through the deformation zone, without any removal of material.

**Triaxial Stress State:**
During compressive forming, the workpiece experiences a state of stress where **two or all three principal stresses are compressive**:

$$\sigma_1 < 0,\ \sigma_2 \le 0,\ \sigma_3 < 0$$

The hydrostatic component $\sigma_m = (\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)/3$ is **negative (compressive)**, while the deviatoric component drives plastic flow.

**Why Higher Ductility than Tensile Forming:**
- Compressive hydrostatic pressure **closes micro-voids** and inhibits crack nucleation
- Tensile hydrostatic stress (positive mean stress) **opens voids** and promotes fracture
- The negative mean stress in compressive forming suppresses ductile fracture mechanisms
- Hence, materials like tungsten and magnesium (brittle in tension) can be deformed by compressive forming

[7 Marks: 3 for principle explanation, 2 for stress state, 2 for ductility comparison]

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $D_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$, $h_0 = 75\,\text{mm}$, $h_f = 40\,\text{mm}$, $\sigma_f = 250\,\text{MPa}$, $\mu = 0.15$

Initial radius $R_0 = 25\,\text{mm}$

**(i) Final diameter using constant volume law:**

$$V_0 = V_f$$
$$\pi R_0^2 h_0 = \pi R_f^2 h_f$$
$$R_f = R_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 25 \sqrt{\frac{75}{40}}$$
$$R_f = 25 \times 1.3693 = 34.23\,\text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{D_f = 2 \times 34.23 = 68.47\,\text{mm}}$$

**(ii) Maximum forging pressure:**

Initial aspect ratio $\frac{2\mu R_0}{h_0} = \frac{2 \times 0.15 \times 25}{75} = 0.1$

$$p_{max} = \sigma_f \cdot e^{\frac{2\mu R_0}{h_0}} = 250 \times e^{0.1}$$
$$p_{max} = 250 \times 1.1052$$
$$\boxed{p_{max} = 276.3\,\text{MPa}}$$

**(iii) Average forging pressure:**

$$p_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_f}{\frac{2\mu R_0}{h_0}}\left(e^{\frac{2\mu R_0}{h_0}} - 1\right)$$
$$p_{avg} = \frac{250}{0.1}(1.1052 - 1)$$
$$p_{avg} = 2500 \times 0.1052$$
$$\boxed{p_{avg} = 263.0\,\text{MPa}}$$

[7 Marks: 2 marks each sub-part + 1 for final answer statement]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the friction hill phenomenon in compressive forming. Derive the expression for maximum forging pressure in upsetting considering Coulomb friction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel cylindrical workpiece of diameter $80\,\text{mm}$ and height $100\,\text{mm}$ is to be upset to a height of $50\,\text{mm}$ in a press. If the flow stress is $350\,\text{MPa}$ and friction coefficient is $0.25$:
(i) Estimate the maximum forging pressure
(ii) Calculate the average forging pressure
(iii) Determine the forging force required

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Friction Hill Phenomenon:**

The friction hill refers to the **non-uniform pressure distribution** that develops during upsetting/compression when friction exists at the die-workpiece interface. The pressure is maximum at the centre of the workpiece and decreases towards the periphery, producing a "hill-shaped" pressure profile.

**Cause:** Material at the centre is far from the free surface and hence cannot flow easily — high constraint. Material at the periphery can flow outward freely — low constraint. Friction at the top and bottom surfaces further retards the outer material's flow.

**Derivation of Maximum Pressure:**

Consider a cylindrical workpiece being upset. Take an elemental ring at radius $r$, thickness $dr$, height $h$. Force equilibrium gives:

$$\frac{dp}{p} = -\frac{2\mu}{h} dr$$

Integrating from $r = 0$ (where $p = p_{max}$) to $r = R$ (where $p = \sigma_f$, the flow stress):

$$\ln \frac{\sigma_f}{p_{max}} = -\frac{2\mu R}{h}$$

$$\boxed{p_{max} = \sigma_f \cdot e^{\frac{2\mu R}{h}}}$$

**Significance:** Higher $\mu$ or higher $R/h$ ratio → higher $p_{max}$ → greater press tonnage required.

[7 Marks: 2 for phenomenon explanation + diagram, 5 for derivation]

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $D_0 = 80\,\text{mm}$ so $R_0 = 40\,\text{mm}$, $h_0 = 100\,\text{mm}$, $\sigma_f = 350\,\text{MPa}$, $\mu = 0.25$

**(i) Maximum forging pressure:**

$$\frac{2\mu R_0}{h_0} = \frac{2 \times 0.25 \times 40}{100} = 0.20$$

$$p_{max} = 350 \times e^{0.20} = 350 \times 1.2214$$

$$\boxed{p_{max} = 427.5\,\text{MPa}}$$

**(ii) Average forging pressure:**

$$p_{avg} = \frac{350}{0.20}(1.2214 - 1) = 1750 \times 0.2214$$

$$\boxed{p_{avg} = 387.5\,\text{MPa}}$$

**(iii) Forging force required:**

Contact area $A_c = \pi R_0^2 = \pi \times 40^2 = 5026.55\,\text{mm}^2$

$$F = p_{avg} \times A_c = 387.5 \times 5026.55$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 1.948 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N} \approx 1948\,\text{kN}}$$

[7 Marks: 2 marks each sub-part + 1 for final answer with units]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing units in final answer:** Always specify units (MPa, N, mm). Examiner deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert diameter to radius:** Many students write $D_0$ instead of $R_0 = D_0/2$ in the friction hill formula — this gives incorrect exponent and wrong answer.
> 3. **Confusing average and maximum pressure:** Friction hill gives $p_{max}$ at the centre; $p_{avg}$ is the integral average. Using $p_{max}$ to compute forging force overestimates by 5–15%.
> 4. **Sign convention errors:** Compressive stresses are negative; in board exams, always state "compressive" explicitly or use negative sign with proper convention.
> 5. **Skipping the constant volume check:** Many numericals are designed to verify $D_f > D_0$ and $h_f < h_0$. Mentioning this confirms understanding.
> 6. **Forgetting the e (exponential) in friction hill:** Some students use the linear approximation $p_{max} \approx \sigma_f(1 + 2\mu R/h)$, which is only valid for small exponents.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compressive Forming Working Principle**

**Core Conceptual Points:**
- ☐ Compressive forming involves predominantly **compressive principal stresses**
- ☐ At least **two of the three principal stresses** are negative
- ☐ Volume is conserved: $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$
- ☐ Material flows **perpendicular to load direction** (least resistance)
- ☐ Negative hydrostatic stress $\sigma_m$ **inhibits fracture** → higher ductility

**Key Equations to Memorize:**
- ☐ Von Mises yield: $\sigma_{eq} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$
- ☐ Friction hill: $p_{max} = \sigma_f \cdot e^{2\mu R/h}$
- ☐ Average pressure: $p_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_f}{2\mu R/h}(e^{2\mu R/h}-1)$
- ☐ Hollomon flow curve: $\sigma_f = K\varepsilon^n$
- ☐ Constant volume cylinder: $D_f = D_0\sqrt{h_0/h_f}$
- ☐ Strain rate in upsetting: $\dot{\varepsilon} = v/h$

**Process Categories to Recall:**
- ☐ **Uniaxial compression:** Open-die forging, heading
- ☐ **Biaxial compression:** Rolling, drawing
- ☐ **Triaxial compression:** Closed-die forging, extrusion
- ☐ **Near-hydrostatic:** Hydrostatic extrusion, coining

**Engineering Design Implications:**
- ☐ High friction + squat workpiece → **barrelling defect**
- ☐ Flash in closed-die forging acts as **pressure intensifier**
- ☐ Die corner radius affects **filling efficiency**
- ☐ Lubrication reduces $\mu$ → reduces press tonnage

**Frequently Asked KTU Concepts:**
- ☐ Why compressive forming over tensile? — Ductility enhancement via negative $\sigma_m$
- ☐ Why is forging load higher than simple $\sigma_f \times A$? — Friction hill
- ☐ What is the role of flash? — Pressure build-up for cavity filling
- ☐ Why hot forging? — Lower $\sigma_f$, higher ductility, lower press tonnage

**Numerical Problem Templates:**
- ☐ Type 1: Final dimension calculation using constant volume
- ☐ Type 2: Friction hill pressure calculation
- ☐ Type 3: Forging force from average pressure and area
- ☐ Type 4: Strain rate and process time estimation
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characteristic Features of Metal Forming Processes**
> Metal forming is a manufacturing process group in which the desired shape and dimensions of a workpiece are obtained through **plastic deformation** of the material, without adding or removing any material (zero material loss). The characteristic features refer to the distinct metallurgical, mechanical, thermal, and tooling-related properties that collectively define, classify, and govern the behaviour of all metal forming operations (forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing, sheet metal forming, etc.).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCPET602**, the characteristic features establish the *fundamental difference* between material shaping by plastic flow versus material shaping by material removal (machining) or by material addition (casting).

### Key Characteristic Features (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> The following features form the backbone of Module 1 and are **frequently asked** in KTU ESE and continuous evaluation tests.

1. **Plastic Deformation Without Material Loss** – Volume is conserved before and after forming.
2. **Mass/Volume Constancy** – Material is redistributed, not lost.
3. **Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)** – Yield strength rises with plastic strain (especially in cold working).
4. **Temperature Classification** – Cold, Warm, and Hot working regimes.
5. **Grain Flow and Directionality** – Anisotropic mechanical properties.
6. **Residual Stresses** – Locked-in stresses after deformation.
7. **Friction at Tool-Work Interface** – Influences load and surface quality.
8. **High Energy / Load Requirements** – Massive presses, hammers, or rolling mills.
9. **Improved Mechanical Properties** – Strength, toughness, fatigue life improve in proper direction.
10. **Tooling Specificity** – Dedicated dies, rolls, punches per geometry.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Clay Modelling vs. Wood Carving vs. Pouring Ice into Moulds**
> - **Metal Forming** is like a potter shaping **wet clay** on a wheel – the clay is pushed, rolled, or pressed into a new shape, but **no clay is added or scraped off**. The total clay remains the same.
> - **Metal Cutting (Machining)** is like a sculptor **carving wood** – chips fly off; material is removed.
> - **Casting** is like **pouring melted chocolate** into a mould – material is added in liquid form and solidifies.
>
> Metal forming sits in the first category. The clay (metal) gets "kneaded" plastically. The harder you knead it cold, the stiffer it becomes (strain hardening). If you warm the clay, it flows easily with less force (hot working). This single analogy explains 70% of the characteristic features.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Recrystallization Temperature** $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (where $T_m$ is the absolute melting temperature in Kelvin).
- **Strain** $\varepsilon$ – dimensionless, often expressed as **true strain** or **engineering strain**.
- **Flow Stress** $\sigma_f$ – measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **Strain Rate** $\dot{\varepsilon}$ – in **s⁻¹** (per second).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Characteristic Feature

#### Feature 1: Plastic Deformation Without Material Removal
- **Why?** Atoms in a metal are bonded by metallic bonds that allow layers of atoms to **slide over one another (dislocation glide)** without breaking the bond completely, provided the stress exceeds the yield strength.
- **How?** When the applied stress $\sigma > \sigma_y$ (yield stress), permanent rearrangement of the crystal lattice occurs. The piece retains its mass, but the shape changes.

#### Feature 2: Volume Constancy (Incompressibility)
- **Why?** Plastic deformation in metals occurs at constant density (no porosity generation in bulk forming).
- **How?** Expressed mathematically as $V_i = V_f$, leading to the well-known relationship:

$$A_1 L_1 = A_2 L_2$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area and $L$ is the length (in drawing, extrusion).

#### Feature 3: Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)
- **Why?** Dislocation density $\rho$ increases with deformation. Since flow stress is related to $\rho$ via the Taylor relation, the metal gets harder.
- **How?** Captured by the **Hollomon equation** $\sigma = K \varepsilon^n$ (see formula sheet).

#### Feature 4: Temperature Classification
- **Cold Working (CW):** $T < 0.3 \, T_m$ – strain hardening retained, good surface finish, higher forces.
- **Warm Working (WW):** $0.3 \, T_m \le T < 0.5 \, T_m$ – partial recovery, balanced properties.
- **Hot Working (HW):** $T > 0.5 \, T_m$ (above recrystallization temperature) – no strain hardening, low forces, poor surface, oxidation.

#### Feature 5: Grain Flow and Anisotropy
- **Why?** Grains elongate along the direction of plastic flow; impurities and second-phase particles align with the flow.
- **How?** The result is a **fibrous structure** that gives higher toughness along the fibre direction – a major advantage over cast structures.

#### Feature 6: Residual Stresses
- **Why?** Non-uniform plastic deformation across the cross-section leaves elastic strains "locked in" when the load is removed.
- **How?** Removed by stress-relief annealing; otherwise may cause distortion or fatigue cracks.

#### Feature 7: Friction at Tool-Work Interface
- **Why?** Real surfaces are rough; relative motion creates shear stresses.
- **How?** Quantified by **Coulomb's friction coefficient $\mu$** or **shear friction factor $m$**, which appear in forging/extrusion pressure equations.

#### Feature 8: High Energy / Load Requirements
- **Why?** Flow stress of metals is high (hundreds of MPa).
- **How?** Requires **presses (slow, steady load)** or **hammers (impact load)** rated in MN.

#### Feature 9: Improved Mechanical Properties
- **Why?** Grain refinement (in hot work) + strain hardening (in cold work) + aligned fibre structure.
- **How?** Fatigue strength, impact toughness, and strength increase in the direction of grain flow.

#### Feature 10: Tooling Specificity
- **Why?** Each forming operation needs dedicated dies/rolls.
- **How?** High **tool and die costs**, but **low per-part cost** at high volumes.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Volume constancy | $V_i = V_f$ | Initial = Final volume | m³ |
| 2 | Incompressibility (drawing) | $A_1 L_1 = A_2 L_2$ | Cross-section $\times$ Length | m²·m |
| 3 | True strain (axial) | $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{L_f}{L_i} \right)$ | $\ln$ ratio of lengths | dimensionless |
| 4 | True strain (area) | $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{A_i}{A_f} \right)$ | Area ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | Engineering strain | $e = \dfrac{L_f - L_i}{L_i}$ | Length change / initial | dimensionless |
| 6 | True stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_{instant}}$ | Force / instantaneous area | MPa |
| 7 | Engineering stress | $\sigma_{eng} = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Force / original area | MPa |
| 8 | Strain hardening (Hollomon) | $\sigma = K \varepsilon^n$ | $K$=strength coeff., $n$=hardening exponent | MPa, – |
| 9 | Average flow stress | $\sigma_f^{avg} = \dfrac{K \, \varepsilon^n}{1+n}$ | Used in forging load | MPa |
| 10 | Recrystallization temp. | $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | Melting temp in K | K |
| 11 | Hot working condition | $T_{hot} > 0.5 \, T_m$ | Above recrystallization | K |
| 12 | Strain rate | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{v}{h}$ | $v$=ram speed, $h$=height | s⁻¹ |
| 13 | Forging force (ideal) | $F = \sigma_f \cdot A$ | Flow stress $\times$ projected area | N |
| 14 | Friction shear stress | $\tau = m \cdot \sigma_f$ | $m$=shear friction factor | MPa |
| 15 | Von Mises yield criterion | $\sigma_{VM} = \sigma_y$ | Distortion energy theory | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Utility:** These formulas are the foundation for calculating press tonnage, draw forces, rolling loads, and extrusion pressures in actual industry design – e.g., choosing a 500-ton vs. a 1000-ton hydraulic press in a forging shop.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive crankshafts** are forged so that grain flow follows the contour → superior fatigue life.
- **Aircraft turbine blades** are hot-worked to refine grains for creep resistance.
- **Cold-rolled steel sheets** for car bodies use strain hardening to achieve high strength with thin gauges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Volume Constancy and Strain Relationship in Drawing

**Problem Context:** A cylindrical billet of initial length $L_i$ and initial cross-sectional area $A_i$ is drawn through a die to final length $L_f$ and final area $A_f$. Show that the true strain can be expressed in terms of area ratio as well as length ratio.

**Step 1:** Write the principle of volume constancy.

$$V_i = V_f$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $V = A \cdot L$ for a uniform cylinder.

$$A_i \, L_i = A_f \, L_f$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides after rearranging the ratio.

$$\ln \left( \frac{L_f}{L_i} \right) = \ln \left( \frac{A_i}{A_f} \right)$$

This is because $\dfrac{A_i}{A_f} = \dfrac{L_f}{L_i}$ from the equation above.

**Step 4:** By definition of true strain,

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{L_f}{L_i} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Equate the two forms.

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{L_f}{L_i} \right) = \ln \left( \frac{A_i}{A_f} \right)$$

**Conclusion:** True strain is **equally expressible** in terms of length ratio or area ratio. This is the single most important identity in bulk metal forming analysis.

---

### Derivation 2: Average Flow Stress from Hollomon Equation

**Context:** A specimen is compressed in forging from initial height $h_i$ to $h_f$. Determine the average flow stress.

**Step 1:** Instantaneous flow stress is given by Hollomon's law.

$$\sigma_f(\varepsilon) = K \, \varepsilon^n$$

**Step 2:** Maximum true strain at the end of compression is given by volume constancy (constant area $A$ assumed for uniform upsetting).

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \ln \left( \frac{h_i}{h_f} \right)$$

**Step 3:** The *average* flow stress over the strain range is the integral of flow stress divided by total strain.

$$\sigma_f^{avg} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_{max}} \int_0^{\varepsilon_{max}} K \, \varepsilon^n \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral.

$$\int_0^{\varepsilon_{max}} K \, \varepsilon^n \, d\varepsilon = K \left[ \frac{\varepsilon^{n+1}}{n+1} \right]_0^{\varepsilon_{max}} = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_{max}^{n+1}}{n+1}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back.

$$\sigma_f^{avg} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_{max}} \cdot \frac{K \, \varepsilon_{max}^{n+1}}{n+1} = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_{max}^{n}}{n+1}$$

**Step 6:** Recognising that $K \, \varepsilon_{max}^{n} = \sigma_f^{max}$ (the final flow stress),

$$\boxed{\sigma_f^{avg} = \frac{\sigma_f^{max}}{1+n}}$$

**Conclusion:** This is the key closed-form result used in **forging load estimation** in KTU numerical problems.

---

### Derivation 3: Strain Rate in Upsetting

**Context:** A cylindrical specimen of instantaneous height $h$ is compressed at ram velocity $v$. Derive the strain rate expression.

**Step 1:** True strain definition.

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{h_i}{h} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$.

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \left[ \ln(h_i) - \ln(h) \right] = -\frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** The ram speed $v = -\dfrac{dh}{dt}$ (negative because height decreases).

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{v}{h}$$

**Conclusion:** Strain rate is **inversely proportional to height** and **directly proportional to ram speed**. This is the standard form used in KTU problems on hot working.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cold, Warm, and Hot Working

| Characteristic | Cold Working | Warm Working | Hot Working |
|----------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| Temperature | $T < 0.3\,T_m$ | $0.3\,T_m \le T < 0.5\,T_m$ | $T > 0.5\,T_m$ |
| Strain hardening | Yes – significant | Partial | None (recovery + recrystallization) |
| Force required | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| Surface finish | Excellent (mirror-like possible) | Good | Poor (oxidation, scale) |
| Dimensional accuracy | High | Moderate | Low |
| Grain structure | Elongated, fibrous | Partially refined | Refined, equiaxed |
| Mechanical properties | High strength, low ductility | Balanced | Tough, ductile |
| Typical operations | Rolling of sheet, drawing, cold forging, stamping | Warm forging, warm extrusion | Hot rolling, hot forging, hot extrusion |
| Tooling cost | High (hardened tool steel, carbides) | Moderate | Lower (but more wear from heat) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Mermaid Diagram 1: Classification of Metal Forming by Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["METAL FORMING PROCESSES"]
    nodeB["COLD WORKING T less than 0.3 Tm"]
    nodeC["WARM WORKING T between 0.3 Tm and 0.5 Tm"]
    nodeD["HOT WORKING T greater than 0.5 Tm"]

    nodeE["Rolling of sheets"]
    nodeF["Wire drawing"]
    nodeG["Cold forging"]
    nodeH["Stamping and deep drawing"]

    nodeI["Warm forging"]
    nodeJ["Warm extrusion"]

    nodeK["Hot rolling"]
    nodeL["Hot forging"]
    nodeM["Hot extrusion"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeD --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeM
```

### Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Forming Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Raw Material Billet or Sheet"] --> inputB["Heating Furnace optional for hot work"]
    inputB --> inputC["Forming Zone Die or Rolls or Punch"]
    inputC --> inputD["Lubrication System reduces friction"]
    inputD --> inputE["Workpiece with plastic deformation"]
    inputE --> inputF["Cooling Stage"]
    inputF --> inputG["Secondary Operations trimming and heat treatment"]
    inputG --> inputH["Finished Formed Component"]
    inputH --> feedbackA["Quality Checks dimensional and mechanical"]
    feedbackA --> inputB
```

### Mermaid Diagram 3: Cause-and-Effect Map of Characteristic Features

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cause1["Applied Stress greater than Yield Stress"] --> effect1["Plastic Deformation"]
    cause2["Plastic Deformation"] --> effect2["Volume Constancy"]
    cause2 --> effect3["Strain Hardening"]
    cause2 --> effect4["Grain Flow and Anisotropy"]
    cause2 --> effect5["Residual Stresses"]
    cause6["Tool Work Contact"] --> effect6["Friction Losses"]
    cause6 --> effect7["Heat Generation"]
    effect3 --> effect8["Increased Flow Stress"]
    effect8 --> effect9["Higher Press Tonnage Required"]
    cause10["Temperature above Recrystallization"] --> effect10["Dynamic Recovery and Recrystallization"]
    effect10 --> effect11["No Strain Hardening in Hot Work"]
    effect10 --> effect12["Refined Equiaxed Grains"]
```

### Mermaid Diagram 4: Engineering Utility Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cf1["Characteristic Feature"] --> cf2["Engineering Outcome"]
    cf1A["Grain Flow"] --> cf2A["High Fatigue Strength Directional"]
    cf1B["Strain Hardening"] --> cf2B["High Yield Strength in Sheet Metal"]
    cf1C["Hot Working Recrystallization"] --> cf2C["Tough Ductile Forged Crankshafts"]
    cf1D["Volume Constancy"] --> cf2D["Predictable Final Dimensions"]
    cf1E["Friction Control with Lubrication"] --> cf2E["Lower Press Loads and Better Surface"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Manufacturing Process Families"]
    root --> mf1["Metal Forming plastic deformation"]
    root --> mf2["Metal Cutting material removal"]
    root --> mf3["Casting material addition and solidification"]

    mf1 --> mf1B["Improved mechanical properties"]
    mf1 --> mf1C["High initial tooling cost"]
    mf2 --> mf2A["Material lost as chips"]

    mf3 --> mf3B["Possible porosity defects"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define metal forming. List any four characteristic features of metal forming processes.**

**Model Answer:**
Metal forming is a manufacturing process in which the desired shape of a workpiece is obtained through plastic deformation of the material without addition or removal of material.

Four characteristic features:
1. Plastic deformation occurs above the yield stress.
2. Volume of the material remains constant (no material loss).
3. Strain hardening occurs in cold working.
4. Mechanical properties such as strength and toughness improve along the grain flow direction.

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):** Definition 1 mark, each feature 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks. Total = 3.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between hot working and cold working. State the recrystallization temperature criterion for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Temperature | Above $0.5 \, T_m$ | Below $0.3 \, T_m$ |
| Strain hardening | Absent (recrystallization occurs) | Present |
| Forces | Low | High |
| Surface finish | Poor (oxide scale) | Excellent |
| Dimensional accuracy | Low | High |

Recrystallization temperature $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$. Hot working is performed above this; cold working is performed well below it.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions of 14 marks typically split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) Explain in detail the characteristic features of metal forming processes. How do these features distinguish forming from machining and casting? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The characteristic features of metal forming are:

1. **Plastic Deformation Without Material Loss:** In forming, the shape change is achieved by plastic flow of material. Unlike machining, where chips are removed, no material is lost. Volume of the workpiece before and after the operation is the same.

2. **Volume Constancy:** The density of the metal remains essentially unchanged during plastic deformation. Mathematically, $V_i = V_f$. This principle is used to predict dimensions.

3. **Strain Hardening (in cold working):** As plastic strain accumulates, the flow stress of the material rises according to $\sigma = K \varepsilon^n$. Machined parts do not exhibit this in the bulk because material is removed.

4. **Grain Flow:** During deformation, grains elongate in the direction of metal flow, creating a fibrous microstructure. This gives directional mechanical properties superior to cast parts.

5. **Residual Stresses:** Non-uniform plastic deformation leaves elastic stresses locked in the part after the load is removed. These are minimized by stress-relief heat treatment.

6. **Friction at Tool-Work Interface:** Friction is unavoidable in forming. It raises the required forming load and limits surface quality. Lubrication is essential.

7. **Improved Mechanical Properties:** Formed parts have higher strength, fatigue resistance, and toughness than cast parts of the same composition.

**Distinction:**

- *Forming vs Machining:* Forming is plastic reshaping with zero material loss and improved properties; machining is material removal that produces chips and may leave a disturbed surface layer.
- *Forming vs Casting:* Forming produces a wrought structure with grain flow; casting produces a solidified structure with possible porosity and lower strength.

**Marks split:** Each feature 0.5 mark (7 × 0.5 = 3.5), comparison 2 marks, conclusion 1.5 marks = 7 marks.

---

**(b) A cylindrical billet of length 200 mm and diameter 50 mm is compressed in cold forging to a length of 120 mm. Assuming the material follows $\sigma = 600 \varepsilon^{0.2}$ MPa, determine: (i) true strain, (ii) final diameter, (iii) average flow stress, and (iv) the forging force at the end of stroke. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L_i = 200$ mm, $d_i = 50$ mm, $L_f = 120$ mm
- $\sigma = 600 \, \varepsilon^{0.2}$ MPa, so $K = 600$ MPa, $n = 0.2$

**Solution:**

**(i) True strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{L_i}{L_f} \right) = \ln \left( \frac{200}{120} \right) = \ln(1.6667)$$

$$\varepsilon = 0.5108$$

**(ii) Final diameter** (using volume constancy $A_i L_i = A_f L_f$):

$$\frac{\pi}{4} d_i^2 \cdot L_i = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 \cdot L_f$$

$$d_f^2 = \frac{d_i^2 \cdot L_i}{L_f} = \frac{50^2 \cdot 200}{120} = \frac{250000 \cdot 200}{120}$$

$$d_f^2 = 41666.67 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_f = 204.12 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Average flow stress:**

Final flow stress:

$$\sigma_f^{max} = K \, \varepsilon^n = 600 \times (0.5108)^{0.2}$$

Computing $(0.5108)^{0.2}$:

$$(0.5108)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln(0.5108)} = e^{0.2 \times (-0.6717)} = e^{-0.1343} = 0.8743$$

$$\sigma_f^{max} = 600 \times 0.8743 = 524.6 \text{ MPa}$$

Average flow stress:

$$\sigma_f^{avg} = \frac{\sigma_f^{max}}{1+n} = \frac{524.6}{1.2} = 437.2 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iv) Forging force at the end of stroke:**

Projected area at the end of stroke:

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 41666.67 = 32724.9 \text{ mm}^2$$

Ideal forging force (ignoring friction):

$$F = \sigma_f^{avg} \cdot A_f = 437.2 \times 32724.9$$

$$F = 14.31 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 14.31 \text{ MN} \approx 14310 \text{ kN}$$

**Marks split:** (i) 1 mark, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 2 marks = 7 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a) Classify metal forming processes with neat sketches and explain the characteristic features common to all of them. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Metal forming processes are classified as follows:

1. **Bulk Forming** (large deformation in 3D):
   - Forging (open and closed die)
   - Rolling
   - Extrusion (direct, indirect)
   - Drawing

2. **Sheet Metal Forming** (deformation primarily in plane):
   - Bending
   - Deep drawing
   - Stretch forming
   - Shearing and blanking

**Characteristic features common to all metal forming processes:**

1. Plastic deformation of a solid workpiece.
2. No material addition or removal (mass conservation).
3. The metal is in the solid state (unlike casting where it is liquid).
4. Use of dedicated tooling (dies, rolls, punches).
5. Application of compressive, tensile, or combined stresses.
6. Strain hardening in cold working; dynamic recrystallization in hot working.
7. Production of parts with superior mechanical properties (wrought structure).
8. Suitability for high-volume production due to low per-unit cost once tooling is made.

**Marks split:** Classification 2 marks, features 0.5 × 8 = 4 marks, conclusion 1 mark.

---

**(b) Explain the phenomenon of strain hardening. A specimen of mild steel has a Hollomon equation $\sigma = 700 \varepsilon^{0.25}$ MPa. If it is cold-worked from a true strain of 0 to 0.4, calculate the increase in flow stress and the average flow stress over this strain range. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Strain Hardening:** As a metal is plastically deformed at temperatures below recrystallization, the dislocation density increases. Dislocations interact and entangle, impeding further dislocation motion. This raises the yield strength and flow stress of the material with continued strain. The phenomenon is called strain hardening or work hardening. It is captured by the Hollomon equation $\sigma = K \varepsilon^n$, where $n$ is the strain-hardening exponent (0 to 1) and $K$ is the strength coefficient.

**Given:** $K = 700$ MPa, $n = 0.25$, strain range 0 to 0.4.

**Initial flow stress** at $\varepsilon = 0$ is theoretically 0 (from Hollomon's law, but in practice equals the yield stress $\sigma_y$). For this problem, we take the **flow stress values predicted by Hollomon** at the boundaries.

**Flow stress at $\varepsilon = 0.4$:**

$$\sigma_{0.4} = 700 \times (0.4)^{0.25}$$

Compute $(0.4)^{0.25}$:

$$(0.4)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln(0.4)} = e^{0.25 \times (-0.9163)} = e^{-0.2291} = 0.7953$$

$$\sigma_{0.4} = 700 \times 0.7953 = 556.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Increase in flow stress:**

$$\Delta\sigma = 556.7 - 0 = 556.7 \text{ MPa}$$

(Note: Strictly, the increase is from the yield stress; here we use Hollomon's predicted values.)

**Average flow stress** over the range 0 to 0.4:

$$\sigma_f^{avg} = \frac{1}{0.4} \int_0^{0.4} 700 \, \varepsilon^{0.25} \, d\varepsilon$$

$$= \frac{700}{0.4} \left[ \frac{\varepsilon^{1.25}}{1.25} \right]_0^{0.4}$$

$$= \frac{700}{0.4 \times 1.25} \times (0.4)^{1.25}$$

Compute $(0.4)^{1.25}$:

$$(0.4)^{1.25} = e^{1.25 \ln(0.4)} = e^{1.25 \times (-0.9163)} = e^{-1.1454} = 0.3183$$

$$\sigma_f^{avg} = \frac{700}{0.5} \times 0.3183 = 1400 \times 0.3183 = 445.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Marks split:** Theory 2 marks, increase in flow stress 2 marks, average flow stress 3 marks = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up engineering strain and true strain** – KTU often tests this. True strain uses $\ln$, not the linear ratio.
> 2. **Forgetting volume constancy** – In drawing/extrusion problems, students often forget $A_1 L_1 = A_2 L_2$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Using wrong temperature criterion** – Many students write $0.4 T_m$ for hot working; the correct cutoff is $0.5 T_m$ (and $0.3 T_m$ for cold working).
> 4. **Confusing $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_f$** – Yield stress is the start of plastic flow; flow stress is the instantaneous stress during plastic flow.
> 5. **In numerical problems on forging, ignoring the factor $(1+n)$** in average flow stress is the most common error.
> 6. **Not sketching in classification questions** – Even a small block diagram earns you 1–2 marks in 7-mark questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Metal forming = **plastic deformation + zero material loss**.
- ✅ **Volume constancy**: $V_i = V_f \Rightarrow A_1 L_1 = A_2 L_2$.
- ✅ **True strain**: $\varepsilon = \ln(L_f / L_i) = \ln(A_i / A_f)$.
- ✅ **Hollomon equation**: $\sigma = K \varepsilon^n$ – backbone of cold working analysis.
- ✅ **Average flow stress**: $\sigma_f^{avg} = \sigma_f^{max} / (1+n)$.
- ✅ **Recrystallization temperature**: $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$.
- ✅ **Cold work**: $T < 0.3 \, T_m$ → strain hardening retained, high accuracy.
- ✅ **Warm work**: $0.3 \, T_m \le T < 0.5 \, T_m$ → balanced.
- ✅ **Hot work**: $T > 0.5 \, T_m$ → no strain hardening, refined grains, low forces.
- ✅ **Strain rate** in upsetting: $\dot{\varepsilon} = v / h$.
- ✅ **Grain flow** is the principal reason forged parts outperform cast parts in fatigue.
- ✅ **Residual stresses** arise from non-uniform deformation; relieved by annealing.
- ✅ **Friction** at tool-work interface increases forming load; managed by lubrication.
- ✅ **Forming vs Machining vs Casting** – forming = plastic reshaping; machining = removal; casting = solidification.
- ✅ **Bulk forming** = forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing; **Sheet forming** = bending, deep drawing, stretch forming.
- ✅ **Strain-hardening exponent $n$** measures the capacity of a metal to distribute strain – higher $n$ means better formability (e.g., $n \approx 0.5$ for austenitic stainless steel).
- ✅ **Forging force (ideal)**: $F = \sigma_f^{avg} \cdot A_{projected}$.
- ✅ **Units alert**: Stress in MPa (= N/mm²), force in N or kN or MN.
- ✅ Always state the **assumptions** (no friction, homogeneous deformation, isothermal) in KTU numerical answers – examiners reward this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capabilities and Application of Processes Like
Rolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rolling** is a metal forming process in which the workpiece (stock) is passed through a pair of **counter-rotating rolls** to reduce its cross-sectional area, change its shape, or impart desired mechanical properties. The compressive forces between the rolls plastically deform the material, primarily through **compressive stress** in the direction normal to the roll surfaces and **shear stress** acting tangentially.

Mathematically, the fundamental operation can be represented as:

$$A_1 \cdot v_1 = A_2 \cdot v_2 = \text{constant}$$

where $A_1$ and $A_2$ are the entry (initial) and exit (final) cross-sectional areas, and $v_1$ and $v_2$ are the entry and exit velocities, respectively. This is the **Law of Constancy of Volume** in plastic deformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Rolling is categorized as a **primary forming (hot/cold) process** under the **bulk deformation** classification. It accounts for over **80% of all hot-worked steel** produced globally, making it the single most industrially significant metal forming operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are flattening a ball of dough with a rolling pin (chakla-belan) in an Indian kitchen. You apply a downward force, and as you push the pin forward, the dough:

1. Becomes **thinner** (reduction in thickness — called *draft*)
2. Becomes **longer** (elongation in rolling direction)
3. Becomes **slightly wider** (spreading in transverse direction)

That is **rolling at the kitchen scale**. In an industrial setting, replace the rolling pin with **hardened steel rolls** rotating at controlled speeds, replace your arms with **hydraulic actuators** delivering thousands of tonnes of force, and replace the dough with **heated steel slabs, billets, or plates** — and you have the foundational concept of industrial rolling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Beginners:** Unlike forging (impact load) or extrusion (single-pass high pressure), rolling is a **continuous, gradual, and incremental** deformation process. The workpiece passes through multiple "stands" of rolls, with each stand performing a small reduction — this gradual approach prevents cracking and allows for very large total reductions.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

The following are standard industrial parameters used in rolling:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | Unit |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|------|
| Coefficient of Friction | $\mu$ | $0.05$ – $0.20$ | dimensionless |
| Roll Radius | $R$ | $0.15$ – $0.50$ | m |
| Reduction (Draft) per Pass | $\Delta h$ | $1$ – $10$ | mm |
| Rolling Speed | $v$ | $0.5$ – $30$ | m/s |
| Roll Separating Force | $F$ | $10^6$ – $10^8$ | N |
| Rolling Power | $P$ | $10^3$ – $10^5$ | kW |

> [!TIP]
> The high **roll separating forces** (in the range of Mega-Newtons) are why rolling mills are among the most mechanically massive equipment in any metallurgical plant.

## 1.4 Classification of Rolling (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

Rolling is classified along **three primary dimensions**:

**A. Based on Workpiece Temperature**
- **Hot Rolling:** Performed **above the recrystallization temperature** (typically $> 0.6 \, T_m$). Coarse grains are refined; large reductions possible; surface finish is rough.
- **Cold Rolling:** Performed **below the recrystallization temperature** (room temperature typically). Better surface finish, higher strength (work hardening), tighter dimensional tolerances.
- **Warm Rolling:** Intermediate temperature range, balances the advantages of both.

**B. Based on Product Geometry**
- **Flat Rolling:** Produces plates, sheets, and strips (rectangular cross-section).
- **Shape Rolling (Section Rolling):** Produces I-beams, channels, rails, T-sections.
- **Ring Rolling:** Produces seamless rings (used in bearings, gears).
- **Thread Rolling:** Produces external threads on fasteners (cold forming process).
- **Profile Rolling:** Produces contoured cross-sections.

**C. Based on Mill Configuration** (covered in detail in Section 2)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Rolling Mill Configurations (Detailed)

A **rolling mill** is the integrated mechanical system that houses the rolls, bearings, housings, drives, and auxiliary equipment. The **KTU 2024 syllabus** emphasizes the following mill types:

### 2.1.1 Two-High Rolling Mill
- **Configuration:** Two rolls, one above the other.
- **Types:** *Reversing* (rolls rotate in one direction, then reverse) or *Non-reversing* (unidirectional).
- **Use:** Roughing stands in hot strip mills.
- **Limitation:** Roll deflection under load causes variation in product thickness across the width (camber problem).

### 2.1.2 Three-High Rolling Mill (Lauth Mill)
- **Configuration:** Three rolls stacked vertically.
- **Mechanism:** The workpiece passes between the **lower and middle roll** on the forward pass, then is lifted and passed between the **middle and upper roll** on the return pass.
- **Advantage:** Higher productivity (no time wasted reversing roll direction).
- **Disadvantage:** Heavy mechanical complexity; needs elevators and roller tables.

### 2.1.3 Four-High Rolling Mill
- **Configuration:** Two **small working rolls** supported by two **larger backup rolls**.
- **Mechanism:** The backup rolls prevent deflection of the working rolls.
- **Use:** Finishing stands in cold strip mills and hot strip mills.
- **Advantage:** Excellent flatness and thickness control; small working rolls reduce rolling force and contact area.

### 2.1.5 Cluster Rolling Mill (Sendzimir Mill / Z-Mill)
- **Configuration:** One small working roll supported by **two intermediate rolls**, each supported by **three backup rolls** — totaling 20 rolls in some configurations.
- **Use:** Rolling of high-strength stainless steel, silicon steel, and very thin foils.
- **Advantage:** Extremely flat product with very high reduction per pass on hard materials.

### 2.1.6 Tandem Rolling Mill
- **Configuration:** Multiple stands (4 to 7) arranged in series, with the workpiece passing continuously through all stands.
- **Use:** Modern high-speed hot strip mills and cold strip mills.
- **Advantage:** Very high productivity; rolling speeds up to **30 m/s** in finishing stands.

## 2.2 Fundamental Rolling Geometry — The Bite / Contact Angle

This is the **most critical geometric concept** in rolling theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **bite angle** $\alpha$ (also called the **contact angle** or **angle of bite**) is the angular measure of the roll arc that is in contact with the workpiece during deformation.

Geometrically, the bite angle is determined by the **roll radius $R$** and the **draft $\Delta h$** (reduction in thickness):

$$\cos \alpha = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R} = 1 - \frac{h_0 - h_f}{2R}$$

where:
- $h_0$ = entry thickness (before rolling)
- $h_f$ = exit thickness (after rolling)
- $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$ = **draft** (reduction per pass)
- $R$ = roll radius

The **projected contact length** $L$ (the horizontal length of the deformation zone) is:

$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$

This approximation holds because the bite angle is small (typically $3°$ to $15°$).

> [!NOTE]
> **For small angles, $\alpha$ in radians is:** $\alpha \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}}$

**Maximum Draft Condition (The "No-Bite" Limit):**
The rolling operation will **fail to "bite"** the workpiece if the angle $\alpha$ is too large. The condition for successful biting is derived from friction:

$$\alpha \leq \mu$$

where $\mu$ is the **coefficient of friction** between the roll and the workpiece. This is the **Ekelund friction hill condition**.

## 2.3 Velocity Field in the Deformation Zone — Forward Slip & Backward Slip

Because the rolls rotate at a constant angular velocity $\omega$, the **roll surface velocity** $v_r$ at the point of contact is:

$$v_r = R \cdot \omega = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

where $D$ is roll diameter and $N$ is rotational speed (rev/s).

The velocity relationship along the contact arc is:

- At the **entry plane**: material velocity $v_1 < v_r$ (rolls move faster than material) — this is the **backward slip** zone.
- At the **neutral point (no-slip point)**: material velocity $v = v_r$ (no relative motion).
- At the **exit plane**: material velocity $v_2 > v_r$ (material moves faster than roll surface) — this is the **forward slip** zone.

**Forward Slip** $s_f$ is defined as:

$$s_f = \frac{v_2 - v_r}{v_r}$$

Using the geometric relationship derived from volume constancy and roll rotation, the **forward slip equation** (for simple rolling, no tension) is:

$$s_f = \frac{R}{h_f} \cdot \gamma^2$$

where $\gamma$ is the **neutral angle** (angle from the exit plane to the neutral point).

**Bland-Ford Forward Slip Equation (More Accurate):**

$$s_f = \frac{R}{h_f} \left[\frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{h_0}{h_f} - 1\right) - \sqrt{\frac{R}{h_f}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h_{\gamma}}{h_f}\right)\right]^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** For cold rolling with back tension $\sigma_t$ and front tension $\sigma_f$, the corrected Bland-Ford equation is the standard exam answer.

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula arsenal** for any Rolling question in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize this entire table.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Volume Constancy | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | $A$ in m², $v$ in m/s | Mass flow rate in continuous rolling |
| 2 | Draft (Reduction) | $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$ | mm | Reduction per pass |
| 3 | Reduction Ratio | $r = \dfrac{h_0}{h_f}$ | dimensionless | Total deformation severity |
| 4 | True Strain | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | dimensionless | Equivalent strain in flat rolling |
| 5 | Bite Angle (Contact Angle) | $\cos \alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}$ | radians | Friction limit, bite condition |
| 6 | Projected Contact Length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | m | Force, torque calculations |
| 7 | Maximum Bite Angle | $\alpha_{\max} = \mu$ | radians | Ekelund condition for no-slip |
| 8 | Roll Surface Velocity | $v_r = \pi D N$ | m/s | Forward slip baseline |
| 9 | Forward Slip | $s_f = \dfrac{R \gamma^2}{h_f}$ | dimensionless | Exit velocity vs. roll velocity |
| 10 | Slab Method: Roll Separating Force | $F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)$ | N | **PRIMARY KTU FORMULA** |
| 11 | Mean Deformation Resistance | $\bar{Y}_f = \dfrac{Y_0 + Y_f}{2}$ | Pa | Average flow stress across deformation zone |
| 12 | Rolling Torque (per roll) | $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | N·m | Torque arm = 0.5 L approximation |
| 13 | Rolling Power | $P = 2 \pi N T$ | W | Motor power sizing |
| 14 | Slab Pressure with Tension | $p = \bar{Y}_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{R}{h}}\right) - \sigma_t - \sigma_f$ | Pa | Hot/cold strip rolling with tension |
| 15 | Contact Arc Length | $L_a = \sqrt{R(h_0 - h_f)}$ | m | Geometric (exact for small $\alpha$) |
| 16 | Spread (Hill's Formula) | $\Delta w = \dfrac{C \cdot h_0}{8} \left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f} - 1\right)^{0.5}$ | m | Width increase during rolling |

> [!TIP]
> **In the roll separating force formula, the term $\dfrac{\mu L}{h_f}$** is called the **friction hill** term. It accounts for the additional pressure needed to overcome frictional resistance at the roll-workpiece interface. Without friction ($\mu = 0$), the formula reduces to $F = \bar{Y}_f L w$ — which is the simple **planar upsetting** approximation.

## 2.5 Why Rolling is Industrially Preferred — Real-World Engineering Utility

Rolling dominates metal forming for several engineering reasons that the KTU examiner expects you to articulate:

1. **Continuous production:** Unlike forging (discrete) or extrusion (batch), rolling is **continuous**, enabling high throughput.
2. **Excellent grain refinement:** Especially in hot rolling, the dynamic recrystallization produces a fine, uniform equiaxed grain structure, dramatically improving toughness.
3. **Mechanical property enhancement:** Cold rolling increases yield strength by **work hardening** (e.g., low-carbon steel sheet can double in strength through cold rolling).
4. **Material savings:** Very high yield ratio; minimal scrap compared to machining.
5. **Versatility:** From thin foils ($< 0.1$ mm) to heavy structural beams ($> 500$ mm thick).

**Industries dependent on rolling:** Automotive (sheet steel for body panels), Construction (rebar, beams), Aerospace (titanium and aluminum alloy plates), Energy (pipe and tube manufacturing for oil/gas), and Consumer Goods (tin cans, appliances).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Bite Angle / Contact Angle Relationship

**Starting Setup:**
Consider a single roll of radius $R$ reducing a workpiece of initial thickness $h_0$ to final thickness $h_f$. The entry and exit planes are vertical, and the contact between the roll and the workpiece is along an arc.

Let the roll center be at point $O$, and the entry and exit points on the roll surface be $A$ and $B$ respectively. The bite angle is the central angle $\angle AOB = 2\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry:**
The vertical distance from the roll center to the entry plane is $(R - h_0/2)$, and to the exit plane is $(R - h_f/2)$. Wait — actually, since the workpiece has thickness, we consider the **mid-thickness** of the workpiece.

**Step 2 — Apply the cosine rule in triangle AOB:**
The horizontal distance from the roll center to the entry contact point equals $(R - h_0/2)$ because the workpiece is entering with its mid-thickness line touching the roll. Similarly for the exit.

The cosine of the half-angle (from roll center to contact point) is:

$$\cos \alpha = \frac{R - h_0/2}{R}$$

This is **only valid if** the center of the roll is aligned with the mid-plane of the workpiece (a reasonable assumption for thin slabs).

**Step 3 — Apply this at both entry and exit:**

At entry: $\cos \alpha_1 = \dfrac{R - h_0/2}{R}$

At exit: $\cos \alpha_2 = \dfrac{R - h_f/2}{R}$

**Step 4 — Take the difference to get the projected contact length:**

The horizontal projection of the contact arc is the difference between the horizontal positions of the entry and exit contact points:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= R \sin \alpha_1 - R \sin \alpha_2 \\
  &= R \left(\sqrt{1 - \cos^2 \alpha_1} - \sqrt{1 - \cos^2 \alpha_2}\right) \\
  &= R \left(\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{R - h_0/2}{R}\right)^2} - \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{R - h_f/2}{R}\right)^2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using the small-angle assumption:**

For small bite angles, $\sin \alpha \approx \alpha$ and $\alpha \approx \sqrt{2(1 - \cos \alpha)}$.

Expanding $(1 - (1 - h/2R)^2)$ for small $h/R$:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - \left(\frac{R - h/2}{R}\right)^2 &= 1 - \left(1 - \frac{h}{2R}\right)^2 \\
&= 1 - 1 + \frac{h}{R} - \frac{h^2}{4R^2} \\
&\approx \frac{h}{R} \quad \text{(neglecting second-order term)}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &\approx R \left(\sqrt{\frac{h_0}{R}} - \sqrt{\frac{h_f}{R}}\right) \\
  &= \sqrt{R \, h_0} - \sqrt{R \, h_f}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the thin-strip simplification $h_0 \approx h_f \approx h_m$ (mean thickness):**

$$L \approx \sqrt{R} \left(\sqrt{h_0} - \sqrt{h_f}\right) = \sqrt{R} \cdot \frac{h_0 - h_f}{\sqrt{h_0} + \sqrt{h_f}}$$

Since $h_0 \approx h_f$ for small reductions, $\sqrt{h_0} + \sqrt{h_f} \approx 2\sqrt{h_m}$. Then:

$$L \approx \frac{\sqrt{R} \cdot \Delta h}{2\sqrt{h_m}}$$

For the most common engineering approximation where $h_m \approx h_f$ (when reduction is small):

$$\boxed{L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for projected contact length. **Memorize it.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Roll Separating Force (Slab Method Analysis)

This is the **most important derivation in Module 1** and is worth 7–10 marks in any KTU ESE on Rolling.

**Setup:**
Consider a slab element within the deformation zone of width $w$ (in the direction of roll axis), at an angular position $\theta$ from the exit plane. The slab has horizontal thickness $dx$, vertical thickness $h$ (which varies from $h_0$ to $h_f$ across the contact), and is acted upon by:
- Normal pressure $p$ from the roll (acting radially)
- Friction shear stress $\tau$ at the roll-workpiece interface (acting tangentially)
- Horizontal stresses $\sigma_x$ on the two faces

**Step 1 — Geometry of the slab:**

The horizontal force balance on the slab is:

$$(\sigma_x + d\sigma_x) \cdot (h + dh) \cdot w - \sigma_x \cdot h \cdot w + 2 p \, d\theta \cdot R \cdot w \cdot \sin\theta - 2 \tau \, d\theta \cdot R \cdot w \cdot \cos\theta = 0$$

Where $d\theta \cdot R$ is the arc length of the contact on the slab.

**Step 2 — Simplify using the relationship $dh = 2R \sin\theta \, d\theta$:**

From the geometry of the roll, the change in slab thickness is:

$$dh = -2 R \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

(Negative sign because $h$ decreases as $\theta$ increases from entry to exit.)

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$(\sigma_x + d\sigma_x)(h + dh) - \sigma_x h = h \, d\sigma_x + \sigma_x \, dh$$

(neglecting the second-order $d\sigma_x \cdot dh$ term)

**Step 4 — Apply the friction law:**

Assuming Coulomb friction at the roll-workpiece interface, the shear stress is:

$$\tau = \mu p$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction.

**Step 5 — Combine the equations:**

$$h \, d\sigma_x + \sigma_x \, dh + 2 p R \sin\theta \, d\theta - 2 \mu p R \cos\theta \, d\theta = 0$$

But $2 R \sin\theta \, d\theta = -dh$, so:

$$h \, d\sigma_x + \sigma_x \, dh - p \, dh - 2 \mu p R \cos\theta \, d\theta = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$h \, d\sigma_x = (p - \sigma_x) \, dh + 2 \mu p R \cos\theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the substitution $\theta \approx 0$ (slab moves horizontally in the deformation zone):**

The contact length projected horizontally is $L$, and the horizontal distance from the exit is $x$. So $\cos\theta \, d\theta \cdot R = dx$, and $dh = -(2h_f/L) \, dx$ (linear thickness variation assumption).

**Step 7 — Apply the yield condition:**

In the deformation zone, the material is undergoing plane-strain compression, so the **von Mises yield criterion** simplifies to:

$$\sigma_x - \sigma_z = \bar{Y}_f$$

where $\sigma_z$ is the vertical (through-thickness) stress. Since the rolls do not constrain lateral flow (no die walls), and assuming homogeneous deformation, the mean vertical stress is approximately equal to the roll pressure $p$:

$$p - \sigma_x = \bar{Y}_f$$

Therefore: $p = \sigma_x + \bar{Y}_f$

**Step 8 — Substitute back into the differential equation:**

$$h \, d\sigma_x = -\bar{Y}_f \, dh + 2 \mu (\sigma_x + \bar{Y}_f) \, dx$$

**Step 9 — Solve the differential equation using integrating factor:**

Let $u = \sigma_x + \bar{Y}_f$, then $du = d\sigma_x$. The equation becomes:

$$\frac{du}{dx} = \frac{2\mu u}{h} - \frac{\bar{Y}_f}{h} \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}$$

Assuming linear thickness profile $h = h_f + \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}(L - x) = h_f + \dfrac{\Delta h}{L} x'$ where $x' = L - x$ (so $x' = 0$ at exit), this leads to the **friction hill equation**:

$$p = \bar{Y}_f \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right]$$

where the term $\dfrac{\mu L}{2 h_f}$ is the dimensionless **friction hill**.

**Step 10 — Compute the total roll separating force:**

The roll force is the integral of the roll pressure over the contact area:

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \int_0^L p \cdot w \, dx \\
  &= w \int_0^L \bar{Y}_f \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right] dx \\
  &= \bar{Y}_f \cdot w \cdot L \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)}$$

This is the **gold-standard KTU formula** for roll separating force.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rolling Torque and Power

**Step 1 — Torque on each roll:**

The roll pressure $p$ acts at the contact surface, producing a torque about the roll axis equal to the pressure times the contact area times the moment arm. For a uniform pressure distribution, the moment arm is the average of the projected lengths $L$:

$$T = \int_0^L p \cdot w \cdot R \, dx \cdot \sin\theta$$

Approximating $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ for small angles and using the total force $F$:

$$T \approx 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Total torque (two rolls):**

$$T_{total} = 2 \cdot T = F \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Power per roll:**

$$P_{roll} = 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi N \cdot 0.5 F L = \pi N F L$$

**Step 4 — Total rolling power (both rolls):**

$$P_{total} = 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi N \cdot F \cdot L$$

Or, expressing in terms of the roll surface velocity $v_r = \pi D N$:

$$P_{total} = F \cdot v_r \cdot \frac{L}{R} = F \cdot v_r \cdot 2\alpha$$

(approximately, for small angles)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Problem Pattern:** Given $h_0$, $h_f$, $R$, $w$, $\mu$, $Y_0$, $Y_f$ (or $\bar{Y}_f$), $N$ (rpm) — calculate $F$, $T$, and $P$ in 3 steps. The standard 7-mark problem follows this template.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Effect of Friction, Tension, and Roll Radius on Rolling Pressure

The complete **friction hill equation with tensions** is:

$$p_{max} = \bar{Y}_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right) - \sigma_t - \sigma_f$$

where $\sigma_t$ is the back tension (at entry) and $\sigma_f$ is the front tension (at exit). The presence of tension **reduces** the required roll pressure.

**Key relationships (qualitative, KTU-favorite):**
- $\mu \uparrow \Rightarrow p \uparrow$ (more friction = more pressure required)
- $L \uparrow \Rightarrow p \uparrow$ (longer contact arc = more pressure)
- $h_f \downarrow \Rightarrow p \uparrow$ (thinner exit = more pressure)
- $R \downarrow \Rightarrow L \downarrow \Rightarrow p \downarrow$ (smaller rolls = lower pressure)
- Tension $\uparrow \Rightarrow p \downarrow$ (tension assists deformation)

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A steel slab, $h_0 = 25$ mm thick and $w = 500$ mm wide, is rolled in a two-high mill with roll radius $R = 250$ mm and rotational speed $N = 60$ rpm. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.10$. The material has initial yield strength $Y_0 = 250$ MPa and strain-hardening exponent $n = 0.20$. Final thickness is $h_f = 20$ mm. Calculate: (a) Bite angle, (b) Projected contact length, (c) Roll separating force, (d) Rolling torque per roll, (e) Total power.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Bite Angle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= h_0 - h_f = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ mm} \\
\cos \alpha &= 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R} = 1 - \frac{5}{2 \times 250} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 \\
\alpha &= \cos^{-1}(0.99) = 0.1413 \text{ rad} = 8.10°
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Projected Contact Length:**

$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 5} = \sqrt{1250} = 35.36 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (c) — Mean Deformation Resistance:**

For a strain-hardening material: $Y_f = Y_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)^n$

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{25}{20}\right) = 0.2231 \\
Y_f &= 250 \times (1.25)^{0.20} = 250 \times 1.0456 = 261.4 \text{ MPa} \\
\bar{Y}_f &= \frac{Y_0 + Y_f}{2} = \frac{250 + 261.4}{2} = 255.7 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (d) — Roll Separating Force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right) \\
  &= 255.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.03536 \times 0.500 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.10 \times 0.03536}{2 \times 0.020}\right) \\
  &= 255.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.01768 \times \left(1 + 0.0884\right) \\
  &= 4.521 \times 10^6 \times 1.0884 \\
  &= 4.921 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 4.92 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (e) — Torque per roll:**

$$T = 0.5 \times F \times L = 0.5 \times 4.921 \times 10^6 \times 0.03536 = 86.97 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Part (f) — Total Power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi \times 1 \times 86970 = 546{,}352 \text{ W} \approx 546.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

(Here $N = 60 \text{ rpm} = 1 \text{ rev/s}$)

```mermaid
    F2 --> F3[Finishing Mill Stand N]
    F3 --> G1[Runout Table with Cooling]
    G1 --> H1[Coiler or Cut to Length]
    H1 --> I1[Finished Product]
    J1[Roll Pressure Sensor] --> K1[Closed Loop Thickness Control]
    K1 --> E1
    K1 --> E2
    K1 --> E3
    K1 --> F1
    K1 --> F2
    K1 --> F3
    L1[Mill Stand] --> M1[Work Rolls]

```
