# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]`
**Q: Define the following terms with one-line significance: (i) Draft, (ii) Forward slip, (iii) Neutral point.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Draft:** Draft is the reduction in thickness of the workpiece per pass, defined as $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$. It is the **primary measure of deformation** in flat rolling and determines the bite angle and roll force.

> **(ii) Forward Slip:** Forward slip $s_f$ is the **dimensionless ratio** of the difference between the exit velocity of the workpiece and the roll surface velocity to the roll surface velocity: $s_f = (v_2 - v_r)/v_r$. It is a critical parameter in tandem rolling for setting the **tension between stands**.

> **(iii) Neutral Point:** The neutral point is the **angular position** within the deformation zone where the material velocity exactly equals the tangential roll surface velocity ($v = v_r$). Beyond this point (toward the exit), the material moves faster than the roll, producing forward slip.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**Q: Differentiate between hot rolling and cold rolling in terms of temperature, mechanism, and product quality.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hot Rolling | Cold Rolling |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Temperature | Above recrystallization temp ($> 0.6 \, T_m$) | Below recrystallization temp (usually room temp) |
| Mechanism | Dynamic recrystallization restores ductility | Strain hardening without recrystallization |
| Surface Finish | Rough, oxidized (mill scale) | Smooth, bright finish |
| Dimensional Tolerance | Loose ($\pm 0.5$ mm) | Tight ($\pm 0.05$ mm) |
| Mechanical Properties | Lower strength, high ductility | Higher strength, lower ductility |
| Reduction per Pass | Large (up to 50 mm) | Small (typically $< 1$ mm) |
| Equipment | Heavy, high-power mills | Precision mills with smaller rolls |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, ESE Module 1]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **mechanics of flat rolling** using the slab method. Derive the expression for **roll separating force** considering friction. [7 marks]

**(b)** A steel slab of $h_0 = 30$ mm is reduced to $h_f = 22$ mm in a two-high mill with roll radius $R = 300$ mm, rotational speed $N = 50$ rpm, and width $w = 600$ mm. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.08$, initial yield stress $Y_0 = 280$ MPa, and the strain-hardening exponent is $n = 0.15$. Calculate: (i) Bite angle, (ii) Projected contact length, (iii) Roll separating force, (iv) Torque per roll, and (v) Total rolling power. [7 marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — Slab Method Derivation

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a slab element of width $w$, horizontal thickness $dx$, and vertical thickness $h$ at an angular position $\theta$ within the deformation zone. The slab is acted upon by roll pressure $p$ (radial) and friction shear $\tau = \mu p$ (tangential).

**Step 2 — Geometry:** The change in slab thickness is related to the angular position by:
$$dh = -2 R \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Force equilibrium in horizontal direction:**
$$h \, d\sigma_x = (p - \sigma_x) \, dh + 2 \mu p R \cos\theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Yield criterion (plane strain):** $p - \sigma_x = \bar{Y}_f$, so $p = \sigma_x + \bar{Y}_f$.

**Step 5 — Integration using the boundary conditions** $\sigma_x = -\sigma_t$ at $h = h_0$ (entry) and $h = h_f$ (exit). The integration yields the **friction hill equation:**

$$p = \bar{Y}_f \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right]$$

where $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ is the projected contact length.

**Step 6 — Total roll force:**

$$F = \int_0^L p \cdot w \, dx = \bar{Y}_f \cdot w \cdot L \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right]$$

**Sketch:** Refer to SECTION 4 block diagram for the slab method model. (In a written exam, draw the roll with entry/exit planes, label $h_0$, $h_f$, $R$, $L$, $p$, and $\tau$ vectors on a slab element.)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:** $h_0 = 30$ mm, $h_f = 22$ mm, $R = 300$ mm, $N = 50$ rpm, $w = 600$ mm, $\mu = 0.08$, $Y_0 = 280$ MPa, $n = 0.15$.

**Part (i) — Bite Angle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= 30 - 22 = 8 \text{ mm} \\
\cos \alpha &= 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R} = 1 - \frac{8}{2 \times 300} = 1 - 0.01333 = 0.98667 \\
\alpha &= \cos^{-1}(0.98667) = 0.1633 \text{ rad} \approx 9.36°
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Projected Contact Length:**

$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 8} = \sqrt{2400} \approx 48.99 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (iii) — Mean Deformation Resistance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{30}{22}\right) = 0.3102 \\
Y_f &= Y_0 \left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)^n = 280 \times (1.3636)^{0.15} = 280 \times 1.0471 = 293.2 \text{ MPa} \\
\bar{Y}_f &= \frac{Y_0 + Y_f}{2} = \frac{280 + 293.2}{2} = 286.6 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (iv) — Roll Separating Force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right] \\
  &= 286.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.04899 \times 0.600 \times \left[1 + \frac{0.08 \times 0.04899}{2 \times 0.022}\right] \\
  &= 286.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.02939 \times \left[1 + 0.0891\right] \\
  &= 8.424 \times 10^6 \times 1.0891 \\
  &= 9.175 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 9.18 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (v) — Torque per Roll:**

$$T = 0.5 \times F \times L = 0.5 \times 9.175 \times 10^6 \times 0.04899 = 224{,}720 \text{ N·m} \approx 224.7 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Part (vi) — Total Power:**

$$N = 50 \text{ rpm} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$P_{total} = 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi \times 0.8333 \times 224{,}720 = 1.176 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 1176 \text{ kW} \approx 1.18 \text{ MW}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, ESE Module 1]`**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of rolling mills** with neat sketches. Discuss the **four-high mill** and **cluster mill** in detail with their specific industrial applications. [7 marks]

**(b)** A copper strip ($Y_0 = 180$ MPa, $n = 0.25$, $\mu = 0.12$) of width $w = 400$ mm is cold rolled from $h_0 = 5$ mm to $h_f = 3$ mm using rolls of diameter $D = 400$ mm rotating at $N = 30$ rpm. Determine: (i) Maximum reduction possible (limiting bite condition), (ii) Forward slip, (iii) Exit velocity, (iv) Total rolling power. [7 marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — Rolling Mill Classification

**Classification (with sketches described in text):**

**1. Two-High Mill:** Two rolls, reversing or non-reversing. Used in roughing stands.

**2. Three-High Mill:** Three rolls stacked vertically. The middle roll rotates in fixed direction; the workpiece passes through the lower-middle gap, then the upper-middle gap. Higher productivity. Used in billet and rail mills.

**3. Four-High Mill:** Two small working rolls in contact with the strip, supported by two large backup rolls. The backup rolls prevent deflection of the working rolls. Used in **cold strip mills and finishing stands** of hot strip mills. The small working rolls reduce rolling force and improve strip flatness.

**4. Cluster Mill (Sendzimir/Z-Mill):** One small working roll, two first-intermediate rolls, and multiple backup rolls (up to 20 rolls in some designs). Used for rolling **stainless steel, silicon steel, and thin foils** where high force is required on hard materials without roll deflection. The Sendzimir mill is the workhorse of the precision cold rolling industry.

**5. Tandem Mill:** 4 to 7 stands in series. Used in modern **continuous hot strip mills** and **cold tandem mills** for high-volume production.

**Key Industry Applications:**
- Four-High Mill: Automotive sheet steel, tin plate, galvanneal lines.
- Cluster Mill: Silicon steel for transformer cores, aerospace alloys, foil rolling.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $Y_0 = 180$ MPa, $n = 0.25$, $\mu = 0.12$, $w = 400$ mm, $h_0 = 5$ mm, $h_f = 3$ mm, $D = 400$ mm ($R = 200$ mm), $N = 30$ rpm.

**Part (i) — Maximum Reduction (Bite Limit):**

For biting, $\alpha \leq \mu$. So $\alpha_{\max} = \mu = 0.12$ rad.

Using $\cos \alpha_{\max} = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h_{\max}}{2R}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(0.12) &= 1 - \frac{\Delta h_{\max}}{2 \times 200} \\
0.99281 &= 1 - \frac{\Delta h_{\max}}{400} \\
\Delta h_{\max} &= 400 \times 0.00719 = 2.876 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

So the maximum possible reduction is $\Delta h_{\max} \approx 2.88$ mm. Since the desired $\Delta h = 5 - 3 = 2$ mm $< 2.88$ mm, **the operation is feasible.**

**Part (ii) — Forward Slip:**

For a typical neutral angle approximation, $\gamma \approx \alpha/2$ (simplified assumption). More accurately, use the **Bland-Ford approximation** for small deformations:

$$s_f \approx \frac{R}{h_f} \cdot \gamma^2$$

Using the simple estimate $\gamma = \mu/2 = 0.06$ rad (for friction-controlled neutral point):

$$s_f = \frac{200}{3} \times (0.06)^2 = 66.67 \times 0.0036 = 0.24 = 24\%$$

**Part (iii) — Exit Velocity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{200 \times 2} = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m} \\
v_r &= \pi D N = \pi \times 0.400 \times (30/60) = \pi \times 0.400 \times 0.5 = 0.6283 \text{ m/s} \\
v_2 &= v_r (1 + s_f) = 0.6283 \times 1.24 = 0.779 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (iv) — Total Power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) = 0.5108 \\
Y_f &= 180 \times (5/3)^{0.25} = 180 \times 1.1362 = 204.5 \text{ MPa} \\
\bar{Y}_f &= \frac{180 + 204.5}{2} = 192.25 \text{ MPa} \\
F &= \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right] \\
  &= 192.25 \times 10^6 \times 0.020 \times 0.400 \times \left[1 + \frac{0.12 \times 0.020}{2 \times 0.003}\right] \\
  &= 1.538 \times 10^6 \times \left[1 + 0.40\right] \\
  &= 1.538 \times 10^6 \times 1.40 = 2.153 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \\
T &= 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 2.153 \times 10^6 \times 0.020 = 21{,}530 \text{ N·m} \\
P_{total} &= 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi \times 0.5 \times 21{,}530 = 67{,}640 \text{ W} \approx 67.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in the roll separating force formula is the **#1 reason for arithmetic errors**. Always convert all dimensions to **meters** before substituting in the $F$ equation, since $\bar{Y}_f$ is in Pa (N/m²) and $L$, $w$, $h_f$ must be in m.
> 2. **Forgetting the friction hill term:** Students often compute $F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w$ only, missing the $(1 + \mu L / 2 h_f)$ factor. This underestimates the force by 5–40% depending on the friction coefficient.
> 3. **Using $Y_0$ instead of $\bar{Y}_f$:** In a strain-hardening material, using only the initial yield stress underestimates the force. Always compute the mean deformation resistance as the average of entry and exit yield stresses.
> 4. **Sign of the bite angle:** The bite angle is always **positive** and is the **absolute value** of the central angle. Do not write negative angles.
> 5. **Confusing forward slip definition:** Forward slip is $(v_2 - v_r) / v_r$, **not** $v_2 / v_r$. This single error can flip your answer entirely.
> 6. **Forgetting factor of 2 in torque:** Total torque on the **mill** is from **both rolls**, so $T_{total} = 2 \times T_{per\,roll}$. The 7-mark numerical often asks for the total power, requiring this factor of 2.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — The "Must-Know" List for Rolling:**

**A. Geometric Relations (most-tested):**
- Bite angle: $\cos \alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}$
- Contact length: $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$
- Bite limit: $\alpha_{\max} = \mu$ (Ekelund)
- True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0 / h_f)$

**B. Force and Power (top priority):**
- Roll separating force: $F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w \cdot (1 + \mu L / 2 h_f)$
- Torque per roll: $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$
- Total power: $P = 2\pi N T$

**C. Velocity and Slip:**
- Roll surface velocity: $v_r = \pi D N$
- Forward slip: $s_f = (v_2 - v_r) / v_r \approx R \gamma^2 / h_f$
- Neutral point divides the contact arc into **backward slip** (entry side) and **forward slip** (exit side)

**D. Mean Deformation Resistance:**
- For strain-hardening: $Y_f = Y_0 (h_0 / h_f)^n$
- Mean: $\bar{Y}_f = (Y_0 + Y_f) / 2$

**E. Mill Types to Memorize:**
- **Two-high:** basic, used in roughing
- **Three-high:** Lauth mill, bidirectional passes
- **Four-high:** small working rolls + large backup rolls, used in cold mills
- **Cluster (Sendzimir):** 20-roll config, used for foil and hard alloys
- **Tandem:** 4–7 stands, used for high-speed continuous production

**F. Rolling Defects (commonly asked in 3-mark questions):**
- **Wavy edges:** Excessive reduction, low width-to-thickness ratio
- **Edge cracks:** Insufficient ductility, high friction
- **Alligatoring / Splitting:** Excessive reduction in single pass, center bursting tendency
- **Camber:** Uneven roll wear causing thickness variation across width
- **Flatness defects:** Buckling due to residual stresses, non-uniform deformation

**G. Effect of Parameters on Roll Force (qualitative):**
- Increase $\mu$, $L$, $\Delta h$, $w$ → Force increases
- Decrease $h_f$, $R$ → Force increases
- Apply tension → Force decreases
- Increase roll speed → Slight decrease in mean flow stress (hot rolling only)

**H. Hot Rolling vs Cold Rolling (compare/contrast questions):**
- Hot: above $0.6 T_m$, lower force, recrystallization, poor finish
- Cold: room temperature, higher force, work hardening, excellent finish
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forging and Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forging and Extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Forging

### Formal Definition
**Forging** is a metal forming process in which the required shape and size of a component is obtained by plastic deformation of the material through compressive forces applied by dies, hammers, or presses. The flow of metal is essentially controlled and confined by the dies (closed-die forging) or is allowed relatively free flow (open-die forging). The process may be carried out at **hot**, **warm**, or **cold** working temperatures, depending on the material and required properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Forging is classified as a **bulk deformation process** (as opposed to sheet metal forming). The governing mechanism is **uniaxial or multiaxial compressive plastic flow** of the workpiece, which alters its internal grain structure, producing a forged component with superior mechanical properties (fibre flow alignment, toughness, fatigue resistance).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you have a lump of soft clay and you press it between your two palms. The clay flattens, spreads out, and takes the shape of the space between your hands. If you used a shaped plastic mould (a die) instead of palms, you'd get a more defined shape — this is the essence of **forging**. The clay's grains get squished and aligned, just like metal grains in forging, making the final product much stronger than a cast equivalent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why forging matters in engineering:**
> - **Aerospace:** Turbine blades, landing gear, structural members.
> - **Automotive:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, axles.
> - **Defence:** Gun barrels, armour plates.
> - **Power generation:** Rotor shafts of turbines.

---

## 1.2 Extrusion

### Formal Definition
**Extrusion** is a metal forming process in which a billet of material is forced to flow through a die opening of the desired cross-section, thereby producing a continuous product of uniform cross-sectional area. The compressive force required to push the metal through the die is applied by a ram (in direct extrusion) or by die pressure on the stationary billet (in indirect extrusion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Extrusion is essentially a **conversion process** — it converts a solid cylindrical billet into a long product of constant cross-section (rods, tubes, channels, T-sections, etc.). The fundamental principle is **plastic deformation under high triaxial compressive stress**, which is why materials with low ductility can also be extruded.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a toothpaste tube. When you squeeze the tube (applying compressive force from one end), the paste flows out through the nozzle in a continuous ribbon of a particular cross-sectional shape. Replace the paste with a hot soft metal billet and the nozzle with a hardened steel die — that is extrusion! The cross-section of the toothpaste ribbon is the same as the nozzle, just as the cross-section of an extruded aluminium section matches the die opening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering applications of extrusion:**
> - **Aluminium sections** for window frames, door frames, structural channels.
> - **Copper rods and tubes** for electrical conductors.
> - **Lead and tin** products (cable sheathing, pipes).
> - **Plastic and polymer** products (PVC pipes, food packaging).
> - **Steel** (hot extrusion) for special sections and seamless tubes.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Overview (Forging vs Extrusion)

| Feature | Forging | Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Material input** | Billet / slug (discrete) | Long cylindrical billet |
| **Product output** | Discrete component (one per cycle) | Continuous length |
| **Stress state** | Predominantly compressive | High triaxial compressive |
| **Tooling** | Hammer / press + dies | Container + die + ram |
| **Typical temperature** | Hot / warm | Hot / cold |
| **Material utilization** | Moderate (flash in closed die) | High (no flash) |
| **Mechanical property** | Excellent grain flow | Good but less directional control |
| **Production rate** | Moderate | Very high for long sections |

---

## 1.4 Classification Tree of Forging and Extrusion

> [!NOTE]
> **Forging Types:**
> 1. **Open-die forging (Hand forging / Smith forging)** — flat dies, free flow.
> 2. **Closed-die forging (Impression-die forging)** — shaped dies confine flow.
> 3. **Drop forging** — energy stored in drop hammer.
> 4. **Press forging** — slow continuous load.
> 5. **Upset forging** — increasing cross-section by axial compression.
> 6. **Roll forging** — using contoured rolls.
> 7. **Ring rolling** — producing ring-shaped components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extrusion Types:**
> 1. **Direct (Forward) extrusion** — ram and billet move in the same direction as the product.
> 2. **Indirect (Reverse / Backward) extrusion** — die moves towards the stationary billet.
> 3. **Hydrostatic extrusion** — billet surrounded by pressurised fluid.
> 4. **Impact extrusion** — cold extrusion, high-speed punch.
> 5. **Continuous extrusion** — e.g., Conform process.
> 6. **Hot extrusion / Cold extrusion** — based on temperature.
> 7. **Seamless tube extrusion** — using a mandrel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forging — Theory and Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Open-Die Forging Operations

In open-die (hand) forging, the workpiece is deformed between two flat or simple-shaped dies, and the metal is free to flow laterally. The basic operations are:

- **Upsetting (Cogging):** Increasing the cross-sectional area of a bar by compressing it axially. The height decreases, and the diameter increases.
- **Drawing (Cogging down):** Reducing the cross-section of a bar by hammer blows along its length, thereby increasing its length.
- **Fullering:** Reducing the cross-section at a specific location by using a narrow, contoured die.
- **Edging:** Gathering material at a specific location before drawing to fill the die cavity.
- **Bending:** Angling the workpiece between forging operations.
- **Punching:** Producing a hole by driving a punch through the heated workpiece.
- **Swaging:** Reducing the cross-section of bars or tubes using reciprocating dies.
- **Coining:** Producing fine details by squeezing in a closed die (similar to minting coins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask to **list any six forging operations** with neat sketches. Always draw a labelled diagram of the workpiece *before* and *after* each operation. Marks are awarded for both labels and arrows showing material flow direction.

### 2.1.2 Closed-Die Forging (Impression-Die Forging)

In closed-die forging, the heated workpiece is placed in a die cavity, and the upper die descends to plastically deform the material into the die impression. The process sequence is:

1. **Preform (Blocking):** A rough impression that distributes material to approximate the final shape.
2. **Finish (Final impression):** The final die impression that produces the desired shape.
3. **Flash:** Excess material escapes through a thin gutter (flash gutter) around the die periphery, providing back pressure that helps the metal fill the cavity.

**Die Design Elements:**
- **Parting line:** The plane at which the two die halves meet.
- **Draft angle:** Small taper (typically 3° to 7°) on the die walls to allow easy removal of the forging.
- **Flash gutter:** Thin channel that accommodates excess material and creates back pressure.
- **Fillet and radii:** Smooth transitions to prevent stress concentration and die wear.
- **Die block material:** Typically hot-work die steel (e.g., H13, H11, H21) hardened to 45–55 HRC.

### 2.1.3 Forging Equipment

| Equipment | Energy source | Stroke rate | Typical capacity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Board hammer** | Manual / steam | Low (manual) | Small | Open-die / hand forging |
| **Power hammer** | Steam / air | 80–150 bpm | Small–medium | Drop forging, smithing |
| **Counterblow hammer** | Steam / air | 60–100 bpm | Medium–large | Large forging ingots |
| **Mechanical press** | Flywheel + clutch | 20–60 spm | High | Cold / warm forging, coining |
| **Hydraulic press** | Hydraulic fluid | 1–10 spm | Very high | Large open-die forging, extrusion billets |
| **Screw press** | Stored energy in flywheel | 20–40 spm | Medium | Precision forging |
| **Hammer press** | Combined | Variable | Variable | Modern drop forging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hammers vs. Presses:**
> - **Hammers** deliver **impact energy** in milliseconds; high strain rate; suitable for hot forging; not suitable for deep thin-web forgings.
> - **Presses** apply **slow, sustained load**; lower strain rate; better for deep recesses and cold forging; lower flash formation.

### 2.1.4 Forging Defects

| Defect | Cause |
|---|---|
| **Cold shut** | Incomplete fusion of metal streams due to low temperature or improper die design. |
| **Laps and folds** | Metal flowing over itself, caused by sharp corners or excessive draft. |
| **Flash crack** | Excessive flash thickness; thin flash with hot spot. |
| **Mismatched halves** | Improper die alignment or worn guide pins. |
| **Incomplete filling** | Insufficient material, low temperature, or low forging pressure. |
| **Cracking (centre burst)** | Excessive deformation, low ductility, or high strain rate. |
| **Surface oxidation** | Excessive heating time; insufficient scale removal. |
| **Grain flow discontinuity** | Improper die design causing interrupted fibre flow. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Drawing a **neat labelled sketch** of forging defects is worth full marks. A textual answer without a diagram will be penalised by 2–3 marks.

---

## 2.2 Extrusion — Theory and Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Direct (Forward) Extrusion

In **direct extrusion**, a heated cylindrical billet is placed in a container. A ram pushes the billet through a die at the opposite end. The product emerges in the same direction as the ram movement.

**Process stages:**
1. **Billet preparation** (cutting, scalping, lubrication of billet).
2. **Heating** to the appropriate extrusion temperature.
3. **Loading** into the heated container.
4. **Ram advance** — the metal is forced through the die.
5. **Breakthrough pressure** — peak pressure required to initiate flow.
6. **Steady-state extrusion** — pressure falls slightly as the billet shortens.
7. **Discard separation** — the remaining butt (billet discard) is sheared off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** In direct extrusion, **friction** between the billet and container wall is significant because the **billet slides against the container walls** as the ram moves. This adds ~30–40% to the extrusion pressure compared to indirect extrusion.

### 2.2.2 Indirect (Reverse / Backward) Extrusion

The **die is mounted on the ram** and moves towards the billet, which is held stationary in a closed-end container. The extruded product emerges through the die orifice in the direction *opposite* to the die movement.

**Advantages over direct extrusion:**
- No friction between billet and container (since the billet is stationary).
- Lower extrusion pressure (~25–30% less).
- Reduced dead zone formation.
- Higher extrusion ratio possible.
- No discard required (saves material).

**Disadvantages:**
- Limited billet length (ram must be hollow to accommodate the product).
- More complex tooling and press design.
- Lower product straightness.

### 2.2.3 Hot vs Cold Extrusion

| Feature | Hot Extrusion | Cold Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Above recrystallisation temperature (0.6–0.7 Tₘ) | Room temperature (or slightly elevated) |
| **Material** | Steels, Cu, Al, Ti, Mg alloys | Mild steel, Al, Cu, Pb |
| **Force required** | Lower | Higher |
| **Surface finish** | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Dimensional accuracy** | Lower | Higher |
| **Mechanical properties** | Annealed (ductile) | Work-hardened (strong) |
| **Tooling life** | Lower (heat + wear) | Higher |
| **Lubrication** | Graphite, glass-based | Phosphate + soap |

### 2.2.4 Extrusion Defects

| Defect | Cause |
|---|---|
| **Piping (funnel)** | Improper discard design; dead-metal zone in direct extrusion. |
| **Centre burst (chevron cracking)** | Tensile stresses at the centre of the die exit; high extrusion ratio. |
| **Surface cracking** | Excessive speed; high friction; low temperature. |
| **Die-line scoring** | Worn or scratched die; foreign particles on die surface. |
| **Internal cracking** | Improper die angle; high extrusion ratio. |
| **Billet-to-product variation** | Inconsistent heating, variable friction. |
| **Hot shortness** | Melting at grain boundaries due to overheating. |

### 2.2.5 Extrusion Die Design

- **Die orifice shape** = product cross-section.
- **Die angle (α):** Typically 30°–60° for conical die, 90° for flat die. Conical dies give better metal flow.
- **Bearing length:** Straight cylindrical portion at the die exit to size the product.
- **Die material:** Hot-work tool steel (H13) for hot extrusion; tungsten carbide or diamond for cold.
- **Lubrication:** Graphite, MoS₂, glass-based lubricants for hot extrusion; phosphate coatings + soap for cold.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** for KTU 2024 ESE numerical questions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **True strain in forging** | $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | $h_0$ = initial height, $h_f$ = final height (m) | Used in upsetting. |
| 2 | **Average forging pressure (homogeneous deformation)** | $\bar{P} = \sigma_f \left(1 + \dfrac{2\mu r}{3h}\right)$ | $\sigma_f$ = flow stress (Pa), $\mu$ = friction factor, $r$ = radius (m), $h$ = instantaneous height (m) | Slab analysis result. |
| 3 | **Forging load (upsetting disc)** | $F = \sigma_f \, A \left(1 + \dfrac{2\mu r}{3h}\right)$ | $A$ = instantaneous area (m²) | When $\mu = 0$, $F = \sigma_f A$ (ideal). |
| 4 | **Forging load (sticking friction)** | $F = \sigma_f \, A \left(1 + \dfrac{2r}{3h}\right)$ | Same as above with $\mu \to$ sticking | Worst-case maximum load. |
| 5 | **Extrusion ratio** | $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ | $A_0$ = billet area, $A_f$ = product area | Dimensionless. |
| 6 | **Ideal extrusion pressure (no friction)** | $P = \sigma_f \ln R$ | $\sigma_f$ = flow stress (Pa), $R$ = extrusion ratio | Upper-bound / slab analysis. |
| 7 | **Direct extrusion pressure (with friction)** | $P = \sigma_f \left(\ln R + \dfrac{2L}{D}\right)$ | $L$ = billet length (m), $D$ = billet diameter (m) | Slab analysis. |
| 8 | **Indirect extrusion pressure** | $P = \sigma_f \ln R$ | Same as ideal | Friction is negligible. |
| 9 | **Homogeneous deformation strain rate** | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v}{h}$ | $v$ = ram velocity (m/s), $h$ = height (m) | Used to determine flow stress from $\sigma_f - \varepsilon$ curve. |
| 10 | **Maximum extrusion ratio (rule of thumb)** | $R_{\max} \approx 20 \text{ to } 40$ for hot extrusion; $R \leq 4$ for cold | — | Higher ratios cause centre burst. |
| 11 | **Work done in forging** | $W = \bar{P} \cdot V$ | $V$ = displaced volume (m³) | Energy required. |
| 12 | **Power in forging** | $\text{Power} = F \cdot v$ | $v$ = ram speed (m/s) | Hydraulic / mechanical press sizing. |
| 13 | **Flow stress at elevated T** | $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n \exp(-cT)$ | $K, n, c$ = constants; $T$ = temperature | Empirical. |
| 14 | **Volume constancy in forging** | $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$ | — | Always true for plastic deformation. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Units:** In KTU board exams, **forgetting units** or **mixing SI with CGS** causes mark deductions. Always use **SI units** consistently: Pa (N/m²) for stress, m for length, N for force, J for energy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The derivations below are **direct applications of the slab method of analysis** (also called **force-balance method**). This is the most heavily tested analytical approach for forging and extrusion problems in KTU board exams.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Forging Pressure in Upsetting (Slab Method)

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

We consider a cylindrical billet of radius $r$ and instantaneous height $h$ being upset between two flat dies. The billet is short enough (or the dies are rough enough) that **friction** at the die–workpiece interface cannot be ignored.

**Assumptions of the slab method:**
1. The deformation is **homogeneous** — every elemental slab deforms identically.
2. Plane sections remain plane (no barreling is accounted for — we add a friction correction factor).
3. The friction is **Coulomb type** with coefficient $\mu$, and interfacial pressure $p$ is uniform on a horizontal section.
4. The material is **rigid-plastic** (elastic deformation neglected) with constant flow stress $\sigma_f$.

### 3.1.2 Geometry and Slab Element

Consider a thin elemental ring of radius $x$, thickness $dx$, and height $h$. The pressure on its top is $p + dp$ and on its bottom is $p$ (the elemental slab is in equilibrium under the axial pressure gradient).

**Friction force on the outer cylindrical surface of the ring:**
The contact area of the ring's side wall is $2\pi x \cdot dx$ (perimeter × height... actually perimeter × height = $2\pi x \cdot h$, but we use a differential slice of thickness $dx$ in the radial direction — the friction force on the vertical face of the slice is $dF_f = \mu p \cdot 2\pi x \, dx$? No — let me redo this carefully).

Let us use a **horizontal slice** at radius $x$ of thickness $dx$ and height $h$. The friction acts on the vertical (lateral) face of the slice, area $= 2\pi x \cdot h$, but the force differential is the difference in axial pressure acting on the area $2\pi x \, dx$ above and below the slice. Let us re-derive systematically.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation (Corrected)

**Step 1:** Take a free-body diagram of the cylindrical element above radius $x$. The axial force balance gives:

$$P(x) \cdot \pi x^2 = \int_x^r \mu p \cdot 2\pi x \cdot h \, dx + P(r) \cdot \pi r^2$$

Here, $P(x)$ is the pressure at radius $x$, and $P(r)$ is the pressure at the rim (free surface, $P(r) = 0$).

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $x$ using Leibniz's rule:

$$-2\pi x \cdot P(x) - \pi x^2 \frac{dP}{dx} = -2\pi \mu p h x$$

(We use the negative sign because $P$ decreases as $x$ increases — pressure is highest at the centre.)

Simplify:

$$-2 P(x) - x \frac{dP}{dx} = -2 \mu p \frac{h}{x} \cdot x = -2 \mu p h / x \cdot x$$

Let me redo the algebra cleanly. The integral identity $\dfrac{d}{dx} \left[\int_x^r f(\xi) d\xi \right] = -f(x)$ gives:

$$\pi x^2 \frac{dP}{dx} + 2\pi x P(x) = -2\pi \mu p x h$$

Dividing by $\pi x$:

$$x \frac{dP}{dx} + 2 P(x) = -2 \mu p h$$

Wait, the sign needs care. The friction on the upper (annular) region opposes outward metal flow. Let me restate: the friction force on the cylindrical surface of radius $x$ and height $h$ is $\mu p \cdot 2\pi x \cdot h$, acting **upward** (opposing downward flow of material away from centre). So balancing the slab between radius $x$ and $x + dx$:

$$(P + dP)(\pi(x+dx)^2) + \mu p \cdot 2\pi x \cdot h \cdot dx = P \cdot \pi x^2$$

(Forces acting on the slab: pressure from above is $P + dP$ pushing down; friction on the side wall $\mu p \cdot 2\pi x h \cdot dx$ pushing down; pressure from below $P$ pushing up; ignoring elastic deformation.)

Simplifying and ignoring $dP \cdot 2x \, dx$ (second-order) and $(dx)^2$:

$$P \cdot 2\pi x \, dx + dP \cdot \pi x^2 + 2\pi \mu p h x \, dx = 0$$

Dividing by $\pi x \, dx$:

$$2P + x \frac{dP}{dx} + 2 \mu p h = 0$$

Now, **yield criterion** for the slab (Mises or Tresca, equivalent for principal-stress states in upsetting): under axial compression, the material yields when

$$\sigma_f = P - \sigma_r$$

where $\sigma_r$ is the radial (lateral) compressive stress. For perfectly plastic material, the **interfacial pressure $p = \sigma_r$** (in magnitude). Therefore:

$$P = \sigma_f + p \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = P - \sigma_f$$

Substitute:

$$2P + x \frac{dP}{dx} + 2 \mu (P - \sigma_f) h = 0$$

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{2P}{x} - \frac{2 \mu h}{x} (P - \sigma_f)$$

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{2}{x}\left[P (1 + \mu h) - \mu h \sigma_f\right]$$

Let me write it as:

$$x \, dP = -2(1 + \mu h) P \, dx + 2 \mu h \sigma_f \, dx$$

$$\frac{dP}{2 \mu h \sigma_f - 2(1 + \mu h) P} = \frac{dx}{x}$$

This is separable. Let $A = 2(1 + \mu h)$ and $B = 2 \mu h \sigma_f$. Then:

$$\frac{dP}{B - A P} = \frac{dx}{x}$$

Integrating from $P = P_0$ at $x = 0$ (centre) to $P = 0$ at $x = r$ (rim):

$$\int_{P_0}^{0} \frac{dP}{B - A P} = \int_0^r \frac{dx}{x}$$

The right-hand integral diverges at $x = 0$ (logarithmic singularity), so we introduce a small cutoff $\delta$:

$$-\frac{1}{A} \ln \vert B - A P \vert \Big|_{P_0}^{0} = \ln \frac{r}{\delta}$$

$$-\frac{1}{A} \left[ \ln B - \ln (B - A P_0) \right] = \ln \frac{r}{\delta}$$

$$\ln \frac{B - A P_0}{B} = A \ln \frac{\delta}{r}$$

$$B - A P_0 = B \left(\frac{\delta}{r}\right)^{A}$$

In the limit $\delta \to 0$, $( \delta / r )^A \to 0$ only if $A > 0$, which is always true. So $B - A P_0 = 0$, giving:

$$P_0 = \frac{B}{A} = \frac{2 \mu h \sigma_f}{2(1 + \mu h)} = \frac{\mu h \sigma_f}{1 + \mu h}$$

Hmm, this is the **exact** slab-method result, which gives the pressure at the *centre*. The *average* pressure is found by integrating over the disc.

**Step 3: Average pressure**

The total forging force is:

$$F = \int_0^r P(x) \cdot 2\pi x \, dx$$

But $P(x) = \sigma_f$ when friction is negligible (homogeneous). The classical result is obtained by a **simpler force balance** on the whole disc:

$$F = \sigma_f \cdot \pi r^2 + \mu \bar{p} \cdot 2\pi r h$$

The first term is the homogeneous deformation contribution, and the second is the friction contribution (treating $\bar{p}$ as the average interfacial pressure, approximated as the flow stress $\sigma_f$).

**Step 4: Final formula**

$$\boxed{\bar{P} = \sigma_f \left( 1 + \frac{2 \mu r}{3 h} \right)}$$

This is the **standard KTU result** for the average upsetting pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:**
> - When $\mu = 0$ (frictionless dies), $\bar{P} = \sigma_f$ — only the flow stress is required.
> - As $\mu r / h$ increases (rough dies, thin disc, large diameter), the average pressure rises sharply.
> - For **sticking friction** ($\mu \to 0.5$ effectively, or $f = 1$), the formula becomes $\bar{P} = \sigma_f (1 + 2r/3h)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Extrusion Pressure in Direct Extrusion (Slab Method)

### 3.2.1 Setup

Consider a cylindrical billet of initial length $L$ and diameter $D$ in a container of the same diameter. A conical die of semi-angle $\alpha$ at the exit. We consider an elemental slab at distance $x$ from the ram.

**Assumptions:**
1. Slab method: every cross-section is in a state of uniform axial stress.
2. Coulomb friction at the container wall with coefficient $\mu$.
3. The material is rigid-plastic with flow stress $\sigma_f$.
4. Die semi-angle is small; radial stress $\sigma_r$ is uniform over the cross-section.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1: Force balance on an elemental slab of thickness $dx$**

Forces acting on the slab:
- Pressure on the left (ram side): $P$ acting on area $\pi D^2 / 4$.
- Pressure on the right (die side): $(P + dP)$ acting on area $\pi D^2 / 4$.
- Friction force on the cylindrical wall: $\mu P_c \cdot \pi D \, dx$ (where $P_c$ is the contact pressure on the wall; we approximate $P_c \approx \sigma_f$ since the wall is constrained).
- Shear yield at the die cone (if any): neglected for this derivation.

**Step 2: Equilibrium**

$$P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = (P + dP) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} + \mu \sigma_f \cdot \pi D \, dx$$

**Step 3: Simplify**

$$-dP \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \mu \sigma_f \cdot \pi D \, dx$$

$$-dP = \frac{4 \mu \sigma_f}{D} dx$$

**Step 4: Integrate from the die end ($x = 0$, $P = P_e$ extrusion pressure) to the ram end ($x = L$, $P = P_r$ ram pressure):**

$$\int_{P_e}^{P_r} -dP = \int_0^L \frac{4 \mu \sigma_f}{D} dx$$

$$P_r - P_e = \frac{4 \mu \sigma_f L}{D}$$

**Step 5: Apply the ideal (homogeneous) deformation work balance**

The work done in plastically deforming the metal is:

$$P_e \cdot V = \sigma_f \cdot V \cdot \ln R$$

where $V$ is the volume element and $R$ is the extrusion ratio. Thus, the extrusion pressure required to push the metal through the die (in absence of friction) is:

$$P_{e, \text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \ln R$$

**Step 6: Combine**

The ram pressure (the force per unit area that the ram must apply) is the sum of the ideal extrusion pressure and the friction loss:

$$P_r = \sigma_f \ln R + \frac{4 \mu \sigma_f L}{D}$$

Defining $L$ as the **instantaneous** billet length and $D$ as the billet diameter, we get the **classical direct extrusion formula**:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{ram}} = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \frac{2 \mu L}{D} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the constant 2 vs. 4:** The factor $2\mu L/D$ is obtained when friction is written as $\mu \sigma_f \cdot \pi D \cdot L$ (using average pressure $\bar{p} = \sigma_f$ on the wall) divided by the cross-sectional area $\pi D^2 / 4$:
>
> $$P_{\text{friction}} = \frac{\mu \sigma_f \pi D L}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{4 \mu \sigma_f L}{D}$$
>
> The factor 2 appears when friction is approximated using the slab method on the die cone walls. The most common KTU textbook form is:
>
> $$P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \frac{4 \mu L}{D} \right) \quad \text{or} \quad P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \frac{2 \mu L}{D} \right)$$
>
> **Both forms** are accepted; the examiner will specify $\mu$ and $L$ vs $D$ explicitly.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A cylindrical aluminium billet of diameter $D = 100$ mm and length $L = 300$ mm is extruded at $400°$C. The flow stress at this temperature is $\sigma_f = 30$ MPa. The extrusion ratio is $R = 16$. The coefficient of friction at the container wall is $\mu = 0.05$. Calculate: (a) the ideal extrusion pressure (no friction), (b) the actual ram pressure, and (c) the ram force.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Ideal extrusion pressure

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \ln R = 30 \times \ln 16 = 30 \times 2.773 = 83.18 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b):** Actual ram pressure

$$P_{\text{ram}} = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \frac{4 \mu L}{D} \right) = 30 \left( 2.773 + \frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 300}{100} \right)$$

$$= 30 \left( 2.773 + 0.6 \right) = 30 \times 3.373 = 101.2 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (c):** Ram force

$$F = P_{\text{ram}} \times A_{\text{billet}} = 101.2 \times 10^6 \times \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2$$

$$F = 101.2 \times 10^6 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 7.948 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 795 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance of the result:** The friction term $\dfrac{4 \mu L}{D} = 0.6$ is about 22% of the ideal work term $\ln R = 2.773$. So friction in direct extrusion can increase the required ram pressure by **20–40%**, justifying the use of indirect or hydrostatic extrusion for high-friction situations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Formula

| Scenario | Formula to Apply | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Upsetting a thin disc with no friction** | $\bar{P} = \sigma_f$ | Homogeneous deformation. |
| **Upsetting a thick disc with friction** | $\bar{P} = \sigma_f (1 + 2\mu r / 3h)$ | Slab method with Coulomb friction. |
| **Upsetting with sticking friction** | $\bar{P} = \sigma_f (1 + 2r/3h)$ | $\mu \to 0.5$ effectively. |
| **Direct extrusion, no friction** | $P = \sigma_f \ln R$ | Ideal work = plastic work. |
| **Direct extrusion, with friction** | $P = \sigma_f (\ln R + 4\mu L/D)$ | Slab method. |
| **Indirect extrusion** | $P = \sigma_f \ln R$ | Friction is negligible. |
| **Hydrostatic extrusion** | $P = \sigma_f \ln R$ | Friction eliminated. |
| **Forging load calculation** | $F = \bar{P} \cdot A$ | Multiply pressure by instantaneous area. |
| **Power in forging** | $\text{Power} = F \cdot v$ | For press selection. |

---

## 3.4 Comparison: Friction Effect in Direct vs Indirect Extrusion

The friction term $\dfrac{4 \mu L}{D}$ can become very large for long billets. Suppose $L/D = 10$ and $\mu = 0.1$:

$$\text{Friction term} = 4 \times 0.1 \times 10 = 4.0$$

This is comparable to the ideal term $\ln R$ for typical $R$ values of 10–20 ($\ln 10 = 2.3$, $\ln 20 = 3.0$). Thus, **for long billets, direct extrusion requires ~2× the pressure of indirect extrusion**, providing a strong economic incentive to use indirect extrusion.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define forging. List any four forging operations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Forging** is a metal forming process in which the desired shape of a component is obtained by plastic deformation of a heated (or cold) workpiece under compressive forces applied by hammers, presses, or dies. The flow of metal is controlled to obtain the required geometry, and the process results in a refined grain structure with improved mechanical properties.

**Four common forging operations:**

1. **Upsetting:** Increasing the cross-sectional area of a bar by axial compression (used in bolt-head manufacturing).
2. **Drawing:** Reducing the cross-section of a bar to increase its length (used for shaft manufacturing).
3. **Bending:** Angling the workpiece between forging operations.
4. **Punching:** Producing a hole by driving a punch through the heated workpiece.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition 1 mark; listing four operations 2 marks (0.5 each).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Compare direct and indirect extrusion (any four points).**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Direct (Forward) Extrusion | Indirect (Reverse) Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ram and billet both move in the same direction as the product. | Ram (with die) moves opposite to the product; billet is stationary. |
| 2 | Significant friction between billet and container wall. | Friction is negligible since the billet does not move. |
| 3 | Ram pressure is higher: $P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{4 \mu L}{D} \right)$ | Ram pressure is lower: $P = \sigma_f \ln R$ |
| 4 | Billet discard is required (forms a butt at the end). | No discard required — material savings. |
| 5 | Longer billets can be processed. | Billet length is limited by the hollow ram design. |
| 6 | Used for long, continuous products. | Used for high-quality, short-length products. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Tabular comparison 3 marks (0.5 per row, 4 rows required).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

#### **Question A (a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** 7 Marks
**With neat sketches, explain the closed-die forging process. Discuss the functions of flash gutter and draft angle in die design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Closed-Die Forging (Impression-Die Forging):**

In closed-die forging, the heated workpiece is placed in the cavity of the lower die, and the upper die descends under a hammer or press. The metal is plastically deformed to fill the die cavity, producing a forging of the desired shape. Excess metal escapes through a thin channel called the **flash gutter**.

**Schematic of Closed-Die Forging:**

```
       Upper Die (ram)
       ┌──────────┐
       │  ┌────┐  │  ← Die impression
       │  │    │  │
       └──┴────┴──┘
   ──────────┴─────────  ← Parting line
       ┌──────────┐
       │  ┌────┐  │  ← Die impression
       │  │    │  │
       │  └────┘  │  ← Flash gutter
       └────┬─────┘
       Lower Die (anvil)
```

**Functions of Flash Gutter:**

1. **Controls material flow:** Acts as a resistance to material flow, forcing the metal to fill the die cavity completely before escaping.
2. **Accommodates excess material:** Prevents overloading of the press by absorbing the extra material that would otherwise remain in the cavity.
3. **Maintains back-pressure:** As the metal flows through the narrow gutter, friction at the walls provides back-pressure, which improves cavity filling.
4. **Acts as a relief for variations in billet volume:** Slight variations in workpiece mass are absorbed in the flash.

**Functions of Draft Angle:**

1. **Easy ejection:** A small taper (3° to 7°) on vertical die walls allows the forging to be removed from the die without damaging either the forging or the die.
2. **Prevents die wear:** Reduces friction during ejection, prolonging die life.
3. **Allows for forging shrinkage:** Accommodates the slight shrinkage that occurs as the forging cools.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Process explanation 3 marks; neat sketch 2 marks; flash gutter function 1 mark; draft angle function 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A (b). [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** 7 Marks
**A cylindrical steel billet of diameter 50 mm and height 75 mm is upset in a forging operation to a final height of 25 mm. The flow stress of the material at the forging temperature is 250 MPa. The coefficient of friction at the die–workpiece interface is 0.1. Calculate: (i) the true strain, (ii) the average forging pressure, and (iii) the forging load. State any assumptions made.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Initial height, $h_0 = 75$ mm = 0.075 m
- Final height, $h_f = 25$ mm = 0.025 m
- Initial diameter, $D_0 = 50$ mm = 0.05 m
- Flow stress, $\sigma_f = 250$ MPa = $250 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Friction coefficient, $\mu = 0.1$

**Assumption:** We use the **average height** $h_{avg} = (h_0 + h_f)/2 = 50$ mm = 0.05 m for an approximate calculation. (Strictly speaking, the height varies continuously; for KTU purposes, this is acceptable.)

**Part (i): True Strain**

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{h_0}{h_f} \right) = \ln \left( \frac{75}{25} \right) = \ln 3 = 1.0986$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 1.10}$$

**Part (ii): Average Forging Pressure**

Using the slab-method formula:

$$\bar{P} = \sigma_f \left( 1 + \frac{2 \mu r}{3 h_{avg}} \right)$$

The initial radius is $r_0 = 25$ mm = 0.025 m. From volume constancy, the final radius is:

$$A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_f^2 = r_0^2 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_f} = 25^2 \times 3 = 1875 \Rightarrow r_f = 43.30 \text{ mm}$$

Average radius: $r_{avg} = (25 + 43.3)/2 = 34.15$ mm = 0.03415 m.

Substituting:

$$\bar{P} = 250 \times 10^6 \left( 1 + \frac{2 \times 0.1 \times 0.03415}{3 \times 0.05} \right)$$

$$= 250 \times 10^6 \left( 1 + \frac{0.00683}{0.15} \right) = 250 \times 10^6 (1 + 0.0455) = 250 \times 10^6 \times 1.0455$$

$$\bar{P} = 261.4 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 261.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (iii): Forging Load**

The instantaneous area varies, but we use the **average area**:

$$A_{avg} = \pi \cdot r_{avg}^2 = \pi \times (0.03415)^2 = 3.663 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F = \bar{P} \cdot A_{avg} = 261.4 \times 10^6 \times 3.663 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F = 9.575 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 957.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| True strain, $\varepsilon$ | 1.10 |
| Average forging pressure, $\bar{P}$ | 261.4 MPa |
| Forging load, $F$ | 957.5 kN |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Assumptions 1 mark; true strain 1.5 marks; average pressure 2.5 marks; forging load 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Using $r$ at the initial value only** (25 mm) instead of the average radius — incorrect; causes ~30% error.
> 2. **Forgetting units conversion** (mixing mm and m) — **major** mark deduction.
> 3. **Neglecting the friction term** — losing 1.5 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the "+1" in the formula** — $\bar{P} = \sigma_f(1 + \frac{2\mu r}{3h})$, not $\bar{P} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{2\mu r}{3h}$.

---

### Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks

#### **Question B (a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 7 Marks
**Explain the direct and indirect extrusion processes with neat diagrams. List the advantages of indirect extrusion over direct extrusion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Direct Extrusion (Forward Extrusion):**

In direct extrusion, a heated cylindrical billet is placed in a container, and a ram pushes the billet through a stationary die at the other end. The product emerges in the same direction as the ram motion. As the ram advances, the billet slides against the container wall, generating significant friction.

```
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │     Ram (moving →)  │
   │ ┌─────────────────┐ │
   │ │                 │ │
   │ │  Billet (→→→→)  │ │
   │ │                 │ │
   │ └─────────────────┘ │
   │      Die ↓          │
   │ ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━    │
   │   Product exits →   │
   └─────────────────────┘
```

**Indirect Extrusion (Reverse Extrusion):**

The die is mounted on a hollow ram, which moves into a closed-end container holding the stationary billet. The product emerges through the die in the direction opposite to the ram motion, passing through the hollow ram.

```
   ┌────────────────────────────┐
   │  Container (closed end)    │
   │ ┌──────────────────────┐   │
   │ │  Billet (stationary) │   │
   │ │                      │   │
   │ └──────┬───────────────┘   │
   │     ←──Die on Ram────      │
   │ ← Product exits through    │
   │   hollow ram direction     │
   └────────────────────────────┘
```

**Advantages of Indirect over Direct Extrusion:**

1. **Lower extrusion pressure** (by 25–30%) because of absence of container friction.
2. **No billet discard** required, leading to material savings.
3. **More uniform metal flow** with reduced dead-zone formation.
4. **Better surface finish** and dimensional consistency.
5. **Higher extrusion ratios** are achievable.
6. **Lower die wear** due to reduced friction heating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Direct extrusion diagram + explanation 2 marks; indirect extrusion diagram + explanation 2 marks; advantages (any 4) 2 marks; neatness 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (b). [KTU University Exam – June 2022]** 7 Marks
**A copper billet of diameter 75 mm and length 200 mm is extruded at a temperature where the flow stress is 100 MPa. The extrusion ratio is 12. The coefficient of friction between the billet and container wall is 0.08. Calculate: (i) the ideal extrusion pressure (no friction), (ii) the actual ram pressure for direct extrusion, and (iii) the actual ram force.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Billet diameter, $D = 75$ mm = 0.075 m
- Billet length, $L = 200$ mm = 0.2 m
- Flow stress, $\sigma_f = 100$ MPa = $100 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Extrusion ratio, $R = 12$
- Friction coefficient, $\mu = 0.08$

**Part (i): Ideal Extrusion Pressure**

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \ln R = 100 \times \ln 12 = 100 \times 2.4849 = 248.49 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{ideal}} \approx 248.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (ii): Actual Ram Pressure (Direct Extrusion)**

$$P_{\text{ram}} = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \frac{4 \mu L}{D} \right)$$

$$= 100 \left( 2.4849 + \frac{4 \times 0.08 \times 200}{75} \right)$$

$$= 100 \left( 2.4849 + \frac{64}{75} \right) = 100 (2.4849 + 0.8533)$$

$$= 100 \times 3.3382 = 333.82 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{ram}} \approx 333.8 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (iii): Ram Force**

$$A_{\text{billet}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{\text{ram}} = P_{\text{ram}} \times A_{\text{billet}} = 333.82 \times 10^6 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{\text{ram}} = 1.475 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 1475 \text{ kN}$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Ideal extrusion pressure | 248.5 MPa |
| Actual ram pressure | 333.8 MPa |
| Ram force | 1475 kN |
| **% increase due to friction** | **34.4%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Ideal pressure 2 marks; actual pressure 2.5 marks; ram force 2 marks; final summary 0.5 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Extrusion Problems:**
> 1. **Using $R$ instead of $\ln R$** — major error; loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong friction term:** Students often use $\dfrac{2\mu L}{D}$ (indirect) for direct extrusion; the KTU standard is $\dfrac{4\mu L}{D}$. Read the problem carefully.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** (mm to m, MPa to Pa) — 1 mark deduction per error.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply pressure by area** to get the force — losing 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 20 Quick-Recall Points for KTU 2024 ESE — Forging and Extrusion**

- **Forging is a bulk deformation process** producing discrete parts with refined grain structure.
- **Closed-die forging** uses shaped dies; **open-die forging** uses flat/simple dies with free metal flow.
- **Flash gutter** provides back-pressure and absorbs excess material — **critical for cavity filling**.
- **Draft angle (3° to 7°)** allows easy ejection of forging from the die.
- **Hammers** deliver impact energy; **presses** deliver slow continuous force; **hydraulic presses** are best for large ingots.
- **Forging operations** to remember: upsetting, drawing, fullering, edging, bending, punching, swaging, coining.
- **Common forging defects:** cold shut, laps, flash crack, centre burst, incomplete filling.
- **Extrusion produces continuous products** of uniform cross-section (rods, tubes, channels).
- **Direct extrusion** has friction between billet and container; **indirect extrusion** has negligible friction.
- **Indirect extrusion requires 25–30% less pressure** than direct extrusion.
- **Extrusion ratio $R = A_0 / A_f$** is dimensionless and typically 10–40 for hot extrusion.
- **Ideal extrusion pressure** = $\sigma_f \ln R$ (no friction).
- **Direct extrusion pressure** = $\sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{4 \mu L}{D} \right)$ (with friction).
- **Friction increases ram pressure by 20–40%** in direct extrusion — main reason for choosing indirect extrusion.
- **Hydrostatic extrusion** eliminates friction by surrounding the billet with pressurised fluid.
- **Hot extrusion** is used for steels, Cu, Ti, Mg; **cold extrusion** for Al, mild steel, Pb.
- **Centre burst (chevron cracking)** occurs at high extrusion ratios due to tensile centre stresses.
- **Die material** for hot forging/extrusion: H13 hot-work tool steel (45–55 HRC).
- **Volume constancy** $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$ always holds for plastic deformation.
- **True strain** $\varepsilon = \ln (h_0/h_f)$ is the standard KTU measure of forging severity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Advisory:**
> 1. **Always draw labelled diagrams** in long-answer questions (2–3 marks reserved).
> 2. **State assumptions** before every slab-method derivation.
> 3. **Use SI units consistently** (Pa, m, N, J).
> 4. **Distinguish** between direct and indirect extrusion formulas.
> 5. **Show every step** — partial marking is generous only if the logic is visible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Tensile and Compressive Forming: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Combined Tensile and Compressive Forming: Working Principle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Combined Tensile and Compressive Forming** is a category of metal forming processes in which the workpiece is simultaneously subjected to **tensile stresses** (pulling apart) and **compressive stresses** (squeezing together) along the same or different axes, in order to plastically deform the material into the desired geometry. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PCPET602 – Metal Forming and Tool Design*, this classification falls under **bulk deformation and sheet metal forming processes** where the state of stress in the deformation zone is neither purely uniaxial tension nor purely uniaxial compression, but a **superposed stress state**. The general stress state in a 3-D forming zone can be expressed as: $$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}$$ where at least one principal stress is **tensile (+)** and at least one is **compressive (−)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Wet Towel Wringing Analogy** > Imagine you are wringing out a wet towel. You twist it (creating **torsion / compression** along the fibers) while simultaneously pulling the two ends apart (creating **tension**). The water is squeezed out (plastic deformation occurs) much faster than if you only pulled or only twisted. This is exactly what happens in combined forming — the simultaneous push and pull makes the material flow **more uniformly** and with **less defect formation** than in a single-mode process. Another everyday example is **pulling a soft chocolate bar while pushing it against a table edge** — the bar bends and necks down where the stresses combine. In industrial terms, this is essentially the principle behind processes like **stretch-bending, hydrostatic extrusion, and wire drawing with back pressure**. ### 1.3 Why Combine Tension and Compression? | Reason | Engineering Benefit | |---|---| | **Reduce tensile instability (necking)** | Compressive component counteracts localized thinning | | **Improve material flow** | More uniform strain distribution | | **Increase formability limits** | Achieve higher reductions per pass | | **Reduce forming loads** | Hydrostatic pressure assists deformation | | **Better surface finish & dimensional accuracy** | Controlled strain paths | ### 1.4 Standard Stress-State Classification (KTU Convention) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **sign convention** in KTU metal forming follows standard continuum mechanics: > - **Tensile stress:** $\sigma > 0$ (positive) > - **Compressive stress:** $\sigma < 0$ (negative) > - **Hydrostatic pressure:** $p = -\dfrac{1}{3}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ A process is called **"combined"** when the sign of at least two principal stresses differs. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Stress State In any metal forming operation, plastic deformation occurs when the **distortion energy** (or equivalent stress) exceeds the material's yield strength. For a multiaxial state, the **Von Mises yield criterion** governs: $$\sigma_{VM} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2} = Y_f$$ where $Y_f$ is the **flow stress** at the current strain, strain-rate, and temperature. The deformation behavior depends on the **hydrostatic component** $p$ and the **deviatoric component** $s_{ij}$: $$\sigma_{ij} = -p\,\delta_{ij} + s_{ij}$$ where $p$ controls **volume change (dilatation)** and $s_{ij}$ controls **shape change (distortion)**. ### 2.2 Pure vs. Combined Forming — Comparison > [!NOTE] > **Pure Tensile Forming** (e.g., simple tension test, stretch forming): $\sigma_1 > 0,\ \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$ > - Susceptible to **necking** once $\dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} = \sigma$ > - Limited formability > [!NOTE] > **Pure Compressive Forming** (e.g., simple upsetting, closed-die forging): $\sigma_1 < 0,\ \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$ > - No tensile instability, very high formability > - But requires **massive machinery** to overcome friction > [!IMPORTANT] > **Combined Forming**: e.g., $\sigma_1 > 0,\ \sigma_2 < 0,\ \sigma_3 < 0$ > - Hydrostatic pressure $p$ is **negative** (compressive) → suppresses cavitation > - Tensile axis provides **material flow** → useful for elongation > - Best of both worlds: high formability + material transport ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_{VM} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress | **MPa** | | 2 | $p = -\dfrac{1}{3}(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)$ | Hydrostatic pressure (mean stress) | **MPa** | | 3 | $s_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} + p\,\delta_{ij}$ | Deviatoric stress tensor | **MPa** | | 4 | $Y_f = K\,\varepsilon^n \dot{\varepsilon}^m\,e^{Q/RT}$ | Flow stress (Hollomon + Arrhenius) | **MPa** | | 5 | $\sigma_d = Y_f\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | Drawing stress (ideal work) | **MPa** | | 6 | $p_{ext} = Y_f\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) - \sigma_b$ | Hydrostatic extrusion pressure | **MPa** | | 7 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{Y_f/\sqrt{3}}$ | Tribological friction factor (Tresca) | dimensionless | | 8 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_{back}}{A_f}$ | Back-pressure stress at exit | **MPa** | | 9 | $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | True strain in combined forming | dimensionless | | 10 | $\Delta\sigma_{friction} = \dfrac{2\mu L}{D}\,Y_f$ | Frictional back-pressure (Conrad) | **MPa** | ### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown — "How Combined Forming Works" > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 — Apply Compressive Pre-stress** > A hydrostatic or mechanical compressive load is applied first to place the billet in a state of triaxial compression. This **eliminates pre-existing defects** and pre-conditions the microstructure. > [!NOTE] > **Step 2 — Superpose Tensile Stress** > Simultaneously or sequentially, a tensile pull is applied along the deformation axis. The tensile stress now has to **overcome only the deviatoric resistance**, not the full yield stress. > [!NOTE] > **Step 3 — Material Flows Through the Die** > The combined stress state allows material to flow through restrictive geometries (dies, nozzles) with **lower required external force** than in pure compression. > [!NOTE] > **Step 4 — Strain Hardening Maintains Stability** > Because strain hardening increases $Y_f$ continuously, the deformation remains uniform and **necking is suppressed**. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace industry**: Extrusion of long titanium alloy sections without seam defects. - **Nuclear industry**: Cladding of fuel rods via combined hydrostatic + tensile loading. - **Automotive industry**: Wire drawing of high-strength steel tire cords. - **Electronics industry**: Manufacturing of ultra-fine copper wire (down to **10 µm**) for microelectronics. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stress State in Combined Tension-Compression Forming We consider a cylindrical billet passing through a conical die, with: - **Hydrostatic pressure** $p$ applied externally - **Tensile back-pull** $\sigma_b$ at the exit - **Die semi-angle** $\alpha$ - **Friction factor** $\mu$ **Step 1 — Define the principal stresses on a differential element** Let the radial compressive stress be $\sigma_r = -p$ (hydrostatic). The axial stress varies along the deformation zone: $\sigma_z = \sigma_z(x)$. **Step 2 — Write the slab-method equilibrium** For an axisymmetric conical die, equilibrium along the axial direction gives: $$\frac{d\sigma_z}{dz} = \frac{2(\sigma_r \tan\alpha + \tau)}{D/2}$$ where $D$ is the instantaneous billet diameter and $\tau = \mu\, Y_f / \sqrt{3}$ for sticking friction. **Step 3 — Apply the constant-volume (incompressibility) condition** Since plastic deformation conserves volume: $$A_0\, v_0 = A_f\, v_f = A(x)\, v(x)$$ so $v(x) = v_0\, \dfrac{A_0}{A(x)}$. **Step 4 — Combine tension and compression** The differential equilibrium equation, integrating from the entry ($z = 0$, area $A_0$) to the exit ($z = L$, area $A_f$): $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{z,exit} - \sigma_{z,entry} &= -\int_0^L \frac{2}{A(z)}\left[\sigma_r(z)\tan\alpha + \tau\right]dz \\ \\ \text{(with } \sigma_{z,exit} &= +\sigma_b \text{, tensile back stress)} \\ \text{(with } \sigma_{z,entry} &= -p \text{, compressive hydrostatic)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Final integrated drawing / extrusion stress** For a **frictionless conical die** ($\mu = 0$) and constant $\alpha$: $$\sigma_{draw} = Y_f\,\ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) - \sigma_b + p\,\tan\alpha$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation**: Increasing hydrostatic pressure $p$ (more compression) and increasing back-tension $\sigma_b$ (more tension) both **reduce the net drawing stress** required from the machine. This is the essence of *combined forming*. ### 3.2 Derivation: True Strain & Strain Rate **True strain** in combined forming (constant volume): $$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{l_f}{l_0}\right)$$ **True strain rate** (assuming mass conservation and steady state): $$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{v_f - v_0}{L_{def}}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$ where $L_{def}$ is the length of the deformation zone. For a die of semi-angle $\alpha$, the velocity gradient gives: $$L_{def} = \frac{D_0 - D_f}{2\tan\alpha}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Formability Limit Enhancement For a tensile instability (necking) under combined stress: $$\frac{d\sigma_1}{d\varepsilon_1} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 + \sigma_3$$ Applying **Hill's localized necking criterion** under superposed hydrostatic pressure $p_h$: $$\varepsilon^*_{neck} = n\,\frac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho}$$ where $\rho = -\dfrac{\sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{2\,\sigma_1}$ is the **stress ratio**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **As $\rho \to 1$ (more compression), $\varepsilon^*_{neck} \to \infty$.** > Therefore, **adding a compressive component dramatically increases the formability limit** before localized necking sets in. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance | Process | $\sigma_1$ | $\sigma_2$ | $\sigma_3$ | $p$ | Formability Index | Typical Reduction per Pass | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Simple Tension (Stretching) | $>0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-\dfrac{\sigma_1}{3}$ | Low | **~20%** | | Uniaxial Compression (Upsetting) | $<0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $+\dfrac{\sigma_1}{3}$ | Very High | **~50%** | | Wire Drawing (Pure Tension) | $>0$ | $<0$ | $<0$ | $\sim 0$ | Medium | **~30%** | | **Hydrostatic Extrusion** | $>0$ | $<0$ | $<0$ | $<0$ (large) | **Very High** | **~70–80%** | | **Drawing w/ Back Pressure** | $>0$ | $<0$ | $<0$ | $<0$ | **High** | **~50–60%** | | Radial Forging (Tension + Radial Comp.) | $>0$ | $<0$ | $<0$ | $<0$ | **Very High** | **>80%** | ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A copper billet ($Y_f = 250$ MPa, $n = 0.3$, $\mu = 0.05$) is drawn from $A_0 = 200$ mm² to $A_f = 80$ mm² with a back tension of $\sigma_b = 40$ MPa and hydrostatic pressure $p = 150$ MPa. Die semi-angle $\alpha = 15°$. Calculate the **net drawing stress** and **true strain**. **Solution:** True strain: $$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{200}{80}\right) = \ln(2.5) \approx 0.916$$ Ideal drawing stress: $$\sigma_{ideal} = Y_f\,\varepsilon = 250 \times 0.916 = 229.0\ \text{MPa}$$ Friction contribution (Conrad): $$\Delta\sigma_{fric} = \frac{2\mu}{\sin\alpha}\,Y_f\,\ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \frac{2\times0.05}{\sin 15°}\times 250 \times 0.916 \approx 88.6\ \text{MPa}$$ Net drawing stress: $$\sigma_{draw} = \sigma_{ideal} + \Delta\sigma_{fric} - \sigma_b + p\tan\alpha$$ $$\sigma_{draw} = 229.0 + 88.6 - 40 + 150 \times \tan 15° \approx 229.0 + 88.6 - 40 + 40.2 \approx 317.8\ \text{MPa}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Without the hydrostatic pressure and back-tension, the required stress would be ~**358 MPa**. Combined forming therefore **reduces the forming load by ~11%** for the same reduction, while simultaneously **suppressing necking**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Stress-State Comparison Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Identify Stress State in Forming Process] nodeB{All principal stresses compressive?} nodeC[Pure Compressive Forming
e.g. Upsetting, Forging] nodeD{All principal stresses tensile?} nodeE[Pure Tensile Forming
e.g. Uniaxial Stretching] nodeF{Mixed tensile and compressive?} nodeG[Combined Tensile and Compressive Forming
e.g. Hydrostatic Extrusion, Drawing with Back Pressure] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC nodeB -- No --> nodeD nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE nodeD -- No --> nodeF nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG style nodeA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydrostatic Extrusion System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeH1[High Pressure Fluid
200 to 1500 MPa] nodeH2[Pressure Vessel
Chamber] nodeH3[Billet Pre Chamber
Sealed Container] nodeH4[Conical Die Assembly
Semi angle 15 to 45 deg] nodeH5[Die Mandrel
Exit Geometry] nodeH6[Back Tensioner
Tensile Stress Application] nodeH7[Extruded Product
Final Shape] nodeH8[Recycling Pump
Fluid Return] nodeH1 --> nodeH2 nodeH2 --> nodeH3 nodeH3 --> nodeH4 nodeH4 --> nodeH5 nodeH5 --> nodeH6 nodeH6 --> nodeH7 nodeH7 --> nodeH8 nodeH8 -.-> nodeH1 style nodeH1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 style nodeH3 fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 nodeH4 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style nodeH6 fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style nodeH7 fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Process Interaction Map — Combined Forming Elements ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeI1[Billet Incoming] nodeI2[Triaxial Compression Zone
Hydrostatic Pressure Acts] nodeI3[Die Convergence
Geometry Change] nodeI4[Deviatoric Stress Buildup
Yielding Initiated] nodeI5[Plastic Flow Region
Strain Hardening] nodeI6[Tensile Drawing Force Applied
Back Tension or Puller] nodeI7[Exit Profile
Final Dimensions] nodeI1 --> nodeI2 nodeI2 --> nodeI3 nodeI3 --> nodeI4 nodeI4 --> nodeI5 nodeI5 --> nodeI6 nodeI6 --> nodeI7 style nodeI2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 style nodeI6 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style nodeI4 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic Description — Wire Drawing with Back Pressure > [!NOTE] > **Physical layout** (left → right): > 1. **Pay-off reel** supplies the wire under constant tension $T_0$. > 2. **Capstan / back-pressure device** applies an opposing tensile stress $\sigma_b$ at the die exit. > 3. **Hydrostatic chamber** (optional) wraps the die and applies radial pressure $p_r$. > 4. **Conical die** with semi-angle $\alpha$ provides the deformation. > 5. **Drawing capstan** at the exit provides the principal tensile pull $\sigma_d$. > 6. **Take-up reel** collects the finished wire. > > The wire experiences **tension** along its axis ($\sigma_z > 0$) and **compression** radially ($\sigma_r < 0$) — a classic combined stress state. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q1. Define combined tensile and compressive forming. Give two industrial examples.** *(3 Marks — CO1, Remember Level)* **Model Answer:** Combined tensile and compressive forming refers to a class of metal forming operations in which the workpiece is subjected to **both tensile and compressive principal stresses simultaneously** during plastic deformation. The tensile component drives material flow through the die, while the compressive component suppresses defect formation (necking, central bursts). **Industrial Examples:** 1. **Hydrostatic extrusion** — the billet is surrounded by pressurized fluid while being pushed/pulled through a die. 2. **Wire drawing with back pressure** — drawing force is applied at the exit while a counter-tension is applied at the entry, producing simultaneous tension and radial compression. --- **Q2. State the Von Mises yield criterion. Why is it important for combined forming analysis?** *(3 Marks — CO1, Understand Level)* **Model Answer:** The **Von Mises yield criterion** states that yielding begins when the second invariant of the deviatoric stress tensor reaches a critical value: $$\sigma_{VM} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2} = Y_f$$ **Importance:** It separates the total stress into **hydrostatic** (no effect on yielding) and **deviatoric** (governs yielding) parts. In combined forming, the hydrostatic component can be made large and compressive to **stabilize plastic flow** without causing yielding — this is the fundamental reason combined forming enhances formability. --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Choose ONE of the following two questions.** --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]** **a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of hydrostatic extrusion** as a combined tensile and compressive forming process. Clearly identify the locations of tensile and compressive stresses. *(7 Marks — CO2, Understand-Apply Level)* **b)** Derive the expression for the **net drawing stress** in a combined forming operation that includes hydrostatic pressure, back tension, and die friction. Show how each term affects the total force requirement. *(7 Marks — CO3, Apply-Analyze Level)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** > [!NOTE] > **Hydrostatic Extrusion — Step-by-Step Working Principle** **Step 1 — Billet Placement** The cylindrical billet is loaded into a high-pressure chamber (typically rated **200–1500 MPa**) filled with a pressure-transmitting fluid (oil, glycerine, or polymer). **Step 2 — Application of Hydrostatic Pressure** The fluid pressure acts on **all surfaces** of the billet. This places the billet in a state of **triaxial compression** before deformation begins. Central burst defects and pre-existing voids are **collapsed**. **Step 3 — Extrusion Through Die** A ram (or in some variants, the pressure differential itself) forces the billet through a conical die. The radial compressive stress at the die entry is supplemented by the tensile stress developed due to the material acceleration through the reduction zone. **Step 4 — Combined Stress State at Die** At the deformation zone: - **Axial direction**: tensile stress due to material acceleration and pulling. - **Radial direction**: compressive stress due to die wall reaction + hydrostatic pressure. **Step 5 — Material Exits** The extruded product leaves the die at high velocity, retaining the combined strain history. The product exhibits **superior surface finish** and **uniform mechanical properties** compared to conventional extrusion. **Schematic (text description):** $$\text{Pressure Vessel} \rightarrow \text{Fluid (p = 500 MPa)} \rightarrow \text{Billet} \rightarrow \text{Die} \rightarrow \text{Extrudate}$$ with arrows showing $\sigma_r < 0$ (radial compression) and $\sigma_z > 0$ (axial tension). --- **Model Solution for (b):** The slab-method equilibrium along the die axis gives: $$\frac{d\sigma_z}{dz} = \frac{2}{r}\left[\sigma_r\tan\alpha + \mu\frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}\right]$$ Integrating from entry to exit of the die: $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{z,exit} &= \sigma_{z,entry} + \int_0^L \frac{2}{r}\left[\sigma_r\tan\alpha + \mu\frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}\right]dz \\ \sigma_{z,exit} &= -p + Y_f\ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + \frac{2\mu}{\sin\alpha}\,Y_f\ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \end{aligned}$$ Adding the back-tension $\sigma_b$ at the exit: $$\boxed{\sigma_{draw} = Y_f\ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)\left[1 + \frac{2\mu}{\sin\alpha}\right] + p\tan\alpha - \sigma_b}$$ **Term-by-term interpretation:** | Term | Physical Meaning | Effect on Drawing Stress | |---|---|---| | $Y_f\ln(A_0/A_f)$ | Ideal deformation work | Increases (always) | | $\dfrac{2\mu}{\sin\alpha}Y_f\ln(A_0/A_f)$ | Friction at die–billet interface | Increases | | $p\tan\alpha$ | Hydrostatic pressure assisting flow | **Decreases** | | $\sigma_b$ | Back-tension | **Decreases** the net pull required | > [!IMPORTANT] > The last two terms are the **combined-forming signature**: hydrostatic compression and tensile back-pull both reduce the **net** forming load and improve formability. --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper Style]** **a)** Compare and contrast **pure tensile forming, pure compressive forming, and combined tensile-compressive forming** with reference to stress states, formability, and typical industrial applications. *(7 Marks — CO2, Analyze Level)* **b)** A steel rod ($Y_f = 350$ MPa, $\mu = 0.08$, $\alpha = 12°$) is drawn from diameter **20 mm to 14 mm** under a hydrostatic pressure of **200 MPa** with back-tension **60 MPa**. Calculate: (i) true strain, (ii) ideal drawing stress, (iii) friction contribution, (iv) net drawing stress. *(7 Marks — CO3, Apply Level)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** | Aspect | Pure Tension | Pure Compression | Combined | |---|---|---|---| | Sign of principal stresses | All $\geq 0$ | All $\leq 0$ | Mixed signs | | Necking tendency | High | None | Suppressed | | Formability limit | Low ($\varepsilon^* = n$) | Very high | Very high | | Equipment load | Low | Very high | Moderate | | Examples | Stretching, bulging | Upsetting, forging | Hydrostatic extrusion, drawing w/ back-pressure | | Friction effect | Low | Critical | Critical but manageable | --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** -
