$Y_f = 350$ MPa, $\mu = 0.08$, $\alpha = 12°$, $p = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_b = 60$ MPa - $D_0 = 20$ mm, $D_f = 14$ mm **(i) True strain:** $$\varepsilon = 2\ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right) = 2\ln\!\left(\frac{20}{14}\right) = 2\ln(1.4286) = 0.7133$$ **(ii) Ideal drawing stress:** $$\sigma_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \times 0.7133 = 249.65\ \text{MPa}$$ **(iii) Friction contribution:** $$\Delta\sigma_{fric} = \frac{2\mu}{\sin\alpha}\,Y_f\cdot\varepsilon = \frac{2 \times 0.08}{\sin 12°}\times 249.65$$ $$= \frac{0.16}{0.2079}\times 249.65 = 0.7696 \times 249.65 \approx 192.1\ \text{MPa}$$ **(iv) Net drawing stress:** $$\sigma_{draw} = \sigma_{ideal} + \Delta\sigma_{fric} + p\tan\alpha - \sigma_b$$ $$= 249.65 + 192.1 + 200\times\tan 12° - 60$$ $$= 249.65 + 192.1 + 42.53 - 60$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{draw} \approx 424.3\ \text{MPa}}$$ **Percentage reduction:** $$r = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} = 1 - \left(\frac{D_f}{D_0}\right)^2 = 1 - (0.7)^2 = 0.51 = 51\%$$ This is well above the **30%** typical limit of pure tension — confirming the formability benefit of combined forming. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in Combined Forming problems:** > > 1. **Sign Confusion**: Forgetting that hydrostatic pressure $p$ is **compressive (negative)** and must be subtracted when computing equivalent stress. Use $p = -\sigma_h$, not $+p$. > 2. **Confusing true strain and engineering strain**: Always use $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_f)$, **not** $\Delta A/A_0$, in drawing/extrusion. > 3. **Missing the friction term**: In **sticking friction** condition, use $\tau = \mu Y_f/\sqrt{3}$ (Tresca) or $Y_f/2$ (constant shear). Forgetting this loses **3–4 marks**. > 4. **Not stating units**: Every numerical answer must carry **MPa** or **mm²**. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units. > 5. **Confusing back-pressure and back-tension**: Back-pressure is **compressive** (radial); back-tension is **tensile** (axial). Do not interchange. > 6. **Skipping the schematic**: Part (a) of 14-mark questions **always requires a labelled diagram**. Drawing earns at least **2 marks**. --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Combined Tensile & Compressive Forming** - ✅ Combined forming = **simultaneous tensile + compressive principal stresses**. - ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure** suppresses defects and reduces required forming force. - ✅ **Back-tension** reduces net drawing force and stabilizes deformation. - ✅ **Von Mises criterion** governs yielding; **mean stress** $p$ does not cause yielding. - ✅ Combined forming **extends formability limits** because compressive components prevent localized necking (Hill's criterion). - ✅ Key formula: $\sigma_{draw} = Y_f\ln(A_0/A_f)\left[1 + \dfrac{2\mu}{\sin\alpha}\right] + p\tan\alpha - \sigma_b$ - ✅ True strain in drawing: $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_f) = 2\ln(D_0/D_f)$. - ✅ Industrial examples: **Hydrostatic extrusion, wire drawing with back pressure, radial forging, stretch-bending**. - ✅ Friction factor $\mu$ ranges from **0 (frictionless)** to **0.5–1 (sticking)**. - ✅ Die semi-angle $\alpha$ typically **6°–20°** for drawing; **30°–60°** for extrusion. - ✅ Material **flow stress** $Y_f$ increases with strain (work hardening), strain rate, and decreases with temperature. - ✅ Combined forming allows **larger reductions per pass** (up to **70–80%**) compared to pure tension (~20–30%). - ✅ Always draw a **schematic** in Part B answers — KTU values visualization skills.

topics.title: Characteristic Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Forming** is a manufacturing process group in which the desired shape and dimensions of a workpiece are obtained through **permanent plastic deformation** of the material, without any significant change in mass or composition, by applying external forces (compressive, tensile, or shear) that exceed the material's yield stress.

The **Characteristic Features** of metal forming processes are the inherent attributes that collectively distinguish these processes from material-removal (machining) and material-addition (casting, welding) processes. These features govern process selection, die design, equipment selection, and product quality in industries such as automotive, aerospace, forging, sheet-metal, and fastener manufacturing.

The key characteristic features of metal forming include:

1. **Plastic (Permanent) Deformation** — Elastic recovery is negligible compared to total strain.
2. **Mass Conservation (Incompressibility)** — Volume remains constant during plastic flow (for bulk forming).
3. **State of Stress Dominance** — Multi-axial compressive or tensile stresses dominate.
4. **Strain-Rate Sensitivity** — Flow stress depends on strain rate (especially at elevated temperatures).
5. **Temperature Dependency** — Flow stress decreases as temperature increases.
6. **Tool–Workpiece Interface Friction** — Influences load, die wear, and material flow.
7. **Microstructural Evolution** — Grain refinement, work hardening, and recrystallization occur simultaneously.
8. **No Change in Mass / Composition** — Only shape is altered.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Rolling Play-Doh into a Chapati**
> Imagine a child rolling a ball of **Play-Doh (clay)** with a wooden pin (a rolling pin) on a flat table. The clay does not lose any of its mass, no chips are removed, and no new clay is added. The clay merely *flows* and reshapes itself under the pressure of the pin. If you press too fast or in the wrong direction, the clay tears; if you apply the right pressure and speed, you obtain a smooth, uniform **chapati**. This is exactly how metal forming operates — the workpiece (Play-Doh) is forced by tools (rolling pin) to undergo **plastic flow** into the desired shape while its mass and composition remain unchanged.

| Characteristic Feature | Play-Doh Analogy | Industrial Metal-Forming Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic deformation | Clay flattens permanently | Billet is forged/rolled into a new shape |
| Incompressibility | Volume of clay stays the same | $\Delta V \approx 0$ during plastic flow |
| Friction at interface | Clay sticks to the rolling pin | Die–workpiece friction affects load |
| Temperature effect | Warm clay flows easily | Hot working reduces flow stress |
| Strain hardening | Clay becomes stiffer when worked | Work hardening raises yield stress |

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard values are essential for solving KTU 2024 problems:
> - **Recrystallization Temperature** $\approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin)
> - **Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$) in cold working (with lubricant):** $\mu \approx 0.05$ – $0.10$
> - **Coefficient of Friction in hot working (without lubricant):** $\mu \approx 0.20$ – $0.50$
> - **Standard Engineering Strain in cold working:** $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$ (typically)
> - **Hot working temperature range:** $0.6 \, T_m$ to $0.9 \, T_m$ in Kelvin

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Characteristic Features

The eight characteristic features are explained step-by-step below with the **'Why' and 'How'** reasoning required for KTU board answers.

#### Feature 1: Plastic (Permanent) Deformation
- **Why it matters:** Only permanent shape change produces useful geometry. Elastic recovery (springback) is treated as a *defect* that must be compensated in die design.
- **How it occurs:** When applied stress $\sigma > \sigma_y$ (yield stress), dislocations glide, causing irreversible atomic-plane slip.

#### Feature 2: Mass Conservation / Incompressibility
- **Why it matters:** Allows the use of **Continuity Equations** to predict final dimensions.
- **How it works:** For bulk forming,
$$V_0 = V_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_0 \, h_0 = A_f \, h_f$$
- **Engineering use:** Predicting rolling exit thickness, forging flash, and extrusion die dimensions.

#### Feature 3: State-of Stress Dominance
- **Why it matters:** Multi-axial stress states either *promote* or *suppress* fracture.
- **How it works:** A **compressive hydrostatic component** suppresses crack initiation and propagation, making compressive forming operations (forging, rolling, extrusion) preferred for brittle materials.

#### Feature 4: Strain-Rate Sensitivity
- **Why it matters:** Flow stress increases with strain rate — critical for high-speed operations like drop forging and high-speed rolling.
- **How it works:** Quantified by the **Strain-Rate Sensitivity Exponent $m$** in:
$$\sigma_f = K \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m}$$

#### Feature 5: Temperature Dependency
- **Why it matters:** Determines whether the process is *cold*, *warm*, or *hot* working. Each regime gives different product quality and tooling load.
- **How it works:** Flow stress drops sharply with temperature; ductility rises.

#### Feature 6: Friction at the Tool–Workpiece Interface
- **Why it matters:** Friction increases forming load, causes die wear, produces surface defects, and creates *dead-metal zones*.
- **How it works:** Modelled by **Coulomb's Law** ($\tau = \mu p$) or **constant shear friction** ($\tau = m_s \, \tau_y$).

#### Feature 7: Microstructural Evolution
- **Why it matters:** Determines the final mechanical properties (strength, toughness) of the formed part.
- **How it works:** During deformation, grains elongate (cold work) or recrystallize dynamically (hot work).

#### Feature 8: No Change in Mass / Composition
- **Why it matters:** This is the **single most distinguishing feature** of metal forming vs. machining or casting.
- **How it works:** No chips, no flash material loss (ignoring flash in forging), no filler metal added.

### 2.2 Classification of Metal Forming Processes (Based on Temperature)

| Class | Temperature Range | Recrystallization | Flow Stress | Surface Finish | Typical Processes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Working** | $T < 0.3\,T_m$ | Does not occur | **High** | **Excellent** | Cold rolling, deep drawing, cold extrusion, wire drawing |
| **Warm Working** | $0.3\,T_m \leq T < 0.6\,T_m$ | Partial | Intermediate | Good | Warm forging, warm extrusion |
| **Hot Working** | $T \geq 0.6\,T_m$ | Occurs simultaneously | **Low** | Poor (oxidation) | Hot rolling, hot forging, hot extrusion, hot drawing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The most-tested distinction is between **Cold and Hot Working**. Memorize the comparative table — it is a guaranteed 3-mark question in every ESE.

### 2.3 Comparative Table: Hot vs. Cold vs. Warm Working

| Parameter | Cold Working | Warm Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Below $0.3\,T_m$ | $0.3\,T_m$ – $0.6\,T_m$ | Above $0.6\,T_m$ |
| Recrystallization | None | Partial | Complete |
| Yield Strength | High | Medium | Low |
| Ductility | Low | Medium | High |
| Forming Load | **High** | Medium | **Low** |
| Surface Finish | **Excellent** | Good | Poor (scale) |
| Dimensional Accuracy | **High** | Good | Low |
| Work Hardening | Yes | Slight | None (dynamic recovery) |
| Grain Refinement | No | Limited | **Yes** |
| Equipment Tonnage | **High** | Medium | **Low** |
| Tooling Cost | **High** (carbide) | Medium | Low (hot die steel) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-tested** and appear in 80%+ of KTU ESE papers on this topic.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | Recrystallization temperature (K) | K |
| 2 | $\sigma_f = K \, \varepsilon^{\,n}$ | Hollomon flow-curve equation | MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_f = K \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m}$ | Strain-rate dependent flow stress | MPa |
| 4 | $V_0 = V_f$ (Bulk forming) | Incompressibility / mass conservation | m³ |
| 5 | $h_f = h_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon}$ | Height reduction in compression | m |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ | True strain in compression | dimensionless |
| 7 | $J_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\,s_{ij}\,s_{ij}$ | Von Mises yield function | MPa² |
| 8 | $\tau = \mu p$ | Coulomb friction at die interface | MPa |
| 9 | $\tau = m_s \, \tau_y$ | Constant shear friction (Tresca) | MPa |
| 10 | $p_{avg} = \sigma_f \, (1 + \tfrac{\mu \, d}{3\,h})$ | Average forging pressure (slab method) | MPa |

> [!TIP]
> **In formulas, "$\mu$" denotes the coefficient of friction, "$\dot{\varepsilon}$" is the strain rate (s⁻¹), and "$K$" is the strength coefficient in MPa.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The characteristic features form the **decision-making framework** used by process engineers:

- **Automotive Crankshafts** → Hot forging (low flow stress, complex 3D shape).
- **Aluminium Beverage Cans** → Cold deep drawing (excellent surface, thin walls).
- **Steel Rails** → Hot rolling (large reductions, low load).
- **Copper Wires** → Cold drawing (high accuracy, work hardening raises strength).
- **Titanium Aerospace Forgings** → Warm working (avoids die-chilling, retains properties).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The recognition of these characteristic features is the **foundation** for selecting the right process, designing the right die, choosing the right press tonnage, and predicting the final product quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume Constancy and Final Dimension Prediction

**Problem Setup:** A cylindrical billet of initial height $h_0 = 100$ mm and diameter $d_0 = 50$ mm is upset in a cold-forging operation (no barreling assumed). The final height after compression is $h_f = 60$ mm. Find the final diameter $d_f$ and the true strain.

**Step 1 — State the volume conservation law** (a direct consequence of the *incompressibility* characteristic feature):
$$V_0 = V_f$$

**Step 2 — Express the volumes of a right circular cylinder:**
$$V_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_0^{\,2}\,h_0 \qquad V_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_f^{\,2}\,h_f$$

**Step 3 — Equate and cancel common factors $\pi/4$:**
$$\frac{\pi}{4}\,d_0^{\,2}\,h_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_f^{\,2}\,h_f$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**
$$d_0^{\,2}\,h_0 = d_f^{\,2}\,h_f$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $d_f$:**
$$d_f = d_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$d_f = 50\,\sqrt{\frac{100}{60}} = 50 \times 1.290 = 64.55\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate true strain (in height direction):**
$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{100}{60}\right) = \ln(1.667) = 0.511$$

**Step 8 — Engineering interpretation:**
The diameter grew by $29.1\%$ while the height reduced by $40\%$, *with no loss of material*, demonstrating the **mass conservation** characteristic feature.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Classification of Process by Temperature for a Given Material

**Setup:** AISI 1020 steel has a melting point $T_m = 1510\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 1783\,\text{K}$. Classify forming operations performed at (a) $300\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, (b) $700\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, and (c) $1100\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Convert all temperatures to Kelvin:**
$$T_a = 300 + 273 = 573\,\text{K}$$
$$T_b = 700 + 273 = 973\,\text{K}$$
$$T_c = 1100 + 273 = 1373\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute homologous temperature $T/T_m$:**
$$\left(\frac{T}{T_m}\right)_a = \frac{573}{1783} = 0.321$$
$$\left(\frac{T}{T_m}\right)_b = \frac{973}{1783} = 0.546$$
$$\left(\frac{T}{T_m}\right)_c = \frac{1373}{1783} = 0.770$$

**Step 3 — Compare with classification limits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T/T_m < 0.3 &\Rightarrow \text{Cold Working} \\
0.3 \le T/T_m < 0.6 &\Rightarrow \text{Warm Working} \\
T/T_m \ge 0.6 &\Rightarrow \text{Hot Working}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Assign classification:**
- (a) $0.321$ → **Warm Working** (just above the cold-working limit)
- (b) $0.546$ → **Warm Working**
- (c) $0.770$ → **Hot Working**

> [!NOTE]
> The threshold $0.3\,T_m$ is *not* fixed in the KTU syllabus; some textbooks use $0.4\,T_m$. Always state the threshold you adopt before solving.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hollomon Flow Curve for Average Flow Stress

**Setup:** A material has $K = 600$ MPa, $n = 0.20$, and is strained from $\varepsilon_1 = 0$ to $\varepsilon_2 = 0.50$. Compute (a) the **average flow stress** $\bar{\sigma}_f$ and (b) the **final flow stress** $\sigma_{f,\max}$.

**Step 1 — Write the Hollomon equation:**
$$\sigma_f = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum (final) flow stress:**
$$\sigma_{f,\max} = K\,\varepsilon_2^{\,n} = 600 \times (0.50)^{0.20} = 600 \times 0.8706 = 522.4\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Set up the average flow stress integral:**
$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1}\int_{\varepsilon_1}^{\varepsilon_2} K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}\,d\varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$\int K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}\,d\varepsilon = \frac{K\,\varepsilon^{\,n+1}}{n+1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the limits:**
$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{K}{(n+1)\,\varepsilon_2}\,\varepsilon_2^{\,(n+1)} = \frac{K\,\varepsilon_2^{\,n}}{n+1}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerical value:**
$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{600 \times (0.50)^{0.20}}{1.20} = \frac{522.4}{1.20} = 435.3\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation:**
The *average* flow stress is $16.7\%$ lower than the final flow stress — this is the value used in **forging-load estimation** because plastic work is integrated over the entire deformation history.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application of Characteristic Features

| Feature | Governing Equation / Principle | Numerical Test Case | Key Insight for KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Incompressibility | $V_0 = V_f$ | $h_0=100,\, d_0=50$ → $d_f=64.55$ mm | Used to predict final dimensions |
| True strain | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ | $\varepsilon = 0.511$ | Logarithmic, not linear |
| Hollomon flow curve | $\sigma_f = K\,\varepsilon^n$ | $K=600,\, n=0.20$ | Work-hardening behavior |
| Average flow stress | $\bar{\sigma}_f = K\varepsilon^n/(n+1)$ | $435.3$ MPa | Used for forging-load calculation |
| Recrystallization limit | $T_{rec} = 0.4\,T_m$ | AISI 1020: $713\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Boundary between cold and hot |
| Coulomb friction | $\tau = \mu p$ | $\mu = 0.1,\, p = 500$ MPa | $\tau = 50$ MPa |
| Strain-rate sensitivity | $\sigma_f = K\dot{\varepsilon}^m$ | $m = 0.1$ typical hot | Higher $\dot{\varepsilon}$ → higher $\sigma_f$ |
| Die pressure (slab) | $p_{avg} = \sigma_f(1 + \mu d/3h)$ | $d/h=1$ → factor $1.33$ | Friction raises forging load |

### 3.5 Complete Worked Example — Conceptual Integration

**Problem:** A copper billet ($T_m = 1085\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 1358\,\text{K}$, $K = 450$ MPa, $n = 0.30$, $\mu = 0.08$) is hot-forged at $800\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Initial dimensions: $h_0 = 80$ mm, $d_0 = 40$ mm. Final height: $h_f = 40$ mm. Calculate (i) classification temperature ratio, (ii) true strain, (iii) final flow stress, (iv) average flow stress, and (v) average forging pressure assuming friction factor $d/3h = 0.5$.

**Step-by-step:**

**Step i — Homologous temperature:**
$$T = 800 + 273 = 1073\,\text{K},\quad \frac{T}{T_m} = \frac{1073}{1358} = 0.790 \;\;>\; 0.6$$

∴ **Hot working confirmed.** ✓

**Step ii — True strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \ln(80/40) = \ln(2) = 0.693$$

**Step iii — Final flow stress (Hollomon):**
$$\sigma_{f,\max} = 450 \times (0.693)^{0.30} = 450 \times 0.8923 = 401.5\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step iv — Average flow stress:**
$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{401.5}{1.30} = 308.8\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step v — Average forging pressure (with friction):**
$$p_{avg} = 308.8 \times (1 + 0.5) = 308.8 \times 1.5 = 463.3\,\text{MPa}$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** Even with friction, the forging load is moderate because hot working reduces $K$ and $n$ substantially.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`).
> No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names.
> Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text.

### 4.2 Diagram 1 — Overview of the Eight Characteristic Features

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["METAL FORMING PROCESSES"] --> nodeB["CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["1 PLASTIC DEFORMATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["2 MASS CONSERVATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["3 STRESS STATE DOMINANCE"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["4 STRAIN RATE SENSITIVITY"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["5 TEMPERATURE DEPENDENCY"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["6 TOOL WORKPIECE FRICTION"]
    nodeB --> nodeI["7 MICROSTRUCTURAL EVOLUTION"]
    nodeB --> nodeJ["8 NO MASS OR COMPOSITION CHANGE"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["EXCEEDS YIELD STRESS"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["VOLUME CONSTANT V0 EQUALS VF"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["MULTIAXIAL COMPRESSION OR TENSION"]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["FLOW STRESS RISES WITH STRAIN RATE"]
    nodeG --> nodeG1["CLASSIFY COLD WARM OR HOT"]
    nodeH --> nodeH1["COULOMB OR SHEAR FRICTION"]
    nodeI --> nodeI1["RECRYSTALLIZATION OR WORK HARDENING"]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ1["ONLY SHAPE CHANGES NOT MASS"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 2 — Classification Flow by Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA0["START"] --> nodeA1["GIVEN PROCESSING TEMPERATURE T"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2["COMPUTE HOMOLOGOUS TEMPERATURE T OVER TM"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3{"IS T OVER TM LESS THAN 0 POINT 3"}
    nodeA3 -->|"YES"| nodeA4["COLD WORKING"]
    nodeA3 -->|"NO"| nodeA5{"IS T OVER TM LESS THAN 0 POINT 6"}
    nodeA5 -->|"YES"| nodeA6["WARM WORKING"]
    nodeA5 -->|"NO"| nodeA7["HOT WORKING"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA8["HIGH STRENGTH GOOD FINISH HIGH LOAD"]
    nodeA6 --> nodeA9["INTERMEDIATE PROPERTIES"]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA10["LOW LOAD SCALING GRAIN REFINEMENT"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Forming Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeB1["INPUT BILLET OR BLANK"] --> nodeB2["HEATING OR AMBIENT FURNACE"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3["DIE SET AND TOOLING"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4["PLASTIC DEFORMATION ZONE"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5["COOLING STAGE"]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6["FINISHED COMPONENT"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB7["FRICTION INTERFACE"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB8["STRAIN RATE FIELD"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB9["STRESS STATE FIELD"]
    nodeB7 --> nodeB10["DIE WEAR AND LOAD INCREASE"]
    nodeB8 --> nodeB11["FLOW STRESS MODULATION"]
    nodeB9 --> nodeB12["YIELD CRITERION CHECK"]
    nodeB10 --> nodeB13["PROCESS CONTROL LOOP"]
    nodeB11 --> nodeB13
    nodeB12 --> nodeB13
    nodeB13 --> nodeB3
```

### 4.5 Diagram 4 — Comparative Decision Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeC1["COLD WORKING T LESS THAN 0 POINT 3 TM"] --> nodeC1a["LOW TEMPERATURE"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b["NO RECRYSTALLIZATION"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1c["HIGH FLOW STRESS"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1d["EXCELLENT SURFACE"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1e["HIGH PRESS TONNAGE"]
    nodeC2["HOT WORKING T GREATER THAN 0 POINT 6 TM"] --> nodeC2a["HIGH TEMPERATURE"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b["DYNAMIC RECRYSTALLIZATION"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c["LOW FLOW STRESS"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2d["OXIDIZED SURFACE"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2e["LOW PRESS TONNAGE"]
    nodeC3["WARM WORKING 0 POINT 3 TO 0 POINT 6 TM"] --> nodeC3a["INTERMEDIATE TEMPERATURE"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3b["PARTIAL RECRYSTALLIZATION"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3c["MEDIUM FLOW STRESS"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3d["GOOD SURFACE"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3e["MEDIUM PRESS TONNAGE"]
```

### 4.6 Diagram 5 — Cause-Effect Chain of Characteristic Features

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeD1["APPLIED FORCE EXCEEDS YIELD"] --> nodeD2["PLASTIC FLOW BEGINS"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3["VOLUME CONSERVED"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD4["FRICTION AT INTERFACE"]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD5["FINAL SHAPE PREDICTABLE"]
    nodeD4 --> nodeD6["DIE PRESSURE RISES"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD7["MICROSTRUCTURE CHANGES"]
    nodeD7 --> nodeD8["WORK HARDENING OR RECRYSTALLIZATION"]
    nodeD8 --> nodeD9["MECHANICAL PROPERTIES OF PRODUCT"]
    nodeD5 --> nodeD9
    nodeD6 --> nodeD9
```

### 4.7 Diagram 6 — Process Selection Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeE1["REQUIRED SHAPE AND TOLERANCE"] --> nodeE2{"LARGE CROSS SECTION CHANGE"}
    nodeE2 -->|"YES"| nodeE3["BULK FORMING"]
    nodeE2 -->|"NO"| nodeE4["SHEET FORMING"]
    nodeE3 --> nodeE5["FORGING OR EXTRUSION OR ROLLING"]
    nodeE4 --> nodeE6["DEEP DRAWING OR BENDING OR STRETCHING"]
    nodeE5 --> nodeE7{"MATERIAL IS BRITTLE"}
    nodeE6 --> nodeE7
    nodeE7 -->|"YES"| nodeE8["USE COMPRESSIVE STRESS DOMINANT PROCESS"]
    nodeE7 -->|"NO"| nodeE9["USE STANDARD PROCESS"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define metal forming. List any four characteristic features of metal forming processes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Metal forming is a manufacturing process in which the desired shape of a metallic workpiece is obtained by **plastic deformation** under applied forces, without any change in mass or composition.

**Four characteristic features:**
1. **Plastic deformation** — only permanent shape change is useful.
2. **Mass conservation** — volume remains constant during plastic flow.
3. **State of stress** — multi-axial compressive or tensile stresses dominate.
4. **Strain-rate sensitivity** — flow stress depends on the rate of deformation.
5. **Temperature dependency** — flow stress decreases with temperature.
6. **Friction at tool–workpiece interface** — affects load and die wear.
7. **Microstructural evolution** — work hardening or recrystallization occurs.
8. **No change in mass or composition** — only shape changes.

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between hot working and cold working with respect to (i) recrystallization temperature, (ii) flow stress, and (iii) surface finish.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Recrystallization | Recrystallization occurs simultaneously with deformation | No recrystallization occurs |
| (ii) Flow stress | **Low** flow stress (material is soft) | **High** flow stress (material is hard) |
| (iii) Surface finish | Poor — surface oxidizes and forms scale | **Excellent** — bright, scale-free finish |

**Additional supporting points:** Hot working requires temperatures above $0.6\,T_m$ and gives good ductility; cold working is below $0.3\,T_m$ and produces work hardening with high dimensional accuracy.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Classify metal forming processes with neat sketches, and explain the characteristic features of metal forming processes in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical steel billet of diameter $50$ mm and height $80$ mm is hot forged at $1100\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to a final height of $40$ mm. The melting point of steel is $1500\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. For the material, $K = 500$ MPa, $n = 0.25$, and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.1$. Determine:
- (i) The homologous temperature and confirm whether the process is hot working.
- (ii) The final diameter of the forged component.
- (iii) The true strain.
- (iv) The average flow stress.
- (v) The average forging pressure using the slab method. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification of metal forming processes:**

1. **Based on temperature:**
   - Cold working ($T < 0.3\,T_m$)
   - Warm working ($0.3\,T_m \le T < 0.6\,T_m$)
   - Hot working ($T \ge 0.6\,T_m$)

2. **Based on type of stress:**
   - **Compressive:** forging, rolling, extrusion.
   - **Tensile:** wire drawing, deep drawing, stretch forming.
   - **Combined:** sheet-metal forming operations.

3. **Based on workpiece geometry:**
   - **Bulk forming** (large volume change): forging, extrusion, rolling, drawing.
   - **Sheet-metal forming** (thin-wall parts): bending, deep drawing, spinning.

4. **Based on tool geometry:**
   - **Two-die processes:** closed-die forging, swaging.
   - **Single-tool processes:** spinning, rolling, deep drawing.

**Characteristic features (elaborated as in Section 2.1 above).**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Homologous temperature:**
$$T = 1100 + 273 = 1373\,\text{K},\qquad T_m = 1500 + 273 = 1773\,\text{K}$$
$$\frac{T}{T_m} = \frac{1373}{1773} = 0.7745$$
Since $0.7745 > 0.6$, the process is **hot working** ✓.

**(ii) Final diameter** (using volume constancy):
$$d_f = d_0\sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 50 \times \sqrt{\frac{80}{40}} = 50 \times \sqrt{2} = 70.71\,\text{mm}$$

**(iii) True strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{80}{40}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931$$

**(iv) Final flow stress (Hollomon):**
$$\sigma_{f,\max} = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n} = 500 \times (0.6931)^{0.25} = 500 \times 0.9143 = 457.1\,\text{MPa}$$

**Average flow stress:**
$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{\sigma_{f,\max}}{n+1} = \frac{457.1}{1.25} = 365.7\,\text{MPa}$$

**(v) Average forging pressure** (slab method, with $d/3h$ factor given as $0.5$):
$$p_{avg} = \bar{\sigma}_f \times \left(1 + \frac{\mu\,d}{3h}\right) = 365.7 \times (1 + 0.5) = 365.7 \times 1.5 = 548.6\,\text{MPa}$$

**Final answers:**
- (i) $T/T_m = 0.7745$, hot working
- (ii) $d_f = 70.71$ mm
- (iii) $\varepsilon = 0.693$
- (iv) $\bar{\sigma}_f = 365.7$ MPa
- (v) $p_{avg} = 548.6$ MPa

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the classification of metal forming processes based on (i) temperature, (ii) type of stress, and (iii) type of product. Compare hot, warm, and cold working in a tabular form, listing at least six parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold-working operation is performed on an aluminium cylinder with initial dimensions $h_0 = 120$ mm, $d_0 = 60$ mm. Final height $h_f = 80$ mm. The material has $K = 350$ MPa, $n = 0.15$. The melting point of aluminium is $660\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the operation is done at $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Compute:
- (i) The homologous temperature ratio.
- (ii) The final diameter.
- (iii) The true strain and engineering strain.
- (iv) The maximum flow stress and the average flow stress.
- (v) The forming load if the final cross-sectional area is $A_f = 4000\,\text{mm}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

See Sections 2.2 and 2.3 above for the complete classification and comparison. A high-scoring answer will include:
- A clear three-way classification with examples.
- A neat comparative table with **at least six parameters** (temperature, recrystallization, flow stress, ductility, surface finish, dimensional accuracy, equipment tonnage, work hardening, grain refinement).
- Engineering examples for each working regime.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Homologous temperature:**
$$T = 27 + 273 = 300\,\text{K},\qquad T_m = 660 + 273 = 933\,\text{K}$$
$$\frac{T}{T_m} = \frac{300}{933} = 0.3216$$
This is in the **warm working** range, but since the problem states "cold working", the classification is taken as given (some KTU papers do this; the actual numerical calculation is what matters for marks).

**(ii) Final diameter** (volume constancy):
$$d_f = d_0\sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 60 \times \sqrt{\frac{120}{80}} = 60 \times \sqrt{1.5} = 60 \times 1.2247 = 73.48\,\text{mm}$$

**(iii) True strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln(1.5) = 0.4055$$

**Engineering strain:**
$$e = \frac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0} = \frac{120-80}{120} = 0.3333 = 33.33\%$$

**(iv) Maximum flow stress (Hollomon):**
$$\sigma_{f,\max} = 350 \times (0.4055)^{0.15} = 350 \times 0.8826 = 308.9\,\text{MPa}$$

**Average flow stress:**
$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{308.9}{1.15} = 268.6\,\text{MPa}$$

**(v) Forming load:**
$$F = p_{avg} \times A_f = 268.6 \times 4000 = 1{,}074{,}400\,\text{N} = 1074.4\,\text{kN}$$

**Final answers:**
- (i) $T/T_m = 0.3216$
- (ii) $d_f = 73.48$ mm
- (iii) $\varepsilon = 0.4055$, $e = 0.333$
- (iv) $\sigma_{f,\max} = 308.9$ MPa, $\bar{\sigma}_f = 268.6$ MPa
- (v) $F = 1074.4$ kN

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points — KTU 2024 Board Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** for the homologous temperature. *Always add 273 first.* Failure to do so = lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing true strain and engineering strain** in the same calculation. KTU examiners deduct $0.5$–$1$ mark per misuse.
> 3. **Using engineering stress in a forging problem.** Always use **flow stress $\sigma_f$**, not $\sigma = F/A_0$.
> 4. **Forgetting the friction factor** in the slab method ($1 + \mu d/3h$). This is a 2-mark trap.
> 5. **Wrong threshold for cold/hot working** ($0.3$ vs. $0.4$ vs. $0.6$). Always **state the threshold you adopt** at the start of the answer.
> 6. **Not sketching the classification diagram** in 7-mark theory questions. Sketches are **mandatory** and carry 2 marks in the KTU marking scheme.
> 7. **Computing only $\sigma_{f,\max}$ instead of $\bar{\sigma}_f$** for forging-load problems. The question usually asks for *average*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Characteristic Features of Metal Forming**

- ✅ Metal forming = **plastic deformation** of solid metal, no mass/composition change.
- ✅ **Eight characteristic features** to memorize: plastic deformation, mass conservation, stress-state dominance, strain-rate sensitivity, temperature dependency, friction, microstructural evolution, no mass change.
- ✅ **Volume constancy equation:** $V_0 = V_f$ — universal for bulk forming.
- ✅ **True strain:** $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ (not $\Delta h / h_0$).
- ✅ **Hollomon flow curve:** $\sigma_f = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n}$ — $K$ in MPa, $n$ dimensionless (typical $0.05$–$0.50$).
- ✅ **Average flow stress:** $\bar{\sigma}_f = K\,\varepsilon^{\,n} / (n+1)$.
- ✅ **Strain-rate sensitivity:** $\sigma_f = K\,\dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m}$ (typical $m$ = $0.05$–$0.30$).
- ✅ **Recrystallization temperature:** $T_{rec} \approx 0.4\,T_m$ (in Kelvin).
- ✅ **Cold working:** $T < 0.3\,T_m$ — high accuracy, high load, no recrystallization, work hardening.
- ✅ **Hot working:** $T \ge 0.6\,T_m$ — low load, dynamic recrystallization, scale formation.
- ✅ **Warm working:** $0.3\,T_m \le T < 0.6\,T_m$ — intermediate regime.
- ✅ **Friction at die interface:** Coulomb ($\tau = \mu p$) or Tresca ($\tau = m_s \tau_y$).
- ✅ **Forging pressure (slab method):** $p_{avg} = \bar{\sigma}_f(1 + \mu d/3h)$.
- ✅ **Always convert °C to K** in homologous-temperature problems.
- ✅ **Always state the threshold** you are using (cold/warm/hot working limits).
- ✅ **Always include neat sketches** in 7-mark theory answers (classification diagrams are compulsory).
- ✅ **Differentiate $\sigma_{f,\max}$ vs. $\bar{\sigma}_f$** — the question wording is the deciding factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capabilities and Application of Processes Like
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Capabilities and Application of Processes Like

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Forming** is defined as a family of **manufacturing processes** in which the desired shape and dimensions of a workpiece are obtained through **plastic deformation** of the material, without removal of any chips or flash. The material is forced to flow under the action of **external mechanical forces**, **thermal effects**, or a combination of both, to achieve permanent shape change while preserving mass.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET602 - Module 1), the broad **family of metal forming processes** is categorized based on the **type of stress state**, **temperature of working**, **type of equipment used**, and the **nature of the workpiece** (bulk vs. sheet). The phrase *"Capabilities and Application of Processes Like..."* refers to understanding **what each process can achieve** and **where each is deployed in industrial production**.

## 1.2 Classification of Metal Forming Processes

Metal forming processes are systematically classified into two major categories:

### A. Bulk Deformation Processes (Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing)
- Operate on workpieces with **high volume-to-surface-area ratio** (billets, blooms, ingots).
- Significant **mass redistribution** occurs in three dimensions.

### B. Sheet Metal Working Processes (Bending, Deep Drawing, Stretch Forming, Shearing)
- Operate on workpieces with **relatively small thickness compared to length and width**.
- Deformation is primarily in **two dimensions** (length and width), with thickness remaining nearly constant or slightly reduced.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Clay Sculpture vs. Origami"**
> 
> Imagine you are given a lump of **wet clay** and asked to shape it into a cup. You **press, push, and squeeze** the clay with your hands — you are essentially performing a **bulk forming operation** (similar to **forging or extrusion**). The clay's volume stays the same; only its shape changes because you forced it to flow.
> 
> Now imagine taking a **flat sheet of paper** and folding it into an airplane. You **bend, crease, and stretch** the paper — this is analogous to **sheet metal forming** like bending or deep drawing. The thickness of the paper stays roughly constant; you just redistribute it in space.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Metal Forming

The following **dimensional, mechanical, and process parameters** are universally used across all forming processes:

- **Flow Stress ($\sigma_f$)** — measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **True Strain ($\varepsilon$)** — dimensionless quantity.
- **Strain Rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$)** — measured in **s⁻¹**.
- **Forming Temperature** — categorized as **Cold Working (< 0.3 Tₘ)**, **Warm Working (0.3–0.5 Tₘ)**, and **Hot Working (> 0.5 Tₘ)**, where **Tₘ** is the **melting temperature in Kelvin**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1, students must remember the **temperature thresholds** for cold, warm, and hot working — this is a recurring 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bulk Deformation Processes — Capabilities and Applications

### 2.1.1 Forging

**Definition:** Forging is a metal forming process in which a workpiece is shaped by **compressive forces** applied through hammers, presses, or dies.

**Capabilities:**
- Can produce parts with **superior grain flow alignment**, leading to **high mechanical strength** and **fatigue resistance**.
- Suitable for **large components** (shafts, gears, connecting rods, turbine disks).
- Capable of producing parts weighing from **a few grams (precision forging)** to **several tons (open-die forging)**.

**Applications:**
- **Automotive industry:** crankshafts, connecting rods, steering arms.
- **Aerospace:** turbine disks, landing gear components, structural fittings.
- **Defense:** gun barrels, armor plates.
- **Power generation:** rotor shafts, flanges.

**Types of Forging:**
1. **Open-Die Forging** — flat or simple-shaped dies; used for large simple shapes.
2. **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging** — complex shaped dies; produces intricate geometries.
3. **Upset Forging** — increases cross-sectional area by compression along the axis.

### 2.1.2 Rolling

**Definition:** Rolling is the process of **reducing the thickness** (or changing the cross-section) of a workpiece by passing it between two **counter-rotating rolls**.

**Capabilities:**
- Highest **production rate** among all forming processes — continuous operation possible.
- Excellent **dimensional accuracy** and **surface finish**.
- Can reduce thickness from **~250 mm** (initial slab) to **< 0.1 mm** (thin foil).
- Can produce both **flat products** (plates, sheets, strips) and **structural sections** (I-beams, channels, rails).

**Applications:**
- **Steel mills:** production of slabs, blooms, billets, plates, sheets.
- **Aluminum industry:** foil production, beverage can stock.
- **Railway industry:** rails and sleepers.
- **Construction:** structural beams, channels, angles.

### 2.1.3 Extrusion

**Definition:** Extrusion is a process in which a billet is forced through a **die orifice** to produce a continuous product of uniform cross-section.

**Capabilities:**
- Can produce **complex cross-sectional profiles** that are difficult or impossible to achieve by rolling.
- Excellent for **long continuous lengths** of uniform section.
- Two main variants:
  - **Direct (Forward) Extrusion** — billet and product move in the same direction.
  - **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion** — die moves toward the stationary billet (lower friction).

**Applications:**
- **Aluminum industry:** window frames, door profiles, heat sinks.
- **Plastics industry:** pipes, tubes, sheets.
- **Food industry:** pasta, snack foods (analogous process).
- **Automotive:** gear blanks, drive shafts.

### 2.1.4 Drawing

**Definition:** Drawing is a process in which a workpiece is **pulled through a die** to reduce its cross-section and increase its length.

**Capabilities:**
- Produces **high-precision** products with **excellent surface finish**.
- Capable of **multi-pass reductions** to achieve very fine wire diameters (**< 0.01 mm**).
- Improves **mechanical properties** through strain hardening.
- Two main variants:
  - **Wire Drawing** — through round dies.
  - **Tube Drawing** — with or without mandrel.

**Applications:**
- **Electrical industry:** copper and aluminum wires.
- **Medical devices:** catheters, stents, guidewires.
- **Musical instruments:** strings.
- **Automotive:** control cables, tire reinforcements.

## 2.2 Sheet Metal Working Processes — Capabilities and Applications

### 2.2.1 Bending

**Definition:** Bending is the deformation of a sheet metal blank along a **straight or curved axis** to obtain an angular shape.

**Capabilities:**
- Produces simple angular changes (**V-bends, U-bends, channel bends**).
- **Springback** compensation is required for accuracy.

**Applications:** brackets, enclosures, frames, panels.

### 2.2.2 Deep Drawing

**Definition:** Deep drawing is the process of forming a **flat sheet blank** into a **hollow cup or box-shaped part** using a punch and die.

**Capabilities:**
- Produces **seamless hollow components**.
- Drawing ratio (**DR = blank diameter / punch diameter**) typically limited to **2.0–2.5** per stage.

**Applications:** beverage cans, automotive body panels, kitchen sinks, cookware.

### 2.2.3 Stretch Forming

**Definition:** Stretch forming involves clamping a sheet at its edges and **stretching it over a die** to produce large curved surfaces.

**Applications:** aircraft skins, automotive panels, architectural panels.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **examination-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme Part B numerical questions. All formulas use **\vert** instead of pipe characters to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Process | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Forging (Compression) | $F = K_f \cdot A$ | Forging force; $K_f$ = forge force constant (MPa), $A$ = projected area |
| 2 | Forging — Strain | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | True strain in compression; $h_0, h_f$ = initial/final heights |
| 3 | Rolling — Rolling Force | $F = \sigma_f \cdot L \cdot w$ | $L$ = contact arc length, $w$ = width, $\sigma_f$ = flow stress |
| 4 | Rolling — Contact Length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | $R$ = roll radius, $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$ |
| 5 | Rolling — True Strain | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | Same form as forging strain |
| 6 | Extrusion — Pressure | $p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | Extrusion pressure; $A_0, A_f$ = billet/product areas |
| 7 | Extrusion — Force | $F = p \cdot A_b$ | $A_b$ = billet cross-sectional area |
| 8 | Drawing — Drawing Stress | $\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | Drawing stress; $A_0, A_f$ = initial/final wire areas |
| 9 | Drawing — Max Reduction | $\Delta A_{\max} \approx 0.63 \, A_0$ | Empirical limit to avoid wire breakage |
| 10 | Bending — Minimum Radius | $R_{\min} \approx t \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}\right)$ | $t$ = thickness, $E$ = elastic modulus, $\sigma_y$ = yield stress |
| 11 | Deep Drawing — Drawing Ratio | $DR = \dfrac{D_0}{D_p}$ | $D_0$ = blank diameter, $D_p$ = punch diameter |
| 12 | True Stress–Strain | $\sigma_T = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$ | $K$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = strain-hardening exponent |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

The **capability of each process** directly determines its **industrial selection**:

- **Forging** is selected when **mechanical strength** is paramount (aerospace, automotive safety-critical parts).
- **Rolling** is selected for **high-volume, continuous production** (sheet metal supply industry).
- **Extrusion** is selected when **complex profiles** are required (aluminum architectural sections).
- **Drawing** is selected for **precision wires and tubes** (electronics, medical).
- **Sheet metal processes** are selected for **lightweight, large-area components** (automotive body, aircraft skin, appliances).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — True Strain in Compression (Forging)

**Problem Context:** A cylindrical billet of initial height $h_0$ and final height $h_f$ is upset in a forging operation. Derive the expression for true strain.

**Step 1:** Define instantaneous true strain as the ratio of infinitesimal change in height to current height.

$$
d\varepsilon = -\dfrac{dh}{h}
$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from initial height $h_0$ to final height $h_f$:

$$
\varepsilon = \int_{h_0}^{h_f} -\dfrac{dh}{h}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$
\varepsilon = -\ln(h) \,\Big\vert_{h_0}^{h_f}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute limits:

$$
\varepsilon = -\ln(h_f) + \ln(h_0) = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)}
$$

> This is the **fundamental formula** used in both forging and rolling strain calculations in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Roll Separating Force (Approximate Method)

**Problem Context:** A sheet of initial thickness $h_0$ and final thickness $h_f$ is passed through rolls of radius $R$. Derive the **horizontal (rolling) force component**.

**Step 1:** The contact length between the roll and sheet is derived from the geometry of the roll bite. Using the Pythagorean theorem on the contact arc:

$$
\left(R + \dfrac{h_0}{2}\right)^2 = \left(R + \dfrac{h_f}{2}\right)^2 + L^2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the approximation that $h_0$ and $h_f$ are very small compared to $R$, and that $L^2 \approx R \cdot \Delta h$:

$$
L \approx \sqrt{R \cdot (h_0 - h_f)} = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}
$$

**Step 3:** The vertical force component (acting on the roll from the strip) equals the average flow stress times the projected contact area:

$$
F_v = \sigma_f \cdot L \cdot w
$$

where $w$ is the width of the sheet.

**Step 4:** The horizontal force $F_h$ is related to $F_v$ by the friction angle $\beta$ at the roll-strip interface:

$$
F_h = F_v \cdot \tan\beta
$$

**Step 5:** The roll separating force (perpendicular to roll axis) is the resultant of horizontal and vertical components:

$$
F = \sqrt{F_v^2 + F_h^2} = \sigma_f \cdot L \cdot w \cdot \sqrt{1 + \tan^2\beta}
$$

**Final Result (simplified form used in KTU):**

$$
\boxed{F \approx \sigma_f \cdot L \cdot w = \sigma_f \cdot w \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Extrusion Pressure (Work-Energy Balance)

**Problem Context:** Derive the extrusion pressure for a billet of initial area $A_0$ being extruded through a die to final area $A_f$.

**Step 1:** The work done by the ram force $F$ over the stroke $L_s$ equals the ideal plastic deformation work plus friction work. For **ideal work** (no friction):

$$
W_{ideal} = F \cdot L_s = V \cdot \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma_f \, d\varepsilon
$$

**Step 2:** The volumetric strain is given by:

$$
\varepsilon_f = \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)
$$

(For steady-state extrusion, volume is conserved: $V = A_0 \cdot L_s$.)

**Step 3:** Assuming constant flow stress $\sigma_f$ (simplified for hot working where strain hardening is negligible):

$$
W_{ideal} = A_0 \cdot L_s \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the ram force:

$$
F = A_0 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Extrusion pressure is force divided by billet area:

$$
p = \dfrac{F}{A_0} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Process Selection Guide

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This matrix is **frequently asked** in Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize the key differentiators.

| Selection Criterion | Forging | Rolling | Extrusion | Drawing | Sheet Metal |
|---------------------|---------|---------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Stress State | Compression | Compression | Compression | Tension | Tension–Compression |
| Typical Product | Shafts, gears | Sheets, plates | Profiles, tubes | Wires, tubes | Panels, enclosures |
| Min Section Size | ~10 mm | ~0.1 mm | ~1 mm | ~0.01 mm | ~0.5 mm |
| Surface Finish | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| Tooling Cost | High (dies) | Very High | High | Low–Moderate | Moderate |
| Production Rate | Moderate | Very High | High | High | Moderate |
| Grain Flow | Excellent | Directional | Directional | Directional | Variable |
| Dimensional Accuracy | High | Very High | High | Very High | Moderate |

## 3.5 Workability vs. Temperature Selection Logic

The selection of **hot, warm, or cold working** is governed by:

$$
T_{hot} > 0.5 \, T_m, \quad T_{warm} = 0.3 \, T_m \text{ to } 0.5 \, T_m, \quad T_{cold} < 0.3 \, T_m
$$

- **Hot working** → Lower flow stress, higher ductility, **no strain hardening** retained, scale formation, poor surface finish.
- **Cold working** → Higher flow stress, limited ductility, **strain hardening retained**, superior surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
