# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define metal forming. List any **four bulk deformation processes** with one industrial application of each.

**Model Answer:**
Metal forming is a manufacturing process in which the desired shape is obtained by **plastic deformation** of the material under applied forces, without removal of material.

| Process | Industrial Application |
|---------|------------------------|
| Forging | Connecting rods in automobiles |
| Rolling | Steel sheets and plates |
| Extrusion | Aluminum window frames |
| Drawing | Copper electrical wires |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correct listing with applications)*

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **hot working** and **cold working** of metals. State the **temperature criteria** for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Temperature | Above $0.5 \, T_m$ | Below $0.3 \, T_m$ |
| Flow Stress | Low | High |
| Ductility | High | Limited |
| Strain Hardening | Not retained | Retained |
| Surface Finish | Poor (scale) | Excellent |
| Dimensional Accuracy | Low | High |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for temperature criteria + 1 for differentiation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 1 Choice 1]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain with neat sketches the **capabilities and applications** of the following processes:
   (i) Forging
   (ii) Rolling

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Forging — Capabilities:**
- Produces parts with **superior mechanical properties** due to favorable grain flow.
- Suitable for both **small precision parts** and **large heavy components**.
- Can handle a wide range of materials including **steels, titanium, nickel alloys**.
- Achieves **near-net-shape** components, minimizing subsequent machining.

**Applications:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, turbine disks, aircraft landing gear.

**(ii) Rolling — Capabilities:**
- **Highest production rate** among all forming processes (continuous operation).
- Produces flat products with **tight thickness tolerances**.
- Capable of producing sections from heavy plates (>250 mm) to thin foils (<0.1 mm).
- Can also produce **structural sections** (I-beams, channels).

**Applications:** Steel slabs and sheets, aluminum foil, railway rails, structural beams.

*(7 marks: 3 for forging explanation + 3 for rolling explanation + 1 for sketches)*

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A steel slab of **thickness 150 mm** and **width 800 mm** is hot-rolled in a **two-high rolling mill** with roll radius **400 mm**. After one pass, the thickness is reduced to **120 mm**. Calculate the:
   (i) True strain
   (ii) Contact length
   (iii) Approximate rolling force (assume flow stress = 200 MPa)

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Initial thickness: $h_0 = 150$ mm
- Final thickness: $h_f = 120$ mm
- Width: $w = 800$ mm
- Roll radius: $R = 400$ mm
- Flow stress: $\sigma_f = 200$ MPa

**(i) True Strain:**

$$
\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{150}{120}\right) = \ln(1.25) = 0.2231
$$

**(ii) Contact Length:**

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{400 \times (150 - 120)} = \sqrt{400 \times 30} = \sqrt{12000}
$$

$$
L = 109.54 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Approximate Rolling Force:**

$$
F = \sigma_f \cdot L \cdot w = 200 \times 109.54 \times 800
$$

$$
F = 17{,}526{,}400 \text{ N} \approx 17.53 \text{ MN}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for strain + 2 for contact length + 3 for rolling force with unit verification)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 1 Choice 2]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **capabilities and applications** of:
   (i) Extrusion
   (ii) Deep Drawing

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Extrusion — Capabilities:**
- Produces **complex cross-sectional profiles** in a single operation.
- Excellent for **long continuous lengths** of constant cross-section.
- Two variants: **Direct extrusion** (high friction) and **Indirect extrusion** (lower friction, finer tolerance).
- Suitable for **non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, Mg) due to lower required forces.

**Applications:** Aluminum window frames, automotive gear blanks, heat sinks, structural channels.

**(ii) Deep Drawing — Capabilities:**
- Produces **seamless hollow parts** with no weld lines.
- Achieves **high depth-to-diameter ratios** through multiple drawing stages with intermediate annealing.
- Capable of producing parts with **complex curved surfaces**.
- Limiting factor: drawing ratio $DR = D_0 / D_p \leq 2.5$ typically.

**Applications:** Beverage cans, automotive fuel tanks, kitchen sinks, cookware, battery casings.

*(7 marks: 3 for extrusion + 3 for deep drawing + 1 for diagrams)*

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A copper billet of **diameter 100 mm** is extruded to a **final diameter of 30 mm**. The flow stress is **180 MPa**. Calculate the:
   (i) Extrusion ratio
   (ii) Extrusion strain
   (iii) Extrusion pressure and ram force

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Initial diameter: $D_0 = 100$ mm $\Rightarrow A_0 = \pi \times 50^2 = 7853.98$ mm²
- Final diameter: $D_f = 30$ mm $\Rightarrow A_f = \pi \times 15^2 = 706.86$ mm²
- Flow stress: $\sigma_f = 180$ MPa

**(i) Extrusion Ratio:**

$$
R_{ext} = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{7853.98}{706.86} = 11.11
$$

**(ii) Extrusion Strain:**

$$
\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln(11.11) = 2.408
$$

**(iii) Extrusion Pressure:**

$$
p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = 180 \times 2.408 = 433.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Ram Force:**

$$
F = p \cdot A_0 = 433.5 \times 7853.98 = 3.40 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 3.40 \text{ MN}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for extrusion ratio + 2 for strain + 3 for pressure and force with units)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in KTU Exams — Loss of Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — e.g., leaving radius in **mm** when computing force in N. Always use **N and mm** consistently, or **convert to SI base units**.
> 
> 2. **Using engineering strain instead of true strain** — KTU 2024 Scheme specifically asks for **true strain** $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$. Using $\varepsilon = (h_0 - h_f)/h_0$ will give **zero marks**.
> 
> 3. **Not mentioning the temperature threshold criterion** for hot/cold/warm working — examiners look for the **0.3 Tₘ and 0.5 Tₘ** values explicitly.
> 
> 4. **Confusing extrusion ratio with drawing strain** — extrusion uses **area ratio**, drawing uses **stress-based formula**. Mixing them is a frequent error.
> 
> 5. **Omitting sketches** in 7-mark descriptive questions — even a **rough block diagram** earns 1–2 marks. Skipping diagrams loses easy marks.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (e.g., constant flow stress, negligible friction, plane strain) — KTU marking scheme awards **partial credit** for clearly stated assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary:**

### A. Process Identification by Stress State
- **Compression-dominated:** Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Upsetting.
- **Tension-dominated:** Drawing, Stretch Forming, Tensile Testing.
- **Bending-dominated:** Bending, Roll Bending, Flanging.
- **Combined stress:** Deep Drawing (tension + bending), Spinning.

### B. Temperature Thresholds (Critical Numbers)
- **Cold Working:** $T < 0.3 \, T_m$
- **Warm Working:** $0.3 \, T_m \leq T \leq 0.5 \, T_m$
- **Hot Working:** $T > 0.5 \, T_m$

### C. Master Formulas to Memorize
- **True Strain (Compression):** $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0 / h_f)$
- **Contact Length (Rolling):** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$
- **Rolling Force:** $F = \sigma_f \cdot L \cdot w$
- **Extrusion Pressure:** $p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(A_0 / A_f)$
- **Drawing Stress:** $\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(A_0 / A_f)$
- **Drawing Ratio (Deep Drawing):** $DR = D_0 / D_p$

### D. Industrial Application Quick Reference
- **Aerospace** → Forging (turbine disks), Rolling (sheet skin), Stretch Forming.
- **Automotive** → Forging (crankshafts), Deep Drawing (body panels), Rolling (chassis).
- **Construction** → Rolling (beams, rails), Extrusion (window frames).
- **Electronics** → Drawing (copper wires), Rolling (thin foils).
- **Medical** → Drawing (catheters, stents).

### E. KTU-Specific Points
- Always **mention process temperature regime** in descriptive answers.
- **Block diagrams** are mandatory for 7-mark questions.
- Use **NEP 2020** aligned answer structure: *Definition → Principle → Capabilities → Applications → Diagram → Conclusion*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tube Drawing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tube Drawing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Tube Drawing** is a cold-working (or warm-working) metal forming process in which a tube is pulled (drawn) through a die — with or without an internal mandrel/plug — to reduce its **outer diameter, inner diameter, and wall thickness simultaneously**, while improving dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and mechanical strength through strain hardening.

### Classification of Tube Drawing Processes

Tube drawing is broadly classified into **five (5) major variants** based on whether and how an internal mandrel/plug is used:

| Sl. No. | Process | Mandrel Used? | Wall Reduction |
|---------|--------------------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Tube Sinking** | ❌ No | Walls may thicken |
| 2 | **Drawing over Fixed Plug** | ✅ Yes (stationary) | Controlled precisely |
| 3 | **Drawing with Floating Plug** | ✅ Yes (self-positioned) | Controlled |
| 4 | **Drawing with Moving Mandrel**| ✅ Yes (long rod) | Controlled |
| 5 | **Drawing using Moving Die** | ✅ Yes (stationary long mandrel) | Controlled |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Toothpaste Tube" Analogy**
> Imagine squeezing a half-empty toothpaste tube. As you push from the back, the tube's diameter shrinks and its walls get thicker because nothing inside is supporting them — this is exactly **Tube Sinking**.
> Now imagine placing a thin pencil (the **plug**) inside the tube before squeezing. The pencil prevents the walls from collapsing inward and forces them to thin uniformly around it — this is **Drawing over a Plug**. The result is a tube with precise, reduced wall thickness and a clean inner bore.

### Key Engineering Metrics

- **Flow Stress (Yf):** Typically **280 MPa – 700 MPa** for steels, **100 – 250 MPa** for aluminum and copper alloys after strain hardening.
- **Die Semi-Angle (α):** Standard range **6° – 12°** (≈ 0.105 – 0.21 rad).
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** **0.04 – 0.10** with proper lubricant; can exceed **0.15** in poor lubrication.
- **Maximum Practical Reduction per Pass:**
  - Tube Sinking: **20 – 25%** (diameter reduction), limited by buckling.
  - Plug Drawing: **30 – 45%** (area reduction), much higher because the plug stabilises the tube.
- **Typical Products:** Bicycle frames, hydraulic cylinders, refrigeration copper tubes, gun barrels, heat-exchanger tubes, fuel injection lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Tube Drawing over Extrusion?**
> Extrusion produces tubes with **thicker walls and rougher surfaces**. Tube drawing is used as a **secondary finishing operation** to attain tighter tolerances (as low as **±0.025 mm**), smoother finishes (Ra **0.4 – 0.8 µm**), and superior mechanical properties through cold work.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanics of Each Process

#### (a) Tube Sinking (No Mandrel)
- The tube passes through a conical die; **no internal support**.
- Wall thickness often **increases** because material is displaced inward (the tube folds on itself to some extent).
- Used when **only the outer diameter** needs reduction and inner surface quality is non-critical (e.g., electrical conduit pipes).
- **Critical limit:** Tube **buckles** (wrinkles or collapses) if drawing stress exceeds the critical buckling stress.

#### (b) Drawing over a Fixed Plug (Stationary Mandrel)
- A precisely ground plug is held **stationary** at the die exit on a long shank.
- The tube is pulled over the plug.
- Outer diameter is set by the die; **inner diameter is set by the plug** → both wall thickness and diameters are precisely controlled.
- **Drawback:** Plug length is limited (typically < 5 m); not suitable for very long tubes.

#### (c) Drawing with a Floating Plug
- The plug is **not mechanically held** — it floats freely inside the deformation zone, supported by the tube wall.
- Self-positions by force balance.
- Suitable for **long continuous tubes**; used heavily in copper and brass tube production.
- Less precise than a fixed plug, but much more versatile.

#### (d) Drawing with a Moving Mandrel
- A long, slender mandrel rod extends the **full length of the tube**.
- The tube is drawn over the mandrel, and the mandrel moves with the tube.
- Used for **very long tubes** (e.g., gun barrels, hydraulic cylinders).
- Limitation: Mandrel can only be used once (it gets drawn into the tube).

#### (e) Drawing using a Moving Die (Stationary Long Mandrel)
- The long mandrel is held stationary while the die travels along the tube.
- Equivalent mechanical effect to the moving mandrel method.
- Allows reuse of the mandrel.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Code)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below assume:** cold working, constant average flow stress **Yf**, conical die of semi-angle **α**, coefficient of friction **μ**, plug diameter **d₀**.

| # | Process | Drawing Stress (σd) | Drawing Force (F) | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Tube Sinking** (no mandrel) | σd = Yf (1 + μ/α) ln(D₀/D₁) | F = σd · A₀ | D₀, D₁ = initial, final OD; A₀ = initial area |
| 2 | **Plug Drawing** (fixed/floating) | σd = Yf [ln(D₀/d₀) + 2μ/α] | F = σd · A₀ | d₀ = plug diameter (= final ID) |
| 3 | **Plug Drawing** (long cylindrical land) | σd = Yf [ln(D₀/d₀) + 2μL/d₀] | F = σd · A₀ | L = length of die–tube contact |
| 4 | **Maximum Reduction** (sinking, ideal) | rmax = 1 − e^(−1/2) ≈ **0.393** (≈ 39% in OD) | — | Theoretically when μ/α → 0 |
| 5 | **Maximum Reduction** (sinking, with friction) | rmax = 1 − exp[ −1 / (2(1+μ/α)) ] | — | More realistic bound |
| 6 | **Contact Length** (conical die) | L = (D₀ − d₀) / (2 tan α) | — | Used in friction terms |
| 7 | **Buckling Stress** (thin-walled tube) | σcr ≈ Yf · (t/r)² | — | t = thickness, r = mean radius |
| 8 | **Ideal Work** (no friction) | Wi = Yf · V · ln(A₀/Af) | — | Homogeneous deformation |

> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** ***"Sinking has 1, Plug has 2"*** — the friction multiplier is **1** in sinking and **2** in plug drawing because friction acts on **two surfaces** (die outer + plug inner).

### 2.3 Effect of Key Parameters (Real-World Utility)

| Parameter | Increase Causes | Engineering Consequence |
|-----------|-----------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Reduction per pass | ↑ Drawing stress | Risk of tube fracture if σd > UTS |
| Friction (μ) | ↑ σd (linearly) | Surface scoring, higher power needed |
| Die semi-angle (α) | ↑ α → μ/α ↓ → σd ↓ | But too small α → metal sticks in die |
| Flow stress (Yf) | ↑ σd | Annealing may be needed between passes |
| Plug length (L) | ↑ σd (linearly) | Sinking limits without plug |
| Number of passes | ↑ Total strain | Higher strength, lower ductility |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use:** Tube drawing is the **only economical method** to produce precision thin-walled stainless-steel tubes (e.g., for medical hypodermic needles, aircraft hydraulic lines). Each pass hardens the tube by **5–15%** in tensile strength; intermediate **annealing** is performed every 2–4 passes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Drawing Stress in Tube Drawing with a Fixed Plug (Full Slab Method)

We consider a tube being drawn through a conical die over a stationary plug of diameter **d₀**. We analyse a thin conical element of the tube in the deformation zone.

**Given / Assumed:**
- Initial outer diameter: **D₀**, Final outer diameter at die exit: **D_f = d₀ + 2t_f** (≈ plug diameter for thin walls)
- Plug diameter: **d₀** (constant — this is also the final ID)
- Die semi-angle: **α**
- Average flow stress: **Y_f**
- Coefficient of friction: **μ** (same at die and plug)

**Step 1 — Geometry of the deformation zone:**

At any cross-section located a distance *x* from the die exit, the outer diameter is:

$$
D(x) = d_0 + 2x \tan\alpha
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium of a thin element (Slab Method):**

Consider an annular slice of thickness *dx*. Forces acting on it:
- Drawing stress on the exit face: σ_d
- Resisting stress on the entry face: σ_d + dσ_d
- Radial compressive stress **p** acting on the outer conical surface (against the die)
- Friction force **μp** acting opposite to drawing direction on outer surface

Force balance along drawing axis (projecting all onto axis):

$$
A \, d\sigma_d = p \cdot \pi D \cdot dx \cdot \sin\alpha + \mu \, p \cdot \pi D \cdot \frac{dx}{\cos\alpha} \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

The second term's cosines cancel because we project friction onto the axis. Simplify:

$$
A \, d\sigma_d = p \cdot \pi D \left( \sin\alpha + \mu \right) dx
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d_0^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Yield criterion (Von Mises / Tresca):**

The radial pressure **p** is balanced by the axial drawing stress through the flow stress:

$$
p \approx \frac{Y_f}{\sin\alpha}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
A \, d\sigma_d = \frac{Y_f}{\sin\alpha} \cdot \pi D (\sin\alpha + \mu) \, dx
$$

$$
A \, d\sigma_d = Y_f \cdot \pi D \left(1 + \frac{\mu}{\sin\alpha}\right) dx
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute D(x) and integrate:**

With $D = d_0 + 2x\tan\alpha$, we have $dD = 2\tan\alpha \, dx$, so $dx = dD / (2\tan\alpha)$. Also, for small α, $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$ (radians), so:

$$
\frac{1 + \mu/\sin\alpha}{2\tan\alpha} \, dD \approx \frac{1 + \mu/\alpha}{2\alpha} \, dD
$$

For plug drawing, the dominant friction is on the inner surface too. By careful integration over both surfaces:

$$
\int_{\sigma_d}^{0} d\sigma_d = -Y_f \int_{D_0}^{d_0} \left[\frac{1}{D} + \frac{2\mu}{\alpha \, d_0}\right] \cdot \frac{dD}{2}
$$

(Simplified using constant d₀ in friction term because plug diameter is fixed.)

Carrying out the integration:

$$
-\sigma_d = -Y_f \left[ \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{D_0}{d_0} + \frac{\mu L}{d_0} \right]
$$

where contact length $L = (D_0 - d_0)/(2\tan\alpha)$. Replacing $L/d_0 = 1/\tan\alpha \approx 1/\alpha$ for small α:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_d = Y_f \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{d_0}\right) + \frac{2\mu}{\alpha} \right]}
$$

**Step 5 — Drawing Force:**

$$
\boxed{F = \sigma_d \cdot A_0 = Y_f \cdot A_0 \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{d_0}\right) + \frac{2\mu}{\alpha} \right]}
$$

with

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_0^2 - d_{i,\text{initial}}^2)
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A tube of initial outer diameter **D₀ = 50 mm** and inner diameter **dᵢ = 40 mm** is drawn over a fixed plug of diameter **d₀ = 30 mm**. The die semi-angle is **α = 6°**, average flow stress **Y_f = 280 MPa**, and coefficient of friction **μ = 0.08**. Calculate the **drawing force**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert α to radians:**

$$
\alpha = 6^\circ \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.1047 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ideal deformation term:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{d_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{50}{30}\right) = \ln(1.6667) = 0.5108
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the friction term:**

$$
\frac{2\mu}{\alpha} = \frac{2 \times 0.08}{0.1047} = \frac{0.16}{0.1047} = 1.5281
$$

**Step 4 — Compute drawing stress:**

$$
\sigma_d = 280 \, \text{MPa} \times (0.5108 + 1.5281) = 280 \times 2.0389 = 570.9 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute initial cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_0^2 - d_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(50^2 - 40^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(900) = 706.86 \, \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Compute drawing force:**

$$
F = \sigma_d \times A_0 = 570.9 \times 706.86 = 403{,}530 \, \text{N} \approx 403.5 \, \text{kN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** **F ≈ 403.5 kN**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Processes

| Feature | Sinking | Fixed Plug | Floating Plug | Moving Mandrel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wall reduction control | Poor (thickens) | Excellent | Very good | Excellent |
| Inner surface finish | Rough (die only) | Excellent (plug) | Good | Excellent |
| Maximum reduction per pass | 20–25% (dia) | 30–45% (area) | 25–40% | 30–45% |
| Tube length limit | Unlimited | Short (< 5 m) | Long | Very long |
| Tooling cost | Low | Medium | Medium-high | High |
| Tolerance achievable | Low | Very high (IT7) | High (IT9) | Very high |
| Typical product | Conduit pipe | Heat-exchanger | Copper refrig. tube | Gun barrel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Family Overview (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TUBE DRAWING PROCESS"] --> B["WITHOUT MANDREL"]
    A --> C["WITH MANDREL OR PLUG"]

    B --> B1["TUBE SINKING"]
    B1 --> B1a["Tube compresses inward"]
    B1 --> B1b["Wall thickness increases"]
    B1 --> B1c["Used for conduit pipes"]

    C --> C1["FIXED PLUG DRAWING"]
    C1 --> C1a["Plug held stationary at die exit"]
    C1 --> C1b["Both OD and ID precisely controlled"]
    C1 --> C1c["Used for heat exchanger tubes"]

    C --> C2["FLOATING PLUG DRAWING"]
    C2 --> C2a["Plug self positions in deformation zone"]
    C2 --> C2b["Suitable for long continuous tubes"]
    C2 --> C2c["Used for copper refrigeration tubes"]

    C --> C3["MOVING MANDREL DRAWING"]
    C3 --> C3a["Long mandrel moves with tube"]
    C3 --> C3b["Used for very long tubes"]
    C3 --> C3c["Used for gun barrels and hydraulic cylinders"]

    C --> C4["MOVING DIE WITH STATIONARY LONG MANDREL"]
    C4 --> C4a["Die travels along stationary mandrel"]
    C4 --> C4b["Mandrel is reusable"]
    C4 --> C4c["Alternative to moving mandrel"]
```

### 4.2 Deformation Zone Schematic — Fixed Plug Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DZ["DEFORMATION ZONE INSIDE DIE"]
        direction LR
        IN["INLET TUBE D0 di0"] -->|"Wall thins over plug"| MID["MIDDLE STAGE"]
        MID -->|"Outer diameter shrinks"| OUT["OUTLET TUBE D1 = plug diameter"]
    end

    P["FIXED PLUG d0"] -.->|"Supports inner wall"| MID
    DIE["CONICAL DIE semi angle alpha"] -->|"Compresses outer wall"| MID
    F["DRAWING FORCE F acts left to right"] -->|"Pulls tube"| OUT
```

### 4.3 Force-Analysis Element in Deformation Zone (Slab Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EL["THIN CONICAL ELEMENT OF TUBE dx THICK"] --> F1["Force sigma d plus d sigma d on entry face"]
    EL --> F2["Force sigma d on exit face"]
    EL --> F3["Radial pressure p acts perpendicular to die surface"]
    EL --> F4["Friction mu p acts opposite to drawing direction on conical surface"]
    EL --> EQ["Equilibrium: A d sigma d equals p sin alpha plus mu p times projected area"]
    EQ --> YC["Yield criterion: p approx Yf divided by sin alpha"]
    YC --> INT["Integrate from D0 to d0 to obtain drawing stress"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams represent the **functional/architectural flow** of the tube drawing process family. In a KTU exam, you should also draw a **labelled sectional view** of the die-tube-plug assembly showing the conical die, plug position, deformation zone, and direction of drawing force for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **tube sinking** and **tube drawing with a plug** in terms of wall thickness control and typical applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tube Sinking | Tube Drawing with Plug |
|--------|-------------|------------------------|
| Mandrel used | No | Yes (fixed/floating/moving) |
| Wall thickness control | Poor — wall tends to **thicken** | Excellent — wall **thins** uniformly |
| OD/ID precision | OD only | Both OD and ID precisely controlled |
| Typical products | Electrical conduit, structural pipes | Heat-exchanger tubes, hydraulic lines |
| Maximum reduction | Lower (risk of buckling) | Higher |

*(1 mark for concept identification, 1 mark for wall-thickness difference, 1 mark for example/application)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Why is a **floating plug** preferred over a **fixed plug** for drawing long copper tubes? Mention any two reasons.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Length Limitation:** A fixed plug requires a long shank extending outside the die, limiting tube length to ~5 m. A floating plug self-positions inside the deformation zone, allowing **continuous drawing of long coils** (1.5 marks).
2. **Reduced Friction & Better Surface:** The floating plug's position adjusts dynamically to minimise friction and avoid scoring, producing superior inner surface finish suitable for refrigeration and plumbing tubes (1.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Choose **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Plug Drawing Analysis

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **drawing stress in tube drawing with a fixed plug**, clearly stating all assumptions and showing the slab-method equilibrium. State the final equation for drawing force. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Define geometry: initial OD $D_0$, plug diameter $d_0$, die semi-angle $\alpha$, contact length $L$. (1 mark)
2. Slab equilibrium of element: $A \, d\sigma_d = p \sin\alpha \, dA_{\text{proj}} + \mu p \, dA_{\text{fric}}$. (2 marks)
3. Apply yield criterion: $p = Y_f/\sin\alpha$. (1 mark)
4. Substitute and integrate from $D_0$ to $d_0$. (2 marks)
5. Final result: $\sigma_d = Y_f [\ln(D_0/d_0) + 2\mu/\alpha]$ and $F = \sigma_d \cdot A_0$. (1 mark)

**(b)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A brass tube of initial dimensions **OD = 60 mm, ID = 50 mm** is drawn over a fixed plug of diameter **40 mm**. The die semi-angle is **8°**, average flow stress **Y_f = 350 MPa**, and **μ = 0.06**. Determine the **(i) drawing stress** and **(ii) drawing force**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert angle:**

$$
\alpha = 8^\circ \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.1396 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Ideal term:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{d_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{60}{40}\right) = \ln(1.5) = 0.4055
$$

**Step 3 — Friction term:**

$$
\frac{2\mu}{\alpha} = \frac{2 \times 0.06}{0.1396} = \frac{0.12}{0.1396} = 0.8596
$$

**Step 4 — Drawing stress:**

$$
\sigma_d = 350 \times (0.4055 + 0.8596) = 350 \times 1.2651 = 442.8 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Initial area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(60^2 - 50^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1100) = 863.94 \, \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Drawing force:**

$$
F = 442.8 \times 863.94 = 382{,}580 \, \text{N} \approx \mathbf{382.6 \, kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often use **D₁** (final OD) in place of **d₀** (plug diameter) inside the log term. **D₀/d₀ ≠ D₀/D₁** in plug drawing — always use plug diameter.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert **α from degrees to radians** leads to the friction term being off by a factor of ~57. Always use **rad** in sin, tan, cot.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Using $A_0 = \pi D_0^2/4$ (full circle) instead of the **annular** area $\pi(D_0^2 - d_i^2)/4$. Lose 1–2 marks easily.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Reporting force in N without converting to a sensible unit (kN or MN) for a 14-mark numerical.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain any **four** types of tube drawing processes and state their relative merits and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

| Type | Sketch Description | Merits | Limitations |
|------|--------------------|--------|--------------|
| Sinking | Tube through conical die, no plug | Simple tooling, unlimited length | Wall thickens, low precision |
| Fixed plug | Plug on shank at die exit | High precision, both OD & ID controlled | Short tubes only |
| Floating plug | Plug self-positioned in zone | Long tubes, continuous production | Lower precision than fixed |
| Moving mandrel | Long rod through full tube length | Very long tubes possible | Mandrel single-use, expensive |

*(2 marks per correctly explained process, 1 mark for comparison table)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **maximum reduction in tube sinking**. Derive the expression for the **theoretical maximum reduction** when friction effects are negligible. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why a maximum exists:**
Without a plug, the tube can buckle (wrinkle) under the radial compressive stress in the deformation zone. There is a critical reduction beyond which the tube becomes unstable. (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Drawing stress (frictionless):**

$$
\sigma_d = Y_f \ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{D_1}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Stability criterion:**
For the tube not to fracture or buckle, the drawing stress should not exceed the material's flow stress. Setting $\sigma_d \le Y_f$ (with safety factor of 0.5 for buckling):

$$
Y_f \ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{D_1}\right) = 0.5 \, Y_f
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{D_0}{D_1}\right) = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for reduction:**

$$
\frac{D_0}{D_1} = e^{1/2} = 1.6487
$$

$$
r_{\max} = 1 - \frac{D_1}{D_0} = 1 - e^{-1/2} = 1 - 0.6065 = \mathbf{0.3935 \; \text{or} \; 39.35\%}
$$

*(1 mark criterion, 2 marks derivation, 2 marks final number)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake:** Confusing **diameter reduction** with **area reduction**. For thin-walled tubes, area reduction ≈ 2 × diameter reduction.
> - **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the safety factor of **0.5** when applying the buckling criterion. Without it, the theoretical max becomes 63%, which is unrealistically high.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Tube Drawing**

- ✅ **Tube Drawing** is a **cold-working** process used for precise dimensional control of tubes.
- ✅ **5 variants:** Sinking, Fixed Plug, Floating Plug, Moving Mandrel, Moving Die.
- ✅ **Plug Drawing** controls **both** OD and ID; **Sinking** controls only OD and walls thicken.
- ✅ **Master Formula (Plug):** $\sigma_d = Y_f[\ln(D_0/d_0) + 2\mu/\alpha]$; Force $F = \sigma_d \cdot A_0$.
- ✅ **Master Formula (Sinking):** $\sigma_d = Y_f(1 + \mu/\alpha)\ln(D_0/D_1)$.
- ✅ **"1 for Sinking, 2 for Plug"** — friction multiplier mnemonic.
- ✅ **Maximum reduction (sinking, ideal):** $r_{\max} = 1 - e^{-1/2} \approx 39.35\%$.
- ✅ **Practical reduction:** Sinking ~20–25% (diameter), Plug ~30–45% (area).
- ✅ Always convert **α to radians**: $\alpha_{\text{rad}} = \alpha_{\text{deg}} \times \pi/180$.
- ✅ Always use the **annular area** $A_0 = \pi(D_0^2 - d_i^2)/4$ — never full-circle area.
- ✅ **Plug diameter d₀ = final inner diameter** of the tube.
- ✅ Typical **die semi-angle** range: **6°–12°**.
- ✅ Typical **μ range** with lubricant: **0.04 – 0.10**.
- ✅ **Intermediate annealing** required every 2–4 passes to restore ductility.
- ✅ Drawing improves **surface finish (Ra 0.4–0.8 µm)** and **dimensional tolerance (IT7)**.
- ✅ Common defects: **scoring, eccentricity, centre-burst, wall thinning, buckling**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Drawing or Cupping and Redrawing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deep Drawing or Cupping and Redrawing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Deep Drawing (also called Cupping)** is a sheet metal forming process in which a flat blank of sheet metal is drawn over a die and into a cavity by means of a punch, producing a hollow, three-dimensional part (typically cup-shaped) with a depth greater than the diameter, without any intentional change in the sheet thickness. The process relies on the **plastic flow** of the material from the flange region (the unsupported portion outside the die cavity) over the die radius and into the die cavity under the action of a punch.

**Redrawing** is a secondary deep drawing operation performed on a previously drawn cup to further reduce its diameter (and/or change its proportions), thereby increasing the depth-to-diameter ratio of the final part. Because the LDR (Limiting Drawing Ratio) of a single-stage draw is limited (typically ~2.0 for steels), multi-stage drawing is required to produce deep parts such as beverage cans, cartridge cases, and automotive panels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Drawing** = sheet thickness is *intentionally* preserved (pure radial drawing).
> - **Ironing** = sheet thickness is *intentionally* reduced (stretching over a tight clearance).
> These are often combined in commercial presses (e.g., can-making uses draw + iron + redraw).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine taking a **circular piece of dough (chapati)** on a flat plate and pushing a **cylindrical glass** straight down into the centre of it. The dough around the glass is forced to flow up the sides of the glass, forming a "cup" of dough. The portion of dough under the glass (the bottom of the cup) doesn't move much, while the outer ring (the **flange**) flows inward and upward to become the walls of the cup.

In **deep drawing of metal**:
- The **dough** = sheet metal blank
- The **plate** = die
- The **glass** = punch
- The **flange** = the part being pulled into the cavity (this is where most of the deformation occurs)
- If you push too hard, the dough **tears** near the rim (just like metal fails in deep drawing if draw force is too high).

**Redrawing** is like taking that first cup of dough and pushing a *smaller* glass into it to make the cup even deeper and narrower — you cannot do this in one step because the dough would tear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 emphasizes: **(i) Drawing Ratio, (ii) Drawing Force, (iii) Blank Holder Force, (iv) Earing and Wrinkling defects, and (v) Redrawing (direct and indirect methods).** Master these five pillars.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics Used

- **Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)** = Maximum blank diameter / punch diameter = **2.0 to 2.5** for low-carbon steel.
- **Drawing Force** = typically **30–60% of blank holder force** magnitude.
- **Blank Holder Pressure** = **0.7 to 1.4 MPa** for steel, **0.3 to 0.7 MPa** for aluminum.
- **Die Corner Radius (r_d)** = **5 to 15 × sheet thickness (t)**.
- **Punch Nose Radius (r_p)** = **3 to 10 × t**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Deep Drawing Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The process is executed in the following logical sequence:

1. **Blanking:** A circular blank of diameter **D₀** is cut from sheet metal of thickness **t**.
2. **Placement:** The blank is positioned on the die cavity (radius = punch radius approximately), resting on the die with the **blank holder** pressing down on the flange.
3. **Initial Contact:** The punch contacts the centre of the blank; this region becomes the **cup bottom** (no further deformation here).
4. **Drawing Phase:** As the punch descends, the **flange** (outer ring) is forced to flow radially inward over the **die corner radius** and down into the die cavity, forming the **wall** of the cup.
5. **Completion:** When the punch has travelled a distance equal to the desired cup depth, the part is stripped off and the cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain States:**
> - **Flange region:** Biaxial tension + compression through thickness (drawing-in).
> - **Die corner:** Bending + unbending (additional strain).
> - **Wall region:** Uniaxial tensile strain (height elongation).
> - **Bottom region:** Essentially no strain (rigid body motion).
> - **Punch nose region:** Bending over punch radius (additional strain).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Failure — Mechanics of Wrinkling and Tearing

| Phenomenon | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| **Flange Wrinkling** | Compressive hoop stress in flange (outside fibres) when blank holder force is too low | Increase blank holder force; add draw beads |
| **Wall Tearing (Bottom-corner fracture)** | Tensile stress exceeds material UTS, typically near punch nose radius | Reduce draw (lower DR); increase punch radius; use better lubrication |
| **Earing** | Anisotropy in rolled sheet (planar anisotropy, Δr ≠ 0) | Use balanced sheet; rotate blank 90° between operations |
| **Wrinkling in Wall** | Axial compression in unsupported wall (if die clearance > 1.4 t) | Reduce clearance; use ironing |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DR = \dfrac{D_0}{D_p}$ | **Drawing Ratio** (blank dia / punch dia) | Dimensionless; should be < LDR |
| 2 | $LDR \approx 2.0$ for steel | **Limiting Drawing Ratio** (max possible in one draw) | Empirical; depends on material |
| 3 | $F_{draw} = \pi \cdot D_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{draw}$ | **Total Drawing Force** (Sievekes/Eymann) | N; where $\sigma_{draw} \approx 0.7 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$ for steel |
| 4 | $F_{draw} = \pi \cdot (D_0 - D_p) \cdot t \cdot Y_{avg} \cdot \left(1.1 \right)$ | **Siebel's Drawing Force** (more accurate) | N; includes strain hardening |
| 5 | $\sigma_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(DR)$ | **Average drawing stress** (Sievekes) | Pa; $\bar{Y}$ = mean flow stress |
| 6 | $F_{BH} = 0.01 \text{ to } 0.02 \cdot (D_0^2 - D_p^2) \cdot p$ | **Blank Holder Force** (empirical rule) | N; $p$ = blank holder pressure |
| 7 | $c = (D_d - D_p) / 2$ | **Die-Punch Clearance (single wall)** | m; $c \approx 1.1 \cdot t$ (no ironing); $c \approx t$ (with ironing) |
| 8 | $r_d = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.15) \cdot D_d$ | **Die corner radius (empirical)** | m; or 5–15 × t |
| 9 | $r_p = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot D_p$ | **Punch nose radius (empirical)** | m; or 3–10 × t |
| 10 | $\Delta r = \dfrac{(r_0 + r_{90}) - (r_{45} + r_{45})}{2}$ | **Planar Anisotropy** (earing tendency) | Dimensionless; $\Delta r = 0$ ⇒ no earing |
| 11 | $R_{redraw} = \dfrac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}$ | **Redrawing Ratio** | $D_1$ = current dia, $D_2$ = new dia |
| 12 | $LDR_{total} = LDR_1 \times LDR_2 \times LDR_3 \cdots$ | **Multi-stage LDR** (approximate) | For n draws, total ≈ 2.0ⁿ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Winning Tip:** KTU 2024 numerical problems *frequently* ask you to compute $F_{draw}$ given blank diameter, punch diameter, sheet thickness, and a material's UTS/mean flow stress. Always state that you are using **Sievekes' formula** or **Siebel's formula** explicitly — examiners give credit for named formulas.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deep drawing + redrawing is the **workhorse process** of the **packaging industry** (beverage cans, food tins, aerosol cans), the **automotive industry** (fuel tanks, body panels via deep drawing of sheet), the **ammunition/defense industry** (cartridge cases, shell casings), and the **cookware industry** (stainless steel pots and pans). Modern beverage cans go through a **draw + iron + redraw + iron** sequence, taking a flat aluminum blank to a final can in 3–4 stages within milliseconds at production rates of **> 500 cans/minute**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Drawing Force (Sievekes / Sachs Approach)

We derive the total drawing force for a cylindrical cup drawn in one operation. The approach is to compute the **radial drawing stress** at the die corner and integrate.

**Setup:**
- Blank diameter: $D_0$
- Punch diameter: $D_p$
- Sheet thickness: $t$
- Current flange outer diameter: $D$ (varies from $D_0$ down to $D_p$ during draw)
- Mean flow stress of material at the die corner: $\bar{Y}$
- Friction coefficient at die corner and blank holder: $\mu$ (combined ≈ 0.05 to 0.1)

**Step 1 — True strain in the flange:**
The flange is reduced in diameter from $D_0$ to $D$. The circumferential strain is the dominant plastic strain:

$$
\varepsilon_\theta = \ln\left(\dfrac{\pi D / 2}{\pi D_0 / 2}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{D}{D_0}\right)
$$

Since $D < D_0$, this strain is **negative** (compressive in hoop), but its magnitude is what drives the radial drawing stress.

**Step 2 — Strain hardening update (mean flow stress):**
Assuming a power-law hardening material $\bar{\sigma} = K \varepsilon^n$, the mean flow stress at strain $\varepsilon$ is:

$$
\bar{Y} = \dfrac{K \cdot \varepsilon^n}{1 + n}
$$

**Step 3 — Radial drawing stress at the die corner (Sievekes):**
Using equilibrium of a flange element with bending over die radius and friction:

$$
\sigma_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(DR)
$$

The factor **1.1** accounts for bending + unbending over the die corner and friction. For a *first approximation*, $\sigma_{draw} \approx 0.7 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$ for low-carbon steel.

**Step 4 — Total drawing force:**
The drawing force acts on the cylindrical wall of area $\pi \cdot D_p \cdot t$:

$$
F_{draw} = \sigma_{draw} \cdot A_{wall} = \sigma_{draw} \cdot \pi \cdot D_p \cdot t
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting:**

$$
F_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(DR) \cdot \pi \cdot D_p \cdot t
$$

This is the **Sievekes drawing force equation**, valid for cylindrical deep drawing without ironing.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Die Radius Correction:** A more refined version (Eymann) adds a term for die corner radius $r_d$:
> $$\sigma_{draw} = 1.1 \bar{Y} \left[ \ln(DR) + \dfrac{\mu}{2} \cdot \dfrac{D_p}{r_d} \right]$$
> which shows that **smaller die radii** dramatically increase the drawing stress (because of increased bending + friction at the corner).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)

The **LDR** is the maximum drawing ratio beyond which the wall will tear. It is determined by the condition:

$$
\sigma_{draw,max} = \sigma_{UTS}
$$

Setting $\sigma_{draw} = \sigma_{UTS}$ in the Sievekes equation:

$$
1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(LDR) = \sigma_{UTS}
$$

For a power-law hardening material with $n \approx 0.2$ and $K/\sigma_{UTS} \approx 1.4$ (typical for low-carbon steel), this gives:

$$
LDR \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 2.2
$$

For aluminum alloys (less strain-hardening capacity, $n \approx 0.1$): $LDR \approx 1.8$ to 2.0. For stainless steel (high $n \approx 0.4$): $LDR$ can reach 2.3 to 2.5.

## 3.3 Derivation of Blank Holder Force (BHF)

The blank holder must apply a compressive force on the flange sufficient to **suppress wrinkling** but not so large as to **cause tearing**. The wrinkling tendency is governed by a critical compressive hoop stress.

**Critical wrinkle-suppression pressure** (Hill's buckling criterion for a circular plate clamped at inner edge):

$$
p_{cr} = \dfrac{c \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^3}{(1-\nu^2) \cdot k}
$$

where $c$ is a constant (≈ 0.33 for circular blank), $E$ is Young's modulus, $t$ is thickness, $D$ is current flange diameter, $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio, and $k$ depends on blank holder geometry.

For practical purposes, KTU uses the empirical **rule of thumb**:

$$
F_{BH} = (0.01 \text{ to } 0.02) \cdot (D_0^2 - D_p^2) \cdot p
$$

where $p$ is the blank holder pressure (≈ **0.7 to 1.4 MPa** for steel).

**Step-by-step BHF for a typical problem:**
- $D_0 = 200$ mm, $D_p = 100$ mm, $p = 1.0$ MPa.
- $F_{BH} = 0.015 \cdot (200^2 - 100^2) \cdot 1.0$
- $F_{BH} = 0.015 \cdot (40000 - 10000) \cdot 1.0$
- $F_{BH} = 0.015 \cdot 30000 = 450 \text{ N (per unit pressure multiplier)}$

## 3.4 Redrawing — Mechanics and Force

In **redrawing**, the cup is inverted (or used as-is in indirect redrawing) and pulled over a smaller die. There are two methods:

| Method | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Forward) Redraw** | Cup is placed in die; punch pushes down, walls are drawn in directly | Most common; simple tooling |
| **Indirect (Reverse) Redraw** | Cup is inverted and pushed *up* through a die; cup bottom becomes the new bottom | Reduces wall bending strain, used for deep parts |

**Redrawing Force (per stage n):**

$$
F_{redraw,n} = \pi \cdot D_{p,n} \cdot t \cdot 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(RR_n)
$$

where $RR_n = D_{p,n-1} / D_{p,n}$ is the redraw ratio at stage $n$.

**Multi-stage Drawing:** For a part requiring total reduction ratio $R_{total} = D_0 / D_{final}$, choose $n$ stages such that:

$$
LDR_{total} \approx LDR_1 \times LDR_2 \times \cdots \times LDR_n
$$

**Optimization rule (KTU-favorite):** Make all stages have **equal drawing ratios**, i.e.,

$$
R_1 = R_2 = \cdots = R_n = R_{total}^{1/n}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A circular blank of **200 mm diameter** and **1.2 mm thickness** made of **low-carbon steel** ($\sigma_{UTS} = 350$ MPa, mean flow stress $\bar{Y} = 280$ MPa) is to be deep-drawn into a cup with **punch diameter = 100 mm**. Compute the drawing force and check if the operation is feasible in a single draw.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute drawing ratio.

$$
DR = \dfrac{D_0}{D_p} = \dfrac{200}{100} = 2.0
$$

Since $DR = 2.0 \leq LDR_{steel} = 2.0$, the operation is at the *limiting* edge — feasible in a single draw, but risky. **Recommendation: use a redraw stage for safety.**

**Step 2:** Compute drawing stress (Sievekes).

$$
\sigma_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(DR) = 1.1 \cdot 280 \cdot \ln(2.0)
$$

$$
\sigma_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot 280 \cdot 0.693 = 213.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Check against UTS.

$$
\sigma_{draw} = 213.4 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{UTS} = 350 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Safety factor:** $350 / 213.4 = 1.64$ (acceptable).

**Step 4:** Compute drawing force.

$$
F_{draw} = \sigma_{draw} \cdot \pi \cdot D_p \cdot t
$$

$$
F_{draw} = 213.4 \times 10^6 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.100 \cdot 0.0012
$$

$$
F_{draw} = 213.4 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.77 \times 10^{-4} = 80,452 \text{ N} \approx 80.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute blank holder force (assume $p = 1.0$ MPa).

$$
F_{BH} = 0.015 \cdot (D_0^2 - D_p^2) \cdot p = 0.015 \cdot (0.20^2 - 0.10^2) \cdot 10^6
$$

$$
F_{BH} = 0.015 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 10^6 = 450 \text{ N}
$$

(Note: the dimensionless 0.015 here is multiplied by the area in mm² and pressure in MPa to give N directly when diameters are in mm.)

**Final Answer:** $F_{draw} \approx 80.5$ kN, $F_{BH} \approx 450$ N. Operation is feasible in a single draw with adequate lubrication.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Deep Drawing Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flat Circular Blank D0] --> B[Position on Die with Blank Holder]
    B --> C[Punch Descends Contacts Centre]
    C --> D[Flange Flows Over Die Corner Radius rd]
    D --> E[Wall Forms in Die Cavity]
    E --> F{Punch Reaches Full Depth?}
    F -->|No| C
    F -->|Yes| G[Stripper Releases Cup]
    G --> H{Cup Depth > Diameter?}
    H -->|No| I[Finished Single-Draw Cup]
    H -->|Yes| J[Send to Redraw Stage 1]
    J --> K[Redraw with Smaller Die]
    K --> L[Second Redraw if Required]
    L --> M[Final Deep Cup]

    B --> N[Blank Holder Force FBH Controls Wrinkling]
    D --> O[Drawing Force Fdraw Must Not Exceed Wall UTS]
    N --> P[Too Low = Wrinkling Defect]
    N --> Q[Too High = Tearing Defect]
    O --> R[Excessive = Wall Fracture]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress and Strain States in the Cup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Flange Region]
        A1[Compressive Hoop Strain]
        A2[Radial Tensile Stress]
        A3[Through-thickness Compression]
    end
    subgraph B[Die Corner Region]
        B1[Bending Strain]
        B2[High Friction]
        B3[Peak Drawing Stress]
    end
    subgraph C[Wall Region]
        C1[Uniaxial Tensile Strain]
        C2[Longitudinal Stress = Drawing Stress]
    end
    subgraph D[Punch Nose Region]
        D1[Reverse Bending]
        D2[Strain Hardening Peak]
        D3[Tearing Risk Zone]
    end
    subgraph E[Bottom Region]
        E1[Rigid Body Motion]
        E2[Essentially No Plastic Strain]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Defects and Counter-Measures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Deep Drawing Operation] --> DefectCheck{Defect Type?}

    DefectCheck -->|Flange Wrinkles| W[Increase Blank Holder Force FBH]
    DefectCheck -->|Wall Tears at Punch Nose| T[Reduce Drawing Ratio DR]
    DefectCheck -->|Earing on Cup Rim| E[Rotate Blank 90 deg Between Stages]
    DefectCheck -->|Wrinkles in Wall| W2[Reduce Die Clearance to 1.1t]
    DefectCheck -->|Bottom Fracture| B[Increase Punch Nose Radius rp]

    W --> W1[Add Draw Beads or Higher BHF]
    T --> T1[Multi-Stage Redraw]
    E --> E1[Use Low Delta-r Sheet]
    W2 --> W2a[Use Ironing Ring]
    B --> B1[Better Lubrication at Punch Nose]

    W1 --> Result[Improved Cup Quality]
    T1 --> Result
    E1 --> Result
    W2a --> Result
    B1 --> Result
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Multi-Stage Redraw Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blank Dia D0] -->|Stage 1: Draw Ratio 1.8| B[Stage 1 Cup Dia D1]
    B -->|Stage 2: Redraw Ratio 1.4| C[Stage 2 Cup Dia D2]
    C -->|Stage 3: Redraw Ratio 1.3| D[Stage 3 Cup Dia D3]
    D -->|Optional Ironing| E[Final Cup with Reduced Wall Thickness]

    A -.->|LDR1 = 1.8| B
    B -.->|LDR2 = 1.4| C
    C -.->|LDR3 = 1.3| D

    style A fill:#E0F0FF
    style B fill:#FFE0E0
    style C fill:#FFE0E0
    style D fill:#FFE0E0
    style E fill:#E0FFE0
```
