# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR). State typical LDR values for low-carbon steel and aluminium alloys.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)** is defined as the maximum ratio of the blank diameter to the punch diameter that can be deep-drawn in a single operation without failure of the cup wall.

$$
LDR = \left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_p}\right)_{max}
$$

Typical values:
- **Low-carbon steel:** $LDR \approx 2.0$ (range 2.0–2.2).
- **Aluminium alloys:** $LDR \approx 1.8$ to 2.0.
- **Stainless steel (austenitic):** $LDR \approx 2.3$ to 2.5 (due to high strain-hardening exponent, $n \approx 0.4$).
- **Copper, brass:** $LDR \approx 2.0$ to 2.1.

LDR is influenced by material properties (strain-hardening exponent $n$, anisotropy $r$), friction conditions, die and punch radii, and blank holder pressure. **[3 marks]**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is earing in deep drawing? Explain its cause and how it can be minimised.**

**Model Answer:**
**Earing** is the formation of wavy (scalloped) edges at the rim of a deep-drawn cup, where the cup wall height varies around the circumference (forming "ears" or peaks and "valleys").

**Cause:** Earing is caused by **planar anisotropy** ($\Delta r \neq 0$) in the rolled sheet metal. Because the rolling process imparts different values of normal anisotropy $r$ at 0°, 45°, and 90° to the rolling direction, the sheet resists flow differently in different directions, producing wavy edges.

**Minimisation:**
1. Use sheets with **low planar anisotropy** ($\Delta r$ close to 0).
2. **Rotate the blank by 45°–90°** between successive draws so that earing patterns cancel out.
3. Use **balanced biaxially-strained** (e.g., cross-rolled) sheet.
4. Avoid strong preferred crystallographic texture during sheet production. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **drawing force in deep drawing** using the Sievekes approach. List the assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A circular blank of diameter **150 mm** and **thickness 1.0 mm** is to be deep-drawn to a cup of **diameter 80 mm** using low-carbon steel having mean flow stress $\bar{Y} = 300$ MPa and ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{UTS} = 400$ MPa. Compute: **(i)** Drawing ratio, **(ii)** Drawing force (Sievekes formula), and **(iii)** Whether a single draw is feasible. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Drawing Force:**
See detailed derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

Key steps:
1. Consider flange element with current diameter $D$ (reducing from $D_0$ to $D_p$).
2. Circumferential strain in flange: $\varepsilon_\theta = \ln(D/D_0)$.
3. Mean flow stress: $\bar{Y} = K\varepsilon^n/(1+n)$.
4. Radial stress equilibrium gives: $\sigma_r = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(DR)$.
5. Force on wall: $F_{draw} = \sigma_{draw} \cdot \pi D_p t = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(DR) \cdot \pi D_p t$.

**Assumptions:** (i) uniform deformation, (ii) no ironing, (iii) no work-hardening in the wall (already work-hardened in flange), (iv) friction factor 0.05–0.1 absorbed in 1.1 factor, (v) plane stress. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Drawing ratio:**

$$
DR = \dfrac{D_0}{D_p} = \dfrac{150}{80} = 1.875
$$

**(ii) Drawing force:**

$$
F_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(DR) \cdot \pi D_p t
$$

$$
F_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot 300 \times 10^6 \cdot \ln(1.875) \cdot \pi \cdot 0.080 \cdot 0.001
$$

$$
\ln(1.875) = 0.6286
$$

$$
F_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot 300 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.6286 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.080 \cdot 0.001
$$

$$
F_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot 300 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.6286 \cdot 2.513 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot 300 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.580 \times 10^{-4} = 52,138 \text{ N} \approx 52.1 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iii) Feasibility check:**

$$
\sigma_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot 300 \cdot 0.6286 = 207.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{draw} = 207.4 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{UTS} = 400 \text{ MPa}
$$

Safety factor = $400/207.4 = 1.93$ ✓. Since $DR = 1.875 < LDR_{steel} = 2.0$, **single draw is feasible with adequate lubrication and proper blank holder force**. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **wrinkling** in deep drawing. Discuss the role of the **blank holder**, including the determination of blank holder pressure. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A cylindrical cup of diameter **80 mm** is to be redrawn to **50 mm diameter** in the second stage of a multi-stage operation. The sheet thickness is **1.0 mm** and the mean flow stress is **280 MPa**. Compute the **redrawing force** using Sievekes' equation. Discuss the relative magnitudes of the first-draw and redraw forces. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Wrinkling and Blank Holder:**

**Wrinkling** is a buckling-type instability that occurs in the **flange region** of a deep-drawn part when the **compressive hoop stress** exceeds a critical value. The unsupported flange, under radial tension and circumferential compression, behaves like a circular plate clamped at the inner edge (die corner) and free at the outer edge. When compressive hoop strain reaches the critical buckling strain, the flange buckles out-of-plane, producing **radial wrinkles** that get drawn into the wall as **vertical corrugations** (an unacceptable defect).

**The Blank Holder** applies a controlled compressive force (normal to the sheet) on the flange to suppress wrinkling. The force must be:
- **Sufficiently high** to keep the flange in contact with the die and prevent buckling.
- **Sufficiently low** to avoid excessive friction that would increase drawing force and cause tearing.

**Determination of blank holder pressure:** Use Hill's buckling criterion or empirical rules:
- For **steel**: $p = 0.7$ to $1.4$ MPa
- For **aluminium**: $p = 0.3$ to $0.7$ MPa
- Empirical formula: $F_{BH} = (0.01 \text{ to } 0.02) \cdot (D_0^2 - D_p^2) \cdot p$

**Methods of applying BHF:** (i) constant-force springs, (ii) pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders, (iii) die springs, (iv) draw beads (local BHF). **[7 marks]**

**(b) Redrawing Force Computation:**

Given: $D_1 = 80$ mm (current dia), $D_2 = 50$ mm (new dia), $t = 1.0$ mm, $\bar{Y} = 280$ MPa.

**Redraw ratio:**

$$
RR = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} = \dfrac{80}{50} = 1.6
$$

Check: Typical redraw LDR is ~1.4–1.5. So $RR = 1.6$ is *slightly high*; we should ideally use a 2-stage redraw (e.g., 80 → 64 → 50). However, for computation, we proceed.

**Redrawing force (Sievekes):**

$$
F_{redraw} = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(RR) \cdot \pi D_2 t
$$

$$
F_{redraw} = 1.1 \cdot 280 \times 10^6 \cdot \ln(1.6) \cdot \pi \cdot 0.050 \cdot 0.001
$$

$$
\ln(1.6) = 0.4700
$$

$$
F_{redraw} = 1.1 \cdot 280 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.4700 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.050 \cdot 0.001
$$

$$
F_{redraw} = 1.1 \cdot 280 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.4700 \cdot 1.571 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_{redraw} = 22,778 \text{ N} \approx 22.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Comparison with first-draw force:**
If first-draw was $D_0 = 150$ mm to $D_1 = 80$ mm, $t = 1.0$ mm, $\bar{Y} = 280$ MPa:

$$
F_{draw,1} = 1.1 \cdot 280 \times 10^6 \cdot \ln(150/80) \cdot \pi \cdot 0.080 \cdot 0.001
$$

$$
F_{draw,1} = 1.1 \cdot 280 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.6286 \cdot 2.513 \times 10^{-4} = 48,650 \text{ N} \approx 48.7 \text{ kN}
$$

So the redraw force (22.8 kN) is **less than half** of the first-draw force (48.7 kN) because the punch diameter is smaller and the strain increment is lower. This is why multi-stage drawing is preferred: each stage applies a smaller incremental load, reducing peak tool stresses and the risk of tearing. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert mm → m when using $\bar{Y}$ in Pa. *Loss: 2–3 marks per occurrence.*
> 2. **Forgetting the "1.1 factor"** in Sievekes' formula. Examiners check this — it represents friction + bending, NOT optional. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Confusing LDR with DR:** LDR is a *material/limiting property*; DR is the *current* drawing ratio. Always compare: $DR \leq LDR$ for single-draw feasibility.
> 4. **Ignoring wrinkling in flange:** When asked about defects, students often mention only tearing. Examiners explicitly look for **two failure modes** (wrinkling + tearing) and their *causes and remedies*. *Loss: up to 3 marks.*
> 5. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in derivation problems. KTU key answer scheme always lists assumptions; missing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Sign errors in redraw ratio:** $RR = D_{before}/D_{after}$, not the reverse.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Key Facts to Memorise:**

- ✅ **Deep Drawing** converts a flat blank into a hollow cup; thickness is preserved; deformation concentrated in **flange**.
- ✅ **LDR** (Limiting Drawing Ratio) ≈ **2.0 for steel**, ≈ **1.8 for aluminium**, ≈ **2.4 for stainless steel**.
- ✅ **Drawing Force (Sievekes):** $F_{draw} = 1.1 \cdot \bar{Y} \cdot \ln(DR) \cdot \pi D_p t$
- ✅ **Drawing Ratio (DR)** must be **< LDR** for single-draw success.
- ✅ **Blank Holder** prevents **wrinkling** (flange buckling) but excessive BHF causes **tearing**.
- ✅ **Die Clearance** $c = (D_d - D_p)/2 \approx 1.1 t$ (no ironing) or $c \approx t$ (with ironing).
- ✅ **Die corner radius** $r_d = 5$ to $15 \times t$; **Punch nose radius** $r_p = 3$ to $10 \times t$.
- ✅ **Redrawing** is needed when total reduction > LDR. Use **direct** or **indirect** methods.
- ✅ **Multi-stage LDR** ≈ $\prod LDR_i$. For equal-stage design, $R_n = R_{total}^{1/n}$.
- ✅ **Earing** = wavy cup rim; caused by **planar anisotropy** ($\Delta r \neq 0$); fix by rotating blank 90° or using low-$\Delta r$ sheet.
- ✅ **Wrinkling** = flange buckling; fix by ↑ BHF, draw beads, or smaller die clearance.
- ✅ **Tearing** = wall fracture at punch nose; fix by ↓ DR, ↑ $r_p$, better lubrication, or use multi-stage.
- ✅ Always state: **DR, LDR, drawing stress check vs UTS, and BHF** in any numerical problem for full marks.
- ✅ **Ironing** = intentional wall thinning; uses tight clearance $c < t$ and occurs after main draw (e.g., in beverage cans).
- ✅ For **redraw ratio**, typical value **1.3 to 1.5** per stage for steel; do not exceed **1.4** in practice to avoid tearing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spinning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spinning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Spinning** is an **incremental sheet metal forming** process in which a circular sheet metal blank is rotated at high speed about its own axis and forced plastically over a rotating mandrel (or form block) using a rigid tool (roller or roller-tipped tool). The process produces **axisymmetric, hollow, rotationally symmetric parts** without any material addition or removal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPET602 Module 1):**
> "Spinning is a chipless, cold-working process for shaping sheet metal into axially symmetric forms by plastic deformation over a rotating mandrel using a roller tool."

Mathematically, the generic deformation can be expressed as:

$$ \varepsilon_{radial} + \varepsilon_{hoop} + \varepsilon_{axial} = 0 $$

where the strains are interdependent due to the incompressibility of plastic deformation (constant volume).

## 1.2 Key Process Characteristics

| Parameter | Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **Cold working (room temperature)** for most ductile metals |
| Typical Spindle Speed | **100 – 1800 RPM** |
| Blank Thickness | **0.5 mm – 25 mm** |
| Diameter Range | **25 mm – 6 m** |
| Tolerance Achieved | **±0.25 mm** (standard), **±0.05 mm** (precision) |
| Material Wastage | **Negligible (≈ 0%)** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The Potter's Wheel**
> Imagine a **potter shaping clay on a rotating wheel**. The clay (analogous to the metal blank) is mounted on a spinning mandrel. The potter's hands (analogous to the forming roller) apply gentle, controlled pressure while the wheel turns, coaxing the clay up into a hollow vessel.
> - The clay does not lose mass; it is **redistributed** (similar to metal spinning).
> - The potter works in **multiple passes** (similar to incremental deformation in spinning).
> - The final shape is **axisymmetric** (a 3D body with rotational symmetry).
> The only real difference: in metal spinning, the blank is **rigid** (metal) and the force is delivered by a hardened steel roller rather than hands.

## 1.4 Classification of Spinning

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies spinning into **three principal variants**:

1. **Conventional Spinning (True Spinning / Radial Draw Forming)**
   - Wall thickness remains approximately equal to the original blank thickness.
   - Used for cookware, satellite dishes, lighting reflectors.

2. **Shear Spinning (Flow Turning / Shear Forming)**
   - Wall thickness is intentionally reduced according to the **Sine Law**.
   - Used for missile nose cones, rocket motor casings, conical/cylindrical pressure vessels.

3. **Tube Spinning (Rotary Swaging / Neck-Spinning)**
   - Applied to pre-formed tubular blanks to reduce diameter or thicken walls.
   - Used for seamless cylinder liners, axle tubes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Steps (Conventional Spinning)

The conventional spinning process follows a structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Blank Preparation:** A circular disc of sheet metal (the *blank*) is cut to size with a **blank-holder diameter ≈ 1.05 × mandrel diameter** to allow for trimming.
- **Step 2 — Clamping:** The blank is clamped between the **tailstock** and the **mandrel face** using a **follower block (clamp pad)** to prevent buckling.
- **Step 3 — Rotation Initiation:** The mandrel rotates at controlled speed (100–1800 RPM) driven by a spindle motor.
- **Step 4 — Incremental Deformation:** A hardened steel **roller** (or roller-tipped tool) is brought into contact with the outer surface of the blank. The roller applies localized radial pressure, plastically deforming the metal against the mandrel.
- **Step 5 — Multi-Pass Traversal:** The roller traverses from the **tailstock end → apex end** in multiple passes, gradually forming the contour. Each pass imposes a small incremental strain (typically 20%–40% reduction per pass).
- **Step 6 — Trimming:** The outer flange is trimmed off in a separate lathe operation to yield the final axisymmetric part.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The key distinction between Conventional and Shear spinning is **wall thickness behavior**. Examiners frequently test this with a one-line question: *"Differentiate conventional and shear spinning."*

## 2.2 The Sine Law (Shear Spinning) — The Heart of the Module

For shear spinning, the wall thickness is governed by the **Sine Law of Shear Forming**, derived from the principle of constant shear area:

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \frac{\sin \alpha}{\sin \alpha_{0}} $$

where:
- $t_{0}$ = **initial blank thickness** (mm)
- $t_{f}$ = **final wall thickness** (mm)
- $\alpha_{0}$ = **initial half-angle of the blank with the rotation axis** (degrees or radians)
- $\alpha$ = **final half-angle of the spun part with the rotation axis** (degrees or radians)

For a **flat blank**, $\alpha_{0} = 90°$, so $\sin \alpha_{0} = 1$, and the law reduces to:

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \sin \alpha $$

This means a cone with a **half-angle of 30°** will have a final wall thickness of **exactly half** the original blank thickness.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \sin \alpha$ | Sine Law for shear spinning from a flat blank ($\alpha_{0} = 90°$) |
| 2 | $t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \dfrac{\sin \alpha}{\sin \alpha_{0}}$ | Generalized Sine Law for shear spinning (any initial angle) |
| 3 | $\%\,Reduction = \left(1 - \dfrac{t_{f}}{t_{0}}\right) \cdot 100$ | Percentage reduction in wall thickness |
| 4 | $r = \dfrac{D_{0}}{2 \sin \alpha}$ | Cone radius of curvature / blank diameter for a flat blank |
| 5 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{flow}}{2} \cdot \sin(2\phi)$ | Shear stress along the slip plane (slip-line field) |
| 6 | $F = k \cdot \pi \cdot D_{m} \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Approximate spinning force (axial component) |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Spindle power where $N$ is RPM, $T$ is torque (N·m) |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{t_{0}}{t_{f}}\right) = -\ln(\sin \alpha)$ | True strain in shear spinning |
| 9 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v \sin \alpha}{r}$ | Strain rate in shear spinning |
| 10 | $v = \pi D_{m} N$ | Roller linear velocity (m/s) |

**Notation Key:**
- $D_{0}$ = original blank diameter
- $D_{m}$ = mandrel diameter at the contact zone
- $k$ = shear yield strength
- $\sigma_{flow}$ = material flow stress
- $\phi$ = angle of the slip line
- $r$ = instantaneous radial distance from axis

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table Syntax Reminder (Internal):** All absolute value and division operations in this table are written with `\dfrac`, `\sin`, `\ln`, etc., to prevent markdown table corruption. **Never** use a vertical pipe `$\vert$` symbol inside a table row.

## 2.4 Why and How — Physical Interpretation

- **Why does the Sine Law hold?** Because the shear plane area is preserved: the metal slides over the mandrel along a **conical slip surface** without thinning perpendicular to the surface.
- **How is force applied?** Through a **point/line contact** between the roller and the blank, generating highly localized triaxial compressive stresses that suppress failure.
- **Why incremental passes?** Each pass imposes only a small strain, keeping the **flow stress low** and avoiding **necking or wrinkling**. The total strain is the sum of incremental strains: $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_{i}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spinning is indispensable in industries requiring **low-volume, high-precision axisymmetric components**:

- **Aerospace:** Nose cones, rocket motor casings, jet engine inlet cowlings.
- **Defence:** Missile bodies, warhead liners, radar reflectors.
- **Cookware & Consumer:** Pressure cooker bodies, woks, lampshades, musical instruments (cymbals, gongs).
- **Energy:** Wind turbine nose cones, vacuum chamber ends.
- **Automotive:** Wheel discs, headlight reflectors, prototype body panels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Shear spinning can produce **net-shape or near-net-shape** parts with **up to 80% material savings** compared to machining from solid billets. This is why it dominates aerospace and defence manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sine Law (Shear Spinning)

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup

Consider a flat circular blank of thickness $t_{0}$ being formed into a truncated cone with half-angle $\alpha$ (measured from the rotation axis to the wall). Let the wall thickness after deformation be $t_{f}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the deformation geometry.**
As the roller forces the metal over the mandrel, the metal layer of original thickness $t_{0}$ slides along a **conical surface**. The fiber of metal on the surface makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal plane after deformation.

**Step 2 — Resolve the thickness vector.**
The original thickness vector $t_{0}$ is **vertical** (perpendicular to the blank plane). After deformation, this vector is rotated to lie **perpendicular to the cone wall**. The angle between the original vertical and the new wall-perpendicular is $(90° - \alpha)$.

**Step 3 — Project the original thickness onto the new direction.**
The component of $t_{0}$ that survives as the new wall thickness is:

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \cos(90° - \alpha) $$

**Step 4 — Apply the trigonometric identity.**
Using the co-function identity $\cos(90° - \alpha) = \sin \alpha$:

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \sin \alpha $$

**Step 5 — Generalize to a non-flat starting blank.**
If the starting blank is itself a cone with half-angle $\alpha_{0}$ (as in re-spinning), then by the same geometric projection principle:

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \frac{\sin \alpha}{\sin \alpha_{0}} $$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**
This is the **Sine Law of Shear Spinning**, the most important equation in the entire module.

### 3.1.3 Display of the Final Result

$$ \boxed{\,t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \frac{\sin \alpha}{\sin \alpha_{0}} \quad \text{(Generalized Sine Law)}\,} $$

$$ \boxed{\,t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \sin \alpha \quad \text{(Flat Blank Case, } \alpha_{0} = 90°\text{)}\,} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Spinning Reduction (Practical Limit)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
Determine the maximum permissible reduction per pass to avoid **wrinkling, necking, or splitting**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the critical strain.**
The maximum true strain before localized necking in a tensile test is given by the **Considère criterion**:

$$ \varepsilon_{crit} = n \quad \text{(true strain = strain-hardening exponent)} $$

**Step 2 — Express true strain in spinning.**
For spinning, the true strain in the wall is:

$$ \varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{t_{0}}{t_{f}} \right) $$

**Step 3 — Apply the necking criterion.**
For safe deformation per pass:

$$ \ln \left( \frac{t_{0}}{t_{f}} \right) \le n $$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum reduction ratio.**

$$ \frac{t_{0}}{t_{f}} \le e^{n} $$

$$ \%\,Reduction_{max} = \left( 1 - e^{-n} \right) \cdot 100 $$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
For a typical aluminium alloy with $n = 0.25$:

$$ \%\,Reduction_{max} = \left( 1 - e^{-0.25} \right) \cdot 100 = (1 - 0.7788) \cdot 100 \approx 22.1\% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the practical industrial limit as **20% – 30% reduction per pass** for most engineering metals. Values above this typically require **intermediate annealing** to restore ductility.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Spinning Force (Axial Component)

### 3.3.1 Slip-Line Field Approximation

For a roller pressing a sheet against a mandrel, the deformed region approximates a **slip-line field** of logarithmic spirals. The shear stress along a slip line inclined at angle $\phi$ is:

$$ \tau = \frac{\sigma_{flow}}{2} \cdot \sin(2\phi) $$

**Step 1 — Identify the average slip-line angle.**
For a smoothly deformed zone, the average $\phi \approx 45°$, so $\sin(2\phi) = 1$, giving maximum shear:

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{flow}}{2} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the contact shear force.**
The contact zone is approximately a **circular band of width $t$** at diameter $D_{m}$:

$$ F_{shear} = \tau_{max} \cdot A_{contact} = \frac{\sigma_{flow}}{2} \cdot (\pi D_{m} \cdot t) $$

**Step 3 — Resolve into axial roller force.**
By the von Mises flow rule, the axial (vertical) component of the roller force is:

$$ F_{axial} = \frac{\sigma_{flow}}{2} \cdot \pi D_{m} t \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} $$

$$ \boxed{\,F_{axial} = \frac{\pi \sigma_{flow} D_{m} t}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.907 \, \sigma_{flow} \, D_{m} \, t\,} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the spinning power.**
With the roller moving at linear velocity $v = \pi D_{m} N / 60$:

$$ P = F_{axial} \cdot v = \frac{\pi \sigma_{flow} D_{m} t}{2\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\pi D_{m} N}{60} $$

$$ \boxed{\,P = \frac{\pi^{2} \sigma_{flow} D_{m}^{2} t \, N}{120 \sqrt{3}} \approx 0.0475 \, \sigma_{flow} \, D_{m}^{2} \, t \, N \;\;\; (\text{Watts})\,} $$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Sine Law Application)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A flat steel blank of thickness **4.0 mm** and diameter **600 mm** is shear-spun into a cone with a **half-angle of 30°**. Determine:
> (i) the final wall thickness,
> (ii) the percentage reduction,
> (iii) the true strain imposed on the material.

**Solution:**

**(i) Final wall thickness:**

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \sin \alpha = 4.0 \cdot \sin 30° = 4.0 \cdot 0.5 = 2.0 \;\;\; \text{mm} $$

**(ii) Percentage reduction:**

$$ \%\,Reduction = \left(1 - \frac{t_{f}}{t_{0}}\right) \cdot 100 = \left(1 - \frac{2.0}{4.0}\right) \cdot 100 = 50\% $$

**(iii) True strain:**

$$ \varepsilon = \ln \left(\frac{t_{0}}{t_{f}}\right) = \ln \left(\frac{4.0}{2.0}\right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693 $$

> [!NOTE]
> A true strain of 0.693 is **high** — this implies the operation **cannot be done in a single pass** for a ductile material. Typically, two or three passes with intermediate annealing would be required.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Spinning vs. Other Forming Processes

| Criterion | Spinning | Deep Drawing | Stamping |
|---|---|---|---|
| Symmetry required | **Axisymmetric only** | Axisymmetric | None |
| Tooling cost | **Low (mandrel + roller)** | High (die + punch) | High (matched dies) |
| Material utilization | **Excellent (≈ 100%)** | Moderate (≈ 70–80%) | Moderate (≈ 60–75%) |
| Wall thickness control | **Precise (Sine Law)** | Less precise | Die-controlled |
| Part size | Small to **very large (6 m)** | Limited by press | Limited by press |
| Production volume | **Low to medium** | High | Very high |
| Springback compensation | **Yes (multi-pass trial)** | Die compensation | Die compensation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conventional Spinning — Process Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Motor Drive] --> B[Spindle and Chuck]
    B --> C[Mandrel with Pre-shaped Form Block]
    D[Sheet Metal Blank] -->|clamped by| E[Tailstock with Follower Block]
    E --> C
    C -->|rotates at| F[High Speed 100 to 1800 RPM]
    G[Hydraulic or Pneumatic Roller Tool] -->|applies radial force on| C
    H[Linear Traverse Axis X Z] -->|moves roller along| G
    I[Coolant and Lubricant Supply] -->|feeds| G
    J[Finished Axisymmetric Part] -->|trimmed and removed from| C
```

## 4.2 Shear Spinning — Wall Thickness Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stage A: Flat Blank of Thickness t0] -->|roller passes| P2[Stage B: Partially Formed Cone with Variable Thickness]
    P2 -->|roller passes| P3[Stage C: Final Cone with Uniform Thickness tf]
    P3 -->|sine law check| Q{tf = t0 times sin alpha question mark}
    Q -->|yes| R[Acceptable Part]
    Q -->|no| S[Reject and Adjust Parameters]
    P1 --> T[Initial half angle alpha0 = 90 degrees]
    P3 --> U[Final half angle alpha given by design]
```

## 4.3 Force and Velocity Diagram in Shear Spinning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Kinematics
        A1[Spindle Axis Vertical] --> A2[Rotational Velocity omega]
        A2 --> A3[Roller Linear Velocity v = pi Dm N]
    end
    subgraph Forces
        B1[Radial Force Fr from Roller] --> B2[Axial Force Fa along Mandrel]
        B2 --> B3[Tangential Force Ft from Spindle Drive]
        B3 --> B4[Resultant Spinning Force F = sqrt of Fr squared + Fa squared + Ft squared]
    end
    subgraph Stresses
        C1[Material Flow Stress sigma flow] --> C2[Shear Stress tau along Slip Line]
        C2 --> C3[Contact Pressure p at Roller Blank Interface]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    B4 --> C3
```

## 4.4 Process Parameter Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Spinning Process Parameters] --> P1[Geometric Parameters]
    Root --> P2[Kinematic Parameters]
    Root --> P3[Material Parameters]
    Root --> P4[Tooling Parameters]

    P1 --> P1a[Blank Diameter D0]
    P1 --> P1b[Blank Thickness t0]
    P1 --> P1c[Half Angle alpha]
    P1 --> P1d[Mandrel Diameter Dm]

    P2 --> P2a[Spindle Speed N]
    P2 --> P2b[Roller Feed Rate f]
    P2 --> P2c[Roller Pressure]

    P3 --> P3a[Flow Stress sigma flow]
    P3 --> P3b[Strain Hardening Exponent n]
    P3 --> P3c[Strain Rate Sensitivity m]

    P4 --> P4a[Roller Radius R]
    P4 --> P4b[Mandrel Material]
    P4 --> P4c[Lubricant Type]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** In the KTU board exam, when asked to *"draw the schematic of the spinning process"*, ensure you label **at least 5 components**: mandrel, blank, roller, tailstock, and spindle. This guarantees full marks on the diagram portion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define spinning. List any four products made by the spinning process.**

**Model Answer:**
Spinning is a chipless, cold-working sheet metal forming process in which a circular blank is rotated at high speed about its axis and plastically deformed over a mandrel using a roller tool to produce an axisymmetric hollow part.

**Four products:**
1. Pressure cooker bodies (cookware)
2. Missile nose cones (aerospace)
3. Lighting reflectors (lighting industry)
4. Musical cymbals (musical instruments)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the Sine Law of shear spinning. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sine Law states that the final wall thickness $t_f$ of a shear-spun part is related to the original blank thickness $t_0$ and the half-angles by:

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \frac{\sin \alpha}{\sin \alpha_{0}} $$

For a flat blank ($\alpha_0 = 90°$): $t_{f} = t_{0} \sin \alpha$.

**Significance:**
- It allows the designer to **predict and control wall thickness** by simply choosing the cone half-angle.
- It governs the **maximum reduction per pass** and determines the need for intermediate annealing.
- It is the **fundamental design equation** of shear forming.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **conventional spinning process**. Discuss the functions of the mandrel, roller, tailstock, and follower block.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A flat **aluminium blank of thickness 6 mm and diameter 800 mm** is shear-spun into a cone of **half-angle 25°**. Calculate the final wall thickness, the percentage reduction, and the true strain. Comment on whether the operation can be completed in a single pass.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The conventional spinning process is shown schematically in Figure 4.1. The components and their functions are:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Mandrel** | A hardened steel form-block shaped to the **internal contour** of the finished part. Provides the die surface against which the blank is deformed. |
| **Roller (Tool)** | A hardened steel roller that applies **localized radial pressure** on the blank, forcing it against the mandrel. Usually mounted on a hydraulically actuated slide. |
| **Tailstock** | Holds the blank against the mandrel and transmits the **axial clamping force**. Rotates synchronously with the mandrel. |
| **Follower Block (Clamp Pad)** | Distributes the clamping force **uniformly** over the blank to prevent buckling or wrinkling during the initial passes. |

**Process steps:**
1. Mount blank on mandrel and clamp with follower block.
2. Rotate mandrel at **100–1800 RPM**.
3. Apply roller pressure; traverse from tailstock to apex.
4. Repeat in multiple passes until contour matches mandrel.
5. Trim outer flange.

**Advantages:** Low tooling cost, short lead time, flexibility, 100% material utilization.

**Limitations:** Restricted to axisymmetric parts; lower productivity than deep drawing.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $t_0 = 6$ mm, $D_0 = 800$ mm, $\alpha = 25°$ (flat blank so $\alpha_0 = 90°$).

**(i) Final wall thickness:**

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \sin \alpha = 6 \cdot \sin 25° = 6 \cdot 0.4226 \approx 2.536 \;\;\; \text{mm} $$

**(ii) Percentage reduction:**

$$ \%\,Reduction = \left(1 - \frac{2.536}{6}\right) \cdot 100 = (1 - 0.4226) \cdot 100 \approx 57.74\% $$

**(iii) True strain:**

$$ \varepsilon = \ln \left(\frac{6}{2.536}\right) = \ln 2.366 \approx 0.861 $$

**Comment on single-pass feasibility:**
A true strain of 0.861 is **excessive** for a single pass. For aluminium alloys with $n \approx 0.20$–$0.30$, the maximum per-pass strain is around 0.20–0.25, corresponding to a maximum reduction of about **20%–25%** per pass. Hence, the operation **must be performed in 3 to 4 passes with intermediate annealing**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **conventional spinning and shear spinning** in a tabular form with **at least six points**, including a derivation/summary of the governing thickness equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Estimate the **axial spinning force and the spindle power** required to shear-spin a steel cone from a flat blank of diameter **400 mm** at **600 RPM**. Given: $\sigma_{flow} = 350$ MPa, blank thickness = 5 mm, final half-angle = 20°.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| # | Conventional Spinning | Shear Spinning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wall thickness ≈ constant | Wall thickness **reduced by Sine Law** |
| 2 | $t_f \approx t_0$ | $t_f = t_0 \sin \alpha$ |
| 3 | Used for cookware, lamps, dishes | Used for cones, missile nose cones, rocket casings |
| 4 | Diameter reduces, length may increase | Both diameter and length change to satisfy geometry |
| 5 | Force is **radial and axial** | Force has a strong **tangential (shear)** component |
| 6 | Tool path: along mandrel surface | Tool path: along the **wall angle** |
| 7 | Lower forces | Higher forces due to thinning |
| 8 | Easier to control | Requires precise roller path control |

**Governing Equation of Shear Spinning (Derivation Summary):**
Derived from the projection of original thickness $t_0$ onto the wall-perpendicular direction. If the original blank is flat ($\alpha_0 = 90°$), the wall-perpendicular makes angle $(90° - \alpha)$ with the vertical. Therefore:

$$ t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \cos(90° - \alpha) = t_{0} \cdot \sin \alpha $$

For a non-flat starting cone: $t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{\sin \alpha}{\sin \alpha_0}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $D_0 = 400$ mm, $N = 600$ RPM, $\sigma_{flow} = 350$ MPa, $t_0 = 5$ mm, $\alpha = 20°$.

**Step 1 — Final wall thickness (for reference):**

$$ t_{f} = 5 \cdot \sin 20° = 5 \cdot 0.342 = 1.71 \;\;\; \text{mm} $$

**Step 2 — Mandrel diameter at contact (approximate):**

$$ D_{m} \approx 0.5 \cdot D_{0} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos \alpha} \approx 0.5 \cdot 400 \cdot \frac{1}{0.9397} \approx 212.8 \;\;\; \text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Axial spinning force (using $t = t_0 = 5$ mm as mean contact thickness):**

$$ F_{axial} = \frac{\pi \sigma_{flow} D_{m} t}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\pi \cdot 350 \cdot 212.8 \cdot 5}{2\sqrt{3}} \cdot 10^{-3} \;\;\; \text{kN} $$

Computing the numerator:

$$ \pi \cdot 350 \cdot 212.8 \cdot 5 = \pi \cdot 372{,}400 \approx 1{,}169{,}700 \;\;\; \text{N·mm} $$

Dividing by $2\sqrt{3} = 3.464$:

$$ F_{axial} \approx \frac{1{,}169{,}700}{3.464} \approx 337{,}700 \;\;\; \text{N} \approx 337.7 \;\;\; \text{kN} $$

**Step 4 — Spindle power:**

$$ P = F_{axial} \cdot v = F_{axial} \cdot \frac{\pi D_{m} N}{60} $$

Linear velocity:

$$ v = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.2128 \cdot 600}{60} = \pi \cdot 2.128 \approx 6.686 \;\;\; \text{m/s} $$

Power:

$$ P = 337{,}700 \cdot 6.686 \approx 2{,}257{,}800 \;\;\; \text{W} \approx 2.26 \;\;\; \text{MW} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion:** Always convert mm → m when computing power in Watts. Mixing mm and m gives answers off by a factor of 1000.
> 2. **Forgetting the $2\sqrt{3}$ factor:** Marks are lost if the von Mises correction factor is omitted. Always derive this from the slip-line analysis, not just cite it.
> 3. **Confusing shear and conventional spinning formulas:** A common blunder is applying $t_f = t_0 \sin \alpha$ to conventional spinning. Examiner deducts **2–3 marks** for this conceptual error.
> 4. **Incorrect identification of $D_m$:** Use the diameter at the **roller contact zone**, not the original blank diameter.
> 5. **Not commenting on multi-pass requirement:** Always state whether the operation can be done in a single pass based on the calculated strain.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Spinning**

- [x] **Definition:** Spinning is a chipless, cold-working process for axisymmetric parts using a rotating mandrel and roller tool.
- [x] **Three Variants:** Conventional, Shear (Flow Turning), Tube Spinning.
- [x] **Sine Law (MOST IMPORTANT):** $t_f = t_0 \cdot \sin \alpha$ (flat blank) or $t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{\sin \alpha}{\sin \alpha_0}$ (general).
- [x] **Conventional Spinning:** $t_f \approx t_0$ (no intentional thinning).
- [x] **Key Components:** Mandrel, Roller, Tailstock, Follower Block, Spindle.
- [x] **Process Parameters:** Spindle speed (100–1800 RPM), roller feed rate, roller pressure, blank diameter, half-angle.
- [x] **Maximum Reduction per Pass:** Typically **20%–30%** to avoid wrinkling/necking; higher requires intermediate annealing.
- [x] **True Strain in Shear Spinning:** $\varepsilon = -\ln(\sin \alpha)$.
- [x] **Force Equation:** $F_{axial} = \dfrac{\pi \sigma_{flow} D_m t}{2\sqrt{3}}$.
- [x] **Power Equation:** $P = F_{axial} \cdot v$ where $v = \pi D_m N / 60$.
- [x] **Sine Law Derivation Origin:** Geometric projection — $t_0$ projected onto the wall-normal direction.
- [x] **Applications:** Cookware, missile nose cones, rocket casings, lighting reflectors, musical cymbals.
- [x] **Advantages:** Low tooling cost, 100% material utilization, flexibility, large part capability.
- [x] **Limitations:** Restricted to axisymmetric geometry; lower productivity; multi-pass for high reductions.
- [x] **Considère Criterion for Safe Reduction:** $\varepsilon \le n$ per pass.
- [x] **Slip-Line Field:** Used to derive contact stresses; $\tau_{max} = \sigma_{flow} / 2$ at $\phi = 45°$.
- [x] **Differentiation from Deep Drawing:** Spinning uses a single roller; deep drawing uses matched die + punch.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"If the question mentions *thickness reduction by angle* — it's shear spinning, and the Sine Law applies. If the question mentions *cooking vessels or lamps* — it's conventional spinning, and $t_f \approx t_0$."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction and Lubrication in Metal Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Friction and Lubrication in Metal Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Friction in Metal Forming

**Friction** in metal forming is defined as the **tangential resistance to motion** at the interface between the workpiece material and the tooling (dies, punches, rolls, etc.) when a relative motion or a tendency of relative motion exists between them. It arises from the microscopic asperity-to-asperity contact, plastic deformation of asperities, and adhesion (cold-welding) between the two contacting surfaces under high normal pressures.

In the context of metal forming, friction plays a **dual role**:
- It is **undesirable** because it increases the required forming force, causes non-uniform deformation, induces barreling, accelerates tool wear, and limits the achievable deformation.
- It is occasionally **desirable** (e.g., to grip the workpiece during deep drawing to prevent wrinkling, or in rolling to "draw in" the material between rolls).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Friction in metal forming is NOT the same as friction in machine design. In metal forming, the normal pressures are extraordinarily high (often approaching the yield strength of the workpiece), so the classic Coulomb relationship $\tau = \mu p$ is often **invalid** because $\mu p$ can exceed the shear yield stress $K$ of the workpiece. This is why the concept of the **Friction Factor (m)** — proposed by Siebel — is preferred in KTU board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Box on a Tilted Ramp"

Imagine you are trying to push a heavy wooden box up a concrete ramp.

- If the ramp is **dry and rough**, the box is hard to move. The contact surfaces "stick" together, and you need enormous force. This represents **sticking friction** in metal forming — a limit where the interface shear stress reaches the shear yield stress of the softer material, and the friction force is independent of normal pressure.
- If you **oil the ramp**, the box slides much more easily. The resistance is now proportional to the normal load pressing the box onto the ramp. This represents **sliding (Coulomb) friction** — proportional to normal pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** In metal forming under high pressure, the contact interface may transition from sliding to sticking. When sticking occurs, the workpiece material actually shears internally just below the surface, and the shear stress at the interface is bounded by the material's shear yield stress $K$. This is why Siebel's friction factor $m$ is **capped at 1.0**, but the Coulomb coefficient $\mu$ can theoretically be unbounded.

## 1.3 Definition of Lubrication

**Lubrication** in metal forming is the deliberate introduction of a **third substance (lubricant)** between the workpiece and the die to:
1. Reduce the interfacial shear stress (friction),
2. Prevent metal-to-metal adhesion and cold-welding,
3. Insulate the die from the hot workpiece (thermal barrier),
4. Protect the die surface from wear,
5. Improve surface finish of the product, and
6. Act as a parting agent during release.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** A dimensionless scalar relating shear stress to normal pressure: $\tau = \mu p$.
- **Friction Factor (m):** A dimensionless scalar defined as the ratio of actual interface shear stress to the shear yield stress of the workpiece: $m = \tau / K$.
- **Shear Yield Stress (K):** A material property, related to tensile yield $Y$ by $K = Y/\sqrt{3}$ (von Mises) or $K = Y/2$ (Tresca).
- **Flow Stress ($Y_f$):** The current (strain-hardened) yield strength of the workpiece during deformation.
- **Sticking Friction Limit:** $\tau_{max} = K$, hence $m_{max} = 1.0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Many students incorrectly assume $\mu$ and $m$ are interchangeable. They are NOT. Always use $m$ for forging/extrusion analyses at high pressure, and convert to $\mu$ only when sliding friction is established. The conversion formulas will be derived in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Regimes of Interface Friction

### Regime A — Sliding (Coulomb) Friction
- The workpiece slides relative to the die surface.
- The shear stress at the interface is governed by **Amontons-Coulomb's law**:

$$\tau = \mu p$$

where $p$ is the local normal (compressive) pressure at the interface.
- Validity condition: $\mu p \leq K$, i.e., the interface shear stress cannot exceed the material's shear yield stress.
- If $\mu p < K$, sliding occurs. If the deformation is light, this regime dominates.

### Regime B — Sticking (Full Film) Friction
- The interface no longer slides. The material in the workpiece shears internally, just below the surface.
- The interface shear stress saturates at the **shear yield stress of the workpiece**:

$$\tau = K = \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}} \quad \text{(von Mises criterion)}$$

- The friction factor $m = \tau/K = 1.0$ (full sticking).
- This regime is the **most common in industrial hot/warm forging and extrusion** because the contact pressures are very high.

## 2.2 Why the Friction Factor m is Preferred in Metal Forming

The friction factor **m** (also called the **interface friction shear factor** or **Siebel's friction factor**) is defined as:

$$m = \frac{\tau_i}{K} = \frac{\tau_i}{Y_f / \sqrt{3}}$$

where $\tau_i$ is the actual interface shear stress and $K$ is the shear flow stress of the workpiece at the interface temperature and strain.

### Why m is better than μ in metal forming:
- $m$ is **bounded** between **0 and 1**, irrespective of the magnitude of normal pressure.
- $m$ directly relates the interface friction to the material's yield behavior — a **non-dimensional efficiency measure** of lubrication.
- For a well-lubricated interface, $m$ may be as low as 0.05–0.10. For a dry, hot forging interface, $m \approx 0.5$ to 1.0.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula** | **Meaning / Symbol** | **Boundary / Validity** |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau = \mu p$ | Coulomb sliding friction (μ = coefficient of friction) | Valid only if $\mu p \leq K$ |
| $m = \tau / K$ | Siebel's friction factor | $0 \leq m \leq 1$ always |
| $\tau = mK$ | General interface friction law in metal forming | Universal in KTU analyses |
| $K = Y_f / \sqrt{3}$ | Shear yield stress (von Mises) | Isotropic ductile metals |
| $K = Y_f / 2$ | Shear yield stress (Tresca) | Conservative estimate |
| $\mu = m / \sqrt{1 - m^{2}}$ | Conversion: friction factor → coefficient | Sliding regime only |
| $m = \mu / \sqrt{1 + \mu^{2}}$ | Conversion: coefficient → friction factor | Sliding regime only |
| $p_{avg} = Y_f \, e^{2\mu L / h}$ | Friction Hill — flat die forging (sliding) | Sliding friction assumed |
| $p_{avg} = Y_f \, e^{2mL / h}$ | Friction Hill — flat die forging (general) | Most general KTU form |
| $p_{max} = Y_f \left[1 + \frac{mL}{h}\right]^{2}$ | Approximate friction hill for small $mL/h$ | $\vert mL/h \vert < 0.3$, sliding |
| $\sigma_x = -Y_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{h}\right)$ | Axial stress in slab forging (sliding) | At the die entry, $x = 0$ |
| $\sigma_x = -Y_f \left(1 + \frac{2\mu L}{h}\right)$ | Axial stress at the center ($x = L/2$) | For long slab, $\tau = \mu p$ |
| $D_f / D_0 = $ fn($m$, $\Delta h / h_0$) | Ring compression test calibration | Empirical / Avitzur chart |
| $\Delta h / h_0 = 1 - h_f / h_0$ | Height strain in ring test | $h_0$ = initial height |
| $m = K_b \, s^n$ | Power-law friction model (rate-sensitive) | High-temperature forging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** In a forging problem, students often forget that when $mL/h > \pi/4$ (approximately 0.785), the friction hill analysis is no longer valid because **sticking** dominates and the slab element assumption breaks down. Always check the validity of the friction-hill equation before applying it.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Die Design:** The friction-hill pressure dictates the **maximum die pressure** a forging die must withstand. Dies are designed with a safety factor (typically 1.5–2.5) above the calculated $p_{max}$ to prevent yielding.
- **Press Sizing:** Total forging force $F = p_{avg} \times A_{proj}$ determines the tonnage rating of hydraulic or mechanical presses.
- **Process Selection:** High-friction operations (e.g., extrusion of titanium) require aggressive lubrication; low-friction operations (e.g., wire drawing through dies) need only mild lubrication.
- **Tribology Research:** Friction measurement methods like the ring compression test are the **gold standard** for laboratory tribological evaluation of new lubricants.
- **Quality Control:** Inconsistent friction in a production line causes inconsistent part dimensions and surface finish, so lubricant performance is a critical QC variable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: The Friction Hill Equation (Slab Method)

### Problem Setup
Consider a short cylindrical billet of height $h$, width $w$ (or diameter $D$), being upset between two flat, parallel dies. The die-workpiece interface has a friction factor $m$ (or coefficient $\mu$ if sliding). We wish to find the **axial stress $\sigma_x$** and the **average die pressure $p_{avg}$** required to continue plastic deformation.

### Geometric and Force Definition
Let $x$ be measured from the center of the billet. Consider an elemental slice of width $dx$ located at position $x$ from the center. The forces acting on this element along the $x$-axis are:

1. **Right face pressure** (acting in $-x$ direction): $p \cdot w \cdot h$
2. **Left face pressure** (acting in $+x$ direction): $(p + dp)(w + dw) h$
3. **Friction shear on top die** (acting in $-x$ if compression moves material to the right): $\tau \cdot w \cdot dx$
4. **Friction shear on bottom die** (acting in $-x$ if compression moves material to the right): $\tau \cdot w \cdot dx$

### Force Equilibrium (along x-axis)
The sum of forces on the element must equal zero in the static case:

$$(p + dp)(w + dw)h - pwh - 2\tau w dx = 0$$

### Simplification Step 1: Neglecting the $dw \, dp$ Second-Order Term
Expand and drop the product of two differentials:

$$pwh + p \, h \, dw + w \, h \, dp - pwh - 2\tau w \, dx = 0$$

$$p \, h \, dw + w \, h \, dp - 2\tau w \, dx = 0$$

### Simplification Step 2: Volume Constancy
Since plastic deformation conserves volume, $w \cdot h = w_0 h_0 = $ constant. Differentiating:

$$h \, dw + w \, dh = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad dw = -\frac{w}{h} dh$$

### Simplification Step 3: Substitute and Combine
Substituting $dw$ back:

$$-p \cdot w \, dh + w \, h \, dp - 2\tau w \, dx = 0$$

Divide through by $w$:

$$h \, dp - p \, dh - 2\tau \, dx = 0$$

### Simplification Step 4: Replace Shear by Friction Law
Substitute $\tau = \mu p$ (Coulomb sliding) and divide by $h$:

$$dp - \frac{p}{h} dh = 2\mu p \, \frac{dx}{h}$$

### Simplification Step 5: Introduce Strain Compatibility
For plastic compression, the differential height strain $d\varepsilon_h = -dh/h$ and the lengthening strain is $d\varepsilon_x = dx/x$ (but we use the die-workpiece length increment). A key geometric constraint from **constancy of volume in a slab of unit length** gives:

$$dx = \frac{x}{h} dh$$

For the simpler case of **sustained forging** (steady-state die pressure), the relationship reduces via plastic flow rule to:

$$dp = \frac{2\mu p}{h} dx$$

### Final Integration
Integrate from the centerline ($x = 0$, where $p = Y_f$ the flow stress) to the edge ($x = L$, where $p = p_{avg}$):

$$\int_{Y_f}^{p_{avg}} \frac{dp}{p} = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{2\mu}{h} dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{p_{avg}}{Y_f}\right) = \frac{2\mu L}{h}$$

$$\boxed{p_{avg} = Y_f \, e^{2\mu L / h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the Friction Factor Form:** Replacing $\mu$ with the friction factor $m$ gives the more general form $p_{avg} = Y_f \, e^{2mL/h}$, which is the **standard KTU board form** for forging analyses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Conversion Between Friction Factor (m) and Coefficient of Friction (μ)

### Mohr's Circle at the Interface
At the die-workpiece interface, the stress state is a combination of normal pressure $p$ and shear stress $\tau$. The Mohr's circle for this state has:
- Center at $\sigma = -p$ (compressive)
- Radius $R = \sqrt{p^{2} + \tau^{2}}$

The **shear yield criterion** states that the maximum shear stress equals the shear yield stress $K$. The maximum shear stress is exactly the radius of Mohr's circle:

$$K = \sqrt{p^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

### Solve for Shear Stress
Rearranging for $\tau$:

$$\tau = \sqrt{K^{2} - p^{2}}$$

### Apply Definitions
By Coulomb's law: $\tau = \mu p$. By Siebel's law: $\tau = mK$.

Setting these equal:

$$\mu p = mK$$

Square both sides:

$$\mu^{2} p^{2} = m^{2} K^{2}$$

Substitute $K^{2} = p^{2} + \tau^{2} = p^{2} + \mu^{2} p^{2} = p^{2}(1 + \mu^{2})$:

$$\mu^{2} p^{2} = m^{2} p^{2} (1 + \mu^{2})$$

Cancel $p^{2}$:

$$\mu^{2} = m^{2} + m^{2} \mu^{2}$$

$$\mu^{2}(1 - m^{2}) = m^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{m}{\sqrt{1 - m^{2}}}}$$

And by inversion:

$$\boxed{m = \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{1 + \mu^{2}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit Case Check:** As $m \to 1$ (full sticking), $\mu \to \infty$ — confirming that Coulomb's law becomes unbounded, which is why $m$ is preferred. As $m \to 0$, $\mu \to 0$ — confirming both formulations agree at low friction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Sliding vs. Sticking Transition Pressure

The interface transitions from sliding to sticking when $\mu p$ reaches the shear yield stress $K$:

$$\mu p_{crit} = K \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{crit} = \frac{K}{\mu} = \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}\,\mu}$$

For a forging operation where the local pressure $p$ exceeds $p_{crit}$, the interface is in the sticking regime and $\tau$ is capped at $K$. Below $p_{crit}$, sliding friction with $\tau = \mu p$ holds.

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4: The Ring Compression Test — Theoretical Background

### Test Setup
A flat ring specimen of initial inner diameter $D_0$, outer diameter $D_0 + w_0$, and height $h_0$ is compressed axially between two flat dies.

### Velocity Field Assumption (Upper Bound)
By symmetry, material flows radially. Assume a uniform radial velocity field. The plastic strain rate is concentrated near the corners. The internal diameter response depends on the friction:

- **Low friction (m small):** Material flows preferentially inward → ID decreases.
- **High friction (m large, near 1):** Material at the die-workpiece interface is "stuck" → outer diameter grows while ID **increases** (barrel forms).

### The Avitzur / Male-Cockcroft Calibration
Using the upper-bound theorem with an admissible velocity field, the relationship between height reduction $\Delta h / h_0$, inner diameter change $(D_0 - D_f) / D_0$, and friction factor $m$ is derived. The analytical result is:

$$\frac{D_f}{D_0} = \left[1 + \frac{h_0}{D_0}\sqrt{\frac{2m}{\sqrt{3}}} \cdot f(\varepsilon_h)\right]^{-1}$$

where $f(\varepsilon_h)$ is a function of the logarithmic height strain $\varepsilon_h = \ln(h_f/h_0)$. In practice, the relationship is presented as a **calibration chart** (Avitzur curves) in every metal forming textbook.

### Step-by-Step Procedure for the KTU Exam
1. Measure the initial ring dimensions: $D_0$, $w_0$, $h_0$.
2. Compress the ring to a known height $h_f$.
3. Measure the new inner diameter $D_f$.
4. Compute the percent height reduction: $\% \Delta h = (h_0 - h_f)/h_0 \times 100\%$.
5. Compute the percent ID change: $\% \Delta D = (D_f - D_0)/D_0 \times 100\%$.
6. Read the friction factor $m$ from the calibrated Avitzur chart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** The ring compression test is one of the most frequently asked **descriptive** questions in KTU exams. Always state: (i) principle, (ii) specimen geometry, (iii) procedure, (iv) the meaning of inward vs. outward barreling, and (v) the role of the calibration curve.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Friction Model

| **Process Condition** | **Friction Model to Use** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Hot forging of steel, $T > 0.6 T_m$ | Sticking: $\tau = K$ ($m = 1$) | High pressure, oxide scale may be present, sliding unstable |
| Cold forging with phosphate + soap | Sliding: $\tau = \mu p$, $m \approx 0.05$–$0.10$ | Excellent boundary lubrication |
| Cold extrusion (hydrostatic) | Sliding: $m \approx 0.05$ | Fluid film separates the surfaces |
| Hot extrusion of aluminum | Mixed: $m = 0.2$–$0.4$ | Lubricant decomposes at high T |
| Rolling (cold) | Sliding: $\mu \approx 0.05$–$0.10$ | Liquid lubricant film |
| Rolling (hot) | Sticking/Neutral: $m = 0.3$–$0.5$ | No lubricant, oxide scale |
| Deep drawing | Mixed: $m \approx 0.10$–$0.20$ | Controlled friction — too low causes wrinkling |
| Wire drawing | Boundary: $m \approx 0.05$ | Soap/dry soap film |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Friction Regime Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Interface Under Pressure p] --> B{Compare μp to K}
    B -- μp < K --> C[Sliding Friction Regime]
    C --> D[Apply τ = μp]
    C --> E[Use m = μ / sqrt of 1 + μ squared]
    B -- μp >= K --> F[Sticking Friction Regime]
    F --> G[Apply τ = K]
    F --> H[Use m = 1.0]
    D --> I[Compute Die Pressure from Friction Hill]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compare with Die Yield Strength]
    J -- Safe --> K[Process Feasible]
    J -- Unsafe --> L[Use Better Lubricant or Larger Press]
```

## 4.2 Lubrication Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Boundary Lubrication]
        A1[Thin adsorbed film] --> A2[Few molecular layers]
        A2 --> A3[Low load capacity]
    end
    subgraph B[Hydrodynamic Lubrication]
        B1[Thick fluid film] --> B2[Full separation]
        B2 --> B3[No asperity contact]
    end
    subgraph C[Mixed Lubrication]
        C1[Partial film] --> C2[Some asperity contact]
        C2 --> C3[Transitional regime]
    end
    subgraph D[Solid Film Lubrication]
        D1[Graphite or MoS2] --> D2[High temperature use]
        D2 --> D3[Self-lubricating layer]
    end
    A --> E[Metal Forming Application]
    B --> E
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Choose by Process Type}
    F -- Cold Forging --> A
    F -- Hydrostatic Extrusion --> B
    F -- Hot Forging --> C
    F -- Hot Extrusion of Ti --> D
```

## 4.3 Ring Compression Test — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ring Specimen D0, w0, h0] --> B[Place Between Flat Dies]
    B --> C[Apply Compression to h_f]
    C --> D{Measured Inner Diameter}
    D -- D_f decreases --> E[Low Friction - m small]
    D -- D_f increases --> F[High Friction - m large, near 1]
    E --> G[Read m from Avitzur Chart]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Tabulate m vs Lubricant Type]
    H --> I[Compare with Reference Values]
    I -- m in expected range --> J[Lubricant Approved]
    I -- m outside range --> K[Re-formulate Lubricant]
```

## 4.4 Friction Hill — Visual Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Centerline p equals Y_f] --> B[Pressure Rises Exponentially Toward Edge]
    B --> C[Peak Pressure p_avg at x equals L/2]
    C --> D[Exponential Decay to p equals Y_f at x equals L]
    D --> E[Total Area = Forging Force]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The friction hill is **bell-shaped** (actually the absolute value of an exponential rise-and-fall). The total forging force is the **area under the curve**. The peak pressure $p_{avg}$ at the die edge often exceeds $Y_f$ by a factor of 2 to 5 in practical forging, depending on the $L/h$ ratio and friction.
