# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six characteristic features of metal forming processes.**

**Model Answer (6 key points, ~150 words):**

1. **State of stress:** The dominant stress state in the deformation zone — compressive in forging/rolling/extrusion, tensile in drawing, and combined in sheet metal operations.
2. **Strain magnitude:** True strain $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ varies from 0.1 (rolling) to 5.0 (severe extrusion).
3. **Strain rate:** Range of 0.1 to 1000 s⁻¹ depending on process speed.
4. **Temperature regime:** Hot working (>0.6 T_m) enables recrystallization; cold working (<0.4 T_m) causes strain hardening.
5. **Deformation zone geometry:** Defines the volume of material undergoing plastic flow — arc-shaped in rolling, conical in extrusion/drawing, cylindrical in forging.
6. **Tool-workpiece interface friction:** Governs redundant work and force amplification through the friction hill effect.
7. **Forming force and power requirement:** Determines press/mill capacity, motor rating, and energy cost.
8. **Product characteristics:** Dimensional accuracy, surface finish, grain flow, mechanical properties, and residual stresses.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "characteristic features" with "advantages and disadvantages" or "applications." Stay strictly on the **process-defining parameters** — stress, strain, strain rate, temperature, friction, geometry, force, and product features.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare the characteristic features of hot working and cold working in tabular form.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | > 0.6 T_m | < 0.4 T_m |
| Recrystallization | Occurs | Does not occur |
| Flow stress | Low | High |
| Force required | Low | High |
| Strain hardening | Negligible | Significant |
| Dimensional accuracy | Poor | Excellent |
| Surface finish | Poor (oxide scales) | Good (bright) |
| Ductility | High | Reduced |
| Grain structure | Refined equiaxed | Elongated, fibrous |
| Mechanical properties | Lower strength, higher toughness | Higher strength, lower toughness |
| Equipment | Larger, less rigid | Smaller, more rigid |
| Typical products | Blooms, slabs, rails, structural sections | Sheet metal, wire, thin tubes, precision parts |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A. (a) Explain in detail the seven characteristic features of metal forming processes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The seven characteristic features that uniquely identify a metal forming process are:

**1. State of Stress in the Deformation Zone:**
The triaxial stress state dictates workability. Forging, rolling, and extrusion are dominated by **compressive (often hydrostatic) stress**, which suppresses fracture and permits very large strains. Drawing is dominated by **tensile stress**, which limits the reduction per pass. Sheet metal bending has a combination of tension on the outer fiber and compression on the inner fiber, with a neutral axis at the middle.

**2. Strain and Strain Magnitude:**
Strain is the geometric measure of deformation. True strain $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ is the standard KTU convention. Different processes operate in different strain ranges:
- Rolling: 0.1–0.5
- Forging: 0.2–2.0
- Extrusion: 1.0–5.0
- Drawing: 0.2–0.8
- Sheet forming: 0.1–1.0

**3. Strain Rate:**
Strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon} = d\varepsilon/dt$ controls whether the material behaves in a rate-insensitive (low $m$) or rate-sensitive (high $m$, e.g., superplastic) manner. Hot processes typically run at higher strain rates (1–1000 s⁻¹) than cold drawing (1–100 s⁻¹).

**4. Temperature Regime:**
Hot working occurs **above the recrystallization temperature (≈ 0.6 T_m)**, eliminating strain hardening. Cold working is done **below ≈ 0.4 T_m** with full strain hardening. The temperature regime influences flow stress, force, surface quality, and grain structure.

**5. Deformation Zone Geometry:**
The shape and size of the plastically deforming region. In rolling, it is an arc of length $L = \sqrt{R\Delta h}$. In extrusion and drawing, it is a cone of semi-angle $\alpha$. In forging, it is a cylinder being compressed between flat or shaped dies.

**6. Friction at the Tool–Workpiece Interface:**
Friction is characterized by the friction factor $m$ or the coefficient $\mu$. It causes the **friction hill** — an amplification of the forming pressure given by $(1 + \mu L/2h)$. High friction increases force, non-uniformity, and tool wear.

**7. Forming Force, Power, and Product Characteristics:**
The required force and power are derived from flow stress, geometry, and friction. The product is characterized by dimensional accuracy (cold > hot), surface finish (cold > hot), mechanical properties (cold stronger, less ductile), and grain flow (favorable in forging).

---

**A. (b) A 50 mm thick aluminum slab is rolled in a single pass to 40 mm in a mill with roll radius 250 mm. The roll speed is 30 rpm. Determine (i) the true strain, (ii) the average strain rate, and (iii) the roll force if the average flow stress is 120 MPa and the coefficient of friction is 0.1. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $h_0 = 50$ mm
- $h_f = 40$ mm
- $R = 250$ mm
- $N = 30$ rpm
- $K_f = 120$ MPa
- $\mu = 0.1$

**Step 1 — True Strain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{50}{40}\right) = \ln(1.25) \\
\varepsilon &= 0.2231
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Contact Length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= h_0 - h_f = 50 - 40 = 10 \text{ mm} \\
L &= \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 10} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Roll Surface Velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{2\pi R N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 250 \times 30}{60} \\
v &= 785.4 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Strain Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{avg} &= \frac{h_0 + h_f}{2} = \frac{50 + 40}{2} = 45 \text{ mm} \\
\dot{\varepsilon} &= \frac{v \cdot L}{R \cdot h_{avg}} = \frac{785.4 \times 50}{250 \times 45} \\
\dot{\varepsilon} &= 3.49 \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Average Roll Pressure (Friction Hill):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{avg} &= K_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_{avg}}\right) = 120 \left(1 + \frac{0.1 \times 50}{2 \times 45}\right) \\
p_{avg} &= 120 \left(1 + \frac{5}{90}\right) = 120 \times 1.0556 \\
p_{avg} &= 126.67 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Roll Force (per unit width first, then multiplied by width if given):**
Assuming unit width $w = 1$ mm (since width not specified):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= p_{avg} \cdot L \cdot w = 126.67 \times 50 \times 1 \\
F &= 6333.5 \text{ N per mm width}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\varepsilon = 0.223$
- (ii) $\dot{\varepsilon} = 3.49$ s⁻¹
- (iii) $F = 6333.5$ N (per mm width)

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**B. (a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the characteristic features of forging, rolling, extrusion, and drawing processes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Forging:**
- **Stress state:** Compressive (uniaxial or triaxial depending on die shape)
- **Strain:** 0.2 to 2.0
- **Strain rate:** 1 to 10³ s⁻¹
- **Temperature:** Hot (most common) or cold
- **Material flow:** Lateral spread + axial compression
- **Tool–workpiece interface:** Open or closed dies; friction is critical
- **Products:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, turbine disks
- **Feature advantage:** Superior grain flow → high fatigue strength

**Rolling:**
- **Stress state:** Compressive between two counter-rotating rolls
- **Strain:** 0.1 to 0.5 per pass
- **Strain rate:** 1 to 10³ s⁻¹
- **Temperature:** Hot (primary rolling) or cold (finish rolling)
- **Material flow:** Through the roll gap, arc-shaped deformation zone
- **Tool–workpiece interface:** Friction draws material into the rolls ("no-slip condition")
- **Products:** Plates, sheets, strips, I-beams, rails
- **Feature advantage:** Continuous, high-throughput production

**Extrusion:**
- **Stress state:** Hydrostatic compression, with final tensile stress at die exit
- **Strain:** 1.0 to 5.0 (very high)
- **Strain rate:** 0.1 to 10² s⁻¹
- **Temperature:** Hot (most metals) or cold (soft alloys like Al, Cu)
- **Material flow:** Directional through a die orifice
- **Tool–workpiece interface:** Container friction + die friction
- **Products:** Tubes, complex hollow sections, window frames
- **Feature advantage:** Complex cross-sections in a single operation

**Drawing:**
- **Stress state:** Tensile (the only tensile-dominant bulk process)
- **Strain:** 0.2 to 0.8 per pass
- **Strain rate:** 1 to 10² s⁻¹
- **Temperature:** Almost always cold
- **Material flow:** Axial through a converging die
- **Tool–workpiece interface:** Die friction and back tension
- **Products:** Wires, thin tubes, precision rods
- **Feature advantage:** Excellent dimensional accuracy and surface finish

---

**B. (b) A copper billet of diameter 100 mm is extruded to a rod of diameter 40 mm at a flow stress of 200 MPa. Calculate (i) the extrusion ratio, (ii) the true strain, and (iii) the extrusion force assuming no friction. Take the billet length as 200 mm. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D_0 = 100$ mm
- $D_f = 40$ mm
- $K_f = 200$ MPa
- $L_0 = 200$ mm
- No friction

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 100^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_f &= \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 40^2 = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Extrusion Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{7853.98}{1256.64} = 6.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — True Strain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln(R) = \ln(6.25) \\
\varepsilon &= 1.8326
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Extrusion Force (no friction, using the standard formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A_0 \cdot K_f \cdot \ln(R) \\
F &= 7853.98 \times 200 \times 1.8326 \\
F &= 2{,}878{,}834 \text{ N} \approx 2879 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $R = 6.25$
- (ii) $\varepsilon = 1.833$
- (iii) $F = 2879$ kN

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Errors in This Question:**
> 1. Students use **engineering strain** $(D_0 - D_f)/D_0$ instead of **true strain** — KTU strictly requires $\ln(A_0/A_f)$.
> 2. Forgetting that the **formula $F = A_0 K_f \ln R$** applies only when **friction is neglected**. If friction is included, multiply by $(1 + 2L_0/D_0)$ for container friction.
> 3. Mixing up **extrusion ratio** $R = A_0/A_f$ (always > 1) with **reduction ratio** $r = (A_0 - A_f)/A_0$ (between 0 and 1).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for "Characteristic Features" — KTU 2024**

- [x] **Definition:** Characteristic features = the parameters that uniquely identify a metal forming process (stress state, strain, strain rate, temperature, geometry, friction, force, product).
- [x] **Stress state classification:** Forging/Rolling/Extrusion = compressive; Drawing = tensile; Sheet = combined.
- [x] **True strain formula:** $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f) = \ln(A_0/A_f)$.
- [x] **Strain rate (rolling):** $\dot{\varepsilon} = v \cdot L / (R \cdot h_{avg})$.
- [x] **Flow stress:** $\sigma_f = K\varepsilon^n \dot{\varepsilon}^m \exp(Q/RT)$ — increases with strain and strain rate, decreases with temperature.
- [x] **Average roll pressure (friction hill):** $p_{avg} = K_f(1 + \mu L/2h_{avg})$.
- [x] **Roll force:** $F = p_{avg} \cdot L \cdot w$.
- [x] **Forging force (max):** $F = K_f A_0 e^{\varepsilon}$.
- [x] **Extrusion force (no friction):** $F = A_0 K_f \ln R$.
- [x] **Drawing force:** $F = A_f K_f \ln R (1 + \mu \cot \alpha)$.
- [x] **Hot working temperature:** > 0.6 T_m → no strain hardening, low force, poor accuracy, refined grains.
- [x] **Cold working temperature:** < 0.4 T_m → strain hardening, high force, high accuracy, stronger but less ductile.
- [x] **Extrusion ratio:** $R = A_0/A_f$; **True strain:** $\varepsilon = \ln R$.
- [x] **Drawing pass design:** $\sigma_d \le \sigma_f$ gives $R_{max} = e^{1/(1+\mu\cot\alpha)}$.
- [x] **Process-product matching:** Forging (discrete, strong parts); Rolling (continuous sections); Extrusion (complex profiles); Drawing (wires, tubes); Sheet forming (panels, cans).

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Answer Writing:**
> Whenever asked about "characteristic features," always structure your answer using the **seven-pillar framework** (stress → strain → strain rate → temperature → geometry → friction → force/product). Examiners reward systematic, structured answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capabilities and Applications - V-bend
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Capabilities and Applications - V-bend # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **V-bend** is a sheet-metal forming operation performed in a **press brake** (or bending press) using a **punch-and-die set in which the die cavity has a V-shaped opening** and the punch has a matching male V-profile. The sheet-metal blank is laid over the die opening, and the descending punch forces the blank into the die cavity, plastically deforming it around the punch tip to produce a straight-line bend of a specified included angle along a single bend axis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** > *V-bending is a three-point bending process in which the sheet is bent between a V-die and a V-punch. It is the most versatile bending operation and is used for producing components of varying bend angles, bend radii, and lengths on a single machine simply by changing the tooling.* The four geometric parameters that completely define a V-bend are: 1. **Bend angle (θ)** — included angle of the finished part. 2. **Bend radius (Rᵢ)** — inner radius of the bend. 3. **Material thickness (t)** — sheet gauge. 4. **Die opening (W)** / **V-die width** — die throat dimension. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Book Spine & Bookmark" Model:** > > Imagine you place a **bookmark** flat on a table. Now press the **spine of a closed book** (the punch) down onto the middle of the bookmark, with the bookmark resting over a narrow gap (the V-die). The bookmark snaps into a sharp V-shape. Move the book slightly to one side — you get an *asymmetric* V. Lift the book up only partway — the bookmark has a *shallow* (obtuse) bend. Push the book all the way down so the punch tip touches the die bottom — the bookmark is *flattened (coined)* into a sharp angle. > > **The book = punch**, the **table-gap = V-die**, the **bookmark = sheet metal blank**. This mental picture maps perfectly onto a press brake V-bend. ## 1.3 Capabilities — What a V-bend CAN Do A V-bend operation, in its standard configuration on a press brake, possesses the following **capabilities**: - **Range of bend angles:** From very **acute (~30°)** to fully **flat (180°)** in bottoming, covering the entire **obtuse-to-acute spectrum**. - **Variable bend radii:** Achieved by changing punch tip radius (Rₚ). - **Multiple bends per part:** Sequential indexing produces channels, hats, U-channels, Z-sections. - **Wide thickness range:** From **0.4 mm** (foils) to **25 mm** (heavy plate) using appropriate tonnage. - **Mixed materials:** Mild steel, stainless steel, aluminium, copper, brass, HSLA. - **Long parts:** Bends up to **6 m** in length on tandem press brakes. - **Quick tool change:** Modular punches & dies (American, European, Wila, Promecam tool systems). - **Adjustable angle:** Same die can produce different angles by varying punch penetration (air bending). ## 1.4 Limitations — What a V-bend CANNOT Do > [!WARNING] > **Boundary Constraints of V-bending:** > - **Sharp inside radii < sheet thickness (t)** cause outer-surface cracking → requires coining or larger R. > - **Bend line on existing bend** → springback compounds; needs compensation (overbend). > - **Hole/emboss near bend line** → distortion; minimum distance rule (see Section 3). > - **Bend edge of previously sheared edge** → burr-induced cracking; deburr or shift bend line. > - **Very thick plates (>25 mm)** → tonnage requirements exceed standard press brakes → must use folding machines or press forging. ## 1.5 Industrial Applications V-bending is the **workhorse** of sheet-metal fabrication. Applications span: | Industry Sector | Typical V-Bent Component | |---|---| | **Automotive** | Door frames, chassis cross-members, seat brackets, bumper reinforcements | | **Aerospace** | Ribs, spars, brackets, fuselage skin doublers | | **HVAC** | Duct flanges, elbows, transition pieces | | **Electrical Enclosures** | Cabinet frames, server-rack chassis, switchgear panels | | **White Goods** | Washing-machine tubs, refrigerator panels, oven housings | | **Furniture (Steel)** | Drawer cabinets, locker bodies, shelving | | **Agricultural** | Plough bodies, harvester panels, grain-bin rings | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A V-bend is executed in three sequential mechanical phases: **Phase A — Elastic Contact (Punch Approach):** - The punch descends and makes **line contact** with the blank resting across the die opening. - The blank deforms **elastically**; reaction force is small (< 5% of peak). **Phase B — Plastic Wrapping (Air-Bending Mode):** - Punch pushes the blank into the die throat. - Blank wraps around the **punch tip radius (Rₚ)** forming the inner bend radius. - Bend angle θ is a function of **punch penetration depth (h)**: $$h \approx \frac{W}{2 \cdot \tan(\theta/2)}$$ **Phase C — Bottoming / Coining (Optional):** - If punch tip contacts die bottom, the part is **bottomed** (sharp, accurate angle, high force). - If punch tip **coins** the metal against die walls (tonnage > coining threshold), it becomes a **coining bend** (zero springback but ~5× force). ## 2.2 Three Modes of V-Bending | Mode | Description | Springback | Tonnage Factor | Surface Mark | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Air Bending** | Blank does not touch die bottom; bend radius = punch radius | High (~0.5°–3°) | **k = 1.3** | Slight | | **Bottoming** | Punch contacts die bottom; metal forced into die walls | Low (~0°–0.5°) | **k = 1.5–2.0** | Marked | | **Coining** | Punch penetrates metal surface; plastic compression across thickness | Negligible | **k = 3.0–5.0** | Strong impression | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU exams, always assume AIR BENDING unless explicitly stated. Air bending is the most flexible and the most examined operation in the syllabus.** ## 2.3 Geometric Relationship Between Die Width (W), Sheet Thickness (t), and Bend Radius (Rᵢ) > [!NOTE] > **The 8t Rule (Industry Empirical Standard):** > > The recommended **minimum die opening** for air bending is **W = 8 × t**. > > For **W < 8t** → bend becomes a *bottoming* mode, tonnage rises sharply. > For **W > 12t** → bend becomes *springy*, loses accuracy, large bend radius. > > **Inner bend radius** Rᵢ is approximately **equal to punch tip radius** (in air bending): **Rᵢ ≈ Rₚ ≈ (0.16 to 0.20) × W**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print / Memorize This Table — KTU Repeatedly Tests These:** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bending Force | $F = \dfrac{k \cdot L \cdot t^{2} \cdot \sigma_{b}}{W}$ | N (Newtons) | Air bending baseline | | 2 | Force Factor (Air) | $k \approx 1.3$ | dimensionless | For σᵦ in MPa, dimensions in mm | | 3 | Min Die Width | $W_{min} \approx 8\,t$ | mm | Empirical air-bend rule | | 4 | Inner Bend Radius | $R_{i} \approx 0.16\,W$ to $0.20\,W$ | mm | Air bending only | | 5 | Punch Penetration | $h = \dfrac{W}{2\tan(\theta/2)}$ | mm | θ = included bend angle | | 6 | Neutral Axis Radius | $R_{n} = R_{i} + \dfrac{\lambda \cdot t}{2}$ | mm | λ = neutral-axis factor (~0.33 for R/t > 1) | | 7 | Outside Fiber Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\ln(R_{o}/R_{i})}{R_{o}/R_{i}-1}$ | dimensionless | Avoid > 75% RA reduction | | 8 | Min Flange Length | $L_{f} \approx R_{i} + \dfrac{W}{2}$ | mm | For full V-die cavity clearance | | 9 | Bend Allowance | $BA = (\pi/180)\,\theta\,(R_{i} + \lambda\,t)$ | mm | Flat-pattern layout | | 10 | Springback Angle | $\Delta\theta \approx \dfrac{\sigma_{Y}}{E}\cdot\dfrac{R_{i}}{t}\cdot\dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | degrees | Compensation needed | | 11 | Press Tonnage | $T = \dfrac{F}{\eta_{m}}$ | N or kN | ηₘ = mechanical efficiency (~0.6–0.7) | | 12 | Min Hole-to-Bend Dist. | $d_{min} \approx R_{i} + 2t$ | mm | To avoid hole distortion | **Symbol Legend:** - $L$ = bend length (mm) - $t$ = sheet thickness (mm) - $W$ = die opening width (mm) - $\sigma_b$ = ultimate tensile strength (MPa = N/mm²) - $\sigma_Y$ = yield strength (MPa) - $E$ = Young's modulus (MPa) - $R_i$ = inner bend radius (mm) - $R_o$ = outer bend radius (mm) = $R_i + t$ - $\theta$ = included bend angle (degrees) - $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency of press ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Process planning:** Formula (1) determines **press-brake tonnage selection** — a 6 mm mild-steel plate (σᵦ ≈ 410 MPa) bent over a 50 mm die in a 2 m length requires **F ≈ 1.3 × 2000 × 36 × 410 / 50 ≈ 767 kN ≈ 78 tonnes**, well within a 100-tonne brake. - **Tool design:** $W_{min}$ rule selects die inventory. - **Flat-pattern development:** Bend Allowance formula enables unfolding of 3D sheet-metal parts to 2D blanks for laser/plasma cutting. - **Quality control:** Springback prediction informs **CNC back-gauge compensation** programs. - **Cost estimation:** Tonnage × strokes/hour × hourly rate → manufacturing cost. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bending Force in Air Bending We derive **F = k·L·t²·σᵦ / W** from first principles using a simple plastic-bending beam analogy. **Step 1 — Set up the bending geometry.** The sheet acts as a beam simply supported at the die edges, loaded centrally by the punch. The reaction at each die edge is **R = F/2**. **Step 2 — Bending moment at the punch centerline.** Span between die edges ≈ W. Reaction = F/2. Moment at centre: $$M_{center} = \frac{F}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{2} = \frac{F \cdot W}{4}$$ **Step 3 — Plastic bending moment of a rectangular cross-section sheet (b = L, h = t).** For a fully plasticized rectangular cross-section, the plastic section modulus is: $$Z_p = \frac{b \cdot h^{2}}{4} = \frac{L \cdot t^{2}}{4}$$ The plastic moment capacity is: $$M_p = Z_p \cdot \sigma_b = \frac{L \cdot t^{2} \cdot \sigma_b}{4}$$ **Step 4 — Equate applied moment to plastic moment capacity.** Set $M_{center} = M_p$: $$\frac{F \cdot W}{4} = \frac{L \cdot t^{2} \cdot \sigma_b}{4}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for F.** Cancel the /4 on both sides: $$F \cdot W = L \cdot t^{2} \cdot \sigma_b$$ $$F = \frac{L \cdot t^{2} \cdot \sigma_b}{W}$$ **Step 6 — Introduce empirical die-factor k to account for friction, die-edge radius, material anisotropy, and strain-hardening.** In real forming, the bending moment is slightly higher than the idealised plastic moment because: - Material strain-hardens as it wraps around the punch. - Friction at die edges adds lateral load. - Die-edge radius produces contact stress concentration. Empirically, **k = 1.3 for air bending** (bottoming requires k = 1.5–2.0; coining k = 3–5): $$\boxed{F = \frac{k \cdot L \cdot t^{2} \cdot \sigma_{b}}{W}}$$ This is the **KTU board-exam baseline formula**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Bending Force Calculation **Given data (typical KTU problem):** - Material: Mild steel (σᵦ = 410 MPa) - Sheet thickness: **t = 5 mm** - Bend length: **L = 1500 mm** - V-die opening: **W = 40 mm** (= 8 × t ✓ air-bending rule) - Mode: Air bending → **k = 1.3** **Solution:** $$F = \frac{k \cdot L \cdot t^{2} \cdot \sigma_{b}}{W}$$ $$F = \frac{1.3 \times 1500 \times (5)^{2} \times 410}{40}$$ $$F = \frac{1.3 \times 1500 \times 25 \times 410}{40}$$ **Numerator:** $$1.3 \times 1500 = 1950$$ $$1950 \times 25 = 48750$$ $$48750 \times 410 = 19{,}987{,}500$$ **Division by denominator:** $$F = \frac{19{,}987{,}500}{40} = 499{,}687.5\ \text{N}$$ **Convert to kN:** $$F \approx 499.7\ \text{kN} \approx 51\ \text{tonnes}$$ **Required press capacity (with mechanical efficiency ηₘ = 0.65):** $$T = \frac{F}{\eta_m} = \frac{499.7}{0.65} = 768.8\ \text{kN} \approx 78.4\ \text{tonnes}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering decision:** Select the next standard press size → **100-tonne hydraulic press brake**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Punch Penetration Depth for a Given Bend Angle In air bending, the **included bend angle θ** is controlled by **how far the punch descends (h)**. **Step 1 — Geometry of punch entry into V-die.** The die opening is a symmetric V. The punch tip is at depth h below the top surface of the die. The half-width of the die at depth h is: $$\frac{W}{2} - h \cdot \tan(\phi_{die}/2)$$ where φdie = included die angle. For most standard V-dies, φdie = 90°, so $\tan(45°) = 1$. **Step 2 — For a 90° die (φdie = 90°), the bend angle relates to h by:** $$\tan\left(\frac{\phi_{die} - \theta}{2}\right) = \frac{W/2 - h}{t/2}$$ Solving for h: $$\boxed{h = \frac{W}{2} - \frac{t}{2}\tan\left(\frac{90^{\circ} - \theta}{2}\right)}$$ **Step 3 — For thin sheets (t ≪ W), this simplifies to:** $$h \approx \frac{W}{2 \cdot \tan(\theta/2)}$$ (commonly given as the practical formula on shop floors). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Springback Compensation **Step 1 — Define springback.** After elastic unloading, the bend angle **opens** slightly: $$\Delta\theta = \theta_{tool} - \theta_{part}$$ **Step 2 — Elastic strain recovery at outer fiber.** During loading, the outer-fiber strain is: $$\varepsilon_{outer} = \frac{(R_o - R_n)}{R_n} = \frac{\lambda \cdot t / 2}{R_n}$$ where $R_n$ is neutral-axis radius and λ is neutral-axis shift factor. **Step 3 — On unloading, elastic strain is recovered:** $$\Delta\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$ **Step 4 — Geometric translation of strain recovery to angle:** $$\frac{\Delta\theta}{180/\pi} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E} \cdot \frac{R_n}{R_n}$$ Wait — properly: $$\Delta\theta \cdot \frac{R_n}{\pi/180} \cdot \frac{1}{L_{arc}} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$ Standard KTU result: $$\boxed{\Delta\theta \approx \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}\cdot\frac{R_i}{t}\cdot\frac{180}{\pi}\ \text{degrees}}$$ **Step 5 — Tool compensation.** The punch must over-bend by Δθ so that the part springs back to the required angle: $$\theta_{punch} = \theta_{part} + \Delta\theta$$ **Example:** Mild steel, σᵧ = 250 MPa, E = 210 GPa, Rᵢ = 5 mm, t = 5 mm. $$\Delta\theta = \frac{250}{210{,}000}\cdot\frac{5}{5}\cdot 57.296 = 0.0682\ \text{rad} \approx 3.91^{\circ}$$ So for a **90° part**, the punch must travel to produce an **86.1° tool angle** in the press. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose What | Application Requirement | Recommended V-Bend Mode | Tooling | Springback? | |---|---|---|---| | Prototyping, many angle changes | Air bending | Standard V-die + radius punch | Yes — compensate | | High-volume, single angle | Bottoming | Hardened bottoming die | Minimal | | Aerospace precision (zero SB) | Coining | Coining die | Negligible | | Thick plate (>6 mm) | Air bending + large W | Heavy-duty brake | Yes | | Long flange, narrow bend | Air bending with relief | Stepped punch | Yes | | Acute angle < 30° | Two-step bending | Acute-angle die | Yes | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 V-Bend Tool Architecture (Functional Block Flow) The following mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a V-bending setup — i.e., the interaction between the press mechanism, tooling, workpiece, and process parameters. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Hydraulic Press Brake
Ram + Bed"] -->|Provides stroke force F| nodeB["Upper Tool Holder"] nodeB -->|Clamps| nodeC["V Punch
Tip Radius Rp"] nodeC -->|Presses down on| nodeD["Sheet Metal Blank
Thickness t"] nodeD -->|Rests across| nodeE["V Die Opening
Width W"] nodeE -->|Clamped on| nodeF["Lower Tool Holder"] nodeF -->|Reaction force R| nodeA nodeD -->|After forming| nodeG["Bent Part
Angle theta, Radius Ri"] nodeC -.->|Penetration h controls| nodeG nodeH["CNC Controller"] -->|Commands stroke depth| nodeA nodeI["Back Gauge System"] -->|Positions blank axis| nodeD nodeJ["Springback Comp Unit"] -.->|Adjusts h by delta h| nodeA ``` ## 4.2 V-Bend Process State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> LoadBlank LoadBlank --> ElasticContact : Punch descends ElasticContact --> AirBending : Plastic flow begins AirBending --> Bottoming : Optional: punch reaches die floor AirBending --> PartComplete : Punch retracts at target h Bottoming --> PartComplete : Sharp angle achieved PartComplete --> UnloadPart UnloadPart --> ElasticRecovery : Springback delta theta ElasticRecovery --> FinalPart FinalPart --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Tool-Geometry Cross-Section (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Punch
Tip Radius Rp"] -->|"Contact line"| nodeB["Blank t"] nodeB -->|"Wraps over radius"| nodeA nodeB -->|"Settles into cavity"| nodeC["V-Die
Opening W"] nodeC -->|"Die angle phi-die"| nodeC nodeD["Bend Angle theta"] -.->|"Inner radius Ri = Rp"| nodeB nodeE["Neutral Axis Rn = Ri + lambda t/2"] -.-> nodeB ``` ## 4.4 Force & Reaction Vector Map ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Applied Punch Force F (downward)"] -->|F| nodeB["Blank at punch line"] nodeB -->|"F/2 each"| nodeC["Left Die Edge Reaction"] nodeB -->|"F/2 each"| nodeD["Right Die Edge Reaction"] nodeC -->|"R = F/2 upward"| nodeE["Press Bed"] nodeD -->|"R = F/2 upward"| nodeE nodeF["Friction at edges mu x R"] -.-> nodeB nodeG["Bending Moment M = F x W / 4"] -.-> nodeB ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** The four-diagram set above is the **KTU-recommended way to draw a V-bend schematic in an exam** — combine the punch-die geometry (4.3) with the force map (4.4) for a full-marks answer. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define V-bending and list any **four capabilities** of the process. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > V-bending is a sheet-metal forming operation performed on a press brake using a V-shaped punch and a matching V-die, in which the sheet is bent plastically around the punch tip to a specified included angle. > > **Four capabilities:** > 1. Produces bends over a wide range of angles (30° to 180°). > 2. Variable bend radii achieved by changing punch tip radius. > 3. Multiple bends per part by sequential indexing. > 4. Quick tooling change for prototyping and small-batch production. > 5. Works on various materials — steel, aluminium, stainless steel, copper. > 6. Long bend lengths up to several metres on tandem brakes. > *(Any four = 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2.** State the **recommended minimum die-opening rule** for air bending and its significance. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Rule:** The minimum die-opening width for air bending should be **W ≥ 8 × t**, where *t* is the sheet thickness. > > **Significance:** > 1. **Below 8t** — the bend transitions to bottoming mode; tonnage requirement rises sharply and tooling may fail. > 2. **Above 12t** — the bend becomes springy, angle control is poor, and the inner bend radius grows too large. > 3. The 8t rule provides a balance between acceptable bend radius, controlled springback, and reasonable press tonnage. --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Module typically carries **one 14-mark question with internal choice (a) or (b)**. Either (a) or (b) is given. Provide complete solutions for **both** alternatives below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Format** **(a)** Explain the **three modes of V-bending** with neat sketches. Compare their springback, tonnage, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **mild-steel sheet** of thickness **6 mm** and **ultimate tensile strength 410 MPa** is to be bent in **air-bending mode** on a press brake. The **bend length is 2 m** and the **V-die opening is 50 mm**. Calculate: 1. The bending force required. 2. The rated press tonnage assuming mechanical efficiency 0.65. 3. The inner bend radius (using Rᵢ ≈ 0.18 W). 4. The springback angle if σᵧ = 250 MPa and E = 210 GPa. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution A (a) — Three Modes of V-Bending [7 marks]** | Mode | Description | Springback | Tonnage Factor k | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Air bending** | Blank bent around punch tip without touching die bottom. Bend angle controlled by punch penetration depth h. | **High** (0.5°–3°) | **1.3** | Most versatile, prototyping, multi-angle jobs | | **Bottoming** | Punch forces blank into V-die bottom; metal contacts die walls. | **Low** (< 0.5°) | **1.5–2.0** | Production runs with fixed angle, sharper radii | | **Coining** | Punch penetrates blank surface (compression over full thickness); plastic deformation of entire volume. | **Negligible** | **3.0–5.0** | Precision aerospace parts, jewellery, coin blanks | **Sketch (ASCII representation acceptable in exam):** ``` AIR BENDING BOTTOMING COINING /\ /\ ___ / \punch / \ / \ / R \ / \ / \ ___/______\___ __/______\__ __/_______\__ \ W / \ / \ / \_________/ \________/ \/______\/ V-die (open bottom) V-die (closed) Coining die ``` **Key observations:** 1. **Air bending** is most flexible — same tooling produces different angles. 2. **Bottoming** gives accurate angle but requires higher force. 3. **Coining** produces near-net-shape parts with negligible springback but **3–5× air-bending tonnage**, used only for thin, precision parts. --- ### **Model Solution A (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]** **Given:** - $t = 6\ \text{mm}$ - $\sigma_b = 410\ \text{MPa}$ - $L = 2000\ \text{mm}$ - $W = 50\ \text{mm}$ - Air bending → $k = 1.3$ - $\eta_m = 0.65$ - $\sigma_Y = 250\ \text{MPa}$ - $E = 210{,}000\ \text{MPa}$ **(i) Bending force [2 marks]:** $$F = \frac{k \cdot L \cdot t^{2} \cdot \sigma_b}{W}$$ $$F = \frac{1.3 \times 2000 \times 36 \times 410}{50}$$ Numerator: $$1.3 \times 2000 = 2600$$ $$2600 \times 36 = 93{,}600$$ $$93{,}600 \times 410 = 38{,}376{,}000$$ $$F = \frac{38{,}376{,}000}{50} = 767{,}520\ \text{N}$$ $$\boxed{F \approx 767.5\ \text{kN}}$$ **(ii) Press tonnage [2 marks]:** $$T = \frac{F}{\eta_m} = \frac{767.5}{0.65}$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 1181\ \text{kN} \approx 120\ \text{tonnes}}$$ *Select next standard press size → 125-tonne hydraulic press brake.* **(iii) Inner bend radius [1 mark]:** $$R_i \approx 0.18 \cdot W = 0.18 \times 50$$ $$\boxed{R_i = 9\ \text{mm}}$$ **(iv) Springback angle [2 marks]:** $$\Delta\theta \approx \frac{\sigma_Y}{E} \cdot \frac{R_i}{t} \cdot \frac{180}{\pi}$$ $$\Delta\theta = \frac{250}{210{,}000} \cdot \frac{9}{6} \cdot 57.296$$ Step 1: $\dfrac{250}{210{,}000} = 0.001190$ Step 2: $0.001190 \times \dfrac{9}{6} = 0.001190 \times 1.5 = 0.001786$ Step 3: $0.001786 \times 57.296 = 0.1023\ \text{rad}$ Wait — the formula is already in degrees when $\frac{180}{\pi}$ is included: $$\Delta\theta = 0.001190 \times 1.5 \times 57.296 = 0.1023\ \text{rad} \times \frac{180}{\pi}$$ Let me redo carefully: $$\Delta\theta = \frac{250}{210{,}000}\cdot\frac{9}{6}\cdot\frac{180}{\pi}$$ $$= 0.001190 \times 1.5 \times 57.296$$ $$= 0.1023\ \text{rad in dimensionless ratio}$$ But $\frac{180}{\pi}$ converts rad → deg, so the product is: $$0.001190 \times 1.5 \times 57.296 = 0.1023$$ Hmm, that's only valid if we treat $\frac{R_i}{t}$ as dimensionless ratio already. The correct KTU expression: $$\Delta\theta\ (\text{degrees}) = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}\cdot\frac{R_i}{t}\cdot\frac{180}{\pi}$$ $$= \frac{250}{210{,}000}\times\frac{9}{6}\times\frac{180}{\pi}$$ $$= 0.00119 \times 1.5 \times 57.296$$ $$= \boxed{0.102^{\circ}}$$ So the springback is **~0.1°**, which is small because Rᵢ/t = 1.5 is low (thick sheet). The punch must overbend by ~0.1° to compensate. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Discuss the **capabilities and industrial applications** of V-bending. List **at least six capabilities** and explain each with an example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a **neat sketch**, describe the **construction and working of a press brake** used for V-bending. Explain the role of the **back gauge** and **CNC controller**. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution B (a) — Capabilities & Applications [7 marks]** **Capabilities of V-Bending:** 1. **Wide range of bend angles** — From ~30° to 180° on a single machine. *Example:* HVAC duct elbows (30°) to enclosure flanges (180°). 2. **Variable bend radii** — Achieved by changing punch tip radius. *Example:* Punch Rp = 2 mm gives sharp fold; Rp = 25 mm gives gentle curve. 3. **Multiple bends per workpiece** — Sequential indexing produces channels, Z-sections, hat sections. *Example:* Steel locker body (5 bends per panel). 4. **Wide thickness range** — From 0.4 mm foil to 25 mm plate. *Example:* Aircraft skin (1.2 mm) to shipbuilding plate (20 mm). 5. **Mixed material capability** — Mild steel, stainless, aluminium, copper, brass, titanium. *Example:* Aluminium aerospace ribs and stainless-steel kitchen sinks. 6. **Long bend lengths** — Up to 6 m on tandem press brakes. *Example:* Truck chassis side-rails. 7. **Quick tooling change** — Modular tool systems (American, European, Wila). *Example:* Job-shop changeover < 5 minutes. 8. **Adjustable angle in air-bending** — Same V-die produces any angle by varying punch depth. *Example:* Prototype sheet-metal trial runs. **Industrial Applications:** | Industry | Component | |---|---| | Automotive | Door inner panels, seat frames, battery trays | | Aerospace | Ribs, brackets, structural clips | | HVAC | Duct flanges, elbows, transitions | | Electronics | Server racks, switchgear enclosures | | Architecture | Flashings, copings, façade panels | | White Goods | Washing-machine tops, refrigerator liners | --- ### **Model Solution B (b) — Press Brake Construction & Working [7 marks]** **Sketch (description for exam):** ``` ┌──── Hydraulic cylinder ────┐ │ [Ram slides vertically]│ │ ▼ │ │ ┌──────┐ │ │ │ Punch│ │ │ └──┬───┘ │ │ │ ↓ stroke F │ │ ════════════════════ │ ← Bed │ ╲ V-die ╱ │ │ ╲__________╱ │ └────────────────────────────┘ ↑ Back-gauge fingers ``` **Constructional elements:** 1. **Frame** — Heavy C-frame or closed-loop (tandem) steel structure to resist deflection. 2. **Hydraulic / Mechanical Ram** — Provides vertical punch stroke. 3. **Upper Tool Holder** — Clamps the punch; allows quick tool change. 4. **Lower Tool Holder / Bed** — Holds the V-die. 5. **Hydraulic Power Pack** — Supplies pressurised oil to cylinders. 6. **CNC Controller** — Stores bend programs, controls ram depth and back-gauge position. 7. **Back Gauge System** — Motorised X-R axis to position the blank precisely for each bend axis. 8. **Crowning System** — Hydraulic or mechanical compensation for ram deflection across long bends. **Working:** - Operator (or robot) loads the blank against the back gauge. - CNC controller commands the ram to descend to a programmed depth (h) corresponding to the required bend angle θ. - Punch forces blank into V-die; bend forms by air-bending mode. - Ram retracts; back gauge repositions blank for the next bend axis. - Finished part is unloaded; springback compensation has been pre-programmed. **Role of Back Gauge:** - Ensures **dimensional accuracy** of bend location (linear position to ±0.1 mm). - Enables **repeatability** for batch production. - Allows **flange length** control (the distance from bend line to blank edge). **Role of CNC Controller:** - Stores **bend programs** for each part number. - Computes **punch penetration depth (h)** from desired angle θ and tooling data. - Applies **springback compensation** automatically. - Coordinates **multi-axis back-gauge motion** for complex parts. - Provides **production data logging** and **tooling library** management. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch in F formula** — Convert all to mm and MPa (or use SI consistently). Mixing cm with mm loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the factor k** — Students often write F = L·t²·σᵦ / W and lose 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing σᵦ vs σᵧ** — Bending force uses **ultimate tensile strength σᵦ**; springback uses **yield strength σᵧ**. > 4. **Springback angle units** — Final answer must be in **degrees**, not radians. Show conversion explicitly. > 5. **Sketch quality** — A V-bend sketch without labels (W, t, R, θ) gets only 2 of 4 marks for the diagram. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Must-Know Before KTU Exam"** - [ ] **V-bend definition** — three-point bending in a V-die; punch + die + sheet. - [ ] **Three modes:** Air bending, bottoming, coining — know tonnage factors (1.3, 1.5–2, 3–5). - [ ] **Force formula:** $F = \dfrac{k L t^{2} \sigma_b}{W}$ with **k = 1.3 for air bending**. - [ ] **8t rule:** $W_{min} \approx 8t$ for air bending. - [ ] **Inner radius:** $R_i \approx 0.16 W$ to $0.20 W$. - [ ] **Penetration vs angle:** $h = \dfrac{W}{2\tan(\theta/2)}$ (approximation). - [ ] **Springback formula:** $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{E}\cdot\dfrac{R_i}{t}\cdot\dfrac{180}{\pi}$ degrees. - [ ] **Springback compensation:** Tool angle = Part angle + Δθ. - [ ] **Press tonnage:** $T = F / \eta_m$, typically ηₘ = 0.6 to 0.7. - [ ] **Min hole-to-bend distance:** $d_{min} \approx R_i + 2t$. - [ ] **Min flange length:** $L_f \approx R_i + W/2$. - [ ] **Bend allowance:** $BA = (\pi/180)\,\theta\,(R_i + \lambda\,t)$. - [ ] **Capabilities:** wide angle range, variable radii, multiple bends, mixed materials, long lengths, quick change. - [ ] **Applications:** automotive, aerospace, HVAC, enclosures, white goods, structural steel. - [ ] **Limitations:** sharp inside radius, multi-bend springback, hole distortion, very thick plate. - [ ] **Always use σᵦ for force, σᵧ for springback** — never interchange. - [ ] **Always show units** in final numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

topics.title: U-bend
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** U-bend

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **U-bend** (also called a **channel bend** or **U-channel forming operation**) is a fundamental **sheet metal bending operation** in which a flat sheet-metal blank is plastically deformed along **two parallel bend lines** to produce a cross-section resembling the alphabet letter "U". It is one of the most widely used operations in press-brake forming and is classified under **air bending / bottoming / coining** depending on the punch penetration into the die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPET602, Module 2):**
> *"U-bending is a non-straight bending operation in which a sheet-metal blank is bent along two parallel lines to form a U-shaped channel, governed by bend allowance, bend deduction, neutral axis radius (K-factor), springback, and minimum flange length criteria."*

### 1.2 Operational Classification

| Classification Parameter | Category |
|---|---|
| Process Type | Sheet metal plastic deformation (cold/warm forming) |
| Sub-classification | Non-straight / multi-bend forming |
| Stress State | Plane-strain bending |
| Tooling | Press brake with V-die and U-punch |
| Typical Machine | Mechanical / Hydraulic press brake |
| Material Range | Mild steel, stainless steel, aluminium, copper alloys |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Folding a piece of cardboard into a 'U'**
>
> Imagine you are holding a rectangular strip of thick cardboard. If you press down on the **middle** of the strip with your finger while supporting both **ends** on a table edge, the cardboard folds into a **"V"** shape — that is a single bend.
>
> Now, suppose you press down on **two parallel lines** (say, 50 mm apart) on the same cardboard strip. The strip folds into a **"U"** shape, with a flat bottom (the distance between the two bend lines) and two vertical walls (the side flanges).
>
> In metal forming, the **press brake** plays the role of your finger, the **V-die** plays the role of the table edge, and the **punch** has a U-shaped tip that produces the channel. The metal behaves just like the cardboard — except that we must account for **elastic recovery (springback)**, **material thickness shift (neutral axis)**, and the **bending force** required to permanently deform the sheet.

### 1.4 Why is U-Bending Important in KTU Context?

U-bends are ubiquitous in industry because they form the basic building block of:
- **Automotive chassis members** (longitudinal rails, cross-members)
- **Electrical enclosures and control panels**
- **HVAC ducts and frames**
- **Furniture (steel almirahs, racks)**
- **Ship and aircraft structural panels**

Understanding U-bending equips the engineer to estimate **material utilization, tooling cost, press tonnage, and achievable dimensional accuracy** — all of which are core outcomes (COs) of PCPET602.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometry of a U-Bend

A typical U-bend is defined by the following geometric parameters:

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $t$ | Sheet thickness | **0.5 mm – 25 mm** |
| $R_i$ | Inner bend radius | $0.6\,t$ to $2\,t$ |
| $R_o$ | Outer bend radius | $R_i + t$ |
| $R$ | Neutral axis radius | $R_i + K \cdot t$ |
| $K$ | K-factor (neutral axis position) | **0.33 (soft) to 0.5 (hard)**, standard **0.446** |
| $\theta$ | Bend angle (per bend) | Typically 90° for U-bend |
| $L_f$ | Flange length (leg length) | $\geq 8t$ to $10t$ minimum |
| $L_b$ | Bottom length (between bend lines) | Application-dependent |
| $B_a$ | Bend allowance per bend | Computed |
| $B_d$ | Bend deduction per bend | Computed |
| $L_f^{flat}$ | Flat blank length (developed length) | Sum of flanges + bottom + bend allowances |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1 — Material Selection & Blank Preparation
- Sheet stock of thickness $t$ and grade (e.g., SS304, CRCA steel, Al 5052) is sheared or laser-cut to the **flat blank length** $L_f^{flat}$.
- Grain direction is identified; bend lines are ideally oriented **perpendicular to the rolling direction** to avoid cracking.

### Step 2 — Tool Selection
- A **U-shaped punch** (male) and matching **V-die** (female) are mounted on the press brake.
- Die opening $V \approx 6t$ to $12t$ (air bending rule of thumb).
- Punch tip radius $R_p \approx R_i$ (to be achieved on the part).

### Step 3 — Alignment & Clamping
- The blank is positioned so that the two intended bend lines lie over the die edges.
- Backgauge sets the **flange length** $L_f$.

### Step 4 — Bending Action
- Punch descends, pushing the sheet into the die V-opening.
- Plastic deformation localizes at the two bend lines (stress concentration at die shoulders).
- The sheet wraps around the punch tip radius $R_p$.

### Step 5 — Springback Compensation
- Upon unloading, the elastic portion of strain recovers → **springback**.
- Punch is **over-bent** by an angle $\Delta\theta$ to compensate.

### Step 6 — Unloading & Inspection
- Part is removed; bend angle $\theta$ and radius $R_i$ are verified with protractor and radius gauge.

## 2.3 Why "Neutral Axis" Matters (Core Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a sheet is bent, the **outer fibers stretch** and the **inner fibers compress**. Somewhere between them lies a **neutral axis (NA)** that neither stretches nor compresses. The location of this NA is described by the **K-factor**: $K = \dfrac{t_{NA}}{t}$, where $t_{NA}$ is the distance from the inner surface to the NA.

- A **lower K** ⇒ NA is closer to the inner surface ⇒ less material is on the tensile side ⇒ more accurate for sharp bends.
- A **higher K** ⇒ NA is closer to the centre ⇒ typical for soft materials and large radii.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = R_i + K \cdot t$ | Neutral axis radius | mm |
| 2 | $L_{arc} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot R \cdot \theta}{180°}$ | Arc length along NA (bend allowance for one bend) | mm |
| 3 | $B_a = 2\pi (R_i + K t) \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{360°}$ | Bend allowance per bend | mm |
| 4 | $B_d = 2 (R_i + t) \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) - B_a$ | Bend deduction per bend | mm |
| 5 | $B_d = 2R_o \tan(\theta/2) - B_a$ | Alternate bend-deduction form | mm |
| 6 | $L_f^{flat} = L_{f1} + L_{f2} + L_b + B_{a1} + B_{a2}$ | Developed flat blank length (U-bend) | mm |
| 7 | $\theta_p = \theta + \Delta\theta$ | Over-bend angle to offset springback | degrees |
| 8 | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180°}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{R_i}{t} \cdot f(\theta)$ | Springback angle (approximation) | degrees |
| 9 | $F \approx \dfrac{C \cdot k \cdot t^2 \cdot L}{V}$ | Approximate bending force (air bending) | N or kN |
| 10 | $L_{f,min} \approx 0.7V$ to $1.0V$ | Minimum flange length (so punch clears die) | mm |
| 11 | $K \approx 0.65 \cdot \dfrac{R_i}{t} \; \text{(capped at 0.5)}$ | Empirical K-factor (for $R_i/t \leq 0.8$) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $K \approx 0.33 + 0.18 \cdot \dfrac{R_i}{t} - 0.013 \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_i}{t}\right)^2$ | Improved K-factor (DIN 6935) | dimensionless |

Where:
- $C$ ≈ empirical constant (≈ 1.33 for V-bending, ≈ 0.7 for U-bending per side)
- $k$ ≈ ultimate tensile strength of material (MPa)
- $L$ = length of bend (mm)
- $V$ = die opening (mm)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (MPa)
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength (MPa)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The U-bend is the **workhorse** of sheet-metal product design. Mastery of its equations allows a tool designer to:

1. **Reduce material waste** by computing the exact blank length (no trial-and-error shearing).
2. **Select the correct press tonnage**, avoiding under-powered or over-specified machines.
3. **Predict and compensate for springback**, ensuring first-piece accuracy.
4. **Avoid cracking** by validating that $R_i/t$ lies above the material's minimum bend radius.
5. **Standardize tooling** using K-factors and DIN/ISO tables.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Neutral Axis Radius $R$

Consider a sheet of thickness $t$ bent around an inner radius $R_i$. Define a Cartesian coordinate $y$ measured **outward from the inner surface**, with $0 \leq y \leq t$.

- Inner fiber strain: $\varepsilon_i = -\dfrac{y_i}{R_i}$ (compression, $y_i = 0$, so $\varepsilon_i = 0$? — see correction below)

A more rigorous form uses the radius of curvature $\rho(y) = R_i + y$. The arc length of a fiber at distance $y$ from the inner surface after bending through angle $\theta$ is:

$$
L(y) = \rho(y) \cdot \theta = (R_i + y) \cdot \theta
$$

The original length of the same fiber (assumed equal to the neutral fiber length) is $L_0 = R \cdot \theta$, where $R$ is the neutral axis radius.

The longitudinal strain in the fiber is:

$$
\varepsilon(y) = \dfrac{L(y) - L_0}{L_0} = \dfrac{(R_i + y) - R}{R}
$$

We seek the location $y = y_{NA}$ where the **axial force resultant vanishes** (pure bending, no net axial load):

$$
\int_0^t \sigma(y) \, dA = 0
$$

Assuming elastic–perfectly-plastic material for simplicity and using the elastic-strain equivalence $y_{NA} = K \cdot t$:

$$
R = R_i + K \cdot t
$$

$$
\boxed{R = R_i + K \cdot t}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The neutral axis lies at a distance $K \cdot t$ from the inner surface, fraction $K$ of the way through the thickness.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bend Allowance $B_a$

The bend allowance is the **arc length of the neutral axis** over the bend angle $\theta$ (in degrees):

$$
B_a = \dfrac{2\pi R}{360°} \cdot \theta = \dfrac{\pi R \theta}{180°}
$$

Substituting $R = R_i + K t$:

$$
B_a = \dfrac{\pi \theta}{180°} (R_i + K t)
$$

$$
\boxed{B_a = \dfrac{\pi \theta}{180°} (R_i + K t)}
$$

For the standard 90° U-bend, $\theta = 90°$:

$$
B_a = \dfrac{\pi (R_i + K t)}{2}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bend Deduction $B_d$

The **bend deduction** accounts for the fact that the two straight legs of the U-bend, if extended, would have intersected beyond the actual arc — the **deduction** is the difference between those extensions and the actual arc.

Consider one bend. If the two straight portions (each of unit length) were extended as tangents, the total length on either side of the bend vertex is:

$$
L_{tangent} = 2 (R_i + t) \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

The actual material occupying that region is the arc length $B_a$ along the NA. So the **excess** (i.e., the amount to deduct from the flat pattern) is:

$$
B_d = 2 (R_i + t) \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) - B_a
$$

$$
\boxed{B_d = 2R_o \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) - B_a}
$$

where $R_o = R_i + t$ is the outer radius.

> **Note:** Some textbooks define $B_d$ with a sign such that it is **subtracted** from the sum of the outer-edge tangents; the formula above is the **standard positive form** used in CNC press-brake programming.

## 3.4 Developed Flat Blank Length for a U-Bend

A U-bend has **two bend lines**, each with the same angle $\theta$ (typically 90°), and the blank consists of:
- Two side flanges of length $L_{f1}$ and $L_{f2}$ (along the outer edges),
- A bottom of length $L_b$,
- Two bend allowances $B_{a1}$ and $B_{a2}$.

$$
L_f^{flat} = L_{f1} + L_{f2} + L_b + B_{a1} + B_{a2} - B_{d1} - B_{d2}
$$

For a **symmetric U-bend** ($L_{f1} = L_{f2} = L_f$, $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 90°$, $R_{i1} = R_{i2} = R_i$):

$$
L_f^{flat} = 2 L_f + L_b + 2 B_a - 2 B_d
$$

Substituting the standard expressions and simplifying with $\theta = 90°$:

$$
L_f^{flat} = 2 L_f + L_b + 2 \left[\dfrac{\pi (R_i + K t)}{2}\right] - 2 \left[2 (R_i + t) \tan 45° - \dfrac{\pi (R_i + K t)}{2}\right]
$$

$$
L_f^{flat} = 2 L_f + L_b + \pi (R_i + K t) - 4 (R_i + t) + \pi (R_i + K t)
$$

$$
\boxed{L_f^{flat} = 2 L_f + L_b + 2\pi (R_i + K t) - 4 (R_i + t)}
$$

> **Alternative compact form using bend deduction:**
> $$\boxed{L_f^{flat} = 2L_f + L_b + 2B_a - 2B_d}$$

## 3.5 Approximate Springback Compensation

Springback arises because the elastic strain $\varepsilon_e = \sigma_y / E$ recovers on unloading. For a bent sheet, the change in curvature gives an angular springback:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta\theta}{\theta} \approx \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R_i}{t} \quad \text{(small-deflection approximation)}
$$

So the punch must over-bend to:

$$
\theta_p = \theta + \Delta\theta
$$

For **mild steel** ($\sigma_y \approx 250$ MPa, $E \approx 210$ GPa) with $R_i = 2t$ and $\theta = 90°$:

$$
\Delta\theta \approx \dfrac{250}{210000} \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{180°}{\pi} \approx 0.137° \text{ (per 90° bend)}
$$

In practice, an empirical multiplier of **1.5×–2×** is used to account for work-hardening and friction.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Model Question):** A symmetric U-bend is to be produced in a **2 mm thick cold-rolled steel (CRCA) sheet**. The required inner bend radius is $R_i = 4$ mm. Each side flange must be 30 mm long, and the bottom of the U must be 50 mm. The bend angle on each side is 90°. Take $K = 0.446$ (standard for soft steel).
>
> Calculate: (a) the neutral axis radius, (b) the bend allowance per bend, (c) the bend deduction per bend, and (d) the total flat blank length.

**Solution:**

### (a) Neutral Axis Radius

$$
R = R_i + K t = 4 + 0.446 \times 2
$$

$$
R = 4 + 0.892 = 4.892 \text{ mm}
$$

### (b) Bend Allowance per Bend ($\theta = 90°$)

$$
B_a = \dfrac{\pi (R_i + K t)}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \times 4.892}{2}
$$

$$
B_a = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 4.892}{2} = \dfrac{15.371}{2} = 7.685 \text{ mm}
$$

### (c) Bend Deduction per Bend

$$
B_d = 2(R_i + t) \tan 45° - B_a = 2(4 + 2)(1) - 7.685
$$

$$
B_d = 12 - 7.685 = 4.315 \text{ mm}
$$

### (d) Total Flat Blank Length (Symmetric U-Bend)

$$
L_f^{flat} = 2L_f + L_b + 2B_a - 2B_d
$$

$$
L_f^{flat} = 2(30) + 50 + 2(7.685) - 2(4.315)
$$

$$
L_f^{flat} = 60 + 50 + 15.37 - 8.63 = 116.74 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_f^{flat} \approx 116.74 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Practical blank length** (with 2 mm shearing allowance per side): $\approx 120.74$ mm → round up to **121 mm**.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — U-Bend Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flat Sheet Blank] --> B[Shearing / Laser Cut to Lf_flat]
    B --> C[Position on Press Brake Bed]
    C --> D[Align Bend Lines with Die Shoulders]
    D --> E[Clamp via Backgauge]
    E --> F[Punch Descends into V-Die]
    F --> G[Plastic Deformation at Two Bend Lines]
    G --> H[Wrap Around U-Punch Tip]
    H --> I[Springback Compensation - Overbend]
    I --> J[Unload and Inspect]
    J --> K{Acceptable?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Finished U-Bend Part]
    K -->|No| M[Adjust K-Factor and Re-run]

    style L fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009900,color:#000
```
