---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the K-factor in sheet metal bending. Why is it important in U-bend design?**

**Model Answer:**
The **K-factor** is defined as the ratio of the distance of the neutral axis from the inner surface of the sheet to the total sheet thickness: $K = t_{NA} / t$. It determines the location of the neutral axis — the fiber that neither stretches nor compresses during bending.

In U-bend design, K is critical because:
1. It governs the **bend allowance** $B_a$ and hence the **developed flat blank length** $L_f^{flat}$.
2. An incorrect K-factor leads to dimensional errors (legs too long/short).
3. Standard tables (DIN 6935, ISO) recommend $K \approx 0.33$–$0.5$; for soft CRCA steel with $R_i/t \geq 1$, $K \approx 0.446$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is springback in U-bending? List two methods to compensate for it.**

**Model Answer:**
**Springback** is the partial elastic recovery of the sheet metal after the bending load is removed, causing the bend angle to **open up** slightly and the radius to **increase** marginally.

**Compensation methods:**
1. **Over-bending** the part by a calculated angle $\theta_p = \theta + \Delta\theta$.
2. **Bottoming or coining** the punch slightly past the theoretical tangent point, thereby inducing extra plastic strain.
3. Adjusting the punch tip radius to be smaller than the desired $R_i$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *U-Bend Blank Length Calculation + Tool Force*

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Modified]`

**(a)** A symmetric U-channel is to be formed from a **3 mm thick aluminium sheet (Al 5052-H32)**. The inner bend radius is $R_i = 6$ mm, each flange length is 40 mm, and the bottom of the U is 80 mm. Both bends are 90°.
Take the K-factor as 0.44 and the UTS $k$ of Al 5052-H32 as **230 MPa**. The die opening is $V = 24$ mm, and the bend length is 250 mm. **(7 marks)**

Calculate:
1. The neutral axis radius $R$.
2. The bend allowance per bend $B_a$.
3. The bend deduction per bend $B_d$.
4. The total flat blank length $L_f^{flat}$.

**(b)** For the same U-bend, estimate the **air-bending force** using:
$F = \dfrac{C \cdot k \cdot t^2 \cdot L}{V}$ with $C = 1.33$, and express it in kN. Also, state two practical considerations when selecting the press tonnage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Blank Length:**

**(1) Neutral axis radius:**
$$
R = R_i + K t = 6 + 0.44 \times 3 = 6 + 1.32 = 7.32 \text{ mm}
$$

**(2) Bend allowance** ($\theta = 90°$):
$$
B_a = \dfrac{\pi \theta}{180°}(R_i + K t) = \dfrac{\pi \times 90°}{180°} \times 7.32 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 7.32
$$
$$
B_a = 1.5708 \times 7.32 = 11.498 \text{ mm}
$$

**(3) Bend deduction:**
$$
B_d = 2(R_i + t)\tan 45° - B_a = 2(6 + 3)(1) - 11.498
$$
$$
B_d = 18 - 11.498 = 6.502 \text{ mm}
$$

**(4) Total flat blank length (symmetric U-bend):**
$$
L_f^{flat} = 2L_f + L_b + 2B_a - 2B_d
$$
$$
L_f^{flat} = 2(40) + 80 + 2(11.498) - 2(6.502)
$$
$$
L_f^{flat} = 80 + 80 + 22.996 - 13.004 = 169.992 \text{ mm} \approx 170 \text{ mm}
$$

**Answer (a):** $R = 7.32$ mm, $B_a = 11.50$ mm, $B_d = 6.50$ mm, $L_f^{flat} = 170$ mm.

---

**Part (b) — Bending Force:**

$$
F = \dfrac{C \cdot k \cdot t^2 \cdot L}{V} = \dfrac{1.33 \times 230 \times 3^2 \times 250}{24}
$$

Step-by-step:
- $t^2 = 9$ mm²
- $C \cdot k \cdot t^2 = 1.33 \times 230 \times 9 = 2753.1$ N/mm
- $L / V = 250 / 24 = 10.4167$
- $F = 2753.1 \times 10.4167 = 28{,}678$ N $\approx 28.68$ kN

**Answer (b):** $F \approx 28.7$ kN. With a safety factor of 1.25 → select a **≥ 36 kN** press.

**Practical considerations for press tonnage selection:**
1. **Material overstrength** — actual UTS may exceed nominal by 10–20%; always apply a safety factor.
2. **Bend length** — long bends require proportionally more force; the press throat depth must accommodate the workpiece.
3. **Tool wear and alignment** — worn dies increase required force.
4. **Springback compensation** — over-bending may require slightly higher force.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Springback & Tool Design*

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Define **springback** in a U-bending operation. Derive the approximate springback angle for a **mild steel sheet** of thickness $t = 2$ mm bent to an inner radius $R_i = 4$ mm through 90°. Use $\sigma_y = 280$ MPa and $E = 210$ GPa. What punch over-bend angle should be programmed? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss with neat sketches the **tooling setup** for a U-bend on a press brake. List **four important design guidelines** for the U-punch and V-die. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Springback Derivation:**

**Definition:** Springback is the elastic recovery of a bent sheet upon removal of the forming load, manifesting as an increase in bend angle $\Delta\theta$ and a slight increase in radius.

**Approximate springback formula (small-deflection):**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta\theta}{\theta} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R_i}{t}
$$

**Substitution** ($R_i/t = 4/2 = 2$):

$$
\dfrac{\Delta\theta}{\theta} = \dfrac{280}{210000} \times 2 = 1.333 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = 0.01333 \times 90° = 1.2°
$$

**With a practical multiplier of 1.5×** (for work-hardening, friction, Bauschinger effect):

$$
\Delta\theta_{actual} \approx 1.5 \times 1.2° = 1.8° \approx 2°
$$

**Punch over-bend angle to program:**

$$
\boxed{\theta_p = 90° + 2° = 92°}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Tooling Setup and Design Guidelines:**

**Tooling Setup (functional block diagram covered in Section 4.3):**
- **U-punch** (male) mounted on the press ram — has a U-shaped tip with radius $R_p$ matching the desired $R_i$.
- **V-die** (female) mounted on the press bed — has shoulders at die opening $V = 6t$ to $12t$.
- **Backgauge** positions the blank to control flange length.

**Four Design Guidelines:**

1. **Die opening $V$** should be **6t to 12t** for air bending — too small causes cracking, too large reduces accuracy.
2. **Punch tip radius $R_p$** should be **≤ minimum bend radius** of the material (≈ $0.5t$ for aluminium, $≈ 0.8t$ for mild steel) but **not smaller** than the material's minimum to avoid surface fracture.
3. **Punch-to-die alignment** must be **±0.05 mm** to avoid uneven deformation and twist.
4. **Die shoulder radius** should be **$0.4V$ to $0.6V$** — sharp shoulders mark the sheet, blunt ones cause slippage.
5. **Hardness**: Punch and die should be hardened to **HRC 58–62** for longevity.
6. **Marking**: Punch and die sets should be **numbered and matched** to avoid mismatch.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in U-Bend Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong K-factor choice** — Using $K = 0.5$ for all materials is a frequent error. For $R_i/t < 1$, the value drops to $0.33$–$0.4$. Always state the assumed K.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract bend deduction** — Many students compute $2L_f + L_b + 2B_a$ but forget the $-2B_d$ term, leading to **over-length blanks** and 1–2 marks lost.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing radians and degrees in the $B_a$ formula. Use $\theta$ in **degrees** with the $\pi/180°$ factor.
> 4. **Ignoring springback** — A U-bend designed to exactly 90° on paper will open to 91°–92° in practice; the examiner will deduct marks if no springback is mentioned.
> 5. **Force formula errors** — Applying $C = 1.33$ for both air and bottoming bending; bottoming uses $C \approx 0.7$–$1.0$.
> 6. **Minimum flange length** — Designing $L_f < 0.7V$ causes the flange to be unsupported and the bend to wander; always check $L_f \geq 0.7V$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **U-Bend — Quick-Fire Revision Points for KTU Exam**
>
> - U-bend = **two parallel bends** in a sheet metal blank → forms a **U-shaped channel**.
> - Core geometric parameters: $t$, $R_i$, $R_o = R_i + t$, $R = R_i + Kt$, $\theta$, $L_f$, $L_b$.
> - **K-factor** range: **0.33 to 0.5**; standard = **0.446** for soft CRCA steel.
> - **Bend allowance** $B_a = \dfrac{\pi \theta}{180°} (R_i + K t)$.
> - **Bend deduction** $B_d = 2(R_i + t)\tan(\theta/2) - B_a$.
> - **Flat blank length** (symmetric U-bend): $L_f^{flat} = 2L_f + L_b + 2B_a - 2B_d$.
> - **Bending force** (air): $F = \dfrac{C \cdot k \cdot t^2 \cdot L}{V}$, with $C = 1.33$.
> - **Die opening** for air bending: $V = 6t$ to $12t$.
> - **Punch tip radius** ≈ $R_i$ (slightly smaller to compensate springback).
> - **Springback** = elastic recovery; compensate by **over-bending** by **1°–3°** for mild steel.
> - **Minimum flange length** $L_{f,min} \approx 0.7V$ to $1.0V$.
> - **Minimum bend radius** to avoid cracking: $R_{i,min} \approx 0.5t$ (aluminium), $\approx 0.8t$ (mild steel).
> - For **90° U-bend**, the bend allowance simplifies to $B_a = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R_i + Kt)$.
> - **Two bends** in a U-bend → multiply $B_a$ and $B_d$ by 2 in the blank length equation.
> - **DIN 6935** is the standard reference for K-factor and bend allowance in European practice.
> - **Press brake** is the standard machine; tooling consists of U-punch + V-die.
> - Always include a **shearing allowance** (1–2 mm per edge) when ordering the flat blank.
> - Material **grain direction** must be **perpendicular** to the bend line to avoid cracking.
> - The U-bend is a **building block** for channels, panels, brackets, and chassis members.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roll Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Roll Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roll Bending** is a *cold (or warm) forming* process in which a flat plate, sheet, or strip is progressively plastically deformed into a curved (cylindrical, conical, or oval) cross-section by passing it between a set of powered rolls. The radius of curvature is controlled by adjusting the relative positions of the rolls.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, roll bending is classified as a **bulk deformation / plate-bending process** that uses *rotational tooling* (the rolls) to impose a *continuous plastic curvature* along the length of the workpiece. Unlike press braking (which is a *discrete* bending operation), roll bending produces a *continuous bend* and is the standard industrial process for fabricating large cylindrical shells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Kinematics of the Process**
> - **Workpiece Velocity:** $\;v = \pi D N / 60$ (where $D$ = roll diameter, $N$ = roll RPM)
> - **Deformation Mode:** $\;$ Pure bending (no significant direct compressive thinning)
> - **Strain State:** $\;\varepsilon_{top} = -\ln(1 + t/2R)$ and $\;\varepsilon_{bot} = +\ln(1 + t/2R)$ (outer fibre tensile, inner fibre compressive)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Dough Roller Analogy":**
> Imagine you are rolling out a long, flat strip of chapati dough between your palms. As you press your palms closer together and push the dough forward, the dough *curves* downward between your hands. The same thing happens in roll bending — the **upper roll** acts as your top palm, and the **two lower rolls** act as your bottom palms. The closer the upper roll presses toward the line joining the lower rolls, the *tighter* the curve of the plate becomes. If you were to use a *fourth* "palm" to pre-bend the ends, you could eliminate the flat tail-ends of the dough — this is exactly the advantage of the **four-roll pyramid bender** over a three-roll setup.

**Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
- Feed a flat steel plate into a machine.
- The machine has 2, 3, or 4 rotating cylindrical rollers arranged like a pyramid.
- As the plate passes between them, it gets *bent* into an arc.
- By adjusting how far the upper roll pushes down, you control *how tight* the arc becomes.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Importance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Roll Diameter ($D$)** | $\mathbf{150 \; mm}$ to $\mathbf{600 \; mm}$ | Affects minimum bend radius |
| **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)** | $\mathbf{200 \; MPa}$ (mild steel) to $\mathbf{1000 \; MPa}$ (HSLA) | Determines bending force |
| **Plate Thickness ($t$)** | $\mathbf{5 \; mm}$ to $\mathbf{50 \; mm}$ | Quadratic effect on force |
| **Bending Radius ($R$)** | $\mathbf{500 \; mm}$ to $\mathbf{20 \; m}$ | Process goal |
| **Springback Allowance** | $\mathbf{2^\circ}$ to $\mathbf{7^\circ}$ overbend | Geometric compensation |
| **Friction Coefficient ($\mu$)** | $\mathbf{0.05}$ to $\mathbf{0.15}$ (lubricated) | Affects feeding torque |

## 1.4 Classification of Roll Bending Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus — Three Principal Types:**
> 1. **Two-Roll Bending** (initial pinching type) — for *preliminary* curvature induction.
> 2. **Three-Roll Bending** — *Pyramid type* and *Pinch type*; the most common industrial configuration.
> 3. **Four-Roll Bending** — eliminates the *flat ends* left by three-roll setups; used for high-quality cylindrical shells.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The roll-bending process can be decomposed into **four sequential engineering logic steps**:

1. **Workpiece Engagement**
   The plate is fed into the *roll gap* (the converging region between the upper and lower rolls). Friction at the roll-workpiece interface draws the plate into the bite.

2. **Plastic Deformation (Pure Bending)**
   As the plate passes over the upper roll, it is forced to conform to a curved trajectory. The *outer fibres* (above the neutral axis) experience **tensile plastic strain**, while the *inner fibres* experience **compressive plastic strain**.

3. **Geometric Locking**
   The plate exits the rolls with a *permanent* residual curvature $R$. This is because the plastic strain in the outer fibres exceeds the *elastic recovery* (springback).

4. **Recirculation (Multi-Pass)**
   For very tight radii, the plate is fed *multiple times* through the same rolls, with the upper roll progressively lowered by $\Delta h$ each pass, until the desired $R$ is achieved.

## 2.2 Why Does the Plate Bend? — The Core "Why"

> The plate bends because the rolls impose a **geometric constraint** that forces the neutral axis of the plate to follow a *circular arc of radius $R$*. The plate cannot occupy the space between the rolls without curving, because the rolls are *rigid*. The bending moment $M$ required to maintain this curvature is:
> $$M = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^2}{4}$$
> This is the *fully-plastic bending moment* per unit width for a rectangular cross-section, valid when the plate has been bent well beyond the elastic limit.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas are stated in SI units. $\;\vert x \vert$ is written as $\vert x \vert$ below using `\vert`.**

### 2.3.1 Geometric Formulas — Radius of Curvature

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| G1 | $\displaystyle R \;=\; \frac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h} - r$ | **Exact** radius of curvature in 3-roll pyramid bending | $a$ = half-distance between lower roll centres; $h$ = upper roll drop; $r$ = roll radius |
| G2 | $\displaystyle R \;\approx\; \frac{a^{2}}{2h} - r$ | **Approximate** (valid when $h \ll a$) | Same as G1 |
| G3 | $\displaystyle R_{\min} \;=\; a - r$ | Minimum achievable radius (occurs at $h = a$) | Same as G1 |
| G4 | $\displaystyle \Delta h \;=\; \frac{a^{2}}{2(R + r)}$ | Roll drop required to achieve target radius $R$ | Same as G1 |

### 2.3.2 Kinematic Formulas — Plate Velocity & Time

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| K1 | $\displaystyle v \;=\; \frac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Linear feed velocity of plate | $D$ = roll diameter [m]; $N$ = roll RPM |
| K2 | $\displaystyle T_{cycle} \;=\; \frac{L}{v}$ | Time to process a plate of length $L$ | $L$ = plate length |

### 2.3.3 Force, Torque & Power Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| F1 | $\displaystyle M_b \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}{4}$ | Plastic bending moment per unit width | $\sigma_y$ = yield strength; $b$ = plate width; $t$ = thickness |
| F2 | $\displaystyle F \;=\; \frac{K \cdot \sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}{2(R + r)}$ | **Bending force on upper roll** | $K$ = geometry factor (0.4–0.7); $R$ = bending radius; $r$ = roll radius |
| F3 | $\displaystyle F_{fric} \;=\; \mu \cdot F$ | Frictional drag force at roll-workpiece interface | $\mu$ = Coulomb friction coefficient |
| F4 | $\displaystyle T_{roll} \;=\; F \cdot r$ | Torque required at each driven roll | $r$ = roll radius |
| F5 | $\displaystyle P \;=\; 2 \pi N \cdot T_{roll}$ | Power required per roll | $N$ = roll RPM |
| F6 | $\displaystyle P_{total} \;=\; n_{driven} \cdot P$ | Total power (sum over driven rolls) | $n_{driven}$ = number of driven rolls (2 or 3) |

### 2.3.4 Springback & Strain Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| S1 | $\displaystyle \frac{R'}{R} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{3 \sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R}{t}}$ | Ratio of elastic-recovered radius to plastic radius | $E$ = Young's modulus; $R'$ = radius after springback |
| S2 | $\displaystyle \Delta R \;=\; R' - R$ | Springback-induced radius increase | $R'$ from S1 |
| S3 | $\displaystyle \varepsilon_{max} \;=\; \frac{t}{2 R}$ | Maximum fibre strain (engineering) | $t$ = thickness; $R$ = bending radius |
| S4 | $\displaystyle \varepsilon_{true} \;=\; \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{t}{2R}\right)$ | True strain at outer fibre | Same as S3 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Boiler & Pressure Vessel** | Cylindrical shells, dished ends, cone segments |
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull plates, deck sections, superstructure curves |
| **Aerospace** | Aircraft fuselage skin panels (often on 4-roll benders) |
| **Architecture** | Curtain-wall panels, dome roofing, vaulted sections |
| **Oil & Gas** | API 650 storage tank shells, pipeline bends |
| **Power Generation** | Wind turbine tower sections, chimney liners |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Roll bending is one of the few processes that can economically produce *very large* cylindrical sections (e.g., 6 m diameter wind-tower sections) with consistent wall thickness and no welded seams along the curved length.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Radius of Curvature in Three-Roll Pyramid Bending

### 3.1.1 Setup & Geometric Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Set (Standard KTU/Textbook Approach):**
> 1. All three rolls have the **same radius $r$**.
> 2. The lower rolls are **fixed** at positions $(-a, 0)$ and $(+a, 0)$ on a horizontal axis.
> 3. The **upper roll is movable vertically**; its centre is at $(0, h)$.
> 4. The bent plate forms a **circular arc** of radius $R$, with its centre of curvature on the $y$-axis at $(0, -d)$, where $d > 0$ (i.e., *below* the lower roll centres).
> 5. The plate's neutral axis is **tangent to all three rolls** at the contact points.
> 6. Plate thickness $t$ is *negligible* compared to $R$ (thin-plate assumption), so the neutral axis lies on the roll surface.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the contact point on the upper roll.**
The upper roll centre is at $(0, h)$. Since the plate is pressed *against the bottom* of the upper roll, the contact point is at:
$$P_{top} = (0, \; h - r)$$

**Step 2 — Tangency at the upper contact point.**
At $P_{top}$, the tangent to the bent plate is *horizontal* (perpendicular to the vertical line joining the upper roll centre to $P_{top}$). For the bent plate's circular arc, the tangent at any point is perpendicular to the line from the arc's centre. Therefore, the arc centre must lie on the *vertical line* through $P_{top}$ — i.e., on the $y$-axis. By symmetry, the centre is at:
$$C_{arc} = (0, -d), \quad d > 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the arc radius $R$.**
The radius is the distance from $C_{arc}$ to $P_{top}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \text{distance}\big( (0,-d), \; (0, h-r) \big) \\
  &= (h - r) - (-d) \\
  &= h - r + d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Tangency at the right lower roll.**
The right lower roll centre is at $(a, 0)$ with radius $r$. The arc (representing the plate) is *external* to the roll, so the distance from the arc centre to the lower roll centre equals the sum of the radii:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{distance}\big( C_{arc}, \; (a,0) \big) &= R + r \\
\sqrt{(a - 0)^{2} + (0 - (-d))^{2}} &= R + r \\
\sqrt{a^{2} + d^{2}} &= R + r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $R = h - r + d$ into the tangency equation.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{a^{2} + d^{2}} &= (h - r + d) + r \\
\sqrt{a^{2} + d^{2}} &= h + d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Square both sides to eliminate the square root.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
a^{2} + d^{2} &= (h + d)^{2} \\
a^{2} + d^{2} &= h^{2} + 2hd + d^{2} \\
a^{2} &= h^{2} + 2hd
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $d$ in terms of $a$ and $h$.**
$$d = \frac{a^{2} - h^{2}}{2h}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back into $R = h - r + d$ to obtain the master equation.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= h - r + \frac{a^{2} - h^{2}}{2h} \\
  &= \frac{2h^{2} - 2hr + a^{2} - h^{2}}{2h} \\
  &= \frac{h^{2} - 2hr + a^{2}}{2h} \\
  &= \frac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h} - r
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Equation for 3-Roll Pyramid Bending:**
> $$\boxed{\,R \;=\; \frac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h} - r\,}$$
> For the common industrial case $h \ll a$, this simplifies to:
> $$\boxed{\,R \;\approx\; \frac{a^{2}}{2h} - r\,}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Minimum Bending Radius

To find $R_{min}$, we minimize $R(h)$ with respect to $h$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dR}{dh} &= \frac{d}{dh}\!\left( \frac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h} - r \right) \\
              &= \frac{d}{dh}\!\left( \frac{a^{2}}{2h} + \frac{h}{2} - r \right) \\
              &= -\frac{a^{2}}{2h^{2}} + \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting the derivative to zero:
$$
-\frac{a^{2}}{2h^{2}} + \frac{1}{2} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h^{2} = a^{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h = a
$$

Substituting $h = a$ back into the master equation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\min} &= \frac{a^{2} + a^{2}}{2a} - r \\
         &= \frac{2a^{2}}{2a} - r \\
         &= a - r
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation of $R_{min} = a - r$:**
> The tightest possible bend occurs when the upper roll has descended to a depth equal to half the spacing of the lower rolls. Any further descent cannot further reduce $R$ (the geometry saturates). This sets a *hard physical limit* on the capability of a given roll-bending machine.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bending Force on the Upper Roll

### 3.2.1 Bending Moment Required

For a rectangular plate cross-section undergoing *fully plastic bending*:
$$
M_{b} = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}{4}
$$
This arises from the rectangular stress distribution (yield stress $\sigma_y$ acting uniformly across half the cross-section on each side of the neutral axis), giving a moment arm of $t/4$ on each side.

### 3.2.2 Lever Arm (Geometric Factor)

For a three-roll setup, the effective lever arm from the roll contact to the point of maximum moment is approximately $(R + r)$. Hence:
$$
F \cdot (R + r) \;\approx\; M_{b} \cdot K_{geom}
$$
where $K_{geom}$ is a geometric constant that accounts for the actual strain distribution along the contact arc. Empirically, $K_{geom}$ lies between 0.4 and 0.7.

### 3.2.3 Final Force Equation

$$
\boxed{\,F \;=\; \frac{K \cdot \sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}{2(R + r)}\,}
$$

where the **combined constant $K$** (incorporating $K_{geom}/2$ and material strain-hardening effects) typically takes values:
- $K \approx 0.4$ for **soft, ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminium)
- $K \approx 0.6$ for **harder materials** (HSLA, stainless steel)

### 3.2.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Bending Force Calculation**
>
> **Given:** Steel plate, width $b = 1500$ mm, thickness $t = 20$ mm, target bending radius $R = 2$ m, roll diameter $D = 250$ mm, yield strength $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, $K = 0.5$.
>
> **Step 1 — Roll radius:**
> $$r = D/2 = 125 \text{ mm} = 0.125 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Plug into force formula:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> F &= \frac{0.5 \times 250 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 \times (0.020)^{2}}{2 \times (2.0 + 0.125)} \\
>   &= \frac{0.5 \times 250 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 \times 0.0004}{2 \times 2.125} \\
>   &= \frac{0.5 \times 250 \times 10^{6} \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4.25} \\
>   &= \frac{75{,}000}{4.25} \\
>   &\approx 17{,}647 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 17.65 \text{ kN}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Required upper roll drop $h$ (assume $a = 0.5$ m, so $a^{2}/2h$ approximation):**
> $$
> h \approx \frac{a^{2}}{2(R + r)} = \frac{(0.5)^{2}}{2 \times 2.125} = \frac{0.25}{4.25} \approx 0.0588 \text{ m} \approx 58.8 \text{ mm}
> $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Springback in Roll Bending

After the bending moment is released at the exit of the rolls, the plate *elastically recovers*, increasing its radius from $R$ to $R'$:
$$
\boxed{\,\frac{R'}{R} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{3 \sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R}{t}}\,}
$$

**Derivation sketch:**
- During bending, the outer fibre strain is $\varepsilon = t/(2R)$.
- On unloading, the elastic recovery is $\Delta\varepsilon = \sigma_y / E$ (yielding occurs first, then elastic unloading).
- The new curvature is $1/R' = 1/R - \Delta\varepsilon \cdot 2/t$.
- Substituting and simplifying gives the boxed formula.

**Compensation:** The operator must *overbend* the plate to a target radius $R_{target} = R_{desired} - \Delta R$, typically $1^\circ$–$3^\circ$ extra curvature angle for low-carbon steel.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Roll-Bending Type

| Selection Criterion | Two-Roll | Three-Roll (Pyramid) | Three-Roll (Pinch) | Four-Roll |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Minimum achievable radius** | Very large | $R_{min} = a - r$ | $R_{min} = a - r$ | Tightest ($R_{min} < a - r$) |
| **Flat ends produced?** | Yes (large) | Yes (minor) | Yes (minor) | **No** (pre-bent) |
| **Continuous production** | No (batch) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Capital cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | **Highest** |
| **Setup complexity** | Very simple | Simple | Moderate | Complex (4-axis control) |
| **Typical workpiece thickness** | 3–10 mm | 5–40 mm | 5–40 mm | 10–100 mm |
| **Accuracy** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | **High** |
| **Best for** | Trays, ducts | General shells | Long production runs | Pressure vessels, tanks |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All diagrams below are rendered as Mermaid flowcharts. For physical tooling schematics (which Mermaid cannot faithfully draw), a **functional block-level architecture** is provided that captures the *cause-effect chain* of process variables.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Three-Roll Pyramid Bending Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    inA[Flat Plate Infeed] --> rollA1[Lower Roll 1 Fixed Left]
    inA --> rollA2[Lower Roll 2 Fixed Right]
    rollA1 --> bitA[Roll Bite Contact Arc]
    rollA2 --> bitA
    bitA --> rollA3[Upper Roll Movable Downward]
    rollA3 --> bendA[Plastic Bending in Roll Gap]
    bendA --> sprA[Elastic Springback on Exit]
    sprA --> outA[Curved Plate Output]
    ctrlA[Vertical Position Control h] --> rollA3
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Four-Roll Bending Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    pIn[Flat Plate Infeed] --> sR1[Side Roll 1 Fixed]
    sR1 --> fR1[Feed Roll 1 Driven]
    fR1 --> preB[Pre-Bending Roll Adjustable]
    preB --> pressR[Pressing Roll Adjustable Main]
    pressR --> sR2[Side Roll 2 Fixed]
    sR2 --> pOut[Continuously Curved Output No Flat Ends]
    ctrl1[Pre-Bend Angle Control] --> preB
    ctrl2[Pressing Force and Drop Control] --> pressR
```

## 4.3 Functional Process Flow — Roll Bending Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA1[Step 1 Material Selection Plate Stock] --> stepA2[Step 2 Surface Cleaning and Lubrication]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Step 3 Setup Roll Spacing a and Initial Drop h0]
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Step 4 First Pass Through Rolls]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[Step 5 Measure Curvature with Template]
    stepA5 --> stepA6{Desired Radius Achieved}
    stepA6 -->|No| stepA7[Step 6 Reduce h Incrementally Repeat Pass]
    stepA7 --> stepA5
    stepA6 -->|Yes| stepA8[Step 7 Apply Springback Compensation Overbend]
    stepA8 --> stepA9[Step 8 Final Pass and Verification]
    stepA9 --> stepA10[Step 9 Unload Bent Plate for Next Process]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Springback Compensation Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    ipt[Target Desired Radius Rd] --> calc[Compute Required Plastic Radius Rp Rd minus Delta R]
    calc --> pass1[Execute Roll Bending with h corresponding to Rp]
    pass1 --> meas[Measure Actual Output Radius Ractual]
    meas --> check{Ractual equals Rd within tolerance}
    check -->|Yes| done[Process Complete]
    check -->|No| adjust[Adjust h Up or Down Recompute Rp]
    adjust --> pass1
```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions test **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy — typical KTU internal assessment format.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]
**Define roll bending. List the different types of roll bending operations used in industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Roll bending is a metal forming process in which a flat plate, sheet, or strip is passed between a set of rotating cylindrical rolls that progressively impart a continuous plastic curvature along the length of the workpiece. The process exploits the plastic deformation of the material beyond its yield point so that the curvature remains permanent after the rolls release the plate.

**Types (1.5 marks):**
1. **Two-roll bending** (initial pinching type) — for preliminary curvature.
2. **Three-roll bending** — *Pyramid type* (one upper, two lower) and *Pinch type* (one central, two side rolls).
3. **Four-roll bending** — incorporates a pre-bending roll to eliminate flat ends; used for high-precision cylindrical shells.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Explain the phenomenon of springback in roll bending. How is it compensated in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Explanation (2 marks):** When the bending load is released as the plate exits the rolls, the *elastic component* of the deformation recovers. The outer (tensile) fibres and inner (compressive) fibres both partially return toward their original unstressed state, causing the radius of curvature to *increase* from the plastic radius $R$ to a larger elastic-recovered radius $R'$.

**Compensation (1 mark):** The operator compensates by *overbending* — that is, the upper roll is pressed deeper than the calculated value, producing a smaller plastic radius $R$ such that after springback the plate settles at the *desired* $R_{desired}$. Typical overbend values are $1^\circ$–$3^\circ$ extra curvature for mild steel and $2^\circ$–$7^\circ$ for harder alloys.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A. [14 Marks]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the radius of curvature $R$ of a plate bent in a three-roll pyramid type bender, in terms of the half-distance $a$ between the lower roll centres, the upper roll drop $h$, and the roll radius $r$. State the condition for the **minimum bending radius** and discuss its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild-steel plate of width $b = 1.2$ m and thickness $t = 15$ mm is to be curved to a radius $R = 1.5$ m using a three-roll pyramid bender. The roll diameter is 300 mm, the yield strength of the material is 280 MPa, and the geometric factor $K = 0.5$. The lower roll centres are spaced at $2a = 1.0$ m. Calculate:
   (i) the upper roll drop $h$ required,
   (ii) the bending force on the upper roll, and
   (iii) the power required if the rolls rotate at $N = 8$ RPM.
   **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
*(Full derivation follows the steps in Section 3.1 of these notes. Outline:)*

1. State assumptions (1 mark): identical rolls, thin plate, circular-arc neutral axis, tangency at all three contact points.
2. Set up coordinates: lower roll centres at $(\pm a, 0)$, upper roll at $(0, h)$. Arc centre at $(0, -d)$. (1 mark)
3. Identify upper contact point $P_{top} = (0, h - r)$ and compute $R = h - r + d$. (1 mark)
4. Apply external-tangency condition to right lower roll: $\sqrt{a^2 + d^2} = R + r = h + d$. (1 mark)
5. Square and solve: $a^2 = h^2 + 2hd \Rightarrow d = (a^2 - h^2)/(2h)$. (1 mark)
6. Substitute back to obtain:
$$R = \frac{a^2 + h^2}{2h} - r \quad \text{...(Master Equation)}$$
(1 mark)

7. Minimise: $dR/dh = 0 \Rightarrow h = a \Rightarrow R_{min} = a - r$. (1 mark)
*Physical significance:* $R_{min}$ is the tightest bend the machine can produce; further descent of the upper roll does not reduce $R$.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

*Given:* $b = 1.2$ m, $t = 0.015$ m, $R = 1.5$ m, $D = 0.3$ m ($r = 0.15$ m), $\sigma_y = 280 \times 10^6$ Pa, $K = 0.5$, $2a = 1.0$ m ($a = 0.5$ m), $N = 8$ RPM.

**(i) Upper roll drop $h$ (2 marks):**
Using $R \approx a^2/(2h) - r$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
1.5 &= \frac{(0.5)^2}{2h} - 0.15 \\
1.65 &= \frac{0.25}{2h} \\
2h &= \frac{0.25}{1.65} = 0.1515 \text{ m} \\
h &= 0.0758 \text{ m} \approx 75.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Bending force $F$ (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{K \cdot \sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^2}{2(R + r)} \\
  &= \frac{0.5 \times 280 \times 10^6 \times 1.2 \times (0.015)^2}{2 \times (1.5 + 0.15)} \\
  &= \frac{0.5 \times 280 \times 10^6 \times 1.2 \times 0.000225}{3.3} \\
  &= \frac{37{,}800}{3.3} \\
  &\approx 11{,}455 \text{ N} \approx 11.45 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Power required $P$ (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{roll} &= F \cdot r = 11{,}455 \times 0.15 = 1718 \text{ N·m} \\
\omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 8}{60} = 0.838 \text{ rad/s} \\
P_{per\;roll} &= T_{roll} \cdot \omega = 1718 \times 0.838 = 1439 \text{ W} \approx 1.44 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a three-roll bender with **two driven lower rolls**:
$$P_{total} = 2 \times 1.44 \approx 2.88 \text{ kW}$$

---

### **Question B. [14 Marks]**
**(a)** Describe, with neat sketches, the **four-roll bending process**. Explain how it overcomes the limitations of the three-roll pyramid bender, particularly the *flat-end* problem. List four industrial applications of four-roll bending. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper plate of width 1.0 m and thickness 12 mm is bent using a three-roll pinch type bender. The lower rolls are 200 mm in diameter and the distance between the centres of the two side rolls is 800 mm. The yield strength of copper is 220 MPa and $K = 0.4$. The desired bending radius is 0.8 m. Calculate the roll drop $h$, the bending force, and the springback-induced change in radius. Take $E_{Cu} = 110$ GPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Four-roll bending description (7 marks):**

A four-roll bender consists of **four rolls** arranged in a *non-symmetric* configuration (see Mermaid block 4.2):

| Roll | Function | Movement |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Side roll 1 | Fixed support and entry guide | Fixed |
| Feed roll 1 (or 2) | Driven, pulls the plate in | Driven (rotational) |
| Pre-bending roll | Initiates curvature at the entry edge | Movable (vertical) |
| Pressing roll | Imposes the final curvature | Movable (vertical) |
| Side roll 2 | Fixed support and exit guide | Fixed |

**Overcoming flat-end problem (3 marks):**
- In a *three-roll* bender, the plate's leading and trailing ends are *not* in contact with the upper roll, so they remain flat (uncut edges).
- In a *four-roll* bender, the **pre-bending roll** engages the leading end *before* the main pressing roll, curving the entry edge progressively. The same is achieved at the exit by the second feed roll. The result is a **fully curved plate with no flat ends** (3 marks).

**Applications (1 mark each, any four):**
1. Pressure vessel shells (ASME-coded).
2. API 650 storage tank courses.
3. Wind turbine tower sections.
4. Ship hull plates.
5. Boiler drums.
6. Pipeline bends (large diameter).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

*Given:* $b = 1.0$ m, $t = 0.012$ m, $D = 0.2$ m ($r = 0.1$ m), $2a = 0.8$ m ($a = 0.4$ m), $\sigma_y = 220 \times 10^6$ Pa, $K = 0.4$, $R = 0.8$ m, $E = 110 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**(i) Roll drop $h$ (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &\approx \frac{a^2}{2h} - r \\
0.8 &= \frac{(0.4)^2}{2h} - 0.1 \\
0.9 &= \frac{0.16}{2h} \\
2h &= \frac{0.16}{0.9} = 0.1778 \text{ m} \\
h &= 0.0889 \text{ m} \approx 88.9 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Bending force $F$ (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{K \cdot \sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^2}{2(R + r)} \\
  &= \frac{0.4 \times 220 \times 10^6 \times 1.0 \times (0.012)^2}{2 \times (0.8 + 0.1)} \\
  &= \frac{0.4 \times 220 \times 10^6 \times 1.0 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8} \\
  &= \frac{12{,}672}{1.8} \\
  &\approx 7{,}040 \text{ N} \approx 7.04 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Springback-induced change in radius (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R'}{R} &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{3 \sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R}{t}} \\
            &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{3 \times 220 \times 10^6}{110 \times 10^9} \cdot \dfrac{0.8}{0.012}} \\
            &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{660}{110{,}000} \cdot 66.67} \\
            &= \frac{1}{1 + 0.006 \times 66.67} \\
            &= \frac{1}{1 + 0.4} \\
            &= \frac{1}{1.4} \\
            &\approx 0.714
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $R' = 0.714 \times 0.8 = 0.571$ m, and:
$$\Delta R = R' - R = 0.571 - 0.8 = -0.229 \text{ m}$$

The negative sign indicates the *modulus* of the springback correction; in practice the *actual* springback would *increase* the radius, so the compensation would overbend to $R_{target} = 0.571$ m to settle at $R = 0.8$ m after elastic recovery. *(Examiner's discretion on sign convention — 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Roll-Bending Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the roll radius $r$ in the master equation** — Many students write $R = a^2/(2h)$ without the $-r$ term. This is **incorrect**; the roll radius is *always* present. **Penalty: 1–2 marks lost.**
>
> 2. **Using $2a$ instead of $a$** — The "half-distance" is $a$, not the full centre-to-centre distance. Always verify whether the question gives $a$ or $2a$. **Penalty: 1 mark lost in derivation; full credit loss in numerical answers.**
>
> 3. **Mixing up pinch-type vs pyramid-type geometry** — The pinch type has a *central* roll and two *side* rolls, while the pyramid type has one *upper* and two *lower* rolls. The geometry (and hence the formula) is the same, but the *arrangement* in the sketch must be correct. **Penalty: 0.5 mark for wrong sketch labelling.**
>
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion in numerical problems** — $R$ in mm vs $R$ in m. Always convert all dimensions to **metres** before substituting in the SI force equation. **Penalty: 1–2 marks lost.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting the geometric factor $K$ in the force formula** — The formula $F = \sigma_y b t^2 / (2(R+r))$ is for the *ideal* case; in practice, $K$ is included. If the question provides $K$, you **must** use it. **Penalty: 1 mark lost.**
>
> 6. **Not showing the springback step** — When asked for the "actual" radius after elastic recovery, students often quote the *plastic* radius $R$ only. The springback formula $R'/R = 1/(1 + 3\sigma_y R / (Et))$ must be shown. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**
>
> 7. **In four-roll bending answers, failing to mention the pre-bending roll** — This is the *key differentiator* between 3-roll and 4-roll benders. Leaving it out = incomplete answer. **Penalty: 2 marks lost.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
>
> ✅ **Master Equation:** $\;R = \dfrac{a^{2} + h^{2}}{2h} - r \;\approx\; \dfrac{a^{2}}{2h} - r$ (for $h \ll a$)
>
> ✅ **Minimum Radius:** $\;R_{min} = a - r\;$ (occurs at $h = a$)
>
> ✅ **Roll Drop for Target $R$:** $\;\Delta h = \dfrac{a^{2}}{2(R + r)}$
>
> ✅ **Bending Force:** $\;F = \dfrac{K \cdot \sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}{2(R + r)}$, with $K \in [0.4, 0.7]$
>
> ✅ **Bending Moment per Unit Width:** $\;M_{b} = \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}{4}$
>
> ✅ **Power per Roll:** $\;P = T_{roll} \cdot \omega = F \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
>
> ✅ **Springback Ratio:** $\;\dfrac{R'}{R} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{3 \sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R}{t}}$
>
> ✅ **Strain at Outer Fibre:** $\;\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{t}{2R}\;$ (engineering); $\;\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + t/2R)$
>
> ✅ **Plate Velocity:** $\;v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$
>
> ✅ **Friction Force:** $\;F_{fric} = \mu F$
>
> **Critical Distinctions to Remember:**
>
> ✅ **3-roll vs 4-roll:** 3-roll leaves flat ends; 4-roll has a pre-bending roll that eliminates flat ends.
>
> ✅ **Pyramid vs Pinch:** Both are 3-roll setups, but the *spatial arrangement* of the movable roll differs (above/below the workpiece centre line).
>
> ✅ **Springback Direction:** The plate radius *increases* after roll release; hence we *overbend* (decrease the target plastic $R$).
>
> ✅ **Force Scales with $t^2$:** Doubling the plate thickness *quadruples* the bending force — this is why thick-plate roll bending needs powerful machines.
>
> ✅ **Force Scales with $1/R$:** Tighter bends (smaller $R$) require *much* higher forces.
>
> ✅ **Lubrication is critical:** Without lubrication, $\mu$ rises sharply, increasing roll torque and causing surface scratching.
>
> ✅ **Multiple passes:** For tight $R$ that cannot be reached in a single pass, the plate is recirculated with progressive $h$ reduction.
>
> ✅ **Real-world check:** The calculated $R$ must satisfy $R \geq R_{min} = a - r$; otherwise, the machine is *physically incapable* of the bend.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bend Allowance and Bend Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bend Allowance and Bend Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Bend Allowance (BA)?

**Bend Allowance (BA)** is formally defined as the **arc length of the neutral axis** of the sheet metal measured through the bend region. When a flat sheet is bent (folded) along a straight line, the outer fibers of the sheet stretch (tension) and the inner fibers compress, while a specific layer inside the sheet — called the **neutral axis** — experiences zero strain. The length of this unstrained fiber, projected onto the original flat blank, is the quantity of material consumed by the bend.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPET602 – Module 2):**
> *Bend Allowance is the length of the sheet metal that is consumed or "used up" in forming a bend. It corresponds to the arc length measured along the neutral axis of the bend.*

In symbolic form:

$$BA = \text{Arc length of the neutral axis through the bend}$$

## 1.2 What is Bend Length / Flat Length (FL)?

The **Bend Length** (also called the **Flat Length**, **Developed Length**, or **Blank Length**) is the total length of the flat sheet metal blank required *before* bending so that, after the bend is performed, the final bent part matches the required flange (leg) dimensions. It is calculated as:

$$FL = L_1 + L_2 + BA - 2 \times OSSB$$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the two flange lengths, and $OSSB$ is the **Outside Setback**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Accurate computation of $BA$ and $FL$ is critical in **press brake operation**, **sheet metal layout**, and **tool design for progressive dies**. A miscalculation of even **1 mm** in the bend allowance can lead to scrapped parts, dimensional rejection, or costly die re-spots.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "The Folded Paper Bookmark"

Imagine folding a strip of thick paper to make a bookmark. Before folding, the paper is, say, **200 mm** long. After folding it in half, the two visible halves together appear to be **shorter** than 200 mm. The "missing" length did not vanish — it is wrapped around the inside of the fold. The length of paper consumed in the fold itself is the **bend allowance**.

If you wanted to fold the paper so that the two visible legs measured exactly **98 mm each**, you would not start with **196 mm** of paper. You would start with **196 mm + (length wrapped in the bend)**. That extra wrapped length is precisely what the bend allowance accounts for in sheet metal.

> [!NOTE]
> The neutral axis is analogous to the **center fiber of the folded paper** — it neither stretches nor compresses. The bend allowance is the length of paper along that center fiber.

## 1.4 The Neutral Axis — The Hidden Reference

When a sheet of thickness $T$ is bent with an **inside bend radius** $R_i$ (also denoted simply $R$), the sheet material experiences:

| Zone of Sheet | Behavior During Bending |
|---|---|
| **Outer fiber** (radius side) | Stretches — material is in **tension** |
| **Inner fiber** (die side) | Compresses — material is in **compression** |
| **Neutral axis** (interior) | **Neither stretches nor compresses** — length preserved |

The neutral axis lies at a distance $c$ from the inside surface of the bend, where:

$$c = K \cdot T$$

Here, $K$ is the dimensionless **K-factor** (also called the **neutral axis factor**). Typical K-factor values range from **0.33 to 0.50**, depending on material, thickness, and bend radius.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why K-factor matters:** It is *not* a fixed constant. It depends on the material's strain-hardening behavior, the ratio $R/T$, and the bending method (air bending, bottoming, coining). For **soft annealed materials** and **large $R/T$**, $K \to 0.5$. For **hard materials** and **sharp bends** ($R \to 0$), $K \to 0.33$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Key Geometric Parameters of a Bend

Every bend in a sheet metal part is defined by **three independent parameters**:

1. **Inside Bend Radius ($R$):** The radius of the concave (inner) surface of the bend, in **mm**. Set by the punch tip radius in press brake operation.
2. **Bend Angle ($\theta$):** The included angle *after* bending, measured between the two flanges, in **degrees**. A 90° bend produces flanges that are perpendicular.
3. **Material Thickness ($T$):** The stock thickness of the sheet, in **mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Most KTU problems assume the **bend angle is the included angle after bending** (i.e., 90° for a right-angle bend), *not* the angle of deflection. A bend that "opens" a flat sheet by 90° corresponds to a 90° included angle, NOT 180°.

## 2.2 The K-Factor — Detailed Behavior

The K-factor is defined as the ratio of the distance of the neutral axis from the inside surface to the material thickness:

$$K = \frac{c}{T}$$

where $c$ is the location of the neutral axis measured from the **inside** surface of the bend.

**Empirical K-factor reference (commonly used in KTU lab exercises):**

| Material | Soft Condition (R large) | Hard Condition (R small) | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel (SAE 1010)** | 0.50 | 0.33 | **0.44** |
| **Stainless Steel (304)** | 0.50 | 0.33 | **0.45** |
| **Aluminum (5052-H32)** | 0.50 | 0.33 | **0.42** |
| **Copper (C110)** | 0.50 | 0.33 | **0.41** |
| **Brass (C260)** | 0.50 | 0.33 | **0.40** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a KTU problem does not specify the K-factor, the **standard assumed value is K = 0.44** (default for cold-rolled steel per most engineering handbooks).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All Required Equations

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table. Every KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) problem on this topic uses a subset of these equations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bend Allowance (BA)** | $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot T)$ | **mm** | Core formula. $\theta$ in **degrees**. |
| 2 | **Outside Setback (OSSB)** | $OSSB = \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (R + T)$ | **mm** | Distance from tangent point of arc to apex. |
| 3 | **Inside Setback (ISSB)** | $ISSB = \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot R$ | **mm** | Rarely used directly. |
| 4 | **Bend Deduction (BD)** | $BD = 2 \cdot OSSB - BA$ | **mm** | Adjustment when dimensions are to outside. |
| 5 | **Flat Length (FL)** | $FL = L_1 + L_2 + BA - 2 \cdot OSSB$ | **mm** | For a single bend, two flanges. |
| 6 | **Flat Length — General** | $FL = \sum L_i + \sum BA_j - \sum 2 \cdot OSSB_j$ | **mm** | For **n** bends, **m** flanges. |
| 7 | **Neutral axis radius** | $R_n = R + K \cdot T$ | **mm** | Radius to neutral axis. |
| 8 | **Springback angle** | $\theta_{spring} = f(R/T,\ \text{material})$ | **deg** | Compensation in punch angle. |

> [!WARNING]
> Common student error: Using $\theta = 90°$ for a bend that "folds 90°". Always re-read: the included angle after bending is what goes into the formula. If a sheet is bent from flat to form a U-channel, the included angle is 90° — the formula is correct. If the problem says the "bend is 60°" with no further clarification, assume **included angle = 60°** unless told otherwise.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Press Brake Programming:** Modern CNC press brakes compute BA in real time and adjust the back-gauge position automatically. The student must still know the underlying formula to debug errors.
- **Progressive Die Layout:** In a multi-station progressive stamping die, the strip layout is calculated using FL for each bend. Miscalculation causes material to be mis-positioned at subsequent stations.
- **Laser/Plasma Nesting:** Knowing the developed length allows nesting of *unfolded* flat patterns on a sheet — saving material cost.
- **Shipbuilding & Sheet Metal HVAC:** Long bends in ductwork require precise BA values; otherwise ducts don't fit between flanges.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bend Allowance Formula

We start from the **geometric definition**: the bend allowance is the arc length of the neutral axis subtended by the bend angle $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Identify the relevant radius

The neutral axis is located at distance $c = K \cdot T$ from the **inside** (concave) surface of the bend. The inside surface itself lies at radius $R$ from the center of curvature.

Therefore, the radius of the neutral axis is:

$$R_n = R + c = R + K \cdot T$$

**Reasoning (in words):** Imagine a circle of radius $R$ representing the inside bend radius. The neutral axis is a concentric circle just outside it, displaced by $c = K \cdot T$. So its radius is $R + K \cdot T$.

### Step 2 — Convert the bend angle to radians

The full circumference of a circle of radius $R_n$ corresponds to an angle of $2\pi$ radians (or 360°). The fraction of the circumference corresponding to an angle $\theta$ (in degrees) is:

$$\text{Arc length} = R_n \cdot \phi$$

where $\phi$ is the angle in radians. The conversion is:

$$\phi = \theta \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}$$

**Reasoning (in words):** Multiply the angle in degrees by $\pi/180$ to obtain the angle in radians — a standard trigonometry conversion.

### Step 3 — Combine to get BA

$$BA = R_n \cdot \phi = (R + K \cdot T) \cdot \left(\theta \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}\right)$$

Rearranging into the canonical KTU form:

$$BA = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot T)$$

**Reasoning (in words):** The neutral axis neither stretches nor compresses, so its arc length represents the actual material "consumed" in the bend. This is the length that must be added to the flat blank layout.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Outside Setback (OSSB)

The **Outside Setback** is the distance from the **tangent point** of the bend arc to the **apex (point of intersection)** of the two extended outer surfaces of the flanges.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

Consider the two flanges extended as straight lines until they meet at the apex, forming a V-shape. The angle at the apex is the **bend angle** $\theta$ (included angle after bending).

The **tangent point** of the bend arc lies on the outside (convex) surface of the bend, at a horizontal distance $(R + T)$ from the apex (because the outside radius is $R + T$).

### Step 2 — Use tangent of half-angle

The line from the apex to the tangent point makes a right triangle with the flange. The half-angle at the apex is $\theta/2$. The opposite side of this right triangle is $(R + T)$, and the adjacent side is the OSSB.

$$\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{R + T}{OSSB}$$

Solving for OSSB:

$$OSSB = \frac{R + T}{\tan(\theta/2)} = (R + T) \cdot \cot\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

This is mathematically equivalent to the form commonly given in KTU texts:

$$OSSB = \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (R + T) \quad \text{(only valid when measured from a different reference)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The two common formulations — $OSSB = (R+T) \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$ and $OSSB = (R+T) / \tan(\theta/2)$ — refer to **different reference geometries** (setback from apex vs. setback from tangent point along the flange). The KTU 2024 convention is:

$$\boxed{\,OSSB = \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (R + T)\,}$$

for the case where OSSB is measured from the **tangent point of the outer fiber to the apex** measured along the flange axis. Always confirm with the problem's figure.

## 3.3 Derivation of Bend Deduction (BD)

The **Bend Deduction** is the amount by which the *sum of the flange outside dimensions* exceeds the *flat blank length*, after accounting for the bend allowance.

### Step 1 — Add the flange outside dimensions

If you measure from outside-corner to outside-corner of a U-shaped part (one bend), the two flange lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ together span:

$$L_{\text{outside}} = L_1 + L_2$$

But this overshoots the actual flat length, because the bend region "saves" material in the layout.

### Step 2 — Subtract the BA term and add the proper setback adjustment

The flat length can be expressed as:

$$FL = L_1 + L_2 + BA - 2 \cdot OSSB$$

The term $2 \cdot OSSB$ represents the overshoot at *both* ends of the bend (one for each flange).

### Step 3 — Identify the Bend Deduction

By definition, the Bend Deduction is the quantity that converts the *outside* dimensions to the *flat* length:

$$FL = L_1 + L_2 - BD$$

Comparing with the previous expression:

$$L_1 + L_2 - BD = L_1 + L_2 + BA - 2 \cdot OSSB$$

Cancelling $L_1 + L_2$ on both sides:

$$-BD = BA - 2 \cdot OSSB$$

Multiplying by $-1$:

$$\boxed{\,BD = 2 \cdot OSSB - BA\,}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A sheet metal part is to be made from a **2 mm** thick cold-rolled steel blank. It has a single **90° bend** with an **inside bend radius of 4 mm**. The two flanges must each measure **50 mm** along the outside surface. Calculate: **(a)** Bend Allowance, **(b)** Outside Setback, **(c)** Bend Deduction, **(d)** Flat Length of the blank. Take **K = 0.44**.

**Given:** $T = 2$ mm, $\theta = 90°$, $R = 4$ mm, $L_1 = L_2 = 50$ mm, $K = 0.44$.

### (a) Bend Allowance

$$BA = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot T)$$

Substitute values:

$$BA = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot 90 \cdot \bigl(4 + 0.44 \cdot 2\bigr)$$

Compute $K \cdot T$:

$$K \cdot T = 0.44 \cdot 2 = 0.88 \text{ mm}$$

Compute the radius term:

$$R + K \cdot T = 4 + 0.88 = 4.88 \text{ mm}$$

Compute the angle factor:

$$\frac{\pi}{180} \cdot 90 = \frac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708$$

Compute BA:

$$BA = 1.5708 \cdot 4.88 = 7.665 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{BA \approx 7.67 \text{ mm}}$$

### (b) Outside Setback

$$OSSB = \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (R + T)$$

Compute the half-angle:

$$\frac{\theta}{2} = 45°$$

Compute $\tan(45°)$:

$$\tan(45°) = 1$$

Compute $R + T$:

$$R + T = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ mm}$$

Compute OSSB:

$$OSSB = 1 \cdot 6 = 6 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{OSSB = 6.00 \text{ mm}}$$

### (c) Bend Deduction

$$BD = 2 \cdot OSSB - BA$$

Substitute:

$$BD = 2 \cdot 6 - 7.665 = 12 - 7.665$$

$$\boxed{BD = 4.335 \text{ mm}}$$

### (d) Flat Length

$$FL = L_1 + L_2 + BA - 2 \cdot OSSB$$

$$FL = 50 + 50 + 7.665 - 12$$

$$FL = 95.665 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification** using the BD form:

$$FL = L_1 + L_2 - BD = 50 + 50 - 4.335 = 95.665 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{FL \approx 95.67 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final blank length to be cut from the sheet = 95.67 mm.** After bending, the part will measure exactly **50 mm × 50 mm** on the outside surfaces.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — Bent Sheet Geometry

The figure below is a **functional block-level schematic** showing the geometric construction of a bent sheet and the relationship between the parameters used in the Bend Allowance formula.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FLAT BLANK LENGTH FL] --> B[Two Flanges L1 and L2 measured on OUTSIDE]
    B --> C[BEND REGION]
    C --> D[Inside Bend Radius R - concave surface]
    C --> E[Outside Bend Radius R plus T - convex surface]
    C --> F[Neutral Axis Radius Rn = R plus K times T]
    F --> G[BEND ALLOWANCE BA = arc length of neutral axis]
    D --> H[OSSB = tan theta over 2 times R plus T]
    G --> I[BD = 2 times OSSB minus BA]
    H --> I
    I --> J[FL = L1 plus L2 plus BA minus 2 times OSSB]
    B --> J
    G --> J

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style J fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **flat blank length** $FL$ is the total length of material to be cut before bending.
- The **two flanges** $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the dimensions specified by the designer, measured from the outside surfaces.
- The **bend region** contains three concentric arcs: inside (radius $R$), outside (radius $R+T$), and **neutral axis** (radius $R+K\cdot T$).
- The **neutral axis arc** length is the bend allowance $BA$.
- The **outside setback** is the geometric distance between the flange-tangent point and the apex.
- The **bend deduction** combines these into a single correction factor.
- The **final equation** for $FL$ is computed at the bottom of the flow.

## 4.2 Mermaid Cross-Section Schematic — Concentric Arcs of the Bend

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Center of Curvature O] --- P2[Inside Surface - radius R]
    P2 --- P3[Neutral Axis - radius R plus K T]
    P3 --- P4[Outside Surface - radius R plus T]
    P1 -->|Radius R| P2
    P1 -->|Radius R + K*T| P3
    P1 -->|Radius R + T| P4
    P4 --> P5[Flange 1 - Outside Tangent]
    P2 --> P6[Flange 2 - Inside Tangent]
    P5 --> P7[Apex Point]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[OSSB Distance along Flange]

    style P1 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P8 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The three concentric arcs (radii $R$, $R + K \cdot T$, and $R + T$) all share the **same center of curvature O**.
- The **outside tangent** on Flange 1 and the **inside tangent** on Flange 2 meet at the **apex**.
- The **OSSB** is the linear distance from the apex to the tangent point along the flange axis.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Solution Procedure for KTU Problems

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[START: Read the problem statement] --> S2[Identify T, R, theta, K, L1, L2]
    S2 --> S3{Is K given?}
    S3 --|No| S4[Use K = 0.44 for mild steel default]
    S3 --|Yes| S5[Use given K value]
    S4 --> S6[Compute Rn = R + K*T]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Compute BA = pi over 180 times theta times Rn]
    S7 --> S8[Compute OSSB = tan theta over 2 times R + T]
    S8 --> S9[Compute BD = 2 times OSSB minus BA]
    S9 --> S10[Compute FL = L1 + L2 + BA - 2 times OSSB]
```
