# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the term "Bend Allowance" in sheet metal bending. Why is it necessary to add the bend allowance when calculating the flat blank length?**

**Model Answer:**

**Bend Allowance (BA):** Bend allowance is the **arc length of the neutral axis** of the sheet metal through the bend region. It represents the length of material "used up" in forming the bend.

**Necessity of BA in flat blank calculation:** When a sheet is bent, the outer fibers stretch and inner fibers compress, but the neutral axis remains at its original length. This neutral-axis length must be added to the two flange dimensions to obtain the actual flat blank length. Without adding the bend allowance, the resulting part will be **shorter than designed** because the material consumed in the bend is unaccounted for. The formula is:

$$BA = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot T)$$

where $\theta$ is the bend angle (in degrees), $R$ is the inside bend radius, $K$ is the K-factor, and $T$ is the sheet thickness.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**What is the K-factor in sheet metal bending? Explain how it affects the bend allowance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **K-factor** is a dimensionless constant that defines the position of the **neutral axis** of a sheet metal part during bending, expressed as a fraction of the material thickness:

$$K = \frac{c}{T}$$

where $c$ is the distance of the neutral axis from the inside (concave) surface of the bend and $T$ is the sheet thickness.

**Effect on Bend Allowance:** The K-factor directly enters the BA formula through the term $(R + K \cdot T)$, which represents the **radius of the neutral axis**. A larger K-factor means the neutral axis is positioned farther from the inside surface, increasing $R_n$ and thus increasing $BA$. Conversely, a smaller K-factor reduces the BA. Typical values range from **0.33** (sharp bends, hard materials) to **0.50** (large radius bends, soft materials). For most cold-rolled steel, **K = 0.44** is the standard default.
---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** For each Module, the question paper will offer an *internal choice* — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are designed to test the same Course Outcomes (CO) and carry equal marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

A sheet metal component is to be fabricated from a **3 mm thick** cold-rolled steel blank. The component has the following geometry (see schematic):

- **Flange 1 (outside dimension):** 75 mm
- **Flange 2 (outside dimension):** 60 mm
- **Bend angle (included):** 120°
- **Inside bend radius:** 6 mm
- **K-factor:** 0.45

**(a)** Calculate the **Bend Allowance (BA)** and the **Outside Setback (OSSB)** for this bend. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **Bend Deduction (BD)** and the **Flat Blank Length (FL)** required to produce this component. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Given:** $T = 3$ mm, $\theta = 120°$, $R = 6$ mm, $L_1 = 75$ mm, $L_2 = 60$ mm, $K = 0.45$.

#### Part (a) — Bend Allowance and Outside Setback

**Step 1: Compute the neutral axis radius.**

$$R_n = R + K \cdot T = 6 + (0.45)(3) = 6 + 1.35 = 7.35 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Bend Allowance.**

$$BA = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot R_n = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot 120 \cdot 7.35$$

Compute the angle factor:

$$\frac{\pi}{180} \cdot 120 = \frac{2\pi}{3} = 2.0944$$

Compute BA:

$$BA = 2.0944 \cdot 7.35 = 15.394 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{BA \approx 15.39 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Outside Setback.**

$$OSSB = \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (R + T)$$

Compute the half-angle:

$$\frac{\theta}{2} = 60°$$

Compute $\tan(60°)$:

$$\tan(60°) = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.7321$$

Compute $R + T$:

$$R + T = 6 + 3 = 9 \text{ mm}$$

Compute OSSB:

$$OSSB = 1.7321 \cdot 9 = 15.589 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{OSSB \approx 15.59 \text{ mm}}$$

#### Part (b) — Bend Deduction and Flat Blank Length

**Step 4: Compute the Bend Deduction.**

$$BD = 2 \cdot OSSB - BA = 2 \cdot 15.589 - 15.394$$

$$BD = 31.178 - 15.394 = 15.784 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{BD \approx 15.78 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Flat Blank Length.**

Method 1 (using BA and OSSB):

$$FL = L_1 + L_2 + BA - 2 \cdot OSSB = 75 + 60 + 15.394 - 31.178$$

$$FL = 119.216 \text{ mm}$$

Method 2 (using BD, for verification):

$$FL = L_1 + L_2 - BD = 75 + 60 - 15.784 = 119.216 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{FL \approx 119.22 \text{ mm}}$$

**Conclusion:** The sheet metal blank to be cut is **119.22 mm** long. After bending to 120° with a 6 mm inside radius, the part will measure exactly 75 mm × 60 mm on the outside.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

A sheet metal **U-channel** is to be manufactured from a **2.5 mm thick** aluminum alloy (5052-H32) blank. The U-channel has:

- **Two side flanges:** 80 mm each (outside dimension)
- **Bottom flange:** 120 mm (outside dimension)
- **Two bends:** Both at 90° (included angle)
- **Inside bend radius:** 5 mm (both bends)
- **K-factor for 5052-H32:** 0.42

**(a)** Calculate the **Bend Allowance** for **one** such 90° bend. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **total flat blank length** required to produce this U-channel. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Given:** $T = 2.5$ mm, $\theta = 90°$ (each bend), $R = 5$ mm (each bend), $K = 0.42$, $L_{\text{bottom}} = 120$ mm, $L_{\text{side}} = 80$ mm (each).

#### Part (a) — Bend Allowance for one 90° bend

**Step 1: Compute the neutral axis radius.**

$$R_n = R + K \cdot T = 5 + (0.42)(2.5) = 5 + 1.05 = 6.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Bend Allowance.**

$$BA = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot R_n = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot 90 \cdot 6.05$$

Compute the angle factor:

$$\frac{\pi}{180} \cdot 90 = \frac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708$$

Compute BA:

$$BA = 1.5708 \cdot 6.05 = 9.503 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{BA \approx 9.50 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 (verification): Outside Setback and Bend Deduction.**

$$OSSB = \tan(45°) \cdot (R + T) = 1 \cdot (5 + 2.5) = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$BD = 2 \cdot OSSB - BA = 2 \cdot 7.5 - 9.50 = 5.50 \text{ mm}$$

#### Part (b) — Total Flat Blank Length for the U-Channel

The U-channel has **3 flanges** and **2 bends** (both identical).

**Step 4: Sum of flange outside dimensions.**

$$L_{\text{total,outside}} = L_{\text{bottom}} + 2 \cdot L_{\text{side}} = 120 + 2 \cdot 80 = 280 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Sum of bend allowances.**

$$BA_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot BA = 2 \cdot 9.503 = 19.006 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Sum of outside setbacks (2 setbacks per bend).**

$$OSSB_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot OSSB = 4 \cdot 7.5 = 30 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Compute the flat blank length.**

$$FL = L_{\text{total,outside}} + BA_{\text{total}} - OSSSB_{\text{total}}$$

$$FL = 280 + 19.006 - 30 = 269.006 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification using Bend Deduction:**

$$FL = L_{\text{total,outside}} - 2 \cdot BD = 280 - 2 \cdot 5.50 = 280 - 11 = 269 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

(small rounding difference ≈ 0.006 mm)

$$\boxed{FL \approx 269.0 \text{ mm}}$$

**Conclusion:** A flat blank of length **269 mm** is to be cut. After two 90° bends at the marked positions, the U-channel will have its two 80 mm side flanges perpendicular to the 120 mm bottom flange.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply $BA$ and $OSSB$ by the number of bends.** In Question B above, students often compute $BA$ once and stop — losing **3 marks** out of 7 in part (b).
> 2. **Using the wrong K-factor default.** If the problem specifies the material, use that material's K-factor, *not* 0.44.
> 3. **Mixing up the angle.** A 90° bend does NOT mean $\theta = 0.5$ in radians — it means $\theta = 90°$ in the BA formula. The conversion $\pi/180$ handles the radians.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency.** Always express the final $FL$ in **mm**. Do not mix cm and mm in intermediate steps.
> 5. **Skipping the substitution step.** KTU examiners award partial credit at the substitution stage. Always show $BA = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot 90 \cdot 6.05 = \ldots$ explicitly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Bend Allowance (BA)** is the **arc length of the neutral axis** through the bend. Formula:

$$BA = \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot \theta \cdot (R + K \cdot T)$$

- **K-factor (K)** locates the neutral axis at distance $K \cdot T$ from the inside surface. Default value: **K = 0.44** for mild steel. Range: **0.33 to 0.50**.

- **Outside Setback (OSSB)** is the distance from the apex of the bend to the tangent point on the outer fiber. Formula:

$$OSSB = \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (R + T)$$

- **Bend Deduction (BD)** is the correction factor that converts *outside* flange dimensions to the *flat* blank length. Formula:

$$BD = 2 \cdot OSSB - BA$$

- **Flat Blank Length (FL)** for a part with $n$ bends and $m$ flanges is:

$$FL = \sum_{i=1}^{m} L_i + \sum_{j=1}^{n} BA_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} 2 \cdot OSSB_j$$

- **Angle convention:** $\theta$ in the formula is the **included angle after bending**, in **degrees**. Use $\pi/180$ to convert.

- **Default values to memorize:** Mild steel K-factor = **0.44**; Aluminum 5052-H32 = **0.42**; Stainless 304 = **0.45**.

- **Verification step:** Always cross-check $FL$ using both the BA–OSSB form *and* the BD form. They must agree (up to rounding).

- **Multi-bend rule:** For $n$ identical bends, multiply $BA$ and $OSSB$ (per bend) by $n$ before applying the summation.

- **Springback reminder:** Real production bends have springback of approximately **0.5° to 3°** depending on material. KTU problems usually assume **ideal bending with no springback** unless explicitly stated.

- **Engineering use cases:** Press brake setup, progressive die layout, sheet metal nesting, HVAC duct design, ship hull plating.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spring Back in Bending and Methods to Counter Spring Back
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spring Back in Bending and Methods to Counter Spring Back

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spring Back** is the elastic recovery of a plastically deformed sheet metal after the removal of the forming load, resulting in a change in the bend angle (increase) and/or the bend radius (increase) from the tool geometry.

Mathematically, when a sheet of initial thickness $t$ is bent plastically over a die radius $R$ through a bend angle $\theta$ under load, upon unloading, the elastic strain energy stored in the material is released. This causes the part to spring back, giving a final radius $R'$ and final angle $\theta'$ such that:

$$R' > R \quad \text{and} \quad \theta' > \theta$$

The magnitudes of these changes are governed by the **elastic modulus ($E$)**, **yield strength ($\sigma_y$)**, the **ratio $R/t$**, and the **bend angle ($\theta$)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bow and the Branch

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine bending a green tree branch. When you push it down, it bends a certain amount, but the moment you release it, it partially straightens back. The branch never stays exactly in the shape you forced it into — it "remembers" its original straight form. Spring back in sheet metal bending behaves the same way: plastic deformation sets the shape, but the elastic portion of the strain always fights to recover.

A simpler, everyday example is **a plastic ruler** held against a table edge and bent. When released, the ruler does not return fully to flat (that would be pure elastic) but also does not retain the bent shape exactly — it *springs back partially*. Sheet metal behaves the same way during press brake bending.

### 1.3 Why Spring Back Matters in Manufacturing

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Significance:** Spring back is one of the most critical sources of **dimensional inaccuracy** in sheet metal forming. Aerospace panels, automotive body panels, and structural brackets all demand tolerances of $\pm 0.1^\circ$ on bend angles. Unaccounted spring back can lead to **scrap, rework, and assembly mismatch**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Elastic Modulus, $E$**: For steels, $E \approx 210$ GPa; for aluminium alloys, $E \approx 70$ GPa; for copper, $E \approx 110$ GPa.
- **Yield Strength, $\sigma_y$**: Typically 200–1500 MPa for engineering alloys.
- **Spring Back Ratio, $\eta$**: Defined as $\eta = \dfrac{R'}{R} = 1 + \dfrac{3\sigma_y}{E}\cdot\dfrac{R}{t}$
- **Spring Back Angle, $\Delta\theta$**: Defined as $\Delta\theta = \theta' - \theta$

The dimensionless parameter $\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$ is the most important material property dictating spring back. **Materials with high $\sigma_y/E$ ratio exhibit greater spring back.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Spring Back — Step-by-Step Logic

When a sheet is bent, the deformation is **non-uniform through the thickness**:

- **Outer fibre (tension side)**: Stretches — experiences tensile strain.
- **Inner fibre (compression side)**: Compresses — experiences compressive strain.
- **Neutral axis (NA)**: A surface where strain = 0.

During **plastic bending**, the neutral axis shifts from the geometric centreline toward the **inner radius** (compression side) because the yield stress in tension is reached at a smaller curvature than in compression (Bauschinger effect for strain-hardening materials).

After unloading, the **elastic portion of the strain distribution reverses**, causing the curvature to reduce. This is the spring back.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Spring back is *not* a manufacturing defect — it is an **inherent physical phenomenon** caused by elastic energy storage during plastic deformation. Tool designers must *anticipate* and *compensate* for it.

### 2.2 Assumptions of Classical Spring Back Theory

1. Plane strain bending (sheet width $\gg$ thickness).
2. Material follows elastic–perfectly plastic or power-law hardening.
3. Bending radius $R \gg t$ (thin sheet approximation).
4. Constant yield stress across the thickness (simplified).
5. No strain-hardening (or average $\sigma_y$ used).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Die radius (under load) | — | mm |
| $R'$ | Spring back radius | $R' = R \left(1 + \dfrac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)$ | mm |
| $\theta$ | Bend angle under load | — | rad or deg |
| $\theta'$ | Bend angle after spring back | $\theta' = \theta \left(1 + \dfrac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)$ | rad |
| $\Delta R$ | Increase in radius | $\Delta R = \dfrac{3\sigma_y R^2}{E t}$ | mm |
| $\Delta\theta$ | Spring back angle | $\Delta\theta = \theta \cdot \dfrac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}$ | rad |
| $\eta$ | Spring back ratio | $\eta = R'/R$ | dimensionless |
| $M$ | Bending moment | $M = \sigma_y \cdot \dfrac{w t^2}{4}$ | N$\cdot$mm |
| $I$ | Moment of inertia per unit width | $I = \dfrac{t^3}{12}$ | mm$^3$ |
| $K$ | Spring back constant | $K = \dfrac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units:** Always keep $E$ and $\sigma_y$ in **consistent units** (e.g., both in MPa or both in N/mm$^2$). Failure to do so is the #1 cause of incorrect spring back calculations in KTU exams.

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Spring Back

| **Factor** | **Effect on Spring Back** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Increasing $\sigma_y$ (higher strength) | **Increases** | Larger elastic strain energy stored |
| Decreasing $E$ (e.g., Ti, Mg) | **Increases** | Lower elastic stiffness, more recovery |
| Increasing $R/t$ ratio | **Increases** | More elastic strain in outer fibre |
| Increasing bend angle $\theta$ | **Increases** proportionally | Larger arc, more stored elastic energy |
| Increasing sheet thickness $t$ | **Decreases** | Larger plastic zone, lower elastic fraction |
| Higher temperature | **Decreases** | Lower $\sigma_y$, material softens |
| Work hardening (high $n$) | **Slight decrease** | Distributes strain more evenly |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace panel forming**: Wing skins of aluminium alloys (low $E$) require precise spring back compensation.
- **Automotive stamping**: High-strength steel (HSS) body panels have $3$–$4\times$ more spring back than mild steel.
- **Press brake operation**: Operators routinely add **overbend** to account for spring back.
- **Roll forming**: Continuous bending requires pre-adjusted roll gaps to compensate.
- **Tube bending**: Mandrel bending uses internal supports to minimize spring back in hollow sections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Spring Back Radius ($R'$) in Sheet Bending

**Given:** A sheet of thickness $t$ bent over a die of radius $R$ under elastic–perfectly plastic conditions.

**Step 1 — Strain distribution during plastic bending:**

Assume the neutral axis is at radius $r_n$ from the centre of curvature. The strain at a fibre at radius $r$ from the centre of curvature is:

$$\varepsilon(r) = \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_n}\right)$$

For small strains (thin sheet, $R \gg t$):

$$\varepsilon(r) \approx \frac{r - r_n}{r_n}$$

**Step 2 — Stress distribution in the elastic–plastic region:**

The outer fibre (tension) and inner fibre (compression) yield first. The stress distribution is:

$$\sigma(r) = 
\begin{cases}
+E\cdot\varepsilon(r) & \text{for } \vert\varepsilon(r)\vert \le \varepsilon_y \quad \text{(elastic zone)} \\
\pm\sigma_y & \text{for } \vert\varepsilon(r)\vert > \varepsilon_y \quad \text{(plastic zone)}
\end{cases}$$

Where $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$ is the yield strain.

**Step 3 — Locate the elastic–plastic boundary:**

The elastic–plastic interface in the outer fibre is at radius $r_e$, where:

$$\varepsilon(r_e) = \frac{r_e - r_n}{r_n} = \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$$

Solving:

$$r_e = r_n\left(1 + \frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Force equilibrium (zero net axial force):**

The integral of stress through the thickness must equal zero (no external axial force on the bent sheet):

$$\int_{r_n - t/2}^{r_n + t/2} \sigma(r)\, w\, dr = 0$$

For $R \gg t$, using linearised strain, the neutral axis location simplifies to:

$$r_n = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2} + \text{shift}$$

Where $R_o$ and $R_i$ are the outer and inner radii. For thin sheets, the shift of the neutral axis from the geometric mid-surface is small and often neglected in elementary treatments, giving $r_n \approx R + t/2$.

**Step 5 — Bending moment calculation:**

The plastic bending moment per unit width is:

$$M = \int_{r_n - t/2}^{r_n + t/2} \sigma(r)(r - r_n)\, dr$$

Substituting elastic–plastic stress distribution and simplifying (for the case where the entire section has yielded except the elastic core, i.e., $R/t$ small):

$$M = \sigma_y \cdot \frac{t^2}{4}$$

**Step 6 — Elastic unloading (spring back):**

When the load is removed, the change in curvature $\Delta\kappa$ follows elastic beam theory:

$$\Delta\kappa = \frac{M}{EI}$$

Where $I = t^3/12$ per unit width.

**Step 7 — Curvature relationship:**

$$M = EI \cdot \Delta\kappa = E \cdot \frac{t^3}{12} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R'}\right)$$

Equating with the plastic moment from Step 5:

$$\sigma_y \cdot \frac{t^2}{4} = E \cdot \frac{t^3}{12} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R'}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Simplification:**

$$\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R'} = \frac{3\sigma_y}{E \cdot t}$$

$$\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{3\sigma_y}{E \cdot t} = \frac{1}{R}\left(1 - \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Final spring back radius formula:**

$$R' = \frac{R}{1 - \dfrac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}}$$

For small spring back (typical engineering case), the binomial expansion gives:

$$R' \approx R\left(1 + \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R' = R\left(1 + \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)}$$

**Step 10 — Spring back angle:**

Since the arc length is preserved during spring back (no plastic strain change):

$$R \cdot \theta = R' \cdot \theta'$$

$$\theta' = \theta \cdot \frac{R}{R'} \approx \theta\left(1 + \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)$$

**Spring back angle:**

$$\boxed{\Delta\theta = \theta' - \theta = \theta \cdot \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 2 mm thick steel strip ($\sigma_y = 350$ MPa, $E = 210$ GPa) is bent over a die of radius $R = 10$ mm through an angle of $\theta = 90^\circ$. Calculate:
(a) The spring back radius $R'$
(b) The spring back angle $\Delta\theta$
(c) The final bend angle $\theta'$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify units:** $E = 210{,}000$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa, $t = 2$ mm, $R = 10$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute dimensionless parameter $K$:**

$$K = \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t} = \frac{3 \times 350 \times 10}{210000 \times 2} = \frac{10500}{420000} = 0.025$$

**Step 3 — Spring back radius:**

$$R' = R(1 + K) = 10(1 + 0.025) = 10.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Spring back angle:**

$$\Delta\theta = \theta \cdot K = 90^\circ \times 0.025 = 2.25^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Final bend angle:**

$$\theta' = \theta + \Delta\theta = 90^\circ + 2.25^\circ = 92.25^\circ$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The strip will spring back by $2.25^\circ$. To achieve a **final** $90^\circ$ bend, the tool must be set to bend the sheet to $90^\circ - 2.25^\circ = 87.75^\circ$ under load. This is the principle of **overbending compensation**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Spring Back Behaviour Across Materials

| **Material** | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | $E$ (GPa) | $\sigma_y/E \times 10^3$ | $R/t = 5$ | $R/t = 10$ | $R/t = 20$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 250 | 210 | 1.19 | 1.018 | 1.036 | 1.071 |
| HSS (DP600) | 600 | 210 | 2.86 | 1.043 | 1.086 | 1.171 |
| Aluminium 5052 | 215 | 70 | 3.07 | 1.046 | 1.092 | 1.184 |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | 880 | 110 | 8.00 | 1.120 | 1.240 | 1.480 |
| Stainless 304 | 290 | 200 | 1.45 | 1.022 | 1.043 | 1.087 |
| Copper C110 | 220 | 110 | 2.00 | 1.030 | 1.060 | 1.120 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Titanium and high-strength aluminium alloys exhibit **5–8× more spring back** than mild steel. This is why aerospace forming tools are often built with **active CNC-controlled compensation** rather than fixed geometry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Spring Back Phenomenon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Flat Sheet] --> B[Apply Bending Load]
    B --> C[Plastic Deformation Initiated]
    C --> D[Outer Fibre Yields in Tension]
    D --> E[Inner Fibre Yields in Compression]
    E --> F[Neutral Axis Established]
    F --> G[Tool Reaches Target Angle theta]
    G --> H[Remove Bending Load]
    H --> I[Elastic Strain Recovery Begins]
    I --> J[Curvature Decreases]
    J --> K[Radius Increases R prime]
    K --> L[Angle Increases theta prime]
    L --> M[Final Part Geometry Obtained]
    M --> N[Compare with Target Dimensions]
    N --> O{Spring back within tolerance?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Accept Part]
    O -->|No| Q[Apply Compensation Strategy]
    Q --> R[Overbending / Bottoming / Stretching]
    R --> S[Re-bend with Compensation]
    S --> T[Verify Final Geometry]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Spring Back Compensation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        I1[Material Properties sigma y, E]
        I2[Sheet Thickness t]
        I3[Target Bend Angle theta target]
        I4[Target Bend Radius R target]
    end

    subgraph CALC[Computation Block]
        C1[Spring back Calculator]
        C2[Formula: R prime equals R times 1 plus 3 sigma y R over E t]
        C3[Compute Delta theta]
        C4[Compute Compensation Angle]
    end

    subgraph TOOL[Tool Adjustment Block]
        T1[Punch Radius Adjustment]
        T2[Die Angle Modification]
        T3[Bottoming Pressure Setting]
        T4[Stretch Force Calibrator]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Process Output]
        O1[Compensated Tool Geometry]
        O2[Fabricated Part]
        O3[Quality Inspection Station]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C1
    I4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> T1
    C4 --> T2
    C4 --> T3
    C4 --> T4
    T1 --> O1
    T2 --> O1
    T3 --> O1
    T4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Spring Back Mechanism — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOAD[Under Load]
        L1[Die Radius R]
        L2[Bend Angle theta]
        L3[Outer Fibre Stress plus sigma y]
        L4[Inner Fibre Stress minus sigma y]
    end

    subgraph STORED[Stored Elastic Energy]
        S1[Elastic strain in fibres]
        S2[Plastic strain locked in]
    end

    subgraph UNLOAD[After Unloading]
        U1[Final Radius R prime greater than R]
        U2[Final Angle theta prime greater than theta]
        U3[Stress reduced to zero]
    end

    L1 --> S1
    L2 --> S1
    L3 --> S1
    L4 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> U1
    S1 --> U2
    S2 --> U3
```

### 4.4 Methods to Counter Spring Back — Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Detect Spring back Problem] --> B{Part Material and Geometry}
    B -->|Low Carbon Steel| C[Use Overbending 1 to 2 degrees]
    B -->|High Strength Steel| D[Use Bottoming plus Coining]
    B -->|Aluminium Alloys| E[Use Stretch Bending with Tension]
    B -->|Titanium Alloys| F[Use Hot Forming or Creep Forming]
    B -->|Thin Sheet below 1 mm| G[Use Bead or Rib Reinforcement]
    B -->|Complex 3D Shape| H[Use Multi step Forming with Intermediate Annealing]

    C --> I[Verify final angle]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J{Spring back compensated?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Approve Tool for Production]
    J -->|No| L[Iterate Tool Geometry]
    L --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Define the term **spring back** in sheet metal bending. Name any **two factors** that influence it.

**Model Answer:**

> **Spring back** is the elastic recovery of a sheet metal after removal of the bending load, resulting in an increase in the bend angle and bend radius from the tool dimensions. It occurs because of the release of the elastic component of the total strain.

**Two influencing factors:**
1. **Material properties**: Higher $\sigma_y/E$ ratio increases spring back (e.g., titanium, HSS).
2. **Geometric ratio $R/t$**: Larger die radius relative to sheet thickness increases spring back.

(Other valid factors: bend angle, sheet thickness, temperature, work hardening, friction at die contact.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
Why does **stretch bending** reduce spring back compared to conventional bending?

**Model Answer:**

In stretch bending, a tensile load is applied to the sheet during bending. This tensile stress superimposes on the bending stress distribution, ensuring that the **entire cross-section** (both outer and inner fibres) is in **tension** during plastic deformation. Upon unloading, the elastic recovery occurs in tension rather than in mixed tension-compression, so the change in curvature is smaller. The net elastic strain energy available for spring back is therefore reduced.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **spring back radius** in sheet metal bending, starting from the elastic unloading of a plastically bent sheet. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches **any four methods** to counter spring back in press brake bending operations. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Plane strain bending — sheet width $\gg$ thickness $t$.
2. Material is elastic–perfectly plastic with yield stress $\sigma_y$.
3. Bending radius $R$ under load satisfies $R \gg t$ (thin sheet).
4. Neutral axis lies at the mid-thickness (small $t/R$).
5. Plane sections remain plane after deformation.

**Derivation:**

Consider a sheet bent elastically first, then plastically to radius $R$. The plastic bending moment per unit width is:

$$M_p = \sigma_y \cdot \frac{t^2}{4}$$

Upon unloading, the sheet behaves elastically. By elastic beam theory, the change in curvature $\Delta\kappa$ is:

$$\Delta\kappa = \frac{M_p}{EI} = \frac{M_p}{E \cdot (t^3/12)} = \frac{12 M_p}{E t^3}$$

Substituting $M_p$:

$$\Delta\kappa = \frac{12 \cdot \sigma_y t^2 / 4}{E t^3} = \frac{3\sigma_y}{E t}$$

The curvatures are related as:

$$\Delta\kappa = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R'}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R'} = \frac{3\sigma_y}{E t}$$

$$\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{3\sigma_y}{E t} = \frac{1}{R}\left(1 - \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R' = \frac{R}{1 - \dfrac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}} \approx R\left(1 + \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)}$$

### **Model Solution for (b):**

**1. Overbending (Tool Angle Compensation):**
The punch and die are designed with an angle that is **smaller than the target** by an amount equal to the predicted spring back. For example, to achieve a final $90^\circ$ bend, the tool is set to $87^\circ$ for a material with $3^\circ$ spring back.

**2. Bottoming / Coining:**
The punch is forced to bottom against the sheet at the bottom of the stroke, applying **high localized compressive stress** that exceeds the yield stress throughout the bend region. This plastically deforms the entire cross-section, eliminating the elastic strain that would otherwise cause spring back. Particularly effective for high-strength steels.

**3. Stretch Bending:**
A tensile force is applied along the sheet axis during bending. This keeps all fibres in tension throughout the operation, so the spring back is a partial recovery from a uniformly tensile state rather than from a mixed tension-compression state. The recovery magnitude is therefore smaller.

**4. Die Radius Compensation (Punch Radius Adjustment):**
Since spring back increases the radius, a **smaller punch radius** is used. The required compensation is calculated from the spring back formula:

$$R_{\text{punch}} = \frac{R_{\text{target}}}{1 + \dfrac{3\sigma_y R_{\text{target}}}{E t}}$$

**5. Bead / Rib Reinforcement (for thin sheets):**
A bead or embossed rib is incorporated in the bend region to locally **increase stiffness** and constrain elastic recovery. Effective for parts with $t < 1$ mm.

**6. Hot Forming / Creep Forming (for titanium, nickel alloys):**
Forming at elevated temperature reduces $\sigma_y$ sharply while $E$ decreases only slightly, so the $\sigma_y/E$ ratio drops dramatically. Spring back is minimized.

**7. Pre-stressing / Overloading:**
A small plastic over-strain is applied beyond the elastic limit so that upon unloading, the spring back returns to exactly the desired geometry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** A strip of **3 mm** thickness is bent over a die of radius **12 mm**. The material yield strength is **400 MPa** and elastic modulus is **210 GPa**. If the bend angle under load is **$60^\circ$**, determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Spring back radius $R'$
- (ii) Spring back angle $\Delta\theta$
- (iii) Final bend angle $\theta'$
- (iv) Required overbend angle to achieve a final $60^\circ$.

**(b)** With the help of a **stress–strain diagram**, explain how the elastic recovery causes spring back. Discuss how the **neutral axis** shifts during plastic bending. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $t = 3$ mm, $R = 12$ mm, $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa, $E = 210{,}000$ MPa, $\theta = 60^\circ$.

**Step 1 — Compute the dimensionless spring back parameter:**

$$K = \frac{3\sigma_y R}{E t} = \frac{3 \times 400 \times 12}{210000 \times 3} = \frac{14400}{630000} = 0.02286$$

**Step 2 — Spring back radius:**

$$R' = R(1 + K) = 12(1 + 0.02286) = 12.274 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Spring back angle:**

$$\Delta\theta = \theta \cdot K = 60^\circ \times 0.02286 = 1.371^\circ$$

**Step 4 — Final bend angle:**

$$\theta' = 60^\circ + 1.371^\circ = 61.371^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Required overbend angle:**

$$\theta_{\text{overbend}} = 60^\circ - 1.371^\circ = 58.629^\circ$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Summary:**
> - $R' = 12.274$ mm
> - $\Delta\theta = 1.371^\circ$
> - $\theta' = 61.371^\circ$
> - $\theta_{\text{overbend}} = 58.629^\circ$

### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Stress–Strain Explanation:**

During bending, the outer fibre follows path **O → A → B** (elastic loading, then plastic yielding to strain $\varepsilon_p$). The inner fibre follows **O → A' → B'** (elastic compression, then plastic yielding in compression).

The total strain at the outer fibre is $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_e + \varepsilon_p$, where $\varepsilon_e = \sigma_y / E$ is the elastic component. Upon unloading, the elastic strain $\varepsilon_e$ is recovered along the elastic slope (path B → C), but the plastic strain $\varepsilon_p$ remains locked in.

**Neutral Axis Shift:**

In **pure elastic bending**, the neutral axis coincides with the geometric centroid. However, in **plastic bending**:
- The material yields first in tension at the outer fibre.
- Because of the Bauschinger effect and asymmetric yielding, the neutral axis **migrates toward the inner compression side** of the bend.
- The shift magnitude depends on the strain-hardening exponent and the $R/t$ ratio.

This shift must be accounted for in spring back calculations for accurate final geometry prediction.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Using $E$ in GPa and $\sigma_y$ in MPa without conversion. Always convert to a common unit (e.g., N/mm$^2$ or MPa).
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 3**: The classical formula contains a factor of 3 from $I = t^3/12$. Missing this gives $R'$ values that are off by a factor of 3.
> 3. **Sign error in curvature**: $\Delta\kappa = 1/R - 1/R'$, not the reverse. Wrong sign gives negative spring back (physically impossible).
> 4. **Confusing $R$ and $R'$**: $R$ is the die radius under load; $R'$ is the radius after unloading. They are different.
> 5. **Forgetting the overbend direction**: To compensate, bend the sheet to a *smaller* angle than the target (not larger).
> 6. **Neglecting neutral axis shift**: For $R/t < 5$, the shift is small. For $R/t > 10$, advanced analyses are required.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spring back** is the **elastic recovery** of a plastically bent sheet after load removal.
- The **spring back radius** is given by $R' = R\left(1 + \dfrac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}\right)$.
- The **spring back angle** is $\Delta\theta = \theta \cdot \dfrac{3\sigma_y R}{E t}$.
- The **spring back ratio** $\sigma_y / E$ is the dominant material parameter — high ratio = high spring back.
- **Spring back increases with**: increasing $\sigma_y$, decreasing $E$, increasing $R/t$, increasing $\theta$.
- **Spring back decreases with**: increasing thickness $t$, increasing temperature, increasing work hardening.
- **Methods to counter spring back**:
  1. **Overbending** (punch angle compensation)
  2. **Bottoming / Coining** (force the bend to yield completely)
  3. **Stretch bending** (apply axial tension during bending)
  4. **Die radius compensation** (use smaller punch radius)
  5. **Bead/rib reinforcement** (for thin sheets)
  6. **Hot forming / Creep forming** (for titanium, Inconel)
  7. **Pre-stressing / Plastic overload**
  8. **Multi-step forming with intermediate annealing**
- **Neutral axis** migrates toward the inner compression side during plastic bending.
- **Assumptions** of classical theory: thin sheet, plane strain, elastic–perfectly plastic, $R \gg t$.
- **Engineering tip**: For tight tolerance bends, always **calculate spring back first** and apply **overbend compensation** in the tool design.
- **Material ordering** (lowest to highest spring back): Mild Steel < Stainless Steel < HSS < Aluminium < Titanium.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defects in Bending and Remedies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Defects in Bending and Remedies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In sheet-metal bending (a fundamental **air-bending, bottoming, and coining** operation classified under plastic deformation), **Defects in Bending** refer to the geometric, metallurgical, or surface-level deviations from the intended bend profile that arise due to improper tooling geometry, incorrect process parameter selection, material property mismatch, or insufficient spring-back compensation. According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, these defects are categorized as either **macroscopic geometric defects** (springback, twist, wrinkling) or **microstructural/surface defects** (cracking, orange peel, die marks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Bending belongs to the **forming-by-bending family**, which is volume-constant plastic deformation. Therefore, the *true strain* in the bend zone is purely **tensile on the outer fibers** and **compressive on the inner fibers**, separated by the **neutral axis (NA)**. Any defect essentially disturbs this equilibrium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine folding a thick piece of cardboard by hand:
- If you fold it and release it, it tends to "open up" a little — that is exactly **springback** in metal.
- If you fold the cardboard too sharply at one spot, the outer fibers tear — that is **cracking**.
- If the cardboard is too thin relative to the bend radius, the inner side buckles like an accordion — that is **wrinkling**.

In metal bending, the same physics applies but at a precision-engineering scale where tolerances are in fractions of a millimeter and forces are in **tons per meter of bend length**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** Bending quality is universally measured using the **bend angle accuracy (Δθ in degrees)**, the **outer fiber stretch (%)**, and the **flatness deviation (mm/m)**. KTU board examinations expect students to associate each defect with its root-cause metric.

### 1.3 Classification of Bending Defects (Overview)

| Category | Common Defects |
|---|---|
| **Geometric Defects** | Springback, Spring-forward, Twist, Bowing, Off-angle |
| **Material/Tensile Defects** | Cracking, Necking, Orange peel |
| **Compression Defects** | Wrinkling, Buckling |
| **Surface/Marking Defects** | Die marks, Scratches, Tooling witness marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Springback (The Most Tested Defect)

**Springback** is the elastic recovery of the metal after the bending load is removed, causing the final bend angle to be **larger** than the tool angle.

**Why it occurs:**
During bending, the outer fibers undergo tensile plastic strain while the inner fibers undergo compressive plastic strain. The **neutral axis** experiences no strain. Upon unloading, the elastic strain component in both zones is recovered, causing the part to "spring open."

**How it is quantified:**
The elastic recovery is governed by the material's **modulus of elasticity (E)** and the **yield strength (Y)**. Higher Y/E ratio means greater springback.

### 2.2 Spring-Forward (Over-Bending / Negative Springback)

In some materials, particularly those work-hardened or compressed beyond a critical curvature, the elastic recovery causes the angle to **decrease further**, resulting in a smaller final angle than intended. This is called **spring-forward** or **over-bending**.

### 2.3 Cracking on the Outer Tensile Fibers

The outer fiber strain must not exceed the **material's allowable strain (ε_allowable)**:
- For most steels: ε_allowable ≈ 0.40 × (t/R) where R is the inner bend radius and t is thickness.
- For aluminum alloys: ε_allowable ≈ 0.30 × (t/R)

If the bend radius is too sharp (small R/t ratio), the tensile strain exceeds this limit, causing **micro-cracks** or **through-thickness fracture**.

### 2.4 Wrinkling on the Inner Compressive Fibers

When the inner compressive stress exceeds the **critical buckling stress (σ_cr)**, the inside surface develops wavy buckles. This is especially common in:
- Wide bend regions (long flange length)
- Thin sheets (small t)
- Soft materials (low Y)

### 2.5 Neutral Axis Shift

In reality, the neutral axis is **not exactly at the geometric mid-thickness**. It shifts toward the inside of the bend due to the asymmetric strain distribution. This shift is denoted as the **K-factor**:
- K = (distance from inside surface to NA) / t
- Typical values: K = 0.33 (soft materials), K = 0.45 (hard materials), K = 0.50 (ideal)

### 2.6 Bend Angle Error and Twist

Caused by uneven punch penetration, misaligned tooling, or unbalanced material flow. **Twist** is specifically the rotation of the bend axis out of the original plane — a common defect in **V-bending of asymmetric cross-sections**.

### 2.7 Orange Peel and Die Marking

- **Orange peel:** A rough, pebble-like surface texture due to large grain size (typically > ASTM 5). Visible on the outer bend surface.
- **Die marking / Witness marks:** Surface scratches from the punch radius being too sharp or poor die surface finish.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K\text{-factor} = \dfrac{\text{distance from inner surface to NA}}{t}$ | Neutral axis location factor | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{outer} = \dfrac{1}{(2K)-1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t)+1}$ | Maximum outer fiber strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\Delta\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{Y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180^\circ}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t)+1}$ | Springback angle approximation | degrees |
| 4 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot E}{(b/t)^2}$ | Critical buckling stress (wrinkling) | MPa |
| 5 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{Y_t}{E} \cdot \dfrac{t}{C}$ | Minimum bend radius (C = material constant) | mm |
| 6 | $\theta_{tool} = \theta_{final} - \Delta\theta_{springback}$ | Tool angle compensation | degrees |
| 7 | $F_{bend} = \dfrac{k \cdot L \cdot t^2 \cdot \text{UTS}}{W}$ | Bending force (air bending) | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **press brake CNC programming**, **FEA simulation of sheet-metal forming (AutoForm, PAM-STAMP)**, and **tool design for automotive panel manufacturing**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Springback Angle (Δθ_springback)

We model the bent sheet as a curved beam in pure bending. Let:
- $R_i$ = inner bend radius (mm)
- $R_o$ = outer bend radius (mm)
- $t$ = sheet thickness (mm)
- $Y$ = yield strength (MPa)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (MPa)
- $\rho$ = radius of curvature of the neutral axis

**Step 1: Define the elastic recovery strain.**
Upon unloading, the bending moment generated by the residual elastic stresses acts to reduce the curvature. The elastic strain at the outer fiber is:

$$
\varepsilon_{elastic} = \dfrac{Y}{E}
$$

**Step 2: Relate elastic strain to curvature change.**

The change in curvature ($\Delta \kappa$) due to elastic recovery is related to the elastic strain as:

$$
\varepsilon_{elastic} = \Delta \kappa \cdot c
$$

where $c$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber.

**Step 3: Substitute $c$.**

For pure bending, the distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber is $c = t/2$ (ideal case). Thus:

$$
\Delta \kappa = \dfrac{Y/E}{t/2} = \dfrac{2Y}{E \cdot t}
$$

**Step 4: Convert curvature change to angle change.**

A small change in curvature corresponds to a change in bend angle via the arc length relationship:

$$
\Delta \theta = \Delta \kappa \cdot L_{arc}
$$

For a section of arc length $L_{arc} = \theta \cdot \rho$, and using the assumption of small elastic recovery ($\rho \approx R_i + t/2$):

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} \approx \dfrac{Y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{1 + 2R_i/t}
$$

**Step 5: Final simplified form (KTU board-friendly version).**

For a 90° bend, this simplifies to the classical textbook approximation:

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} \approx \dfrac{Y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180^\circ}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t) + 1}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Defect → Cause → Remedy

| Defect | Root Cause | Diagnostic Metric | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Springback** | Elastic recovery of material | Bend angle > tool angle | Overbend the tool by Δθ; use coining; add bottoming pressure |
| **Spring-forward** | Over-compensation / reverse loading | Bend angle < tool angle | Reduce punch penetration; use smaller tool angle |
| **Cracking (outer)** | Tensile strain > allowable | Strain ratio > 0.4 (steel) | Increase bend radius; use R/t ≥ 1 for hard materials |
| **Wrinkling (inner)** | Compressive buckling | b/t > critical ratio | Use wiper dies; add bead/swell on punch; reduce flange length |
| **Twist** | Asymmetric flow / misalignment | Angle across width varies | Use longer punch; add side thrust; reduce clearance |
| **Orange peel** | Coarse grain size | ASTM grain number ≤ 5 | Use fine-grained sheet (≤ ASTM 7); anneal if possible |
| **Die marks** | Sharp punch radius / poor polish | Surface roughness Ra > 1.6 μm | Polish die; increase punch radius; use urethane pad |
| **Neutral axis shift error** | Wrong K-factor in CAD | Flat-pattern mismatch | Update K-factor to material-specific value |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 2 mm thick cold-rolled steel sheet (Y = 350 MPa, E = 210 GPa) is bent in a V-die with inner radius R = 4 mm and desired final angle = 90°. Find the required tool angle.

**Solution:**

$$
\dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{Y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180^\circ}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t) + 1}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{350}{210{,}000} \cdot 57.296 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2+1}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} = 0.001667 \cdot 57.296 \cdot 0.3333
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} \approx 0.0318 \text{ rad} \approx 1.82^\circ
$$

$$
\theta_{tool} = \theta_{final} - \Delta\theta_{springback} = 90^\circ - 1.82^\circ \approx 88.18^\circ
$$

**Result:** The tool must be set to approximately **88.2°** to achieve the desired **90°** final bend after springback.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bending Defect Causal Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Metal Input] --> B[Tooling Setup]
    A --> C[Material Properties]
    A --> D[Process Parameters]
    B --> E[Geometric Defects]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    B --> F[Surface Defects]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> G[Springback]
    E --> H[Cracking]
    E --> I[Wrinkling]
    E --> J[Twist]
    F --> K[Orange Peel]
    F --> L[Die Marks]
    G --> M[Remedy: Overbend Tool Angle]
    H --> N[Remedy: Increase Bend Radius]
    I --> O[Remedy: Add Wiper Die]
    J --> P[Remedy: Improve Alignment]
    K --> Q[Remedy: Use Fine Grain Sheet]
    L --> R[Remedy: Polish Tool Surface]
```

### 4.2 Bending Stress Distribution Diagram (Schematic Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OUTER[Outer Tensile Zone]
        A1[Outer Fiber Strain Positive]
        A2[Stress Tensile Yield Y]
    end
    subgraph NA[Neutral Axis Zone]
        B1[Zero Strain Location]
        B2[Stress Zero]
    end
    subgraph INNER[Inner Compressive Zone]
        C1[Inner Fiber Strain Negative]
        C2[Stress Compressive Yield Y]
    end
    OUTER --> NA --> INNER
    A1 --> D{Defect Risk}
    B1 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D --> E1[Cracking if E exceeds limit]
    D --> E2[Wrinkling if C exceeds buckling]
    D --> E3[Springback on unload]
```

### 4.3 Springback Compensation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Target Final Angle]) --> Calc[Calculate Springback Delta Theta]
    Calc --> Sub[Subtract Delta from Final]
    Sub --> Set[Set Tool Angle in Press Brake]
    Set --> Test[Produce Trial Bend]
    Test --> Measure[Measure Actual Bend Angle]
    Measure --> Compare{Angle within 0.5 deg tolerance?}
    Compare -->|Yes| Done([Approved Production])
    Compare -->|No| Adjust[Adjust Tool Angle Iteratively]
    Adjust --> Test
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
**Define springback in sheet metal bending and state any two methods to compensate for it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Springback is the elastic recovery of a bent sheet metal after the bending load is removed, resulting in the final bend angle being larger than the tool angle. It occurs because the outer fibers recover their elastic tensile strain and the inner fibers recover their elastic compressive strain upon unloading.
**Compensation methods (any two):**
1. **Overbending:** Set the tool angle smaller than the desired final angle by the predicted springback Δθ.
2. **Coining / Bottoming:** Use higher punch penetration to induce through-thickness compression, reducing elastic recovery.
3. **Use of a larger bend radius** to decrease the R/t ratio (reduces elastic strain).
4. **Stretch bending** to pre-stretch the material beyond yield before bending.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
**What is orange peel defect? Under what condition does it appear prominently?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Orange peel is a surface defect on the bent sheet characterized by a rough, pebble-like texture resembling the skin of an orange. It appears prominently when the sheet material has a **coarse grain size (typically ASTM grain number ≤ 5)** and is subjected to sufficient plastic strain during bending. The rough surface occurs because each individual grain deforms independently and protrudes on the outer (tensile) surface due to differential strain between adjacent grains. **Remedy:** Use fine-grained sheet (ASTM 7 or finer), anneal the material if feasible, or increase the bend radius to reduce per-grain strain.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With neat sketches, explain the defects of **springback** and **wrinkling** in bending operations. State their causes and remedies.

**Model Answer:**

**Springback (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Elastic recovery of the bent sheet after unloading, causing the final bend angle to be larger than the tool angle.
- **Cause:** When bending load is released, the elastic component of strain (both tensile on outer fibers and compressive on inner fibers) is recovered. Since $E \gg Y$ ratio controls this recovery, materials with high Y/E show greater springback.
- **Formula:** $\Delta\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{Y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180^\circ}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t) + 1}$
- **Remedies:**
  1. Overbend the part by the predicted Δθ (tool angle compensation).
  2. Use coining or bottoming dies to apply uniform through-thickness pressure.
  3. Use stretch bending to introduce pre-tension that reduces elastic recovery.
  4. Select a larger inner bend radius (higher R/t ratio).

**Wrinkling (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Wave-like buckles that appear on the inside (compression) surface of a bent flange, especially when the flange is long and the sheet is thin.
- **Cause:** The compressive longitudinal stress on the inner fibers exceeds the critical buckling stress $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{kE}{(b/t)^2}$. When the unsupported flange length (b) becomes too large relative to thickness (t), it buckles.
- **Remedies:**
  1. Use **wiper dies** or **bead dies** that apply localized pressure on the flange to suppress buckling.
  2. Reduce the unsupported flange length through redesign.
  3. Use a higher-strength (thicker) sheet material.
  4. Apply side thrust or restraint on the flange.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A 1.5 mm thick aluminum sheet (Y = 180 MPa, E = 70 GPa) is bent in a V-die with inner bend radius R = 3 mm. Calculate the expected springback angle for a 90° bend. Also determine the tool angle required.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute R/t ratio.

$$
\dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{3}{1.5} = 2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the springback formula.

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{Y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180^\circ}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t) + 1}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{180}{70{,}000} \cdot 57.296 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} = 0.002571 \cdot 57.296 \cdot 0.3333
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} \approx 0.0491 \text{ rad} \approx 2.81^\circ
$$

**Step 3:** Required tool angle.

$$
\theta_{tool} = 90^\circ - 2.81^\circ \approx 87.19^\circ
$$

**Final Answer:** Springback ≈ **2.81°**; Tool angle ≈ **87.2°**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the various **surface and metallurgical defects** encountered in bending operations. Discuss their causes and remedies with examples.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Orange Peel (2 marks):**
- **Cause:** Coarse grain size (ASTM ≤ 5); each grain deforms independently at the outer tensile surface, creating a pebble-like texture.
- **Remedy:** Use fine-grained sheet (ASTM 7+); anneal the blank before bending; increase the bend radius to reduce per-grain strain.

**2. Cracking / Fracture (2 marks):**
- **Cause:** Outer fiber tensile strain exceeds the material's allowable strain limit. For steels, $\varepsilon_{max} \approx 0.4 \times (t/R)$. Sharp bend radii or hard materials push this beyond the limit.
- **Remedy:** Increase the bend radius (use R ≥ t for hard steels); anneal the material before bending; bend across the rolling direction where grain flow favors ductility.

**3. Die Marks / Witness Marks (1.5 marks):**
- **Cause:** Sharp punch radius, poor die surface finish, or contamination on tool surface.
- **Remedy:** Polish the punch radius to mirror finish; use urethane or rubber pads; clean tooling between batches; apply protective film on the sheet.

**4. Surface Scratches and Galling (1.5 marks):**
- **Cause:** Relative sliding between punch and sheet under high contact pressure.
- **Remedy:** Use proper lubrication (drawing compound, MoS₂); reduce punch penetration speed; use coated or polished tooling.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A sheet of stainless steel (Y = 290 MPa, E = 200 GPa, t = 2 mm) is to be bent to a final angle of 120° with a bend radius R = 6 mm. Estimate the springback and the corrected tool angle. Comment on whether this bend is safe from cracking.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute R/t.

$$
\dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute springback.

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{290}{200{,}000} \cdot 57.296 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3+1}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} = 0.00145 \cdot 57.296 \cdot 0.25
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{springback} \approx 0.0208 \text{ rad} \approx 1.19^\circ
$$

**Step 3:** Tool angle.

$$
\theta_{tool} = 120^\circ - 1.19^\circ \approx 118.81^\circ
$$

**Step 4:** Outer fiber strain check.

$$
\varepsilon_{outer} \approx \dfrac{1}{(2K)-1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t)+1}
$$

Assuming K = 0.4 for stainless steel:

$$
\varepsilon_{outer} \approx \dfrac{1}{0.8-1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3+1}
$$

Since $2K - 1$ is negative for K < 0.5, we use the standard formula $\varepsilon_{outer} \approx \dfrac{1}{2(R/t)+1}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{outer} \approx \dfrac{1}{2(3)+1} = \dfrac{1}{7} \approx 0.143 \text{ or } 14.3\%
$$

**Step 5:** Comment.
Since $\varepsilon_{outer} = 14.3\% < 40\%$ (allowable limit for steel), the bend is **safe from cracking**.

**Final Answer:** Springback ≈ **1.19°**; Tool angle ≈ **118.8°**; Bend is safe from cracking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *springback* (angle increases after release) with *spring-forward* (angle decreases). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 2. Always include **units** in numerical answers — a missing unit costs 0.5 marks even if the number is correct.
> 3. The springback formula is **valid only for elastic-perfectly plastic materials**; for work-hardening materials (e.g., SS304, aluminum alloys), the actual springback is 10–20% less than the formula predicts.
> 4. When asked to *remedy springback*, do NOT just say "increase the bend radius" — examiners want the **tool angle compensation** as the primary answer.
> 5. KTU board expects a **sketch** in long-answer questions; omitting it can cost 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Springback** = elastic recovery → final angle > tool angle → compensate by **overbending**.
- ✅ **Spring-forward** = reverse recovery → final angle < tool angle → **reduce overbend**.
- ✅ **Cracking** on outer surface → caused by tensile strain > allowable → **increase R/t ratio**.
- ✅ **Wrinkling** on inner surface → caused by compressive buckling → **use wiper dies**.
- ✅ **Neutral axis shift** → use **K-factor** (0.33 to 0.50) in CAD flat-pattern development.
- ✅ **Orange peel** → caused by **coarse grain size** → use **fine-grained sheet (ASTM 7+)**.
- ✅ **Twist** → caused by **misalignment or asymmetric flow** → use longer punch and proper alignment.
- ✅ **Die marks** → caused by **rough tooling** → **polish die and use lubrication**.
- ✅ Key formula: $\Delta\theta_{springback} = \dfrac{Y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180^\circ}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t) + 1}$
- ✅ Tool angle rule: $\theta_{tool} = \theta_{final} - \Delta\theta_{springback}$
- ✅ Always check **outer fiber strain** against the **material allowable** before finalizing bend radius.
- ✅ Use **coining or bottoming** for precision bends (e.g., aerospace, electronics enclosures).
- ✅ Use **wiper dies** for long flanges and thin sheets (e.g., automotive panels).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forming by Shearing: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forming by Shearing: Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forming by Shearing** is a chipless material-removal (actually a *separation*) process in the broader family of metal forming operations, in which the localized plastic deformation of a sheet-metal workpiece is induced by a pair of opposing, sharp-edged tools — typically a **punch** and a **die** — until the material fractures along a narrow shear zone, thereby separating the workpiece into two or more parts without the formation of chips or the use of heat.

In the KTU 2024 scheme classification, shearing falls under the category of **cold-working sheet-metal processes** and forms the foundation for several industrial operations such as **blanking, piercing, slitting, trimming, notching, and cutoff**. It is governed by the principles of **plastic shear deformation** and **ductile fracture mechanics** rather than by chip-removal (machining) thermodynamics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of cutting a piece of paper with a pair of scissors.** When you close the scissors, two sharp blades approach the paper from opposite sides. The paper is not "machined" away; instead, it is *pinched* plastically along a very thin band and then *fractures* cleanly along that band. Industrial shearing works on exactly the same principle — only the "scissors blades" are hardened steel punch-and-die edges, and the "paper" is a metal sheet 0.1 mm to 25 mm thick.

- **Punch** = the upper scissors blade (male tool).
- **Die** = the lower scissors blade (female tool with an opening).
- **Clearance** = the small gap between punch and die (analogous to the gap between the two scissor blades when closed).
- **Shear zone** = the narrow strip of material that actually yields and fractures.
- **Burnish (roll-over) zone** = the smooth, polished band on the cut edge.
- **Fracture zone** = the rougher band where final separation occurs.
- **Burr** = the small ragged projection formed on the workpiece edge.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following empirical constants govern the working principle of shearing and are highlighted in the KTU 2024 scheme data book:

- **Shear Strength of Mild Steel (typical):** $\tau \approx 300 \text{ to } 400 \text{ MPa}$
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) to Shear Strength ratio:** $\tau \approx 0.7 \text{ to } 0.8 \cdot S_{ut}$
- **Standard Optimum Clearance (per side) as a percentage of sheet thickness:** $c / t \approx \mathbf{4\%} \text{ to } \mathbf{10\%}$ depending on material
- **Shear Angle (φ):** typically $0^{\circ} \text{ to} 20^{\circ}$ for standard punches; 0° for parallel-edge tools.

---

# 1.4 Types of Shearing Operations (Syllabus-Relevant Classification)

| Operation | Functional Description | Typical Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Shearing / Cutoff** | Straight-line separation of a strip from a larger sheet | Slitting lines, plate cutting |
| **Blanking** | Punch produces the *required* part; scrap is the surround | Coin blanks, flat washers, gaskets |
| **Piercing** | Punch removes the *scrap* slug; the sheet is the required part | Holes in brackets, gear blanks |
| **Notching** | Edge removal of small shapes from the sheet edge | Sheet-metal enclosures |
| **Trimming** | Removal of excess flash from a previously formed part | Forged/cast cleanup |
| **Slitting** | Longitudinal cutting of a coil into narrower strips | Roll-form feedstock preparation |
| **Shaving** | Secondary finishing to improve edge accuracy and surface | Precision blank finishing |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU-style questions, the terms **blanking** and **piercing** are *not interchangeable* — the distinction is based on **which of the two output pieces is the desired product**. A confusing but classic exam trap!

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Five Sequential Phases of a Shear Cycle

When a punch descends on a sheet-metal workpiece placed over a die opening, the shearing action unfolds in **five well-defined mechanical stages**. Each stage must be clearly understood because the KTU examiner frequently tests the student's ability to identify *which* stage corresponds to *which* physical phenomenon.

1. **Elastic Deformation Stage**
   - The punch contacts the sheet. Stress builds up *elastically* (recoverable) throughout the workpiece.
   - No permanent shape change yet.
   - The stress field follows classical elastic-contact theory (Hertzian in nature).

2. **Plastic Deformation Stage (Penetration)**
   - Local stress exceeds the yield strength; the sheet is *plastically indented* by the punch corners.
   - The material flows around the punch tip — a small rollover (or *burnish*) region forms on the cut edge.
   - This is the **penetration** stage — the punch has typically travelled about 10–25% of the sheet thickness into the material.

3. **Burnishing (Roll-Over) Stage**
   - The punch continues; a *smooth, shiny, burnished band* appears on the cut edge.
   - This is the region where pure plastic shear has occurred without fracture.
   - Burnish percentage is a **measure of tool sharpness and clearance adequacy**.

4. **Fracture Initiation Stage**
   - When the punch force reaches the **fracture limit**, cracks initiate at the punch and die edges.
   - These two crack fronts propagate toward each other through the sheet thickness.
   - **Clearance is the single most important parameter** controlling when and how these cracks meet.

5. **Fracture Completion & Separation**
   - Cracks coalesce; the sheet separates into two pieces.
   - A small **burr** may form on the workpiece edge if clearance is excessive.
   - The cut is complete — punch begins its retraction (stripping) stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Clearance Critical?** — If the clearance is *too small*, the two crack fronts meet prematurely, producing a *secondary shear* and a rough, double-cut edge with excessive tool wear. If the clearance is *too large*, the cracks diverge and meet in the middle of the sheet, producing a *tapered cut with a heavy burr*. Only the **optimum clearance** produces a clean, vertical, single-shear fracture.

## 2.2 The Mechanics of the Shear Zone

Consider a rectangular strip of width $L$ and thickness $t$ being sheared between a punch and die. The shear zone is the parallelogram-shaped band of material that undergoes shear. If $\phi$ is the **shear angle** (the angle between the shear plane and the sheet plane), then:

- The **area of the shear plane** (the actual failure surface) is:

$$A_{s} = \frac{L \cdot t}{\sin \phi}$$

- The **shear length** along which the punch travels before fracture is reduced because of the inclination.
- When $\phi = 0°$ (parallel punch and die edges), $A_s = L \cdot t$ — the **maximum possible shear area** and hence the maximum force.
- As $\phi$ increases, the shear area decreases and the punch force drops, but the cut quality degrades (the cut becomes more inclined and rougher).

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numericals, the standard assumption is $\phi = 0°$ (i.e., a *parallel-edge punch*), giving $A_s = L \cdot t$ unless the problem explicitly specifies a sloped or angled punch.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Punch Force Calculation

The force required to shear a length $L$ through a thickness $t$ of material having shear strength $\tau$ is given by the **basic shearing equation**:

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

But in real practice, an **inclination factor** and a **friction factor** modify this:

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau \cdot (1 + \mu \cot \phi)$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between punch corner and sheet (typical: $\mu \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.15$),
- $\phi$ = shear angle,
- The term $(1 + \mu \cot \phi)$ is the **inclination-friction correction factor**.

For a **round blank** of diameter $D$:

$$F = \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

This is because the perimeter of the shear path is $\pi D$.

## 2.4 Optimum Clearance — Empirical Rules

The optimum clearance is the **clearance that allows the two crack fronts to meet exactly at the sheet's mid-plane**, producing a single clean fracture. Empirical rules (data-book standard for KTU):

| Material Type | Optimum Clearance (\% of $t$ per side) |
|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 7% – 10% |
| Stainless Steel | 6% – 9% |
| Aluminium & Alloys | 5% – 8% |
| Copper & Brass | 4% – 6% |

The general rule of thumb used in KTU problems:

$$c_{optimum} = 0.05 \, t \text{ to } 0.10 \, t \quad \text{(per side)}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for KTU 2024 shearing numericals. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions carefully.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Notes | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shear Area (parallel punch) | $A_s = L \cdot t$ | $L$=cut perimeter, $t$=thickness | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 2 | Shear Area (inclined punch) | $A_s = (L \cdot t) / \sin \phi$ | $\phi$=shear angle | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 3 | Basic Shear Force | $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | $\tau$=shear strength | $\text{N}$ |
| 4 | Shear Force w/ Friction & Inclination | $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau \cdot (1 + \mu \cot \phi)$ | $\mu$=friction coefficient | $\text{N}$ |
| 5 | Shear Force for Round Blank | $F = \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | $D$=blank diameter | $\text{N}$ |
| 6 | Maximum Shear Strain | $\gamma_{max} = \cot \phi$ | pure shear along the shear plane | dimensionless |
| 7 | Shear Stress on Inclined Plane | $\tau = (S_{ut}/2) \cdot \sin 2\theta$ | $\theta$=angle of plane with load axis | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 8 | Optimum Clearance (per side) | $c = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot t$ | empirical rule | $\text{mm}$ |
| 9 | Stripping Force | $F_{strip} = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot F_{punch}$ | 5–10% rule | $\text{N}$ |
| 10 | Work Done (Shearing Energy) | $W = F \cdot (k \cdot t)$ | $k$=penetration fraction (≈0.3 to 0.6) | $\text{J}$ |
| 11 | Power Required | $P = F \cdot v$ | $v$=punch velocity | $\text{W}$ |
| 12 | Shear Strength from UTS | $\tau \approx 0.75 \cdot S_{ut}$ | empirical relation | $\text{MPa}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The shearing principle is the operating foundation of:

- **Press tools** (mechanical and hydraulic presses) used in every automotive sheet-metal shop.
- **Cropping and shearing machines** in steel rolling mills for billet cutoff.
- **Stamping dies** for mass production of washers, brackets, electrical laminations.
- **PCB blanking and lead-cutting** in electronics manufacturing.
- **Tin-can manufacturing** (slitting and blanking at very high speeds, > 600 strokes/min).
- **Blanking of silicon steel laminations** for transformer cores.

The selection of the correct clearance, shear angle, and punch material is what differentiates a *production-grade tool* from an *academic exercise*.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Shear Strain in an Inclined Shear Zone

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a rectangular element of sheet metal of thickness $t$ and unit width, being sheared along a plane inclined at an angle $\phi$ with the sheet surface (the *shear plane*). Determine the engineering shear strain $\gamma$ along the shear plane.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Shear Zone**

The shear zone is a parallelogram. Before deformation, it is a rectangle of width $t$ (along the sheet thickness direction) and unit length. After deformation, the upper face has been displaced by the punch by a small distance $\delta$ (the punch penetration).

The shear plane has length:

$$l = \frac{t}{\sin \phi}$$

**Step 2 — Relate Punch Displacement to Shear Strain**

The displacement $\delta$ is the projection of the shear deformation onto the punch axis. Therefore:

$$\delta = \gamma \cdot l \cdot \cos \phi$$

Substituting the expression for $l$:

$$\delta = \gamma \cdot \frac{t}{\sin \phi} \cdot \cos \phi = \gamma \cdot t \cdot \cot \phi$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Shear Strain**

Rearranging for $\gamma$:

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta \cdot \tan \phi}{t}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Shear Strain Condition**

The maximum value of $\tan \phi$ over the physical range $0 \le \phi \le 90°$ is unbounded, but the **engineering** maximum is taken when $\phi$ is the smallest practical angle. For a parallel punch, $\phi = 0°$, and we observe that $\delta = t \cdot \gamma \cdot \cot \phi$ gives a *degenerate* limit. In KTU problems, the *standard* form used is:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{max} = \cot \phi}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration**

If the punch penetrates $\delta = 0.5 \, t$ and $\phi = 30°$:

$$\gamma = \frac{0.5 \cdot t \cdot \tan 30°}{t} = 0.5 \cdot 0.5774 = 0.2887$$

So the engineering shear strain is **0.2887 (≈ 28.9%)** along the shear plane.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Punch Force for a Rectangular Shear Cut

**Given:** A rectangular blank of length $L$ and sheet thickness $t$ is to be sheared from a strip. The shear strength of the material is $\tau$ and the shear angle is $\phi$. A friction coefficient $\mu$ exists between punch and sheet. Determine the total punch force $F$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Shear Area**

The actual fracture surface (shear plane) lies at angle $\phi$ to the sheet surface. Its area is:

$$A_s = \frac{L \cdot t}{\sin \phi}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Pure Shear Component**

The force component along the shear plane is:

$$F_{shear} = \tau \cdot A_s = \frac{\tau \cdot L \cdot t}{\sin \phi}$$

**Step 3 — Add the Friction Component**

The normal force on the punch face is the same magnitude as the shear force (by equilibrium of the wedge). The friction force opposing punch motion is:

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_{normal} = \mu \cdot F_{shear} \cdot \cot \phi$$

(The factor $\cot \phi$ converts the shear force into its normal component on the inclined face.)

**Step 4 — Total Punch Force**

$$F = F_{shear} + F_{friction} = \frac{\tau L t}{\sin \phi} + \mu \cdot \frac{\tau L t}{\sin \phi} \cdot \cot \phi$$

$$F = \frac{\tau L t}{\sin \phi} \left( 1 + \mu \cot \phi \right)$$

$$\boxed{F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{1 + \mu \cot \phi}{\sin \phi}}$$

**Step 5 — Special Case: Parallel Punch ($\phi = 90°$)**

As $\phi \to 90°$, $\sin \phi \to 1$ and $\cot \phi \to 0$:

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{1 + 0}{1} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

This is the **basic shearing equation** universally used in KTU textbook problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Optimum Clearance as a Function of Sheet Thickness

**Given:** Two opposing crack fronts propagate from the punch edge and the die edge, traveling through the sheet thickness. The angle of propagation of each crack with respect to the sheet normal is $\alpha$ (typically 5°–8° for ductile materials). Determine the optimum clearance $c$ (per side) such that the two crack fronts meet exactly at the mid-plane of the sheet.

**Step 1 — Geometry of Crack Propagation**

Each crack front traverses a horizontal distance $c$ (the clearance) and a vertical distance $t/2$ (half the sheet thickness). The crack angle $\alpha$ satisfies:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{c}{t/2} = \frac{2c}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Optimum Clearance**

$$c = \frac{t \cdot \tan \alpha}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Insert the Empirical Value of $\alpha$**

For mild steel, $\alpha \approx 6°$ to $8°$:

$$c \approx \frac{t \cdot \tan 7°}{2} \approx \frac{t \cdot 0.1228}{2} \approx 0.0614 \cdot t$$

So **$c \approx 6\% \cdot t$ per side** — a clean match to the empirical "6–10%" rule quoted in data books.

$$\boxed{c_{optimum} \approx 0.05 \, t \text{ to } 0.10 \, t \quad \text{(per side)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Shear Cut Quality vs. Clearance

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a *favourite* KTU short-question topic. A student should be able to reproduce it from memory.

| Clearance Condition | Crack Behaviour | Cut Surface | Tool Wear | Dimensional Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Too small** (< 4% t) | Cracks meet *early*, secondary shear occurs | Rough, double-shear, steep | Excessive (tool chipping) | Poor (oversize) |
| **Optimum** (5–10% t) | Cracks meet *exactly* at mid-plane | Smooth, vertical, single shear | Normal | Excellent |
| **Too large** (> 12% t) | Cracks diverge, meet in middle of sheet | Tapered, heavy burr | Mild | Poor (undersize) |

---

## 3.5 Sample Numerical — KTU Style

> A 3 mm thick mild-steel plate is to be sheared to produce a rectangular blank of dimensions 200 mm × 150 mm. The shear strength of the material is 320 MPa. Determine:
> (a) The shearing force required (assume parallel edges, no friction).
> (b) The work done if the punch travels through 45% of the sheet thickness.
> (c) The optimum clearance per side.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** The shearing perimeter is:

$$L = 2 \cdot (200 + 150) = 700 \text{ mm}$$

The shear area is:

$$A_s = L \cdot t = 700 \cdot 3 = 2100 \text{ mm}^2$$

The shearing force is:

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau = 700 \cdot 3 \cdot 320 = 6.72 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 672 \text{ kN}$$

**Part (b):** Punch penetration:

$$\delta = 0.45 \cdot t = 0.45 \cdot 3 = 1.35 \text{ mm}$$

Work done:

$$W = F \cdot \delta = 6.72 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.35 \times 10^{-3} = 907.2 \text{ J}$$

**Part (c):** Optimum clearance (using 7% rule):

$$c = 0.07 \cdot t = 0.07 \cdot 3 = 0.21 \text{ mm per side}$$

> [!TIP]
> Always express the *units* explicitly in KTU answer scripts — examiners often deduct marks for missing units in the final answer.
