# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of sheet-metal shearing. List any three industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

**Working Principle:** Shearing is a cold-working separation process in which a sheet-metal workpiece is plastically deformed along a narrow zone between a punch (male tool) and a die (female tool) until the material fractures. The punch exerts a compressive force on the sheet placed over the die opening. The metal first undergoes elastic deformation, then plastic indentation (penetration), then burnishing, and finally fracture initiation and propagation. The two crack fronts from the punch and die edges meet, separating the sheet into two parts.

**Three Industrial Applications:**
1. **Blanking** of flat washers, gaskets, and coin blanks in mass production.
2. **Piercing** of holes in sheet-metal brackets, gear blanks, and electrical laminations.
3. **Cropping/cutoff** of billets and slabs in steel rolling mills.

> [!TIP]
> For a 3-mark answer, the examiner expects: 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for sketch (mention 5 stages), and 1 mark for applications.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Define "optimum clearance" in shearing. State its significance and the empirical range of values.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Optimum clearance is the small gap (per side) between the punch and die edges that allows the two fracture crack fronts to meet **exactly at the mid-plane** of the sheet thickness, producing a single clean shear fracture.

**Significance:**
- **Too little clearance** → secondary shear, rough cut, excessive tool wear, poor dimensional accuracy.
- **Too much clearance** → tapered cut, heavy burr formation, poor edge quality, reduced part accuracy.
- **Optimum clearance** → clean vertical cut, long tool life, accurate dimensions, minimal burr.

**Empirical Range:**

$$c_{optimum} = 0.05 \, t \text{ to } 0.10 \, t \quad \text{(per side)}$$

(about 5% to 10% of sheet thickness per side, depending on material ductility — softer materials require more clearance).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. The student is expected to attempt **one** of the two alternatives A or B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **complete sequence of stages** through which a sheet-metal blank is sheared between a punch and a die. Explain the role of clearance in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4 mm thick steel plate of shear strength 350 MPa is to be sheared to produce a circular blank of diameter 80 mm. Assuming parallel punch-die edges and a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.08$ between the punch face and the sheet, determine:
- (i) The shearing force required.
- (ii) The optimum clearance per side (use 7% rule).
- (iii) The stripping force.
**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Stages of Shearing:**

The shearing action of a sheet-metal blank proceeds through **five distinct stages**:

**Stage 1: Elastic Deformation** — The punch contacts the sheet. Stresses build elastically. The sheet bends slightly into the die opening. No permanent deformation yet.

**Stage 2: Plastic Indentation (Penetration)** — Stress exceeds the yield strength near the punch and die edges. The material yields plastically, and the punch corner penetrates into the sheet. A small *rollover* (or burnish) region begins to form on the cut edge. The punch has typically travelled 10–25% of the sheet thickness.

**Stage 3: Burnishing** — Plastic flow continues smoothly. A *bright, smooth, burnished band* appears on the cut edge. The percentage of burnish is a measure of tool sharpness.

**Stage 4: Crack Initiation** — When the punch force reaches the fracture limit, micro-cracks initiate at the punch and die edges, propagating at an angle $\alpha$ (≈ 6°–8° for mild steel) into the sheet.

**Stage 5: Fracture and Separation** — The two crack fronts travel toward each other. If clearance is **optimum**, they meet exactly at the sheet's mid-plane, producing a single clean fracture. The blank separates from the surrounding scrap. A small burr may form on the lower edge.

**Role of Clearance:**
- If clearance is **too small** (< 4% t), the cracks meet *above* the mid-plane, causing secondary shear and a rough, double-cut edge.
- If clearance is **optimum** (5–10% t), the cracks meet at the mid-plane — clean, single shear.
- If clearance is **too large** (> 12% t), the cracks diverge and meet in the middle of the sheet, producing a *tapered* cut with a heavy burr.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $t = 4 \text{ mm}$, $\tau = 350 \text{ MPa}$, $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, $\mu = 0.08$, parallel edges ($\phi = 90°$).

**(i) Shearing Force:**

For a circular blank, the shearing perimeter is:

$$L = \pi \cdot D = \pi \cdot 80 = 251.33 \text{ mm}$$

Shear area:

$$A_s = L \cdot t = 251.33 \cdot 4 = 1005.31 \text{ mm}^2$$

With the friction correction factor for $\phi = 90°$, $\cot \phi = 0$, so the factor reduces to $(1 + 0) = 1$:

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau = 251.33 \cdot 4 \cdot 350 = 3.519 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 351.9 \text{ kN}$$

> **Note on Friction:** When $\phi = 90°$ (parallel punch and die edges), the friction term $\mu \cot \phi$ becomes zero, and the friction coefficient $\mu$ does not enter the equation. The "$\mu = 0.08$" data is a distractor in this case, testing whether the student knows the geometric limit.

**(ii) Optimum Clearance (per side):**

$$c = 0.07 \cdot t = 0.07 \cdot 4 = 0.28 \text{ mm per side}$$

**(iii) Stripping Force:**

$$F_{strip} = 0.08 \cdot F_{punch} = 0.08 \cdot 351.9 = 28.15 \text{ kN}$$

(Using the upper bound of the 5–10% range.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **maximum shearing force required to blank a circular disc** of diameter $D$ and thickness $t$ from a sheet metal of shear strength $\tau$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6 mm thick aluminium plate is to be sheared along a straight line of length 500 mm. The shear strength of aluminium is 110 MPa and the UTS is 145 MPa. If the punch has a shear angle of $\phi = 60°$ and the coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.1$, determine:
- (i) The punch force using the inclined-edge formula.
- (ii) The shear strain developed along the shear plane.
- (iii) The work done if the punch penetrates the full thickness.
**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The punch and die edges are parallel and sharp.
2. The material is homogeneous and isotropic.
3. The shear strength $\tau$ is constant throughout the operation.
4. Friction between punch and sheet is neglected (or accounted for separately).
5. The fracture occurs instantaneously once the critical force is reached.

**Derivation:**

Consider a circular disc of diameter $D$ and thickness $t$. The punch pushes the disc through the die opening. The material is sheared along a **cylindrical surface** of height $t$ and circumference $\pi D$.

The shear area is:

$$A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot t$$

The maximum shearing force occurs when the punch has fully engaged and the entire shear area is resisting:

$$\boxed{F_{max} = \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot \tau}$$

This is the **classic blanking force equation** quoted in every KTU data book.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $t = 6 \text{ mm}$, $L = 500 \text{ mm}$, $\tau = 110 \text{ MPa}$, $\phi = 60°$, $\mu = 0.1$.

**(i) Punch Force (Inclined-Edge Formula):**

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{1 + \mu \cot \phi}{\sin \phi}$$

Compute the trigonometric terms:

$$\cot 60° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$$

$$\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$$

Substitute:

$$F = 500 \cdot 6 \cdot 110 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.1 \cdot 0.5774}{0.8660}$$

$$F = 330000 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.0577}{0.8660} = 330000 \cdot \frac{1.0577}{0.8660}$$

$$F = 330000 \cdot 1.2214 = 4.031 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 403.1 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Shear Strain:**

The maximum engineering shear strain along the inclined plane is:

$$\gamma_{max} = \cot \phi = \cot 60° = 0.5774$$

So $\gamma \approx 0.5774$ (≈ 57.74%).

**(iii) Work Done:**

Assuming the punch penetrates the full sheet thickness ($\delta = t = 6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$):

$$W = F \cdot \delta = 4.031 \times 10^5 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-3} = 2418.6 \text{ J} \approx 2.42 \text{ kJ}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing blanking and piercing** — Examiners routinely deduct 1–2 marks for this. Always identify which of the two output pieces is the *desired* product.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\mu \cot \phi$ term** — When $\phi$ is given (not equal to 90°), the friction-inclination factor must be included. A surprising number of students omit it.
> 3. **Using $S_{ut}$ instead of $\tau$** — The basic shearing equation uses shear strength $\tau$, not ultimate tensile strength. Use $\tau \approx 0.75 \cdot S_{ut}$ to convert.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — A 7-mark numerical without proper units typically loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Stripping force ignored** — Stripping force (5–10% of punch force) is a frequent sub-question. Omitting it costs a mark.
> 6. **Clearance given as a *total* value** — Some problems give clearance in the die design; the student must remember that $c$ in the formula is *per side*, not total.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Forming by Shearing: Working Principle**

- **Definition** — Shearing is a *separation* (not material-removal) process using punch-and-die plastic shear.
- **Five Stages of Shearing** — Elastic → Plastic Indentation → Burnish → Crack Initiation → Fracture & Separation.
- **Basic Shear Force Equation** — $\mathbf{F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau}$ (parallel edges, no friction).
- **Inclined-Edge Equation** — $\mathbf{F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau \cdot (1 + \mu \cot \phi) / \sin \phi}$.
- **Round Blank Force** — $\mathbf{F = \pi \cdot D \cdot t \cdot \tau}$.
- **Maximum Shear Strain** — $\mathbf{\gamma_{max} = \cot \phi}$.
- **Optimum Clearance** — $\mathbf{c = 5\% \text{ to } 10\% \text{ of } t}$ per side.
- **Stripping Force** — $\mathbf{F_{strip} = 5\% \text{ to } 10\% \text{ of } F_{punch}}$.
- **Shear Strength vs UTS** — $\mathbf{\tau \approx 0.75 \cdot S_{ut}}$.
- **Effect of Clearance** — Too small → rough, double-shear; Too large → tapered, heavy burr; Optimum → clean, vertical, single-shear.
- **Edge Anatomy** — Roll-over (burnish) zone → Shear zone → Fracture zone → Burr.
- **Crack Angle ($\alpha$)** — typically 6°–8° for ductile mild steel.
- **Difference: Blanking vs Piercing** — Blanking: punched piece is the *product*. Piercing: punched piece is the *scrap*.
- **Industrial Examples** — Coin blanking, gear blank piercing, transformer lamination blanking, can-body slitting.
- **Key Assumption in KTU Numericals** — Parallel punch-die edges ($\phi = 90°$) unless otherwise stated; $\mu = 0$ unless stated.
- **Energy / Work Equation** — $\mathbf{W = F \cdot (k \cdot t)}$ where $k$ is the penetration fraction (≈ 0.3 to 0.6).
- **Power Equation** — $\mathbf{P = F \cdot v}$ where $v$ is punch velocity.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Forming by Shearing: Working Principle**
> *Refer to the KTU 2024 Scheme Data Book for material-specific clearance and strength values during the examination.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **PCPET602 – Metal Forming and Tool Design** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme), the **"Characteristic Features"** of metal forming refer to the universally observable metallurgical, mechanical, and thermodynamical attributes that govern **bulk plastic deformation processes** such as forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing, and sheet-metal forming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2 Anchor Statement):**
> *"Metal forming is a manufacturing process in which the desired shape change is obtained through plastic deformation of the material, without any intentional change in mass or composition, and without removal of metal as chips."*

Mathematically, the principle of **mass conservation (incompressibility of plastic flow)** can be expressed as:

$$\rho_{i} \cdot V_{i} = \rho_{f} \cdot V_{f}$$

For plastic deformation, the volumetric strain is essentially zero, so $\rho_{i} \approx \rho_{f}$, and the **initial volume equals the final volume** during the forming operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Play-Doh Concept"**
> Imagine squeezing a chunk of **Play-Doh** (or *kneaded dough*) between your palms. The dough does not disappear, does not evaporate, and does not change in mass — it merely *changes shape*. The dough flows from a region of high compressive stress to a region of free surface, exactly the way a hot steel billet flows inside a forging die. The forces you apply exceed the dough's **yield strength** (just like exceeding the **flow stress** $\sigma_f$ in metal), and permanent shape change occurs. This is the entire essence of metal forming.

## 1.3 Primary Characteristic Features (KTU-Aligned Bullet List)

The KTU 2024 scheme identifies the following **distinctive characteristic features** that all metal forming processes share:

1. **Plastic Deformation Above Yield Stress** — the flow stress $\sigma_f$ of the workpiece must be exceeded.
2. **Mass / Volume Conservation** — no metal is added or removed; volume is essentially preserved.
3. **Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)** — flow stress increases as strain accumulates.
4. **Grain Flow Continuity** — the internal fibrous structure follows the contour of the deformation (the famous *fibre-flow* pattern).
5. **Frictional Effects at Tool–Work Interface** — governed by **Coulomb's law of friction**: $\tau = \mu \cdot p$ (where $\mu$ is the friction coefficient and $p$ is the interface pressure).
6. **Temperature Dependence** — cold, warm, or hot working regimes based on the **recrystallization temperature** ($T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin).
7. **Residual Stresses** — locked-in elastic stresses after tool removal.
8. **Tooling & Die Wear** — significant, since contact stresses are extremely high (**order of 10³ MPa**).
9. **High Productivity & Material Economy** — near-net-shape manufacturing with minimal scrap.
10. **Directional Mechanical Properties** — strength and toughness vary with the direction of grain flow (anisotropy).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used by KTU Examiners

| S. No. | Metric | Typical Value (KTU Reference) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Recrystallization Ratio | **0.4 $T_m$** | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$) | **0.05 – 0.40** | Dimensionless |
| 3 | True Strain ($\varepsilon$) | **0.1 – 2.0** | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Flow Stress ($\sigma_f$) | **100 – 1500** | MPa |
| 5 | Strain-Rate Sensitivity ($m$) | **0.0 – 0.3** | Dimensionless |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight for KTU 2024:**
> Examiner's *favourite* characteristic features to ask in Part A (3 marks) are: (i) mass conservation, (ii) strain hardening, and (iii) the role of recrystallization temperature in classifying cold / warm / hot working. **Memorize these three first.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Characteristic Feature

### Feature 1 — Plastic Deformation Beyond Yield

- **Why?** Metal forming requires *permanent* shape change, not elastic recovery.
- **How?** The applied stress must satisfy the **von Mises yield criterion** (for ductile metals):

$$\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]} \geq \sigma_y$$

If the equivalent von Mises stress equals or exceeds the **uniaxial yield stress** $\sigma_y$, plastic flow initiates.

### Feature 2 — Volume Constancy (Incompressibility)

- **Why?** Metals in the plastic regime behave like an **incompressible fluid** because the hydrostatic component of stress produces only elastic volume change (negligible).
- **How?** The **dilatation** $\theta = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$.

In a 2-D rolling analogy:

$$h_0 \cdot L_0 = h_f \cdot L_f$$

where $h_0$, $L_0$ are initial thickness and length, and $h_f$, $L_f$ are final.

### Feature 3 — Strain (Work) Hardening

- **Why?** Dislocation density increases during plastic flow, raising the resistance to further deformation.
- **How?** The empirical **Hollomon power-law** describes the flow curve:

$$\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

where:
- $\sigma_f$ = **flow stress (MPa)**
- $K$ = **strength coefficient (MPa)**
- $\varepsilon$ = **true strain**
- $n$ = **strain-hardening exponent** (typically **0.0 – 0.5**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine repeatedly bending a paper clip. The first bend is easy; the second bend at the same spot is *harder*. That is strain hardening.

### Feature 4 — Grain Flow Alignment

- **Why?** Grains elongate in the direction of maximum principal strain, producing **anisotropic** mechanical properties.
- **How?** Fibre flow lines wrap around die contours, increasing impact toughness in service directions.

### Feature 5 — Tool–Work Interface Friction

- **Why?** Friction opposes material flow, raises forming load, and may cause surface defects.
- **How?** Coulomb's friction law at the interface:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot p \quad \text{(for sticking–sliding condition: } \tau = \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{3}} \text{)}$$

The **sticking friction** limit (Tresca) sets the **maximum frictional shear** at $\tau_{max} = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{3}$.

### Feature 6 — Temperature Classification (Cold / Warm / Hot Working)

- **Why?** Temperature controls recrystallization, which in turn controls whether strain hardening is "locked in" or "erased".
- **How?** Compare working temperature $T_w$ to the recrystallization temperature $T_{rec}$:

$$T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \cdot T_m \quad (\text{Kelvin})$$

- **Cold Working:** $T_w < 0.3 \, T_m$ — strain hardening retained, no grain refinement.
- **Warm Working:** $0.3 \, T_m \leq T_w \leq 0.5 \, T_m$ — partial recovery.
- **Hot Working:** $T_w > 0.5 \, T_m$ — full recrystallization, no residual hardening.

### Feature 7 — Residual Stresses

- **Why?** After unloading, non-uniform plastic strain leaves elastic stresses locked inside.
- **How?** Magnitude may reach **30–50%** of yield stress; relieved by **stress-relief annealing**.

### Feature 8 — Tooling & Die Wear

- **Why?** Contact pressures of the order of the workpiece's flow stress (**typically 500 – 2000 MPa**).
- **How?** Tool life is governed by adhesive wear, abrasive wear, and thermal fatigue. Lubrication reduces $\mu$ from ~0.4 to ~0.05.

### Feature 9 — High Productivity & Material Economy

- **Why?** No chips → **yield > 90%** (vs. 50–70% for machining).
- **How?** Near-net-shape capability reduces post-processing.

### Feature 10 — Directional Mechanical Properties (Anisotropy)

- **Why?** Fibre flow creates a *forging* direction with higher toughness.
- **How?** Quantified by the **Lankford coefficient (R-value)** and the **normal anisotropy** $\bar{R}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_i V_i = \rho_f V_f$ | Mass conservation | All forming ops |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$ | Incompressibility | Forging, rolling |
| 3 | $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$ | Hollomon flow curve | Cold working |
| 4 | $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n \dot{\varepsilon}^m$ | Cowper–Symonds (rate-sensitive) | Hot working |
| 5 | $T_{rec} = 0.4 \, T_m$ | Recrystallization temperature | Classify working regime |
| 6 | $\tau_{max} = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{3}$ | Sticking friction (Tresca) | Friction limit |
| 7 | $\tau = \mu p$ | Coulomb friction | Sliding interface |
| 8 | $\bar{R} = (R_0 + 2R_{45} + R_{90})/4$ | Normal anisotropy | Sheet forming |
| 9 | $\sigma_{vM} \geq \sigma_y$ | von Mises yield | Plastic-flow onset |
| 10 | $F = \sigma_f \cdot A$ | Forming force (basic) | Quick load estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These ten formulas cover ~90% of the numerical problems asked in **KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)** for Module 2. Memorize and practice.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry:** Hot forging of turbine discs relies on grain flow continuity (Feature 4) for fatigue life.
- **Automotive Crankshafts:** Drop-forged using hot work to exploit zero residual hardening.
- **Wire Drawing:** Cold working exploits strain hardening to gain tensile strength.
- **Sheet Metal Stamping (Car Body Panels):** Friction and anisotropy (Features 5 & 10) determine *formability* and *earing* defects.
- **Nuclear Reactor Components:** Hot isostatic pressing (HIP) of near-net-shape powder parts.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** KTU 2024 examiners often frame a 14-mark question as *"Discuss the characteristic features of metal forming processes with neat sketches."* The trick is to group the features under **Mechanical, Metallurgical, Thermal, and Tribological** headings — a 4-pillar classification that scores the highest.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume Constancy from Plastic Incompressibility

**Given:** A cylindrical billet of initial length $L_0$ and cross-sectional area $A_0$ is upset (compressed) axially between two flat dies. Final length = $L_f$.

**Step 1 — Initial Volume:**

$$V_0 = A_0 \cdot L_0$$

**Step 2 — Final Volume:**

$$V_f = A_f \cdot L_f$$

**Step 3 — Apply Mass Conservation (no material loss):**

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_f \cdot L_f$$

**Step 4 — Define True Strain (in axial direction):**

$$\varepsilon_z = \ln \left( \frac{L_f}{L_0} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Express Length Ratio:**

$$\frac{L_f}{L_0} = e^{\varepsilon_z}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into Volume Equation:**

$$A_f = A_0 \cdot \frac{L_0}{L_f} = A_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon_z}$$

**Step 7 — Define Radial True Strain (cylindrical symmetry, $\varepsilon_r = \varepsilon_\theta$):**

$$\varepsilon_r = \ln \left( \frac{r_f}{r_0} \right)$$

**Step 8 — Enforce Incompressibility (sum of principal strains = 0):**

$$\varepsilon_z + 2\varepsilon_r = 0$$

**Step 9 — Solve for Radial Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_r = -\frac{\varepsilon_z}{2}$$

**Step 10 — Express Final Radius:**

$$r_f = r_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon_z / 2}$$

**Conclusion:** A 50% reduction in length ($\varepsilon_z = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$) forces the radius to grow by 41.4% ($r_f / r_0 = e^{-0.347} \approx 1.414$). This is the *forging bulge* phenomenon — the basis of all forging design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Forging Pressure in Plane-Strain Compression

**Given:** A slab of width $w$ is compressed by a flat die of length $L$. The interface friction coefficient is $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Free-body Element of Length $dx$:**

The pressure $p$ on the element and the friction shear $\tau = \mu p$ on top and bottom.

**Step 2 — Horizontal Force Balance on Element:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p + dp) \cdot w \cdot h - p \cdot w \cdot h - 2 \tau \cdot w \cdot dx &= 0 \\
dp \cdot h &= 2 \tau \, dx \\
dp \cdot h &= 2 \mu p \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dp}{p} &= \frac{2 \mu}{h} \, dx \\
\int_{p_0}^{p} \frac{dp}{p} &= \int_{0}^{x} \frac{2 \mu}{h} \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Both Sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln \left( \frac{p}{p_0} \right) &= \frac{2 \mu x}{h} \\
p(x) &= p_0 \cdot e^{2 \mu x / h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Average Pressure over Length L:**

$$
p_{avg} = \frac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} p_0 \cdot e^{2 \mu x / h} \, dx
$$

**Step 6 — Perform the Integration:**

$$
p_{avg} = \frac{p_0 \, h}{2 \mu L} \left( e^{2 \mu L / h} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result (KTU-Board-Standard Form):**

$$
\boxed{\,p_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_f \, h}{2 \mu L} \left( e^{2 \mu L / h} - 1 \right)\,}
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- If $\mu L / h$ is small (well-lubricated, thin slab): $p_{avg} \approx \sigma_f$ → **homogeneous deformation**.
- If $\mu L / h$ is large (dry friction, thick slab): $p_{avg} \gg \sigma_f$ → **non-uniform flow**, high die pressure, possible die failure.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Strain-Rate Sensitivity (Hot Working Context)

**Given:** Flow stress depends on strain and strain rate per the **Cowper–Symonds model**:

$$\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^m$$

**Step 1 — Take Natural Logarithm:**

$$\ln \sigma_f = \ln K + n \ln \varepsilon + m \ln \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Strain Rate at Constant Strain:**

$$
m = \left. \frac{\partial (\ln \sigma_f)}{\partial (\ln \dot{\varepsilon})} \right\vert_{\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical Meaning:**

- $m \approx 0$ → cold working, no rate sensitivity.
- $m \approx 0.2$ → typical hot work, superplastic behaviour approached as $m \to 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** For Part A (3 marks), the examiner may ask *"Define the strain-rate sensitivity exponent and state its typical range."* Use the answer: $m = \partial \ln \sigma_f / \partial \ln \dot{\varepsilon}$, with values **0.0 – 0.3** for most metals, and approaching **0.5 – 1.0** for superplastic alloys.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Choose Which Working Regime)

| Property | Cold Working | Warm Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | $T_w < 0.3 T_m$ | $0.3 T_m \leq T_w \leq 0.5 T_m$ | $T_w > 0.5 T_m$ |
| Strain Hardening | Retained | Partial | Erased (recrystallization) |
| Surface Finish | Excellent | Good | Poor (oxidation) |
| Dimensional Accuracy | High | Moderate | Low |
| Flow Stress ($\sigma_f$) | High | Medium | Low |
| Tooling Cost | High (carbide dies) | Medium | Low (cheap die steels) |
| Typical $m$ value | ~0.0 | ~0.1 | ~0.2 |
| Examples | Cold rolling, wire drawing, deep drawing | Warm forging, tube bending | Hot rolling, forging, extrusion |

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem (Q. Style):** A cylindrical steel billet of diameter 50 mm and height 80 mm is upset to a height of 40 mm in a forging operation. If the flow curve is given by $\sigma_f = 600 \cdot \varepsilon^{0.2}$ (MPa), determine:
(i) The true axial strain
(ii) The final diameter (assume no barreling)
(iii) The average flow stress during the operation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — True Axial Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_z = \ln \left( \frac{L_f}{L_0} \right) = \ln \left( \frac{40}{80} \right) = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

Take magnitude: $\vert \varepsilon_z \vert = 0.693$.

**Step 2 — Apply Incompressibility:**

$$\varepsilon_r = -\frac{\varepsilon_z}{2} = \frac{0.693}{2} = 0.347$$

**Step 3 — Final Radius:**

$$r_f = r_0 \cdot e^{\varepsilon_r} = 25 \cdot e^{0.347} = 25 \cdot 1.414 = 35.36 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Final Diameter:**

$$d_f = 2 r_f = 70.72 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Average Flow Stress (Hollomon):**

$$
\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f} \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K \varepsilon^n \, d\varepsilon = \frac{K \cdot \varepsilon_f^n}{n+1}
$$

$$
\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{600 \cdot (0.693)^{0.2}}{1.2} = \frac{600 \cdot 0.929}{1.2} = \frac{557.6}{1.2} \approx 464.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Answer:** (i) 0.693, (ii) 70.7 mm, (iii) **~ 465 MPa**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** KTU 2024 valuation expects **three significant figures** in numerical answers. Always include units. Step-wise working carries **70% of the marks**; the final answer alone is worth only ~30%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture — The 4-Pillar Classification of Characteristic Features

The following Mermaid block captures the *engineering taxonomy* of the characteristic features of metal forming, mapped into four engineering domains.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Characteristic Features of Metal Forming] --> P1[Mechanical Domain]
    A0 --> P2[Metallurgical Domain]
    A0 --> P3[Thermal Domain]
    A0 --> P4[Tribological Domain]

    P1 --> M1[Plastic Deformation Beyond Yield]
    P1 --> M2[Volume Constancy]
    P1 --> M3[Strain Rate Sensitivity]

    P2 --> N1[Strain Hardening Work Hardening]
    P2 --> N2[Grain Flow and Fibre Alignment]
    P2 --> N3[Residual Stresses]

    P3 --> T1[Cold Working T less than 0.3 Tm]
    P3 --> T2[Warm Working 0.3 to 0.5 Tm]
    P3 --> T3[Hot Working T greater than 0.5 Tm]
    T3 --> R1[Full Recrystallization at Trec equal to 0.4 Tm]

    P4 --> F1[Coulomb Friction tau equals mu p]
    P4 --> F2[Sticking Friction tau max equals sigma0 over root 3]
    P4 --> F3[Lubrication Strategy]

    M1 --> S1[Von Mises Criterion]
    M2 --> S2[Mass Conservation]
    N1 --> S3[Hollomon Equation sigma f equals K epsilon n]
    M3 --> S4[Cowper Symonds Model]
    F1 --> S5[Friction Hill Pressure]
```

## 4.2 Hot vs Cold Working — Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST[Start Selecting Working Regime] --> Q1{Working Temperature Tw}
    Q1 -- less than 0.3 Tm --> CW[Cold Working]
    Q1 -- between 0.3 Tm and 0.5 Tm --> WW[Warm Working]
    Q1 -- greater than 0.5 Tm --> HW[Hot Working]

    CW --> CW1[Strain Hardening Retained]
    CW --> CW2[High Dimensional Accuracy]
    CW --> CW3[Surface Finish Excellent]

    WW --> WW1[Partial Recrystallization]
    WW --> WW2[Moderate Tooling Cost]

    HW --> HW1[Full Recrystallization]
    HW --> HW2[No Residual Hardening]
    HW --> HW3[Lower Forming Load]
    HW --> HW4[Oxidation and Scale Risk]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Energy Flow in a Forming Operation

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Input Energy from Press or Hammer] --> TR[Transmission via Die and Ram]
    TR --> UF[Useful Work on Plastic Deformation]
    TR --> FR[Frictional Losses at Die Workpiece]
    TR --> TH[Heat Generation in Workpiece]
    TR --> EL[Elastic Strain Energy in Frame]
    UF --> OUT[Desired Shape Change]
    FR --> W1[Die Wear]
    TH --> W2[Microstructural Change]
    EL --> W3[Deflection of Machine Frame]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Flowchart — How a Billet "Becomes" a Part

```mermaid
graph TD
    BL[Billet or Slug] --> H1[Heating to Working Temperature]
    H1 --> H2[Apply Force Exceeding Flow Stress]
    H2 --> H3[Plastic Flow Through Die Cavity]
    H3 --> H4[Fibre Flow Alignment with Die Walls]
    H4 --> H5[Ejection from Die]
    H5 --> H6[Cooling and Shrinkage]
    H6 --> H7[Stress Relief if Required]
    H7 --> FG[Finished Forged Component]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These block diagrams are designed to be directly transcribable by students during KTU ESE preparation. They are intentionally text-based (Mermaid-compatible) so the logical flow of the **characteristic features** is preserved without requiring complex 2-D illustrations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State the principle of mass conservation as it applies to metal forming processes. Why is it important in forging design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The principle of mass conservation states that **the volume of the workpiece remains constant during plastic deformation** because plastic flow is essentially *incompressible* ($\rho_i V_i = \rho_f V_f$).
>
> **Importance in forging design (2 marks):** It allows the die designer to compute the *final dimensions* of the forged part from the *initial billet dimensions*. Without this principle, the final geometry cannot be predicted accurately, leading to *underfill* or *flash-heavy* forgings. Practically, it enables the calculation of *billet size* required to produce a finished forging of given mass and density.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Differentiate between cold working and hot working based on the recrystallization temperature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The recrystallization temperature is empirically given by $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$, where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin.
>
> | Aspect | Cold Working | Hot Working |
> |---|---|---|
> | Temperature | $T_w < 0.3 \, T_m$ | $T_w > 0.5 \, T_m$ |
> | Recrystallization | Does **not** occur | **Complete** recrystallization |
> | Strain hardening | **Retained** | **Erased** by new grain formation |
>
> In cold working, the deformed grain structure persists, raising strength; in hot working, new strain-free grains nucleate, restoring ductility and reducing forming load.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Sub-part Choice Pattern)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the characteristic features of metal forming processes. Group them under the headings: *Mechanical, Metallurgical, Thermal,* and *Tribological*. Support your answer with a labelled sketch of the **stress–strain curve** showing the *yield point* and the *flow curve*.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **average forging pressure** in plane-strain compression considering Coulomb friction at the die–workpiece interface.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Metal forming shapes metal by plastic deformation. The characteristic features are classified into four engineering domains.

2. **Mechanical Features (2 marks):**
   - Plastic deformation occurs only when von Mises stress $\sigma_{vM} \geq \sigma_y$.
   - Volume constancy: $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$.
   - Strain-rate sensitivity, characterized by the exponent $m$.

3. **Metallurgical Features (2 marks):**
   - Strain hardening via the Hollomon equation $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$.
   - Grain flow alignment along die contours → *anisotropy*.
   - Residual stresses after unloading (relieved by annealing).

4. **Thermal Features (1 mark):** Cold, warm, and hot working classified by $T_{rec} = 0.4 \, T_m$.

5. **Tribological Features (1 mark):** Coulomb friction $\tau = \mu p$; sticking friction limit $\tau_{max} = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{3}$.

**Sketch (Stress-Strain Curve) — Verbal Description for Board Exam:**
- Draw a *linear* elastic region up to yield point $\sigma_y$.
- Beyond $\sigma_y$, mark a smooth concave-up curve labelled *flow curve*.
- Annotate the Hollomon region with the equation $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Refer to the **full derivation** in **Section 3.2** above. The final result is:

$$p_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_f \, h}{2 \mu L} \left( e^{2 \mu L / h} - 1 \right)$$

**Marking Allocation (7 marks):**
- FBD sketch with $\tau = \mu p$ on both surfaces — **2 marks**
- Differential pressure balance $dp \cdot h = 2 \mu p \, dx$ — **1 mark**
- Integration yielding $p(x) = p_0 e^{2\mu x/h}$ — **2 marks**
- Final averaging and boxed result — **2 marks**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** A cylindrical billet of diameter 60 mm and height 100 mm is forged at room temperature. The flow curve of the material is given by $\sigma_f = 750 \cdot \varepsilon^{0.25}$ MPa. Find:
>   (i) The axial true strain
>   (ii) The final diameter assuming uniform deformation
>   (iii) The average flow stress
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the significance of the *strain-hardening exponent* $n$ and the *strain-rate sensitivity exponent* $m$ in metal forming. Indicate their typical ranges for cold, warm, and hot working.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

- (i) $\varepsilon_z = \ln(h_f/h_0) = \ln(40/100) = -0.916$ → magnitude **0.916** — **1 mark**
- (ii) $\varepsilon_r = -\varepsilon_z/2 = 0.458$; $r_f = 30 \cdot e^{0.458} = 41.6$ mm → $d_f \approx \textbf{83.2 mm}$ — **3 marks**
- (iii) $\bar{\sigma}_f = K \varepsilon_f^n / (n+1) = 750 \cdot (0.916)^{0.25} / 1.25 = 750 \cdot 0.978 / 1.25 \approx \textbf{587 MPa}$ — **3 marks**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

- **Strain-hardening exponent $n$ (3 marks):** Governs how flow stress rises with strain. High $n$ → better formability (good for sheet stretching). Typical values: **0.0 – 0.5** (steel ~0.15, aluminium ~0.30, brass ~0.45).
- **Strain-rate sensitivity exponent $m$ (3 marks):** Governs resistance to localized necking; high $m$ → superplastic behaviour. Cold: $m \approx 0$; Warm: $m \approx 0.05 - 0.1$; Hot: $m \approx 0.1 - 0.3$; Superplastic: $m > 0.5$.
- **Comparative table (1 mark):** Clearly delineate their roles in cold, warm, and hot regimes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Engineering Strain with True Strain (–2 marks typical):**
>    Engineering strain $e = \Delta L / L_0$ is valid only for small strains. For forming operations, **always use true strain** $\varepsilon = \ln(L_f / L_0)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Volume Constancy in Multi-Axial Problems (–3 marks):**
>    In forging/rolling/extrusion, students often forget that $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$. Without this, the final dimensions are computed incorrectly.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up Hollomon and Cowper–Symonds Equations (–1 mark):**
>    Hollomon is for *cold* work (no rate dependence). Cowper–Symonds includes $\dot{\varepsilon}^m$ for *hot* work. Examiners will deduct marks if the wrong equation is used.
>
> 4. **Not Labelling Diagrams Properly (–2 marks):**
>    A free-body diagram (FBD) without $\tau = \mu p$ and pressure arrows will be marked *incomplete*. Always annotate the friction direction.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 mark):**
>    If diameter is in *mm* and area is in *m²*, the final force is wrong. Convert all to **SI units (m, Pa, N)** before computing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Flash-Card (Memorize before ESE):**

- ☐ **Mass conservation:** $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ (always true in plastic flow).
- ☐ **Incompressibility:** Sum of principal true strains = **0**.
- ☐ **True strain formula:** $\varepsilon = \ln(L_f / L_0)$.
- ☐ **Hollomon equation:** $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n$ for cold work.
- ☐ **Cowper–Symonds:** $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^n \dot{\varepsilon}^m$ for hot work.
- ☐ **Recrystallization rule:** $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (Kelvin).
- ☐ **Cold / Warm / Hot classification:** $T_w < 0.3 T_m$, $0.3-0.5 T_m$, $> 0.5 T_m$.
- ☐ **Coulomb friction:** $\tau = \mu p$; **Sticking friction limit:** $\tau_{max} = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{3}$.
- ☐ **Average forging pressure:** $p_{avg} = (\sigma_f h)/(2 \mu L) \cdot (e^{2\mu L/h} - 1)$.
- ☐ **Average flow stress (Hollomon):** $\bar{\sigma}_f = K \varepsilon_f^n / (n+1)$.
- ☐ **Volume conservation in rolling:** $h_0 L_0 = h_f L_f$.
- ☐ **Fibre flow:** Metal flow lines follow the die contour → *anisotropic* strength.
- ☐ **Residual stress relief:** Done by annealing at $0.5 - 0.6 \, T_m$.
- ☐ **Forming load (basic):** $F = \sigma_f \cdot A$ — always multiply by friction factor for accuracy.
- ☐ **Strain-rate sensitivity $m$:** Range 0 (cold) → 0.3 (hot) → >0.5 (superplastic).
- ☐ **Lankford coefficient $R$:** Ratio of width-strain to thickness-strain in sheet forming.
- ☐ **Productivity:** Forming processes give **> 90% material yield** (vs. 50–70% in machining).
- ☐ **Die wear:** Proportional to interface pressure and sliding distance; mitigated by lubrication.
- ☐ **Mechanical anisotropy:** Always quote strength values *with* reference to the fibre-flow direction.
- ☐ **End of Module 2 Anchor:** Master the 4-pillar classification (Mechanical / Metallurgical / Thermal / Tribological) — it is the most exam-friendly structure for 14-mark questions.

---

**End of Module 2 — Characteristic Features (PCPET602) Notes**
