topics.title: Capabilities and Applications - Blanking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capabilities and Applications - Blanking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Blanking** is a precision sheet-metal cutting (shearing) process in which a punch and die are used to shear out a closed-contour workpiece (the "blank") from a sheet-metal strip. The slug that drops through the die opening is the *desired product*, while the surrounding strip becomes the *scrap skeleton*. It is fundamentally classified as a *separating (cutting) operation* under cold-working / sheet-metal forming, governed by the mechanics of shear-induced fracture along the tool-workpiece interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Blanking, Piercing, and related shearing operations (Lancing, Notching, Perforating) constitute the backbone of Module 2 in PCPET602. Mastery of *clearance*, *blank-holder pressure*, and *cutting force estimation* is mandatory for board questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cookie Cutter" 🍪

Imagine using a **sharp cookie cutter** on a rolled-out slab of dough:
- The cutter presses down → shears through → the small shaped piece falls out as a cookie (this is the **blank**).
- The remaining flat sheet with a hole is the **scrap strip** (or "skeleton").
- The clearance between the cutter and the base is what gives a clean cut vs. a torn, burred one.

In industrial blanking, the **punch** = cookie cutter, the **die** = base, and the **sheet metal strip** = dough. The blank that drops through the die is the finished component.

## 1.3 Core Process Variables

- **Punch and Die Clearance (c)** – the per-side gap between punch and die walls. **Typically 3 % to 8 % of sheet thickness (t)** for low-carbon steel.
- **Sheet Thickness (t)** – usually 0.1 mm to 6 mm for blanking; up to 25 mm in heavy blanking.
- **Shear Strength (τ)** – the maximum shear stress the material can sustain before fracture.
- **Ductility / % Elongation** – dictates the roll-over and burnish zone widths.
- **Punch Diameter (D)** – governs the perimeter and cutting force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction of Material Flow:** In blanking, the *blank is the product*; in piercing, the *slug is the scrap*. This subtle distinction is a frequently-asked 2-mark question in KTU exams.

## 1.4 Classification of Blanking

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Conventional Blanking** | Punch diameter < sheet thickness × 5; simple round/rectangular shapes | Washers, gaskets, shims |
| **Precision Blanking (Fine Blanking)** | Very low clearance (0.5 %), triple-action press, V-ring clamping | Gears, linkage plates |
| **Shim Blanking** | Multiple short strokes stacking thin laminations | Transformer/Electric motor shims |
| **Progressive Die Blanking** | Multiple stations in one die, coil-fed | High-volume automotive stampings |
| **Transfer Die Blanking** | Blanks transferred between stations by mechanical fingers | Large panels |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of the Shear Zone

When the punch descends and contacts the sheet metal, the cutting action proceeds through **four distinct stages**:

1. **Elastic Deformation Stage** – Punch contacts sheet; material elastically compresses below the punch edge.
2. **Plastic Deformation (Burnish) Stage** – Material flows plastically; the punch begins to push a slug into the die; a shiny, burnished region forms on the cut edge.
3. **Fracture (Shear) Stage** – Once the induced shear stress exceeds the material's shear strength, a crack initiates at the punch and die edges and propagates toward the centre.
4. **Material Separation Stage** – Cracks meet; the blank separates and drops through the die.

> The total *depth of punch travel* required to achieve separation is called the **Punch Penetration Depth (h_p)**, typically equal to the sheet thickness t (for normal blanking).

## 2.2 Clearance — The Heart of Shear-Quality Trade-Off

Clearance is the **single most critical parameter** in blanking tool design. It controls:

- **Burr height and direction** (favourable burr = on the scrap side, not the blank)
- **Cut edge quality** (smooth burnish + controlled fracture)
- **Cutting force magnitude**
- **Tool life** (excess clearance → rapid punch/die wear)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Rule of Thumb:  
> - *Normal blanking:* c = 3 % – 8 % of t (per side)  
> - *Fine blanking:* c = 0.5 % of t  
> - *Soft / ductile materials (Al, Cu):* upper end (8 %–10 %)  
> - *Hard / brittle materials:* lower end (2 %–3 %)

The relationship between total clearance (C_total) and the cut-edge appearance is given by:

$$
C_{total} = 2c \quad \text{(per-side clearance summed both sides)}
$$

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Force Term

The total **blanking force (F)** is the summation of three terms:

1. **Pure Shear Force (F_s)** – required to overcome the material's shear strength across the cut area. This is the dominant contributor (~85 %–95 % of total force).
2. **Bending/Stripping Force (F_b)** – small component arising from elastic spring-back of the sheet.
3. **Friction & Edge-Drawing Force (F_f)** – accounts for material being slightly pulled into the die throat.

The dominant term F_s is calculated using:

$$
F_s = L \cdot t \cdot \tau
$$

Where:
- L = perimeter of the blank (mm)
- t = sheet thickness (mm)
- τ = shear strength of the material (N/mm² or MPa)

A **press capacity safety margin** of 30 %–40 % is generally added to F_s to obtain the design press tonnage.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Shear Cutting Force | $F_s = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | N (Newton) | Primary equation |
| 2 | Perimeter of Round Blank | $L = \pi D$ | mm | D = blank diameter |
| 3 | Perimeter of Rectangular Blank | $L = 2(a+b)$ | mm | a, b = side lengths |
| 4 | Press Tonnage (with margin) | $F_{press} = 1.3 \text{ to } 1.4 \cdot F_s$ | N | Add 30–40 % safety |
| 5 | Optimal Clearance (per side) | $c = (0.03 \text{ to } 0.08) \cdot t$ | mm | Material-dependent |
| 6 | Total Die Clearance | $C_{total} = 2c$ | mm | |
| 7 | Work Done (energy) | $W = 0.5 \cdot F_s \cdot t$ | N·mm (J) | Approximate |
| 8 | Shear Strength (mild steel) | $\tau \approx 0.7 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$ | MPa | Empirical |
| 9 | Stripping Force | $F_{strip} = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.1 \cdot F_s$ | N | Empirical |
| 10 | Punch Penetration Depth | $h_p \approx t$ | mm | For conventional blanking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Pitfall:** When the problem gives **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** instead of shear strength, always convert using $\tau \approx 0.7 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$ for low-carbon steels. For aluminium alloys, the ratio drops to about **0.65**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Blanking is the **starting point of nearly every stamped metal component**:

- **Automotive:** Body panels, brackets, gear blanks, clutch plates.
- **Electrical:** Laminations for motors and transformers (using progressive dies with electrical steel).
- **Consumer Goods:** Coin blanks (in mints!), watch components, washer and shim stacks.
- **Aerospace:** Aluminium and titanium blanks for subsequent forging/machining.
- **Appliance Industry:** White-goods panels, fan blades, mounting plates.

A typical modern high-speed blanking press operates at **600–1200 strokes/minute**, producing millions of blanks per day from a coil-fed strip — a testament to the industrial importance of this seemingly simple shearing process.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Shear Cutting Force

The shear force on a plane inclined at angle $\phi$ from the punch face during blanking can be expressed by considering the shear failure criterion. For a simple punch descending into sheet metal of thickness t:

### Step 1: Define the shear area
The shear plane is cylindrical, generated around the punch perimeter. Its area is:

$$
A_{shear} = L \cdot t
$$

where $L$ is the perimeter of the cut and $t$ is the sheet thickness.

### Step 2: Apply the shear stress criterion
At fracture, the shear stress $\tau$ acting on the area $A_{shear}$ equals the material's shear strength $\tau_{max}$:

$$
\tau = \frac{F_s}{A_{shear}}
$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to obtain the force
Solving for $F_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= \tau \cdot A_{shear} \\
    &= \tau \cdot (L \cdot t) \\
    &= L \cdot t \cdot \tau
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental blanking force equation** used across all KTU problems.

> **Logic:** The cut is treated as a pure shear failure along the cylindrical surface defined by the punch perimeter extruded through the sheet thickness.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A circular washer of diameter 32 mm is to be blanked from a 3.2 mm thick cold-rolled steel strip. The shear strength of the material is 320 MPa. Determine:
1. The blanking force.
2. The recommended press tonnage.
3. The optimum clearance per side.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 32$ mm
- $t = 3.2$ mm
- $\tau = 320$ MPa $= 320$ N/mm²

### Step 1: Perimeter of the blank

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \pi \cdot D \\
  &= \pi \times 32 \\
  &= 100.531 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Shear cutting force

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= L \cdot t \cdot \tau \\
    &= 100.531 \times 3.2 \times 320 \\
    &= 102{,}943.7 \text{ N} \\
    &\approx 102.94 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Press tonnage (with 35 % safety margin)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{press} &= 1.35 \times F_s \\
          &= 1.35 \times 102.94 \\
          &= 138.97 \text{ kN} \\
          &\approx 14.17 \text{ tonnes-force}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** 1 tonne-force $\approx 9.81$ kN. Hence $138.97 / 9.81 \approx 14.17$ tonnes.

### Step 4: Optimum clearance per side (using 6 % rule for mild steel)

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= 0.06 \times t \\
  &= 0.06 \times 3.2 \\
  &= 0.192 \text{ mm per side}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer Box

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Blanking Force $F_s$ | **102.94 kN** |
| Press Tonnage $F_{press}$ | **≈ 139 kN (≈ 14.2 tonnes)** |
| Optimum Clearance (per side) | **0.192 mm** |
| Die Opening Diameter | $D + 2c = 32 + 0.384 = 32.384$ mm |
| Punch Diameter | $D = 32$ mm |

## 3.3 Derivation of Clearance for a Specified Cut Quality

If a problem specifies that the **fracture zone** should be a certain percentage of the cut edge, we use the geometric relationship:

$$
c = t \cdot \tan\theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the angle of fracture propagation from the punch edge. For a fracture-zone of about **½ of sheet thickness** (a common KTU assumption), $\theta \approx 45°$, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= t \cdot \tan(45°) \\
  &= t \times 1 \\
  &= 1.0 \, t \quad \text{(per side)}
\end{aligned}
$$

But for the *actual production-clearance rule* (3–8 %), the small-angle approximation leads to:

$$
c = (0.03 \text{ to } 0.08) \cdot t
$$

> **Engineering Reasoning:** Larger clearance → cracks propagate further before meeting → bigger fracture zone, more rollover, higher burr. Smaller clearance → cracks meet sooner → cleaner cut but faster tool wear.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for KTU Problem Solving

| Given Quantity in Problem | Step to Apply | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---------------------------|---------------|-------------------------|
| UTS only | Convert: $\tau = 0.7 \sigma_{UTS}$ | Using UTS directly in force formula |
| Shear strength directly given | Use as $\tau$ directly | None |
| Material is aluminium | Use $\tau = 0.65 \sigma_{UTS}$ | Using steel ratio |
| Required press tonnage only | Add 30–40 % to $F_s$ | Forgetting safety margin |
| Rectangular blank | Use $L = 2(a+b)$ | Using $\pi D$ formula |
| Sheet thickness > 6 mm | Add blank-holder force | Ignoring spring-back |
| Hollow shape (e.g., washer) | Use outer perimeter for blanking | Adding inner perimeter (that's piercing) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Blanking Tool

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a complete blanking die assembly, mapping all interacting subsystems and their roles during the shearing action.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Coil Strip Inlet] --> B[Strip Feeder Unit]
    B --> C[Strip Guide Rails]
    C --> D[Locating Pin Stop]
    D --> E[Pressure Pad / Blank Holder]
    E --> F[Strip Clamping Zone]
    F --> G[Punch Descends - Shear Begins]
    G --> H{Punch Tip Reaches Cut Depth}
    H --> I[Punch Edge Shears Through Sheet]
    I --> J[Crack Initiation - Die Edge]
    I --> K[Crack Initiation - Punch Edge]
    J --> L[Cracks Propagate Toward Each Other]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Material Separation]
    M --> N[Blank Drops Through Die Opening]
    N --> O[Scrap Skeleton Retracts]
    M --> P[Slug Ejection Channel]
    O --> Q[Press Ram Retracts - Cycle Reset]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Strip Advances by Pitch Length]
    R --> D
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the arrows from coil feed → strip advancement → punch stroke → shear mechanics → blank/slug separation → retraction. This is the **complete operational cycle** of a single blanking stroke.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Blanking Tool Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1[Punch Holder] --> T2[Punch]
    T3[Die Set Upper Shoe] --> T1
    T4[Die Block] --> T5[Die Opening]
    T6[Die Set Lower Shoe] --> T4
    T7[Stripper Plate] --> T2
    T8[Guide Pins and Bushings] --> T1
    T8 --> T6
    T9[Backing Plate] --> T4
    T10[Die Springs] --> T7
    P1[Sheet Metal Strip] --> T2
    P1 --> T4
    T2 --> C1[Shear Zone - Clearance c per side]
    T4 --> C1
    C1 --> B1[Blank Product]
    C1 --> B2[Slug or Scrap]
```

## 4.3 Shear-Zone Mechanics — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Stage 1: Elastic Contact] --> S2[Stage 2: Plastic Burnish Zone]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Fracture Initiation]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Crack Propagation]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Material Separation]
    S5 --> S6[Blank Released into Die Cavity]
```

> **Stage-Stage Visualisation:**  
> - *Stage 1:* Punch just touches strip — small elastic deformation.  
> - *Stage 2:* Material flows — burnish band forms.  
> - *Stage 3:* Cracks nucleate at the punch and die edges.  
> - *Stage 4:* Cracks travel toward each other at angle governed by clearance.  
> - *Stage 5:* Cracks meet → clean separation. The blank falls; the skeleton indexes forward.

## 4.4 Clearance Effect on Cut-Edge Profile

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Clearance Setting] --> B[Low Clearance c approx 0.5 percent t]
    A --> C[Normal Clearance c approx 3 to 8 percent t]
    A --> D[Excess Clearance c greater than 10 percent t]
    B --> E1[Clean Cut, High Punch Stress, Faster Wear]
    C --> E2[Balanced Burnish plus Fracture, Standard Production]
    D --> E3[Large Burr, Tapered Cut, Poor Dimensional Accuracy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Blanking and Piercing operations. Which one produces the slug as the desired product?**

**Model Answer:**

Blanking and piercing are both shearing operations using a punch and die, but they differ in the role of the punched part:

| Aspect | Blanking | Piercing |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Desired Output | The blank (punched-out piece) | The remaining strip with hole |
| Scrap | The skeleton/leftover strip | The slug (punched-out piece) |
| Application | Coin blanks, washers | Holes in chassis, slots |
| Tool Design | Die opening ≈ blank size + 2c | Punch ≈ hole size |
| Direction of Burr | On the slug (unwanted) | On the strip (often acceptable) |

**In blanking, the *blank* is the desired product, not the slug.** The slug (or the leftover strip) is the scrap. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "clearance" in a blanking die? State its effect on the quality of the blanked edge.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Clearance is the per-side gap between the punch and the die wall, expressed as a percentage of sheet thickness.

$$
c = (0.03 \text{ to } 0.08) \times t
$$

**Effects on cut-edge quality:**

1. **Too small clearance (< 2 %):** Secondary shear occurs, produces double burnish, increases punch load, accelerates tool wear.
2. **Optimum clearance (3 %–8 %):** Produces a clean cut with controlled burnish and fracture zones, minimal burr.
3. **Excess clearance (> 10 %):** Cracks diverge, producing large rollover, deep burrs, tapered edge, poor dimensional accuracy.

A well-chosen clearance ensures *good edge quality, longer tool life, and lower press tonnage*. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each.

### Question A — Choice Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A rectangular washer of dimensions 60 mm × 40 mm with a central hole of 25 mm diameter is to be **pierced** from a 4 mm thick mild-steel strip. The shear strength of the material is 350 MPa.

**(a)** Calculate the **piercing force** and the **press tonnage required**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Suggest the **optimum clearance** per side. If the press available is 100 tonnes, is the operation feasible? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Outer dimensions: 60 mm × 40 mm (this defines the blank size; for *piercing*, we only need the hole perimeter)
- Hole diameter: $D_h = 25$ mm
- Sheet thickness: $t = 4$ mm
- Shear strength: $\tau = 350$ N/mm²

### Part (a) — Piercing Force

#### Step 1: Perimeter of the hole (perimeter being sheared)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \pi \cdot D_h \\
  &= \pi \times 25 \\
  &= 78.54 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Piercing (shear) force

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= L \cdot t \cdot \tau \\
    &= 78.54 \times 4 \times 350 \\
    &= 109{,}956 \text{ N} \\
    &\approx 109.96 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Press tonnage (with 35 % safety)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{press} &= 1.35 \times F_s \\
          &= 1.35 \times 109.96 \\
          &= 148.45 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to tonnes:

$$
\frac{148.45}{9.81} \approx 15.13 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Answer (a):** Piercing force ≈ **110 kN**, Press tonnage required ≈ **15.1 tonnes**.

---

### Part (b) — Optimum Clearance & Feasibility Check

#### Step 1: Optimum clearance (per side, using 6 % rule for mild steel)

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= 0.06 \times t \\
  &= 0.06 \times 4 \\
  &= 0.24 \text{ mm per side}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (b.1):** Optimum clearance = **0.24 mm per side**, total die clearance = **0.48 mm**.

#### Step 2: Feasibility check

- Available press capacity: 100 tonnes = 100 × 9.81 = **981 kN**
- Required press tonnage: 15.13 tonnes
- Safety check: $100 \gg 15.13$ — *operation is highly feasible* with substantial reserve capacity.

However, the student should note that for **piercing the hole** alone we computed 15.13 tonnes. If the question demanded *blanking the outer rectangle AND piercing the hole in one stroke*, we would have to sum the two shearing perimeters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_{outer} + L_{hole} \\
          &= 2(60+40) + \pi(25) \\
          &= 200 + 78.54 \\
          &= 278.54 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_{combined} = 278.54 \times 4 \times 350 = 389{,}956 \text{ N} \approx 39.75 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Even with combined operation, **100 tonnes is sufficient**.

> **Final Verdict (b.2):** Operation is **feasible** on the 100-tonne press. The optimum per-side clearance is **0.24 mm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. *Forgetting to convert N to tonnes* ($1 \text{ tonne} = 9810$ N). Many students leave the answer in kN only.
> 2. *Including the outer rectangle perimeter* in the *piercing* calculation. The hole is what is being punched out; the outer contour is the blank boundary.
> 3. *Omitting the safety margin* (30–40 %) when specifying press tonnage.
> 4. *Using UTS instead of shear strength* when only $\sigma_{UTS}$ is given — must convert using $\tau = 0.7 \sigma_{UTS}$.

---

### Question B — Choice Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Explain the **capabilities and applications of blanking**. A flat washer of inner diameter 18 mm and outer diameter 36 mm is to be blanked from a 2.5 mm thick aluminium strip. The UTS of the aluminium is 180 MPa. Calculate the **blanking force** and the **optimum clearance per side**. **(14 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Capabilities and Applications of Blanking (7 marks)

**Capabilities:**

1. **Production of flat precision components** with tolerances of ±0.05 mm achievable in fine blanking.
2. **High-speed mass production** — up to 1200 strokes/minute on modern presses.
3. **Wide range of materials** — ferrous, non-ferrous, and non-metallic (paper, fibre, plastic laminates).
4. **Sheet thickness range** — from 0.1 mm (foils) up to 25 mm (heavy plate).
5. **Complex outlines** — through computer-controlled wire-EDM die manufacturing, intricate shapes including internal slots, gear forms, and laminations are blankable.
6. **Compatibility with progressive die automation** — multiple operations in one die.

**Applications:**

1. **Automobile industry:** Gear blanks, clutch plates, brake shims, body brackets.
2. **Electrical industry:** Transformer & motor laminations (silicon steel).
3. **Domestic appliances:** Washing-machine panels, fan blades, switchgear parts.
4. **Coinage:** Mint blanks for currency coins.
5. **Aerospace:** Aluminium and titanium blanks for downstream forging.
6. **Hardware:** Washers, gaskets, fastener blanks, lock parts.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Inner diameter: $d = 18$ mm
- Outer diameter: $D = 36$ mm
- Thickness: $t = 2.5$ mm
- UTS: $\sigma_{UTS} = 180$ MPa
- Material: Aluminium

#### Step 1: Convert UTS to shear strength (using $\tau = 0.65 \sigma_{UTS}$ for aluminium)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= 0.65 \times 180 \\
    &= 117 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Perimeter of the blank (outer circumference)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \pi \cdot D \\
  &= \pi \times 36 \\
  &= 113.097 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Only the outer perimeter is used for *blanking*; the inner hole (if any) would be a *piercing* operation in a separate station (compound die) or pre-formed.

#### Step 3: Blanking force

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= L \cdot t \cdot \tau \\
    &= 113.097 \times 2.5 \times 117 \\
    &= 33{,}081.4 \text{ N} \\
    &\approx 33.08 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Press tonnage (with 35 % safety)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{press} &= 1.35 \times 33.08 \\
          &= 44.66 \text{ kN} \\
          &\approx 4.55 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Optimum clearance (using 8 % rule for ductile aluminium)

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= 0.08 \times t \\
  &= 0.08 \times 2.5 \\
  &= 0.20 \text{ mm per side}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Box (b):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Shear strength $\tau$ | 117 N/mm² |
| Blanking force $F_s$ | **33.08 kN** |
| Press tonnage required | **≈ 4.55 tonnes** |
| Optimum clearance (per side) | **0.20 mm** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes for this Question:**
> 1. *Using 0.7 as the conversion factor* (correct for steel, but **aluminium requires 0.65**). This single error can lose 2 marks.
> 2. *Adding the inner diameter perimeter* to the outer perimeter — this is a **compound die** operation, not blanking. The inner hole is a separate piercing step.
> 3. *Forgetting units* — shear strength should be expressed in N/mm² (= MPa) for the formula to yield Newtons.
> 4. *Wrong clearance percentage* — applying 3–6 % (steel values) instead of 8–10 % for soft, ductile aluminium.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ☐ **Blanking** = shearing where the *blank* is the product; **Piercing** = the *slug* is the scrap.
- ☐ **Fundamental Force Equation:** $F_s = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ — Memorise verbatim.
- ☐ **Conversion Rules:** $\tau = 0.7 \sigma_{UTS}$ (steel), $\tau = 0.65 \sigma_{UTS}$ (aluminium).
- ☐ **Clearance Rule:** $c = 3$–$8\% \cdot t$ (steel); $c = 8$–$10\% \cdot t$ (aluminium, copper); $c = 2$–$3\% \cdot t$ (hard/brittle).
- ☐ **Press Tonnage Safety Margin:** Always add **30 %–40 %** to $F_s$.
- ☐ **Perimeter Formulas:** Round → $L = \pi D$; Rectangle → $L = 2(a+b)$.
- ☐ **Punch Penetration Depth** ≈ Sheet thickness (for conventional blanking).
- ☐ **Stripping Force** ≈ $0.05$–$0.1 \times F_s$ (used to size stripper springs).
- ☐ **Total Die Clearance** = $2 \times$ per-side clearance.
- ☐ **Four Stages of Shear:** Elastic → Plastic (Burnish) → Fracture → Separation.
- ☐ **Edge Quality Composition:** Roll-over + Burnish + Fracture + Burr (proportions depend on clearance).
- ☐ **Unit Conversions:** $1$ tonne-force $= 9.81$ kN $= 9810$ N.
- ☐ **Compound Die:** Combines blanking + piercing in a single stroke.
- ☐ **Progressive Die:** Multiple stations in a single die, coil-fed.
- ☐ **Fine Blanking:** $c \approx 0.5\% \cdot t$, triple-action press, near-net shape.

> **Examiner's Final Tip:** Always state *units* explicitly in every step. KTU valuators award partial marks for correct *method* even if the final numerical answer is slightly off — but units must be consistent throughout. Practice at least 3 numerical problems of varying shapes (round, rectangular, and washer-type) before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Punching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Punching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Punching** is a sheet-metal shearing (cutting) operation in which a hardened tool, called a **punch**, is forced through a sheet-metal blank (strip or coil) into a corresponding opening in a **die**, thereby shearing out a slug of metal to produce a hole of the desired shape. The slug (or "blank") that falls through the die opening is generally scrap, while the parent sheet, now containing the punched hole, is the workpiece that moves on to subsequent forming stations in a progressive die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *Punching is a cold-working shearing process wherein a punch and die assembly, with a controlled radial clearance, sever a slug from the sheet by inducing a localized shear failure along the punch-die cutting edges, producing a hole whose geometry replicates the punch profile.*

The operation belongs to the broader family of **shearing processes** that include blanking, piercing, notching, trimming, and shaving. Strictly speaking:
- **Punching / Piercing** → the slug is scrap; the parent sheet is the useful part.
- **Blanking** → the slug is the useful part; the parent sheet is scrap.

In a progressive die, the *first station is generally a pilot-hole piercing station* whose output (the pilot) is used for accurate strip location in all subsequent stations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine using a **hole-punch plier** (the kind used by schoolchildren or leather workers to punch a clean hole in a leather belt or a stack of paper). When you squeeze the handles:
1. A cylindrical steel rod (the *punch*) descends.
2. It presses the sheet into a matching cylindrical cavity in a hardened bottom block (the *die*).
3. A small disk of material pops out as a clean disk-shaped chip.

Now scale this idea up to an industrial press where the punch may be several millimetres thick, the sheet may be **1.5–6 mm** thick mild steel, and the punch may have any desired profile — round, square, oblong, D-shaped, or even a complex contour. Instead of squeezing by hand, a **mechanical, hydraulic, or servo press** delivers several hundred kiloNewtons of force, and the cycle time is typically **0.5 s to 2 s per stroke**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Things That Make Punching Tick:**
> 1. **Force concentration** – the punch edge concentrates force onto a thin ring of metal.
> 2. **Clearance** – a small, deliberate gap between punch and die (1–10 % of stock thickness per side).
> 3. **Controlled fracture** – the metal doesn't just "melt apart"; it is sheared past its elastic limit, undergoes plastic flow, then fractures along a clean plane.

## 1.3 Industrial Significance and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range (Industrial) |
|---|---|
| Sheet thickness (t) | **0.1 mm – 12 mm** |
| Punching force per unit perimeter | **0.5 × t × τ_u** where τ_u is ultimate shear strength |
| Clearance per side (% of t) | **3 % – 10 %** |
| Puncture (penetration depth before fracture) | **1/3 t to 1/2 t** |
| Aspect ratio (t / narrowest hole dimension) | ≤ 1 (recommended), max ≈ 1.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **KTU 2024 scheme (PCPET602 Module 2)**, the punching topic must be studied alongside its *twin operation* **blanking**, since the force formula, clearance theory, and tool-design principles are common. Examiners routinely frame problems that interchange blanking ↔ punching.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Punching — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

When a punch descends on the sheet, the metal does not separate instantly. A precise sequence of events occurs:

1. **Elastic Contact Stage:** The punch face touches the sheet. A localized compressive stress (equal to the punch edge contact pressure) builds up. The sheet elastically deforms beneath the punch edge but does not yield.
2. **Plastic Indentation / Burnishing Stage:** Once the local stress exceeds the **compressive yield strength (σ_y)** of the sheet, plastic flow begins. The punch begins to penetrate the metal, leaving a smooth, burnished band on both the punched hole-wall and the slug-wall.
3. **Penetration / Puncture Stage:** The punch continues down. The metal in the shear zone undergoes intense shear strain. The clearance gap plays its critical role here: a small clearance forces the metal into a tight, near-vertical shear zone; a large clearance lets the metal bend, producing a large rollover and burr.
4. **Fracture / Separation Stage:** When plastic strain reaches the fracture limit, a **crack initiates** at the punch edge AND simultaneously at the die edge. The two cracks travel toward each other through the remaining uncut metal. They meet (or the remaining ligament shears off), and the slug separates from the parent sheet.
5. **Stripping / Springback Stage:** The punch ascends. The slug (or workpiece) may be lifted slightly with the punch by elastic springback. A **stripper plate** is used to push the workpiece off the punch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Clearance Matters:** If clearance is **too small**, the fracture cracks meet prematurely and a *secondary shear* (double-shear) zone forms, drawing a tall burr and an oversized slug. If clearance is **too large**, the cracks travel a long distance and the slug edge is severely tapered, with a big rollover and an oversized, ragged hole.

## 2.2 The Anatomy of a Punched Edge (Cross-Section)

The vertical wall of a punched hole is never a clean, sharp 90° cylinder. It consists of four distinct zones (this is a **high-yield KTU question** — examiners love asking you to *label the zones*):

| Zone | Cause | Approx. % of thickness |
|---|---|---|
| **Rollover (R)** | Plastic bending & burnishing under the punch radius | **5 – 25 %** |
| **Burnish (B)** | Clean shearing during punch penetration | **20 – 50 %** |
| **Fracture (F)** | Rough, torn zone where cracks meet | **30 – 60 %** |
| **Burr (D)** | Tiny protrusion at the die exit due to clearance / ductility | **< 5 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *good* punching job minimises **Rollover** and **Burr**, while producing a **vertical, clean burnish band** that occupies ~50 % of the edge. KTU exams frequently ask you to *sketch and label* these zones for both the punched hole and the slug.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the entries in the table below. Every KTU punching/blanking numerical in the last 5 years draws from this exact set.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Punching Force (basic) | $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u$ | N | $L$ = perimeter of cut (mm), $t$ = stock thickness (mm), $\tau_u$ = ultimate shear strength (MPa) |
| 2 | Shear strength in terms of UTS | $\tau_u \approx 0.75 \cdot \sigma_u$ (for steels) | MPa | Empirical; use when $\tau_u$ not given |
| 3 | Force with shear-strength factor | $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u \cdot k_s$ | N | $k_s$ = 1.0 to 1.3 (allowance for edge wear, uneven strip) |
| 4 | Clear-cutting (perimeter) factor | $P_s = \pi \cdot d$ (round); $P_s = 2(L+B)$ (rectangle) | mm | Use actual cut length, not nominal |
| 5 | Optimum clearance per side | $c = c_r \cdot t$ | mm | $c_r$ = clearance ratio; **typical 3 – 8 %** |
| 6 | Diameter of die opening | $D = d + 2c$ | mm | $d$ = punch diameter |
| 7 | Slug (blank) diameter (punching) | $D_{slug} = d + 2c$ | mm | Same as die opening |
| 8 | Punching force via UTS (alt.) | $F = 0.75 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u$ | N | Substitute $\tau_u = 0.75\,\sigma_u$ |
| 9 | Penetration depth to fracture | $h_f \approx 0.4\,t$ | mm | For ductile metals |
| 10 | Stripping force | $F_{strip} \approx 0.05\,F$ to $0.08\,F$ | N | Empirical; depends on $c$ and lubrication |
| 11 | Centre of pressure (complex contour) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i \, L_i}{\sum L_i}$, $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum y_i \, L_i}{\sum L_i}$ | mm | Used for non-symmetric punches to find the balancing flange / shank position |
| 12 | Press tonnage required | $T = F + F_{strip}$ (add ~25 % safety) | N or kN | Always round up to nearest standard press capacity |
| 13 | Energy to punch | $U = F \cdot \tau_u \cdot t / (2 \cdot \sigma_u)$ — approx. $U \approx 0.5\,F\,t$ | J | Work done in shearing |
| 14 | Aspect-ratio limit | $t/d \leq 1.0$ (ideal); $\leq 1.5$ (max) | — | Beyond this, punch buckling/ejection fails |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Punching is the workhorse of mass-production sheet-metal industries:
- **Automotive body panels** – thousands of holes per car (mounting, hemming, welding) are pierced in progressive dies.
- **Electrical laminations** for motors and transformers – tight-tolerance slugs (very thin silicon steel).
- **PCB / FPC punching** – precision blanking of copper-clad laminates.
- **White goods, appliance chassis** – holes for fasteners, hinges, ventilation.
- **Coin blank production** – technically a blanking operation, but the punch-die mechanics are identical.

The engineer's task is to balance **edge quality**, **tool life**, **press tonnage**, and **cycle time**. Mastery of clearance, force, and tool geometry is what separates a competent tool designer from a novice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Punching Force Equation

We model the punching operation as a *pure shear* over the cut perimeter. The punch edge contacts the sheet along the line of cut, and the punch must generate enough shear stress across the uncut thickness to fracture the metal.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Shear Stress on the Cut Plane

Consider a punch of perimeter $L$ pushing down on a sheet of thickness $t$. The shear plane is a cylindrical surface (for a round punch) or prismatic surface (for a non-round punch) wrapping the cut edge, with:
- **Area of shear plane:** $A = L \cdot t$
- **Shear stress acting:** $\tau$ (uniformly distributed as a first approximation)

By equilibrium, the downward force exerted by the press equals the resisting shear force:

$$F = \tau \cdot A$$

### 3.1.2 Substituting the Geometry

$$F = \tau \cdot (L \cdot t)$$

### 3.1.3 Choosing the Shear Stress

For a *minimum-force* design we would use the yield shear strength $\tau_y = 0.577\,\sigma_y$. However, the metal does **not** yield cleanly — it must be **fractured** to separate. The fracture occurs at the **ultimate shear strength** $\tau_u$, which is empirically tied to the ultimate tensile strength by:

$$\tau_u \approx 0.75 \cdot \sigma_u \quad \text{(for steels; 0.65 – 0.80 range)}$$

Therefore, the **punching force** required to fully sever the slug is:

$$\boxed{\,F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u \,}$$

In terms of the more commonly tabulated UTS:

$$\boxed{\,F = 0.75 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u \,}$$

### 3.1.4 Including a Safety / Edge-Wear Factor

Real dies have dulling edges after thousands of strokes, the strip thickness varies, and lubrication is imperfect. The KTU board-recommended practice is:

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u \cdot k_s$$

where $k_s = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.3$ is the **shear strength factor**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Round Hole (Full Solution)

**Problem:** A 12 mm diameter hole is to be punched through a 4 mm thick mild-steel plate. The ultimate tensile strength of the plate is $\sigma_u = 410$ MPa. Take clearance ratio $c_r = 6\%$ per side and shear-strength factor $k_s = 1.2$. Determine:
(a) the punching force,
(b) the die opening diameter,
(c) the slug diameter,
(d) the stripping force (assume 7 % of punching force),
(e) the recommended press tonnage.

### Step 1 — Cut Perimeter

For a round punch, $L = \pi d$:

$$L = \pi \cdot 12 = 37.699 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2 — Shear Strength from UTS

Using $\tau_u = 0.75 \cdot \sigma_u$:

$$\tau_u = 0.75 \cdot 410 = 307.5 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Punching Force (basic)

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u$$

$$F = 37.699 \cdot 4 \cdot 307.5$$

$$\boxed{F = 46{,}369.6 \text{ N} \approx 46.37 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Edge-Wear Factor

$$F_{design} = F \cdot k_s = 46{,}369.6 \cdot 1.2$$

$$\boxed{F_{design} = 55{,}643.5 \text{ N} \approx 55.64 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 5 — Die Opening Diameter

Clearance per side: $c = c_r \cdot t = 0.06 \cdot 4 = 0.24$ mm.

$$D = d + 2c = 12 + 2(0.24)$$

$$\boxed{D = 12.48 \text{ mm}}$$

### Step 6 — Slug Diameter

The slug that drops out of the die has the diameter of the die opening:

$$\boxed{D_{slug} = 12.48 \text{ mm}}$$

### Step 7 — Stripping Force

$$F_{strip} = 0.07 \cdot F_{design} = 0.07 \cdot 55{,}643.5$$

$$\boxed{F_{strip} \approx 3{,}895 \text{ N} \approx 3.90 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 8 — Recommended Press Tonnage

Total working load = $F_{design} + F_{strip}$:

$$T = 55{,}643.5 + 3{,}895 = 59{,}538.5 \text{ N}$$

Apply a 25 % safety factor for press selection:

$$T_{press} = 1.25 \cdot 59{,}538.5$$

$$\boxed{T_{press} \approx 74{,}423 \text{ N} \approx 75 \text{ kN}}$$

Select a standard press of **80 kN (8 tonne)** capacity.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Rectangular Slot with Centre of Pressure

**Problem:** A rectangular slot of dimensions $40 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm}$ is to be punched in a 3 mm thick aluminium sheet. The ultimate shear strength of aluminium is $\tau_u = 120$ MPa. Find (a) the punching force and (b) the position of the **centre of pressure** (needed to design the punch shank's balancing flange).

### Step 1 — Cut Perimeter

For a rectangle: $L = 2(L_1 + L_2)$:

$$L = 2(40 + 25) = 130 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2 — Punching Force

$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u = 130 \cdot 3 \cdot 120$$

$$\boxed{F = 46{,}800 \text{ N} = 46.8 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 3 — Centre of Pressure

For a rectangle, the centre of pressure coincides with the geometric centroid:

$$\bar{x} = L_1 / 2 = 40/2 = 20 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = L_2 / 2 = 25/2 = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = (20, 12.5) \text{ mm from a chosen corner}}$$

### Step 4 — Illustrative Centre-of-Pressure for an L-Shaped Hole

For an L-shape with three segments $(L_1 = 30, L_2 = 40, L_3 = 50)$ mm located at midpoints $(x_1, y_1) = (15, 5)$, $(x_2, y_2) = (40, 15)$, $(x_3, y_3) = (25, 35)$:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{30 \cdot 15 + 40 \cdot 40 + 50 \cdot 25}{30 + 40 + 50}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{450 + 1600 + 1250}{120} = \frac{3300}{120} = 27.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{30 \cdot 5 + 40 \cdot 15 + 50 \cdot 35}{120}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{150 + 600 + 1750}{120} = \frac{2500}{120} \approx 20.83 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = (27.5, 20.83) \text{ mm}}$$

The punch shank's retention flange is centred at this point so that side-thrusts during cutting are balanced and the punch does not tilt.

## 3.4 Derivation of Optimum Clearance

The classical **Sieber–Koehler / Boston clearance recommendation** is derived from fracture-mechanics and empirical observation:

$$c_{opt} = c_r \cdot t$$

| Material | Recommended $c_r$ (per side, as % of $t$) |
|---|---|
| Aluminium, copper | **3 – 5 %** |
| Mild steel | **5 – 7 %** |
| Stainless steel | **7 – 10 %** |
| High-strength steel | **8 – 12 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> The physical reasoning: the clearance must be large enough to let the two fracture cracks (one from punch edge, one from die edge) traverse the remaining thickness without prematurely colliding (which would draw a burr), but small enough to keep the cut edge vertical and clean.

## 3.5 Summary Equation Block (for Rapid Recall)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u \cdot k_s \\[4pt]
\tau_u &= 0.75 \cdot \sigma_u \quad \text{(for steels)} \\[4pt]
D &= d + 2c \quad ; \quad c = c_r \cdot t \\[4pt]
\bar{x} &= \frac{\sum_i x_i L_i}{\sum_i L_i} \quad , \quad \bar{y} = \frac{\sum_i y_i L_i}{\sum_i L_i} \\[4pt]
F_{strip} &\approx 0.05\,F \text{ to } 0.08\,F \\[4pt]
T_{press} &= 1.25\,(F + F_{strip})
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Process Flow of a Punching Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Metal Strip Fed into Die] --> B[Punch Descends via Press Ram]
    B --> C{Elastic-Plastic Contact}
    C --> D[Plastic Indentation Stage]
    D --> E[Penetration Through Shear Zone]
    E --> F{Frac. Strain Reached?}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Crack Initiation at Punch and Die Edges]
    G --> H[Crack Propagation Toward Each Other]
    H --> I[Slug Separates from Parent Sheet]
    I --> J[Press Ram Retracts]
    J --> K[Stripper Pushes Workpiece Off Punch]
    K --> L[Strip Indexed to Next Station]
    L --> M[Cycle Repeats]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Punch–Die–Stripper–Press Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PressAssembly[Press Tool Assembly]
        P1[Press Ram / Slide] --> P2[Punch Holder / Shank]
        P2 --> P3[Punch Cutting Edge]
        P3 --> P4[Workpiece - Sheet Metal]
        P4 --> P5[Die Opening - Clearance c per side]
        P5 --> P6[Die Block / Lower Shoe]
        P7[Stripper Plate] -.strips workpiece.-> P4
        P8[Stripper Spring or Urethane] --> P7
        P9[Backing Plate / Bolster] --> P6
    end
    P3 -- Shear Failure --> P5
    P4 -- Slug falls through --> P6
```

## 4.3 Functional State Diagram — Punch Cycle Phases

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Descending: Ram Downstroke
    Descending --> ElasticContact: Punch Touches Sheet
    ElasticContact --> PlasticIndent: Stress exceeds Sigma_y
    PlasticIndent --> Shearing: Punch penetrates
    Shearing --> FractureInitiation: Strain reaches limit
    FractureInitiation --> CrackPropagation: Cracks grow inward
    CrackPropagation --> Separation: Slug detaches
    Separation --> Ascending: Ram Upstroke
    Ascending --> Stripping: Stripper pushes workpiece
    Stripping --> Idle
    Idle --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Edge Zones of a Punched Hole Wall

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top of Hole - Exit Side] --> B[Zone 1 - Rollover R]
    B --> C[Zone 2 - Burnish Band B]
    C --> D[Zone 3 - Fracture Surface F]
    D --> E[Zone 4 - Burr at Die Exit]
    E --> F[Bottom of Hole - Die Entry Side]
    style B fill:#ffe5b4
    style C fill:#ffd1dc
    style D fill:#cce5ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagram:** From top to bottom of the cut edge, the metal first **rolls over** (R) under the punch radius, then exhibits a clean **shear burnish** (B), then a torn **fracture** (F) where the cracks meet, and finally a small **burr** at the bottom edge. KTU exam questions frequently ask students to *identify and quantify* these zones.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Q:** Define punching. How is it different from blanking?

**Model Answer:**
Punching (or piercing) is a cold-working shearing operation in which a punch and die are used to remove a slug of metal from a sheet, producing a hole whose shape matches the punch profile. The slug is generally scrap.

The difference from blanking lies in which piece is the useful output:
- In **punching**, the parent sheet (containing the hole) is the useful workpiece; the slug is scrap.
- In **blanking**, the slug is the useful workpiece (e.g., a flat washer blank); the parent sheet is scrap.

In both operations, the mechanics of cutting — punch force, clearance, fracture stages — are identical.

### Question A2 — Clearance & Edge Quality
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** What is meant by *clearance* in punching? State its typical range for mild steel.

**Model Answer:**
Clearance in punching is the intentional small gap provided between the punch and the die on each side (radial), expressed as a percentage of the sheet thickness. It allows the fracture cracks initiated at the punch edge and at the die edge to propagate inward and meet cleanly without premature collision.

For mild steel, the recommended clearance is **5 % to 7 % per side** of stock thickness. Too little clearance produces a tall burr and a secondary shear zone; too much clearance produces a large rollover and a tapered, ragged cut.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Question A:**
(a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the punching operation and the various zones formed on the cut edge of a punched hole. **(7 marks)**
(b) A square hole of $30 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$ is to be punched in a $5 \text{ mm}$ thick mild-steel plate having an ultimate tensile strength of $450$ MPa. Take clearance ratio as $6\%$ per side and shear-strength factor as $1.25$. Calculate:
  - (i) the punching force, **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) the die opening size, **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) the slug dimensions, **(1 mark)**
  - (iv) the required press tonnage (assume 7 % stripping force and 25 % safety). **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The punching operation involves forcing a hardened punch through the sheet into a matching die cavity. The stages are:
1. **Elastic contact** – punch face touches sheet.
2. **Plastic indentation / burnishing** – sheet yields locally, smooth band forms on the hole wall.
3. **Penetration** – punch enters the metal through the shear zone.
4. **Fracture** – cracks initiate at punch edge and die edge, propagate inward.
5. **Separation** – cracks meet, slug drops through die.
6. **Stripping** – on ram upstroke, a stripper plate ejects the workpiece off the punch.

The four zones of the cut edge (from top to bottom) are:
- **Rollover (R):** formed by plastic bending under the punch radius, ~5–25 % of t.
- **Burnish (B):** smooth sheared band, ~20–50 % of t.
- **Fracture (F):** rough torn zone where cracks meet, ~30–60 % of t.
- **Burr (D):** tiny protrusion at die-exit, < 5 % of t.

A sketch should clearly show: punch, die with clearance, sheet, the four labeled zones on the hole wall, and a matching set on the slug.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Perimeter of cut:**
$$L = 2(L + B) = 2(30 + 30) = 120 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Shear strength from UTS:**
$$\tau_u = 0.75 \cdot \sigma_u = 0.75 \cdot 450 = 337.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Punching force with safety factor:**
$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u \cdot k_s$$
$$F = 120 \cdot 5 \cdot 337.5 \cdot 1.25$$
$$\boxed{F = 253{,}125 \text{ N} \approx 253.1 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Die opening size:**
Clearance per side: $c = 0.06 \cdot 5 = 0.30$ mm.
$$D = 30 + 2(0.30) = 30.60 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(each side)}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Die opening: } 30.60 \text{ mm} \times 30.60 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Slug dimensions:**
The slug matches the die opening:
$$\boxed{30.60 \text{ mm} \times 30.60 \text{ mm} \times 5 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 6 — Stripping force:**
$$F_{strip} = 0.07 \cdot 253{,}125 = 17{,}719 \text{ N} \approx 17.72 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Total working load:**
$$T = F + F_{strip} = 253{,}125 + 17{,}719 = 270{,}844 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Press tonnage with 25 % safety:**
$$T_{press} = 1.25 \cdot 270{,}844$$
$$\boxed{T_{press} = 338{,}555 \text{ N} \approx 339 \text{ kN} \approx 35 \text{ tonne}}$$

Select a standard **40-tonne press**.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**Question B:**
(a) Explain the role of clearance in punching. How does excess or insufficient clearance affect the cut-edge quality? **(7 marks)**
(b) An L-shaped hole with three sides of lengths $L_1 = 40$ mm at $(x_1, y_1) = (20, 5)$ mm, $L_2 = 30$ mm at $(x_2, y_2) = (40, 15)$ mm, and $L_3 = 50$ mm at $(x_3, y_3) = (25, 35)$ mm is to be punched through a 4 mm thick aluminium sheet. The ultimate shear strength is $110$ MPa. Determine:
  - (i) the punch perimeter, **(1 mark)**
  - (ii) the punching force, **(1.5 marks)**
  - (iii) the centre of pressure of the punch face, **(2 marks)**
  - (iv) the stripping force and recommended press tonnage. **(2.5 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Role of clearance:** Clearance is the small radial gap between punch and die. Its function is to allow the fracture cracks starting from the punch edge and the die edge to meet inside the stock thickness at the optimal location.

**Insufficient clearance (too tight):**
- Fracture cracks collide prematurely near the middle of the thickness.
- A *secondary shear* zone forms, drawing a tall burr and an oversized slug.
- Edge is rough, with excessive die wear.
- Punching force increases.

**Excessive clearance (too loose):**
- Cracks travel a long oblique path; they meet far from the punch-die midline.
- Cut edge is severely tapered; large rollover and large burr.
- Dimensional accuracy is lost; the slug/hole becomes oversized.
- Punching force decreases but quality and tool life suffer.

The optimum clearance is **3–10 % per side** depending on material ductility and thickness.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Punch perimeter:**
$$L = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 40 + 30 + 50 = 120 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Punching force:**
$$F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u = 120 \cdot 4 \cdot 110$$
$$\boxed{F = 52{,}800 \text{ N} = 52.8 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Centre of pressure:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 L_1 + x_2 L_2 + x_3 L_3}{L_1 + L_2 + L_3} = \frac{20 \cdot 40 + 40 \cdot 30 + 25 \cdot 50}{120}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{800 + 1200 + 1250}{120} = \frac{3250}{120} \approx 27.08 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{y_1 L_1 + y_2 L_2 + y_3 L_3}{L_1 + L_2 + L_3} = \frac{5 \cdot 40 + 15 \cdot 30 + 35 \cdot 50}{120}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{200 + 450 + 1750}{120} = \frac{2400}{120} = 20.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = (27.08, 20.00) \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Stripping force (assume 6 %):**
$$F_{strip} = 0.06 \cdot 52{,}800 = 3{,}168 \text{ N} \approx 3.17 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Recommended press tonnage (with 25 % safety):**
$$T = 1.25 \cdot (F + F_{strip}) = 1.25 \cdot (52{,}800 + 3{,}168)$$
$$T = 1.25 \cdot 55{,}968$$
$$\boxed{T = 69{,}960 \text{ N} \approx 70 \text{ kN} \approx 7 \text{ tonne}}$$

Select a standard **10-tonne press**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in Punching problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — mixing mm² × MPa = N works only if you use N/mm² for MPa (i.e., 1 MPa = 1 N/mm²). State this explicitly in the first line.
> 2. **Using σ_y instead of σ_u (or τ_y instead of τ_u)** — the formula requires *ultimate* shear strength, because the metal must be **fractured**, not merely yielded. Writing $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_y$ is the single most common error.
> 3. **Ignoring the shear-strength factor $k_s$** — KTU board marking schemes deduct 1 mark if the factor is omitted, since real dies have dull edges and strip thickness varies.
> 4. **Confusing punching force with press tonnage** — press tonnage includes stripping force AND a 25 % safety margin. Always show the final step clearly.
> 5. **Wrong clearance sign** — clearance is **added** to the punch dimension on each side to get the die opening (for punching); it is **subtracted** for blanking where the blank is the slug.
> 6. **Centre of pressure mistake** — examiners expect you to weight each segment's *length* by its centroid coordinates, not its corner coordinates. Many students use midpoints of edges vs. ends, leading to wrong results.
> 7. **Omitting the sketch in part (a)** — A "neat sketch" is worth **2 to 3 marks** out of 7 in the KTU marking scheme. Always draw: punch, die, sheet, cut edge with four zones labeled.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Punching — Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2):**

- ☐ Punching = shearing operation where the **slug is scrap** and the **parent sheet is the workpiece**; converse is blanking.
- ☐ Cutting sequence: **elastic → plastic indent → penetration → fracture → separation → strip**.
- ☐ Edge zones: **Rollover, Burnish, Fracture, Burr** — in this order from top to bottom.
- ☐ **Master Formula:** $F = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_u \cdot k_s$.
- ☐ For steels: $\tau_u = 0.75\,\sigma_u$ (empirical, board-approved).
- ☐ **Clearance** = small radial gap per side, expressed as **% of t**; typical 3–10 %.
- ☐ Die opening = punch dimension + 2c (for punching).
- ☐ Slug diameter = die opening diameter.
- ☐ **Stripping force** = 5–8 % of punching force.
- ☐ **Press tonnage** = $1.25 \cdot (F + F_{strip})$, then round UP to standard press capacity.
- ☐ **Centre of pressure** for irregular punches: $\bar{x} = \sum x_i L_i / \sum L_i$, $\bar{y} = \sum y_i L_i / \sum L_i$.
- ☐ Recommended aspect ratio $t/d \leq 1.0$ (ideal); never exceed 1.5.
- ☐ Penetration before fracture ≈ **0.4 t** for ductile metals.
- ☐ Always include a *neat sketch* for full-mark part (a) answers.
- ☐ Units check: $L$ (mm) × $t$ (mm) × $\tau_u$ (MPa = N/mm²) = **N** (Newtons).

> **End of Module 2 — Topic: Punching | PCPET602 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Notching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Notching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Notching** is a precision sheet metal shearing operation in which a small, localized cut (notch) is removed from the edge, corner, or interior of a sheet metal blank using a punch-and-die set. It is classified as a **partial shearing / blanking variant** under the broader family of cold-working press operations governed by the mechanics of shear failure along a defined line of cut.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Notching is a sheet metal cutting operation in which metal is removed from the edge of a sheet (or plate) to produce a desired shape, a relief cut for subsequent bending, or a feature that allows assembly. The operation is fundamentally a **shearing process** where material separation occurs along the shear plane between the punch and die.

The operation is governed by standard sheet metal shearing mechanics:
- The punch descends onto the sheet clamped over the die.
- The metal is plastically deformed, then sheared along the clearance gap.
- A slug (the notched-out piece) drops through the die opening.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a rectangular piece of cardboard and you want to create a small rectangular "bite" out of one of its edges so that it can interlock with another piece, or so that it can be bent cleanly without the metal cracking or buckling at the bend line.

- **Without notching**: When you bend a piece of sheet metal, the inside of the bend compresses and the outside stretches. If you bend near an edge, the adjacent material acts like a wall and the bend becomes distorted, cracked, or wavy.
- **With notching**: You remove that "wall" of material first, giving the metal room to flow and bend cleanly. The notch is essentially a **stress-relief channel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Paper Tab:** Think of a cardboard box flap that has a small slit cut into the corner so it folds flat. That slit is exactly what a notch does for sheet metal — it allows controlled deformation (bending) without unwanted buckling or tearing.

### 1.3 Why Notching is Important in Metal Forming

Notching serves **four primary engineering purposes**:

1. **Edge Preparation for Bending** — relieves stress concentration, prevents tearing.
2. **Profile Generation** — produces tabs, lugs, or interlocking features.
3. **Material Removal** — removes unwanted scrap portions of a blank.
4. **Assembly Features** — creates slots for fasteners, hinges, or mating parts.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Shear strength (τ)** of mild steel: **≈ 300–400 MPa**
- **Tensile strength (σᵤ)** of mild steel: **≈ 400–500 MPa**
- **Standard die clearance**: **5–10 % of sheet thickness per side**
- **Typical punch penetration depth**: **0.5 to 1.0 × sheet thickness** before fracture
- **Clearance formula (general)**: **c = k × t**, where k ranges from **0.03 to 0.10** depending on material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Frequently Tested):** Notching is **NOT** blanking. In **blanking**, the *punched-out piece* is the desired product and the surrounding stock is scrap. In **notching**, the *surrounding sheet* is the desired product and the small removed slug is scrap. The mechanics are identical; only the designation of "useful part" changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Notching Operations

Notching is broadly classified based on **location** and **geometry** of the cut:

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Edge Notching** | Notch cut along the straight edge of a sheet | Pre-bending relief, edge preparation |
| **Corner Notching** | Notch cut at the corner of a sheet (often 90° or 45°) | Bend relief at corners |
| **Side Notching (Edge Trimming)** | Multiple notches cut on the edge in succession | Producing gear-tooth-like profiles |
| **Nibbling** | Sequential punching of overlapping small notches | Cutting large irregular contours |
| **Dinking (Dinking Die Notching)** | Notching using a mallet and steel-rule die | Leather, rubber, thin-gauge sheets |
| **Internal Notching (Piercing-type)** | Notch located entirely within the sheet (slug drops out) | Creating slots/windows |

### 2.2 Mechanics of the Notching Process — Stepwise

The notching operation follows the same four-stage mechanics as shearing:

1. **Elastic Deformation Stage** — Punch contacts sheet; sheet deflects elastically. Stress rises rapidly.
2. **Plastic Deformation Stage** — Stress exceeds yield; metal flows plastically into the die clearance. Workpiece surfaces become burnished.
3. **Shear/Fracture Stage** — Once stress reaches the shear strength, a crack initiates at both punch and die edges and propagates inward, meeting at the fracture point.
4. **Slug Separation Stage** — Punch completes stroke; slug is ejected (or retained in the die cavity depending on spring-back stripper design).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** The quality of the cut edge depends heavily on the **die clearance**. Insufficient clearance causes double-shear (secondary fracture), producing a ragged edge. Excessive clearance causes excessive draw-in, producing a large rollover zone and tapered burr.

### 2.3 Edge Quality Zones (Cross-Section of Sheared Edge)

A sheared/notched edge exhibits three distinct zones:

1. **Rollover Zone (Rounded Shear)** — Deformed by punch penetration, smooth & burnished.
2. **Shear Zone (Bright Cut)** — Cleanly fractured surface, shiny.
3. **Fracture Zone (Burr)** — Rough, torn region at the bottom of the cut.

The relative sizes of these zones are controlled by clearance and material ductility.

### 2.4 Critical Design Parameters

#### 2.4.1 Die Clearance (c)

$$c = k \cdot t$$

where:
- **c** = clearance per side (mm)
- **k** = clearance factor (dimensionless)
- **t** = sheet thickness (mm)

Typical values of **k**:
- Mild Steel: **0.05 – 0.10**
- Stainless Steel: **0.04 – 0.06**
- Aluminum Alloys: **0.04 – 0.06**
- Brass/Copper: **0.04 – 0.05**

#### 2.4.2 Punch Penetration / Penetration Depth (p)

The depth to which the punch must travel before fracture occurs:

$$p = t \cdot \left(0.5 \text{ to } 1.0\right)$$

Higher ductility → deeper penetration before fracture.

#### 2.4.3 Bending Allowance (for notch preceding bend)

When a notch is cut to relieve bending stress at a corner, the notch depth must satisfy:

$$d_n \geq r_b + t + c_{pad}$$

where:
- **d_n** = notch depth
- **r_b** = bend radius (inside)
- **t** = sheet thickness
- **c_pad** = safety clearance (≈ 0.1 t to 0.5 t)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Notching Formulas at a Glance**

| Formula # | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| **N1** | $F_s = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | $F_s$ = Shear force (N); $L$ = cut perimeter (mm); $t$ = thickness (mm); $\tau$ = shear strength (MPa) | Basic notching force |
| **N2** | $L = 2(L_n + W_n)$ | $L_n, W_n$ = notch length & width (mm) | Rectangular edge notch |
| **N3** | $L = \pi \cdot d$ | $d$ = hole/notch diameter (mm) | Circular notch/hole |
| **N4** | $F_p = F_s \cdot k_s$ | $F_p$ = press capacity; $k_s$ = safety factor (1.2–1.5) | Press tonnage selection |
| **N5** | $P_{strip} = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{strip}$ | $P_{strip}$ = stripping force; $\sigma_{strip}$ = stripper stress (≈ 0.005 σᵤ) | Stripper design |
| **N6** | $c = k \cdot t$ | $c$ = clearance per side; $k$ = 0.03–0.10 | Die clearance |
| **N7** | $W_d = W_p + 2c$ | $W_d$ = die opening; $W_p$ = punch width | Die/punch sizing |
| **N8** | $d_n \geq r_b + t + c_{pad}$ | Notch depth before bend | Bend-relief notch design |
| **N9** | $Sh = (p/t) \times 100\%$ | $Sh$ = shear percentage on cut edge | Cut quality indicator |
| **N10** | $F_{nibbling} = n \cdot F_{single}$ | $n$ = number of nibbling strokes | Nibbling total force |

### 2.6 Edge Notch Geometry — Bend Relief

When sheet metal is bent near a previously sheared edge, the geometry demands careful notch design. Without relief, **the bend line terminates at a point of high stress concentration**, leading to:

- Edge cracking
- Distortion of the bend profile
- Tool breakage in progressive dies

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Practice:** A notch depth equal to the **inside bend radius + sheet thickness + small clearance** is universally accepted in press tool design.

### 2.7 Industrial & Engineering Applications

Notching is employed extensively in:

1. **Automotive Industry** — Body panel cutouts, hinge reliefs, bracket tabs.
2. **Appliance Manufacturing** — Refrigerator cabinet corners, washer/dryer panels.
3. **Electrical Enclosures** — Ventilation slots, knockouts for cable glands.
4. **Aerospace** — Rib cutouts in wing panels, access door frames.
5. **HVAC Ductwork** — Edge notching for locking seams (Pittsburgh lock, drive cleat).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shearing Force for Rectangular Notch

**Given:**
- Rectangular notch of length $L_n$ and width $W_n$ cut from the edge of a sheet.
- Sheet thickness $t$.
- Material shear strength $\tau$.

**Objective:** Derive the total shear force required to perform the notching operation.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the Shear Line**

The punch must shear the metal along **three sides** of the notch (the fourth side is the open edge of the sheet, which is not sheared). Therefore the total shear perimeter is:

$$L_{total} = L_n + 2W_n$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Shear Area**

The shear area is the perimeter of the cut multiplied by the sheet thickness (since shear occurs through the full thickness):

$$A_s = L_{total} \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Shear Stress Equation**

The shear force is the shear stress multiplied by the shear area:

$$F_s = \tau \cdot A_s$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Area Expression**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= \tau \cdot (L_n + 2W_n) \cdot t \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Notch Note:** For a notch cut at the edge, only **three sides** are sheared. If the notch is completely internal (e.g., a square hole inside a sheet), **all four sides** are sheared and $L_{total} = 2(L_n + W_n)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Press Tonnage (with Safety Factor)

**Given:** Shear force $F_s$ from the previous derivation.

**Objective:** Determine required press capacity.

**Step 1 — Apply Safety Factor**

Press tools are sized with a safety factor to account for:
- Material property scatter (±15 %)
- Tool wear-induced force increase
- Punch misalignment forces

$$F_p = k_s \cdot F_s$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values (Worked Example)**

For a notch of $L_n = 50$ mm, $W_n = 10$ mm, $t = 3$ mm, $\tau = 320$ MPa, $k_s = 1.25$:

**Step 2a — Calculate perimeter:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_n + 2W_n \\
&= 50 + 2(10) \\
&= 70 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2b — Calculate shear area:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= L_{total} \cdot t \\
&= 70 \cdot 3 \\
&= 210 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2c — Calculate shear force:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= \tau \cdot A_s \\
&= 320 \cdot 210 \\
&= 67200 \text{ N} \\
&= 67.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2d — Calculate press capacity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_p &= k_s \cdot F_s \\
&= 1.25 \cdot 67200 \\
&= 84000 \text{ N} \\
&= 84.0 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 50 mm × 10 mm edge notch on 3 mm mild steel requires approximately **84 kN press tonnage** for safe operation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Bend-Relief Notch Depth

**Given:**
- A sheet of thickness $t$ is to be bent at a 90° angle.
- Inside bend radius $r_b$.
- The bend line terminates at the edge of the sheet.

**Objective:** Determine the minimum notch depth required to prevent edge cracking.

**Step 1 — Recognize the Geometric Requirement**

The bend line must "float" — that is, the material adjacent to the bend line must be free to flow inward as the outer fiber stretches. This requires that the edge of the sheet be removed to a depth greater than the bend zone.

**Step 2 — Define the Bend Zone**

The bend zone extends from the theoretical bend line (neutral axis intersection) outward through the bend radius plus the sheet thickness. Empirically:

$$d_{min} = r_b + t$$

**Step 3 — Add Safety Clearance**

A practical safety clearance $c_{pad} = 0.5 t$ is added to account for tool positioning and burr accumulation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_n &= d_{min} + c_{pad} \\
d_n &= r_b + t + 0.5 t \\
d_n &= r_b + 1.5 t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

For $t = 2$ mm, $r_b = 3$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_n &= 3 + 1.5(2) \\
&= 3 + 3 \\
&= 6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

A notch of depth **6 mm** must be cut at the edge adjacent to the bend line to avoid cracking.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Notching Decision Table

| Question to Ask | If YES | If NO |
|-----------------|--------|-------|
| Is the cut at the edge of the sheet? | Use 3-side shear formula $L = L_n + 2W_n$ | Use 4-side shear formula $L = 2(L_n + W_n)$ |
| Will the sheet be bent near the notch? | Add bend relief ($d_n \geq r_b + 1.5t$) | No relief needed |
| Is material thickness > 3 mm? | Use higher clearance factor $k = 0.08–0.10$ | Use lower clearance $k = 0.04–0.06$ |
| Is the contour highly irregular? | Use nibbling operation | Use single-stroke die |
| Is ductility of material high (Al, Cu)? | Use smaller clearance | Increase clearance |
| Is annual production volume high (>10,000)? | Use progressive die | Use hand notching / bench operation |

---

### 3.5 Stripping Force Derivation

After notching, the sheet must be stripped off the punch. The stripping force is required to overcome the frictional grip of the punch on the sheet (due to elastic recovery / spring-back around the punch).

**Step 1 — Define Stripping Stress**

The stripper must push the sheet with a stress approximately **0.5 %** of the ultimate tensile strength:

$$\sigma_{strip} = 0.005 \cdot \sigma_u$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Stripping Force**

$$P_{strip} = L_{total} \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{strip}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example**

For the previous example ($L_{total} = 70$ mm, $t = 3$ mm, $\sigma_u = 450$ MPa):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{strip} &= 0.005 \cdot 450 \\
&= 2.25 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{strip} &= 70 \cdot 3 \cdot 2.25 \\
&= 472.5 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 0.47 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Notching Press Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Metal Blank Input] --> B[Loading Station - Manual or Feeder]
    B --> C[Locating Pins and Back Gauge]
    C --> D[Punch Descends - Shear Stage]
    D --> E[Die Cavity - Slug Drops]
    E --> F[Stripper Plate Releases Sheet]
    F --> G[Notched Sheet Output]
    E --> H[Slug Collection Bin]
    D --> I{Press Tonnage Sufficient?}
    I -- Yes --> F
    I -- No --> J[Stall - Tool Damage Risk]
    J --> K[Reduce Material Hardness or Increase Press Capacity]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Edge Notching Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Identify Cut Location] --> P2[Step 2: Calculate Cut Perimeter]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Determine Die Clearance using k x t]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Size Punch and Die Opening]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute Shear Force]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Select Press Tonnage with Safety Factor]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Setup Tooling and Test Cut]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Inspect Cut Edge Quality]
    P8 --> P9{Quality Acceptable?}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[Production Run Approved]
    P9 -- No --> P11[Adjust Clearance or Tool Sharpening]
    P11 --> P7
```

### 4.3 Cross-Section of the Three Zones of a Notched Edge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z1["ROLLOVER ZONE - Plastic Deformation"]
        R1[Punch Edge Contact Region]
        R2[Burnished Smooth Surface]
    end
    subgraph Z2["SHEAR ZONE - Clean Fracture"]
        S1[Bright Sheared Surface]
        S2[Sharp Fracture Line]
    end
    subgraph Z3["FRACTURE ZONE - Secondary Break"]
        F1[Rough Torn Region]
        F2[Possible Burr Formation]
    end
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
```

### 4.4 Geometry Diagram — Bend Relief Notch at Corner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Sheet Edge - Original Line] --> M2[Notch Cut Removes Material]
    M2 --> M3[Bend Line - Radius r_b]
    M3 --> M4[Notch Depth d_n Required]
    M4 --> M5[Geometric Constraint: d_n greater than r_b + 1.5t]
    M5 --> M6[Stress Relief Achieved - Clean Bend]
```

### 4.5 Tool Assembly — Notching Die Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Press Ram] --> T2[Punch Holder]
    T2 --> T3[Notching Punch]
    T3 --> T4[Sheet Metal Blank]
    T4 --> T5[Die Block with Opening]
    T5 --> T6[Die Set Base]
    T4 --> T7[Stripper Plate with Springs]
    T7 --> T4
    T5 --> T8[Slug Drop Hole]
```

---
