## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Notching" in sheet metal operations. How is it different from blanking?**

**Model Answer:**

Notching is a sheet metal cutting operation in which a small portion of material is removed from the **edge of a sheet** (or strip) to produce a desired shape, a bend-relief feature, or a cutout.

**Difference from Blanking:**

| Aspect | Notching | Blanking |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Location of cut | Edge of sheet | Anywhere (typically interior) |
| Useful product | Surrounding sheet (parent metal) | Punched-out slug |
| Scrap designation | Removed slug is scrap | Surrounding strip is scrap |
| Common use | Bend relief, tabs | Producing flat blanks for further forming |

In essence, **notching is mechanically identical to blanking**, but the designation of "useful piece" is reversed.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State the function of die clearance in a notching operation. What happens if clearance is too low or too high?**

**Model Answer:**

**Function:** Die clearance is the small gap between the punch and die on each side. It controls the fracture propagation, cut edge quality, and tool life.

**Effect of Low Clearance (< 3 % t):**
- Double-shear fracture occurs, producing a secondary crack line.
- Edge appears ragged with a heavy burr.
- Punch wear is accelerated due to high localized stress.

**Effect of High Clearance (> 10 % t):**
- Excessive material draw-in before fracture.
- Large rollover zone, tapered edge.
- Slug may not separate cleanly (sticking in die).
- Burr formation on the underside.

**Optimum:** Typically **5–8 % of sheet thickness per side** for mild steel.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly lose marks in notching questions by:
> 1. Forgetting to add the **safety factor** when computing press tonnage.
> 2. Confusing **shear perimeter** for edge notch (3 sides) vs. internal hole (4 sides).
> 3. Not specifying **units** in final answers — KTU examiners strictly deduct for missing units.
> 4. Omitting the **bend relief** geometric justification.
> 5. Skipping the **die clearance** calculation — a mandatory parameter per KTU marking scheme.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** A rectangular notch of dimensions **60 mm × 12 mm** is to be cut along the edge of a **4 mm thick mild steel sheet**. The shear strength of the material is **350 MPa**. Calculate:
(i) Shear perimeter
(ii) Shear area
(iii) Shear force required

**(b)** If a safety factor of **1.3** is recommended, determine the required press tonnage. Also, determine the appropriate **die clearance per side** and the **die opening width** for a clearance factor of **k = 0.06**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Shear Perimeter:**

For an **edge notch**, only three sides are sheared (one side is open):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_n + 2 W_n \\
&= 60 + 2(12) \\
&= 60 + 24 \\
&= 84 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a) (ii) Shear Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= L_{total} \cdot t \\
&= 84 \cdot 4 \\
&= 336 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a) (iii) Shear Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= \tau \cdot A_s \\
&= 350 \cdot 336 \\
&= 117600 \text{ N} \\
&= 117.6 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Press Tonnage with Safety Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_p &= k_s \cdot F_s \\
&= 1.3 \cdot 117.6 \\
&= 152.88 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Die Clearance per Side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= k \cdot t \\
&= 0.06 \cdot 4 \\
&= 0.24 \text{ mm per side}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Die Opening Width (along the 60 mm direction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_d &= W_p + 2c \\
&= 60 + 2(0.24) \\
&= 60.48 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Die Opening Width (along the 12 mm direction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_d &= W_p + 2c \\
&= 12 + 2(0.24) \\
&= 12.48 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary:**
> - Shear Perimeter = **84 mm**
> - Shear Area = **336 mm²**
> - Shear Force = **117.6 kN**
> - Required Press Tonnage = **152.88 kN**
> - Clearance per side = **0.24 mm**
> - Die opening (long side) = **60.48 mm**
> - Die opening (short side) = **12.48 mm**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **three zones** of a sheared/notched edge. Briefly state the effect of die clearance on each zone.

**(b)** A sheet of **2.5 mm thick** stainless steel is to be bent at **90° with an inside bend radius of 4 mm**. Calculate the **minimum notch depth** required at the edge of the sheet adjacent to the bend line to prevent edge cracking. If the bend relief notch is cut at the corner with a width of 8 mm, calculate the **shear force** required. Take shear strength of stainless steel as **450 MPa**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Zones of a Sheared Edge:**

```
         ┌─────────┐
         │ Punch   │
         ├─────────┤
         │  ↓↓↓↓↓  │
    ─────┤█████████├─────  ← Top of Sheet
         │  Zone 1 │       (ROLLOVER - smooth, rounded)
         │░░░░░░░░░│
         │░░Zone 2░│       (SHEAR - bright, fractured)
         │░░░░░░░░░│
         │░ Zone 3 ░│       (FRACTURE - rough, burred)
         ├─────────┤
         │ Die     │
         └─────────┘
```

**Zone 1 — Rollover Zone (≈ 25–35 % of thickness):**
- Caused by plastic deformation as the punch initially penetrates.
- Surface is smooth and burnished.
- **Effect of clearance:** Higher clearance → larger rollover zone.

**Zone 2 — Shear Zone (≈ 40–60 % of thickness):**
- Formed by clean shear fracture.
- Surface is bright and shiny.
- **Effect of clearance:** Optimum clearance → maximum bright zone; poor clearance → secondary fracture in this zone.

**Zone 3 — Fracture Zone (≈ 15–25 % of thickness):**
- Rough, torn region from secondary fracture.
- Source of burr.
- **Effect of clearance:** Higher clearance → larger burr; lower clearance → cleaner fracture.

**(b) Minimum Notch Depth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{min} &= r_b + t + c_{pad} \\
&= 4 + 2.5 + (0.5 \cdot 2.5) \\
&= 4 + 2.5 + 1.25 \\
&= 7.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt standard value: **d_n = 8 mm** (rounded up).

**Shear Perimeter of the Corner Notch:**

The notch is at the corner of the sheet — two sides are sheared (the third side is the open corner). Width of notch = 8 mm, depth = 8 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 2 \cdot W_n + d_n \\
&= 2(8) + 8 \\
&= 24 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Alternatively, treating it as a small square notch at the corner: $L = 2 \cdot 8 + 8 = 24$ mm.)

**Shear Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= L_{total} \cdot t \\
&= 24 \cdot 2.5 \\
&= 60 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shear Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= \tau \cdot A_s \\
&= 450 \cdot 60 \\
&= 27000 \text{ N} \\
&= 27.0 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary:**
> - Three zones identified: Rollover, Shear, Fracture
> - Minimum notch depth = **7.75 mm → adopt 8 mm**
> - Shear Perimeter = **24 mm**
> - Shear Area = **60 mm²**
> - Shear Force = **27.0 kN**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Notching**

- ☐ **Definition:** Removal of a small piece from the edge of a sheet to create a cutout, bend relief, or feature.
- ☐ **Mechanism:** Identical to blanking/shearing — punch descends, plastic deformation, fracture, slug ejection.
- ☐ **Shear Force Formula:** $F_s = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ (always multiply by safety factor for press capacity).
- ☐ **Edge notch:** 3 sides sheared → $L = L_n + 2W_n$.
- ☐ **Internal notch/hole:** 4 sides sheared → $L = 2(L_n + W_n)$.
- ☐ **Die clearance:** $c = k \cdot t$, where $k = 0.04$–$0.10$ depending on material.
- ☐ **Die opening:** $W_d = W_p + 2c$.
- ☐ **Three cut edge zones:** Rollover (top, smooth) → Shear (middle, bright) → Fracture (bottom, rough/burr).
- ☐ **Bend relief notch depth:** $d_n \geq r_b + t + 0.5t = r_b + 1.5t$.
- ☐ **Nibbling:** Sequential overlapping punches to cut large irregular profiles.
- ☐ **Stripping force:** $P_{strip} = L \cdot t \cdot (0.005 \cdot \sigma_u)$.
- ☐ **Always include units** in KTU answers — penalty for missing units.
- ☐ **Applications:** Automotive panels, HVAC ducts, electrical enclosures, appliance cabinets.
- ☐ **Key difference from blanking:** In notching, the slug is scrap; in blanking, the slug is the product.
- ☐ **Industrial relevance:** Notching is critical for **bend relief** to prevent edge cracking in 90° bends.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Shearing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Shearing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism of Shearing** is a fundamental metal cutting/separation process in which a sheet metal blank is plastically deformed along localized shear planes until fracture occurs, resulting in clean separation of the material. It is governed by the punch–die geometry, the clearance between cutting edges, and the inherent shear strength of the workpiece material.

In the context of the **PCPET602 syllabus (Module 2)**, the *Mechanism of Shearing* specifically refers to the controlled fracture-based separation process employed in **press-tool operations** such as blanking, piercing, punching, notching, trimming, and shaving. The mechanism describes **how** the metal actually fails during the cutting cycle — moving from elastic strain, to plastic flow, to ductile fracture, and finally to material separation.

Mathematically stated:

$$P_{\text{shear}} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_s$$

Where:
- $L$ = **Perimeter of the cut** (mm)
- $t$ = **Stock thickness** (mm)
- $\tau_s$ = **Shear strength of material** (MPa or N/mm²)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Everyday Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the office paper hole-punch.**

When you press a paper hole-punch downward:
1. First, the **paper bends slightly** (elastic + plastic deformation).
2. Then the **inner cylinder pushes the metal into the die cavity**.
3. The paper between the punch edge and die edge gets squeezed.
4. A **thin zone of intense stress** forms around the punch circumference.
5. Finally, the paper **snaps clean along that ring** — this is fracture.

In sheet metal, the same phenomenon occurs — except with thousands of times more force and far more precise metallurgy. The **clearance** between punch and die is analogous to the **tiny gap** between the punch cylinder and the anvil ring. If the gap is wrong, instead of a clean cut you get a **jagged, burred, or torn edge** — exactly like a dull paper punch leaves rough paper.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard engineering metrics used throughout this topic:
>
> - **Recommended Clearance per side:** $c = (0.03 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot t$ for most steels
> - **Shear Strength Range:** $\tau_s \approx 0.8 \times \sigma_u$ (where $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength)
> - **Tensile Strength Range:** $\sigma_t \approx 0.7 \text{ to } 0.9 \times \sigma_u$
> - **Die Life Limit on Wear:** Typically **0.5–1.0 mm edge radius** before regrinding
> - **Standard units:** Force in **N** or **kN**, Stress in **MPa** (N/mm²), Thickness in **mm**.

These constants are **bold-emphasized** because they appear directly in numerical problems in KTU end-semester examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Operational Breakdown

The **Mechanism of Shearing** is NOT a single instant event. It is a sequence of four distinct physical stages that the material undergoes as the punch descends. Understanding each stage is **critical** for KTU Module 2 questions.

### Stage 1 — Elastic Deformation
- The punch contacts the sheet metal.
- Stress builds up **linearly** below the punch edge.
- The material deforms reversibly (would spring back if punch were removed now).
- No permanent damage occurs in this stage.

### Stage 2 — Plastic Deformation (Penetration Begins)
- Stress exceeds the **yield strength** of the material locally.
- The punch edge begins to **penetrate** the workpiece to a depth of roughly $(0.1 \text{ to } 0.3) \cdot t$.
- Material flows plastically, becoming work-hardened near the cut edge.
- The **shear zone** is being formed — a narrow band of intense shear strain.

### Stage 3 — Fracture Initiation
- Stress reaches the **shear strength / fracture limit**.
- Tiny **micro-voids and cracks** nucleate within the shear zone.
- A **fracture line (secondary shear zone)** initiates, typically inclined at an angle to the punch edge.
- For **ductile materials** (low-carbon steel, aluminum, copper), this happens after substantial plastic flow.
- For **brittle materials** (cast iron, hardened spring steel), fracture initiates with very little plastic flow.

### Stage 4 — Material Separation (Final Shear)
- The micro-cracks propagate and **coalesce** into a single continuous fracture surface.
- The slug (or blank) separates cleanly from the parent strip.
- The freshly cut edge shows characteristic zones: **roll-over (burr), burnish (smooth), fracture (rough), and burr**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does clearance matter so much?**
> If clearance is **too small** → the fracture lines from punch edge and die edge do not align → secondary shear, doubled burnish, jagged edge, **excessive punch force**.
> If clearance is **too large** → the material is **pulled and torn** rather than sheared → excessive burr, large roll-over zone, poor dimensional accuracy.

## 2.2 The Shear Zone Geometry — The Heart of the Mechanism

In the deformed zone, the metal is treated as a continuous plastic medium sliding along a **primary shear plane**. This is the **classical Lee-Shaffer shear-angle model**, which KTU students MUST understand.

Key angles:
- $\phi$ = **Shear angle** (between shear plane and punch face)
- $\alpha$ = **Rake angle** of punch (usually $0°$ in blanking — flat-faced punch)

For a **flat-faced punch** ($\alpha = 0°$):
$$\phi = 45°$$ (theoretically, for an ideal rigid-plastic material)

In reality, $\phi$ lies between **$30°$ and $45°$** depending on:
- Material ductility (more ductile → smaller $\phi$)
- Clearance (larger clearance → smaller effective $\phi$)
- Strain hardening exponent

The **chip thickness ratio** $r$ relates input and output dimensions:
$$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2}$$

Where $t_1$ = original thickness, $t_2$ = thickness in shear zone.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below contains EVERY formula you will need for KTU Module 2 numerical problems on Mechanism of Shearing.** Memorize these.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $P = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_s$ | Punching force (basic) | N |
| 2 | $P = 0.8 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u$ | Punching force using ultimate tensile strength | N |
| 3 | $P_{\text{strip}} = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Stripping force (pushback of slug) | N |
| 4 | $P_{\text{blank hold}} = (0.10 \text{ to } 0.20) \cdot P$ | Blank-holder force (in drawing) | N |
| 5 | $c = k \cdot t$ | Clearance per side | mm |
| 6 | $k = 0.03 \text{ to } 0.10$ | Clearance factor for mild steel | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\tau_s \approx 0.8 \cdot \sigma_u$ | Empirical shear-tensile relation | MPa |
| 8 | $\phi = 45°$ (theoretical) | Shear angle for flat punch | degrees |
| 9 | $A_s = \frac{L \cdot t}{\sin\phi}$ | Shear area | mm² |
| 10 | $T_{\text{punch}} = t + 0.25 \cdot \text{dia}$ | Punch penetration depth for clean cut | mm |
| 11 | $P_{\text{shaving}} = L \cdot t_{\text{shave}} \cdot \tau_s$ | Shaving force | N |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{shear}} = \frac{A_{\text{burnish}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ | Shear quality ratio (good cut ≈ 0.5–0.8) | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The mechanism of shearing governs **every** mass-produced sheet-metal part in industry — automotive body panels, electrical enclosures, washing-machine drums, aerospace skin panels, and even simple tin cans. Designers rely on accurate force prediction to:
>
> - **Size the press** (tonnage selection: 10 kN bench press → 20,000 kN automotive blanking line).
> - **Estimate tooling life** (number of strokes before die regrinding).
> - **Determine strip layout** for minimum scrap.
> - **Predict edge quality** (burnish percentage indicates acceptable cut).
> - **Choose appropriate clearance** to control burr and dimensional tolerance.

Failure to correctly apply these formulas in industry leads to **tool breakage, poor edge quality, excessive scrap, and production downtime** — costs that easily run into millions of rupees annually.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Punching Force from Shear Strength

We start from the **fundamental definition of shear stress**:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A}$$

Where $A$ is the area resisting shear.

**Step 1:** Identify the shear area.

For a blanking/piercing operation, the resisting area is the **cylindrical (or perimeter-defined) surface** along which the material separates. For a uniform sheet of thickness $t$:

$$A = L \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the shear stress equation.

$$\tau_s = \frac{P}{L \cdot t}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the punching force $P$.

$$P = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_s$$

**Step 4:** Convert using ultimate tensile strength.

Using the empirical relation $\tau_s \approx 0.8 \cdot \sigma_u$:

$$\boxed{P = 0.8 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u}$$

This is the **industry-standard formula** used to specify press tonnage. The factor **0.8** accounts for the empirical difference between laboratory shear strength and actual in-process shear strength in a press tool.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shear Angle from Cutting Geometry

Consider the elementary shear zone model. The **undeformed chip thickness** is $t_1$ (the sheet thickness) and the **shear plane length** is $l_s$.

**Step 1:** Geometry of the shear plane.

From the geometry of the deformation zone:

$$l_s = \frac{t_1}{\sin\phi}$$

**Step 2:** The shear strain in the primary zone.

$$\gamma = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\phi \cdot \cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

For a **flat-faced punch** where $\alpha = 0°$:

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{\sin\phi \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{2}{\sin 2\phi}$$

**Step 3:** Find the shear angle that minimizes cutting energy.

Setting $\frac{d\gamma}{d\phi} = 0$:

$$\frac{d}{d\phi}\left(\frac{2}{\sin 2\phi}\right) = 0$$

$$-\frac{4\cos 2\phi}{\sin^2 2\phi} = 0$$

This yields $\cos 2\phi = 0$, therefore:

$$\boxed{2\phi = 90° \Rightarrow \phi = 45°}$$

This is the **theoretical minimum-energy shear angle** for a perfectly plastic, non-strain-hardening material with a flat punch.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stripping Force

After the slug is sheared, the punch must **withdraw** from the die. The slug is gripped tightly inside the die cavity due to elastic springback. The **stripping force** pushes the slug back through the die.

**Step 1:** The friction/interference pressure on the slug wall approximates the **tensile strength** of the freshly cut edge:

$$p \approx \sigma_t$$

**Step 2:** The contact area is the cylindrical slug wall:

$$A_{\text{strip}} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t$$

**Step 3:** The stripping force is:

$$P_{\text{strip}} = A_{\text{strip}} \cdot p = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

Or in the **general perimeter form**:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{strip}} = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

Typically $P_{\text{strip}} \approx (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot P_{\text{punch}}$ — a **small but critical** force for ejection system design.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 3 mm thick mild steel sheet ($\sigma_u = 400$ MPa) is to be blanked to produce a circular disc of diameter 60 mm. Determine:
> (a) The blanking force.
> (b) The work done if the punch penetrates the full thickness.
> (c) The stripping force.

**Given:**
- $t = 3$ mm
- $\sigma_u = 400$ MPa = 400 N/mm²
- $d = 60$ mm

**Solution:**

**(a) Blanking force:**

Perimeter of cut:
$$L = \pi \cdot d = \pi \cdot 60 = 188.496 \text{ mm}$$

Punching force:
$$P = 0.8 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u$$

$$P = 0.8 \cdot 188.496 \cdot 3 \cdot 400$$

$$P = 0.8 \cdot 226195.2$$

$$\boxed{P = 180956.16 \text{ N} \approx 181 \text{ kN}}$$

**(b) Work done:**

Assuming the punch travels a distance equal to sheet thickness:
$$W = P \cdot t = 180956.16 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-3} = 542.87 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 542.87 \text{ J}}$$

**(c) Stripping force:**

Using $\sigma_t \approx 0.7 \cdot \sigma_u = 280$ N/mm²:

$$P_{\text{strip}} = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 188.496 \cdot 3 \cdot 280$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{strip}} = 158336.6 \text{ N} \approx 158.3 \text{ kN}}$$

Wait — this is **unrealistically high**. The correct interpretation uses the **empirical 5–10% rule**:

$$P_{\text{strip}} \approx 0.10 \cdot P = 0.10 \cdot 181 = 18.1 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a classic KTU trap.** Students often apply $L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ literally, getting a force nearly equal to the punching force itself. The correct KTU-accepted convention is to use the **empirical 5–10% rule** for stripping force. Examiner will award **partial credit** for showing the formula but **dock 2 marks** if the literal magnitude is left unquestioned.

## 3.5 The Sheared Edge Profile — Analytical Zone Breakdown

The freshly cut edge contains **four distinct zones** that examiners love to test:

| Zone | Width | Cause | Appearance |
|------|-------|-------|------------|
| **Roll-over** | $(0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot t$ | Plastic bending before fracture | Smooth, rounded, often burred |
| **Burnish** | $(0.20 \text{ to } 0.40) \cdot t$ | Pure shearing along the fracture plane | Bright, shiny, smooth |
| **Fracture** | $(0.50 \text{ to } 0.70) \cdot t$ | Ductile fracture propagation | Rough, matte, granular |
| **Burr** | $(0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot t$ | Tearing at final separation | Sharp, irregular protrusion |

A **high-quality blank** has burnish ≥ 50% of the edge. **Excessive roll-over** indicates insufficient clearance; **excessive fracture zone** indicates excessive clearance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Stage-by-Stage Shearing Mechanism

    C -->|Yield point exceeded| D{Stage 3: Fracture Initiation}
    D -->|Microvoids coalesce| E{Stage 4: Material Separation}
    E --> F[Rolled Edge Zone forms]
    E --> G[Burnish Zone forms]
    E --> H[Fracture Zone forms]
    E --> I[Burr Formation]
    F --> J[Slugs separates cleanly]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[End: Part separated, slug in die]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"Define the mechanism of shearing. List the four stages involved."**

**Model Answer:**

Mechanism of shearing is the controlled fracture-based separation process in sheet metal where material is plastically deformed along a localized shear zone until fracture occurs.

The four stages are:

1. **Elastic deformation** — reversible strain under punch loading.
2. **Plastic deformation** — punch penetrates to roughly 10–30% of thickness; material work-hardens.
3. **Fracture initiation** — micro-voids nucleate in the shear zone when stress reaches shear strength.
4. **Material separation** — cracks coalesce; the slug separates, exposing roll-over, burnish, fracture, and burr zones on the cut edge.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for stages)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"What is meant by clearance in shearing operations? Why is it important?"**

**Model Answer:**

Clearance $c$ is the **gap per side** between the punch edge and the die opening edge.

$$c = k \cdot t$$

where $k = 0.03$ to $0.10$ depending on material.

It is critical because:
- **Proper clearance** aligns the punch-side fracture line with the die-side fracture line, producing a clean, vertical cut with maximum burnish.
- **Insufficient clearance** causes secondary shear, doubled burnish, jagged edges, and excessive punch force.
- **Excessive clearance** causes material pulling, tearing, oversized burrs, and poor dimensional accuracy on the slug.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + formula, 2 marks importance)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Complete Solution

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the punching force in a blanking operation, starting from the basic definition of shear stress. Discuss the role of clearance in determining the quality of the sheared edge. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 2 mm thick aluminium sheet (ultimate tensile strength = 180 MPa) is to be pierced to produce square holes of side 25 mm. If 12 holes are pierced per stroke, calculate: (i) total punching force per stroke, (ii) work done per stroke, (iii) recommended clearance per side, and (iv) press tonnage required if a 25% safety factor is used. **(7 marks)**

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Derivation:**

Starting from $\tau = P/A$:

$$\tau_s = \frac{P}{A_s}$$

For a blanked perimeter $L$ across thickness $t$:

$$A_s = L \cdot t$$

Therefore:

$$P = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_s$$

Since $\tau_s \approx 0.8 \cdot \sigma_u$:

$$P = 0.8 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u$$

**Clearance discussion:**

Clearance $c = k \cdot t$ where $k$ ranges from 0.03 to 0.10. For aluminium, $k \approx 0.05$ typically.

**Quality zones on the cut edge:**
- **Roll-over zone** = $(0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot t$
- **Burnish zone** = $(0.20 \text{ to } 0.40) \cdot t$ (desired)
- **Fracture zone** = $(0.50 \text{ to } 0.70) \cdot t$
- **Burr zone** = $(0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot t$

Optimal cut quality requires burnish ≥ 50% — achieved when the punch-side and die-side fracture lines **meet precisely**.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for clearance formula, 2 for quality discussion)*

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $t = 2$ mm
- $\sigma_u = 180$ MPa
- Side $a = 25$ mm
- Number of holes $n = 12$

**Step (i):** Perimeter of one square hole:

$$L_1 = 4 \cdot a = 4 \cdot 25 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

Total perimeter per stroke:

$$L = 12 \cdot 100 = 1200 \text{ mm}$$

Total punching force:

$$P = 0.8 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u$$

$$P = 0.8 \cdot 1200 \cdot 2 \cdot 180$$

$$\boxed{P = 345600 \text{ N} = 345.6 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step (ii):** Punch travel ≈ thickness = 2 mm.

$$W = P \cdot t = 345600 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{W = 691.2 \text{ J}}$$

**Step (iii):** Recommended clearance for aluminium:

$$c = 0.05 \cdot t = 0.05 \cdot 2 = 0.10 \text{ mm per side}$$

$$\boxed{c = 0.10 \text{ mm/side}}$$

**Step (iv):** Press tonnage with 25% safety factor:

$$P_{\text{press}} = 1.25 \cdot P = 1.25 \cdot 345.6$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{press}} = 432 \text{ kN (approx 45-ton press)}}$$

*(7 marks: 1.5 for each of parts i, ii, iii, and 2.5 for part iv with safety factor)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Complete Alternative Solution

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Alternative]**

> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Lee-Shaffer single shear plane model** for chip formation in shearing. Show that the theoretical shear angle for a flat-faced punch with orthogonal cutting is **45°**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A blanking operation is performed on a 4 mm thick cold-rolled steel strip with ultimate tensile strength of 500 MPa. The blank shape is a rectangle 80 mm × 50 mm with rounded corners of radius 6 mm. Calculate: (i) the perimeter of the blank, (ii) the blanking force, (iii) the centre of pressure location from the geometric centre for proper die design, and (iv) the work done assuming the punch travels 1.2 × thickness. **(7 marks)**

**Solution to Part (a):**

The Lee-Shaffer model assumes:
- The chip is formed by **shearing along a single plane** (the primary shear plane).
- Material flows as a **rigid block** that slides along this plane.
- A **continuous chip** is produced.

The shear strain in the primary zone:

$$\gamma = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\phi \cdot \cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

For flat punch: $\alpha = 0°$

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{\sin\phi \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{2}{\sin 2\phi}$$

Setting $\frac{d\gamma}{d\phi} = 0$:

$$\cos 2\phi = 0 \Rightarrow \phi = 45°$$

Hence proved. This minimum-energy shear angle is the classical result. *(7 marks: 3 for model description, 4 for derivation)*

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $t = 4$ mm, $\sigma_u = 500$ MPa, rectangle 80 × 50 mm with $r = 6$ mm corner radius.

**Step (i):** Perimeter calculation:

Straight portions:
$$L_{\text{straight}} = 2(80 - 2 \cdot 6) + 2(50 - 2 \cdot 6) = 2(68) + 2(38) = 136 + 76 = 212 \text{ mm}$$

Curved portions (4 quarter-circles = 1 full circle):
$$L_{\text{curve}} = 2\pi r = 2\pi(6) = 37.70 \text{ mm}$$

Total perimeter:
$$\boxed{L = 212 + 37.70 = 249.70 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step (ii):** Blanking force:

$$P = 0.8 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u = 0.8 \cdot 249.70 \cdot 4 \cdot 500$$

$$\boxed{P = 399520 \text{ N} \approx 399.5 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step (iii):** Centre of pressure — for a rectangle it lies at the geometric centroid. For an off-centre die or compound die with multiple operations, the pressure centre is calculated using moments. For a simple rectangle, the centre of pressure coincides with the geometric centre. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step (iv):** Work done with punch travel = $1.2 \cdot t = 4.8$ mm:

$$W = P \cdot s = 399520 \cdot 4.8 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{W = 1917.7 \text{ J} \approx 1.92 \text{ kJ}}$$

*(7 marks distributed as 1.5, 2, 1.5, 2)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on Mechanism of Shearing problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.8 factor** when converting $\sigma_u$ to $\tau_s$. Examiner docks 1 mark. Always write $P = 0.8 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u$.
>
> 2. **Wrong units** — mixing mm and cm. Stay entirely in **mm** for length and **MPa = N/mm²** for stress. Resultant force is in **N**.
>
> 3. **Ignoring safety factor** on press tonnage — KTU expects a **20–30% safety factor**. Forgetting this costs 1.5 marks.
>
> 4. **Not stating the four stages** clearly in mechanism questions — examiner wants bullet-pointed stages, not prose. You get 1 mark per stage = 4 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing "clearance per side" with "total clearance"** — Total clearance = $2c$. Examiner deducts 1 mark if confused.
>
> 6. **Stripping force — using $L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ literally** — This gives unrealistic magnitudes. Use the **empirical 5–10% rule** of punch force.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to convert J from N·mm** — Remember $1 \text{ J} = 10^3 \text{ N·mm}$. So multiply by $10^{-3}$ when N is in N and mm is converted to m.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and review before every KTU exam:**

- [x] Mechanism of shearing = elastic → plastic → fracture → separation (4 stages)
- [x] **Punching force formula:** $P = 0.8 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u$
- [x] **Stripping force:** $\approx (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot P$
- [x] **Clearance per side:** $c = k \cdot t$, with $k = 0.03$ to $0.10$
- [x] **Shear angle** for flat punch: $\phi = 45°$ theoretically
- [x] **Cut edge has 4 zones:** roll-over, burnish, fracture, burr
- [x] **Optimal cut** = maximum burnish (≥ 50% of edge)
- [x] **Too little clearance** → secondary shear, jagged edge
- [x] **Too much clearance** → tearing, large burr, poor tolerance
- [x] **Shear strength** $\tau_s \approx 0.8 \cdot \sigma_u$
- [x] **Tensile strength** $\sigma_t \approx (0.7 \text{ to } 0.9) \cdot \sigma_u$
- [x] **Press tonnage** must include **25% safety factor**
- [x] **Work done** $W = P \cdot s$ where $s$ is punch travel
- [x] **Lee-Shaffer model** = single shear plane, rigid block sliding
- [x] **Always convert** mm to m for J calculation: multiply by $10^{-3}$

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Mechanism of Shearing. Best of luck for your KTU examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Punch and Die Design for Blanking and Punching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Punch and Die Design for Blanking and Punching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blanking** is a shearing operation in which a punch and die are used to separate a piece of material (the **blank**) from the surrounding stock. The separated piece becomes the desired part, while the remaining stock is **scrap**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Punching** is a shearing operation in which a punch and die are used to separate a piece of material (the **slug**) from the surrounding stock. Here, the separated piece is **scrap**, and the remaining stock is the desired part.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a sheet of dough and a **cookie cutter**:
- **Blanking** → You press the cutter down and the **cookie-shaped piece** is your product. The leftover dough is waste.
- **Punching** → You press a small cutter down to make a **decorative hole** in a flat chapati. The small piece that drops out is waste; the chapati with the hole is your product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The terminology depends on **what is the useful product**, NOT on the mechanics of the operation. Mechanically, blanking and punching are identical processes. The tools used are simply inverted versions of each other.

## 1.2 The Tooling System — Punch and Die

The two primary components of any blanking/punching tool are:

- **Punch (Male Tool):** Usually mounted on the upper shoe of the press; it pushes the material into the die.
- **Die (Female Tool):** Mounted on the lower shoe; it provides the opening against which shearing takes place.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Metric:** The **radial gap** between the punch and die walls is called **Clearance (C)**. Clearance is the single most important design parameter — it governs cut quality, tool life, force, and dimensional accuracy. For a sheet of thickness **t**, the **clearance per side** is denoted **c**, so total clearance is **2c**.

## 1.3 Typical Clearance Values (Per Side)

| Material Class | Clearance per side (c) as % of thickness (t) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Very soft (aluminium, brass) | **8–10 %** |
| Mild steel, soft copper | **6–8 %** |
| Medium-hard steel | **4–6 %** |
| Hard steel, stainless | **3–4 %** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often quote clearance as "**2c**" without realising that standard tables and Sieber's formula give the **per-side** value. Always read carefully which definition the question uses.

## 1.4 Real-World Significance

Blanking and punching are the **largest tonnage-forming operations** in the world — automotive bodies, electrical laminations, washing machine panels, PCB mounting holes, and razor blades are all produced by these processes. Tool design decisions directly affect **burr height, dimensional tolerance, die wear, and press tonnage required**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Shearing with Clearance

When the punch descends, stress is not purely vertical. Because of the **clearance gap**, the material experiences a **rolling and bending moment** as it is forced through the die opening. The deformation proceeds as follows:

- **Stage 1 — Elastic deformation:** Punch contacts sheet; localised elastic strain.
- **Stage 2 — Plastic deformation (penetration):** Punch penetrates ~**1/3 of thickness (t)** for ductile materials. Material flows plastically around the cutting edges.
- **Stage 3 — Fracture initiation:** Cracks nucleate at the punch edge AND die edge.
- **Stage 4 — Fracture propagation:** Cracks travel towards each other. **If clearance is correct**, the cracks meet → clean cut. **If clearance is too small**, cracks overlap → secondary shear, double burnish. **If clearance is too large**, cracks diverge → large rollover, big burr.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimum Clearance Principle:** Cracks should originate at the **punch edge** (top) and **die edge** (bottom) and **meet each other** at approximately the centre of the sheet thickness. This yields a vertical burnished band with minimum rollover and minimum burr.

## 2.2 Standard Clearance Formulae

### 2.2.1 Percentage Rule (Industry Standard)

$$c_{per\ side} = k \cdot t$$

where **k** is a material-dependent factor (typically **0.03 to 0.10**), and **t** is sheet thickness in mm.

### 2.2.2 Sieber's Formula (Empirical, widely used in Indian textbooks)

When **shear strength σ_s** is expressed in **kgf/mm²** and **t** in **mm**:

$$c_{per\ side} = 0.04 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula is the most-tested version in KTU exams. Note the unit dependency — if σ_s is in **MPa**, divide by **9.81** to convert to kgf/mm², OR use the SI form: **c = 0.005·t·√σ_s** when σ_s is in MPa.

## 2.3 Punch and Die Size Design — The Golden Rules

This is the **highest-frequency topic** in KTU Module 2. The rule depends on whether the operation is blanking or punching:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule for BLANKING:** The **die** determines the blank size. The **punch** is smaller.
> - Die size = Required blank size
> - Punch size = Die size − 2c

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule for PUNCHING:** The **punch** determines the hole size. The **die** is larger.
> - Punch size = Required hole size
> - Die size = Punch size + 2c

### Rationale:
- In **blanking**, the part must match the drawing → tolerance is put on the **die** (the larger member) because the punch wears faster.
- In **punching**, the hole must match the drawing → tolerance is put on the **punch** (the smaller member).

## 2.4 Tolerance Assignment

A minimum manufacturing tolerance of **±0.025 mm** is assigned to the **sizing member**. For example:

- Blanking a **50 mm × 30 mm** part with **t = 2 mm, c = 0.1 mm per side**:
  - Die = **50.025 × 30.025 mm**
  - Punch = **49.825 × 29.825 mm**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Clearance per side | c | k·t  OR  0.04·t·√σ_s | mm | σ_s in kgf/mm² for Sieber |
| Total radial clearance | C | 2c | mm | Gap between punch and die walls |
| Shear strength | σ_s | ≈ 0.8·σ_u | MPa | σ_u = ultimate tensile strength |
| Shear force | P_shear | L·t·τ  ≈  L·t·(0.8·σ_u) | N | L = perimeter cut (mm) |
| Max. punch force | P_max | 0.5·L·t²·σ_s / tan φ | N | φ = shear-failure angle |
| Optimum φ (for ductile mat.) | φ | ≈ 8°–12° | ° | Gives minimum peak force |
| Stripping force | P_strip | (5–10 %)·P_max | N | Required to separate sheet from punch |
| Centre of pressure | CP | Σ(A_i · x_i) / Σ A_i | mm | For aligning die with press ram |
| Push-back force | P_pb | ≈ 0.05·P_max | N | To eject blank/slug from die |
| Stock utilisation | η | (A_blank · n) / (W·s) | % | n = parts per strip, W = strip width |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0.8 factor?** In pure shear, a ductile metal fails at approximately **80 %** of its ultimate tensile strength. This empirical constant (von Mises-related) is universally used in KTU problems.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

The punch–die clearance dictates the **type of cut surface**:
- **Burnish zone** (smooth, bright) — from plastic deformation before fracture.
- **Fracture zone** (rough, dull) — from crack propagation.
- **Rollover** (rounded edge) — from initial bending into the die.
- **Burr** (protrusion on exit side) — from uncontrolled fracture.

By selecting appropriate clearance, the engineer controls **edge quality** for subsequent operations like bending (which requires a smooth, burr-free edge).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Punching Force (Analytical Method)

### Assumptions
- Material is homogeneous and isotropic.
- Shear failure occurs along a **single inclined plane** at angle **φ** from the horizontal (the vertical face of the cut).
- Shear strength **τ** is constant along the failure plane.

### Geometry Setup

Consider a single elemental strip of the cut edge. The punch pushes down with force **P**. The sheet of thickness **t** fails along an inclined plane of length **l**:

$$l = \frac{t}{\sin\phi}$$

The cross-sectional area of the inclined shear plane (per unit length of cut) is:

$$A_{shear} = \frac{t}{\sin\phi} \cdot 1 = \frac{t}{\sin\phi}$$

### Force Balance

The punch force must overcome shear stress over this plane:

$$P = \tau \cdot A_{shear} \cdot L_{perimeter}$$

$$P = \tau \cdot L \cdot \frac{t}{\sin\phi}$$

where **L** is the perimeter of the cut. Using **τ = σ_s (shear strength)** and the trig identity **1/sin φ = csc φ**:

$$P = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_s \cdot \csc\phi$$

### Differentiation to Find Maximum Force

To find the **worst-case (peak) force**, differentiate with respect to **φ** and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{d\phi} = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_s \cdot (-\csc\phi \cdot \cot\phi) = 0$$

This equation equals zero when **cos φ = 0**, i.e., **φ = 90°** — but this corresponds to vertical shearing only, NOT a maximum. We must examine the behaviour: as **φ → 0**, the shear plane becomes very long, and the resisting shear stress component projected vertically becomes:

$$P = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_s \cdot \frac{1}{\tan\phi} = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_s \cdot \cot\phi$$

Wait — careful with the failure direction. The actual projection onto the punch force axis:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_s}{2 \cdot \tan\phi}}$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dP}{d\phi} = -\frac{L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_s}{2} \cdot \csc^2\phi$$

Since this is always negative, **P is maximum when φ is minimum**. The smallest feasible φ is governed by ductility — typically **φ = 5° to 8°** for ductile materials.

Substituting the minimum practical value **φ = 0°** as the upper-bound limit:

$$\lim_{\phi \to 0}\ P = \frac{L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_s}{2 \cdot \tan\phi} \to \infty$$

Hence, in **practice**, we use empirical values:
- For **soft ductile material** (aluminium, copper): **φ ≈ 5°–8°**
- For **medium steel**: **φ ≈ 8°–12°**

A **standard design value** used in KTU problems is **φ = 8°**, giving:

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_s}{2 \cdot \tan 8°} \approx 3.56 \cdot L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **penetration force** equation — the peak force needed to push the punch through the sheet. It is **NOT** the simple L·t·σ_s force (which is the shear force when fracture has already occurred along a vertical plane). KTU questions frequently ask for both.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Punch and Die Design

### Problem Statement
Design a punch and die to **blank** a circular washer of outer diameter **40 mm** and inner diameter **22 mm** from a **2 mm thick mild steel sheet**. The shear strength of mild steel is **320 MPa**. Take tolerance = **±0.025 mm**.

### Step 1 — Determine Clearances

Using the **percentage rule** (mild steel ⇒ c = **6 %** of t):

$$c = 0.06 \times 2 = 0.12\ \text{mm (per side)}$$

### Step 2 — Design the Outer Circle (Blanking — outer diameter is the blank's outside)

The outer **die** fixes the outer blank size:

$$D_{die,\ outer} = 40.000 + 0.025 = 40.025\ \text{mm}$$

$$D_{punch,\ outer} = 40.025 - 2 \times 0.12 = 40.025 - 0.24 = 39.785\ \text{mm}$$

### Step 3 — Design the Inner Circle (Punching — inner diameter is a hole)

The inner **punch** fixes the inner hole size:

$$d_{punch,\ inner} = 22.000 - 0.025 = 21.975\ \text{mm}$$

$$d_{die,\ inner} = 21.975 + 2 \times 0.12 = 21.975 + 0.24 = 22.215\ \text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **opposite sign conventions** — outer uses die as the sizing member (with **+** tolerance), inner uses punch as the sizing member (with **−** tolerance). This is a classic KTU trap.

### Step 4 — Calculate Shear Force

Perimeter of the cut **L** = circumference of outer + circumference of inner:

$$L = \pi \cdot (40 + 22) = \pi \cdot 62 = 194.78\ \text{mm}$$

Shear force:

$$P_{shear} = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_s = 194.78 \times 2 \times 320$$

$$P_{shear} = 124659.2\ \text{N} \approx 124.66\ \text{kN}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Maximum Penetration Force (φ = 8°)

$$P_{max} = \frac{L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_s}{2 \cdot \tan 8°}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{194.78 \times 4 \times 320}{2 \times 0.1405}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{249318.4}{0.281} = 887253\ \text{N} \approx 887.25\ \text{kN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students often quote only one of these two forces. The **correct answer for press tonnage selection is P_max**, not P_shear. The press must be capable of P_max.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| Question Asked | Use This Equation | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Find die size for blanking | Die = Blank size + tol. | Die is the sizing member |
| Find punch size for blanking | Punch = Die − 2c | Punch is smaller |
| Find punch size for punching | Punch = Hole size − tol. | Punch is the sizing member |
| Find die size for punching | Die = Punch + 2c | Die is larger |
| Find press tonnage | P_max formula | Worst-case penetration |
| Find strip width (with bridge) | W = D + 2b + tolerance | b = bridge / margin width |
| Find centre of pressure | Weighted centroid | For aligning compound dies |

## 3.4 Derivation of Centre of Pressure

For a compound die performing multiple operations, the resultant cutting force must pass through the **press ram centre**, otherwise tilting and uneven wear occur. The **centre of pressure (CP)** is the centroid of all simultaneous cuts:

$$x_{CP} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (L_i \cdot x_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i}$$

$$y_{CP} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (L_i \cdot y_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i}$$

Each punch has cutting length **L_i** at location **(x_i, y_i)**. The die is positioned so that this CP coincides with the press ram axis. This is **directly asked** in KTU 14-mark questions on compound die design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tooling System for Blanking/Punching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sheet Stock] --> B[Stripper Plate]
    B --> C{Punch Descends}
    C --> D[Material Trapped Between Punch and Die]
    D --> E[Plastic Shear Zone Forms at φ degrees]
    E --> F[Fracture Initiates at Punch Edge]
    F --> G[Fracture Propagates Toward Die Edge]
    G --> H{Final Separation}
    H --> I[Blank Product Ejected Through Die]
    H --> J[Scrap Strip Pushed Up Stripper]
    J --> K[Cycle Repeats]
    I --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Clearance Effect on Cut Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Material Sheet Thickness t] --> B{Clearance Selection}
    B -->|c Too Small| C1[Secondary Shear]
    B -->|c Optimum 4-8 percent t| C2[Clean Vertical Cut]
    B -->|c Too Large| C3[Large Rollover and Burr]
    C1 --> D1[Double Burnish, High Force, Tool Damage]
    C2 --> D2[Single Burnish Band, Acceptable Burr, Long Tool Life]
    C3 --> D3[Tapered Cut, Loose Slug, Excessive Burr]
```

## 4.3 Architecture of Punch and Die Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph UpperShoe[Upper Shoe of Press]
        P1[Punch Holder]
        P2[Punch Shank]
        P3[Cutting Punch with Profile]
    end
    subgraph LowerShoe[Lower Shoe of Press]
        D1[Die Set Back Plate]
        D2[Die Plate with Profile Opening]
        D3[Stripper Plate with Guide Bushings]
        D4[Backup Plate]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 -->|Enters Die with clearance 2c| D2
    D4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D3 -->|Guides Strip and ejects scrap upward| P3
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Blanking vs Punching Sizing Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the cut piece the useful product or scrap?}
    Q1 -->|Useful Product| R1[BLANKING]
    Q1 -->|Scrap Slug| R2[PUCHING]
    R1 --> S1[Die fixes Blank Size]
    R1 --> S2[Punch equals Die minus 2c]
    R2 --> T1[Punch fixes Hole Size]
    R2 --> T2[Die equals Punch plus 2c]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams represent the **functional architecture** of the tooling system — the physical arrangement of components in a press tool. In KTU theory questions, students are expected to **draw and label** such diagrams by hand.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between blanking and punching with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Blanking:** The operation in which the punch and die are used to cut a workpiece from the stock such that the **cut piece is the desired product** and the remaining stock becomes scrap.
- **Punching:** The operation in which the punch and die are used to make a hole in the stock such that the **separated piece (slug) is the scrap** and the remaining stock is the desired product.

Mechanically, both are identical shearing operations. The distinction lies only in **what is being kept as the product**. Blanking uses a die whose opening matches the blank shape; punching uses a punch whose profile matches the hole shape.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define clearance in blanking. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

**Clearance (C)** is the intentional radial gap provided between the punch and die walls during blanking/punching, expressed either as **total gap (2c)** or **per-side gap (c)**.

Importance:
1. It allows cracks initiated at the punch edge and die edge to **meet at the centre** of the sheet thickness, producing a clean cut.
2. It reduces the **punching force** required.
3. It prevents **secondary shear** and **tool damage**.
4. It ensures good **dimensional accuracy** and minimum burr.
5. Optimum clearance per side is typically **3 % to 10 %** of sheet thickness, depending on material.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End Semester Examinations follow the **module-internal choice** pattern. Either Question A OR Question B must be attempted. Both alternatives are given below.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) Derive an expression for the maximum punching force, considering a single shear plane inclined at angle φ. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider the punch pushing through a sheet of thickness **t**. Shear failure occurs along a plane inclined at angle **φ** from the horizontal. The length of the shear plane per unit perimeter is:

$$l = \frac{t}{\sin\phi}$$

The cross-sectional area of this plane (per unit length of cut perimeter **L**) is:

$$A = l \cdot 1 = \frac{t}{\sin\phi}$$

The vertical component of the shear resistance is the punch force:

$$P = \tau \cdot A \cdot \cos\phi \cdot L$$

Simplifying with **τ = σ_s** (shear strength) and using trig identities:

$$P = \sigma_s \cdot L \cdot \frac{t \cdot \cos\phi}{\sin\phi} = \sigma_s \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \cot\phi$$

This is valid for a single inclined plane. For ductile materials where fracture actually proceeds by **two shear planes symmetrically inclined**, the integrated form becomes:

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_s}{2 \cdot \tan\phi}}$$

The maximum force is reached at the minimum practical angle **φ = 8°**, giving the design equation used in press selection.

**(b) A circular disc of diameter 60 mm is to be blanked from a 3 mm thick aluminium sheet. The shear strength of aluminium is 110 MPa. Determine the punch and die sizes. Also calculate the maximum blanking force. Assume clearance = 6 % of thickness per side. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Clearance per side:**

$$c = 0.06 \times 3 = 0.18\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Die size (sizing member for blanking):**

$$D_{die} = 60 + 0.025 = 60.025\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Punch size:**

$$D_{punch} = D_{die} - 2c = 60.025 - 0.36 = 59.665\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Perimeter of cut:**

$$L = \pi \cdot D = \pi \cdot 60 = 188.496\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum blanking force (φ = 8°, tan 8° = 0.1405):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_s}{2 \cdot \tan\phi}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{188.496 \times 9 \times 110}{2 \times 0.1405}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{186611.04}{0.281} = 664096\ \text{N} \approx 664.1\ \text{kN}$$

**Final Answers:** Die = 60.025 mm, Punch = 59.665 mm, P_max ≈ 664.1 kN.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) Explain the design procedure for punch and die for blanking and punching operations with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**General Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Identify Operation:**
- If the cut piece is the product → **Blanking**.
- If the slug is scrap → **Punching**.

**Step 2 — Select Clearance:**
Use either the **percentage rule** (c = 0.04·t to 0.10·t) or **Sieber's formula** (c = 0.04·t·√σ_s in kgf/mm² units).

**Step 3 — Assign Sizing Member:**
- **Blanking:** Die = Required size ± tolerance.
- **Punching:** Punch = Required size ± tolerance.

**Step 4 — Compute the Mating Member:**
- **Blanking:** Punch = Die − 2c.
- **Punching:** Die = Punch + 2c.

**Step 5 — Check Force and Press Capacity:**
Calculate P_max using **P_max = (L·t²·σ_s) / (2·tan φ)** and verify press tonnage.

**Example — Blanking a 50 mm square from 1.5 mm mild steel sheet:**

Assume c = 6 % · t = 0.09 mm per side.

- Die size = **50.025 × 50.025 mm**
- Punch size = **49.845 × 49.845 mm**
- L = 4 × 50 = 200 mm
- σ_s = 320 MPa
- P_max = (200 × 1.5² × 320) / (2 × 0.1405) = 512,455 N ≈ 512.5 kN

**(b) For a compound die performing simultaneous blanking and piercing of the washer profile (Outer Ø = 50 mm, Inner Ø = 25 mm) from 2 mm thick steel (σ_s = 350 MPa), determine all punch and die dimensions, the resultant blanking force, and locate the centre of pressure assuming the washer is concentric. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Clearance:**

$$c = 0.06 \times 2 = 0.12\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Outer Profile (Blanking — Die is sizing member):**
- Die OD = 50.025 mm
- Punch OD = 50.025 − 0.24 = 49.785 mm

**Step 3 — Inner Profile (Punching — Punch is sizing member):**
- Punch ID = 25.000 − 0.025 = 24.975 mm
- Die ID = 24.975 + 0.24 = 25.215 mm

**Step 4 — Cutting Lengths:**

$$L_{outer} = \pi \times 50 = 157.08\ \text{mm}$$

$$L_{inner} = \pi \times 25 = 78.54\ \text{mm}$$

$$L_{total} = 157.08 + 78.54 = 235.62\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Blanking Force:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{235.62 \times 4 \times 350}{2 \times 0.1405} = \frac{329868}{0.281} = 1173900\ \text{N} \approx 1173.9\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 6 — Centre of Pressure:**

Since the part is concentric, both forces act along the **same axis**, so:

$$x_{CP} = x_{centre} = 0, \quad y_{CP} = y_{centre} = 0$$

**Final Answer:** All dimensions computed; P_max ≈ 1173.9 kN; CP = geometric centre.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Examiner's Note):**
> 1. **Unit confusion in Sieber's formula** — using σ_s in MPa without unit correction. Always convert to kgf/mm² OR use the SI form.
> 2. **Sign convention on sizing member** — students often give Punch = Die + 2c for blanking (wrong direction). Remember: in blanking, **die is bigger**, so **punch = die − 2c**.
> 3. **Forgetting tolerance** — even when c is correctly applied, omitting the ±0.025 mm manufacturing tolerance on the sizing member loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using P_shear instead of P_max** for press tonnage — P_shear is the post-fracture force, NOT the worst-case. Use P_max for press selection.
> 5. **Omitting the centre of pressure calculation** in compound-die problems — this is a guaranteed 3-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Punch and Die Design for Blanking and Punching**

- ✅ **Blanking vs Punching** — distinguished by **what is the product**, not by the mechanics.
- ✅ **Clearance per side (c)** — typically **3 % to 10 %** of thickness **t**; soft materials need more.
- ✅ **Sieber's formula:** c = **0.04·t·√σ_s** (σ_s in **kgf/mm²**, t in mm, c in mm).
- ✅ **BLANKING — sizing member = DIE:** Die = blank size + tol; Punch = Die − 2c.
- ✅ **PUNCHING — sizing member = PUNCH:** Punch = hole size − tol; Die = Punch + 2c.
- ✅ **Tolerance:** ±**0.025 mm** on the sizing member; never split the tolerance between punch and die.
- ✅ **Shear strength:** σ_s ≈ **0.8·σ_u**.
- ✅ **Shear force:** P_shear = **L·t·σ_s** (post-fracture force, used for stripping force).
- ✅ **Maximum punching force:** P_max = **L·t²·σ_s / (2·tan φ)**, with φ ≈ **8°** for design.
- ✅ **Optimum clearance** — produces a **single burnish band**, minimum rollover, and minimum burr.
- ✅ **Centre of pressure** — weighted centroid of cut perimeters; must coincide with press ram axis.
- ✅ **Die wear** — the sizing member wears first; for blanking, the die wears; for punching, the punch wears.
- ✅ **Cut surface zones** — rollover → burnish → fracture → burr (from top to bottom of the cut edge).
- ✅ **Strip layout** — affects **stock utilisation η**; bridge width ≈ **1.5·t** minimum.
- ✅ **Compound dies** — perform multiple operations in **one press stroke**; CP calculation is mandatory.

> **End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Punch and Die Design for Blanking and Punching**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Forming Operations** are defined as manufacturing processes in which the desired shape and geometry of a workpiece are obtained through **plastic deformation** of the material, without any significant change in mass or volume, and without material removal (as in machining) or addition (as in casting). These operations are classified under the broader domain of **Bulk Deformation Processes** and **Sheet Metal Forming Processes** as per the KTU 2024 Scheme.

In Module 2 of PCPET602, the term **"Operations"** encompasses the principal bulk and sheet metal forming processes used in the manufacturing industry. These include:

- **Bulk Forming Operations:** Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, and Drawing.
- **Sheet Metal Forming Operations:** Blanking, Piercing, Bending, Deep Drawing, and Spinning.

Each operation is characterized by a specific **stress state** (compressive, tensile, shear, or a combination), **strain rate**, **temperature regime** (cold, warm, or hot working), and **tool-workpiece interface** conditions.

## 1.2 Classification of Forming Operations

| Class | Operations | Typical Stress State | Working Temperature |
|:------|:-----------|:--------------------|:--------------------|
| Bulk Deformation | Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing | Compressive / Tensile | Hot / Cold |
| Sheet Metal Forming | Blanking, Piercing, Bending, Deep Drawing | Tensile / Shear / Compressive | Cold / Warm |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Clay Metaphor**
> Imagine you have a lump of **clay** in your hand. If you press it between your palms (compressive force), it flattens — this is **forging/rolling**. If you push the clay through a small hole in a die (forcing it to flow through a constrained orifice), it emerges as a long strip — this is **extrusion**. If you pull the clay through a smaller opening (tensile force pulling it down), it elongates and thins — this is **drawing**. If you stamp a flat sheet of clay with a cookie cutter to make a shape — this is **blanking/piercing**.

The key insight is that in **every** metal forming operation, the metal is made to **flow plastically** under controlled conditions, and the tool (die, punch, roller, mandrel) defines the final geometry.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering parameters and physical constants are foundational to all forming operations:

- **Young's Modulus (E):** ~200 GPa for steel, ~70 GPa for aluminum alloys
- **Yield Strength (Sy):** Material-dependent, typically 250–1000 MPa for steels
- **Flow Stress (Yf):** The instantaneous resistance to plastic deformation
- **Strain Hardening Exponent (n):** Ranges from 0.1 (mild steel) to 0.5 (austenitic stainless steel)
- **Strength Coefficient (K):** MPa, evaluated from log-log true stress-strain plots
- **Friction Coefficient (μ):** 0.05–0.15 (lubricated) and 0.2–0.5 (dry) conditions
- **Specific Heat (cp):** 486 J/kg·K for steel
- **Recrystallization Temperature:** Approximately 0.4 × T_melting (in Kelvin)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forging Operations

Forging is a bulk forming process where the workpiece is shaped by **localized compressive forces** applied via hammers, presses, or upsetters. The metal flows perpendicular to the die axis to fill the die cavity.

### 2.1.1 Types of Forging

- **Open-Die Forging:** Deformation occurs between flat or simple shaped dies; used for large workpieces (shafts, rings).
- **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging:** The workpiece is fully constrained within die cavities; produces complex 3D shapes.
- **Flashless Forging:** Precision forging with no flash formation; requires tight volume control.
- **Upset Forging:** Increasing the cross-sectional area by compressing axially.

### 2.1.2 Forging Force Analysis (Open-Die Upsetting)

When a cylindrical billet of height `h₀` and diameter `d₀` is upset between flat dies, the deformation is essentially **axisymmetric compression**. The forging force is given by:

$$F_{forging} = Y_f \cdot A \cdot (1 + \frac{\mu \cdot d}{3h})$$

where the factor `(1 + μd/3h)` is the **friction hill factor**, accounting for the barreling effect caused by friction at the die-workpiece interface.

### 2.1.3 Steps in Closed-Die Forging

1. **Billet preparation:** Cutting, heating, lubrication.
2. **Preforming / Blocking:** Rough shape in intermediate cavities.
3. **Finishing:** Final cavity imparts exact dimensions.
4. **Trimming:** Removal of flash in a separate trim die.
5. **Cooling and inspection.**

## 2.2 Rolling Operations

Rolling is the process of reducing the cross-sectional area of a workpiece by passing it between two **counter-rotating rolls**. It is the most widely used forming process for producing sheet, plate, foil, and structural sections.

### 2.2.1 Classification of Rolling

- **Hot Rolling:** Performed above the recrystallization temperature; coarse grain structure, lower force required.
- **Cold Rolling:** Performed at room temperature; better surface finish, higher strength due to strain hardening.
- **Flat Rolling:** Produces plates, sheets, strips.
- **Shape Rolling:** Produces I-beams, channels, rails.

### 2.2.2 Rolling Mechanics (Friction-Limited Entry)

The **maximum possible draft** (reduction per pass) is limited by the frictional force that can drag the workpiece into the roll gap:

$$\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 \cdot R$$

where R is the roll radius. If the actual draft `Δh` exceeds this, **slipping** occurs and the rolls cannot pull the stock.

### 2.2.3 Neutral Point and Forward Slip

A point on the roll surface has the same velocity as the workpiece — this is the **neutral point (no-slip point)**. Forward slip `s` is defined as:

$$s = \frac{v_f - v_r}{v_r}$$

where `v_f` is the exit velocity of the workpiece and `v_r` is the roll tangential velocity.

## 2.3 Extrusion Operations

Extrusion forces the workpiece (billet) to flow through a die orifice of smaller cross-section, producing a continuous product of constant cross-section.

### 2.3.1 Types of Extrusion

- **Direct (Forward) Extrusion:** Ram and product move in the same direction.
- **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion:** Ram and product move in opposite directions; lower friction losses.
- **Hydrostatic Extrusion:** Fluid pressure acts on the billet; eliminates die-billet friction.
- **Impact Extrusion:** High-velocity punch drives metal into a die cavity; common for collapsible tubes.

### 2.3.2 Extrusion Force (Slab Analysis)

For direct extrusion with no friction on the billet walls:

$$F_{extrusion} = A_0 \cdot Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

For direct extrusion **with** friction:

$$F_{extrusion} = A_0 \cdot Y_f \cdot \left[\ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + \frac{2L}{D_0}\right] \cdot Y_f \cdot \frac{\mu}{\tan(\alpha)}$$

where:
- `A₀` = original billet cross-sectional area
- `A_f` = final product cross-sectional area
- `L` = billet length
- `D₀` = initial billet diameter
- `α` = die semi-angle

## 2.4 Drawing Operations

Drawing is a tensile-compressive forming process where the workpiece is **pulled** through a die to reduce its cross-section.

### 2.4.1 Wire Drawing

$$F_{draw} = A_f \cdot Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot (1 + \frac{\mu}{\tan(\alpha)})$$

The maximum reduction per pass is limited to avoid tensile fracture. The **ideal work** (homogeneous deformation) is:

$$W_{ideal} = V \cdot Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

### 2.4.2 Tube Drawing

- **Sinking:** No internal support; simple but less precise.
- **Plug Drawing:** Internal mandrel controls inner diameter.
- **Mandrel Drawing:** Long mandrel fixed at one end; high precision.
- **Floating Plug:** Plug self-positions in deformation zone.

## 2.5 Sheet Metal Operations

### 2.5.1 Blanking and Piercing

- **Blanking:** The removed portion is the desired part; the remaining strip is scrap.
- **Piercing:** The remaining sheet is the desired part; the slug is scrap.
- Both use a **punch and die** with a clearance typically 5–10% of stock thickness.

### 2.5.2 Shear Force and Punch Force

$$F_{shear} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$$

where `L` is the perimeter of the cut, `t` is thickness, and `τ` is the shear strength of the material.

For piercing/blanking, the actual punch force is:

$$F_{punch} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_s$$

### 2.5.3 Bending

$$M_{bending} = \frac{Y_f \cdot b \cdot t^2}{4}$$

for pure bending of a sheet of width `b` and thickness `t`.

The **minimum bend radius** is approximately `r_min = 0.5 t` for ductile materials without cracking.

### 2.5.4 Deep Drawing

A flat sheet (blank) is drawn into a die cavity by a punch to form a cup-shaped part. The **limiting draw ratio (LDR)** is:

$$LDR = \frac{D_{max}}{d_p}$$

where `D_max` is the largest blank diameter that can be drawn successfully and `d_p` is the punch diameter. Typical LDR values:
- **Steel:** 2.0–2.2
- **Aluminum:** 1.8–2.0
- **Stainless Steel:** 2.0–2.4

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Operation | Key Formula | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|:----------|:------------|:----------|:--------------------|
| Forging (Upsetting) | $F = Y_f A (1 + \frac{\mu d}{3h})$ | F = forging force, A = contact area | Press tonnage selection |
| Rolling Draft Limit | $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$ | Δh = draft, R = roll radius | Maximum pass reduction |
| Roll Torque | $T = 0.5 F L$ | L = contact length | Drive motor sizing |
| Extrusion Force | $F = A_0 Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f)$ | A₀ = initial area, A_f = final area | Ram force design |
| Drawing Force | $F = A_f Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f)(1 + \mu/\tan\alpha)$ | α = die semi-angle | Capstan capacity |
| Shear / Punch | $F = L t \tau_s$ | L = perimeter, t = thickness, τ = shear stress | Press rating for blanking |
| Bending Moment | $M = \frac{Y_f b t^2}{4}$ | b = width, t = thickness | Bending force |
| Deep Draw LDR | $LDR = D_{max}/d_p$ | D = blank dia, d = punch dia | Drawability check |
| Strain (True) | $\epsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ | h = height | Deformation magnitude |
| Flow Stress | $Y_f = K \epsilon^n$ | K = strength coeff., n = strain hardening | Plasticity calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas assume **homogeneous deformation**, **isotropic material behavior**, and **steady-state process conditions**. Corrections for non-homogeneous flow, temperature effects, and complex die geometries require FEM (Finite Element Method) analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Forging Force in Open-Die Upsetting (Slab Method)

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a cylindrical billet of height `h` and diameter `d` compressed between two flat, parallel, rough dies. The friction at the die-workpiece interface produces a **barreling effect** that raises the pressure required for deformation. We apply the **slab method** (force-balance on an elemental slice).

### 3.1.2 Force Balance on an Element

Consider an elemental horizontal slice of thickness `dh` at height `z` from the bottom die. The horizontal pressure `p` varies with height due to friction drag at the die faces.

Sum of vertical forces on the element (using equilibrium and the Tresca / von Mises yield criterion):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{z} \cdot A &= (\sigma_{z} + d\sigma_{z}) \cdot A + \tau \cdot \pi d \cdot dz \\
\Rightarrow -A \cdot d\sigma_{z} &= \tau \cdot \pi d \cdot dz \\
\Rightarrow d\sigma_{z} &= -\frac{4\tau}{d} \cdot dz
\end{aligned}$$

Using the **friction shear stress** (full sticking friction): `τ = μ · σ_y` (Coulomb's law with the von Mises yield criterion).

### 3.1.3 Integration Across the Height

Integrate from the mid-plane (z = 0, where σ_z = σ_avg) to the die face (z = h/2):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{\sigma_{avg}}^{\sigma_{die}} d\sigma_z &= -\frac{4\mu Y_f}{d} \int_{0}^{h/2} dz \\
\sigma_{die} - \sigma_{avg} &= \frac{2\mu Y_f}{d} \cdot \frac{h}{1} \\
\sigma_{die} &= \sigma_{avg} + \frac{2\mu Y_f h}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

By symmetry, the average die pressure (friction hill pressure) is:

$$p_{avg} = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu d}{3h}\right)$$

The factor `μd/3h` is the **friction hill** (Sanders-Krall) correction. As h → 0, the pressure rises sharply (the classic forging friction hill).

### 3.1.4 Final Forging Force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{forging} &= p_{avg} \cdot A \\
\Rightarrow F_{forging} &= Y_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu d}{3h}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The first term is the homogeneous deformation pressure; the second term accounts for friction-induced barreling.

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Draft in Rolling (Friction Constraint)

### 3.2.1 Geometry of the Roll Bite

Let:
- `R` = roll radius
- `h₀` = entry thickness
- `h_f` = exit thickness
- `Δh = h₀ - h_f` = draft per pass
- `α` = contact angle (angle of bite)

The geometry gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\alpha) &= 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{D} \\
\Rightarrow \alpha &\approx \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}} \quad \text{(for small angles)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.2 Force Equilibrium on the Workpiece

The roll surface must be able to drag the workpiece into the roll gap via friction. The horizontal component of friction must equal or exceed the horizontal component of the radial pressure. For successful biting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu \cdot N &\geq N \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\
\mu &\geq \sin(\alpha) \approx \alpha \\
\mu &\geq \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}
\end{aligned}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$$

This is the **maximum permissible draft** per pass. If exceeded, the rolls "skid" and cannot pull the stock.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** A steel slab is rolled in a 2-high mill with rolls of diameter 500 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.20. Find the maximum possible draft per pass.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta h_{max} &= \mu^2 \cdot R \\
&= (0.20)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{0.500}{2}\right) \\
&= 0.04 \cdot 0.25 \\
&= 0.010 \, m = 10 \, mm
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum draft per pass is **10 mm**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Extrusion Force (Direct Extrusion, Slab Analysis)

### 3.3.1 Geometry and Stress State

A cylindrical billet of initial area `A₀` and length `L` is pushed by a ram through a conical die of semi-angle `α` to a final area `A_f`. The strain in the deformation zone is the **extrusion strain**:

$$\epsilon = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

### 3.3.2 Homogeneous Deformation Work

Using the constant-strain approximation:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ideal} &= A_0 \cdot Y_f \cdot \epsilon \\
&= A_0 \cdot Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.3 Friction Correction (Billet-Container Wall)

In direct extrusion, the billet slides against the container wall, producing an additional friction force:

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot p_{container} \cdot \pi D_0 L$$

Assuming the container pressure ≈ extrusion pressure `Y_f · ln(A₀/A_f)`:

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot \pi D_0 L$$

### 3.3.4 Total Extrusion Force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{extrusion,total} &= A_0 \cdot Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot \left[1 + \frac{2L}{D_0} \cdot \frac{\mu}{\tan(\alpha)}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

The bracketed term is the **extrusion efficiency correction factor**.

### 3.3.5 Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** A 6061-T6 aluminum billet of diameter 100 mm and length 300 mm is extruded through a die to produce a 25 mm diameter rod. Given `Y_f = 150 MPa`, `μ = 0.10`, `α = 45°`, calculate the extrusion force.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi (0.100)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2 \\
A_f &= \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2 \\
\ln(A_0/A_f) &= \ln(16) = 2.773
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ideal} &= 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 150 \times 10^6 \times 2.773 \\
&= 3.267 \times 10^6 \, N \approx 3.27 \, MN
\end{aligned}$$

Friction correction factor:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 + \frac{2L}{D_0} \cdot \frac{\mu}{\tan(\alpha)} &= 1 + \frac{2 \times 0.300}{0.100} \times \frac{0.10}{1.0} \\
&= 1 + 6.0 \times 0.10 = 1.6
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= 3.27 \times 1.6 = 5.23 \, MN
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The required ram force is **approximately 5.23 MN (~533 tonnes-force)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Operations

| Parameter | Forging | Rolling | Extrusion | Drawing | Blanking | Deep Drawing |
|:----------|:--------|:--------|:----------|:--------|:---------|:-------------|
| **Dominant Stress** | Compressive | Compressive | Compressive | Tensile | Shear | Tensile + Compressive |
| **Tool Type** | Hammer / Press | Counter-rotating rolls | Die + Ram | Die + Capstan | Punch + Die | Punch + Die + Blank holder |
| **Temp. Regime** | Hot / Cold | Hot / Cold | Hot / Cold | Cold | Cold | Cold |
| **Typical L/D Limit** | h/d < 2.5 | α < friction angle | L/D < 5:1 | ΔA/A < 30% | L/t = perimeter ratio | LDR < 2.2 |
| **Material Waste** | Flash (5–10%) | Negligible | Discard discard | None | Slug / Scrap | Trimming |
| **Key Force Formula** | $F = Y_f A(1 + \mu d/3h)$ | $F = Y_f L w$ | $F = A_0 Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f)$ | $F = A_f Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f)(1 + \mu/\tan\alpha)$ | $F = L t \tau_s$ | $F \approx \pi d_p t Y_f (LDR)$ |
| **Failure Mode** | Buckling / Fold | Roll slip | Centre burst / Piping | Tensile fracture | Burr / Crack | Wrinkling / Tearing |
