# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define High Energy Rate Forming (HERF). List any three HERF processes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — board-exam standard):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** High Energy Rate Forming is a class of non-conventional metal forming processes in which the workpiece is deformed by the **sudden release of a large amount of energy in a very short time interval (microseconds to milliseconds)**, producing a high-amplitude shock pressure pulse that does the forming work.
>
> **Three HERF processes (2 marks — 1 each, 0.5 deduction for spelling errors):**
> 1. **Explosive Forming (EF)** — chemical energy of detonation.
> 2. **Electrohydraulic Forming (EHF)** — electrical energy released as a plasma-driven shock in water.
> 3. **Electromagnetic Forming (EMF / Magneform)** — electrical energy released as a pulsed magnetic field.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State the typical strain-rate range in HERF and explain why HERF improves the formability of normally-brittle materials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Strain-rate range (1 mark):** HERF operates in the strain-rate range $\dot{\varepsilon} = 10^{2} \text{ to } 10^{4}\ \text{s}^{-1}$, in contrast to conventional forming at $\dot{\varepsilon} = 10^{-4} \text{ to } 10^{0}\ \text{s}^{-1}$.
>
> **Formability improvement (2 marks):** At high strain rates the **flow stress increases** (per the Cowper–Symonds equation) but, more importantly, the **ductility / fracture strain of many alloys also increases** because the microstructural damage mechanisms (void nucleation, grain-boundary sliding) do not have time to activate. The workpiece thus absorbs more plastic strain before fracture, allowing brittle materials such as **tungsten, magnesium alloys, and explosively-clad sheets** to be formed without cracking.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on HERF Questions:**
> 1. **No diagram (-2 marks):** A labelled schematic is *mandatory* for any 7-mark sub-part. Hand-drawn or typed — both are accepted.
> 2. **No formula statement (-1 mark):** Always write the formula symbolically *before* substituting numbers.
> 3. **Missing unit in the final answer (-1 mark):** "P = 73.6" loses a mark; "P = 73.6 MPa" is correct.
> 4. **Confusing EHF with EMF (-2 marks):** EHF uses a *water-plasma* shock; EMF uses a *magnetic-field* force. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **Quoting peak pressure without a process reference (-1 mark):** Always state the *process* (e.g., "For underwater explosive forming at 250 mm standoff…") before quoting the number.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of Explosive Forming (Underwater Configuration)**. Derive an expression for the **peak shock pressure in water** as a function of standoff distance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An explosive charge releases **75 kJ** of chemical energy in water. Estimate the **peak shock pressure at a standoff distance of 300 mm** from the detonation. Take $C_w = 1500$ m/s, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³. Compare your result with the C-J detonation pressure of TNT ($\rho_0 = 1600$ kg/m³, $D = 6900$ m/s, $\gamma = 1.30$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Working Principle + Derivation (7 marks: 4 + 3)

**Schematic (1 mark):** Draw the arrangement — see the underwater EF diagram in Section 4.3 (charge, water tank, blank, die, vacuum).

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

- A **measured mass of high explosive** is suspended in a water tank at a precisely controlled **standoff distance $R$** above a sheet-metal blank clamped over a **female die**.
- Air is evacuated from below the blank (vacuum draw).
- A **detonator** initiates the explosive; the detonation front propagates at velocity $D$.
- The detonation releases a large quantity of chemical energy as a **Chapman–Jouguet shock wave** with pressure $P_{CJ}$.
- The shock wave expands spherically in water, transmitting pressure to the blank.
- The blank is **accelerated to 50 – 200 m/s** in microseconds and impacts the die, undergoing **plastic deformation at strain rates $10^{2} – 10^{4}$ s⁻¹**.
- The deformed blank conforms to the female die cavity; spring-back is minimal because of post-impact plastic flow.

**Derivation (3 marks — Step breakdown as in Section 3.1):**

Energy balance + strong-shock approximation →

$$P_{peak}(R) = C_{w}\sqrt{\frac{3\,\rho_{w}\,E_{det}}{2\pi\,R^{3}}}$$

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3)

**Sub-question (b.1) — Peak shock in water (2 marks):**

$$P_{peak} = 1500 \times \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 1000 \times 75\,000}{2\pi \times (0.300)^{3}}}$$

$$= 1500 \times \sqrt{\frac{2.25 \times 10^{8}}{2\pi \times 0.027}}$$

$$= 1500 \times \sqrt{\frac{2.25 \times 10^{8}}{0.16965}}$$

$$= 1500 \times \sqrt{1.326 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$= 1500 \times 3.642 \times 10^{4}$$

$$= 5.463 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa} \approx 54.63\ \text{MPa}$$

**Sub-question (b.2) — C-J detonation pressure of TNT (2 marks):**

$$P_{CJ} = \frac{1600 \times (6900)^{2}}{1.30 + 1} = \frac{1600 \times 4.761 \times 10^{7}}{2.30}$$

$$= \frac{7.618 \times 10^{10}}{2.30} = 3.312 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Pa} \approx 33.12\ \text{GPa}$$

**Sub-question (b.3) — Comparison (3 marks):**

The C-J detonation pressure ($\approx 33$ GPa) is **three orders of magnitude higher** than the peak pressure in water at 300 mm ($\approx 55$ MPa). The shock has decayed by a factor of $33\,000/55 \approx 600\times$ over the 300 mm standoff, consistent with the $R^{-3/2}$ geometric decay law. The water-standoff configuration is therefore necessary to *protect* the workpiece from the raw detonation pressure (which would shatter it) while still delivering sufficient pressure for forming — confirming the design intent of HERF.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **working principle of Electromagnetic Forming (EMF)** with a labelled schematic. Derive the expression for **magnetic pressure** on the workpiece surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EMF coil generates a pulsed magnetic field of **25 T** at the workpiece surface. Calculate the **magnetic pressure** acting on the workpiece. If the capacitor bank stores **40 kJ** at 10 kV and the process delivers energy to the workpiece with an **overall efficiency of 8%**, estimate the **impact velocity of a 0.4 kg aluminium sheet** initially 2 mm thick. Hence determine the **average strain rate**. Take $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Working Principle + Derivation (7 marks: 3 + 2 + 2)

**Schematic (1 mark):** Reference Section 4.4 — show the capacitor, switch, work coil, pulsed field, eddy currents in workpiece, and the die.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

- A **capacitor bank** is charged to a high DC voltage and then **discharged** through a work coil by a triggered switch.
- The fast-rising current (di/dt $\sim 10^{10}$ A/s) produces a **time-varying magnetic field** $B(t)$ around the coil.
- The field is *concentrated* in the small gap between the coil and the conductive workpiece.
- By **Faraday's law**, the time-varying flux induces **eddy currents** $J$ in the workpiece (Lenz's law — opposing the change).
- The interaction of the coil's primary current and the workpiece's induced eddy currents produces a **repulsive Lorentz force** $F = J \times B$ that **accelerates the workpiece away from the coil**.

**Derivation (2 + 2 = 4 marks):**

Starting from the Maxwell stress tensor (Section 3.2) and applying the EMF boundary conditions ($B_t = 0$, $B_n = B$, perfect eddy-current shielding), we get:

$$P_{mag} = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$$

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2)

**Sub-question (b.1) — Magnetic pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_{mag} = \frac{(25)^{2}}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{625}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= 2.487 \times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa} \approx 248.7\ \text{MPa}$$

**Sub-question (b.2) — Impact velocity (3 marks):**

Energy delivered to workpiece: $\eta E_{cap} = 0.08 \times 40\,000 = 3200$ J.

$$v_w = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 3200}{0.4}} = \sqrt{16\,000} \approx 126.5\ \text{m/s}$$

**Sub-question (b.3) — Average strain rate (2 marks):**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{v_w}{h_0} = \frac{126.5}{0.002} = 6.32 \times 10^{4}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

This is two orders of magnitude above the HERF threshold, confirming that the process is firmly in the high-energy-rate regime.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in (b.2):** Some students use $E_{cap} = 0.5 CV^{2}$ to *verify* the storage, but they then forget to apply $\eta$ when computing $v_w$. The KTU valuation key explicitly penalises this oversight. Always: *Storage energy → efficiency → kinetic energy → velocity*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Sentence Module-2 Cheat Sheet (Memorize These Verbatim for KTU 2024):**
> 1. HERF releases a large quantity of energy in **microseconds to milliseconds**, generating shock pressures of **10² – 10⁴ MPa** and strain rates of **10² – 10⁴ s⁻¹**.
> 2. The three HERF variants are **Explosive Forming** (chemical), **Electrohydraulic Forming** (electrical via plasma in water), and **Electromagnetic Forming** (electrical via magnetic field).
> 3. The **C-J detonation pressure** for an explosive is $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^{2}/(\gamma+1)$; it decays as **$R^{-3/2}$** (or empirically $R^{-1.0}$–$R^{-1.2}$) with standoff in water.
> 4. **EHF** stores energy in a capacitor ($E = 0.5 CV^{2}$), discharges it across a water spark gap, and uses the **plasma-bubble shock** to form the workpiece.
> 5. **EMF** uses **Maxwell-stress-driven magnetic pressure** $P_{mag} = B^{2}/(2\mu_0)$ and requires the workpiece to be **electrically conductive**; it needs no physical pressure medium.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonics for the KTU Board Exam:**
> - **"C-V-L-I"** for energy-storage formulas: **C**apacitor ($0.5CV^{2}$), **C**oil ($0.5LI^{2}$), **K**inetic ($0.5mv^{2}$).
> - **"No Medium = No Meddle"** — Only EMF works in vacuum; the other two *need* a physical medium.
> - **"EF for big, EMF for small, EHF for medium"** — practical process-selection rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision Cross-References:**
> - Strain-rate definition: $\dot{\varepsilon} = v/h_0$ — used in **every** HERF problem.
> - Cowper–Symonds equation: $\sigma_f = \sigma_{f,0}(1 + \lambda \ln \dot{\varepsilon})$ — explains *why* formability improves at high $\dot{\varepsilon}$.
> - Spring-back rule: $S_{b} \propto 1/\dot{\varepsilon}$ — therefore HERF → minimal spring-back.
> - Process efficiency ordering (typical): **EMF ($\eta \sim 20\%$) > EHF ($\eta \sim 5$–$10\%$) > EF ($\eta \sim 1$–$3\%$)** — a frequently-asked 3-mark comparison question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Metal Forming** is a manufacturing process group in which the desired shape and dimensions of a workpiece are obtained through **plastic deformation** of the material, without any intentional change in mass or volume, by the application of suitable external forces (compressive, tensile, or shear) that exceed the yield strength of the material.

**Characteristic Features of Metal Forming** are the inherent, distinctive attributes that collectively define the behaviour, mechanics, and outcome of a metal forming operation. They distinguish forming from other manufacturing processes (such as casting or machining) and govern the selection of process parameters, tooling, lubrication, and machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "The characteristic features of metal forming are the fundamental properties of the deformation process — including plastic flow, mass/volume conservation, predominance of compressive stresses, strain hardening, anisotropy due to grain flow, generation of residual stresses, and the strong influence of friction, temperature, and strain rate — that must be understood before any tooling or process design can be attempted."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Reader

Imagine you have a lump of **playdough** (clay) in your hand. You can:

- Squeeze it between your palms → it flattens but the **total amount stays the same**.
- Roll it into a long snake → it stretches, but **nothing is added or removed**.
- Press a cookie cutter on it → you deform the surface, but the **piece just below the cutter flows sideways**.

Now, contrast this with using a **knife** on a carrot: the knife *removes* material, the carrot loses mass, and chips are produced. **Metal forming is the playdough, not the knife.** The metal is *persuaded* to flow into a new shape rather than *cut away*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** In metal forming, *the shape changes, the mass never does.* This is the single most defining feature — the law of **volume constancy** — and every other characteristic flows from it.

## 1.3 The Ten Core Characteristic Features (Master List)

The KTU 2024 scheme groups the characteristic features of metal forming into the following ten fundamental properties:

1. **Plastic Deformation** — deformation beyond the elastic limit, permanent in nature.
2. **Mass / Volume Constancy** — no material is added or removed.
3. **Predominance of Compressive Stresses** — most forming operations apply squeezing forces.
4. **Yielding of the Material** — stress must exceed the yield stress to initiate flow.
5. **Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)** — flow stress rises as deformation accumulates.
6. **Grain Flow and Directional Properties** — fibrous grain structure develops, giving anisotropy.
7. **Residual Stresses** — locked-in elastic stresses remain after the load is removed.
8. **Friction at Tool–Workpiece Interface** — strongly influences load, surface finish, and flow.
9. **Temperature and Strain-Rate Sensitivity** — flow stress depends strongly on $T$ and $\dot{\varepsilon}$.
10. **No Change in Composition / Weight** — mechanical working, not chemical or material-additive.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "P-M-Y-S-G-R-F-T-C"** for the 10 features:
> **P**lastic, **M**ass constant, **Y**ielding, **S**train hardening, **G**rain flow, **R**esidual stress, **F**riction, **T**emperature, **C**ompressive — a simple memory hook for the KTU board exam.

These characteristics will be mathematically formalised in the next section.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feature 1 — Plastic Deformation

Plastic deformation is **permanent, non-recoverable** shape change that occurs when the applied stress crosses the **yield point** $Y$ of the material. Below the yield point, the strain is elastic and reversible. Above it, the material flows.

- Below yield: $\sigma < Y \Rightarrow$ elastic, recoverable.
- At/above yield: $\sigma \geq Y \Rightarrow$ plastic, permanent.

The deformation is governed by the **flow curve** of the material, which is a plot of true stress versus true strain.

> [!NOTE]
> The "yield stress" in cold working is the **initial yield strength** $Y_0$. The "flow stress" $Y_f$ is the *instantaneous* resistance offered by the material *during* plastic flow — it changes with strain due to work hardening.

## 2.2 Feature 2 — Volume / Mass Constancy

For an incompressible plastic solid (most metals behave as incompressible during plastic flow because volume change is < 0.1 %), the volume is conserved:

$$V_0 = V_f$$

For a generic body,

$$A_0 \, L_0 = A_f \, L_f$$

For axisymmetric upsetting of a cylinder (height $h$, diameter $d$):

$$\frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 \, h_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 \, h_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_f = d_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}}$$

This single equation is the **backbone** of every bulk forming analysis and appears in nearly every KTU 14-mark problem.

## 2.3 Feature 3 — Predominance of Compressive Stresses

Although some forming operations use tension (e.g., wire drawing, stretch forming) and some use shear (e.g., blanking), the **majority** of forming processes — forging, rolling, extrusion, deep drawing — rely on **compressive** stresses because:

- Compressive loading suppresses fracture and cracks.
- Hydrostatic compressive stress promotes ductility.
- Compressive flow stress is the highest resistance the material can offer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For bulk forming (forging, extrusion, rolling):** state of stress is essentially **compressive + hydrostatic**.
> **For sheet forming (deep drawing, bending):** state of stress is **tensile on outer fibres, compressive on inner fibres**.

## 2.4 Feature 4 — Yielding (Initiation of Plastic Flow)

Plastic flow begins when the stress state satisfies a **yield criterion**. For ductile metals, the two most common criteria are:

**Tresca Criterion (Maximum Shear Stress):**

$$Y = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$$

**Von Mises Criterion (Distortion Energy):**

$$Y = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}$$

In **plain strain** (rolling, forging), Von Mises reduces to the simpler form $Y = 1.155 \, \sigma_y$ for uniaxial comparison.

## 2.5 Feature 5 — Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)

As the material deforms, its dislocation density increases, and the resistance to further deformation **rises**. The empirical power-law flow curve (Hollomon equation) is the most widely used:

$$Y_f = K \, \varepsilon^{\,n}$$

where:
- $Y_f$ = true flow stress
- $K$ = strength coefficient
- $n$ = strain-hardening exponent
- $\varepsilon$ = true strain

For mild steel, $n \approx 0.15$ to $0.25$. For perfectly plastic (no hardening), $n = 0$.

The **average flow stress** is required to estimate the forming load:

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f} \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} K \, \varepsilon^{\,n} \, d\varepsilon = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{1 + n}$$

## 2.6 Feature 6 — Grain Flow and Directional Properties

During plastic deformation, the individual grains of the polycrystalline metal **elongate** in the direction of metal flow. The grain boundaries align into a continuous fibrous pattern, called the **fibre flow** or **grain flow**.

- This gives **anisotropy**: strength, ductility, and toughness differ along versus across the fibre.
- It is *beneficial* for fatigue and impact resistance (e.g., forged crankshafts).
- It is *harmful* if subsequent machining cuts across the fibres.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks — *"Why are forged components stronger than cast components?"* The standard answer is: **grain flow alignment** in forging follows the contour of the part, eliminating weak grain boundaries perpendicular to the load.

## 2.7 Feature 7 — Residual Stresses

When the forming load is removed, elastic recovery occurs. Because the deformation is non-uniform (e.g., die corners are more heavily worked than the bulk), the elastic recovery is also non-uniform. This locks in **residual stresses**:

- **Tensile residual stresses** on the surface → promote fatigue cracks and stress-corrosion.
- **Compressive residual stresses** below the surface → beneficial, used in **shot peening** and **autofrettage** of pressure vessels.

Residual stresses can be relieved by **stress-relief annealing** (heating below the recrystallisation temperature).

## 2.8 Feature 8 — Friction at the Tool–Workpiece Interface

Friction is **the single most troublesome feature** in bulk forming because:

- It increases the forming load.
- It causes non-uniform deformation (barreling in upsetting).
- It wears the dies.
- It influences surface finish.

Friction is quantified by the **friction factor** $m$ and the **friction shear stress** $\tau$:

$$m = \frac{\tau}{Y_f} \quad \text{(range 0 to 0.5)}$$

The interface condition is expressed by the **slip-line field** and **upper-bound** theorems; for simple upsetting, the friction hill increases the pressure:

$$p = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{m \, d}{3 \, h}\right)$$

This is the famous **Sieveka / friction-hill equation**, which frequently appears in KTU numericals.

## 2.9 Feature 9 — Temperature and Strain-Rate Sensitivity

The flow stress depends strongly on temperature and strain rate, summarised by the **constitutive equation**:

$$Y_f = K \, \varepsilon^{\,n} \, \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m} \, e^{Q/RT}$$

| Working Regime | Temperature Range | Strain Rate Range | Effect on $Y_f$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Working** | $T < 0.3 \, T_m$ | Low–Moderate | $Y_f$ **high**, surface finish good |
| **Warm Working** | $0.3 \, T_m < T < 0.5 \, T_m$ | Moderate | $Y_f$ moderate, less cracking |
| **Hot Working** | $T > 0.5 \, T_m$ | High | $Y_f$ **low**, no work hardening (dynamic recovery/recrystallisation) |

where $T_m$ = melting point in Kelvin, $\dot{\varepsilon}$ = strain rate, $m$ = strain-rate sensitivity, $Q$ = activation energy.

## 2.10 Feature 10 — No Change in Composition or Mass

Unlike casting (which involves solidification shrinkage) or machining (which removes chips), forming is a **purely mechanical** redistribution of mass. This is why:

- Weight is conserved → easy to estimate final dimensions from initial billet size.
- Composition is unchanged → alloy chemistry is preserved.
- No chips → high material utilisation (≥ 90–95 %).

## 2.11 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0 / h_f)$ | True strain in upsetting | dimensionless |
| 2 | $e = (h_0 - h_f)/h_0$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = -\ln(1 - e)$ | Convert engineering → true strain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ | Volume constancy | m³ |
| 5 | $d_f = d_0 \sqrt{h_0 / h_f}$ | Diameter after upsetting (axisymmetric) | m |
| 6 | $Y_f = K \, \varepsilon^{\,n}$ | Hollomon flow curve | Pa |
| 7 | $\bar{Y_f} = K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n} / (1+n)$ | Average flow stress | Pa |
| 8 | $F = \bar{Y_f} \cdot A_f \cdot K_f$ | Forming load (with shape factor $K_f$) | N |
| 9 | $p = Y_f (1 + m d / 3 h)$ | Friction-hill (Sieveka) pressure | Pa |
| 10 | $m = \tau / Y_f$ | Friction factor (0 to 0.5) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = d\varepsilon / dt$ | Strain rate | s⁻¹ |
| 12 | $Y_f = K \varepsilon^{\,n} \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m} e^{Q/RT}$ | Full constitutive equation | Pa |
| 13 | $P_{\text{forge}} = Y_f \, A$ (no friction) | Ideal forging load | N |
| 14 | $W = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \bar{Y_f} \, d\varepsilon \cdot V$ | Plastic deformation work | J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX note for KTU exam scripts:** Use $\vert$ (or `\mid`) instead of the bare pipe character `\vert x \vert` for absolute values inside text to avoid any typographical confusion with table separators.

## 2.12 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding these characteristics is the foundation of **process selection** in industry:

- **Aerospace forging (e.g., turbine disc):** exploits grain flow alignment → high fatigue life.
- **Automotive sheet stamping:** relies on strain-hardening exponent $n$ → uniform drawability.
- **Cold-heading of bolts:** uses volume constancy to predict final head dimensions.
- **Hot-rolling of steel slabs:** uses temperature sensitivity → low rolling loads at high $T$.
- **Friction hill analysis:** mandatory for press tonnage selection in forging.

These features are the *lingua franca* of forming-process design — every die, every press, and every lubricant choice is justified by one or more of these ten characteristics.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between True Strain and Engineering Strain

**Statement:** Show that $\varepsilon = -\ln(1 - e)$ where $\varepsilon$ is the true strain and $e$ is the engineering strain in a uniaxial upsetting operation.

**Step 1 — Definition of Engineering Strain.**

The engineering strain is the ratio of the *change* in height to the *original* height:

$$e = \frac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0} = 1 - \frac{h_f}{h_0}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrangement.**

Solve the above for the height ratio $h_f / h_0$:

$$\frac{h_f}{h_0} = 1 - e$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous (True) Strain Definition.**

True strain is defined as the *incremental* change in height divided by the *instantaneous* height, integrated over the deformation:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{h_0}^{h_f} \frac{dh}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluation of the Integral.**

$$\varepsilon = \Big[ \ln h \Big]_{h_0}^{h_f} = \ln h_f - \ln h_0 = \ln \left( \frac{h_f}{h_0} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Result from Step 2.**

$$\varepsilon = \ln (1 - e)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Sign Convention.**

Because $h_f < h_0$ in compression, the ratio $h_f / h_0 < 1$, so $\ln(h_f/h_0)$ is negative. To keep $\varepsilon$ positive (compressive strain convention), introduce the negative sign:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = -\ln(1 - e)}$$

**Step 7 — Verification.**

For $h_0 = 50$ mm, $h_f = 25$ mm:

$$e = \frac{50 - 25}{50} = 0.50$$

$$\varepsilon = -\ln(1 - 0.50) = -\ln(0.50) = 0.693$$

Check directly: $\varepsilon = \ln(50/25) = \ln 2 = 0.693$ ✓.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friction-Hill (Sieveka) Equation for Upsetting

**Statement:** For a cylindrical billet of diameter $d$ and height $h$ being upset with friction factor $m$ at the die interface, derive the average pressure $p$ required:

$$p = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{m \, d}{3 \, h}\right)$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Cylinder of initial diameter $d$ and current height $h$ (homogeneous deformation; friction causes barreling but we average).
2. Constant friction factor $m$ at the interfaces (top and bottom).
3. Plane-strain compression slipline field approximation.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Setup.**

Consider a vertical slice through the cylinder. Let the radial pressure at radius $r$ be $p(r)$ and the axial pressure be $p_z = p$. The friction shear stress at the die face is $\tau = m \, Y_f$ (Coulomb-friction in fully plastic regime).

**Step 2 — Slab Analysis on an Annular Element.**

Take an annular ring of inner radius $r$, outer radius $r + dr$, and height $h$. Force balance in the vertical direction:

$$p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr + dF_{\text{friction, outer}} = (p(r) + dp) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Friction Contribution.**

The friction force acts on the die face *above* the annular element. The friction force is $\tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$, opposing the downward motion. Slab equilibrium in the vertical direction (subtracting friction that resists downward flow):

$$-dp \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \cdot \frac{dr}{h}$$

Wait — the proper derivation (omitting full slipline for brevity) proceeds via the **slipline field** for plane strain, giving the pressure distribution:

$$p(r) = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{m}{h}(d - 2r)\right)$$

**Step 4 — Average Pressure.**

Average the local pressure over the die face area $A = \pi d^2 / 4$:

$$p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{A} \int_0^{d/2} p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Substitute $p(r)$ and integrate:

$$p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\pi d^2/4} \int_0^{d/2} Y_f \left(1 + \frac{m}{h}(d - 2r)\right) 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{8 Y_f}{d^2} \int_0^{d/2} \left(r + \frac{m}{h} (d r - 2r^2)\right) dr$$

**Step 5 — Term-by-Term Integration.**

$$\int_0^{d/2} r \, dr = \frac{d^2}{8}$$

$$\int_0^{d/2} d \, r \, dr = d \cdot \frac{d^2}{8} = \frac{d^3}{8}$$

$$\int_0^{d/2} 2 r^2 \, dr = 2 \cdot \frac{d^3}{24} = \frac{d^3}{12}$$

**Step 6 — Assemble.**

$$p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{8 Y_f}{d^2} \left[\frac{d^2}{8} + \frac{m}{h}\left(\frac{d^3}{8} - \frac{d^3}{12}\right)\right]$$

$$p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{8 Y_f}{d^2} \left[\frac{d^2}{8} + \frac{m}{h} \cdot \frac{d^3}{24}\right]$$

$$p_{\text{avg}} = Y_f \left[1 + \frac{m \, d}{3 \, h}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{p = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{m \, d}{3 \, h}\right)}$$

**Step 8 — Forming Load.**

Multiply the average pressure by the contact area $A = \pi d^2 / 4$:

$$F = p \cdot A = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{m \, d}{3 \, h}\right) \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Verification:** If $m = 0$ (perfectly lubricated), $p = Y_f$ — the result reduces to **ideal homogeneous deformation**, which is correct.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Flow Stress from the Hollomon Equation

**Statement:** Show that for a material obeying $Y_f = K \varepsilon^{\,n}$, the average flow stress over a strain interval $[0, \varepsilon_f]$ is

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{1 + n}$$

**Step 1 — Definition of Average.**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f} \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} Y_f(\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hollomon Law.**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f} \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K \varepsilon^{\,n} \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral.**

$$\int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K \varepsilon^{\,n} \, d\varepsilon = K \cdot \frac{\varepsilon^{\,n+1}}{n+1} \Big|_0^{\varepsilon_f} = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n+1}}{n+1}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $\varepsilon_f$.**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f} \cdot \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n+1}}{n+1} = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{n+1}$$

**Step 5 — Final.**

$$\boxed{\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{1 + n}}$$

**Note:** For $n = 0$ (perfectly plastic, no hardening), $\bar{Y_f} = K = Y_0$, as expected.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Upsetting of a Steel Cylinder

**Problem:** A cylindrical steel billet of $d_0 = 40$ mm, $h_0 = 60$ mm is upset in a press to $h_f = 30$ mm. The flow curve of the steel is $Y_f = 600 \, \varepsilon^{0.20}$ MPa. Friction factor $m = 0.20$. Determine:
(a) True strain, (b) Final diameter (volume constancy), (c) Final flow stress, (d) Average flow stress, (e) Friction-hill pressure, (f) Forging load.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — True strain.**

$$\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{60}{30}\right) = \ln 2 = 0.6931$$

**Step (b) — Final diameter.**

$$d_f = d_0 \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}} = 40 \sqrt{\frac{60}{30}} = 40 \sqrt{2} = 56.57 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (c) — Final flow stress.**

$$Y_f = 600 \times (0.6931)^{0.20}$$

Compute $(0.6931)^{0.20}$:

$$\ln(0.6931) = -0.3665$$

$$0.20 \times (-0.3665) = -0.0733$$

$$e^{-0.0733} = 0.9293$$

$$Y_f = 600 \times 0.9293 = 557.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (d) — Average flow stress.**

$$\bar{Y_f} = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{1 + n} = \frac{600 \times (0.6931)^{0.20}}{1.20} = \frac{557.6}{1.20} = 464.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (e) — Friction-hill pressure.**

$$p = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{m \, d_f}{3 \, h_f}\right) = 557.6 \left(1 + \frac{0.20 \times 56.57}{3 \times 30}\right)$$

$$p = 557.6 \left(1 + \frac{11.314}{90}\right) = 557.6 \times (1 + 0.1257) = 557.6 \times 1.1257 = 627.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (f) — Forging load.**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (56.57)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 3200.2}{4} = 2513.3 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F = p \cdot A_f = 627.7 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 2513.3 \text{ mm}^2 = 1{,}577{,}600 \text{ N} \approx 1578 \text{ kN}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Governing Equations for Each Characteristic

| # | Characteristic | Governing Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plastic Deformation | $\sigma \geq Y_0$ | Stress must exceed yield to initiate flow |
| 2 | Volume Constancy | $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ | Material is redistributed, not lost |
| 3 | Compressive Stress | $\sigma_1 \approx \sigma_2 \approx 0$, $\sigma_3 < 0$ | Hydrostatic compression suppresses fracture |
| 4 | Yielding | $Y = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + \ldots}$ | Von Mises yield criterion |
| 5 | Strain Hardening | $Y_f = K \varepsilon^{\,n}$ | Resistance grows with deformation |
| 6 | Grain Flow | $R_a^{\parallel} > R_a^{\perp}$ | Anisotropic properties due to fibre alignment |
| 7 | Residual Stress | $\sigma_R = Y_f - \sigma_{\text{applied}}$ | Locked-in elastic stresses |
| 8 | Friction | $m = \tau / Y_f$ | Interface shear stress vs. flow stress |
| 9 | Temp / Strain Rate | $Y_f = K \varepsilon^{\,n} \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m} e^{Q/RT}$ | Hot working reduces $Y_f$ |
| 10 | Mass Conservation | $m_{\text{initial}} = m_{\text{final}}$ | No material added or removed |

    S1 --> P6[Stretch Forming]
    S1 --> P7[Bending]
    S1 --> P8[Shearing]

    P1 --> D1[Compressive stress dominant]
    P2 --> D2[Compressive plus shear]
    P3 --> D3[Compressive plane strain]
    P4 --> D4[Tensile dominant]
    P5 --> D5[Tensile plus compressive]
    P6 --> D6[Biaxial tensile]
    P7 --> D7[Flexural stress]
    P8 --> D8[Pure shear]

    style B1 fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1f3a5f
