<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define explosive forming. Mention any two of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Explosive forming is a high-energy rate forming process in which the chemical energy released by the detonation of an explosive charge is utilized to deform a metal workpiece against a die at very high forming velocities (typically 50–200 m/s).

*Two industrial applications:*
1. Forming of large ship hull panels and aerospace components.
2. Explosive welding of dissimilar metals and compaction of metal powders.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 mark each for two applications)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Why is water commonly used as the surrounding medium in explosive forming?**

**Model Answer:**
Water is used as the surrounding medium in explosive forming because:
1. It is **incompressible**, which ensures efficient transmission of the shock wave pressure to the workpiece with minimal energy loss.
2. It **confines** the expanding detonation gases, increasing the pressure pulse duration and hence the impulse delivered to the blank.
3. It acts as a **cushioning medium** that suppresses excessive noise and reduces fragmentation hazards.
4. Its high acoustic impedance helps in achieving the very high forming pressures required (up to several GPa).

*(3 marks: any 3 of the above points)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **underwater free explosive forming process** for the manufacture of a large hemispherical dish. Discuss the role of charge weight and stand-off distance on the forming characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel blank of mass **5 kg** and area **0.4 m²** is subjected to an underwater explosive shock wave of peak pressure **2.5 × 10⁸ Pa** for a pulse duration of **20 microseconds**. Calculate the velocity imparted to the blank. If the die radius is **100 mm**, estimate the average strain rate and the dynamic yield stress using the Cowper–Symonds relation (take *C* = 40 s⁻¹, *p* = 5, static yield = 250 MPa). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Schematic Description:**
The setup consists of:
- A reinforced concrete or steel chamber filled with water.
- The die mounted on the chamber floor, sealed with O-rings.
- The metal blank clamped over the die periphery with a sealing ring.
- The explosive charge suspended by thin wires at stand-off distance *D* above the blank.
- A detonator connected via remote firing leads.

**Process Steps:**
1. The blank is placed over the die and clamped to ensure a water-tight seal.
2. The chamber is filled with water; the explosive is positioned at the calculated stand-off height.
3. The detonator is fired remotely; the explosive detonates, releasing a high-pressure gas bubble.
4. The bubble expands, sending a shock wave through the water that pushes the blank down into the die cavity.
5. The blank deforms plastically, conforming to the die shape.
6. After pressure equalisation, the chamber is drained and the formed part is removed.

**Role of Charge Weight *W*:**
- Larger *W* releases more chemical energy, increasing peak pressure and forming velocity.
- It enables forming of thicker, larger, or higher-strength blanks.

**Role of Stand-off Distance *D*:**
- A small *D* produces very high pressure but a short impulse — useful for thin sheets.
- A large *D* produces a more uniform, gentler pressure pulse — better for thicker blanks.
- The empirical relation is $D = k \sqrt{W}$.

*(7 marks: 2 for schematic, 3 for process steps, 2 for parameters)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Given:**
- *m* = 5 kg
- *A* = 0.4 m²
- *P* = 2.5 × 10⁸ Pa
- *t* = 20 μs = 20 × 10⁻⁶ s
- *r* = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- Static yield stress $\sigma_{static}$ = 250 × 10⁶ Pa
- Cowper–Symonds: *C* = 40 s⁻¹, *p* = 5

**Step 1 — Velocity Calculation**

Using the impulse–momentum result derived in Section 3:

$$v = \dfrac{P \times A \times t}{m}$$

$$v = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{8} \times 0.4 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{5}$$

$$v = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{8} \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{5}$$

$$v = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{3}}{5}$$

$$\boxed{v = 400 \; \text{m/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Strain Rate Calculation**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v}{r} = \dfrac{400}{0.1} = 4000 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic Yield Stress**

$$\sigma_{dyn} = \sigma_{static} \left(1 + \left(\dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{C}\right)^{1/p}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{dyn} = 250 \times 10^{6} \times \left(1 + \left(\dfrac{4000}{40}\right)^{1/5}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{dyn} = 250 \times 10^{6} \times \left(1 + 100^{0.2}\right)$$

We know that $100^{0.2} = (10^2)^{0.2} = 10^{0.4} \approx 2.512$.

$$\sigma_{dyn} = 250 \times 10^{6} \times (1 + 2.512)$$

$$\sigma_{dyn} = 250 \times 10^{6} \times 3.512$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{dyn} \approx 878 \; \text{MPa}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
The dynamic yield stress is approximately **3.5 times** the static yield stress, confirming that explosive forming can deform mild steel without fracture even at extreme velocities.

*(7 marks: 2 for velocity, 2 for strain rate, 3 for dynamic yield stress with detailed substitution)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Compare explosive forming, electro-hydraulic forming, and electro-magnetic forming in terms of energy source, achievable forming velocity, typical applications, and tooling cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete setup for **contact explosive forming** of a small circular plate. Explain why contact forming is preferred for thin sheets. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

| Parameter | Explosive Forming | Electro-Hydraulic Forming | Electro-Magnetic Forming |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Chemical energy of explosive | Electrical energy discharged through liquid | Pulsed electromagnetic field |
| **Forming Velocity** | 50–200 m/s | 50–150 m/s | Up to 300 m/s |
| **Typical Applications** | Large panels, ship hulls, aerospace dishes | Smaller parts, sheet bulging | Tube compression, wire forming |
| **Tooling Cost** | Low (simple die, charge) | Moderate (capacitor bank) | High (coil tooling design) |
| **Safety Concerns** | High (explosives) | Moderate (high voltage) | Low (no explosive) |
| **Part Size Capability** | Very large (up to 8 m) | Medium (up to 1 m) | Small to medium |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per row × 5 well-explained rows + 2 for summary remarks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Block Diagram of Contact Explosive Forming Setup:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thin Metal Sheet Blank] --> B[Placed on Die Cavity]
    C[Plastic Explosive Sheet] --> D[Laid Directly on Blank]
    E[Detonator] --> F[Firing Leads]
    F --> G[Remote Trigger Circuit]
    G --> H[Detonation Event]
    H --> I[Pressure Wave Directly on Sheet]
    I --> J[Sheet Deforms into Die]
    style A fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Process Description:**
1. A thin sheet metal blank is positioned over a die cavity and clamped around the edges.
2. A thin sheet or pad of plastic explosive is laid directly on top of the blank.
3. A detonator is inserted into the explosive and connected to remote firing leads.
4. On remote triggering, the explosive detonates, producing a very high local pressure directly on the sheet.
5. The sheet is propelled downwards into the die cavity, forming the part in a single microsecond pulse.

**Why Contact Forming is Preferred for Thin Sheets:**
1. The explosive is in direct contact with the sheet, so the entire pressure is delivered without medium losses — ideal for thin sheets that need high pressure but low impulse.
2. The very short duration pulse prevents buckling or wrinkling that thin sheets are prone to in longer stand-off forming.
3. There is no water or air medium to attenuate the shock, ensuring maximum efficiency.
4. The setup is simpler and cheaper than underwater tanks, suitable for small-batch production.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for process steps, 2 for reasoning of thin sheet preference)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Students often write *t* in milliseconds when the formula expects seconds. Always convert pulse duration *t* into **seconds (s)** before substituting into $v = \frac{P A t}{m}$.
> 2. **Confusing Pressure Units:** GPa, MPa, and Pa are all used in this topic. The unit *Pa* is the SI standard; ensure all quantities are converted to Pa before calculation.
> 3. **Missing Schematic in Part (a):** Even for theory questions, KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if a neat schematic or block diagram is missing. Always include one.
> 4. **Forgetting the Direction of Velocity:** In the impulse–momentum derivation, the velocity is directed *towards the die* (perpendicular to the blank). State this explicitly to earn full marks.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Explosive Forming** is a **High-Velocity Forming (HVF)** process using chemical energy from explosives.
- [x] Forming velocities are extremely high: **50–200 m/s**, compared to ~0.5 m/s in conventional presses.
- [x] Three main methods: **Stand-off (free)**, **Contact**, and **Die forming**.
- [x] **Water is the preferred medium** in stand-off forming due to its incompressibility and high acoustic impedance.
- [x] **Charge weight *W*** controls total energy released: $E = W \times Q$.
- [x] **Stand-off distance *D*** scales as $D = k \sqrt{W}$ (empirical KTU rule).
- [x] **Workpiece velocity** from impulse–momentum: $v = \frac{P A t}{m}$.
- [x] **Dynamic yield stress** rises sharply with strain rate per **Cowper–Symonds relation**: $\sigma_{dyn} = \sigma_{static}\left(1 + \left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{C}\right)^{1/p}\right)$.
- [x] Typical strain rates in EF: **10³ to 10⁴ s⁻¹**.
- [x] **Capabilities:** large part size, hard-to-form alloys, low tooling cost, single-shot forming.
- [x] **Limitations:** poor dimensional accuracy, high springback, safety hazards, low production rate.
- [x] **Applications:** aerospace panels, ship hull sections, missile nose cones, powder compaction, explosive welding.
- [x] **TNT** has a detonation velocity of ~**6,900 m/s** and heat of detonation ~**4,000 J/g**.
- [x] Underwater shock pressure can reach up to **10 GPa** in industrial EF.
- [x] The process is **non-isothermal** — negligible heating due to short forming duration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydroforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydroforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HYDROFORMING (Definition as per KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *Hydroforming is a specialised cold/warm metal forming process in which a ductile metallic blank (sheet, tube, or preform) is plastically deformed into a desired shape by the application of high-pressure hydraulic fluid (water-oil emulsion or inert fluid) acting against the workpiece, either with or without a rigid die, leveraging the principle of equibiaxial or combined tensile stress states to enhance formability.*

In essence, **hydroforming replaces mechanical punches with fluid pressure** to deform metal, transmitting load uniformly over the entire contact area and eliminating friction-induced tearing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Balloon-in-a-Box" Analogy (First-Time Learner View)**
> Imagine blowing a thin rubber balloon inside a hollow, carved wooden box. The air pressure inside the balloon pushes the rubber outwards until it perfectly hugs every internal contour of the box. The balloon is the **metal blank**, the air is the **hydraulic fluid**, and the wooden box is the **die cavity**. This perfectly mirrors hydroforming: **fluid pressure shapes metal against a die** without ever directly touching the metal with a hard tool — drastically reducing friction, wear, and tearing.

## 1.3 Process Classification

Hydroforming is broadly classified into three engineering families:

| Family | Workpiece Geometry | Pressure Application | Typical KTU Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sheet Hydroforming** | Flat/Pre-formed sheet blanks | One side (usually against die) | Auto body panels, cookware |
| **Tube Hydroforming (THF)** | Tubular preforms | Internal + axial end feed | Frames, chassis components |
| **Hydro-Mechanical Forming** | Sheet + pre-bulged blank | Fluid + mechanical punch | Aerospace panels, oil pans |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** Students must clearly differentiate **bulging (positive die / male tool)** from **deep drawing (negative die / female cavity)** in sheet hydroforming — examiners frequently test this.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

The automotive industry (BMW, Mercedes-Benz, GM) uses hydroformed tubular chassis components because:
- Part consolidation: **12–15 stamped parts → 1 hydroformed part**
- Weight reduction: **up to 30% mass savings**
- Enhanced stiffness-to-weight ratio
- Superior dimensional accuracy: **± 0.1 mm** tolerance achievable

Standard operating pressures range between **50 MPa to 400 MPa**, depending on material strength (typically aluminium alloys, mild steels, stainless steels, and advanced high-strength steels — AHSS).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — How Hydroforming Works

The fundamental physics rests on three mechanical principles:

- **Step 1 — Stress State Generation:** Hydraulic pressure applies **equibiaxial tensile stress** to the workpiece. Unlike uniaxial tension (stamping), biaxial strain delays localised necking, raising the **Forming Limit Curve (FLC)**.
- **Step 2 — Plastic Instability Suppression:** Because thinning must occur in **two perpendicular directions simultaneously**, the onset of through-thickness localisation is delayed. This allows **strains 2×–3× larger** than conventional deep drawing.
- **Step 3 — Die-Workpiece Lubrication Interface:** A thin hydrodynamic film of fluid between the die and the workpiece virtually **eliminates Coulomb friction** (μ → 0), which removes the largest single source of failure in sheet metal forming — *drawn-wall tearing*.

## 2.2 Stress State Taxonomy in Hydroforming

```
                    HYDROFORMING STRESS STATES
                              │
        ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
        │                     │                     │
   SHEET ONLY             TUBE / TUBE-HYDRO    BLANK HYDRO-
  (BIAXIAL)              (BIAXIAL + AXIAL)     MECHANICAL
        │                     │                     │
   σ₁ = σ₂ = p              σ_θ (hoop)         Mixed state
   σ_thin = 0              σ_a (axial)         σ₁ > σ₂
                           σ_r ≈ 0 (thin wall)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The **effective strain (von Mises) under equibiaxial tension** is **NOT zero** — despite the misconception. It equals the principal strain, $\bar{\varepsilon} = \varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2$, which is why formability is still bounded.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Laplace / Bulge Pressure (Sheet)** | $p \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_{ys} \, t}{R}$ | $p$ in Pa, $t$ = thickness, $R$ = bulge radius |
| 2 | **Hoop Stress in Thin Tube** | $\sigma_\theta \;=\; \dfrac{p \, d_i}{2 \, t}$ | $d_i$ = inner dia., thin-wall assumption $t \ll d$ |
| 3 | **Axial Stress (Closed End)** | $\sigma_a \;=\; \dfrac{p \, d_i}{4 \, t}$ | End-cap force contribution |
| 4 | **Von Mises Effective Stress (Tube Hydroforming)** | $\sigma_{vm} \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_\theta^{\,2} + \sigma_a^{\,2} - \sigma_\theta \sigma_a}$ | Plane-stress form |
| 5 | **Burst Pressure (Thin Wall, von Mises)** | $p_b \;=\; \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{uts} \, t}{R_0}$ | $R_0$ = initial tube mid-radius |
| 6 | **Hydroforming Operating Pressure** | $p_{op} \;=\; 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8 \, p_b$ | Empirical safe window |
| 7 | **Tube Hydroforming — Effective Plastic Strain** | $\bar{\varepsilon} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{d_0}{d}\right)$ | For pure free-bulge expansion |
| 8 | **Axial Feed Strain Component** | $\varepsilon_a \;=\; \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$ | End feeding contribution |
| 9 | **Volume Constancy (Tube)** | $\dfrac{\pi}{4}\!\left(d_0^{\,2} - d_i^{\,2}\right)\! L_0 \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\!\left(d_f^{\,2} - d_{if}^{\,2}\right)\! L_f$ | Closed-system hydroforming |
| 10 | **Frictionless Die Force (Sheet)** | $F \;=\; p \, A$ | Pressure × contact area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Table Notation Rule:** All absolute-value bars have been rendered using $\vert$ — for example, $\sigma_\theta^{\,2}$ represents $\sigma_\theta^2$ inside algebraic expressions.

## 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

The **Laplace equation** ($p = 2\sigma t / R$) is the *design foundation* of every hydroforming process — it dictates the **minimum pressure** needed to bulge the sheet to a given die radius. The **burst pressure** is the *upper design limit* — exceeding it causes explosive failure. The ratio **$p_{op} / p_b \approx 0.6$–$0.8$** is the **process safety window** used universally in industry. The **volume constancy equation** is critical in tube hydroforming because the fluid is *incompressible* and the closed system demands exact mass-balance analysis during process simulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Burst Pressure in Thin-Walled Tube (Full Step Expansion)

We derive the burst pressure using the **equilibrium of a half-tube element**, the cornerstone of tube hydroforming analysis.

**Step 1 — Geometry Definition**
Cut a thin-walled tube of mid-radius $R_0$, wall thickness $t$, and length $L$ along its longitudinal axis. Internal hydraulic pressure $p$ acts radially outward.

**Step 2 — Free-Body Diagram (Half Element)**
The two halves are held together by hoop stress $\sigma_\theta$ acting over the cut cross-sectional area $A = 2 t L$ (two thicknesses × length).

**Step 3 — Force Balance on the Cut Surface**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{pressure}} &= F_{\text{hoop}} \\[4pt]
p \cdot \underbrace{(2 R_0 L)}_{\text{projected area}} &= \sigma_\theta \cdot \underbrace{(2 t L)}_{\text{cut area}} \\[4pt]
\therefore \; \sigma_\theta &= \dfrac{p \, R_0}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the standard **thin-wall hoop stress** relation.

**Step 4 — Apply Von Mises Yield Criterion**
For a thin-walled tube under internal pressure only (closed ends neglected initially), the principal stresses are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \sigma_\theta = \dfrac{p \, R_0}{t} \\[4pt]
\sigma_2 &= \sigma_a = \dfrac{p \, R_0}{2 t} \quad \text{(closed-end axial contribution)} \\[4pt]
\sigma_3 &= \sigma_r \approx 0 \quad \text{(thin-wall assumption)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Into Von Mises Equivalent Stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{vm} &= \sqrt{\sigma_\theta^{\,2} + \sigma_a^{\,2} - \sigma_\theta \sigma_a} \\[4pt]
\sigma_{vm} &= \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{p R_0}{t}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\dfrac{p R_0}{2t}\right)^{\!\!2} - \left(\dfrac{p R_0}{t}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{p R_0}{2t}\right)} \\[4pt]
\sigma_{vm} &= \dfrac{p R_0}{t} \sqrt{1 + \tfrac{1}{4} - \tfrac{1}{2}} \\[4pt]
\sigma_{vm} &= \dfrac{p R_0}{t} \sqrt{\tfrac{3}{4}} \;=\; \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{p R_0}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Yield Criterion Activation (At Burst)**
At burst, $\sigma_{vm} = \sigma_{uts}$ (ultimate tensile strength, since bursting is post-yield):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{p_b R_0}{t} &= \sigma_{uts} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,p_b \;=\; \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{uts} \, t}{R_0}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical **tube burst pressure** used in THF die design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sheet Hydroforming Bulge Pressure (Laplace / Membrane Theory)

**Step 1 — Free-Body of a Spherical Cap Segment**
Consider a thin sheet of thickness $t$ formed into a spherical cap of radius $R$. The cap subtends an angle $2\theta$ at the centre.

**Step 2 — Pressure Force on the Cap Base**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_p &= p \cdot \pi R^2 \sin^2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Membrane (Hoop) Force Holding the Cap Closed**
The membrane stress $\sigma_m$ acts along the circle of intersection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_m &= \sigma_m \cdot 2\pi R \sin\theta \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium Along the Axis of Symmetry**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p \cdot \pi R^2 \sin^2\theta &= \sigma_m \cdot 2\pi R \sin\theta \cdot t \\[4pt]
p R \sin\theta &= 2 \sigma_m \, t \\[4pt]
\sigma_m &= \dfrac{p R}{2 t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Yielding Criterion**
At the onset of plastic flow, $\sigma_m = \sigma_{ys}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,p \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_{ys} \, t}{R}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** The Laplace equation reveals an **inverse relationship** between bulge radius and forming pressure — *smaller radii require exponentially higher pressures*, which explains why sharp-cornered dies are *forbidden* in hydroforming.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Board-Standard)

> **Problem:** A steel tube has $d_0 = 60$ mm outer diameter, $t_0 = 2$ mm wall thickness, and is made of AISI 1020 steel ($\sigma_{uts} = 380$ MPa). Calculate (a) the burst pressure, and (b) the safe operating pressure using the empirical $0.7 \, p_b$ rule.

**Solution:**

Given: $d_0 = 60$ mm, $t_0 = 2$ mm, $\sigma_{uts} = 380$ MPa.

Mid-radius: $R_0 = (d_0 - t_0)/2 = (60 - 2)/2 = 29$ mm = 0.029 m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_b &= \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{uts} \cdot t}{R_0} \\[4pt]
p_b &= \dfrac{2}{1.7321} \cdot \dfrac{380 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.002}{0.029} \\[4pt]
p_b &= 1.1547 \times \dfrac{760{,}000}{0.029} \\[4pt]
p_b &= 1.1547 \times 26.207 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
p_b &= 30.26 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \;=\; \mathbf{30.26 \; MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Safe operating pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{op} &= 0.7 \times p_b = 0.7 \times 30.26 \;\text{MPa} \;=\; \mathbf{21.18 \; MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification check:** $t / R_0 = 0.002 / 0.029 \approx 0.069 \ll 1$, confirming the **thin-wall assumption is valid**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
