# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Define hydroforming. List any four advantages of the hydroforming process over conventional stamping.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Hydroforming is a metal forming process in which a ductile metallic workpiece is plastically deformed into a desired shape by the application of high-pressure hydraulic fluid, either with or without a rigid die.

**Four Advantages (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Superior Formability:** Equibiaxial stress state raises the Forming Limit Curve (FLC), allowing deeper draws.
2. **Reduced Friction & Tool Wear:** Fluid film eliminates direct punch-blank contact, virtually eliminating wear and friction.
3. **Part Consolidation:** Multiple stamped components can be replaced by a single hydroformed part (e.g., automotive frame rails).
4. **Enhanced Dimensional Accuracy:** Uniform pressure distribution yields tighter tolerances, typically **±0.1 mm**.
5. *Bonus point:* Lower springback due to compressive residual stresses induced by fluid pressure.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**
**Distinguish between sheet hydroforming and tube hydroforming. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Sheet Hydroforming | Tube Hydroforming |
|---|---|---|
| Workpiece | Flat sheet / blank | Hollow tube / preform |
| Pressure Source | One side of sheet (against die) | Internal pressurisation + axial end feed |
| Stress State | Equibiaxial tension | Biaxial + axial combined |
| Typical Pressure | 50–150 MPa | 100–400 MPa |

**Applications (2 Marks):**
- **Sheet HF:** Automotive body panels (e.g., BMW roof panels), kitchen sinks, washing machine drums.
- **Tube HF:** Engine cradles, chassis frames, exhaust manifolds, bicycle frames.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**

**OR**

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

*(Students are expected to attempt ONE full question from the choice.)*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **tube hydroforming process** for an automotive chassis component. Discuss the role of **axial feeding** during the process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **burst pressure equation** for a thin-walled tube under internal hydraulic pressure using the von Mises yield criterion. Hence, calculate the safe operating pressure for an aluminium tube of $d_0 = 80$ mm, $t_0 = 1.5$ mm, with $\sigma_{uts} = 240$ MPa, using a safety factor of 0.7. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Tube Hydroforming Process — Stepwise Explanation (7 Marks)**

1. **Process Setup (2 Marks):** A tubular preform is placed inside a die cavity shaped like the final part (e.g., an engine cradle). Both ends are sealed by **axial punches** that can move inward to feed material.

2. **Pressurisation Phase (2 Marks):** Hydraulic fluid (typically water-glycol emulsion) is pumped into the tube interior at high pressure, typically **15–25 MPa initially**, rising to **80–150 MPa** in the calibration stage.

3. **Axial Feeding Role (2 Marks):** During bulging, the **axial punches push inward** simultaneously. This:
   - **Suppresses wall thinning** by replenishing material from the tube ends into the bulge region.
   - **Shifts the strain path** from pure biaxial towards plane strain, which improves formability.
   - **Reduces the risk of bursting** in the die-corner (re-entrant) regions.

4. **Calibration & Ejection (1 Mark):** Final high-pressure hold ensures the tube conforms precisely to the die walls; depressurisation releases the part.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by **failing to explain *why* axial feeding is necessary**. Simply stating "axial feed is applied" earns partial credit only. Always link it to **wall thickness preservation** and **strain-path control**.

**(b) Burst Pressure Derivation + Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Derivation (4 Marks):** As derived in Section 3.1 of these notes, the final expression is:

$$
p_b \;=\; \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{uts} \, t_0}{R_0}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given: $d_0 = 80$ mm = 0.080 m, $t_0 = 1.5$ mm = 0.0015 m, $\sigma_{uts} = 240$ MPa, safety factor = 0.7.

Mid-radius: $R_0 = (d_0 - t_0)/2 = (0.080 - 0.0015)/2 = 0.03925$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_b &= \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{240 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.0015}{0.03925} \\[4pt]
p_b &= 1.1547 \times \dfrac{360{,}000}{0.03925} \\[4pt]
p_b &= 1.1547 \times 9.172 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
p_b &= 10.59 \; \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Safe operating pressure:

$$
p_{op} \;=\; 0.7 \times 10.59 \;=\; \mathbf{7.41 \; MPa}
$$

**Check:** $t/R_0 = 0.0015/0.03925 \approx 0.038 \ll 1$ — **thin-wall assumption is satisfied**.

---

### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain with a diagram the **sheet hydroforming process using a female die** (deep-drawing variant). List **four advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Laplace equation for membrane hydroforming**, derive the relationship between bulge pressure and bulge radius. A circular sheet of aluminium alloy ($t = 1.2$ mm, $\sigma_{ys} = 180$ MPa) is to be bulged into a hemispherical die of radius $R = 75$ mm. Determine the required hydraulic pressure. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Sheet Hydroforming with Female Die (7 Marks)**

1. **Schematic Description (2 Marks):** A sheet blank is clamped over a cavity die. Hydraulic fluid pressure is applied from above, pushing the sheet down into the female cavity. Die geometry controls the final shape.

2. **Process Stages (2 Marks):**
   - *Pre-bulge phase:* Pressure bulges the sheet freely.
   - *Drawing phase:* Sheet conforms to die walls.
   - *Calibration:* Final high pressure for corner-filling.

3. **Four Advantages (2 Marks):**
   - Uniform pressure → no localised thinning.
   - No direct punch contact → no surface scoring or marking.
   - Higher draw ratios than conventional deep drawing.
   - Better thickness distribution across the formed part.

4. **Two Limitations (1 Mark):**
   - High capital cost for pressure equipment and sealed dies.
   - Slower cycle times compared to high-speed mechanical stamping.

**(b) Laplace Equation Derivation + Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Derivation (3 Marks):** From Section 3.2, the final relation is:

$$
p \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_{ys} \, t}{R}
$$

**Numerical Solution (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \dfrac{2 \times 180 \times 10^6 \times 0.0012}{0.075} \\[4pt]
p &= \dfrac{432{,}000}{0.075} \\[4pt]
p &= 5.76 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa} \;=\; \mathbf{5.76 \; MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Common Mark-Loss Area):**
> 1. Forgetting to **convert mm to m** before substitution — results in erroneous answers by factor of 1000.
> 2. Using $\sigma_{uts}$ instead of $\sigma_{ys}$ for **Laplace equation** — this is wrong! Laplace equation governs the **onset of yielding**, hence uses **yield strength**, not ultimate strength.
> 3. Ignoring the **thin-wall assumption validity check** ($t/R \ll 1$) — examiners expect this verification step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HYDROFORMING — High-Density Revision Summary**

- **Core Principle:** Fluid pressure replaces mechanical punch — uniform load distribution, frictionless interface.
- **Sheet HF:** $p = 2\sigma_{ys} t / R$ — **Laplace equation** — bulge pressure inversely proportional to die radius.
- **Tube HF:** $p_b = (2/\sqrt{3}) \cdot \sigma_{uts} t / R_0$ — burst pressure using **von Mises**.
- **Safe Operating Pressure:** $p_{op} = (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8) \, p_b$ — universal industry rule.
- **Equibiaxial Stress State:** Delays necking → higher FLC → strains 2×–3× conventional drawing.
- **Axial Feed in THF:** Suppresses wall thinning, controls strain path, prevents bursting.
- **Volume Constancy:** Incompressible fluid → closed-system mass balance ($V_0 = V_f$).
- **Standard Pressures:** Sheet HF: 50–150 MPa, Tube HF: 100–400 MPa.
- **Typical Materials:** Mild steel, AHSS, Aluminium alloys (6061, 5052), Stainless steel (304).
- **Applications:** Auto chassis (BMW, GM, Tesla), aerospace panels, bicycle frames, kitchenware.
- **Draw Ratio Limit:** Conventional ≈ 2.2; Hydroforming ≈ 3.0+.
- **Tool Wear:** Virtually **zero** due to fluid film — life ~10× longer than mechanical dies.
- **Springback:** Significantly reduced due to compressive residual stress from fluid loading.
- **Common Failure Modes:** Bursting (overpressure), wrinkling (under-pressure), buckling (axial feed too aggressive).
- **Key Assumption:** Thin-wall ($t/R \ll 1$) valid for almost all practical hydroforming cases.
- **KTU Exam Focus Areas:** Definition (1 mark), types (2 marks), Laplace equation derivation (4 marks), burst pressure derivation (5 marks), numerical problem (2–3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magneto Forming, etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Magneto Forming, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Magneto Forming** (also widely known as **Electromagnetic Forming / EMF** or **Magnetic Pulse Forming / MPF**) is a high-velocity, contact-less, cold working process belonging to the family of **High Energy Rate Forming (HERF)** processes, in which the kinetic energy required for plastic deformation of a metallic workpiece is supplied by the **repulsive Lorentz force** generated when a rapidly pulsed, high-intensity electromagnetic field interacts with induced eddy currents in the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition Anchor:**
> "Magneto forming is a non-contact, high-velocity forming process in which a sudden discharge of electrical energy through a coil produces a transient magnetic field that induces opposing eddy currents in a nearby conductive workpiece; the mutual repulsion between the primary coil field and the induced field produces a radial magnetic pressure that plastically deforms the workpiece against a die."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Student Intuition)

Imagine throwing a strong **magnet** at a **copper plate**. Even though the magnet never touches the copper, the copper plate **feels a push** and slides away. Now imagine this happening in **millionths of a second** with a magnet many thousands of times stronger than any household magnet, and the copper is wrapped around a shaped die. This is exactly how magneto forming works.

- The **coil** is the "magnet" (electromagnet).
- The **workpiece** is the "copper plate."
- The **discharge pulse** is the "super-fast throw."
- The **die** is the shape the metal is pushed into.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Cue:** No hammer, no punch, no hydraulic ram touches the workpiece. The deformation is done by an **invisible magnetic pressure pulse** lasting only **10 to 100 microseconds**.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Process Family | HERF (High Energy Rate Forming) |
| Energy Source | Capacitor Bank (Electrical) |
| Contact Type | Non-contact |
| Working Temperature | Cold Working (Room Temp) |
| Strain Rate | 10² – 10⁴ s⁻¹ |
| Pulse Duration | 10 – 100 μs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Charging the Capacitor Bank**
A bank of high-voltage capacitors (typically rated **5 kV to 20 kV**, **C = few μF to several hundred μF**) is charged slowly from a DC power supply through a high-value charging resistor.

**Step 2 — Triggering the Coil**
When a **trigatron spark gap** or **thyristor switch** is fired, the stored electrical energy is dumped almost instantaneously into a **working coil** (solenoid).

**Step 3 — Magnetic Flux Build-Up**
The surge of current (peak currents can reach **50 kA to 500 kA**) generates a transient magnetic field of extreme density. Peak flux density **B** ranges between **10 T to 30 T**.

**Step 4 — Induced Eddy Currents**
By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, this rapidly changing flux induces strong **eddy currents** in the surface skin of the conductive workpiece (depth of penetration **δ** is very small — only fractions of a millimeter).

**Step 5 — Lorentz Repulsion (The Core Phenomenon)**
The induced current flows in the **opposite direction** to the coil current (Lenz's Law). Two conductors carrying currents in opposite directions **repel each other**. This produces an outward (or inward, depending on geometry) **magnetic pressure** on the workpiece.

**Step 6 — Plastic Deformation**
The magnetic pressure, acting for only a few microseconds, accelerates the workpiece to velocities of **100 m/s to 500 m/s**. By momentum, this kinetic energy is converted into plastic deformation energy as the metal slams into the die.

**Step 7 — Spring-Back & Ejection**
Because deformation is nearly isothermal (no time for heat conduction), and the workpiece experiences uniform body forces, spring-back is minimal. The formed part is then ejected mechanically.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Physics Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it happens | How it contributes to forming |
|------|---------------|------------------------------|
| Charging | Storing energy gradually avoids heating | Allows huge energy pack in small volume |
| Triggering | Need a fast-rising pulse to maximize dB/dt | Sharper pulse → stronger induced EMF |
| Flux Build-up | Current through coil creates B-field | Field strength sets peak pressure |
| Eddy Currents | Lenz's Law on a conductor in changing B | Source of secondary field that repels coil field |
| Lorentz Force | Opposing currents repel (F = IL × B) | The actual "punch" — magnetic pressure |
| Plastic Flow | Inertia carries workpiece into die | Converts kinetic energy to deformation energy |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU ESE numericals and derivations.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Energy stored in capacitor (Joules) — **C** in Farads, **V** in Volts |
| 2 | $P_{mag} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$ | Magnetic pressure on conductor (Pa) — **B** in Tesla, **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** |
| 3 | $P_{mag} = \dfrac{\mu_{0} \cdot N^{2} I^{2}}{2 L^{2}}$ | Pressure in terms of coil turns **N**, current **I**, coil length **L** (Pa) |
| 4 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\mu_{0}\mu_{r}\omega}}$ | Skin depth of eddy current penetration (m) — **ρ** resistivity, **ω** angular frequency |
| 5 | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$ | Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor (N) |
| 6 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{k}}{m}}$ | Velocity of workpiece from kinetic energy (m/s) |
| 7 | $W_{p} = \int \sigma \cdot d\varepsilon \cdot V$ | Plastic deformation energy absorbed by workpiece (J) |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{p}}{E} \times 100\%$ | Energy conversion efficiency (typically 10 – 25 %) |
| 9 | $\tau = L_{c} \cdot C_{eq}$ | LC time constant — pulse rise time (s) |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_{0}}{t_{f}}\right)$ | True strain in tube bulging / compression |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Welding of dissimilar metals (Al-Ti), tube joining in fuel systems.
- **Automotive:** Crimping of clutch hubs, assembly of drive shafts.
- **Defence:** Forming of large aluminium missile skins, explosive simulation.
- **Electronics:** Sealing of battery cans without contaminating welds.
- **Tube Industries:** Bulging, necking, swaging, expansion onto mandrels in one pulse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because deformation occurs in microseconds, the material behaves **visco-plastically**. Flow stress can rise 2–4× compared to quasi-static forming. This is why KTU questions often ask about **formability enhancement** under EMF.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnetic Pressure from Maxwell's Stress Tensor

**Statement:** The radial pressure exerted on a conductive surface by a magnetic field of flux density **B** is given by $P_{mag} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$.

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Stress Tensor for Magnetic Field:**

The magnetic part of the Maxwell stress tensor in an isotropic medium gives a normal pressure on a surface perpendicular to the field direction equal to:

$$P = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu}$$

For free space (or non-magnetic workpiece where $\mu_{r} \approx 1$):

$$\mu = \mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{mag} &= \frac{B \cdot H}{2} \\
&= \frac{B \cdot (B/\mu_{0})}{2} \\
&= \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numeric Verification (for B = 20 T):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{mag} &= \frac{(20)^{2}}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{400}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 1.59 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 159 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This pressure (~160 MPa) exceeds the yield strength of aluminium (~100 MPa) and even mild steel (~250 MPa) — easily sufficient to deform most metals.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Stored in Capacitor Bank

**Statement:** The electrical energy stored in a charged capacitor is $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.

**Starting from definition of capacitance:**

$$Q = C \cdot V$$

**Work done in moving an infinitesimal charge dQ from one plate to the other against voltage v:**

$$dW = v \cdot dQ = v \cdot C \cdot dv$$

**Integrating from 0 to V:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{0}^{V} v \cdot C \, dv \\
&= C \cdot \int_{0}^{V} v \, dv \\
&= C \cdot \left[\frac{v^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{V} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numeric Check (KTU-type problem):**
A 100 μF capacitor charged to 10 kV:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times (10\,000)^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{8} \\
&= 5000 \text{ J} = 5 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> This 5 kJ is delivered in ~50 μs — equivalent to a **100 kW instantaneous power pulse**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Skin Depth (Depth of Eddy Current Penetration)

**Statement:** AC current in a conductor is not uniform — it concentrates near the surface. The characteristic depth is $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\mu\omega}}$.

**Starting from Faraday's Law and Ohm's Law:**

Inside the conductor, the induced electric field **E** satisfies:

$$\nabla \times E = -\frac{\partial B}{\partial t} = j\omega B$$

**Ampère's Law in the conductor:**

$$\nabla \times B = \mu J = \mu \sigma E$$

**Combining the curls (assuming 1-D decay along x):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2}B}{dx^{2}} &= j\omega\mu\sigma B \\
&= \frac{2j}{\delta^{2}} B \quad \text{where } \delta^{2} = \frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}
\end{aligned}$$

**Using $\rho = 1/\sigma$:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\mu\omega}}$$

**Numeric Example (Copper, ρ = 1.7 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m, μ ≈ μ₀, f = 20 kHz, ω = 2πf):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.7 \times 10^{-8}}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2\pi \times 20\,000}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{3.4 \times 10^{-8}}{1.579 \times 10^{-1}}} \\
&\approx \sqrt{2.15 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&\approx 4.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.46 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why EMF is ideal for forming **thin sheets and tubes**. For thick sections, the surface layer deforms but the bulk does not, causing **uneven strain distribution**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Force on the Workpiece (Integral Form)

For a cylindrical coil of radius **r** carrying current **I**, with workpiece at radius **r + g** (gap = g), the radial force per unit length is derived by treating both as parallel conductors:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{rad} &= \frac{\mu_{0} I_{c} I_{w}}{2\pi r} \quad \text{(per unit length)} \\
P_{rad} &= \frac{F_{rad}}{A} = \frac{\mu_{0} I_{c} I_{w}}{2\pi r L}
\end{aligned}$$

In a well-coupled system, $I_{w} \approx I_{c}$ (workpiece current mirrors coil current), so:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\mu_{0} I^{2}}{2\pi r L}$$

This shows that **halving the radius quadruples the pressure** — a critical design rule for coil sizing.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Process | Energy Density | Strain Rate | Contact | Typical Use |
|---------|---------------|-------------|---------|-------------|
| Conventional Stamping | Low | 1 – 10 s⁻¹ | Yes | Car panels |
| Hydroforming | Medium | 1 – 10 s⁻¹ | Yes (fluid) | Frames |
| **Electromagnetic Forming** | **High** | **10² – 10⁴ s⁻¹** | **No** | **Tube joining, crimping** |
| Explosive Forming | High | 10³ – 10⁴ s⁻¹ | Yes (explosive) | Large panels |
| Electrohydraulic Forming | High | 10³ – 10⁴ s⁻¹ | Yes (shockwave) | Bulging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EMF System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HV DC Power Supply] --> B[Charging Resistor Rc]
    B --> C[Capacitor Bank C]
    C --> D[Trigger Switch Thyristor or Trigatron]
    D --> E[Working Coil Solenoid]
    E --> F[Transient Magnetic Field B]
    F --> G[Induced Eddy Current in Workpiece]
    G --> H[Lorentz Repulsion Force F]
    H --> I[Workpiece Accelerates to High Velocity]
    I --> J[Plastic Deformation against Die]
    J --> K[Formed Component]
    L[Cooling System] --> E
    M[Control and Timing Unit] --> D
    M --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **causal chain** from electrical energy input to mechanical deformation output. The arrows represent **causality and energy flow**, not physical connections.

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — One Forming Pulse Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Charging 1 to 5 s] --> S2[Stage 2 Trigger Firing lt 1 us]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Coil Current Rise 5 to 50 us]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Magnetic Field Build up B max]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Eddy Current Induction in Workpiece]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Lorentz Pressure Acts 10 to 100 us]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 Workpiece Velocity Peak 100 to 500 m per s]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8 Impact on Die Plastic Deformation]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 9 Damping and Spring back Settling]
    S9 --> S10[Stage 10 Component Ejection Ready for Next Cycle]
```

### 4.3 Coil-to-Workpiece Coupling Geometry (Cross-section Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SC[Coil Cross Section]
        C1[Coil Winding N turns]
        C2[Field Concentrator Optional]
    end
    subgraph SW[Workpiece Region]
        W1[Outer Surface where Eddy Currents Flow]
        W2[Workpiece Bulk]
        W3[Inner Surface in contact with Die]
    end
    subgraph SD[Die Region]
        D1[Die Cavity Defines Final Shape]
    end
    C2 -->|Couples flux across gap g| W1
    W1 -->|Skin depth delta| W2
    W2 --> W3
    W3 -->|Presses into| D1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Logic:** The arrows show that the field does not "touch" the workpiece — it bridges an **air gap**. This is the signature of non-contact forming.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
*Define Magneto Forming. State any two of its advantages.*

**Model Answer:**

Magneto forming, also termed electromagnetic forming, is a high-velocity, non-contact metal forming process that uses the **Lorentz force** generated by a transient magnetic field to deform a workpiece.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. No physical contact between tool and workpiece → no tool wear, no marking, no lubrication needed.
2. Very high strain rate enhances formability of materials that are difficult to form quasi-statically.
3. Spring-back is significantly reduced because deformation is nearly isothermal.
4. Can be used to join dissimilar metals that are hard to weld conventionally.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "it is a contact process" or "uses hydraulic pressure." Both are **wrong**. The KTU answer key explicitly tests the words **non-contact** and **Lorentz / magnetic pressure**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*What is the role of the capacitor bank in a magneto forming setup?*

**Model Answer:**

The capacitor bank is the **primary energy storage device**. It is slowly charged to a high DC voltage (5–20 kV) by a power supply. When triggered, it discharges this stored energy in **microseconds** into the working coil, producing the large, rapidly changing current required to generate the high-intensity magnetic pulse.

The energy stored is given by $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$, which determines the available forming energy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are lost if students only say "it stores electricity" without linking it to **rapid discharge** or stating the **energy formula**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in Module 2 carry 14 marks and offer **internal choice**. Either Question A **OR** Question B must be answered. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE – Sample Model Paper 1, Module 2]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a magneto forming system. List its main components.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A capacitor bank of 200 μF is charged to 15 kV. If 60 % of the stored energy is delivered to a coil and 20 % of that is converted into plastic deformation, calculate:* **(7 Marks)**
*(i) Total stored energy*
*(ii) Energy delivered to coil*
*(iii) Energy used in deformation*
*(iv) Equivalent deformation force if workpiece moves 5 mm.*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle & Components:**

Refer to the Block Diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

The system operates as follows:
1. **HV Power Supply** charges the **capacitor bank (C)** through charging resistors.
2. **Trigger switch (trigatron/thyristor)** initiates the discharge.
3. Energy flows into the **working coil**, producing a transient magnetic field **B**.
4. **Eddy currents** are induced in the conductive **workpiece**.
5. **Lorentz repulsion** between coil field and induced field accelerates the workpiece.
6. The workpiece impacts a **die** and plastically deforms.

**Main Components:**
- HV DC power supply
- Charging resistor
- Capacitor bank
- Triggering circuit (spark gap / thyristor)
- Working coil (with optional field shaper)
- Work-holding fixture and die
- Control and timing unit
- Safety and earthing system

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: **C = 200 μF = 200 × 10⁻⁶ F**, **V = 15 kV = 15 000 V**, **η_coil = 60 %**, **η_deform = 20 %**, **s = 5 mm = 0.005 m**

**(i) Total stored energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times (15\,000)^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.25 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 22\,500 \text{ J} = 22.5 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Energy delivered to coil:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{coil} &= 0.60 \times E_{total} \\
&= 0.60 \times 22\,500 \\
&= 13\,500 \text{ J} = 13.5 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Energy used in deformation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{def} &= 0.20 \times E_{coil} \\
&= 0.20 \times 13\,500 \\
&= 2\,700 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Equivalent deformation force (assuming average force):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{avg} &= \frac{E_{def}}{s} \\
&= \frac{2\,700}{0.005} \\
&= 540\,000 \text{ N} = 540 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 14-Mark Question:**
> 1. Forgetting to square the voltage (15 000²) → loses 2 marks.
> 2. Applying the percentages in the **wrong sequence** (e.g., multiplying total by 80 % first) → loses 1 mark.
> 3. Not converting μF to F → loses 1 mark.
> 4. Forgetting units in the final answer → loses 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU ESE – Sample Model Paper 2, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Explain the three basic configurations of magneto forming: expansion, compression, and flat sheet forming. Sketch each and give one industrial application.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Derive the expression for magnetic pressure on a workpiece in terms of flux density. A coil produces a peak magnetic field of 18 T at the workpiece surface. Compute the peak pressure and the corresponding acceleration of a 200 g aluminium slug if the pressure acts uniformly over its 30 cm² cross-section.* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Configurations:**

**1. Expansion (Tube Bulging):**
- Coil is placed **inside** a tubular workpiece.
- Repulsive force pushes the tube **outward** against a surrounding die.
- Used for **bulging, flaring, and tube end forming**.

**2. Compression (Tube Necking / Swaging):**
- Coil is placed **outside** the workpiece.
- Repulsive force pushes the tube **inward** against an internal die or mandrel.
- Used for **crimping, necking, swaging, and joining tubes to fittings**.

**3. Flat Sheet Forming:**
- A **flat spiral (pancake) coil** sits above a flat sheet.
- Sheet is accelerated **downward** into a die cavity.
- Used for **shallow draws, embossing, and explosive simulation** on laboratory scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Keyword Check:** "Inside vs. outside coil placement" determines whether deformation is **outward (expansion)** or **inward (compression)**.

---

**(b) Derivation & Numerical:**

**Derivation** (already covered in Section 3.1):

$$P_{mag} = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$$

**Calculation:** Given **B = 18 T**, **m = 200 g = 0.2 kg**, **A = 30 cm² = 30 × 10⁻⁴ m²**

**Peak Pressure:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{mag} &= \frac{(18)^{2}}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{324}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 1.289 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 128.9 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Force on slug:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= P \times A \\
&= 1.289 \times 10^{8} \times 30 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 3.867 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Acceleration:**

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{F}{m} \\
&= \frac{3.867 \times 10^{5}}{0.2} \\
&= 1.934 \times 10^{6} \text{ m/s}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

This acceleration is roughly **200 000 times** gravitational acceleration — illustrating the extreme impulsive nature of EMF.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 14-Mark Question:**
> 1. Writing $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ correctly with units → mandatory; missing unit loses 1 mark.
> 2. Not converting **g to kg** and **cm² to m²** → loses 2 marks.
> 3. Skipping the derivation steps (Section 3.1) and writing only the final formula → loses up to 3 marks in part (b).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember for KTU 2024 Board Exam — Magneto Forming**

- ✅ Magneto forming = **Electromagnetic Forming (EMF)** = **Magnetic Pulse Forming (MPF)** — all synonyms.
- ✅ It is a **High Energy Rate Forming (HERF)** process — **non-contact**, **cold working**.
- ✅ Working principle: **Lorentz force** from **induced eddy currents** (Lenz's Law).
- ✅ Energy source: **Capacitor bank** → $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.
- ✅ Peak magnetic flux density **B ≈ 10 – 30 T**.
- ✅ Magnetic pressure: $P_{mag} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$, with $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- ✅ Skin depth: $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\mu\omega}}$ — very small, hence **thin sections preferred**.
- ✅ Pulse duration: **10 – 100 μs**; workpiece velocity: **100 – 500 m/s**.
- ✅ Three configurations: **Expansion** (coil inside), **Compression** (coil outside), **Flat sheet** (pancake coil).
- ✅ Overall efficiency: **10 – 25 %** — most energy lost as heat and sound.
- ✅ Advantages: no tool wear, no lubrication, low spring-back, can join dissimilar metals, enhanced formability at high strain rates.
- ✅ Limitations: limited to **conductive (non-ferrous) workpieces**, thick sections deform unevenly, capacitor banks are expensive, high noise (≈ 130 dB) needs ear protection.
- ✅ Industrial uses: aerospace tube joining, automotive crimping, battery can sealing, missile skin forming.
- ✅ **Formula flashcard for last-minute revision:**
  - $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$
  - $P = B^{2}/(2\mu_{0})$
  - $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho/(\mu\omega)}$
  - $F = BIL\sin\theta$
  - $v = \sqrt{2E_k/m}$

> **End of Module 2 — Magneto Forming Notes (PCPET602, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Production Engineering** and **Tool Design**, the term **"Limits"** does NOT refer to the mathematical limits of calculus. Instead, it refers to the **Engineering Limits of Size** as defined under the **ISO System of Limits and Fits** (Indian Standard **IS 919 : 1993**, equivalent to **ISO 286**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Limits* are the two extreme permissible sizes (maximum and minimum) between which a manufactured part (a hole or a shaft) is allowed to vary. They define the boundaries of acceptability for a single dimension on a technical drawing.

Mathematically, for any given nominal size $D$:

$$L_{max} = D + ES \quad \text{(for a hole)} \quad \text{or} \quad D + es \quad \text{(for a shaft)}$$

$$L_{min} = D + EI \quad \text{(for a hole)} \quad \text{or} \quad D + ei \quad \text{(for a shaft)}$$

Where:
- $L_{max}$ = **Maximum Limit of Size**
- $L_{min}$ = **Minimum Limit of Size**
- $ES$ / $es$ = **Upper Deviation**
- $EI$ / $ei$ = **Lower Deviation**
- $D$ = **Nominal Size**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Door Frame & Door" System:**
> Imagine buying a ready-made door for a doorway. The manufacturer cannot make every door to the *exact* millimetre (that would be impossibly expensive). So they decide: "Any door between **2050 mm** and **2070 mm** tall will fit my standard doorframe." The **2070 mm** is the **Upper Limit**, the **2050 mm** is the **Lower Limit**, and the gap between them (20 mm) is the **Tolerance**. The actual manufactured size (say, 2063 mm) is the **Actual Size**. As long as the actual size stays within the limits, the part is acceptable.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Size** | $D$ | The "called-out" size on the drawing (e.g., 50 mm). |
| **Basic Size** | $D$ | The exact reference size from which deviations are calculated. |
| **Actual Size** | — | The measured size of the finished part. |
| **Maximum Limit** | $L_{max}$ | The largest permissible size. |
| **Minimum Limit** | $L_{min}$ | The smallest permissible size. |
| **Upper Deviation** | $ES / es$ | Algebraic difference between max limit and nominal size. |
| **Lower Deviation** | $EI / ei$ | Algebraic difference between min limit and nominal size. |
| **Tolerance** | $T$ | Total permissible variation, $T = L_{max} - L_{min}$. |
| **Allowance** | $A$ | Minimum clearance (positive) or maximum interference (negative) between mating parts. |
| **Fit** | — | The range of looseness or tightness resulting from the limits of two mating parts. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital letters (H, S, ES, EI)** refer to **holes**, and **lowercase letters (s, h, es, ei)** refer to **shafts**. This is a strict IS 919 / ISO 286 convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the ISO Limit System

The entire system rests on three cornerstones:

**Pillar 1 — Tolerance Grade (IT Grade):**
- Defines the **magnitude** (size) of the tolerance zone.
- 18 standard grades exist: **IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2 … IT18**.
- Higher IT number → Larger tolerance → Less precise manufacturing required.
- Example: **IT7** is used for general engineering, **IT11** for rough machining.

**Pillar 2 — Fundamental Deviation:**
- Defines the **position** of the tolerance zone relative to the nominal size line (zero line).
- Each letter (**H, h, g, p, etc.**) corresponds to a specific deviation for either a hole or shaft.
- For the **Hole**: The lower deviation $EI = 0$ (zero line is the lower limit).
- For the **Shaft h**: The upper deviation $es = 0$ (zero line is the upper limit).

**Pillar 3 — Nominal Size Step (Diameter Range):**
- The standard is divided into nominal size ranges (e.g., 0–3 mm, 3–6 mm, 6–10 mm, ..., up to 3150 mm).
- Tolerance value depends on the nominal size step.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Limit Calculation

1. **Identify** the nominal size $D$ and locate its size step (e.g., 30 mm falls in the "30–50 mm" range).
2. **Select** the IT grade (e.g., IT8) from design requirements.
3. **Look up** the standard tolerance unit $i$ (or $I$) for that size step.
4. **Compute** total tolerance $T = K \cdot i$, where $K$ is a multiplier that depends on IT grade.
5. **Determine** the fundamental deviation letter (e.g., 'H' for hole, 'h' for shaft).
6. **Apply** the formula to find $L_{max}$ and $L_{min}$.

### 2.3 Standard Tolerance Unit Formulas (IS 919 / ISO 286)

The **tolerance unit** $i$ (for shafts, in micrometres) is calculated as:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the **geometric mean** of the nominal size range, expressed in **millimetres**, and $i$ comes out in **micrometres ($\mu m$)**.

The **tolerance unit** $I$ (for holes) is:

$$I = 0.4 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.010 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For practical KTU problems, students are given the standard tolerance value directly from tables. You only need the formula for derivation-type or theory questions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tolerance | $T = L_{max} - L_{min}$ | mm |
| 2 | Tolerance (alt form) | $T = \vert ES - EI \vert = \vert es - ei \vert$ | mm or $\mu m$ |
| 3 | Maximum Limit | $L_{max} = D + ES$ (or $+es$) | mm |
| 4 | Minimum Limit | $L_{min} = D + EI$ (or $+ei$) | mm |
| 5 | Allowance (Clearance Fit) | $A = L_{min,Hole} - L_{max,Shaft}$ | mm |
| 6 | Allowance (Interference Fit) | $A = L_{max,Hole} - L_{min,Shaft}$ | mm |
| 7 | Fit Type Test | If $L_{min,H} > L_{max,S}$ → Clearance; If $L_{max,H} < L_{min,S}$ → Interference; Otherwise → Transition | — |
| 8 | Shaft Tolerance Unit | $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | $\mu m$ |
| 9 | Hole Tolerance Unit | $I = 0.4 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.010 \cdot D^{2/3}$ | $\mu m$ |
| 10 | IT Grade Tolerance | $T = K \cdot i$ (K = grade factor) | $\mu m$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The ISO limit system is the **universal language of manufacturing interchangeability**. It allows:
- A gear manufactured in **Germany (DIN)** to mesh perfectly with a shaft made in **India (BIS)**.
- Mass production of bolts/nuts with guaranteed assembly.
- Selective assembly to upgrade fit quality from a batch of standard components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tolerance Unit Formula (Shaft)

**Given:** Nominal shaft diameter $D$ mm.

**Step 1 — Empirical Basis:**
IS 919 derived the tolerance unit from empirical observations of manufacturing capability versus diameter. For small diameters, tolerance varies as $\sqrt[3]{D}$; for large diameters, it grows linearly with $D$.

**Step 2 — Combine the Two Effects:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tolerance Unit } i &\propto \sqrt[3]{D} \\
\text{Tolerance Unit } i &\propto D \\
\therefore \quad i &= A \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + B \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Standard Coefficients (IS 919):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad [\text{in } \mu m]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note:** $0.45$ and $0.001$ are experimentally determined constants ensuring the calculated $i$ matches tabulated standard values across the entire size range.

---

### 3.2 Worked Problem 1 — Calculating Limits for a Hole Basis Fit

**Problem:** A **60 H8/h7** fit is specified. Given:
- Geometric mean of size range (50–80 mm) = $D = 63.25$ mm
- IT8 standard tolerance for hole (50–80 mm) = **46 $\mu m$ = 0.046 mm**
- IT7 standard tolerance for shaft (50–80 mm) = **30 $\mu m$ = 0.030 mm**
- Fundamental deviation for **H** hole: $EI = 0$
- Fundamental deviation for **h** shaft: $es = 0$

**Find:** The limits for hole and shaft, tolerance, allowance, and type of fit.

**Step 1 — Hole limits:**
For H-hole, $EI = 0$. So minimum limit is at the nominal size line.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{min,Hole} &= D + EI = 60 + 0 = 60.000 \text{ mm} \\
L_{max,Hole} &= L_{min,Hole} + T_{Hole} = 60.000 + 0.046 = 60.046 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Shaft limits:**
For h-shaft, $es = 0$. So maximum limit is at the nominal size line.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max,Shaft} &= D + es = 60 + 0 = 60.000 \text{ mm} \\
L_{min,Shaft} &= L_{max,Shaft} - T_{Shaft} = 60.000 - 0.030 = 59.970 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Fit Type:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Clearance}_{min} &= L_{min,Hole} - L_{max,Shaft} = 60.000 - 60.000 = 0.000 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Clearance}_{max} &= L_{max,Hole} - L_{min,Shaft} = 60.046 - 59.970 = 0.076 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since clearance is always $\geq 0$, this is a **Clearance Fit**. Specifically, because the minimum clearance is zero, it is a **Sliding Fit (also called Locational Clearance)**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Problem 2 — Interference Fit

**Problem:** A **60 H7/p6** fit. Given:
- IT7 hole tolerance = **30 $\mu m$ = 0.030 mm**
- IT6 shaft tolerance = **19 $\mu m$ = 0.019 mm**
- Fundamental deviation for **p-shaft** (50–80 mm range): $ei = +26 \text{ } \mu m = +0.026 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Hole limits (H7):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{min,Hole} &= 60 + 0 = 60.000 \text{ mm} \\
L_{max,Hole} &= 60 + 0.030 = 60.030 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Shaft limits (p6):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{min,Shaft} &= 60 + 0.026 = 60.026 \text{ mm} \\
L_{max,Shaft} &= L_{min,Shaft} + T_{Shaft} = 60.026 + 0.019 = 60.045 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fit Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interference}_{min} &= L_{max,Hole} - L_{min,Shaft} = 60.030 - 60.026 = 0.004 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Interference}_{max} &= L_{min,Hole} - L_{max,Shaft} = 60.000 - 60.045 = -0.045 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Negative result means **shaft is always larger than hole** — a **Pure Interference (Press) Fit**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Fit Type

| Test Condition | Result | Fit Type |
|---|---|---|
| $L_{min,Hole} > L_{max,Shaft}$ | Always loose | **Clearance Fit** |
| $L_{max,Hole} < L_{min,Shaft}$ | Always tight | **Interference Fit** |
| $L_{min,Hole} \leq L_{max,Shaft}$ AND $L_{max,Hole} \geq L_{min,Shaft}$ | Sometimes loose, sometimes tight | **Transition Fit** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show the **fit equation** with full numerical substitution and the **final value in mm with 3 decimal places**. Never leave answers in $\mu m$ without converting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Steps for Solving a Limit Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Fit Designation e.g. 60 H8 f7] --> B[Identify Nominal Size D]
    B --> C[Determine Size Range and Geometric Mean]
    C --> D[Look up IT Grade Tolerances for Hole and Shaft]
    D --> E[Identify Fundamental Deviations ES/es and EI/ei]
    E --> F[Calculate Hole Limits Lmax H and Lmin H]
    F --> G[Calculate Shaft Limits Lmax S and Lmin S]
    G --> H[Compute Clearance or Interference]
    H --> I{Is it always loose?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Clearance Fit]
    I -- No --> K{Is it always tight?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Interference Fit]
    K -- No --> M[Transition Fit]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Tolerance Zone Positions for Holes & Shafts

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Zero_Line[Zero Line Nominal Size D]
    ZL((D))
    end
    subgraph H_Zone[Hole H Position]
    H_L[Lmin at D]
    H_R[Lmax above D]
    H_L --> H_R
    end
    subgraph h_Zone[Shaft h Position]
    h_R[Lmax at D]
    h_L[Lmin below D]
    h_R --> h_L
    end
    subgraph p_Zone[Shaft p Position]
    p_L[Lmin above D]
    p_R[Lmax further above D]
    p_L --> p_R
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Architecture of the ISO Limit System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Nominal Size D] --> RangeStep[Determine Size Range]
    RangeStep --> ITLookup[Select IT Grade]
    ITLookup --> DevLookup[Select Deviation Letter]
    DevLookup --> CalcT[Compute Tolerance T]
    CalcT --> CalcLimits[Compute Lmax and Lmin]
    CalcLimits --> MatPair[Pair Hole and Shaft]
    MatPair --> FitType[Classify Fit Type]
```

### 4.4 Reference Table Block Diagram — IT Grades & Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[IT01 to IT5] --> App1[Precision Instruments Gauges]
    G2[IT6 to IT8] --> App2[General Engineering Fits]
    G3[IT9 to IT11] --> App3[Rough Machining Loose Fits]
    G4[IT12 to IT18] --> App4[Sheet Metal Rolling Castings]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the terms *Tolerance*, *Allowance*, and *Fit* as applied in a limits and fits system. Mention their units.

**Model Answer:**
- **Tolerance ($T$):** The total permissible variation in the size of a part, equal to the difference between maximum and minimum limits. $T = L_{max} - L_{min}$. Unit: **mm**.
- **Allowance ($A$):** The intentional difference between the maximum material condition of mating parts; it is the minimum clearance or maximum interference. Unit: **mm**.
- **Fit:** The degree of tightness or looseness between two mating parts (a hole and a shaft). Unitless as a term but quantified in mm.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State the **IS 919 / ISO 286** standard and explain the difference between **Hole Basis System** and **Shaft Basis System**.

**Model Answer:**
- **IS 919 : 1993** is the Indian Standard (aligned with ISO 286) that specifies the fundamental rules for the ISO system of limits and fits.
- **Hole Basis System:** The **hole** is kept constant at the basic size (minimum limit = nominal, with H designation), and the required fit is obtained by varying the **shaft** tolerance.
- **Shaft Basis System:** The **shaft** is kept constant (maximum limit = nominal, with h designation), and the fit is obtained by varying the **hole** tolerance.
- **Industry Preference:** The **Hole Basis System** is universally preferred because standard drills, reamers, and broaches are cheaper to produce than corresponding shaft tools.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Limits & Interference Fit Analysis)*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A fit is designated as **80 H7/p6**. Given:
- Size range 63–80 mm, geometric mean $D = 71.65$ mm.
- IT7 tolerance (hole, 50–80 range) = **30 $\mu m$**.
- IT6 tolerance (shaft, 50–80 range) = **19 $\mu m$**.
- For shaft **p**, fundamental lower deviation $ei = +26 \text{ } \mu m$.
- For hole **H**, $EI = 0$.

Calculate the **limits of hole and shaft**, and identify the **type of fit** with full justification. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **ISO System of Limits and Fits** with a neat sketch showing tolerance zone positions for hole basis fits **H7/g6**, **H7/h6**, and **H7/k6**. State one engineering application for each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hole Limits (80 H7):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{min,Hole} &= D + EI = 80 + 0 = 80.000 \text{ mm} \\
L_{max,Hole} &= 80.000 + 0.030 = 80.030 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shaft Limits (80 p6):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{min,Shaft} &= 80 + 0.026 = 80.026 \text{ mm} \\
L_{max,Shaft} &= 80.026 + 0.019 = 80.045 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fit Classification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interference}_{min} &= L_{max,Hole} - L_{min,Shaft} = 80.030 - 80.026 = +0.004 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Interference}_{max} &= L_{min,Hole} - L_{max,Shaft} = 80.000 - 80.045 = -0.045 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The shaft is **always larger** than the hole → **Interference (Press) Fit**. This fit is used for permanent couplings, bushings pressed into housings, and gear hubs on shafts.

**(b) ISO System Explanation:**

The ISO system uses three elements: **IT Grade** (tolerance magnitude), **Fundamental Deviation** (tolerance position), and **Nominal Size Step**. Each tolerance is described by a letter+number code (e.g., H7).

**Tolerance Zone Sketch (text representation):**

```
      ZERO LINE      |
       (D)           |
                     |    ___________
H7/g6 (Clearance):   |   | hole H7  |     <-- Shaft g6 below
                     |   |__________|
                     |   | shaft g6 |
                     |    __________
H7/h6 (Locational):  |   | hole H7  |
                     |   |__________|
                     |       shaft h6 (touching zero)
                     |
                     |    __________
H7/k6 (Transition):  |   | hole H7  |
                     |   |__________|
                     |      shaft k6 straddles below & overlaps
```

**Applications:**
- **H7/g6** → Sliding fits, machine tool slideways, piston in cylinder.
- **H7/h6** → Locational fits, gear mounting on shafts, bearings.
- **H7/k6** → Easy assembly/disassembly with light press, pulleys, couplings.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the **step-by-step procedure** to determine the limits of a hole and shaft when a fit is specified. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **standard tolerance unit formula** for a shaft as per IS 919, and calculate the tolerance unit for a nominal diameter $D = 50$ mm. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**
1. Read the fit designation, e.g., `40 H8/f7`.
2. Extract nominal size $D = 40$ mm; identify the size step (30–50 mm).
3. Determine the **IT grade** tolerances: IT8 (hole) = 39 $\mu m$, IT7 (shaft) = 25 $\mu m$.
4. Identify **fundamental deviations**: $EI = 0$ for H; $es = -25 \text{ } \mu m$ for f.
5. Compute $L_{max,Hole} = D + ES = D + (EI + T) = 40 + 0.039 = 40.039$ mm.
6. Compute $L_{max,Shaft} = D + es = 40 - 0.025 = 39.975$ mm; $L_{min,Shaft} = 39.975 - 0.025 = 39.950$ mm.
7. Compare: Clearance exists → **Clearance Fit (Running Fit)**.

**(b) Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For shafts: } i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad \text{(in } \mu m) \\
\text{For holes: } \quad I &= 0.4 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.010 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D^2} \quad \text{(in } \mu m)
\end{aligned}
$$

These empirical formulas are derived from the principle that manufacturing tolerance scales as the **cube root of diameter** (for fine precision) plus a **linear term** (for coarse tolerance growth at large diameters). The constants are calibrated to match tabulated IS 919 values.

**Numerical Calculation for $D = 50$ mm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50 \\
\sqrt[3]{50} &= 3.6840 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot 3.6840 + 0.001 \cdot 50 \\
i &= 1.6578 + 0.0500 \\
i &= 1.7078 \text{ } \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The tolerance unit $i$ is the *base unit*; the actual tolerance for a given IT grade is $T = K \cdot i$, where $K$ is the IT grade multiplier (e.g., $K = 10$ for IT5, $K = 64$ for IT11).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Capital vs Lowercase:** Writing `ES` for a shaft (must be `es`) or `ei` for a hole (must be `EI`) — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $\mu m$ and mm without conversion. Always convert to **mm with 3 decimal places** in the final answer.
> 3. **Forgetting Sign of Deviations:** For shaft `p` or `r`, lower deviation is **positive** (above zero line) — students often incorrectly subtract.
> 4. **No Sketch in Limit Questions:** A schematic of the tolerance zone is worth **at least 2 marks** — never skip it.
> 5. **Skipping Fit Justification:** KTU examiners require explicit comparison of $L_{min,Hole}$ vs $L_{max,Shaft}$ and $L_{max,Hole}$ vs $L_{min,Shaft}$ — not just naming the fit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - Limits = Max & Min permissible sizes; Tolerance = difference between them.
> - **Hole Basis System** (preferred in industry): Hole is constant, shaft varies.
> - **Hole H**: $EI = 0$ (min limit = nominal). **Shaft h**: $es = 0$ (max limit = nominal).
> - 18 IT grades: IT01 (finest) → IT18 (coarsest).
> - **Clearance Fit** ⇔ Hole always bigger. **Interference Fit** ⇔ Shaft always bigger. **Transition Fit** ⇔ Mixed.
> - Shaft tolerance unit: $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ in $\mu m$.
> - Hole tolerance unit: $I = 0.4 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.010 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D^2}$ in $\mu m$.
> - Allowance = minimum clearance (positive) or maximum interference (negative).
> - IS 919 : 1993 = Indian Standard for Limits and Fits (aligned with ISO 286).
> - Always show a **sketch** of tolerance zones for full marks.
> - Convert all final answers to **mm** with at least **3 decimal places**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fits and Tolerances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fits and Tolerances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Engineering Metrology and Tool Design, **Fits and Tolerances** form the foundation of interchangeable manufacturing. The terms are defined as follows:

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definitions (As per IS 919-1963 / ISO 286)
> - **Tolerance**: It is the difference between the upper limit and the lower limit of a dimension. It represents the permissible variation in the size of a part.
> - **Allowance**: It is the minimum clearance (in clearance fits) or maximum interference (in interference fits) between mating parts. It is the intentional, designed difference between the basic sizes of the hole and shaft.
> - **Fit**: It is the degree of tightness or looseness between two mating parts (a hole and a shaft) when assembled together.
> - **Nominal (Basic) Size**: The standard or reference size from which limits of size are derived by applying tolerances.
> - **Actual Size**: The measured size of the manufactured part.
> - **Limits**: The extreme permissible sizes (upper limit and lower limit) within which the part must lie to be acceptable.

Mathematically, the primary relationship is:

$$ \text{Tolerance} = \text{Upper Limit} - \text{Lower Limit} $$

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Think about a **wooden door and its frame** in a house:
- The door must fit into the frame so that it opens/closes smoothly but doesn't wobble.
- If the door is too large, it will scrape. If too small, it will rattle.
- The carpenter decides an **allowance** (a small gap) for free movement.
- The **tolerance** is how much variation the carpenter allows during cutting the wood — maybe ±2 mm.

In manufacturing, we cannot make every part to an *exact* size due to machine limitations. So, we define a **permissible range** of variation. When two parts (hole + shaft) with these ranges come together, the *combination* of their individual tolerances determines whether the fit is loose, snug, or pressed.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in KTU & Industry

- **Interchangeability**: Parts made in different batches/countries must assemble without rework.
- **Cost Optimization**: Tighter tolerances = higher cost. We choose only as much accuracy as needed.
- **Functional Performance**: A bearing needs a clearance fit; a rivet needs interference; a gear hub can be a transition fit.
- **International Standardization**: ISO 286 ensures global parts speak a common dimensional language.

> [!NOTE] KTU 2024 Exam Hot Spot
> Questions are frequently asked on: identifying fit types from given dimensions, calculating allowances/tolerances, and comparing **hole-basis vs shaft-basis** systems. A 14-mark numerical on H7/g6 or similar fits is almost guaranteed every academic year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Key Nomenclature (Memorize This!)

- **Hole**: The internal (enclosing) feature of a part — always considered as the "**H**" reference.
- **Shaft**: The external (enclosed) feature — "**S**" reference.
- **Upper Deviation**: The algebraic difference between the upper limit and the basic size.
- **Lower Deviation**: The algebraic difference between the lower limit and the basic size.
- **Fundamental Deviation**: The deviation (upper or lower) closest to the basic size. It defines the **position** of the tolerance zone with respect to the basic size.
- **Tolerance Grade**: A standardized set of tolerance magnitudes. ISO defines grades **IT01, IT0, IT1 ... IT16, IT17, IT18** (in order of increasing size).

### 2.2 The Two Reference Systems

| Feature | Hole-Basis System | Shaft-Basis System |
|---|---|---|
| **Hole is taken as** | Reference (Hole size is **fixed**) | Variable |
| **Shaft is taken as** | Variable | Reference (Shaft size is **fixed**) |
| **Standard hole/shaft** | H (Hole) | h (Shaft) |
| **Lower deviation of hole** | EI = 0 (Hole's lower limit = Basic size) | Variable |
| **Upper deviation of shaft** | Variable | es = 0 (Shaft's upper limit = Basic size) |
| **Why preferred?** | Easier & cheaper to make standard tools (drills, reamers) | Used where standard shafts are required |
| **ISO Preference** | **Strongly preferred (90% of fits)** | Used in <10% of cases |

> [!IMPORTANT] For a **hole-basis fit**, the hole is always **H** (lower deviation EI = 0). The shaft tolerance zone is shifted to get the required fit.
> For a **shaft-basis fit**, the shaft is always **h** (upper deviation es = 0). The hole tolerance zone is shifted.

### 2.3 Classification of Fits

Based on the relationship between the limits of the hole and shaft, fits are classified into three groups:

1. **Clearance Fit (LOA - Limits of Allowance)**
   - Always positive clearance between hole and shaft.
   - Maximum clearance = Upper Limit of Hole − Lower Limit of Shaft
   - Minimum clearance = Lower Limit of Hole − Upper Limit of Shaft
   - Used for: sliding parts, bearings, pulleys, machine tool spindles.
   - Examples: **H7/g6, H7/f7, H11/c11**

2. **Interference Fit (Force Fit / Shrink Fit)**
   - Always negative clearance (i.e., the shaft is larger than the hole, requiring force to assemble).
   - Maximum interference = Lower Limit of Hole − Upper Limit of Shaft
   - Minimum interference = Upper Limit of Hole − Lower Limit of Shaft
   - Used for: permanent couplings, bushings, gear blanks on shafts.
   - Examples: **H7/p6, H7/s6, H7/u6**

3. **Transition Fit**
   - May result in either clearance or interference depending on the actual sizes produced.
   - Used for: parts requiring accurate location but not heavy load transmission.
   - Examples: **H7/k6, H7/js6, H7/m6**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] All units are in **micrometers (μm)** when using ISO 286 standard tables, and **millimeters (mm)** for final dimensions.

| # | Quantity | Formula |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | Tolerance | $T = UL - LL$ |
| 2 | Upper Deviation (Shaft) | $es = UL_S - \text{Basic Size}$ |
| 3 | Lower Deviation (Shaft) | $ei = LL_S - \text{Basic Size}$ |
| 4 | Upper Deviation (Hole) | $ES = UL_H - \text{Basic Size}$ |
| 5 | Lower Deviation (Hole) | $EI = LL_H - \text{Basic Size}$ |
| 6 | Shaft Tolerance | $T_S = es - ei$ |
| 7 | Hole Tolerance | $T_H = ES - EI$ |
| 8 | Maximum Clearance | $C_{max} = ES - ei$ |
| 9 | Minimum Clearance | $C_{min} = EI - es$ |
| 10 | Maximum Interference | $I_{max} = EI - es$ |
| 11 | Minimum Interference | $I_{min} = ES - ei$ |
| 12 | Allowance (Hole-basis) | $A = EI_{hole} - es_{shaft} = -es$ (since $EI=0$ for H) |
| 13 | Standard Tolerance (IT Grade) | $T = a \cdot i$ (where $i$ is the standard tolerance unit) |
| 14 | Standard Tolerance Unit | $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$ (in μm, D in mm) |

> [!NOTE] The standard tolerance unit **i** depends only on the nominal size. It is tabulated for size ranges like 0-3, 3-6, 6-10, 10-18, 18-30, 30-50, 50-80, 80-120 mm, etc.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: Piston-cylinder fit, crankshaft bearings, valve guides.
- **Aerospace**: High-precision fits in turbine assemblies.
- **Tool Design**: Punch-die clearances in metal forming depend directly on sheet thickness tolerance.
- **Machine Design**: Bearing fits (loose on rotating rings, tight on stationary rings).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Standard Tolerance Unit

The **standard tolerance unit (i)** in the ISO 286 system is derived empirically to represent the precision achievable by a typical manufacturing process. It is a function of the nominal diameter $D$.

The relationship, valid for nominal sizes up to 500 mm, is:

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad \text{(in μm, D in mm)} $$

**Derivation Logic:**
- $D^{1/3}$ term: Reflects that relative tolerance decreases as size increases. Smaller parts are harder to make accurately to an absolute value.
- $0.001 \cdot D$ term: A linear correction to account for elastic deformation and temperature-induced dimensional changes in larger parts.

Each IT grade has a specific multiplier $a$:

| IT Grade | IT5 | IT6 | IT7 | IT8 | IT9 | IT10 | IT11 |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|------|
| **Multiplier (a)** | 7 | 10 | 16 | 25 | 40 | 64 | 100 |

So: $\quad T = a \cdot i$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Hole-Basis H7/g6 at 25 mm)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A shaft of $\phi 25$ mm and a hole of $\phi 25$ mm are to be assembled with an **H7/g6** fit in the **hole-basis system**. Determine:
> (a) The tolerance limits for the hole and shaft.
> (b) The type of fit.
> (c) The maximum and minimum clearance.
> (d) The allowance.

#### Step 1: Identify Standard Tolerance Values
For a nominal diameter of 25 mm, the size range 18–30 mm applies (using ISO 286 standard table).

$$ i = 0.45 \cdot (25)^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot (25) $$

$$ = 0.45 \cdot 2.924 + 0.025 $$

$$ = 1.316 + 0.025 = 1.341 \ \mu m $$

Applying the multipliers:
$$ IT7 = 16 \cdot i = 16 \cdot 1.341 = 21.46 \ \approx 21 \ \mu m $$
$$ IT6 = 10 \cdot i = 10 \cdot 1.341 = 13.41 \ \approx 13 \ \mu m $$

> [!NOTE] In KTU exams, you may directly use the tabulated values: **IT7 (25 mm) = 21 μm** and **IT6 (25 mm) = 13 μm**. No need to derive $i$ from scratch — but know the formula.

#### Step 2: Hole (H7) Limits
- Lower deviation of H hole: $EI = 0$
- Upper deviation: $ES = +IT7 = +21 \ \mu m = +0.021 \ mm$

Therefore:
$$ LL_H = 25.000 \ mm, \quad UL_H = 25.021 \ mm $$

#### Step 3: Shaft (g6) Limits
For 'g' grade shaft (in range 18–30 mm), the upper deviation from standard tables is:

$$ es = -7 \ \mu m = -0.007 \ mm $$

The lower deviation:
$$ ei = es - IT6 = -7 - 13 = -20 \ \mu m = -0.020 \ mm $$

Therefore:
$$ UL_S = 24.993 \ mm, \quad LL_S = 24.980 \ mm $$

#### Step 4: Type of Fit
Since $LL_S < UL_S < LL_H < UL_H$, we have a **Clearance Fit (LOA)**.

#### Step 5: Maximum & Minimum Clearance
$$ C_{max} = UL_H - LL_S = 25.021 - 24.980 = 0.041 \ mm = 41 \ \mu m $$

$$ C_{min} = LL_H - UL_S = 25.000 - 24.993 = 0.007 \ mm = 7 \ \mu m $$

#### Step 6: Allowance
In a hole-basis system, allowance = minimum clearance:
$$ \text{Allowance} = C_{min} = 7 \ \mu m $$

### 3.3 Second Worked Example (Hole-Basis H7/k6 at 50 mm) — Transition Fit

> **Problem:** Determine the type of fit, maximum clearance, and maximum interference for an H7/k6 fit at 50 mm basic size.

**Step 1: Tolerance values at 50 mm (range 30–50 mm):**
- IT7 at 50 mm = 25 μm
- IT6 at 50 mm = 16 μm

**Step 2: Hole (H7):**
- $EI = 0$, $ES = +0.025$ mm
- $LL_H = 50.000$ mm, $UL_H = 50.025$ mm

**Step 3: Shaft (k6):**
For 'k' shaft at 30–50 mm, the lower deviation from tables:
$$ ei = +18 \ \mu m = +0.018 \ mm $$
$$ es = ei + IT6 = 18 + 16 = +34 \ \mu m = +0.034 \ mm $$
$$ LL_S = 50.018 \ mm, \quad UL_S = 50.034 \ mm $$

**Step 4: Fit Analysis:**
- $LL_H (50.000) < LL_S (50.018) < UL_S (50.034) < UL_H (50.025)$? No, this isn't the case.
- $LL_S (50.018) > LL_H (50.000)$ → indicates the shaft is always at least slightly larger at its lower limit than the hole at its lower limit.
- Compare $UL_H (50.025)$ with $UL_S (50.034)$: The hole's upper limit is **smaller** than shaft's upper limit.

$$ C_{max} = ES_H - ei_S = 0.025 - 0.018 = +0.007 \ mm \ \text{(clearance possible)} $$

$$ I_{max} = EI_H - es_S = 0 - 0.034 = -0.034 \ mm \ \text{(interference possible)} $$

Since we can get **either clearance OR interference**, this is a **Transition Fit**.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Condition on Dimensions | Resulting Fit |
|---|---|
| $LL_S < UL_S < LL_H < UL_H$ | **Clearance Fit** (always clearance) |
| $LL_H < LL_S < UL_H < UL_S$ | **Interference Fit** (always interference) |
| Overlap of shaft range into hole range | **Transition Fit** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Classification of Fits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Fits and Tolerances System"] --> nodeB["Tolerance Zone Position"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Fit Type Classification"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Hole Basis H System"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Shaft Basis h System"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Clearance Fit LOA"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Transition Fit"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Interference Fit"]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["Free Running H7g6"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["Sliding H7f7"]
    nodeF --> nodeF3["Loose Running H11c11"]
    nodeG --> nodeG1["Push Fit H7k6"]
    nodeG --> nodeG2["Driving Fit H7js6"]
    nodeG --> nodeG3["Precision H7m6"]
    nodeH --> nodeH1["Force Fit H7p6"]
    nodeH --> nodeH2["Shrink Fit H7s6"]
    nodeH --> nodeH3["Heavy Force H7u6"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Tolerance Zone Positioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Basic_Size_Line["BASIC SIZE 25 mm Reference Line"]
        nodeLLH["LL Hole H7 equals 25.000 mm"]
        nodeULH["UL Hole H7 equals 25.021 mm"]
        nodeULS["UL Shaft g6 equals 24.993 mm"]
        nodeLLS["LL Shaft g6 equals 24.980 mm"]
    end
    nodeLLS -->|"gap 0.007 mm Minimum Clearance"| nodeULS
    nodeULS -->|"gap 0.028 mm"| nodeLLH
    nodeLLH -->|"tol 0.021 mm Hole IT7"| nodeULH
    nodeULH -->|"gap 0.013 mm"| nodeLLS2["Loop back to LLS"]
    Basic_Size_Line -->|"Hole zone is entirely above basic size in H7 and above shaft zone"| note2["Result Clearance Fit"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Hole-Basis vs Shaft-Basis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HoleBasis["Hole Basis System H Standard"]
        H1["Hole H7: EI equals 0 fixed reference"]
        H2["Shaft varies: g6 k6 p6 etc"]
        H3["Tools: standard reamers broaches preferred"]
    end
    subgraph ShaftBasis["Shaft Basis System h Standard"]
        S1["Shaft h6: es equals 0 fixed reference"]
        S2["Hole varies: G7 K7 P7 etc"]
        S3["Tools: standard shafts preferred"]
    end
    HoleBasis --> ISO["ISO 286 International Standard"]
    ShaftBasis --> ISO
    ISO --> App["Interchangeable global manufacturing"]
```

### 4.4 Block Functional Architecture: Selection of a Fit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Design Requirement"] --> q1{"Type of motion needed?"}
    q1 -->|"Free rotation sliding"| clear["Clearance Fit H7g6 or H7f7"]
    q1 -->|"Accurate location some force"| trans["Transition Fit H7k6 or H7js6"]
    q1 -->|"Permanent rigid assembly"| interf["Interference Fit H7p6 or H7s6"]
    clear --> chk1["Check load and lubrication"]
    trans --> chk2["Check frequency of disassembly"]
    interf --> chk3["Check material yield strength"]
    chk1 --> finalize["Finalize ISO fit designation"]
    chk2 --> finalize
    chk3 --> finalize
    finalize --> doc["Document on engineering drawing with tolerance symbols"]
```
