<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the terms: (i) Tolerance, (ii) Allowance, and (iii) Fit. State the difference between hole-basis and shaft-basis systems.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Tolerance:** It is the difference between the maximum (upper) and minimum (lower) permissible limits of a dimension. It represents the total allowable variation in part size.

$$ T = UL - LL $$

**(ii) Allowance:** It is the minimum clearance (in clearance fit) or maximum interference (in interference fit) between mating parts. It is the intentional difference built into the design.

**(iii) Fit:** It is the relationship between two mating parts (hole and shaft) with respect to their ease of assembly — i.e., whether they are loose, snug, or pressed.

| Hole-Basis | Shaft-Basis |
|---|---|
| Hole is fixed (H standard, EI = 0) | Shaft is fixed (h standard, es = 0) |
| Shaft tolerance zone is varied | Hole tolerance zone is varied |
| Preferred by ISO (90% of cases) | Used for specific applications |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the three main types of fits with one example and one application each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Type of Fit | ISO Example | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Clearance Fit (always clearance) | H7/g6 | Sliding gears, bearing inner rings, machine tool spindles |
| Transition Fit (clearance or interference) | H7/k6 | Couplings, locating pins, keyways |
| Interference Fit (always interference) | H7/s6 | Permanent couplings, bushings, flywheel hubs |

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Valuation Pitfall
> Many students **mix up** the formulas for max/min clearance vs interference. Always remember: **Maximum Clearance** uses **Upper Limit of Hole** and **Lower Limit of Shaft** (the "outermost" points). **Minimum Clearance** uses the **inner-most** points. Mixing these is the #1 reason for full-mark loss.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A hole and shaft assembly is designated as **50 H7/p6** in the hole-basis system. Determine:
(i) Tolerance limits for the hole and shaft
(ii) The type of fit
(iii) Maximum and minimum interference
(iv) Allowance

**(b)** Explain the ISO system of limits and fits. Discuss the role of fundamental deviation and tolerance grade.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:** Basic size = 50 mm, Fit = H7/p6 (Hole-basis)

**Standard values for 50 mm (size range 30–50 mm):**
- IT7 = 25 μm = 0.025 mm
- IT6 = 16 μm = 0.016 mm

**Step 1: Hole (H7)**
- Lower deviation of H: $EI = 0$
- Upper deviation: $ES = +0.025$ mm
- $LL_H = 50.000$ mm
- $UL_H = 50.025$ mm

**Step 2: Shaft (p6)**
For 'p' shaft in range 30–50 mm, fundamental deviation (lower deviation):
$$ ei = +26 \ \mu m = +0.026 \ mm $$
$$ es = ei + IT6 = 26 + 16 = +42 \ \mu m = +0.042 \ mm $$
- $LL_S = 50.026$ mm
- $UL_S = 50.042$ mm

**Step 3: Type of Fit**
Since $LL_S (50.026) > UL_H (50.025)$, the shaft is **always** larger than the hole → **Interference Fit (Force Fit)**.

**Step 4: Maximum & Minimum Interference**
$$ I_{max} = EI_H - es_S = 0 - 0.042 = -0.042 \ mm $$
$$ I_{min} = ES_H - ei_S = 0.025 - 0.026 = -0.001 \ mm $$

**Step 5: Allowance**
In interference fit: Allowance = Maximum Interference = **0.042 mm** (i.e., −0.042 mm)

**Model Solution (b):**

The **ISO System of Limits and Fits** (ISO 286) is the international standard that defines a system of tolerances and deviations for the manufacture of mating parts to ensure **interchangeability** on a global scale.

**Key Components:**

1. **Basic Size (Nominal Size):** The reference size used to identify the part (e.g., 25 mm, 50 mm).

2. **Tolerance Grade:** Defines the *magnitude* of the tolerance zone. There are 20 grades: IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2, ..., IT18. Higher grade numbers = larger tolerance = coarser part.

3. **Tolerance Position (Fundamental Deviation):** Defines the *location* of the tolerance zone relative to the basic size.
   - For shafts: 'a' to 'h' have upper deviation (es) as fundamental deviation (zone below basic size).
   - For shafts: 'j' to 'zc' have lower deviation (ei) as fundamental deviation (zone above basic size).
   - For holes: Capital letters A to H have lower deviation (EI) as fundamental; J to ZC have upper deviation (ES) as fundamental.

4. **Hole-Basis (H) System:** Hole is fixed at H (EI = 0). Shaft letter defines fit. **Strongly preferred by ISO** because standard cutting tools (drills, reamers) produce standard hole sizes economically.

5. **Shaft-Basis (h) System:** Shaft is fixed at h (es = 0). Hole letter defines fit. Used when shaft is standardized (e.g., long shafts, bearing shafts).

**Designation Example:** **50 H7/p6**
- 50 = basic size
- H7 = hole, H position, IT7 grade
- p6 = shaft, p position, IT6 grade

This system allows any engineer in the world to interpret a fit symbol and manufacture to that specification.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** For a **25 H8/e9** hole-basis fit, determine:
(i) The limits of size for the hole and shaft
(ii) Type of fit
(iii) Maximum and minimum clearance
(iv) Allowance

**(b)** Discuss with neat sketches: (i) Clearance fit, (ii) Interference fit, (iii) Transition fit. Show tolerance zones for hole and shaft in each case.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:** Basic size = 25 mm, Fit = H8/e9 (Hole-basis)

**Standard values for 25 mm (range 18–30 mm):**
- IT8 = 33 μm = 0.033 mm
- IT9 = 52 μm = 0.052 mm

**Step 1: Hole (H8)**
- $EI = 0$
- $ES = +0.033$ mm
- $LL_H = 25.000$ mm
- $UL_H = 25.033$ mm

**Step 2: Shaft (e9)**
For 'e' shaft in 18–30 mm range, upper deviation:
$$ es = -32 \ \mu m = -0.032 \ mm $$
$$ ei = es - IT9 = -32 - 52 = -84 \ \mu m = -0.084 \ mm $$
- $UL_S = 24.968$ mm
- $LL_S = 24.916$ mm

**Step 3: Type of Fit**
$LL_S (24.916) < UL_S (24.968) < LL_H (25.000) < UL_H (25.033)$ → **Clearance Fit**.

**Step 4: Clearance Values**
$$ C_{max} = UL_H - LL_S = 25.033 - 24.916 = 0.117 \ mm $$
$$ C_{min} = LL_H - UL_S = 25.000 - 24.968 = 0.032 \ mm $$

**Step 5: Allowance**
In hole-basis system, allowance = minimum clearance = **0.032 mm**

**Model Solution (b):**

The three types of fits are illustrated below by showing the relative positions of the hole and shaft tolerance zones with respect to the basic size line:

**(i) Clearance Fit:**
- The entire tolerance zone of the shaft lies **below** the tolerance zone of the hole.
- Result: Always a positive gap (clearance) between mating parts.
- Example: H7/g6. Used for freely rotating/sliding parts.

**(ii) Interference Fit:**
- The entire tolerance zone of the shaft lies **above** the tolerance zone of the hole.
- Result: Always interference (negative clearance). Force or heat is required for assembly.
- Example: H7/s6. Used for permanent rigid joints.

**(iii) Transition Fit:**
- The tolerance zones of the hole and shaft **partially overlap**.
- Result: Could be either slight clearance or slight interference depending on actual sizes produced.
- Example: H7/k6. Used for parts that must be located precisely and can be disassembled occasionally.

| Property | Clearance | Interference | Transition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shaft zone position | Below hole zone | Above hole zone | Overlaps hole zone |
| Resulting condition | Always loose | Always tight | Either loose or tight |
| Assembly method | By hand | By force or heat | By light tapping |

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Valuation Warning
> In 14-mark questions, examiners **deduct 2–3 marks** if:
> 1. You fail to **state the type of fit explicitly** (Clearance / Transition / Interference).
> 2. You forget to **convert μm to mm** in the final answer (e.g., writing 41 instead of 0.041 mm).
> 3. You **don't show the relationship** (e.g., $LL_S < UL_S < LL_H < UL_H$) that justifies your fit classification.
> 4. For schematic questions, you draw a "block diagram" instead of a proper **tolerance zone sketch** with the basic size line clearly marked.
> 5. You mix up **shaft deviations** (e, i, es) with **hole deviations** (E, I, ES) — remember **small letters for shafts, capital letters for holes**!

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Tolerance** = $UL - LL$; **Allowance** = minimum clearance or maximum interference; **Fit** = the nature of assembly.
- [ ] **Hole-basis** system: Hole = H (EI = 0), shaft varies. **90% of ISO fits** use this.
- [ ] **Shaft-basis** system: Shaft = h (es = 0), hole varies.
- [ ] Three fit types: **Clearance** (always loose), **Interference** (always tight), **Transition** (overlap).
- [ ] **Maximum Clearance** = $ES - ei$; **Minimum Clearance** = $EI - es$.
- [ ] **Maximum Interference** = $EI - es$; **Minimum Interference** = $ES - ei$.
- [ ] **Allowance (hole-basis)** = $EI_{hole} - es_{shaft} = -es$ (since EI = 0 for H).
- [ ] **IT grade** controls tolerance *magnitude*; **Letter** (a–zc / A–ZC) controls tolerance *position*.
- [ ] Standard tolerance unit $i = 0.45 D^{1/3} + 0.001 D$ (in μm).
- [ ] IT grade multipliers: IT5 = 7i, IT6 = 10i, IT7 = 16i, IT8 = 25i, IT9 = 40i.
- [ ] **Capital letters for holes** (H, K, P); **Small letters for shafts** (h, k, p).
- [ ] 'a' to 'h' shafts have **upper deviation** (es) as fundamental; 'j' to 'zc' have **lower deviation** (ei).
- [ ] 'A' to 'H' holes have **lower deviation** (EI) as fundamental; 'J' to 'ZC' have **upper deviation** (ES).
- [ ] Always convert **μm to mm** in final numerical answers: divide by 1000.
- [ ] Common KTU fits to memorize: H7/g6 (clearance), H7/k6 (transition), H7/p6 (interference).
- [ ] In KTU, tabulated values (e.g., IT7 at 25 mm = 21 μm) may be used directly — no need to derive $i$ unless asked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit Gauge Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limit Gauge Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Limit Gauging** is a method of inspection in which the acceptability of a workpiece dimension is determined by checking whether the workpiece accepts (passes) or rejects (fails) a gauging member of known size, rather than by measuring the actual dimension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taylor's Principle of Gauge Design (The Foundation Rule)**
> *"The GO gauge must check the Maximum Material Condition (MMC), while the NOT-GO gauge must check the Least Material Condition with respect to the surface being inspected, and both should check the dimensional feature simultaneously if possible."*

In simpler KTU-board terminology:
- **GO gauge** (Green end) checks the **lower limit** of a hole / **upper limit** of a shaft (i.e., the most material state).
- **NOT-GO gauge** (Red end) checks the **upper limit** of a hole / **lower limit** of a shaft.

A workpiece is **ACCEPTED** if it **passes through the GO gauge** AND **does NOT enter the NOT-GO gauge**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Gatekeeper at a Border"

Imagine a toll plaza with two automatic gates:
- **Gate 1 (GO gate):** Has an opening slightly *smaller* than the maximum width allowed. If a vehicle can pass through this gate, we know its width is within the permissible *minimum* size (most material).
- **Gate 2 (NO-GO gate):** Has an opening slightly *larger* than the maximum size that should NOT be exceeded. If a vehicle gets stuck or passes through, it is rejected.

The two gates together define a **size corridor** — exactly what a limit gauge does for a manufactured shaft or hole. No actual measurement is taken; only a yes/no (accept/reject) decision is made.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Description |
|------|--------|-------------|
| Workpiece tolerance | $T_h$ (hole) / $T_s$ (shaft) | Total tolerance on the hole/shaft |
| Go gauge tolerance | $T_1$ | 10% of workpiece tolerance |
| No-Go gauge tolerance | $T_2$ | 10% of workpiece tolerance |
| Wear allowance | $W$ | 10% of workpiece tolerance |
| Maximum Material Condition (MMC) | – | Hole smallest / shaft largest |
| Least Material Condition (LMC) | – | Hole largest / shaft smallest |
| Virtual Condition (VC) | – | The boundary of acceptable part size |

> [!NOTE]
> The classical British Standards (BS 969) and IS 3455 prescribe that **T₁ = T₂ = W = 10% of the workpiece tolerance**. This 10% rule is the cornerstone of every KTU numerical problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Limit Gauge Works

The KTU board expects you to clearly outline the following sequence while designing any gauge:

1. **Identify the fit** — Hole basis or shaft basis.
2. **Extract hole limits** ($H_{LL}$, $H_{UL}$) and shaft limits ($S_{LL}$, $S_{UL}$).
3. **Compute workpiece tolerance** ($T_h = H_{UL} - H_{LL}$).
4. **Compute gauge tolerances and wear allowance** using the **10% rule**:
   - $T_1 = T_2 = W = 0.1 \times T_h$
5. **Apply Taylor's Principle** to fix the basic sizes of the Go and No-Go members.
6. **Compute the limits** of each gauging member by adding/subtracting $\frac{T_1}{2}$ or $\frac{T_2}{2}$ from the basic size.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these eight equations — they appear in **every** KTU numerical on limit gauge design.

### A. For a HOLE → Design of PLUG GAUGE

| Member | Basic Size | Tolerance | High Limit (HL) | Low Limit (LL) |
|--------|------------|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Go Plug Gauge** | $H_{LL} + W$ | $\pm \frac{T_1}{2}$ | $H_{LL} + W + \frac{T_1}{2}$ | $H_{LL} + W - \frac{T_1}{2}$ |
| **No-Go Plug Gauge** | $H_{UL}$ | $\pm \frac{T_2}{2}$ | $H_{UL} + \frac{T_2}{2}$ | $H_{UL} - \frac{T_2}{2}$ |

### B. For a SHAFT → Design of RING (SNAP) GAUGE

| Member | Basic Size | Tolerance | High Limit (HL) | Low Limit (LL) |
|--------|------------|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Go Ring Gauge** | $S_{UL} - W$ | $\pm \frac{T_1}{2}$ | $S_{UL} - W + \frac{T_1}{2}$ | $S_{UL} - W - \frac{T_1}{2}$ |
| **No-Go Ring Gauge** | $S_{LL}$ | $\pm \frac{T_2}{2}$ | $S_{LL} + \frac{T_2}{2}$ | $S_{LL} - \frac{T_2}{2}$ |

### C. Other Essential Formulae

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Gauge material coefficient of expansion | $\alpha_g$ ≈ **11.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** (Steel) |
| Workpiece material coefficient of expansion | $\alpha_w$ ≈ **23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** (Steel/Aluminium alloy) |
| Standard reference temperature | **20 °C** |
| Length of plug gauge (general rule) | $L_g \approx 2 \times \text{diameter} + 25 \text{ mm}$ |
| Recommended gauge clearance | $C_g = 0.001 \times \text{diameter (mm)} + 0.05 \text{ mm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL exam warning:** Many students confuse "Go for shaft" with "lower limit". Always remember — **GO checks MMC**: for a shaft, MMC is the **upper** limit; for a hole, MMC is the **lower** limit.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Limit gauges are the **backbone of mass-production inspection lines** because:
- They are **3–10× faster** than measuring instruments (micrometers, CMMs).
- They eliminate operator subjectivity (skill-based error).
- They reduce production cost — a plug gauge costs a few hundred rupees versus a coordinate measuring machine that costs lakhs.
- They are highly suited for **go/no-go decisions in statistical quality control**.

Industrial use cases: automobile engine blocks (cylinder bores), bearing races, fasteners, shaft inspection at bearing manufacturing units (SKF, NBC).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Problems

## 3.1 Standard Derivation of Gauge Limits (Plug Gauge for Hole)

Let us derive the limits of a **GO plug gauge** for a hole from first principles.

**Given:**
- Hole lower limit = $H_{LL}$
- Hole upper limit = $H_{UL}$
- Workpiece tolerance = $T_h = H_{UL} - H_{LL}$
- Wear allowance = $W = 0.1 \times T_h$
- Go gauge tolerance = $T_1 = 0.1 \times T_h$

**Step 1 — Why add wear allowance to Go gauge?**
The Go gauge is used continuously, so it wears. By design, its size should be made slightly *smaller* than $H_{LL}$ on the surface that contacts the hole. To bring this into specification, we add the wear allowance to the nominal size. Hence:

$$
\text{Basic Size of Go Plug} = H_{LL} + W
$$

**Step 2 — Apply gauge tolerance on either side:**

$$
\text{High Limit of Go Plug} = H_{LL} + W + \frac{T_1}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Low Limit of Go Plug} = H_{LL} + W - \frac{T_1}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — No-Go plug is fixed at the upper limit of the hole (Taylor's Principle — No-Go checks LMC for hole):**

$$
\text{Basic Size of No-Go Plug} = H_{UL}
$$

$$
\text{High Limit of No-Go Plug} = H_{UL} + \frac{T_2}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Low Limit of No-Go Plug} = H_{UL} - \frac{T_2}{2}
$$

A similar derivation holds for ring gauges by replacing $H_{LL}$ with $S_{UL}$ and $H_{UL}$ with $S_{LL}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Plug Gauge for Hole (KTU Favourite)

**Problem:** Design a plug gauge (Go and No-Go) for a hole of nominal size **40 mm** with tolerance **+0.030 mm / +0.010 mm**.

### Step 1 — Identify Hole Limits

$$
H_{UL} = 40 + 0.030 = 40.030 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H_{LL} = 40 + 0.010 = 40.010 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Workpiece Tolerance

$$
T_h = H_{UL} - H_{LL} = 40.030 - 40.010 = 0.020 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Gauge Tolerances and Wear Allowance (10% Rule)

$$
W = 0.1 \times 0.020 = 0.002 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T_1 = T_2 = 0.1 \times 0.020 = 0.002 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — GO Plug Gauge Dimensions

Basic size of Go plug:

$$
\text{Go Basic Size} = H_{LL} + W = 40.010 + 0.002 = 40.012 \text{ mm}
$$

Tolerance band: $\pm \frac{T_1}{2} = \pm 0.001 \text{ mm}$

$$
\text{Go High Limit} = 40.012 + 0.001 = 40.013 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go Low Limit} = 40.012 - 0.001 = 40.011 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — NO-GO Plug Gauge Dimensions

Basic size of No-Go plug = $H_{UL} = 40.030$ mm. Tolerance band: $\pm \frac{T_2}{2} = \pm 0.001$ mm.

$$
\text{No-Go High Limit} = 40.030 + 0.001 = 40.031 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go Low Limit} = 40.030 - 0.001 = 40.029 \text{ mm}
$$

### Final Answer Table

| Member | Low Limit (mm) | High Limit (mm) |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| GO Plug Gauge | 40.011 | 40.013 |
| NO-GO Plug Gauge | 40.029 | 40.031 |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Ring Gauge for Shaft

**Problem:** Design a Go and No-Go **ring gauge** for a shaft of size **40 mm** with tolerance **+0.012 mm / +0.002 mm**.

### Step 1 — Shaft Limits

$$
S_{UL} = 40 + 0.012 = 40.012 \text{ mm}, \quad S_{LL} = 40 + 0.002 = 40.002 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Workpiece Tolerance

$$
T_s = S_{UL} - S_{LL} = 40.012 - 40.002 = 0.010 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Gauge Tolerances and Wear Allowance

$$
W = T_1 = T_2 = 0.1 \times 0.010 = 0.001 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — GO Ring Gauge Dimensions

Go ring checks MMC of shaft → upper limit of shaft, minus wear:

$$
\text{Go Basic Size} = S_{UL} - W = 40.012 - 0.001 = 40.011 \text{ mm}
$$

Tolerance band: $\pm 0.0005$ mm

$$
\text{Go High Limit} = 40.0115 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Go Low Limit} = 40.0105 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — NO-GO Ring Gauge Dimensions

No-Go ring checks LMC of shaft → lower limit of shaft:

$$
\text{No-Go Basic Size} = S_{LL} = 40.002 \text{ mm}
$$

Tolerance band: $\pm 0.0005$ mm

$$
\text{No-Go High Limit} = 40.0025 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{No-Go Low Limit} = 40.0015 \text{ mm}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Plug Gauge for a Hole with H7/g6 Fit (Is Standard)

**Problem:** Design a Go and No-Go **plug gauge** for a hole designated **60 H7** (lower deviation = 0 µm, upper deviation = +30 µm).

### Step 1 — Hole Limits

For 60 H7 (per IS 919):

$$
H_{LL} = 60.000 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{UL} = 60.030 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Tolerance

$$
T_h = 0.030 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Gauge Tolerances and Wear Allowance

$$
W = T_1 = T_2 = 0.1 \times 0.030 = 0.003 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Go Plug Gauge

$$
\text{Basic Size} = H_{LL} + W = 60.000 + 0.003 = 60.003 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Tolerance} = \pm \frac{T_1}{2} = \pm 0.0015 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go High Limit} = 60.0045 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Go Low Limit} = 60.0015 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — No-Go Plug Gauge

$$
\text{Basic Size} = H_{UL} = 60.030 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Tolerance} = \pm 0.0015 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go High Limit} = 60.0315 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{No-Go Low Limit} = 60.0285 \text{ mm}
$$

### Verification of Taylor's Principle

A hole is accepted when:
- It **passes through** the **GO plug** (meaning hole size ≥ Go plug high limit of approximately 60.0045 mm → near lower limit of hole)
- It **does NOT enter** the **NO-GO plug** (meaning hole size < No-Go plug low limit of 60.0285 mm → below upper limit of hole)

Both checks are satisfied within the hole's tolerance zone of 60.000–60.030 mm.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Limit Gauge Inspection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Enters Inspection Station] --> B{Is Dimension a HOLE?}
    B -- YES --> C[Use PLUG GAUGE]
    B -- NO --> D[Use RING / SNAP GAUGE]
    C --> E{Does workpiece PASS through GO Plug?}
    E -- NO --> F[REJECT - Below Lower Limit]
    E -- YES --> G{Does workpiece ENTER NO-GO Plug?}
    G -- YES --> H[REJECT - Above Upper Limit]
    G -- NO --> I[ACCEPT - Within Tolerance]
    D --> J{Does workpiece PASS through GO Ring?}
    J -- NO --> K[REJECT - Above Upper Limit]
    J -- YES --> L{Does workpiece ENTER NO-GO Ring?}
    L -- YES --> M[REJECT - Below Lower Limit]
    L -- NO --> N[ACCEPT - Within Tolerance]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Knurled Handle - Handle Body] --> B[Shank]
    B --> C[Wear Pin / GO Plug Cylindrical Member]
    B --> D[NO-GO Plug Cylindrical Member]
    C --> C1{Check at Lower Limit of Hole}
    D --> D1{Check at Upper Limit of Hole}
    C1 --> E[Go Gauge Tolerance ±T1/2]
    D1 --> F[No-Go Gauge Tolerance ±T2/2]
    E --> G[Inspected Workpiece Acceptance Decision]
    F --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State Taylor's Principle of Gauge Design.

**Model Answer:**
Taylor's principle states that the **GO gauge** should check the **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** of the workpiece, while the **NOT-GO gauge** should check the **Least Material Condition (LMC)** — both with reference to the surface being inspected. The GO gauge determines that the dimension is not less than the minimum permissible size, and the NOT-GO gauge ensures that it does not exceed the maximum permissible size. Together, they form a "limit" of acceptable size, eliminating the need for actual measurement.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is meant by **virtual condition** in limit gauging?

**Model Answer:**
The virtual condition of a workpiece is the **boundary of acceptable material** — a theoretical constant value generated by the combined effects of the size tolerance and any geometric tolerance. For an internal feature (hole), the virtual condition equals the **upper size limit minus any geometric tolerance**. For an external feature (shaft), it equals the **lower size limit plus any geometric tolerance**. The No-Go gauge is sized to match this virtual condition so that it never passes beyond the boundary of acceptable material.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): Full Plug + Ring Gauge Design **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design the dimensions of a **GO and NO-GO plug gauge** for a hole of nominal diameter **50 mm** with tolerance **+0.040 mm / +0.020 mm**. State clearly all assumptions and the 10% rule applied.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the same component has a **shaft** with tolerance **+0.010 mm / –0.010 mm** fitted to this hole, design the corresponding **GO and NO-GO ring gauge** dimensions.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Given:**
- Hole limits: $H_{LL} = 50.020$ mm, $H_{UL} = 50.040$ mm
- Hole tolerance: $T_h = 50.040 - 50.020 = 0.020$ mm

**Gauge parameters (10% rule):**

$$
W = T_1 = T_2 = 0.1 \times 0.020 = 0.002 \text{ mm}
$$

**Go Plug Gauge:**

$$
\text{Go Basic Size} = H_{LL} + W = 50.020 + 0.002 = 50.022 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go High Limit} = 50.022 + 0.001 = 50.023 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go Low Limit} = 50.022 - 0.001 = 50.021 \text{ mm}
$$

**No-Go Plug Gauge:**

$$
\text{No-Go Basic Size} = H_{UL} = 50.040 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go High Limit} = 50.040 + 0.001 = 50.041 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go Low Limit} = 50.040 - 0.001 = 50.039 \text{ mm}
$$

| Member | Low Limit (mm) | High Limit (mm) |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| GO Plug | 50.021 | 50.023 |
| NO-GO Plug | 50.039 | 50.041 |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Given shaft limits:**
- $S_{LL} = 49.990$ mm (since 50 – 0.010)
- $S_{UL} = 50.010$ mm (since 50 + 0.010)

**Shaft tolerance:**

$$
T_s = 50.010 - 49.990 = 0.020 \text{ mm}
$$

**Gauge parameters:**

$$
W = T_1 = T_2 = 0.1 \times 0.020 = 0.002 \text{ mm}
$$

**Go Ring Gauge:**

$$
\text{Go Basic Size} = S_{UL} - W = 50.010 - 0.002 = 50.008 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go High Limit} = 50.008 + 0.001 = 50.009 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go Low Limit} = 50.008 - 0.001 = 50.007 \text{ mm}
$$

**No-Go Ring Gauge:**

$$
\text{No-Go Basic Size} = S_{LL} = 49.990 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go High Limit} = 49.990 + 0.001 = 49.991 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go Low Limit} = 49.990 - 0.001 = 49.989 \text{ mm}
$$

| Member | Low Limit (mm) | High Limit (mm) |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| GO Ring | 50.007 | 50.009 |
| NO-GO Ring | 49.989 | 49.991 |

---

### Question B (Choice 2): H7/h6 Fit — Gauge Design with IS Deviations **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A hole is designated **80 H7** and the corresponding shaft is **80 h6** as per IS 919. Determine the limits of the **Go and No-Go plug gauge** for inspecting the hole.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the limits of the **Go and No-Go ring gauge** for inspecting the shaft. Also state clearly the function of **wear allowance** and why it is *added* for the Go plug but *subtracted* for the Go ring.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**From IS 919 (for size range 80 mm):**
- 80 H7: Lower deviation = 0 µm; Upper deviation = +30 µm

Hence:

$$
H_{LL} = 80.000 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{UL} = 80.030 \text{ mm}, \quad T_h = 0.030 \text{ mm}
$$

**Gauge parameters:**

$$
W = T_1 = T_2 = 0.1 \times 0.030 = 0.003 \text{ mm}
$$

**Go Plug Gauge:**

$$
\text{Go Basic} = 80.000 + 0.003 = 80.003 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go High Limit} = 80.003 + 0.0015 = 80.0045 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go Low Limit} = 80.003 - 0.0015 = 80.0015 \text{ mm}
$$

**No-Go Plug Gauge:**

$$
\text{No-Go Basic} = 80.030 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go High Limit} = 80.030 + 0.0015 = 80.0315 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go Low Limit} = 80.030 - 0.0015 = 80.0285 \text{ mm}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**From IS 919 (for size 80 h6):**
- 80 h6: Upper deviation = 0 µm; Lower deviation = –19 µm (per IS table for h6, 80 mm nominal)

$$
S_{LL} = 80.000 - 0.019 = 79.981 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
S_{UL} = 80.000 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T_s = 80.000 - 79.981 = 0.019 \text{ mm}
$$

**Gauge parameters:**

$$
W = T_1 = T_2 = 0.1 \times 0.019 = 0.0019 \text{ mm}
$$

**Go Ring Gauge:**

$$
\text{Go Basic} = S_{UL} - W = 80.000 - 0.0019 = 79.9981 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go High Limit} = 79.9981 + 0.00095 = 79.99905 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Go Low Limit} = 79.9981 - 0.00095 = 79.99715 \text{ mm}
$$

**No-Go Ring Gauge:**

$$
\text{No-Go Basic} = S_{LL} = 79.981 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go High Limit} = 79.981 + 0.00095 = 79.98195 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{No-Go Low Limit} = 79.981 - 0.00095 = 79.98005 \text{ mm}
$$

**Explanation of Wear Allowance Direction:**
- For a **Go plug** entering a hole, wear occurs on the outer cylindrical surface, slowly *reducing* its effective diameter. To compensate, we manufacture the plug **slightly larger** (add wear allowance) so that as it wears, it remains within tolerance.
- For a **Go ring** receiving a shaft, wear occurs on the inner cylindrical surface, slowly *enlarging* the bore. To compensate, we manufacture the ring **slightly smaller** (subtract wear allowance) so that as it wears, it remains within tolerance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusion of GO/NO-GO for shaft vs hole:** The most common error is using $H_{LL}$ for both plug and ring gauges. *Always cross-verify Taylor's Principle: GO = MMC of workpiece, NO-GO = LMC.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the ±T/2 split:** KTU board specifically checks both **upper and lower limits** of each gauging member. Writing only the basic size fetches partial credit. Always compute **HL** and **LL** separately.
>
> 3. **Wrong direction of wear allowance:** Wear allowance is **added** for the Go plug (external wear of plug → size decreases → compensate by oversizing), and **subtracted** for the Go ring (internal wear of ring → size increases → compensate by undersizing). Mixing this is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Skipping the 10% rule derivation:** Examiners award 1–2 marks for explicitly stating the 10% rule from IS/BS. Do not just "apply" it silently.
>
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Always carry units (mm) in every numerical. KTU strictly enforces unit consistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (last-night must-read)**

- **Taylor's Principle** — GO checks MMC; NO-GO checks LMC.
- **10% Rule** — Wear allowance $W = T_1 = T_2 = 10\%$ of workpiece tolerance.
- **Plug gauge (for hole):**
  - GO Basic Size = $H_{LL} + W$
  - NO-GO Basic Size = $H_{UL}$
- **Ring gauge (for shaft):**
  - GO Basic Size = $S_{UL} - W$
  - NO-GO Basic Size = $S_{LL}$
- **Tolerance on each gauge member** is split symmetrically: $\pm T_1/2$ or $\pm T_2/2$.
- **Acceptance rule:** Hole accepted if it passes the GO plug AND does not enter the NO-GO plug.
- **Standard reference temperature:** 20 °C (mandatory assumption unless stated).
- **IS 919 / IS 3455 / BS 969** govern hole and shaft deviations and gauge tolerances.
- **Plug gauge material:** Hardened, ground, and lapped tool steel (e.g., high-carbon high-chromium steel, 60–62 HRC).
- **Form gauges** are used for checking complex profiles (threads, splines, contours).
- **Progressive gauges** combine multiple GO/NO-GO sizes in a single body for stepped parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"**GO Green**, **NO Red**; **GO Greater** for shaft, **GO Lesser** for hole."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods for Verification of Straightness and Flatness in Machine Tools
Use of Interferometry in Straightness and Flatness Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Use of Interferometry in Straightness and Flatness Measurements ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Interferometry** is an optical metrology technique based on the principle of **superposition of two coherent light beams** to produce an interference pattern (fringes) from which precise dimensional and form deviations can be quantitatively evaluated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Straightness** is a geometric tolerance that defines the condition where all points of a line element (on the considered surface or feature) lie within a tolerance zone bounded by two parallel straight lines separated by a specified distance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flatness** is the condition of a surface having all elements confined within two parallel planes separated by a specified distance, measured as the minimum separation between two parallel planes that encompass the entire surface. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pond Ripple" Intuition Imagine throwing two pebbles into a still pond at the same instant. Where the ripples **add together** (crest meets crest), you see large waves. Where they **cancel out** (crest meets trough), the water becomes flat. This is exactly what happens when two beams of light meet: - **Bright fringes** = waves adding (constructive interference). - **Dark fringes** = waves cancelling (destructive interference). - The "ripple spacing" of light is the **wavelength**, denoted $\lambda$. In metrology, we use a **reference surface (optical flat)** placed gently on the workpiece. A microscopic wedge-shaped air gap (typically only a few microns) forms between them. When monochromatic light reflects off both surfaces, the two reflected beams interfere and produce **visible dark and bright bands (fringes)**. Any deviation in flatness or straightness bends or distorts these fringes, which the engineer can directly measure. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Wavelength of sodium D-line light**: $\lambda = 589.3 \text{ nm} = 0.5893 \text{ \mu m}$ - **Wavelength of helium–neon (He-Ne) laser**: $\lambda = 632.8 \text{ nm} = 0.6328 \text{ \mu m}$ - **Standard monochromatic source**: Sodium vapor lamp (most common in workshops) - **Resolution of interferometry**: $\lambda/2 \approx 0.294 \text{ \mu m}$ (≈ one-thousandth of a human hair) - **Typical optical flat diameter**: 100 mm to 300 mm - **Flatness of optical flat itself**: better than $\lambda/10$ ($\approx 0.05 \text{ \mu m}$) > [!TIP] > **Why monochromatic light?** White light contains many wavelengths, which produce colored overlapping fringes that cannot be unambiguously counted. A single wavelength produces sharp, countable black-and-yellow fringes (sodium light). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physics of Thin-Film Interference When an optical flat rests on a test surface, a thin film of air of varying thickness $d$ exists between them. The two reflected beams — one from the **bottom face of the optical flat** and one from the **top of the workpiece** — travel different optical paths. The path difference is: $$\Delta = 2d + \lambda/2$$ The extra $\lambda/2$ accounts for the **phase reversal** that occurs when light reflects from a denser medium (the workpiece surface). - **Constructive interference (bright fringe)** occurs when $\Delta = n\lambda$, i.e., $2d = (n - 1/2)\lambda$ - **Destructive interference (dark fringe)** occurs when $\Delta = (n + 1/2)\lambda$, i.e., $2d = n\lambda$ ### 2.2 Formation of Fringes — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Source**: Monochromatic sodium lamp emits near-monochromatic light ($\lambda = 589.3 \text{ nm}$). 2. **Collimation**: Light passes through a small aperture and becomes a near-parallel beam. 3. **Reference surface**: A precision optical flat is gently placed on the workpiece (gravity holds it; no clamping needed). 4. **Air wedge**: Microscopic tilt or roughness creates a wedge-shaped air film of thickness $d(x)$ varying linearly with position $x$. 5. **Reflection**: Two reflections occur — at the lower surface of the flat and at the workpiece surface. 6. **Interference**: The two reflected rays recombine. Locations of equal $d$ produce a fringe. 7. **Observation**: Locus of points with identical air-gap thickness forms a visible **fringe**. ### 2.3 Reading the Fringes — What the Eye Sees | Fringe Appearance | Surface Condition | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | Straight, equally-spaced, parallel bands | Perfectly flat and parallel surfaces | Test surface is flat; no form error | | Straight, parallel, unequally spaced | Flat but tilted | Surface is flat; tilt is the cause | | Curved (convex toward higher pressure) | Convex hill on workpiece | Local high spot of height $h = N\lambda/2$ | | Curved (concave) | Concave valley | Local depression of depth $h = N\lambda/2$ | | Closely packed fringes | Steep slope | Higher slope in that region | | Widely spaced fringes | Gentle slope | Locally flatter region | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Height deviation between two adjacent fringes | $h = \lambda/2$ | $\mu$m | One fringe $\equiv$ $\lambda/2$ in path difference | | 2 | Total flatness deviation | $h_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \lambda/2$ | $\mu$m | $N$ = number of fringes between highest and lowest point | | 3 | Wedge angle (small angle approx.) | $\theta = N\lambda/(2L)$ | radians | $L$ = length over which $N$ fringes are counted | | 4 | Fringe spacing (distance between successive fringes) | $w = \lambda/(2\theta)$ | mm | For a perfect wedge of angle $\theta$ | | 5 | Path difference (with phase change) | $\Delta = 2d + \lambda/2$ | nm | $d$ = air-gap thickness at a point | | 6 | Straightness deviation (fringe displacement method) | $\Delta_{\text{str}} = (a/b)\cdot(\lambda/2)$ | $\mu$m | $a$ = displacement of fringe, $b$ = normal fringe spacing | | 7 | Sensitivity | $\lambda/2 \approx 0.294 \text{ \mu m}$ | $\mu$m | For sodium D-line | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility**: Interferometric flatness and straightness measurements are indispensable in **ultra-precision manufacturing** — gauge blocks, precision slides, machine tool guideways, optical components, hydraulic spool valves, and bearing races. A flatness deviation of even $2\text{ \mu m}$ over a $300 \text{ mm}$ surface can render a precision component scrap. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fringe Spacing for a Wedge-Shaped Air Film **Statement to Prove**: For an air wedge of small angle $\theta$ between an optical flat and the workpiece, the distance $w$ between two successive dark fringes is $w = \lambda/(2\theta)$. **Setup**: Place the optical flat on the workpiece such that one edge rests on a thin slip gauge of thickness $t$. The opposite edge is free, so the flat tilts by a small angle $\theta$. Let the length of the optical flat be $L$, so $\tan\theta \approx \theta = t/L$. **Step 1 — Air-gap thickness at distance $x$ from the contact line** The thickness of the air film grows linearly with distance: $$d(x) = x \cdot \tan\theta \approx x \cdot \theta$$ **Step 2 — Condition for the $n^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe** Dark fringes occur when $2d = n\lambda$. So at position $x_n$: $$2 \cdot x_n \cdot \theta = n\lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_n = \frac{n\lambda}{2\theta}$$ **Step 3 — Condition for the $(n+1)^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe** $$x_{n+1} = \frac{(n+1)\lambda}{2\theta}$$ **Step 4 — Fringe spacing** Subtracting consecutive positions: $$w = x_{n+1} - x_n = \frac{(n+1)\lambda}{2\theta} - \frac{n\lambda}{2\theta} = \frac{\lambda}{2\theta}$$ $$\boxed{w = \frac{\lambda}{2\theta}}$$ **Conclusion**: The fringe spacing is inversely proportional to the wedge angle. For a steeper wedge, fringes appear closer together; for a flatter wedge, they spread apart. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Flatness Deviation from Fringe Count **Statement to Prove**: The flatness error of a surface is given by $h = N \cdot \lambda/2$, where $N$ is the number of dark fringes counted between the highest and lowest point on the test surface. **Step 1 — Place the optical flat on the test surface** A perfectly flat optical flat is placed on the workpiece. The workpiece's real surface has a height variation $h(x,y)$ relative to its ideal plane. **Step 2 — Air-gap thickness as a function of position** Because the bottom of the optical flat is perfectly flat, the air-gap thickness is: $$d(x,y) = h(x,y)$$ (where we choose the reference plane so that $h=0$ at the lowest contact point). **Step 3 — Identify dark-fringe contours** Dark fringes occur where $2d = n\lambda$, i.e., $d = n\lambda/2$. Each dark fringe is therefore a **contour line of constant height** in the surface, with successive dark fringes differing by exactly $\lambda/2$. **Step 4 — Count fringes between extremes** If $N$ dark fringes lie between the lowest point ($h_{\min}$) and the highest point ($h_{\max}$): $$h_{\max} - h_{\min} = N \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Definition of flatness error** Flatness is the minimum separation of two parallel planes enclosing the surface, which equals the peak-to-valley height: $$\boxed{h_{\text{flatness}} = N \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}}$$ **Numerical Illustration**: For sodium light ($\lambda = 0.5893 \text{ \mu m}$), if $N = 8$ fringes are observed between high and low spots: $$h = 8 \times \frac{0.5893}{2} = 8 \times 0.2946 = 2.357 \text{ \mu m}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Straightness Evaluation by Fringe Displacement **Statement to Prove**: For a straightedge test, the straightness deviation at any point equals $\Delta_{\text{str}} = (a/b) \cdot (\lambda/2)$, where $a$ is the local fringe displacement and $b$ is the normal fringe spacing. **Step 1 — Establish a perfectly straight reference** A straight optical flat is placed on the workpiece along the line being measured. **Step 2 — Define the ideal fringe pattern** If both surfaces were perfectly straight, fringes would appear as straight, equally-spaced parallel bands. The fringe spacing $b$ corresponds to a height rise of $\lambda/2$ across that horizontal distance. **Step 3 — Introduce a real-world deviation** Suppose the workpiece has a hump of height $h$ at some location. Locally, the air-gap thickness increases by $h$, pushing that fringe outward. **Step 4 — Measure fringe displacement** The local fringe shifts laterally by a distance $a$ relative to the adjacent undisturbed fringe. Because each full fringe spacing $b$ represents a height of $\lambda/2$, the local height increase is: $$\Delta_{\text{str}} = h = \frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta_{\text{str}} = \frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples **Example A — Flatness of a Surface Plate** A surface plate tested against an optical flat using sodium light shows **6 dark fringes** in the field of view. Calculate the flatness deviation. Given: $N = 6$, $\lambda = 0.5893 \text{ \mu m}$. Solution: $$h_{\text{flatness}} = N \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = 6 \times \frac{0.5893}{2}$$ $$h_{\text{flatness}} = 6 \times 0.29465 = 1.768 \text{ \mu m}$$ $$\boxed{h_{\text{flatness}} \approx 1.77 \text{ \mu m}}$$ **Example B — Wedge Angle from Fringe Spacing** On an optical flat placed on a test bar, 12 dark fringes are observed over a length of $25 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the wedge angle. Given: $N = 12$, $L = 25 \text{ mm} = 25{,}000 \text{ \mu m}$. Solution: $$\theta = \frac{N\lambda}{2L} = \frac{12 \times 0.5893}{2 \times 25{,}000}$$ $$\theta = \frac{7.0716}{50{,}000} = 1.4143 \times 10^{-4} \text{ radians}$$ Converting to arc-seconds: $1 \text{ rad} = 206{,}265''$ $$\theta \approx 29.17 \text{ arc-seconds} \approx 0.5 \text{ arc-minutes}$$ $$\boxed{\theta \approx 1.414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} \approx 29.2''}$$ **Example C — Straightness Error from Fringe Displacement** A straightness test yields fringes of normal spacing $b = 5 \text{ mm}$. At one point, a fringe is displaced by $a = 1.2 \text{ mm}$. Find the straightness deviation. Solution: $$\Delta_{\text{str}} = \frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{1.2}{5} \times 0.29465$$ $$\Delta_{\text{str}} = 0.24 \times 0.29465 = 0.0707 \text{ \mu m}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta_{\text{str}} \approx 0.071 \text{ \mu m} = 71 \text{ nm}}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Architecture — Interferometric Flatness Testing Station ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Sodium Monochromatic Light Source
lambda equals 589.3 nm] --> B[Diffuser and Collimating Lens
Produces Parallel Beam] B --> C[Beam Splitter and Reflecting Mirror
Directs Light Vertically Downward] C --> D[Optical Flat Reference Surface
Flatness better than lambda over 10] D --> E[Thin Air Wedge Film
Thickness d varies over workpiece] E --> F[Test Workpiece Surface
Flatness under evaluation] F --> G[Reflected Beam 1
From bottom of optical flat] E --> H[Reflected Beam 2
From top of workpiece] G --> I[Superposition Region
Constructive and Destructive zones] H --> I I --> J[Interference Fringe Pattern
Observed by eye or camera] J --> K[Fringe Counter and Interpreter
Counts N fringes] K --> L[Numerical Evaluation
h equals N times lambda over 2] L --> M[Flatness Result
in micrometres] ``` ### 4.2 Decision Tree — Interpreting Fringe Patterns ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Observe Fringe Pattern] --> Q1{Fringes are straight and parallel?} Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Equally spaced?} Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Fringes are curved?} Q2 -- YES --> RES1[SURFACE IS FLAT
No form error detected] Q2 -- NO --> RES2[SURFACE IS FLAT BUT TILTED
Tilt angle equals N times lambda over 2L] Q3 -- YES --> Q4{Convex or concave curvature?} Q3 -- NO --> RES3[SURFACE HAS COMPLEX ERROR
Requires full map] Q4 -- CONVEX --> RES4[CONVEX HIGH SPOT
Height h equals N times lambda over 2] Q4 -- CONCAVE --> RES5[CONCAVE DEPRESSION
Depth h equals N times lambda over 2] ``` ### 4.3 Component Cross-Section — Optical Flat on Workpiece (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OF[Optical Flat - Reference Surface] OF1[Top: Anti-reflective coated glass] OF2[Bottom: Polished flat surface lambda over 10] end subgraph AF[Thin Air Wedge Film] AF1[Thickness d varies from 0 to few microns] AF2[Darker shade indicates smaller gap] end subgraph WS[Test Workpiece Surface] WS1[May have hills and valleys under test] end OF2 --> AF1 AF1 --> WS1 BEAM[Incident monochromatic light] --reflects at OF2--> RAY1[Ray 1 from optical flat bottom] BEAM --passes through AF and reflects at WS1--> RAY2[Ray 2 from workpiece] RAY1 --> INTERFERE[Superposition zone] RAY2 --> INTERFERE ``` > [!TIP] > **Diagram Reading Note**: The two reflected rays, **Ray 1** and **Ray 2**, traverse different optical paths. Their path difference depends on the local air-gap thickness $d$. This varying path difference produces the alternating bright/dark bands we observe. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define the term "optical flat" and explain why it is used in interferometric flatness testing.* **Model Answer:** An **optical flat** is a circular or rectangular disc made of fused quartz or BK-7 glass, one face of which is polished to a flatness better than $\lambda/10$ (typically $0.05 \text{ \mu m}$). It serves as the **reference plane** against which the test surface is compared. When monochromatic light is reflected off both the bottom of the optical flat and the top of the workpiece, the resulting **interference fringes** directly reveal the height deviations of the test surface, with each fringe corresponding to a height change of $\lambda/2$. **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Why is monochromatic light essential for interferometric measurements? What is the wavelength of sodium D-line light?* **Model Answer:** Monochromatic light has a **single, well-defined wavelength**, producing sharp, countable interference fringes. White (polychromatic) light contains many wavelengths that overlap and produce colored fringes that cannot be unambiguously interpreted. The wavelength of **sodium D-line light** is: $$\lambda = 589.3 \text{ nm} = 0.5893 \text{ \mu m}$$ Therefore, one fringe spacing corresponds to a height deviation of $\lambda/2 \approx 0.294 \text{ \mu m}$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the principle of **interferometric measurement of flatness** using an optical flat and monochromatic light. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A surface plate is tested using an optical flat and a sodium light source. The fringe pattern shows **9 straight, parallel, equally-spaced dark fringes** over a length of **30 mm**. The plate is then tilted slightly and the same plate shows the fringes bending such that **8 fringes** lie between the highest and lowest visible points. Calculate: (i) The wedge angle in the first test. (ii) The flatness deviation in the second test. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q-A(a):** 1. **Principle**: When a perfectly flat optical flat rests on a test surface, a thin air film of varying thickness is formed between them. When illuminated by monochromatic light, the light reflected from the bottom of the flat and from the test surface interferes, producing alternating bright and dark bands called **interference fringes**. 2. Each dark fringe is a contour line of constant air-gap thickness. Two adjacent dark fringes differ in height by exactly $\lambda/2$. 3. **Straight parallel fringes** indicate a flat surface; **curved fringes** indicate hills or valleys; **number of fringes** between extremes gives the peak-to-valley flatness error. 4. The flatness deviation is calculated as $h = N \cdot \lambda/2$. **Diagram (described verbally for sketch):** - Sodium lamp → Diffuser → Optical flat placed on workpiece → Air wedge between them → Eye/camera observes fringes → Count $N$ → Compute $h = N\lambda/2$. --- **Model Solution for Q-A(b):** **Given:** - Test 1: $N_1 = 9$ fringes, $L_1 = 30 \text{ mm}$, $\lambda = 0.5893 \text{ \mu m}$. - Test 2: $N_2 = 8$ fringes between high and low points. **(i) Wedge angle in Test 1** The wedge angle relates to the total height rise across the length: $$\theta = \frac{N_1 \cdot \lambda}{2 L_1}$$ Converting units: $L_1 = 30 \text{ mm} = 30{,}000 \text{ \mu m}$. $$\theta = \frac{9 \times 0.5893}{2 \times 30{,}000} = \frac{5.3037}{60{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{\theta \approx 8.84 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad} \approx 16.4''}$$ **(ii) Flatness deviation in Test 2** $$h = N_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = 8 \times \frac{0.5893}{2} = 8 \times 0.29465$$ $$\boxed{h \approx 2.357 \text{ \mu m}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the **interferometric method of straightness measurement**. Describe how a deviation from straightness is detected and quantified from the fringe pattern. *(7 marks)* **(b)** In a straightness test, an optical flat placed on a guideway produces **straight, equally spaced fringes** of spacing **$b = 4 \text{ mm}$**. At one location, a fringe is observed to deviate laterally by **$a = 2 \text{ mm}$** from its expected position. Using sodium light, calculate: (i) The straightness deviation at that point. (ii) The minimum length of the optical flat required if the maximum acceptable deviation is $0.5 \text{ \mu m}$. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q-B(a):** **Principle**: A precision optical flat is placed on the surface whose straightness is to be evaluated. Monochromatic light produces interference fringes between the flat's lower surface and the workpiece surface. If the workpiece is perfectly straight, the fringes are straight, parallel, and equally spaced. Any **deviation from straightness** causes the air-gap thickness to vary locally, shifting the fringe laterally. **Quantification**: - The lateral displacement $a$ of a fringe at a point is measured against the normal fringe spacing $b$. - Each full fringe spacing corresponds to a height change of $\lambda/2$. - Hence local deviation is: $$\Delta_{\text{str}} = \frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}$$ - A plot of $\Delta_{\text{str}}$ vs. position along the guideway gives the straightness profile. --- **Model Solution for Q-B(b):** **Given:** $a = 2 \text{ mm}$, $b = 4 \text{ mm}$, $\lambda = 0.5893 \text{ \mu m}$. **(i) Straightness deviation at the displaced location** $$\Delta_{\text{str}} = \frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{2}{4} \times \frac{0.5893}{2}$$ $$\Delta_{\text{str}} = 0.5 \times 0.29465 = 0.1473 \text{ \mu m}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta_{\text{str}} \approx 0.147 \text{ \mu m}}$$ **(ii) Minimum optical flat length for $0.5 \text{ \mu m}$ maximum deviation** The total deviation between two adjacent fringes equals $\lambda/2 = 0.29465 \text{ \mu m}$. If the maximum permissible deviation is $0.5 \text{ \mu m}$, then within one fringe spacing the deviation already exceeds tolerance. We must require that the maximum deviation across the optical flat is $\leq 0.5 \text{ \mu m}$. For a maximum deviation equal to the height per fringe: $$h_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{2} = 0.29465 \text{ \mu m} < 0.5 \text{ \mu m}$$ This is acceptable. The minimum length is set by the requirement to capture **at least one full fringe spacing** so that the deviation can be resolved: $$L_{\min} = b = 4 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{L_{\min} = 4 \text{ mm} \text{ (one fringe spacing)}}$$ (For more conservative engineering practice, one would specify $L_{\min} = 5\text{--}10 \times b$ to obtain a statistically meaningful straightness profile.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Unit confusion trap**: Students often write $\lambda = 589.3 \text{ nm}$ but substitute directly into a formula requiring $\mu\text{m}$. Always convert to a consistent unit before plugging in. *MISTAKE COSTS: 1–2 marks.* > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2**: Each fringe represents a path difference of $\lambda$, but a **height change of $\lambda/2$** because light traverses the air gap twice. Writing $h = N\lambda$ instead of $h = N\lambda/2$ is the single most common error in KTU papers. *MISTAKE COSTS: 3–4 marks.* > 3. **Phase reversal oversight**: Many students omit the $\lambda/2$ phase shift that occurs upon reflection from the denser workpiece surface. This affects whether the central fringe is bright or dark, but **does not affect the fringe spacing formula**. *MISTAKE COSTS: 1 mark.* > 4. **Confusing straightness with flatness**: Straightness is a **line-based** measurement (one dimension), while flatness is a **surface-based** measurement (two dimensions). Both can use interferometry, but the interpretation of fringes differs. *MISTAKE COSTS: 2–3 marks.* > 5. **No diagram, no full marks**: In 7-mark questions, **a labelled schematic of the optical setup is mandatory**. Drawing the setup without labels loses marks. *MISTAKE COSTS: 2 marks.* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Interferometry** uses **superposition of two coherent light beams** reflected from a reference optical flat and the test surface. - The standard light source is the **sodium D-line** with $\lambda = 589.3 \text{ nm} = 0.5893 \text{ \mu m}$. - Each dark fringe represents a height contour; **adjacent fringes differ by $\lambda/2 \approx 0.294 \text{ \mu m}$** in height. - **Flatness error** $h_{\text{flatness}} = N \cdot (\lambda/2)$, where $N$ is the number of fringes between highest and lowest point. - **Wedge angle** $\theta = N\lambda/(2L)$, where $L$ is the length over which $N$ fringes are counted. - **Fringe spacing** for a wedge: $w = \lambda/(2\theta)$. - **Straightness deviation** from fringe displacement: $\Delta_{\text{str}} = (a/b) \cdot (\lambda/2)$, where $a$ is local displacement and $b$ is normal fringe spacing. - **Interpretation rules**: - Straight parallel equally-spaced fringes $\Rightarrow$ flat surface. - Curved fringes $\Rightarrow$ hills (convex) or valleys (concave). - Closer fringes $\Rightarrow$ steeper local slope. - **Path difference** (with phase reversal): $\Delta = 2d + \lambda/2$. - **Dark fringe condition**: $2d = n\lambda$, where $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ - **Resolution**: Interferometry resolves down to $\lambda/2 \approx 0.294 \text{ \mu m}$ for sodium light — far superior to dial indicators or verniers. - **Limitations**: Requires skilled operators, vibration-isolated environment, clean surfaces; cannot measure large surfaces in one setup (requires stitching). - **Applications**: Surface plates, gauge blocks, machine tool guideways, bearing races, optical flats, hydraulic spools, precision dies. - **Always** state units ($\mu$m) in your final answer for KTU valuation; examiners heavily penalize unitless numerical answers.

topics.title: Different Types of Locating and Clamping Devices: Basic Principles of Location and Design
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Different Types of Locating and Clamping Devices: Basic Principles of Location and Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Location** is the process of correctly positioning a workpiece in a JIG or FIXTURE such that all its **degrees of freedom (DOF)** are constrained/restricted in a **repeatable**, **accurate**, and **reproducible** manner relative to the cutting tool, before clamping forces are applied. **Clamping** is the subsequent application of external forces to rigidly hold the located workpiece against the locating surfaces during the machining or forming operation.

In the context of **Metal Forming and Tool Design (PCPET602 – Module 3)**, the dual goals of locating and clamping are:

1. **Dimensional Accuracy** — Holding tolerances of the order of **±0.01 mm to ±0.05 mm**.
2. **Operational Safety & Repeatability** — Preventing slippage, vibration, or deformation of the workpiece during cutting, forming, or forging loads.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Cricket Bat Grip" Principle**
> Imagine placing a cricket bat in a vice:
> - The flat rear jaw (V-block) **locates** the bat so that it cannot roll or twist — it has constrained 5 degrees of freedom.
> - The front jaw then **clamps** with force to prevent the bat from sliding out.
> - The tightening torque is the **clamping force**, and the rear jaw geometry is the **locator**.
>
> In tooling, location = geometry; clamping = force.

### 1.3 Fundamental Background — Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

A free rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF**:
- **3 Translational** (along X, Y, Z axes).
- **3 Rotational** (about X, Y, Z axes).

To fully constrain a workpiece, **all 6 DOF must be restricted**.

| DOF Type | Axis | Restricted by (Example) |
|----------|------|--------------------------|
| Translation X | $\hat{X}$ | Vertical locating pin |
| Translation Y | $\hat{Y}$ | Cylindrical pin in slot |
| Translation Z | $\hat{Z}$ | Flat support pad |
| Rotation about X | $\hat{X}$ | Two pins on a line |
| Rotation about Y | $\hat{Y}$ | Three support pads on a plane |
| Rotation about Z | $\hat{Z}$ | Two pins aligned along Y |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3-2-1 Locating Principle (The "Holy Grail" of Workpiece Location)

This is the **most fundamental principle** in jig and fixture design, first formalized by **Hoffman (1930s)** and adopted as a universal standard in tool design textbooks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-2-1 Principle Statement:** A rigid body in 3D space can be completely and uniquely located using:
> - **3 locators on the primary (datum) surface** (restricts 3 DOF: 1 translation + 2 rotations).
> - **2 locators on the secondary (datum) surface** (restricts 2 DOF: 1 translation + 1 rotation).
> - **1 locator on the tertiary (datum) surface** (restricts 1 DOF: 1 translation).

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: Primary Datum (3 locators on one plane)**
- A plane is defined by **3 non-collinear points**.
- These 3 points restrict: 1 translation (along the surface normal) + 2 rotations (about the two in-plane axes).
- **Remaining DOF: 3** (2 translations in the plane + 1 rotation about the normal).

**Step 2: Secondary Datum (2 locators on a second plane, perpendicular to primary)**
- A line is defined by **2 points**.
- These 2 points restrict: 1 translation (along the line normal) + 1 rotation (about the second plane's normal).
- **Remaining DOF: 2** (1 translation along the line + 1 rotation about the line itself).

**Step 3: Tertiary Datum (1 locator on a third plane, perpendicular to both)**
- A point is defined by **1 point**.
- This 1 point restricts: 1 translation.
- **All 6 DOF are now constrained — the workpiece is fully located.**

### 2.2 Types of Locating Devices

#### A. Locating Based on Flat Surfaces (Button/Rest Supports)

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Fixed Button** | Hardened cylindrical pad fixed to fixture body | Primary datum on rough castings |
| **Adjustable Button** | Screw-adjusted pad with lock nut | Compensates for casting variations |
| **Jack Pin / Jack Screw** | Heavy-duty screw with spherical tip | Large castings, forging dies |
| **Strap with Button** | Locating button under a clamping strap | When the locating surface is small |

#### B. Locating Pins (Used for Holes or External Diameters)

| Type | Function | Geometry |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Cylindrical Pin (fixed)** | Locates against hole wall | L/D = 1 to 1.5 |
| **Diamond Pin** | Eliminates over-constraint, allows ±0.05 mm play | Two parallel flats ground on cylinder |
| **Conical Pin (fastener type)** | Self-centering | Taper ratio 1:5 to 1:20 |
| **Knurled Pin** | Friction-fit for rough holes | Used in cast iron fixtures |
| **Serated Pin** | Fine serrations for self-cleaning | For castings with sand/dirt |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diamond Pin** is the most exam-relevant: it is ALWAYS used as the SECOND pin in a two-pin locating system. It restricts **3 DOF** (2 translations + 1 rotation) while a single round pin restricts **4 DOF** (2 translations + 2 rotations). The diamond geometry is created by grinding two parallel flats such that the chordal distance is slightly less than the hole diameter.

#### C. V-Blocks (Locating Cylindrical Workpieces)

Used for shafts, pins, and turned components. The V-angle commonly used is **90°** (universal) or **120°** (for thin parts).

$$\tan(\alpha/2) = \dfrac{R - r}{h}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = V-block angle (typically 90°).
- $R$ = radius of V-block groove.
- $r$ = radius of workpiece.
- $h$ = depth of contact.

**DOF restricted by one V-block:** 4 (2 translations + 2 rotations).

#### D. Spherical / Conical Locators

- **Conical locators** self-center the workpiece.
- **Spherical pad locators** accommodate ±3° of misalignment.
- Used on rough castings where datum surfaces are uneven.

### 2.3 Types of Clamping Devices

#### A. Manual Clamps

| Device | Working Principle | Force Amplification |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Strap Clamp** | Strap + heel block + screw | $F_{clamp} = F_{screw} \times \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ |
| **Finger Clamp** | Pivoted lever with adjustable finger | Lever ratio 2:1 to 5:1 |
| **Swing Clamp** | Pivots aside during loading | Quick-action, operator-friendly |
| **C-Clamp** | C-frame with screw | For low-volume jobs |
| **Hinged Clamp** | Hinged body, screw force amplified | Force ratio 3:1 |

#### B. Power-Operated Clamps

| Type | Source | Typical Pressure | Application |
|------|--------|------------------|-------------|
| **Hydraulic** | Pump + cylinder | 7–14 MPa | Heavy forging dies |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air | 0.5–0.7 MPa | Machining fixtures |
| **Magnetic** | Permanent or electromagnet | Surface flux 0.5–1.2 T | Thin ferromagnetic blanks |
| **Vacuum** | Suction cups | –0.06 to –0.09 MPa | Sheet metal forming |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{clamp} = \dfrac{M_t}{0.2 \cdot d}$ | Clamping force from tightening torque $M_t$ on a screw of pitch diameter $d$ (assuming $\mu = 0.15$) | $\text{N}$ |
| 2 | $F_{screw} = \dfrac{2 M_t}{d} \cdot \dfrac{\cos\alpha - \mu \tan\lambda}{\cos\alpha + \mu \cot\lambda \cdot \cos\lambda}$ | General screw clamp force (square thread) | $\text{N}$ |
| 3 | $F_{hyd} = P \cdot A_{piston}$ | Hydraulic clamp force | $\text{N}$ |
| 4 | $F_{pneu} = P \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta$ | Pneumatic clamp force (efficiency $\eta \approx 0.85$) | $\text{N}$ |
| 5 | $h_{center} = R - \dfrac{R - r}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$ | Height of center of cylindrical workpiece in V-block | $\text{mm}$ |
| 6 | $F_{total} = n \cdot F_c \cdot K_s$ | Total clamping force (n = number of clamps, $F_c$ = cutting force, $K_s$ = safety factor 1.5–3.0) | $\text{N}$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{contact} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{contact}}$ | Contact stress to check workpiece deformation | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 8 | $M_{clamp} = 0.5 \cdot d \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | Clamping torque on a V-strut clamp | $\text{N·m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **locating** (geometry/position) with **clamping** (force). The locating system fixes the **6 DOF**; the clamping system merely locks this position against process forces. Over-constraining in locating is called **redundant location** and is a common KTU exam trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Clamping Force from Screw Tightening Torque

A common KTU problem: *"A M12 screw is tightened with a torque of 25 N·m. Find the clamping force generated (take thread friction coefficient $\mu = 0.15$, square thread)."*

### Step 1 — Identify Input Parameters
- Screw nominal diameter: $d = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Pitch diameter (approximated as $d$ for square thread): $d_p \approx 12 \text{ mm}$
- Tightening torque: $M_t = 25 \text{ N·m} = 25{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$
- Thread half-angle: $\alpha = 0°$ (square thread)
- Friction coefficient: $\mu = 0.15$
- Lead angle: $\lambda = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{p}{\pi d_p}\right)$ where $p = 1.75 \text{ mm}$ (standard M12 coarse pitch)

### Step 2 — Calculate the Lead Angle

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{p}{\pi d_p}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1.75}{\pi \times 12}\right)$$

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1.75}{37.699}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.0464) = 2.66°$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Screw Clamping Equation

The general screw equation relating torque to axial force is:

$$M_t = \dfrac{F_{clamp} \cdot d_p}{2} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{\cos\alpha - \mu \tan\lambda}{\cos\alpha + \mu \cot\lambda \cdot \cos\lambda} \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi) + \mu_c \cdot \dfrac{d_o}{d_p} \right]$$

For simplification, **KTU standard practice** (neglecting collar friction $\mu_c$ and taking $\alpha = 0$ for square thread):

$$M_t = \dfrac{F_{clamp} \cdot d_p}{2} \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi)$$

where $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu) = \tan^{-1}(0.15) = 8.53°$.

### Step 4 — Compute $\tan(\lambda + \phi)$

$$\lambda + \phi = 2.66° + 8.53° = 11.19°$$

$$\tan(11.19°) = 0.198$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $F_{clamp}$

Rearranging:

$$F_{clamp} = \dfrac{2 M_t}{d_p \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi)}$$

$$F_{clamp} = \dfrac{2 \times 25{,}000}{12 \times 0.198} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{2.376}$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 21{,}043 \text{ N} \approx 21.04 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation
This clamping force must exceed the **tangential cutting force** ($F_c$) at the contact point, with a safety factor of **$K_s = 1.5$ to $3.0$** depending on cut type (interrupted, rough, finishing).

$$F_c = K_s \cdot F_{clamp} = 2.0 \times 21.04 = 42.08 \text{ kN (maximum allowable cutting force)}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: DOF Analysis of Common Locating Systems

| Locating Configuration | DOF Restricted | DOF Remaining | Redundancy? | Application |
|------------------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **3 buttons on a plane** | 3 (1T + 2R) | 3 (2T + 1R) | No | Primary datum |
| **2 buttons on a 2nd plane** | 2 (1T + 1R) | 1 (1T) | No | Secondary datum |
| **1 button on 3rd plane** | 1 (1T) | 0 | No | Tertiary datum (3-2-1 done) |
| **1 round pin in a hole** | 4 (2T + 2R) | 2 (1T + 1R) | No | Locating on hole |
| **2 round pins in 2 holes** | 8 | –2 | **YES – REDUNDANT** | **Common error** |
| **1 round pin + 1 diamond pin** | 4 + 3 = 7 | –1 | No (diamond acts as line) | **Correct dual-hole locating** |
| **1 V-block on a shaft** | 4 (2T + 2R) | 2 (1T + 1R) | No | Shaft machining |
| **2 V-blocks (on same shaft, spaced)** | 5 (2T + 3R) | 1 (1T) | No | Long shaft turning |
| **Flat + 2 pins on a flange** | 3 + 4 = 7 | –1 | **YES** | Avoid unless diamond used |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Golden Rule:** When two holes are used for location, **always make one pin round and one diamond**. Two round pins = redundant location = distortion of workpiece or pins.

---

## 3.3 Diamond Pin Dimension Calculation (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** Design a diamond pin for a hole of diameter $D = 20$ mm, allowing a clearance of $0.05$ mm on each side. The diamond width $w$ should be $D - 0.5$ mm.

**Step 1 — Diamond width (chord length)**

$$w = D - 0.5 = 20 - 0.5 = 19.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Distance of flats from pin center (depth of flat $c$)**

For a diamond pin of diameter $d = 19.95$ mm (accounting for clearance):

$$c = \dfrac{d}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\dfrac{w}{2}\right)^2}$$

$$c = 9.975 - \sqrt{(9.975)^2 - (9.75)^2}$$

$$c = 9.975 - \sqrt{99.50 - 95.06} = 9.975 - \sqrt{4.44} = 9.975 - 2.107$$

$$\boxed{c \approx 7.868 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Check**
- The diamond flat is at depth $c = 7.868$ mm, so the workpiece hole walls (diametrically opposite the flat) only contact the rounded portion of the pin.
- The diamond restricts **only translation along 2 axes + rotation about 1 axis = 3 DOF** (line contact, not point contact), whereas a round pin restricts **4 DOF**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The 3-2-1 Locating Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WP["Rigid Workpiece - 6 DOF Free"]
    subgraph Primary["PRIMARY DATUM PLANE 3 Points"]
        P1["Locator 1"]
        P2["Locator 2"]
        P3["Locator 3"]
    end
    subgraph Secondary["SECONDARY DATUM PLANE 2 Points"]
        S1["Locator 4"]
        S2["Locator 5"]
    end
    subgraph Tertiary["TERTIARY DATUM PLANE 1 Point"]
        T1["Locator 6"]
    end
    CL["Clamping Force Applied"]
    WP --> P1
    WP --> P2
    WP --> P3
    WP --> S1
    WP --> S2
    WP --> T1
    P1 --> CL
    S1 --> CL
    T1 --> CL
    CL --> DONE["Workpiece Fully Located and Clamped - 0 DOF"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Locating vs. Clamping Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Workpiece"] --> B["LOAD onto Fixture Base"]
    B --> C["3-2-1 LOCATION 6 DOF Constrained"]
    C --> D{"Position Correct?"}
    D -- No --> E["Reject / Reposition"]
    D -- Yes --> F["Apply CLAMPING FORCE Fc"]
    F --> G["Perform MACHINING or FORMING"]
    G --> H["Release Clamps"]
    H --> I["Unload Finished Part"]
    I --> J{"Repeat for Next Part?"}
    J -- Yes --> B
    J -- No --> K["End of Cycle"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Clamping Device Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CD["Clamping Devices"]
    CD --> M["Manual Clamps"]
    CD --> PM["Power-Operated Clamps"]
    M --> SC["Strap Clamp"]
    M --> FC["Finger Clamp"]
    M --> SWC["Swing Clamp"]
    M --> CC["C-Clamp"]
    M --> HC["Hinged Clamp"]
    PM --> HY["Hydraulic Cylinder"]
    PM --> PN["Pneumatic Cylinder"]
    PM --> MG["Magnetic Chuck"]
    PM --> VC["Vacuum Chuck"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Locator Geometry and DOF Restriction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["Flat Button Restrict 1 Trans 2 Rot 3 DOF"]
    G2["Cylindrical Pin Restrict 2 Trans 2 Rot 4 DOF"]
    G3["Diamond Pin Restrict 2 Trans 1 Rot 3 DOF"]
    G4["V-Block Restrict 2 Trans 2 Rot 4 DOF"]
    G5["Conical Pin Restrict 2 Trans 1 Rot 3 DOF"]
    G6["Spherical Pad Restrict 1 Trans 0 Rot 1 DOF"]
    G1 --> COMBO["Combined 3-2-1 System 6 DOF Restricted"]
    G2 --> COMBO
    G3 --> COMBO
    G4 --> COMBO
    G5 --> COMBO
    G6 --> COMBO
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1.** What is the **3-2-1 principle** of location? How many degrees of freedom are restricted by each stage? `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The 3-2-1 principle is a standard locating method used in jigs and fixtures to fully constrain a rigid workpiece in 3D space.
- **3 locators on the primary datum plane** restrict **3 DOF** (1 translation perpendicular to the plane + 2 rotations about the two in-plane axes).
- **2 locators on the secondary datum plane** restrict **2 DOF** (1 translation + 1 rotation).
- **1 locator on the tertiary datum plane** restricts **1 DOF** (1 translation).
- **Total DOF restricted = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6** — fully located.

---

### **Q2.** Distinguish between **locating** and **clamping** in a fixture. Why must a workpiece be located **before** clamping? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Locating | Clamping |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Purpose** | Fix the **position** of the workpiece | Apply **force** to retain the position |
| **Mechanism** | Geometric (pins, buttons, V-blocks) | Mechanical (screws, straps) or Power (hydraulic, pneumatic) |
| **Restricts** | Degrees of freedom (DOF) | Does NOT restrict DOF; resists process forces |
| **Order** | Always **first** | Always **after** locating |
| **Why first?** | Clamping on an un-located workpiece leads to inaccurate dimensions, scrap, and possible damage to tool and fixture. Location defines the **datum**; clamping only locks the position. |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **different types of locators** used in jigs and fixtures. Mention the DOF restricted by each. `(7 Marks)`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Flat Surface Locators (Button Supports):**
   - *Fixed button, adjustable button, jack pin, heel block.*
   - Used on cast or forged surfaces.
   - One button restricts **1 DOF** (translation along its axis). Three non-collinear buttons on a plane restrict **3 DOF** (1T + 2R).

2. **Cylindrical Pins:**
   - *Round pin:* Locates in a hole, restricts **4 DOF** (2T + 2R).
   - *Diamond pin:* Locates in a hole with two parallel flats, restricts **3 DOF** (2T + 1R). Used as the second pin in a two-pin system to avoid redundancy.

3. **Conical Pin / Locator:**
   - Self-centering, used in rough castings.
   - Restricts **3 DOF** (2T + 1R).

4. **V-Blocks:**
   - Used for cylindrical workpieces (shafts).
   - Restricts **4 DOF** (2T + 2R). Two V-blocks spaced along the shaft restrict **5 DOF**.

5. **Spherical Seat / Adjustable Pad:**
   - Accommodates misaligned surfaces.
   - Restricts **1 DOF** (translation).

**Sketch Description (Verbal):**
- A casting rests on three button supports.
- A round pin enters a hole on the side (locates 4 DOF).
- A diamond pin enters a second hole (locates 3 DOF).
- A clamp screw at the top applies clamping force.

---

#### **(b)** A **M14 square-thread screw** is used in a strap clamp. The screw is tightened to a torque of **40 N·m**. The thread pitch is **2 mm**, and the friction coefficient is **0.18**. Find the **clamping force** developed. `(7 Marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data**
- $M_t = 40 \text{ N·m} = 40{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$
- $d_p = 14 \text{ mm}$ (square thread → pitch diameter ≈ nominal)
- $p = 2 \text{ mm}$
- $\mu = 0.18$

**Step 2 — Lead Angle**

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{p}{\pi d_p}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{\pi \times 14}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{43.98}\right)$$

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}(0.0455) = 2.604°$$

**Step 3 — Friction Angle**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.18) = 10.20°$$

**Step 4 — Apply Clamping Equation**

$$M_t = \dfrac{F_{clamp} \cdot d_p}{2} \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi)$$

$$\lambda + \phi = 2.604° + 10.20° = 12.804°$$

$$\tan(12.804°) = 0.2273$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $F_{clamp}$**

$$F_{clamp} = \dfrac{2 \times 40{,}000}{14 \times 0.2273} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{3.182}$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 25{,}141 \text{ N} \approx 25.14 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain the **basic principles of clamping**. Compare **manual and power clamping devices** with examples. `(7 Marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Basic Principles of Clamping:**

1. **Clamping must not disturb location** — clamping force should be directed **toward** a fixed locator (button, rest), not against the locating surface in a way that lifts the workpiece.
2. **Clamping force should be adequate** — must exceed the resultant cutting force with a safety factor of **$K_s = 1.5$ to 3.0**.
3. **Clamping should be quick and ergonomic** — minimize operator fatigue.
4. **Clamping should not damage the workpiece** — use soft pads, lead cushions, or auxiliary shoe when the surface is finished.
5. **Clamping direction** — preferably from above (gravity-assisted), or in the direction of cutting force.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Manual Clamping | Power Clamping |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| **Force Source** | Operator muscle / screw | Hydraulic, pneumatic, magnetic |
| **Magnitude** | 5–30 kN (limited by operator) | 20–500 kN |
| **Speed** | Slow (5–30 s) | Fast (0.5–2 s) |
| **Repeatability** | Operator-dependent | Excellent (±2%) |
| **Cost** | Low | High (pump, valves, plumbing) |
| **Examples** | Strap clamp, finger clamp, swing clamp, hinged clamp | Hydraulic cylinder, pneumatic toggle clamp, magnetic chuck, vacuum chuck |

---

#### **(b)** A casting is located in a fixture using **one round pin and one diamond pin** on two holes of **$D = 25$ mm** diameter. The round pin is made to **Diameter 24.95 mm**. The diamond pin is to be designed with **chordal width = 24.50 mm** to allow a **0.05 mm clearance** on the sides. Calculate **(i) the depth of the diamond flat** from the pin center, and **(ii) the height of the diamond flat above the pin center** if the diamond pin is made of **20 mm nominal diameter** (i.e., ground down on one side). `(7 Marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Hole diameter $D = 25$ mm
- Round pin diameter = 24.95 mm (clearance fit)
- Diamond pin nominal diameter $d = 20$ mm
- Chordal width (diamond flat to flat) $w = 24.50$ mm
- Clearance on sides of diamond = 0.05 mm

**Step 1 — Effective Diamond Pin Diameter**

The diamond pin's circular portion must still clear the hole with 0.05 mm clearance:

$$d_{eff} = 20 \text{ mm} \text{ (the cylindrical portion, which fits inside the hole)}$$

Wait — this means the diamond pin is OVERSIZED vs. the hole. This is incorrect; let me re-interpret.

> **Correct Interpretation:** The diamond pin is a cylindrical pin of nominal diameter $d = 20$ mm, ground on two opposite sides to give a chordal width $w = 24.5$ mm. This means the cylinder diameter is actually $d$ such that its chord at the flat = 24.5 mm.

**Re-Step 1 — Actual Cylinder Diameter of Diamond Pin**

The chordal width $w$ at a given depth $c$ from the center of a cylinder of diameter $d$ is:

$$w = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^2 - c^2}$$

For the diamond pin to fit in the hole (25 mm dia) with 0.05 mm clearance:

$$\text{Required cylinder diameter} = 25 - 2(0.05) = 24.90 \text{ mm}$$

So $d = 24.90$ mm.

**Step 2 — Depth of Flat from Center**

$$24.50 = 2 \cdot \sqrt{(12.45)^2 - c^2}$$

$$12.25 = \sqrt{155.0025 - c^2}$$

$$150.0625 = 155.0025 - c^2$$

$$c^2 = 4.94$$

$$\boxed{c \approx 2.222 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Height of Flat Above Center**

The "height of flat" measured perpendicular to the chord (i.e., how far the flat extends radially beyond the original cylinder surface... or rather, how much material is ground off):

$$\text{Depth of cut} = \dfrac{d}{2} - c = 12.45 - 2.222 = 10.228 \text{ mm}$$
