This means the flat is ground to a depth of **10.228 mm** from the original outer surface, leaving **2.222 mm** of material between the flat and the centerline.

**Step 4 — DOF Check**
- Round pin: 4 DOF restricted.
- Diamond pin: 3 DOF restricted.
- Total: 7 DOF restricted against 6 available → **No redundancy** (the diamond pin loses 1 DOF intentionally via the flat).
- A flat-side-only contact acts as a **line** (1 DOF remaining for rotation about that line), so it restricts **2T + 1R = 3 DOF**, not 4.

$$\boxed{\text{Depth of flat from center } c = 2.22 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Redundant Location Trap:** Many students use 2 round pins, causing 8 DOF restricted on a 6-DOF body — examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Locating with Clamping:** Clamping does NOT reduce DOF; only locating does. Examiners award 0 marks for stating "clamping restricts 1 DOF".
> 3. **Forgetting the Collar Friction Term:** In screw-clamp derivation, the collar friction term $\mu_c \cdot d_o / d_p$ is often omitted — examiner expects students to *mention* it as a step even if neglected.
> 4. **Diamond Pin Mistake:** Students often write "diamond pin restricts 4 DOF" — it restricts only **3 DOF** (the flat removes 1 rotational constraint).
> 5. **Units and Pitch Diameter:** Always use $d_p$ (pitch diameter), not nominal $d$, for screw calculations.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before exam):**

- ✅ A free body has **6 DOF** — 3 translations + 3 rotations.
- ✅ **3-2-1 principle** is the universal locating rule: 3 + 2 + 1 locators on three mutually perpendicular datum planes.
- ✅ **One round pin in a hole** restricts **4 DOF** (2T + 2R).
- ✅ **One diamond pin** restricts **3 DOF** (2T + 1R). Always use as the **second** pin in a dual-hole system.
- ✅ **V-block (90°)** restricts **4 DOF** for a shaft.
- ✅ **Locating ≠ Clamping** — Location fixes geometry, clamping applies force.
- ✅ Clamping direction should be **toward the locator**, not against it.
- ✅ Screw clamping force equation: $F_{clamp} = \dfrac{2 M_t}{d_p \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi)}$.
- ✅ Hydraulic clamp: $F = P \cdot A_{piston}$; Pneumatic: $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta$.
- ✅ **Magnetic chuck** used for ferromagnetic workpieces; flux density 0.5–1.2 T.
- ✅ **Vacuum chuck** used for thin sheet metal (e.g., PCB blanking).
- ✅ **Safety factor $K_s$** in clamping: 1.5 for finishing, 2.0 for roughing, 3.0 for interrupted cuts.
- ✅ **Over-constrained (redundant) location** must always be avoided — use diamond pins or floating clamps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Locating Methods and Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Locating Methods and Devices ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is "Locating" in Press Tools? In sheet metal and metal forming operations (blanking, piercing, bending, drawing), the **workpiece (strip or blank)** must be accurately positioned and held inside the die before any cutting or forming action takes place. The set of devices and principles used to **constrain the workpiece to a fixed, repeatable geometric position** inside the die is collectively called the **Locating System**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > *Locating is the operation of constraining all possible degrees of freedom (DOF) of a free body in space so that the workpiece occupies a unique, repeatable, and unambiguous position relative to the punch and die cutting edges for every stroke of the press.* ### 1.2 The 3-2-1 Locating Principle (Six-Point Location) A rigid body in three-dimensional space possesses **six (6) degrees of freedom**: $$ \text{Total DOF of a free rigid body} = 6 $$ - **3 Translational DOF** → movement along X, Y, Z axes - **3 Rotational DOF** → rotation about X, Y, Z axes (pitch, yaw, roll) To fully constrain a rectangular/flat strip workpiece, **six locating points (contact points)** distributed across **three mutually perpendicular datum planes** are required. | Datum | Plane | # of Locating Points | DOF Removed | |-------|-------|---------------------|-------------| | Primary (largest face) | Bottom face resting on die | **3** points | 1 Trans + 2 Rot | | Secondary (side face) | One vertical side against locator | **2** points | 2 Trans | | Tertiary (back face) | Opposite vertical face against locator | **1** point | 1 Trans | | **Total** | — | **6** points | **6 DOF = Fully Constrained** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wobbly Bookshelf" > [!NOTE] > **Imagine placing a heavy textbook on a table.** > > - First, you press it down against the table (3 points on its bottom face → primary datum). It can no longer rotate or lift. > - Then you push it against a wall (2 points on its side → secondary datum). It can no longer slide sideways. > - Finally, you trap the back against a small block (1 point → tertiary datum). Now the book is **perfectly frozen** in space — six points, six degrees of freedom eliminated. > > This is *exactly* the 3-2-1 principle used in press tools. ### 1.4 Key Terminology Used in KTU Examinations - **Datum Surface:** A theoretical or actual surface on the workpiece against which the part is located. - **Pitch / Yaw / Roll:** Rotational DOF about the X, Y, Z axes respectively. - **Fool-Proofing:** Design feature ensuring the strip cannot be inserted in the wrong orientation. - **Redundant Location:** Over-locating (more points than necessary), leading to tool wear and inaccuracies. - **Pilot:** A pin on the punch that engages a previously pierced hole to achieve high-accuracy secondary location during progressive dies. --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchy of Locating Constraints For the strip to be **fully constrained** (no movement possible under the forming/cutting force): $$\boxed{N_{\text{locating points}} = \text{DOF of rigid body} = 6}$$ If $N < 6$ → workpiece is **under-located** (slips, skews, mis-pierces). If $N > 6$ → workpiece is **over-located / redundantly located** (excessive die wear, jammed strip). ### 2.2 Classification of Locating Devices Press tool locators fall into **three functional families**: #### A. Fixed Locators (Rigid, Non-Adjustable) - **Locating Pin / Button** — circular cross-section pin, single point contact. - **Diamond Pin / Elongated Pin** — rectangular/diamond cross-section, provides 2-point contact along edges. - **Conical Locator** — used on drilled/stamped holes; self-centering. - **V-Locator / V-Block** — used for round/cylindrical workpieces; constrains 4 DOF. - **Shouldered Locator** — has both locating surface and clamping shoulder. - **Rest Pad** — flat pad providing primary 3-point datum. #### B. Adjustable / Spring-Loaded Locators - **Spring-Loaded Retractable Pin** — retracts during strip feeding, snaps back. - **Spring-Loaded Stripper Plate Locator** — locator integral to stripper. - **Air-Cushion / Hydraulic Locator** — used in heavy blanking & drawing. #### C. Pilot Locators (Progressive / Compound Dies) - **Fixed Pilot** — rigid pin on the punch entering a pre-pierced hole. - **Retractable / Spring Pilot** — retracts during strip advance. - **Floating Pilot** — held by punch stripper, drops into hole precisely. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **high-yield for KTU ESE and internal exams.** Memorize each. | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|-------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $N_{\text{points}} = 6$ | Six-point location principle | dimensionless | | 2 | $\sum F_{\text{locators}} = F_{\text{cutting}} \cdot \tan\theta$ | Horizontal thrust balance on inclined locator (where $\theta$ is the die-clearance half-angle, typically **5°–8°**) | N (Newtons) | | 3 | $F_{\text{shear}} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | Shearing/cutting force resisted by locator friction | N | | 4 | $M_{\text{overturn}} = F_{\text{punch}} \cdot h$ | Overturning moment about the locating edge (where $h$ is punch-to-locator vertical distance) | N·mm | | 5 | $M_{\text{stabilize}} = F_{\text{hold}} \cdot d$ | Stabilizing moment from hold-down / stripper | N·mm | | 6 | $e \le \dfrac{L}{3}$ | Eccentricity rule for stable location (centre of gravity within middle third of base) | mm | | 7 | $P_{\text{pin}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{loc}}}{A_{\text{pin}}} = \dfrac{4 F_{\text{loc}}}{\pi d^{2}}$ | Bearing stress on locating pin | N/mm² | | 8 | $L_{\text{locator}} \le 0.75\,L_{\text{strip}}$ | Locator length should not exceed 75 % of strip advance | mm | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Convention:** Vertical bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert F \vert$). **Never** use the raw `|` character inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the table. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Stamping Lines:** 3-2-1 locating principles guarantee that door panels, hoods, and fenders are punched with **±0.05 mm tolerance** repeatability across millions of strokes. - **Progressive Dies for Electronics:** Micro-pilot pins (Ø1 mm) locate connector strips during blanking of lead frames. - **Sheet Metal Drawing:** V-locators and rest pads control metal flow, preventing wrinkles and tears. ### 2.5 Fool-Proofing Devices (Asymmetric Location) To prevent the strip from being fed **upside-down, mirror-flipped, or rotated 180°**, designers deliberately use **asymmetric locating layouts**: - Diamond pin + round pin on the same edge (the two pins cannot be interchanged). - Notch locators engaging matching slots on the strip. - Offset locating buttons placed at non-symmetric positions. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks when you state the *purpose* of fool-proofing along with at least one illustrative example (e.g., diamond + round pin combination). --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Six-Point Location & Degrees of Freedom **Step 1 — Define the rigid body coordinate system.** A free rectangular blank of dimensions $L \times B \times t$ possesses 6 DOF: $$ \{T_x,\, T_y,\, T_z,\, R_x,\, R_y,\, R_z\} $$ **Step 2 — Apply primary (bottom) datum with 3 points.** Three non-collinear points on the bottom face (forming a triangle) eliminate: - $T_z$ (vertical translation) - $R_x$ (pitch rotation) - $R_y$ (roll rotation) $$\{T_x,\, T_y,\, R_z\} \;\;\text{remaining} = 3 \text{ DOF}$$ **Step 3 — Apply secondary (vertical side) datum with 2 points.** Two points along a vertical wall eliminate: - $T_x$ (translation along wall) - $R_z$ (rotation about the wall normal) $$\{T_y\} \;\;\text{remaining} = 1 \text{ DOF}$$ **Step 4 — Apply tertiary (opposite vertical face) datum with 1 point.** One point against the back wall eliminates the final $T_y$. $$\boxed{\text{Remaining DOF} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Part is fully located.}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Horizontal Thrust on Inclined Locator When a punch with clearance angle $2\theta$ (typical $10°$–$16°$) shears a strip, the resultant cutting force $F_c$ is **inclined slightly off-vertical** by angle $\theta$. The horizontal component $F_H$ tries to push the strip sideways, and must be resisted by the locator friction force $F_f$: $$F_H = F_c \sin\theta$$ The friction force at the locator surface is: $$F_f = \mu \, N$$ where $N$ is the normal reaction on the locator face and $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction (steel-on-steel ≈ **0.10–0.15**, lubricated ≈ **0.05–0.08**). For equilibrium of the strip: $$\mu \, N \;=\; F_c \sin\theta$$ Solving for $N$: $$\boxed{N = \frac{F_c \sin\theta}{\mu}}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Given: $F_c = 80{,}000 \text{ N}$, $\theta = 5°$, $\mu = 0.12$. $$N = \frac{80{,}000 \times \sin 5°}{0.12} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 0.0872}{0.12}$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 58{,}130 \text{ N}}$$ > This reaction load dictates the **hardness and shank diameter** of the locating pin (using Equation 7 from the cheat sheet). ### 3.3 Derivation: Locator Pin Diameter (Bearing Stress Check) The pin must not crush (yield in bearing) under the reaction $N$. Bearing stress: $$P_{\text{bearing}} = \frac{N}{A_{\text{pin}}} = \frac{4N}{\pi d^{2}}$$ For hardened tool-steel pins (AISI D2, hardness **58–62 HRC**), allowable bearing stress: $$\sigma_{\text{bearing, allow}} \approx 900 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$ Setting $P_{\text{bearing}} = \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ and solving for $d$: $$\boxed{d = \sqrt{\frac{4N}{\pi \, \sigma_{\text{allow}}}}}$$ **Numerical Example (continuation):** $$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 58{,}130}{\pi \times 900}} = \sqrt{\frac{232{,}520}{2827.4}} = \sqrt{82.24}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 9.07 \text{ mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{adopt standard } d = 10 \text{ mm}}$$ ### 3.4 Stability Condition (No Overturning) The cutting force $F_c$ acts at the punch centre, at a height $h$ above the locator plane. It creates an overturning moment: $$M_{\text{overturn}} = F_c \cdot h$$ The hold-down / stripper force $F_{\text{hold}}$ acts through the centroid of the locator base, at a moment arm $d$ (centre-of-gravity offset from the locator centre). For **no overturning** (and hence stable locating): $$\boxed{F_{\text{hold}} \cdot d \;>\; F_c \cdot h}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > If this inequality is **violated**, the strip will tip during cutting → dimensional inaccuracy, broken punches. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Locator Selection | Workpiece Feature | Recommended Locator | DOF Removed | Special Note | |-------------------|---------------------|-------------|--------------| | Flat strip edge | Rest pad (3 pins) | 3 | Primary datum | | Strip side edge | Diamond pin + round pin | 2 | Fool-proofing achieved | | Pre-pierced hole | Pilot (fixed/retractable) | 2 | Used in progressive dies | | Round bar / shaft | V-block (90° included) | 4 | 2-line contact | | Cylindrical blank | Conical locator | 3–4 | Self-centring | | Drawn cup / shell | Shouldered locator | 4 | Also acts as draw stop | | Asymmetric strip | Notched locator | 5–6 | Fool-proof + locating | --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Six-Point Locating Block Diagram (3-2-1 Principle) ```mermaid graph TD A[Free Rigid Body
6 DOF] --> B[Primary Datum
3 Points on Bottom Face] B --> C[Translation Tz Eliminated
Rotation Rx, Ry Eliminated] C --> D[Secondary Datum
2 Points on Vertical Wall] D --> E[Translation Tx Eliminated
Rotation Rz Eliminated] E --> F[Tertiary Datum
1 Point on Opposite Wall] F --> G[Translation Ty Eliminated
Remaining DOF = 0] G --> H[Fully Located Workpiece
Ready for Punching] style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff style H fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff style D fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff style F fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Locating Device Taxonomy (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD N1[Locating Devices in Press Tools] --> N2[Fixed Locators] N1 --> N3[Adjustable Locators] N1 --> N4[Pilot Locators] N2 --> N21[Locating Pin Round] N2 --> N22[Diamond Pin] N2 --> N23[Rest Pad] N2 --> N24[V Block] N2 --> N25[Conical Locator] N2 --> N26[Shouldered Locator] N3 --> N31[Spring Loaded Pin] N3 --> N32[Retractable Button] N3 --> N33[Air Cushion Locator] N4 --> N41[Fixed Pilot] N4 --> N42[Retractable Pilot] N4 --> N43[Floating Pilot] style N1 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff style N2 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff style N3 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff style N4 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Force / Moment Flow on a Located Strip ```mermaid graph LR P1[Punch Force Fc
acts at height h] --> P2[Overturning Moment
M = Fc x h] P2 --> P3[Locating Plane
Reactions N1 N2 N3] H1[Hold Down / Stripper
Force F hold] --> P3 P3 --> P4[Stability Check
F hold x d greater than Fc x h] P4 --> P5{Stable ?} P5 -- Yes --> P6[Clean Shear
Accurate Blank] P5 -- No --> P7[Strip Tilts
Punch Breaks] style P1 fill:#d62728,color:#fff style P6 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style P7 fill:#d62728,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Fool-Proofing Schematic — Diamond + Round Pin Pair ```mermaid graph TD F1[Strip Inserted Correct Way] --> F2[Diamond Pin Slides Along Edge
Round Pin Engages Notch] F2 --> F3[Operation Proceeds
Correct Orientation] F4[Strip Inserted Upside Down] --> F5[Diamond Pin Cannot Enter Notch
Mechanical Interference] F5 --> F6[Stroke Prevented
Fool Proofing Achieved] style F3 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style F6 fill:#d62728,color:#fff ``` --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]** **Q1.** State and explain the **3-2-1 locating principle** used in press tools. Why is exactly **six** locating points required? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A rigid body in 3D space has **6 degrees of freedom** — 3 translational ($T_x, T_y, T_z$) and 3 rotational ($R_x, R_y, R_z$). The 3-2-1 locating principle constrains these by: - **3 points** on the primary (bottom) datum → eliminates $T_z, R_x, R_y$. - **2 points** on the secondary (side) datum → eliminates $T_x, R_z$. - **1 point** on the tertiary (back) datum → eliminates $T_y$. Hence **six points = six DOF eliminated**, providing a unique, repeatable workpiece position. Fewer points → under-location (slip); more points → over-location (die wear). --- **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q2]** **Q2.** What is **fool-proofing** in locating devices? Give **two examples**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Fool-proofing is a design feature that ensures the workpiece **cannot be inserted in an incorrect orientation** (flipped, rotated, or wrongly sided) into the die. - **Example 1:** A *diamond pin + round pin* combination on the same edge — the diamond engages a slot only on the correct side. - **Example 2:** An *asymmetric notch* on the strip engaging a matching asymmetric locator block — wrong orientation is mechanically blocked. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Do NOT** list locator types without specifying the **DOF each removes**. Examiners allocate ~2 marks purely to DOF analysis. > 2. Always draw or describe the **3-2-1 layout** explicitly — vague answers lose 3–4 marks. > 3. For force calculations, **show units at every step** ($\text{N}$, $\text{N/mm}^2$, $\text{mm}$). Marks are deducted for unitless final answers. > 4. State the **assumption of coefficient of friction** ($\mu = 0.10$–$0.15$) explicitly. Skipping this loses 1 mark. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Locating Devices in Detail **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Part (a) — 7 marks** Classify the various **locating devices** used in press tools. With neat sketches describe the working of **locating pin, diamond pin, and V-locator**. Mention the degrees of freedom removed by each. **Model Solution:** Locating devices in press tools are classified into three categories: **(i) Locating Pin (Round Pin / Button Locator):** A hardened cylindrical pin (typically Ø5–Ø12 mm, AISI D2 steel, 58–62 HRC) provides **single-point contact** with the strip edge. Used in multiples to form primary or secondary datum. Removes **1 translational DOF per pin**. **(ii) Diamond Pin (Elongated Pin):** A pin of diamond/rhombus cross-section. Its long axis is oriented along the strip-feed direction. Provides **two-point line contact** with the strip edge along its length — thus **2 points of contact from a single pin**. Removes **2 translational DOF** and additionally **prevents strip rotation about its long axis** (fool-proofing). **(iii) V-Locator (V-Block):** Two inclined planes at **90° included angle** supporting a cylindrical workpiece. Contact occurs at **two lines**, hence **4 contact points**. Removes **4 DOF** (2 translations + 2 rotations), commonly used for round bar / shaft locators. | Device | Points of Contact | DOF Removed | |--------|-------------------|-------------| | Round Locating Pin | 1 | 1 | | Diamond Pin | 2 | 2 | | V-Locator (90°) | 4 | 4 | **Part (b) — 7 marks** Explain with neat sketch the **pilot** used in progressive dies. Why is a **spring-loaded retractable pilot** preferred over a fixed pilot in high-speed presses? **Model Solution:** A **pilot** is a hardened pin attached to the punch (or punch stripper) that engages a **previously pierced hole** in the strip to achieve accurate secondary location in each stroke of a progressive die. **Fixed Pilot:** Rigidly mounted on the punch shank. The strip must be precisely positioned under the punch at every stroke — impractical at high speeds because the pilot might enter the hole *before* the strip is fully advanced, causing jams and breakage. **Retractable (Spring-Loaded) Pilot:** - The pilot is mounted on a spring within the punch body. - During the **strip-feed** portion of the cycle, the pilot is held up (retracted) by the stripper plate. - Once the strip has been precisely advanced to its stop, the stripper releases, and the **spring pushes the pilot down** into the pre-pierced hole just before the punch contacts the strip. - This provides **positive location at the moment of cutting**, eliminating interference during feeding. **Why preferred in high-speed presses:** 1. Eliminates pilot-strip collision during fast strip advance. 2. Reduces pilot wear (only contacts the hole at the instant of cutting). 3. Accommodates **±0.1 mm strip-position tolerance** common in high-speed feeds. 4. Prevents punch breakage from mis-feed jams. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Six-Point Principle & Force Analysis (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Part (a) — 7 marks** Explain the **six-point location principle** with reference to **degrees of freedom**. Sketch the arrangement of locating points on the underside and side of a rectangular strip, indicating primary, secondary, and tertiary datum surfaces. **Model Solution:** A free rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF**: - 3 translations along X, Y, Z axes. - 3 rotations (roll $R_x$, pitch $R_y$, yaw $R_z$) about those axes. The **six-point location principle** (also called the **3-2-1 principle**) removes these 6 DOF by placing locating points against **three mutually perpendicular datum surfaces**: **Primary Datum (Bottom Face — 3 points):** Three non-collinear locating points on the underside of the strip (e.g., rest pads or buttons) — typically arranged as a triangle. These remove: - $T_z$ (vertical lift) - $R_x$ (roll) - $R_y$ (pitch) **Secondary Datum (Vertical Side Face — 2 points):** Two locating points along one vertical edge (e.g., a diamond pin + a round pin) — removes: - $T_x$ (horizontal shift toward the wall) - $R_z$ (yaw about the secondary datum normal) **Tertiary Datum (Opposite Vertical Face — 1 point):** A single locating point against the back vertical wall — removes: - $T_y$ (final translation along the strip-feed direction) Total = **3 + 2 + 1 = 6 points = 6 DOF eliminated**. The strip is now **fully constrained** in a unique, repeatable position. > Sketch the 3 points on the bottom forming a triangle, the 2 points along one side, and 1 point on the opposite side, all three sets lying on mutually perpendicular planes. **Part (b) — 7 marks** A blanking punch exerts a cutting force of **$F_c = 120 \text{ kN}$** on a strip of thickness **$t = 4 \text{ mm}$**. The die clearance half-angle is **$\theta = 6°$** and the coefficient of friction between the strip and the locator surface is **$\mu = 0.10$**. Calculate: (i) The horizontal thrust on the locator. (ii) The required normal reaction on the locator face for equilibrium. (iii) The minimum shank diameter of a hardened locating pin if the allowable bearing stress is **$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 900 \text{ N/mm}^2$**. **Model Solution:** **(i) Horizontal Thrust on the Locator:** The cutting force is inclined by $\theta$ from the vertical. Its horizontal component is: $$F_H = F_c \sin\theta = 120{,}000 \times \sin 6°$$ $$F_H = 120{,}000 \times 0.10453$$ $$\boxed{F_H \approx 12{,}544 \text{ N}}$$ **(ii) Normal Reaction for Equilibrium:** At equilibrium, the friction force on the locator equals the horizontal thrust: $$\mu \, N = F_H$$ $$N = \frac{F_H}{\mu} = \frac{12{,}544}{0.10}$$ $$\boxed{N = 125{,}440 \text{ N}}$$ **(iii) Minimum Pin Shank Diameter:** Bearing stress on a circular pin of diameter $d$: $$\sigma_{\text{bearing}} = \frac{4N}{\pi d^{2}} \;\le\; \sigma_{\text{allow}}$$ Solving for $d$: $$d = \sqrt{\frac{4N}{\pi \, \sigma_{\text{allow}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 125{,}440}{\pi \times 900}}$$ $$d = \sqrt{\frac{501{,}760}{2827.4}} = \sqrt{177.46}$$ $$d \approx 13.32 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{d_{\text{adopted}} = 14 \text{ mm (standard size)}}$$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Locating Methods and Devices** - ☐ A free rigid body has **6 DOF**; location requires **exactly 6 contact points**. - ☐ **3-2-1 principle** = 3 points on primary, 2 on secondary, 1 on tertiary datum. - ☐ Primary datum removes **$T_z, R_x, R_y$** (the "biggest" face). - ☐ Secondary datum (2 points) typically uses a **diamond pin + round pin** for fool-proofing. - ☐ **Pilot** = locating pin entering a previously pierced hole — used in **progressive and compound dies**. - ☐ **Retractable (spring) pilot** is preferred at high speeds. - ☐ **V-locator (90°)** removes **4 DOF** — used for cylindrical/round workpieces. - ☐ **Conical locator** is **self-centring** for pre-pierced holes. - ☐ Horizontal thrust on locator: $F_H = F_c \sin\theta$. - ☐ Required normal reaction: $N = F_c \sin\theta / \mu$. - ☐ Pin diameter: $d = \sqrt{4N / (\pi \sigma_{\text{allow}})}$; for D2 steel at 60 HRC, $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 900 \text{ N/mm}^2$. - ☐ **Fool-proofing** prevents wrong-orientation insertion (diamond + round pin, asymmetric notch, etc.). - ☐ **Over-locating** (>6 points) → die wear and binding → **avoid**. - ☐ **Stability condition:** $F_{\text{hold}} \cdot d \;>\; F_c \cdot h$. - ☐ Coefficient of friction for steel-on-steel (dry): **0.10–0.15**; lubricated: **0.05–0.08**. - ☐ Always state **assumed values of $\mu$ and $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$** explicitly in numerical answers. ---

topics.title: Basic Principles of Clamping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Clamping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Clamping** is the engineering operation of applying external mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or magnetic force to a workpiece to rigidly hold it against the *locating* (supporting) surfaces of a jig, fixture, or die so that the workpiece does not shift, lift, rotate, or vibrate under the action of cutting, forming, or assembly forces.

In the formal **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET602)** terminology, clamping is the *third and final step* in the **3-2-1 Principle of Location** (used in tool design to fully constrain a workpiece in space):
1. **Setting (Loading/Unloading)** – placing the workpiece on the fixture.
2. **Locating** – fixing the workpiece to a known datum (3-2-1 rule).
3. **Clamping** – applying a holding force to retain the workpiece against the locators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize verbatim):** *"Clamping is the process of holding the workpiece securely in the located position by means of clamps, so that it does not move during the operation, by the action of cutting or forming forces."*

The magnitude of the clamping force is denoted $W_c$ (or $F_c$ in some texts), and it is always measured in **Newton (N)** in SI units.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are drilling a hole through a small wooden block on your workbench. If you simply hold the block with one hand, it will spin, slip, or jerk when the drill bit engages. The solution is to lock the block in a **bench vice** — the vice jaws grip the block, applying a horizontal squeezing force, and the block rests on a flat base. Your hand applies the drill force; the vice provides the **clamping** force.

The *flat base* of the vice is the **locator** (positions the block), the *screw* that tightens the jaw is the **clamping mechanism**, and the *jaw face* is the **clamping element**. This is the universal 3-stage mental model:
- **Set** → **Locate** → **Clamp**.

### 1.3 Objectives of Clamping (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the *Six Standard Objectives* expected in KTU answers:
> 1. To hold the workpiece **rigidly** against the locators during the operation.
> 2. To **resist cutting/forming forces** without letting the workpiece move or lift.
> 3. To **prevent vibration** and chatter (improves tool life and surface finish).
> 4. To enable **quick loading and unloading** of the workpiece.
> 5. To **not distort** the workpiece (excess force must be avoided).
> 6. To allow **accessibility** to the tool/cutter at the working region.

### 1.4 Essential Features of a Good Clamp

A clamping device designed as per KTU 2024 scheme should satisfy:

- **Adequate Holding Force** — must exceed the resultant of all working (cutting/forming) forces.
- **Self-Locking Capability** — once tightened, it should *not loosen* due to vibration.
- **Quick Operation** — fast clamping/unclamping (preferably with one hand or a single lever).
- **No Interference** — must not obstruct the tool path or the operator's view.
- **Safe and Ergonomic** — no sharp projections, no pinch points.
- **Economical** — minimum number of parts, standard components.
- **Accessibility** — handles, bolts, and levers must be in operator-friendly positions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 10 Principles of Clamping (Standard KTU/NIFFT Syllabus)

The 10 universally accepted *Principles of Clamping* (also called **General Requirements of a Clamp**) are the cornerstone of jig and fixture design. The KTU board expects students to write at least **5–6 of these** in a 7-mark descriptive question.

| # | Principle | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Adequate holding force** | Clamp force must overcome working force × safety factor. |
| 2 | **Self-locking** | Vibration should not loosen the clamp. |
| 3 | **Simple in construction** | Few parts, easy to manufacture and assemble. |
| 4 | **Quick operation** | Minimum time to clamp/unclamp. |
| 5 | **Positive gripping** | Force should be applied *toward* the locators (downward + inward). |
| 6 | **No distortion** | Clamp pads must be large; high local pressure avoided. |
| 7 | **Operator safety** | No sharp edges, hands kept away from cutting zone. |
| 8 | **Accessibility** | Handles/bolts outside the working area. |
| 9 | **No interference with tool** | Clamp must not project into tool path. |
| 10 | **Easiness of loading/unloading** | Workpiece should slide in/out without obstruction. |

### 2.2 Direction of Clamping Force (Critical Concept)

The direction of the clamping force $F_c$ should always be:
- **Toward the locating surfaces** (most important).
- **Opposite to the cutting/forming force** wherever possible.
- **Downward** (against the base) for milling-type operations to use the fixture body weight as a reaction.

The **three-rule** for direction (from the KTU scheme):
- Rule 1: The clamping force should always be directed **toward the locating surface**.
- Rule 2: The clamping force should be **as close to the cutting tool** as practical.
- Rule 3: Clamp arms should be **short and stout** (rigid) to avoid deflection.

### 2.3 Classification of Clamping Devices

**A) Based on the source of force:**

| Type | Source | Typical Force Range |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| **Manual (Mechanical) Clamps** | Operator hand | $100 \text{ N} \leq F_c \leq 50{,}000 \text{ N}$ |
| **Pneumatic Clamps** | Compressed air | $500 \text{ N} \leq F_c \leq 20{,}000 \text{ N}$ |
| **Hydraulic Clamps** | Pressurized oil | $5{,}000 \text{ N} \leq F_c \leq 500{,}000 \text{ N}$ |
| **Magnetic Clamps** | Permanent / Electromagnet | $20 \text{ N/cm}^2 \leq p \leq 80 \text{ N/cm}^2$ |
| **Vacuum Clamps** | Suction | Used for thin non-ferrous sheets |

**B) Based on mechanism (Mechanical — most common in KTU questions):**
- **Strap clamp (Plain strap)** – with heel block and T-bolt.
- **Strap clamp with swinging latch** – hinged, opens automatically.
- **Screw clamp** – direct screw-down.
- **Cam clamp (rotary/linear)** – rapid-acting.
- **Wedge clamp** – uses inclined plane.
- **Lever clamp** – uses mechanical advantage of lever.
- **Split clamp / Collet clamp** – for cylindrical workpieces.
- **Toe clamp / Edge clamp** – acts at the edge of the workpiece.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).** Memorize the variable meanings along with the formulas.

| # | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | $W_c = F_c \cdot \mu$ | $W_c$ = frictional holding force, $F_c$ = clamp force, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at clamp pad | Basic friction holding |
| 2 | $F_c = F \cdot k$ | $F$ = cutting/forming force, $k$ = safety factor ($k = 2$ to $3$ typical) | Minimum clamp force |
| 3 | $F_c \cdot L_1 = F \cdot L_2$ | $L_1$ = clamp arm, $L_2$ = tool arm | Lever-type clamp equilibrium |
| 4 | $F_c = F \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | $\alpha$ = wedge angle, $\phi$ = friction angle ($\tan^{-1}\mu$) | Wedge clamp |
| 5 | $F_c = F \cdot \tan^2(\alpha + \phi)$ | Same as above | Toggle clamp (toggle joint) |
| 6 | $F_c = F \cdot \frac{a}{b}$ | $a, b$ = lever arms | Cam/lever clamp |
| 7 | $F_c = \dfrac{F}{2\mu}$ | For a horizontal cylindrical workpiece on a V-block | V-block holding |
| 8 | $p = \dfrac{F_c}{A}$ | $p$ = contact pressure, $A$ = contact area | Stress check on clamp pad |
| 9 | $F_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{air}$ | $d$ = piston diameter, $p_{air}$ = air pressure (Pa) | Pneumatic clamp force |
| 10 | $F_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{oil}$ | $d$ = piston diameter, $p_{oil}$ = oil pressure (Pa) | Hydraulic clamp force |
| 11 | $\eta_{toggle} = \dfrac{\tan(\alpha - \phi)}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos\beta}$ | $\beta$ = toggle-link angle | Toggle joint efficiency |
| 12 | $M = F \cdot h = F_c \cdot L$ | $M$ = moment, $h$ = height of cutter, $L$ = distance to clamp | Moment-balance clamp sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **NEVER** use the pipe symbol $\vert$ inside the table. Always write $\vert x \vert$ as $\vert\,x\,\vert$ or use the text form "modulus x".

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of clamping are critical in:
- **Automotive industry**: Engine block machining fixtures, body panel welding jigs.
- **Aerospace**: Wing-skin stretch forming, blade root clamping, composite layup fixtures.
- **Heavy forging**: Holding the die in press frames (bolster clamps, wedge clamps).
- **Sheet metal forming**: Blank holder force calculation in deep drawing.
- **CNC machining**: Modular work-holding (chucks, vises, vacuum tables, magnetic chucks).
- **Injection molding**: Mold clamping unit tonnage calculation $F_{clamp} = A_{proj} \cdot p_{cavity}$.

The clamp must be **over-designed by a factor of 2 to 3** to handle impact loads and dynamic forces that occur in milling, forging, and punching.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Clamping Force for a Workpiece on a Horizontal Surface

**Given scenario:** A horizontal milling cutter exerts a tangential force $F_t$ and a radial force $F_r$ on a workpiece resting flat on the fixture base. A vertical clamp applies force $F_c$ from the top.

**Goal:** Find the minimum $F_c$ that will not let the workpiece lift, slide, or tip.

#### Step 1: Set up the free-body diagram (FBD)

Forces acting on the workpiece:
- Vertical clamp force: $F_c$ (downward, from top)
- Vertical reaction from base: $N$ (upward)
- Vertical cutting force component: $F_{r,vert}$ (upward — tries to lift the workpiece)
- Horizontal cutting force: $F_t$ (tangential)
- Friction from base: $\mu \cdot N$ (resists sliding)

> For typical peripheral milling, the radial force $F_r$ is **directed vertically upward**, so we treat $F_r = F_{r,vert}$ for the lift case.

#### Step 2: Apply vertical equilibrium (no lift condition)

The workpiece must not lift off the base. Therefore, the net upward force must be less than or equal to the net downward force:

$$
F_c + W_{piece} \;\geq\; F_r + N \cdot (\text{fraction})
$$

For a *light* workpiece (where self-weight $W_{piece}$ is negligible compared to cutting forces), and assuming the base fully supports the workpiece at the moment of lift:

$$
F_c \;\geq\; F_r
$$

But to include a **safety factor** $k$ (typically $k = 2$ for roughing, $k = 3$ for interrupted cuts):

$$
\boxed{\,F_c = k \cdot F_r\,}
$$

#### Step 3: Apply horizontal equilibrium (no slip condition)

The base friction must resist the tangential cutting force $F_t$:

$$
\mu \cdot N \;\geq\; F_t
$$

Since $N \approx F_c$ (the clamp provides most of the normal force for a light workpiece):

$$
\mu \cdot F_c \;\geq\; F_t
$$

Solving for $F_c$:

$$
\boxed{\,F_c = \dfrac{F_t}{\mu}\,}
$$

#### Step 4: Combine both criteria

The **governing (larger) value** is selected:

$$
F_{c,\text{design}} = \max\!\left( k \cdot F_r,\; \dfrac{F_t}{\mu} \right)
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-type):**
Given: $F_t = 1500 \text{ N}$, $F_r = 800 \text{ N}$, $\mu = 0.3$ between workpiece and base, $k = 2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{c,1} &= k \cdot F_r = 2 \times 800 = 1600 \text{ N} \\
F_{c,2} &= \dfrac{F_t}{\mu} = \dfrac{1500}{0.3} = 5000 \text{ N} \\
F_{c,\text{design}} &= \max(1600,\; 5000) = 5000 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **sliding criterion governs**, so the clamp must apply at least $5000 \text{ N}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Wedge Clamp Mechanical Advantage

**Given:** A wedge of angle $\alpha$ is driven under the workpiece, with a horizontal applied force $P$.

**Goal:** Find the upward clamping force $F_c$ generated.

#### Step 1: Forces on the wedge

Three forces act on the wedge:
- Applied horizontal force: $P$ (in $-x$ direction)
- Normal reaction from workpiece (vertical face): $N_1$ (perpendicular to the inclined face)
- Friction force on inclined face: $\mu \cdot N_1$ (opposing motion)
- Reaction from the base: $N_2$ (vertical)

The angle of the inclined face with the horizontal is $\alpha$.

#### Step 2: Resolve $N_1$ into components

The normal $N_1$ acts perpendicular to the inclined face. Resolving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{1x} &= -N_1 \sin\alpha \quad (\text{acts in } -x)\\
N_{1y} &= +N_1 \cos\alpha \quad (\text{acts in } +y)
\end{aligned}
$$

The friction force $f = \mu N_1$ acts **opposite to the relative motion**. If the wedge moves in the $+x$ direction, friction on the wedge from the workpiece acts in $-x$ and $-y$ along the inclined plane.

Resolving friction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_x &= -\mu N_1 \cos\alpha \\
f_y &= -\mu N_1 \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Apply equilibrium of forces on the wedge in the vertical direction

The vertical clamping force transmitted to the workpiece equals the vertical equilibrium of the wedge:

$$
F_c = N_1 \cos\alpha - \mu N_1 \sin\alpha = N_1 (\cos\alpha - \mu \sin\alpha)
$$

#### Step 4: Apply horizontal equilibrium of the wedge

$$
P = N_1 \sin\alpha + \mu N_1 \cos\alpha = N_1 (\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)
$$

#### Step 5: Divide vertical by horizontal to get mechanical advantage

$$
\dfrac{F_c}{P} = \dfrac{\cos\alpha - \mu \sin\alpha}{\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos\alpha$:

$$
\dfrac{F_c}{P} = \dfrac{1 - \mu \tan\alpha}{\tan\alpha + \mu}
$$

Using $\mu = \tan\phi$ (where $\phi$ is the friction angle):

$$
\dfrac{F_c}{P} = \dfrac{1 - \tan\alpha \tan\phi}{\tan\alpha + \tan\phi}
$$

By the **tangent addition identity**: $\tan(\alpha + \phi) = \dfrac{\tan\alpha + \tan\phi}{1 - \tan\alpha \tan\phi}$

Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{F_c}{P} = \dfrac{1}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F_c = \dfrac{P}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = P \cdot \cot(\alpha + \phi)\,}
$$

For **self-locking**, we need $F_c > 0$ even when $P = 0$, which requires $\alpha + \phi < 90°$, i.e., $\alpha < 90° - \phi$.

**Numerical example:** $P = 200 \text{ N}$, $\alpha = 10°$, $\mu = 0.2 \Rightarrow \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.2) = 11.31°$

$$
F_c = \dfrac{200}{\tan(10° + 11.31°)} = \dfrac{200}{\tan(21.31°)} = \dfrac{200}{0.3906} = 512.0 \text{ N}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Toggle Clamp Mechanical Advantage (Quick-Acting Clamp)

**Given:** A toggle clamp with two links of length $L$ each, connected at an angle $2\beta$ to the line of action, with a handle applying force $P$ perpendicular to the link.

**Goal:** Find $F_c$ at the clamp end.

The toggle-joint analysis yields:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{F_c}{P} = \dfrac{1}{2 \tan\beta}\,}
$$

When $\beta \to 0$ (links nearly straight), the mechanical advantage $\to \infty$ — this is why toggle clamps are powerful and used for high holding force from a small handle input.

**Derivation (compact form):**

Let the handle apply force $P$ at the joint. Each link carries a compressive force $T$. The component of $T$ along the line of action (clamping direction) is $T \cos\beta$. The handle input balances the components perpendicular to the line of action:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2 \cdot T \cdot \sin\beta \\
F_c &= 2 \cdot T \cdot \cos\beta
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing:

$$
\dfrac{F_c}{P} = \dfrac{2 T \cos\beta}{2 T \sin\beta} = \dfrac{1}{\tan\beta}
$$

Wait — this gives the *ideal* value. With friction in the joints ($\phi$ in each pivot), the **actual** mechanical advantage becomes:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{F_c}{P} = \dfrac{1 - \tan^2\beta}{2 \tan\beta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\tan(\beta + \phi)} \cdot \tan(\beta - \phi) \;\text{... (per standard jig-fixture texts)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, the *ideal* formula $F_c/P = 1/(2 \tan\beta)$ is what is expected in a 7-mark derivation. Include the friction term only if the question explicitly mentions "with friction in joints".

**Numerical example:** $P = 50 \text{ N}$, $\beta = 5°$

$$
F_c = \dfrac{50}{2 \tan 5°} = \dfrac{50}{2 \times 0.0875} = \dfrac{50}{0.175} = 285.7 \text{ N}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Clamp Type vs. Operation

| Operation | Dominant Force | Recommended Clamp | Reason |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| Surface milling (horizontal) | Tangential + vertical | Strap clamp (downward) | Vertical force + side reaction |
| Face milling (vertical spindle) | Radial outward | Edge / toe clamp | Holds against side thrust |
| Drilling (vertical) | Axial feed (downward) | V-block + strap | Self-weight + strap suffice |
| Turning (lathe) | Tangential (circumferential) | 3-jaw chuck (radial) | Cylindrical grip |
| Broaching (horizontal) | High axial pull | Hydraulic clamp | High force, repeatable |
| Forging (hammer) | Impact vertical | Wedge + strap + heel | Impact resistant |
| Deep drawing | Blank holder force | Hydraulic die cushion | Uniform pressure, controllable |
| Sheet-metal punching | Vertical down | Magnetic / pneumatic | Non-distorting, even pressure |
| Grinding | Small tangential | Magnetic chuck | Access from 5 sides |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set A]**
Define clamping. State any four principles of clamping.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Clamping is the process of holding a workpiece rigidly against the locators of a jig or fixture by applying external force, so that the workpiece does not move, lift, or rotate during the cutting or forming operation.

**Any four principles (1.5 marks — 0.5 each, but only 4 needed for full marks):**

1. **Adequate holding force** — clamp force must be greater than the resultant working force multiplied by a safety factor.
2. **Self-locking** — the clamp should not loosen under vibration or cutting forces.
3. **Quick operation** — the clamp should be operable in minimum time.
4. **No interference** — the clamp body must not obstruct the tool path or operator's view.

*(Alternative accepted principles: simple construction, no distortion of workpiece, accessibility of operating handle, safety, easy loading/unloading.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Set B]**
Differentiate between *locating* and *clamping* in jig and fixture design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Locating (1.5 marks) | Clamping (1.5 marks) |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Purpose** | To fix the workpiece in a unique, repeatable position relative to the tool. | To hold the workpiece *in* that position against operating forces. |
| **Method** | Uses fixed datum surfaces — pins, buttons, V-blocks, parallels. | Uses clamps — straps, screws, cams, wedges, hydraulic pistons. |
| **Direction of force** | No force is applied; only geometric contact. | Force is applied *toward* the locators. |
| **After operation** | Locators remain in place permanently. | Clamps are released to unload. |
| **Principle** | 3-2-1 principle (3 primary, 2 secondary, 1 tertiary). | Ten principles of clamping. |
| **Effect of failure** | Wrong size/dimension of the part. | Slippage, accident, tool breakage, scrap. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> *For a horizontal milling operation on a rectangular workpiece, a strap clamp applies a vertical force. The tangential cutting force is $F_t = 2000 \text{ N}$, the radial (upward) cutting force is $F_r = 1200 \text{ N}$, the coefficient of friction between the workpiece and the fixture base is $\mu = 0.25$, and a safety factor $k = 2$ is recommended.*
>
> **(a)** Derive the expressions for the minimum clamping force required to prevent **lifting** and **sliding** of the workpiece.
>
> **(b)** Calculate the design clamping force. If the strap clamp is replaced by a wedge clamp of angle $\alpha = 12°$ and the same coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.25$, determine the horizontal force $P$ that must be applied on the wedge to achieve the same clamping force.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Setup (1 mark):** Consider a workpiece of weight $W$ resting on a horizontal fixture base. The vertical clamp force $F_c$ acts downward through a strap. The radial cutting force $F_r$ acts upward (out of the base). The tangential force $F_t$ acts horizontally.

**Lifting criterion (3 marks):** For the workpiece not to lift off the base, the net downward force must exceed the upward cutting force component.

Taking vertical equilibrium:

$$
F_c + W \geq F_r
$$

For a *light* workpiece where self-weight is negligible compared to the cutting force (worst case), $W \approx 0$:

$$
F_c \geq F_r
$$

Introducing safety factor $k$:

$$
\boxed{\,F_{c,\text{lift}} = k \cdot F_r\,} \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Sliding criterion (3 marks):** For the workpiece not to slide horizontally, the friction force at the base must resist the tangential cutting force.

The friction force available is $f = \mu \cdot N$, where $N$ is the normal reaction from the base. For equilibrium, $N \approx F_c$ (the strap provides the dominant normal force on a light workpiece).

For no slip:

$$
\mu \cdot F_c \geq F_t
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F_{c,\text{slide}} = \dfrac{F_t}{\mu}\,} \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

**Final note (included within marks):** The governing design value is the **larger** of the two:

$$
F_{c,\text{design}} = \max\!\left( k \cdot F_r,\; \dfrac{F_t}{\mu} \right)
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Calculate lifting-clamp and sliding-clamp forces.

$$
F_{c,\text{lift}} = k \cdot F_r = 2 \times 1200 = 2400 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{c,\text{slide}} = \dfrac{F_t}{\mu} = \dfrac{2000}{0.25} = 8000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Select the design value.

$$
F_{c,\text{design}} = \max(2400,\; 8000) = 8000 \text{ N} \quad \text{(sliding governs)}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Recall the wedge clamp formula (derived in Section 3.2):

$$
F_c = P \cdot \cot(\alpha + \phi)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Find the friction angle.

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu) = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04°
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $\alpha + \phi$.

$$
\alpha + \phi = 12° + 14.04° = 26.04°
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Solve for $P$.

$$
P = F_c \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) = 8000 \times \tan(26.04°)
$$

$$
P = 8000 \times 0.4884 = 3907.5 \text{ N} \approx 3.91 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answer:** The wedge clamp must be driven with a horizontal force of approximately $3.91 \text{ kN}$ to achieve the same $8000 \text{ N}$ clamping force.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> *Explain the **10 principles of clamping** in jig and fixture design. With neat sketches, describe the construction and working of:*
> *(a) A strap clamp with heel block and swinging latch, and*
> *(b) A toggle clamp.*
> *Compare the two clamps in terms of speed, force amplification, and typical applications.*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 10 Principles of Clamping (7 marks):**

The 10 principles are standard and accepted uniformly. Each principle carries ~0.7 marks.

1. **Adequate holding force:** The clamp must apply a force greater than the cutting/forming force multiplied by a safety factor ($k = 2$ to $3$).

2. **Self-locking:** The clamp should be self-locking under vibration, i.e., it should not loosen during operation.

3. **Simplicity:** The construction must be simple with a minimum number of parts for ease of manufacture, assembly, and maintenance.

4. **Quick operation:** Loading and clamping should take minimum time — especially in mass production.

5. **Positive gripping:** The clamping force should be directed toward the locators (most important direction rule).

6. **No distortion of workpiece:** Clamp pads should be wide and positioned such that local contact pressure does not deform the workpiece.

7. **Operator safety:** No sharp projections; the operator's hand should not enter the cutting zone during clamping.

8. **Accessibility:** The clamp handle, nut, or lever should be located outside the working area, easily reachable by the operator.

9. **No interference with the tool:** The clamp body must not obstruct the path of the cutting tool or the chip flow.

10. **Ease of loading and unloading:** The workpiece should be placed and removed with minimum effort, ideally without obstruction from the clamp.

**Part (b) — Construction and Working (7 marks — 3.5 each):**

**(i) Strap Clamp with Heel Block and Swinging Latch (3.5 marks):**

*Components (1.5 marks):*
- **Strap** (1.5–2 mm thick mild-steel flat) — bent over the workpiece.
- **Heel block** — fixed to the fixture body, located *opposite* to the clamping side.
- **T-bolt and nut** — passes through a slot in the strap and threads into the fixture body.
- **Swinging latch** — hinged at the heel block; lifts automatically when the nut is loosened.

*Working (2 marks):* The workpiece rests on the base (locator). The strap is placed over the workpiece with its slotted end resting on the heel block. The T-bolt is inserted through the slot, and the nut is tightened. This pulls the strap down at the heel-block end, which in turn pushes down on the workpiece. The swinging latch pivots open when the strap lifts during unloading.

*Sketch description (verbal):* A horizontal flat strap crosses over the rectangular workpiece, supported on the left by a vertical heel block, and on the right is pulled down by a vertical T-bolt. The swinging latch is hinged at the base of the heel block and latches onto the strap when tightened.

**(ii) Toggle Clamp (3.5 marks):**

*Components (1.5 marks):*
- **Handle (lever)**
- **Two link members** of equal length, joined by a pivot pin
- **Clamping arm** connected at the second joint
- **Base pivot** on the fixture body

*Working (2 marks):* The handle is moved through an angle, which straightens the two links into near-collinear position. As the angle $\beta$ between the links decreases, the mechanical advantage $F_c/P = 1/(2 \tan\beta)$ increases drastically. This gives a very high clamping force from a small handle input. Toggle clamps are **quick-acting** and used where the workpiece is small and rapid clamping is required.

*Sketch description:* A handle pivots from the fixture body, connected to a link, which connects to a second link, which connects to the clamp arm pressing on the workpiece.

**Comparison Table (to be written by student — must be at the end of the answer):**

| Parameter | Strap Clamp | Toggle Clamp |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Speed of operation | Slow (needs nut tightening) | Very fast (one-hand lever) |
| Force amplification | ~5–10× (via screw thread) | ~50–500× (near-straight toggle) |
| Typical clamping force | 2–50 kN | 0.5–30 kN |
| Loading/unloading | Slower (two hands) | Quick (single hand) |
| Space required | Larger envelope | Compact |
| Typical applications | Heavy milling, boring | Small parts, sheet-metal welding, assembly |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Suitability for automation | Poor | Excellent (pneumatically actuated) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing location with clamping (–2 marks):** Writing definitions of locating and clamping as the same. KTU examiners *always* allocate 1–2 marks specifically for differentiating. State the **direction of force** — locating has *no applied force*, clamping has an *applied force toward the locators*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor $k$ (–1 mark):** The formula $F_c = F_t / \mu$ is incomplete without a safety factor. Always write $F_c = k \cdot F_t / \mu$ for $k = 2$ to $3$.
>
> 3. **Using $\mu$ without specifying the surface pair (–1 mark):** Coefficient of friction differs between steel-steel ($\mu = 0.2$–$0.3$), steel-bronze ($\mu = 0.1$–$0.15$), rubber-metal ($\mu = 0.6$–$0.8$). State the value used.
>
> 4. **Listing fewer than 6 principles of clamping in a 7-mark question (–2 marks):** KTU board expects at least **6–8 of the 10 principles** in a question on the principles. Memorize all 10.
>
> 5. **Wrong direction of clamp force in sketches (–1 mark):** The clamp force must always be **toward the locator**, not away. An arrow pointing *upward* on a horizontal mill clamp is an *instant* mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the moment/tipping criterion (–2 marks):** In a tall workpiece, the cutter force can tip the workpiece about an edge. Check $\sum M = 0$ about the support edge and include the tipping condition $F_c \cdot L_c \geq F \cdot h$ in long workpieces.
>
> 7. **Unit errors (–1 mark):** Always quote the final clamping force in **Newtons (N)** or **kN**. Mixing $kgf$ and $N$ is a common valuation trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision list for the night before the KTU exam:**

- Clamping is the *third step* after **Set → Locate → Clamp**.
- **Direction rule:** clamp force should always be **toward the locators**.
- **10 principles of clamping** — memorize all 10; KTU asks 6+ in 7-mark questions.
- **Minimum clamp force for sliding:** $F_c = F_t / \mu$.
- **Minimum clamp force for lifting:** $F_c = k \cdot F_r$ ($k = 2$ to $3$).
- **Design value:** $F_{c,\text{design}} = \max(k F_r, F_t / \mu)$.
- **Wedge clamp:** $F_c = P \cdot \cot(\alpha + \phi)$; self-locking if $\alpha < 90° - \phi$.
- **Toggle clamp:** $F_c = P / (2 \tan\beta)$; very fast, very high force for small input.
- **Strap clamp** is the workhorse of milling/turning fixtures; always pair with a **heel block**.
- **Magnetic clamps** are restricted to **ferromagnetic** materials.
- **Hydraulic clamps** are mandatory for forces above ~50 kN (e.g., broaching, die clamping).
- **Safety factor** $k = 2$ (roughing) or $k = 3$ (interrupted cuts, impact loads).
- **Clamp pad area check:** $p = F_c / A$ should be less than allowable bearing stress.
- **Tipping check** for tall workpieces: $F_c \cdot L_c \geq F \cdot h$ where $h$ is the height of cutter force.
- **No interference rule:** clamp body must not lie in the tool path.
- **No distortion rule:** use wide clamp pads; never clamp on a finished/machined surface.
- **Quick-release rule:** prefer swinging-latch straps and cam clamps for production.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Jigs and Fixtures and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Jigs and Fixtures and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Jigs and Fixtures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jig (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *jig* is a work-holding device used in machining operations that **holds, locates, and supports the workpiece** while simultaneously **guiding the cutting tool** along a predetermined path. Jigs are predominantly used for **hole-making operations** such as drilling, reaming, boring, and tapping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fixture (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *fixture* is a work-holding device used in machining operations that **holds, locates, and supports the workpiece** in a fixed position, but **does not guide the cutting tool**. The tool movement is controlled by the machine tool itself. Fixtures are used for milling, turning, grinding, broaching, and shaping operations.

### The Fundamental Distinction

| Parameter | Jig | Fixture |
|---|---|---|
| Tool Guidance | Yes (guides the tool) | No (machine guides the tool) |
| Accuracy Source | Comes from the jig bushing | Comes from the machine slide |
| Primary Operations | Drilling, tapping, reaming, boring | Milling, turning, grinding, broaching |
| Cost | Generally higher (due to bushings) | Generally lower |
| Application | Hole-making only | Multi-operation, complex surfaces |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Tailor's Pattern (Jig):** Imagine a tailor stitching multiple identical shirts. He uses a *cardboard pattern* held over the cloth to **guide his scissors** along an exact path every time. The pattern **both holds the cloth and guides the cutting tool**. This is exactly what a jig does — it holds the workpiece AND guides the drill bit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Carpenter's Vice (Fixture):** Now imagine a carpenter cutting a wooden plank with a hand saw. He clamps the wood in a *bench vice* which **holds the wood steady** but does **not guide the saw** — the carpenter's hand guides the saw freely. This is what a fixture does — it holds the workpiece firmly while the machine tool (guided by its own slides) does the cutting.

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind Jigs and Fixtures

Before jigs and fixtures, machinists would:
- Mark out hole positions using scribers, dividers, and height gauges (a slow, error-prone process called **marking-out**).
- Use hand-eye coordination to align the workpiece with the drill press.

This led to **high rejection rates**, **inconsistent quality**, and **long cycle times**. Jigs and fixtures solved this by:
1. **Eliminating marking-out** — the jig carries the positional information.
2. **Enabling semi-skilled labor** — operators need only load and unload.
3. **Achieving interchangeability** — every part is identical to the next.
4. **Reducing fatigue** — clamping is automatic/powered.
5. **Increasing productivity** — multiple operations possible in one setup.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Jigs (Detailed Analysis)

Jigs are classified along **three independent axes** that examiners frequently test:

### Axis A: Classification by Method of Construction

#### 1. **Plate Jig (or Template Jig)**
- The simplest type — a flat plate with holes for drill bushings.
- Used for drilling simple parts; workpiece clamped directly on the plate.
- **Application:** Thin, flat components like brackets, plates, simple covers.

#### 2. **Channel Jig**
- The body is a U-shaped channel that straddles the workpiece.
- Provides **rigidity** and **protection** to the workpiece.
- **Application:** Small, light components where a plate jig would lack strength.

#### 3. **Angle Plate Jig**
- Built on a right-angle bracket — workpiece sits on one leg, drilling done through the other.
- **Application:** Parts requiring holes at angles (e.g., 90°) to the main surface.

#### 4. **Box Jig (or Closed Jig)**
- A fully enclosed box structure that completely surrounds the workpiece.
- Provides **maximum rigidity** and prevents deflection under heavy cutting forces.
- **Application:** Cylindrical or irregularly shaped parts, e.g., valve bodies.

#### 5. **Trunnion Jig**
- The jig is mounted on two pivots (**trunnions**) and can be tilted to any angle.
- **Application:** Drilling holes at **non-standard angles** (e.g., angular holes in a connecting rod).

#### 6. **Indexing Jig (or Rotary Jig)**
- The jig body rotates about a vertical axis in **discrete steps** (using a Geneva mechanism or index plate).
- **Application:** Drilling **multiple holes arranged in a circle** on a single workpiece (e.g., flange holes, bolt circles on a cylinder).

#### 7. **Multi-Station Jig (or Progressive Jig)**
- Contains **two or more stations** — workpiece is moved from one station to the next for sequential operations.
- **Application:** When multiple operations (drill → ream → tap) must be performed on the same hole to maintain concentricity.

### Axis B: Classification by Method of Operation

| Type | Drive Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hand-Operated Jig** | Manual clamping via screw/lever | Low-volume, simple parts |
| **Pneumatic Jig** | Compressed air (typically 4–6 bar) | Mass production, repetitive cycles |
| **Hydraulic Jig** | Hydraulic fluid (high clamping force) | Heavy-duty, large workpieces |
| **Pneumatic-Hydraulic Jig** | Air-to-hydraulic intensifier | High force with low air consumption |
| **Automatic Jig** | Fully mechanized loading/unloading | Transfer line / FMS integration |

### Axis C: Classification by Type of Machining Operation
- **Drilling Jig** (most common)
- **Boring Jig**
- **Tapping Jig**
- **Reaming Jig**

---

## 2.2 Classification of Fixtures (Detailed Analysis)

Fixtures are classified primarily by the **machine tool** they are designed for:

### 1. **Turning Fixtures (Lathe Fixtures)**
- Mounted on the lathe spindle (rotates with the workpiece).
- Includes **chuck fixtures**, **mandrels**, **collet fixtures**, and **face-plate fixtures**.
- **Application:** Mass production of turned parts with non-cylindrical features (e.g., a square head on a shaft).

### 2. **Milling Fixtures**
- The most numerous fixture category.
- Heavily built to withstand **intermittent cutting forces** of milling.
- **Application:** Slots, pockets, flat surfaces, contours on prismatic parts.

### 3. **Broaching Fixtures**
- Designed to withstand the **very high axial pull of the broach**.
- Built with extra-strong clamping to prevent the workpiece from being dragged by the broach.
- **Application:** Internal keyways, splines, odd-shaped holes.

### 4. **Grinding Fixtures**
- Built with **high precision and surface finish** to avoid introducing errors.
- Often use magnetic chucks as supplementary hold-down.
- **Application:** Surface grinding of small precision parts.

### 5. **Welding Fixtures**
- Hold parts in correct relative position during welding.
- Must allow for **thermal expansion** and **shrinkage** of weldments.
- **Application:** Automobile frames, ship panels, structural fabrications.

### 6. **Assembly Fixtures**
- Hold sub-assemblies in correct relationship while fasteners are applied.
- Often incorporate **automatic fastener-feeding** mechanisms.
- **Application:** Engine assembly, gear-box assembly.

### 7. **Inspection Fixtures (Gauges)**
- Used on Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM) or inspection tables.
- Hold the part in a repeatable, datum-referenced position.
- **Application:** Quality control of complex geometry parts.

---

## 2.3 Essential Components of a Jig (Universal)

Every jig consists of **six functional elements**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Body** — the main frame (cast iron / mild steel) providing structural rigidity.
> 2. **Locating Elements** — pins, plugs, V-blocks that establish the *datum reference* for the workpiece.
> 3. **Clamping Elements** — straps, screws, cams, wedges that apply holding force.
> 4. **Tool Guide Elements** — **drill bushings** (the defining feature of a jig) that direct the cutting tool.
> 5. **Supporting Elements** — rest pads, parallels, risers that prevent workpiece sag.
> 6. **Indexing/Positioning Elements** — used only in indexing jigs for rotation.

### 2.3.1 Drill Bushing Anatomy

| Bushing Type | Construction | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Press-Fit Bushing** | Permanently pressed into jig body | Production jigs, single setup |
| **Renewable Bushing** | Outer sleeve pressed in; inner liner replaceable | Long-run jigs where bush wears |
| **Liner Bushing** | Used inside a renewable bushing | Cost-effective replacement |
| **Special Bushing** | Stepped / non-standard bore | Counter-bore, counter-sink operations |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Drilling & Clamping)

| Formula | Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $F_t = 0.5 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot K_c$ | Tangential cutting force (drilling) | $D$ = drill diameter (mm); $S$ = feed (mm/rev); $K_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| $T = F_t \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | Drilling torque (N·mm) | $D$ = drill diameter |
| $F_a = 0.5 \cdot F_t$ | Approximate axial thrust on drill | Empirical ratio |
| $F_{clamp} \geq \dfrac{F_a \cdot n}{\mu}$ | Minimum clamping force required | $n$ = safety factor (typically 2–3); $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (0.1–0.3) |
| $\text{DOF constrained} = 6$ | Total degrees of freedom eliminated by 3-2-1 | 3 transl. + 3 rot. |
| $\eta = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{ideal}} \cdot 100$ | Locating efficiency (%) | $T_{actual}$ = locator accuracy; $T_{ideal}$ = theoretical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The clamping force formula $F_{clamp} \geq F_a \cdot n / \mu$ is the **single most important design equation** for jig designers. Under-clamping causes the workpiece to spin with the drill — a *catastrophic safety failure*. This equation is therefore tested every KTU cycle.

---

## 2.5 The 3-2-1 Locating Principle (The "Heart" of Work-Holding)

To fully constrain a free body in 3D space, **6 degrees of freedom (DOF)** must be eliminated:

| Locator Group | Number | Location | DOF Eliminated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Locators** | 3 (must not be collinear) | Bottom face (Primary Datum) | 1 translation (Z) + 2 rotations (pitch, roll) = 3 DOF |
| **Secondary Locators** | 2 (must be spaced apart) | Side face (Secondary Datum) | 1 translation (X) + 1 rotation (yaw) = 2 DOF |
| **Tertiary Locators** | 1 | End face (Tertiary Datum) | 1 translation (Y) = 1 DOF |
| **Total** | **6 locators** | Three perpendicular faces | **6 DOF eliminated** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 3-2-1 and not 6-1-1 or 4-2-0?** Using **more than 3 primary locators** causes *over-constraint* — any manufacturing tolerance stack-up will lift the workpiece off one locator, making location unstable and non-reproducible. This is called the **"redundant locating problem"** and is a favourite KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drilling Thrust and Torque Relationship

The cutting mechanics of a drill are the foundation of jig design. The drill is modelled as an **oblique cutting tool** with two cutting lips.

### Step 1: Define the cutting geometry

A twist drill of diameter $D$ mm removes a chip of uncut chip thickness equal to the feed per revolution, $S$ mm/rev. The mean diameter at which the chip is sheared is the radius of the drill:

$$
d_{mean} = \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Explanation:** Although the drill point has two cutting lips, the average chip thickness is governed by the feed rate at the periphery of the lip (assumed equal to the mean diameter for thin web drills).

### Step 2: Apply the Merchant-type cutting force equation

For a single cutting edge, the tangential cutting force is:

$$
F_t = K_c \cdot A_c
$$

where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area of the chip, given by:

$$
A_c = S \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Explanation:** The chip cross-section is rectangular — width = feed $S$, length = mean radius $D/2$.

### Step 3: Substitute to get the **drilling tangential force formula**

$$
F_t = K_c \cdot S \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the *force on one lip*. Since a standard twist drill has **two symmetric lips** that share the load equally, the **total tangential force** becomes:

$$
F_{t,total} = 2 \cdot K_c \cdot S \cdot \frac{D}{2} = K_c \cdot S \cdot D
$$

### Step 4: Derive the drilling torque

Torque is force × moment arm. The mean moment arm is $D/2$:

$$
T = F_{t,total} \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

$$
T = K_c \cdot S \cdot D \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = \frac{K_c \cdot S \cdot D^2}{2}}
$$

**Explanation:** Notice the **quadratic dependence on $D$** — doubling the drill diameter **quadruples** the torque. This is why large-diameter drilling requires either very slow spindle speeds or pre-drilled pilot holes.

### Step 5: Derive the axial thrust force

The axial (thrust) force on a drill is empirically related to the tangential force. For a standard 118° point angle twist drill:

$$
F_{a} = 0.5 \cdot F_{t,total}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_a = 0.5 \cdot K_c \cdot S \cdot D}
$$

**Explanation:** This **axial thrust** is the load that the workpiece reaction must counter via clamping. This is the **direct input to the clamping-force equation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Clamping Force

### Step 1: Define the safety requirement

The clamping force must generate enough friction to prevent the workpiece from rotating/translating due to the axial thrust.

$$
F_{friction} \geq F_a
$$

### Step 2: Express friction force in terms of normal clamping force

By Coulomb's law of friction:

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp}
$$

### Step 3: Equate and solve

$$
\mu \cdot F_{clamp} \geq F_a
$$

$$
F_{clamp} \geq \frac{F_a}{\mu}
$$

### Step 4: Introduce a safety factor

Since real-world friction varies (oil, chips, surface finish), a safety factor $n$ (typically $n = 2$ to $3$) is added:

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} \geq \frac{n \cdot F_a}{\mu}}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Given:** Drilling a $\varnothing 12$ mm hole in mild steel. Feed $S = 0.2$ mm/rev. Specific cutting force $K_c = 2000$ N/mm². Coefficient of friction at the clamp-workpiece interface $\mu = 0.2$. Safety factor $n = 2.5$.

**Step 5.1 — Compute the axial thrust:**

$$
F_a = 0.5 \cdot K_c \cdot S \cdot D
$$

$$
F_a = 0.5 \cdot 2000 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 12
$$

$$
F_a = 2400 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5.2 — Compute the minimum clamping force:**

$$
F_{clamp} \geq \frac{n \cdot F_a}{\mu} = \frac{2.5 \cdot 2400}{0.2}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} \geq 30{,}000 \text{ N} = 30 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5.3 — Select a standard clamping element:**

A M16 socket-head cap screw tightened to 300 N·m torque in a strap clamp provides roughly $30$ kN of clamping force — so this works. A design margin of ~10% brings the final design value to **$33$ kN**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Jig Type

| Workpiece Feature | Recommended Jig | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Flat plate with 2–4 holes | **Plate Jig** | Simple, cheap, fast |
| Small cylindrical bushing | **Box Jig** | Encloses and protects the part |
| Holes at 90° to a face | **Angle Plate Jig** | Built-in angular reference |
| 6 holes on a PCD | **Indexing Jig** | Circular hole pattern |
| Hole at 30° to vertical | **Trunnion Jig** | Tilts to arbitrary angle |
| 50,000 units/year | **Pneumatic Multi-station Jig** | Cycle time, repeatability |
| Drill + ream + tap on one hole | **Multi-station Jig** | Concentricity maintained |

---
