# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**

### Q1. Differentiate between a Jig and a Fixture with two example applications each. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Jig | Fixture |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Guides the cutting tool (drill bushing present) | Does not guide the tool |
| 2 | Used only for hole-making (drilling, tapping, reaming) | Used for milling, turning, grinding, broaching |
| 3 | Example: Drilling jig for a gear hub | Example: Milling fixture for a rectangular block |

> **Examiner's note:** A common error is stating "jig is more accurate than fixture" — this is **not** strictly true. The accuracy of a *drilled hole* from a jig depends on the bushing wear, not the jig type. Avoid absolute statements.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

### Q2. State and briefly explain the 3-2-1 principle of location. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The 3-2-1 principle is a standard method of fully constraining a free body in 3D space using **6 locators** distributed on three mutually perpendicular datum faces:

1. **3 locators** on the *primary datum* — eliminate 1 translation + 2 rotations (3 DOF).
2. **2 locators** on the *secondary datum* — eliminate 1 translation + 1 rotation (2 DOF).
3. **1 locator** on the *tertiary datum* — eliminate 1 translation (1 DOF).

Total DOF eliminated = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 — the body is now *fully constrained*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**

### ❓ Question A: 14 Marks (with sub-parts)

**(a) Classify jigs based on the *method of construction* with neat sketches. List two specific applications for each type. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The jigs are classified into **seven types** by construction:

1. **Plate Jig** — Simplest form, a flat plate with drill bushings. *App:* Drilling holes in brackets, cover plates.
2. **Channel Jig** — U-shaped channel body. *App:* Drilling small cylindrical components.
3. **Angle Plate Jig** — Right-angle bracket body. *App:* Drilling holes perpendicular to a vertical face.
4. **Box Jig** — Fully enclosed box body. *App:* Drilling radial holes in a cylindrical valve body.
5. **Trunnion Jig** — Tilts about trunnion pivots. *App:* Drilling angular holes in a connecting rod.
6. **Indexing Jig** — Rotates in discrete steps. *App:* Drilling 6 bolt-circle holes on a flange.
7. **Multi-Station Jig** — Two or more workstations. *App:* Drill → ream → tap on a single hole for concentricity.

> **Sketch (any two — described in words):**
> - *Plate jig:* rectangular plate with two bushings and clamp screws.
> - *Indexing jig:* circular index plate with Geneva mechanism and a single bushing offset from the centre.

---

**(b) Explain the different types of *fixtures* used for milling, turning, broaching, and welding operations. Highlight the special design consideration for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Fixture Type | Special Design Consideration |
|---|---|
| **Milling Fixture** | Must resist *intermittent* cutting forces — body is heavy, clamping is robust, multiple clamps are used. Common types: plain, indexing, universal. |
| **Turning Fixture** | Mounted on the lathe spindle → must be **balanced** to avoid vibration at high rpm. Often uses expanding mandrels. |
| **Broaching Fixture** | Must withstand the *enormous axial pull* of the broach — clamping must be *downward + sideways*; workpiece is often pre-loaded with a hydraulic clamp. |
| **Welding Fixture** | Must **accommodate thermal expansion and contraction** — locators are designed with sliding/roller supports; clamps must be releasable to prevent residual stress in the weldment. |

> **Conclusion:** Each fixture is a custom solution dictated by the *force system* and *thermal environment* of its specific operation. There is no "universal fixture" for all four operations.

---

### ❓ Question B: 14 Marks (ALTERNATE CHOICE)

**(a) With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the **essential components of a drilling jig**. Explain the role of *drill bushings* in achieving hole accuracy. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A drilling jig consists of **six essential components**:

1. **Body** — A robust cast-iron or fabricated steel frame that holds all other elements. Provides rigidity and absorbs cutting vibrations.
2. **Locating Elements** — Pins, plugs, V-blocks, or rest pads that establish the *primary, secondary, and tertiary datums* of the workpiece per the 3-2-1 principle.
3. **Clamping Elements** — Strap clamps, screw clamps, cam clamps, or swing hooks that hold the workpiece against the locators.
4. **Drill Bushings (Tool Guides)** — Hardened-steel liners (typically HRC 60–62) pressed into the jig body. They have a **precisely ground bore** matching the drill diameter.
5. **Supporting Elements** — Parallels, risers, and rest pads that prevent the workpiece from sagging under clamping force.
6. **Indexing Mechanism** — Present only in indexing jigs — a Geneva drive or index plate that rotates the jig to discrete angular positions.

**Role of Drill Bushings:**

- The drill bushing is the **only path** the drill is allowed to follow — it physically constrains the drill to enter the workpiece **perpendicular to the bushing face** and **at the correct location**.
- The hardened-steel bore (HRC 60–62) resists wear from the rotating drill, maintaining hole position accuracy over thousands of cycles.
- Bushings are available in **press-fit, renewable, and liner** types for cost-effective maintenance.
- For **deep holes** or **counter-boring**, stepped bushings (with a counter-bore at the entry) are used to guide both the drill and the counter-bore tool.

---

**(b) Describe the **3-2-1 locating principle** with a suitable diagram. What is *redundant locating* and why is it harmful? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 3-2-1 principle uses **6 locators** on 3 mutually perpendicular datum faces to fully constrain a free body in 3D space.

**Diagram (described):** A rectangular block sitting on a horizontal plate (3 locators), against a vertical wall (2 locators), and against a perpendicular side wall (1 locator).

**Why 3-2-1?**

- A rigid body in 3D has **6 degrees of freedom** (3 translations: X, Y, Z; 3 rotations: roll, pitch, yaw).
- Each locator eliminates 1 DOF when placed on its datum face.
- **3 primary locators** on the bottom face: eliminate Z-translation, roll, and pitch = 3 DOF.
- **2 secondary locators** on the side face: eliminate X-translation and yaw = 2 DOF.
- **1 tertiary locator** on the end face: eliminate Y-translation = 1 DOF.
- Total = 6 DOF → body is fully constrained.

**Redundant Locating (Over-Constraint):**

If more than 3 locators are placed on the primary datum (e.g., 4 supports instead of 3), the system is *over-constrained*. Due to manufacturing tolerances, only 3 of the 4 locators will actually contact the workpiece — the workpiece will rest on whichever 3 happen to be the highest. This causes:
- **Unpredictable location** — the workpiece may shift between cycles.
- **Workpiece distortion** — if the 4th locator is forced into contact, the part may flex.
- **Loss of repeatability** — quality control fails.

> **Design rule:** *Always use exactly 3-2-1. Never use 4-2-1 or 3-3-1.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Jig and Fixture definitions** — many students write "jig is a tool, fixture is a vice". This costs 1 mark immediately. Use the exact syllabus phrasing: jig *guides* the tool, fixture does *not*.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor** in the clamping force equation — losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Drawing 4 supports on the primary datum** in the 3-2-1 diagram — this is the most common *visual* error and is penalized as a "redundant locating" conceptual failure.
> 4. **Not labelling the drill bushing** in a drilling-jig sketch — examiners specifically look for it. Loss of 1 mark minimum.
> 5. **Mixing up "renewable" and "press-fit" bushings** — remember: renewable = replaceable inner liner; press-fit = permanent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 High-Yield Points for Last-Minute Revision 🔥**

- [x] **Jig guides the tool; fixture does not.** (Most fundamental distinction.)
- [x] **Jigs = hole-making; Fixtures = all other operations.**
- [x] **7 types of jigs by construction:** Plate, Channel, Angle Plate, Box, Trunnion, Indexing, Multi-station.
- [x] **7 types of fixtures by machine tool:** Turning, Milling, Broaching, Grinding, Welding, Assembly, Inspection.
- [x] **6 essential components of any jig:** Body, Locating elements, Clamping elements, Drill bushings, Supporting elements, Indexing mechanism.
- [x] **Drill bushings are the defining feature** of a jig — they provide the hole position and perpendicularity accuracy.
- [x] **3-2-1 principle** eliminates 6 DOF using 6 locators on 3 perpendicular datum faces.
- [x] **Never exceed 3 primary locators** → causes redundant locating and part distortion.
- [x] **Drilling torque:** $T = K_c \cdot S \cdot D^2 / 2$ — quadratic in diameter.
- [x] **Axial thrust:** $F_a = 0.5 \cdot K_c \cdot S \cdot D$.
- [x] **Minimum clamping force:** $F_{clamp} \geq (n \cdot F_a) / \mu$ with $n = 2$ to $3$, $\mu = 0.1$ to $0.3$.
- [x] **Power jigs:** Pneumatic for medium force, Hydraulic for high force, Pneumatic-hydraulic for high force with low air consumption.
- [x] **Turning fixture** must be balanced; **broaching fixture** must resist high axial pull; **welding fixture** must allow for thermal expansion.
- [x] **Locating ≠ Clamping:** Locating fixes the *position*; clamping provides the *holding force*. The two are independent functions.
- [x] **Renewable bushings** are preferred in long-run jigs — they allow cost-effective replacement when the bore wears out.

---

topics.title: Plastic Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plastic Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Plastic Materials (in Metal Forming Context)

In the context of **Metal Forming and Tool Design (PCPET602)**, the term **"Plastic Materials"** does **not** refer to polymer-based plastics. Instead, it refers to the **mechanical and metallurgical behavior of engineering materials (primarily metals and alloys) that undergo permanent, non-recoverable deformation** when subjected to stresses exceeding the elastic limit. This permanent deformation is termed **plastic deformation** and forms the foundational physical phenomenon upon which every metal forming process (forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing, sheet metal forming) is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"A plastic material is one that exhibits a stress-strain response characterized by an elastic region (recoverable) followed by a plastic region (irrecoverable/permanent), governed by a yield criterion, a flow rule, and a hardening law. The mathematical description of its behavior constitutes the **Theory of Plasticity**."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **soft clay block** that you press firmly with your thumb:

- **Light Press (Elastic Region):** You press lightly → clay deforms slightly → when you remove your finger, clay returns to its original shape. The deformation was *recoverable*. This is the **elastic region** governed by **Hooke's Law**.

- **Hard Press (Plastic Region):** You press harder → clay deforms permanently → a thumbprint remains even after you remove your finger. This *permanent* deformation is **plasticity**.

- **Even Harder Press (Strain Hardening):** If you continue pressing, the clay becomes harder to deform — it has *strain-hardened* (work hardening). The same clay now resists further indentation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Student Takeaway:** Metal forming operations (forging, rolling, etc.) are entirely *planned* plastic deformation. We deliberately push the material past its elastic limit so that it permanently reshapes into a useful product (a gear, a sheet, a wire, etc.).

## 1.3 Fundamental Engineering Properties of Plastic Materials

| Property | Symbol | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **GPa** | Stiffness in elastic region |
| Yield Stress | $\sigma_y$ | **MPa** | Stress at which plastic flow begins |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{UTS}$ | **MPa** | Maximum stress before necking |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | dimensionless | Lateral strain / axial strain |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | **GPa** | Resistance to shear distortion |
| Yield Strain | $\varepsilon_y$ | dimensionless | Strain at onset of yielding |
| Hardening Exponent | $n$ | dimensionless | Strain hardening capacity |
| Strength Coefficient | $K$ | **MPa** | Stress at $\varepsilon = 1$ in power law |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants (KTU Standard):** Poisson's ratio for most metals falls in the range $\nu \approx 0.25$ to $\mathbf{0.35}$ (for steel $\nu \approx 0.30$, for aluminum $\nu \approx 0.33$). The relationship $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ must be memorized for KTU derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Elastic vs. Plastic Deformation — The Critical Distinction

The deformation of any engineering material under load progresses through these distinct stages, as visualized on a typical **uniaxial stress-strain curve** (this is the *single most important diagram* in this module):

1. **Stage I — Linear Elastic Region:** Stress is proportional to strain via **Hooke's Law** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. Deformation is fully recoverable upon unloading. The slope is the Young's modulus $E$.

2. **Stage II — Nonlinear Elastic Region (for some materials):** A curved portion where the relationship is no longer linear but deformation is still recoverable (e.g., cast iron, gray iron).

3. **Stage III — Plastic Region (Yield Point $\sigma_y$):** At the **yield point**, the material begins to deform plastically. For low-carbon steel, an *upper* and *lower* yield point exist ($\sigma_{yU}$ and $\sigma_{yL}$). Deformation here is **permanent**.

4. **Stage IV — Strain Hardening (Work Hardening):** The material continues to deform but requires **increasing stress** to do so. This is the *upward sloping* portion after yielding.

5. **Stage V — Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$):** The maximum stress the material can withstand.

6. **Stage VI — Necking and Fracture:** Localized reduction in cross-section leading to failure.

## 2.2 Why Are Plastic Materials Important in Metal Forming?

- **Tool Design Decisions:** Knowing the yield stress and strain-hardening behavior directly determines the **tonnage required** for presses, the **die material selection**, the **lubrication strategy**, and the **draft angles** in forging/casting.
- **Formability Prediction:** The strain hardening exponent $n$ predicts how uniformly a sheet metal will deform in deep drawing operations.
- **Springback Calculation:** Elastic recovery after plastic forming (called *springback*) must be compensated for in tooling geometry.
- **Defect Avoidance:** Plastic instability (necking) and fracture criteria must be understood to avoid cracked, wrinkled, or torn parts.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **must-memorize formula set** for KTU 2024 ESE questions from this topic. Board examiners frequently set derivations directly from these equations.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Application / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hooke's Law (Uniaxial) | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | Elastic region stress-strain |
| 2 | Generalized Hooke's Law | $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \delta_{ij} \varepsilon_{kk} + 2G \varepsilon_{ij}$ | 3D elastic stress state |
| 3 | Relationship between elastic constants | $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Convert between $E, G, K, \nu$ |
| 4 | Bulk Modulus definition | $K = \dfrac{\sigma_m}{3\varepsilon_v} = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Hydrostatic stress response |
| 5 | **Tresca Yield Criterion** | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2k = 2\tau_y = \sigma_y$ | Conservative yield prediction |
| 6 | **Von Mises Yield Criterion** | $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2$ | Most accurate for ductile metals |
| 7 | Equivalent (Von Mises) stress | $\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$ | Yielding check in multiaxial state |
| 8 | **Levy-Mises Flow Rule** | $\dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}_x}{\sigma'_x} = \dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}_y}{\sigma'_y} = \dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}_z}{\sigma'_z} = d\lambda$ | Plastic strain increment ratios |
| 9 | **Prandtl-Reuss Flow Rule** | $d\varepsilon_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{2G}d\sigma'_{ij} + \sigma'_{ij} d\lambda$ | Elastic + plastic strain increments |
| 10 | Hollomon Power Law (Hardening) | $\bar{\sigma} = K \bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n}$ | Strain hardening characterization |
| 11 | Ludwik Hardening Law | $\bar{\sigma} = \sigma_y + K \bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n}$ | Hardening with initial yield |
| 12 | True Stress – True Strain conversion | $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1+\varepsilon_E)$, $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1+\varepsilon_E)$ | Convert from engineering data |
| 13 | Plastic Work per Unit Volume | $W_p = \int_0^{\bar{\varepsilon}} \bar{\sigma} \, d\bar{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{K\bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n+1}}{n+1}$ | Forging / rolling energy |
| 14 | Hill's Quadratic Yield Criterion (Anisotropic) | $F(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + G(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 + H(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 = 1$ | Anisotropic sheet metal forming |
| 15 | Lankford Coefficient (R-value) | $R = \dfrac{\varepsilon_w}{\varepsilon_t} = \dfrac{\ln(w_0/w)}{\ln(t_0/t)}$ | Sheet metal drawability |

**Notation:** $k$ = shear yield stress, $\tau_y$ = yield in pure shear, $\sigma_y$ = uniaxial yield stress, $\sigma'_{ij}$ = deviatoric stress, $d\lambda$ = scalar plastic multiplier, $K$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = strain hardening exponent, $F, G, H$ = anisotropy coefficients.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice the critical relationship $\sigma_y = 2k$ (uniaxial yield stress is twice the shear yield stress). This is derived from the Tresca criterion and is **one of the most frequently asked 3-mark derivations**.

## 2.4 The Deviatoric Stress Tensor — A Critical Concept

In plasticity theory, the stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ is decomposed into two parts:

- **Hydrostatic (Volumetric) Part:** $\sigma_m \delta_{ij}$, where $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$ is the **mean stress**.
- **Deviatoric (Shape-Changing) Part:** $\sigma'_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_m \delta_{ij}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Insight:** Hydrostatic stress causes only *volume change* (no shape change). **Only the deviatoric stress causes plastic deformation** (shape change). This is why materials can be subjected to enormous hydrostatic pressure (deep ocean, forging dies) without yielding, but a small deviatoric component is enough to cause plastic flow.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Plastic Material Knowledge |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Crash energy absorption, sheet metal formability (Lankford $R$-value) |
| **Aerospace** | Superplastic forming of titanium for complex airframe panels |
| **Forging Industry** | Die load calculation, flash design, preform shape optimization |
| **Sheet Metal Industry** | Limiting Dome Height (LDH) tests, FLC (Forming Limit Curve) |
| **Powder Metallurgy** | Cold compaction pressure determination via yield criteria |
| **Bulk Forming** | Rolling force and torque estimation using flow curves |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are written to **KTU Board Examiner standards** — every algebraic step is shown explicitly with a text explanation. No logical leaps are permitted. This is the "show your work" style that KTU examiners reward with full marks.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship $\sigma_y = 2k$ (from Tresca Criterion)

**Objective:** Prove that the uniaxial yield stress is twice the yield stress in pure shear.

**Step 1 — Set up the stress state for pure shear.**
In a pure shear test, the principal stresses are:
$\sigma_1 = +\tau_y, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = -\tau_y$

**Step 2 — Identify the maximum and minimum principal stresses.**
$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_1 = \tau_y$
$\sigma_{\min} = \sigma_3 = -\tau_y$

**Step 3 — Apply the Tresca Yield Criterion.**
According to Tresca, yielding occurs when:
$\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min} = \sigma_y$ (in uniaxial tension reference)

Substituting the pure shear state:
$\tau_y - (-\tau_y) = \sigma_y$
$2\tau_y = \sigma_y$

**Step 4 — Final Result.**
$\boxed{\sigma_y = 2\tau_y = 2k}$
where $k$ is conventionally used to denote the shear yield stress in plasticity textbooks.

**Physical Interpretation:** A material requires *twice* the stress in tension to yield compared to shear. This is the foundational relationship that links uniaxial test data to multiaxial yield behavior.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Proof that Hydrostatic Stress does not cause Yielding (Von Mises)

**Objective:** Show that the Von Mises yield criterion is independent of hydrostatic pressure.

**Step 1 — Write the Von Mises criterion for a general stress state.**
$J_2 = \dfrac{1}{6}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{3}$

**Step 2 — Add a hydrostatic stress $p$ to all three principal stresses.**
The new principal stresses become:
$\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 + p, \quad \sigma_2' = \sigma_2 + p, \quad \sigma_3' = \sigma_3 + p$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Von Mises expression.**
$(\sigma_1+p - \sigma_2-p)^2 = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2$
$(\sigma_2+p - \sigma_3-p)^2 = (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2$
$(\sigma_3+p - \sigma_1-p)^2 = (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the differences are unchanged.**
The hydrostatic stress $p$ cancels out completely because it appears in every term as $+$ and $-$ in pairs. The Von Mises criterion becomes:
$\dfrac{1}{6}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{3}$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
$\boxed{J_2 \text{ is independent of hydrostatic stress } p}$
Therefore, the Von Mises yield criterion correctly predicts that **pure hydrostatic pressure cannot cause plastic yielding** — only the deviatoric part of the stress tensor contributes to yielding.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Proof that the Hydrostatic Stress Component is $\sigma_m \delta_{ij}$

**Objective:** Derive the deviatoric stress tensor and show its trace is zero.

**Step 1 — Define the stress tensor in matrix form.**
The full Cauchy stress tensor is:
$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean (hydrostatic) stress.**
$\sigma_m = \dfrac{1}{3}(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}) = \dfrac{1}{3}\sigma_{kk}$

**Step 3 — Subtract the hydrostatic part to get the deviatoric stress.**
$\sigma'_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_m \delta_{ij}$

where $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta:
$\delta_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } i = j \\ 0 & \text{if } i \neq j \end{cases}$

**Step 4 — Verify the trace of the deviatoric tensor is zero.**
$\sigma'_{kk} = \sigma_{kk} - 3\sigma_m = \sigma_{kk} - 3 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{kk}}{3} = 0$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
$\boxed{\text{tr}(\sigma'_{ij}) = 0}$
The deviatoric stress tensor is **traceless**, which mathematically encodes the fact that it represents pure *shape distortion* (no volume change).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Derivation of the Levy-Mises Flow Rule

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between plastic strain increments and deviatoric stresses.

**Step 1 — State the fundamental postulate of plastic flow (Drucker's Postulate).**
The plastic strain increment $d\varepsilon^p_{ij}$ is proportional to the deviatoric stress $\sigma'_{ij}$:
$d\varepsilon^p_{ij} = d\lambda \, \sigma'_{ij}$
where $d\lambda \geq 0$ is a non-negative scalar (called the **plastic multiplier**).

**Step 2 — Write out the three principal directions.**
$d\varepsilon^p_1 = d\lambda \, \sigma'_1$
$d\varepsilon^p_2 = d\lambda \, \sigma'_2$
$d\varepsilon^p_3 = d\lambda \, \sigma'_3$

**Step 3 — Apply the incompressibility condition (plastic deformation is volume-preserving).**
Plastic flow occurs at constant volume (density does not change significantly in cold forming):
$d\varepsilon^p_1 + d\varepsilon^p_2 + d\varepsilon^p_3 = 0$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio.**
Dividing the first equation by the second:
$\dfrac{d\varepsilon^p_1}{d\varepsilon^p_2} = \dfrac{\sigma'_1}{\sigma'_2}$

**Step 5 — Generalize for all pairs and the full tensor form.**
$\dfrac{d\varepsilon^p_x}{\sigma'_{x}} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon^p_y}{\sigma'_{y}} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon^p_z}{\sigma'_{z}} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon^p_{xy}}{\sigma'_{xy}} = \ldots = d\lambda$

**Step 6 — Final Result (Levy-Mises Equations).**
$\boxed{d\varepsilon^p_{ij} = d\lambda \, \sigma'_{ij}}$

**Physical Interpretation:** The direction of plastic flow is the same as the direction of the deviatoric stress. This is the **normality rule** — the plastic strain increment vector is normal to the yield surface in stress space.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Power Law (Hollomon) Plastic Work Integral

**Objective:** Derive the plastic work per unit volume for a power-law hardening material.

**Step 1 — State the Hollomon equation.**
$\bar{\sigma} = K \bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n}$

**Step 2 — The plastic work per unit volume is the area under the flow curve.**
$W_p = \int_0^{\bar{\varepsilon}} \bar{\sigma} \, d\bar{\varepsilon}$

**Step 3 — Substitute the flow curve.**
$W_p = \int_0^{\bar{\varepsilon}} K \bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n} \, d\bar{\varepsilon}$

**Step 4 — Integrate using $\int x^n dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$.**
$W_p = K \left[\dfrac{\bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n+1}}{n+1}\right]_0^{\bar{\varepsilon}} = \dfrac{K \bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n+1}}{n+1}$

**Step 5 — Final Result.**
$\boxed{W_p = \dfrac{K \bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n+1}}{n+1}}$

**Use Case:** This integral is the basis for **forging load calculations** in the slab method of analysis and **rolling torque/horsepower** estimation.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Yield Criteria

| Feature | Tresca | Von Mises |
|---|---|---|
| Shape on $\pi$-plane (deviatoric plane) | **Hexagon** (6-sided) | **Circle** |
| Conservative / Non-conservative | Conservative (predicts yielding earlier) | More accurate (closer to experiment) |
| Mathematical form | Linear (uses max shear) | Quadratic (uses $J_2$ invariant) |
| Simplicity | Simpler | More complex |
| Best for | Calculations needing closed-form | Numerical simulations (FEA) |
| Predicts same result in pure tension | Yes ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$) | Yes ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$) |
| Pure shear yield stress | $\tau_y = \sigma_y / 2$ | $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ |
