# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are **modeled directly on KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) patterns** for a 14-mark module-level question. KTU uses **Choice-Based questions within modules** since 2024 scheme reform.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define a Plastic Material. How does plastic deformation differ from elastic deformation? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **plastic material** is an engineering material (typically a metal or alloy) that undergoes **permanent, non-recoverable deformation** when subjected to stresses exceeding its **elastic limit** or **yield stress $\sigma_y$**.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Elastic Deformation | Plastic Deformation |
|---|---|---|
| **Recoverability** | Fully recoverable on unloading | Permanent, non-recoverable |
| **Magnitude** | Very small (typically < 0.5%) | Large (up to fracture strain) |
| **Governing Law** | Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) | Flow rule + hardening law |
| **Energy** | Stored as elastic strain energy | Dissipated as heat + microstructural changes |
| **Mechanism** | Atomic bond stretching | Dislocation glide, twinning, grain boundary sliding |
| **Reversibility** | Reversible process | Irreversible process |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Plastic deformation involves the **movement of dislocations** within the crystal lattice, while elastic deformation involves only the **stretching of atomic bonds**. This is the metallurgical basis for the mechanical difference.

### Question A2: State and explain the Tresca Yield Criterion. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** *"Yielding in a ductile material begins when the **maximum shear stress** in the material reaches a critical value equal to the shear yield stress in a uniaxial tension test."*

**Mathematical Form:**
$\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min} = \sigma_y$
where $\sigma_{\max}$ and $\sigma_{\min}$ are the maximum and minimum principal stresses.

**Key Features:**
- **Conservative criterion** — predicts yielding earlier than von Mises (safer for design).
- **Hexagonal yield locus** in the deviatoric ($\pi$-) plane.
- In **pure shear**, it gives $\tau_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2}$.
- **Easy to apply** because it requires only the principal stress values.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive Question on Yield Criteria `[14 Marks]`

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts, typically 7 marks each. The sub-parts are either *subdivisions of the same topic* or *one theory + one derivation*.

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Derive the Von Mises Yield Criterion from strain energy considerations. State its advantages over the Tresca criterion.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total strain energy density.**
The total strain energy per unit volume in a stressed body is:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$

**Step 2 — Volumetric strain energy density (hydrostatic part).**
$U_v = \dfrac{1-2\nu}{6E}\left(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3\right)^2 = \dfrac{3(1-2\nu)}{2E}\sigma_m^2$

**Step 3 — Distortion (shear) strain energy density.**
By subtraction:
$U_d = U - U_v = \dfrac{1+\nu}{3E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]$

**Step 4 — Apply yield condition: $U_d = U_{d,\text{yield}}$.**
In a uniaxial tension test, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y, \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$:
$U_{d,\text{yield}} = \dfrac{1+\nu}{3E}(2\sigma_y^2)$

**Step 5 — Equate and simplify.**
$\dfrac{1+\nu}{3E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \dfrac{1+\nu}{3E}(2\sigma_y^2)$

**Step 6 — Cancel and rearrange.**
$\boxed{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2}$

This is the **Von Mises Yield Criterion**.

**Advantages over Tresca:**
1. **Mathematically smoother** — circular yield surface (vs. hexagonal corners) — easier for FEA numerical convergence.
2. **Experimentally more accurate** — matches observed yielding data within ~15% for most ductile metals.
3. **No ambiguity** at vertices of hexagon (Tresca has discontinuities where $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$).
4. **Distortion energy interpretation** is physically meaningful.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: A steel component is subjected to a biaxial stress state with $\sigma_x = 150$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 80$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 60$ MPa. Using both Tresca and Von Mises criteria, determine if the material yields. Take $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find principal stresses.**
For a 2D stress state:
$\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$

$\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{150+80}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{150-80}{2}\right)^2 + 60^2}$
$\sigma_{1,2} = 115 \pm \sqrt{35^2 + 60^2} = 115 \pm \sqrt{1225 + 3600} = 115 \pm \sqrt{4825}$
$\sigma_{1,2} = 115 \pm 69.46$

Therefore:
$\sigma_1 = 184.46$ MPa
$\sigma_2 = 45.54$ MPa
$\sigma_3 = 0$ (plane stress assumption)

**Step 2 — Apply Tresca criterion.**
$\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min} = 184.46 - 0 = 184.46$ MPa
Compare with $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa.
Since $184.46 < 250$ → **Safe (No yielding per Tresca).**

**Step 3 — Apply Von Mises criterion.**
$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$
$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(184.46-45.54)^2 + (45.54-0)^2 + (0-184.46)^2}{2}}$
$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(138.92)^2 + (45.54)^2 + (184.46)^2}{2}}$
$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{19298.77 + 2073.89 + 34025.49}{2}}$
$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{\dfrac{55398.15}{2}} = \sqrt{27699.08}$
$\bar{\sigma} = 166.43$ MPa

Since $166.43 < 250$ → **Safe (No yielding per Von Mises).**

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
The component is **safe** under both criteria. The factor of safety (FOS) is:
- FOS (Tresca) = $250 / 184.46 = 1.36$
- FOS (Von Mises) = $250 / 166.43 = 1.50$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:** Students frequently forget to **set $\sigma_3 = 0$ for plane stress** (when working in 2D). Forgetting this gives an incorrect principal stress set. Also, the *positive* root always gives $\sigma_1$ and the *negative* root gives $\sigma_2$ when computing $\sigma_{1,2}$. Mixing them up leads to wrong Tresca check.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Comprehensive Question on Flow Rules and Hardening `[14 Marks]`

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: State and derive the Levy-Mises flow rule. Explain its physical significance.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Postulate (Drucker's Normality Rule).**
The plastic strain increment vector is **normal to the yield surface** in stress space:
$d\varepsilon^p_{ij} = d\lambda \, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \sigma_{ij}}$
where $f$ is the yield function and $d\lambda \geq 0$.

**Step 2 — For the Von Mises yield function $f = \bar{\sigma} - \sigma_y = 0$:**
$\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \sigma_{ij}} = \dfrac{\partial \bar{\sigma}}{\partial \sigma_{ij}} = \dfrac{3\sigma'_{ij}}{2\bar{\sigma}}$

**Step 3 — Derive the proportionality.**
$d\varepsilon^p_{ij} = d\lambda \cdot \dfrac{3\sigma'_{ij}}{2\bar{\sigma}}$

Define $d\lambda' = \dfrac{3 d\lambda}{2\bar{\sigma}}$:
$d\varepsilon^p_{ij} = d\lambda' \, \sigma'_{ij}$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio using principal components.**
Since $d\lambda'$ is the same for all components:
$\dfrac{d\varepsilon^p_1}{\sigma'_1} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon^p_2}{\sigma'_2} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon^p_3}{\sigma'_3} = d\lambda'$

**Step 5 — Final Form (Levy-Mises Equations).**
$\boxed{d\varepsilon^p_{ij} = d\lambda' \, \sigma'_{ij}}$

**Physical Significance:**
1. **Coaxiality** — plastic strain increment tensor is **coaxial** (has the same principal directions) as the deviatoric stress tensor.
2. **Proportionality** — components of plastic strain are proportional to the corresponding deviatoric stresses.
3. **Incompressibility** — automatically satisfied since $\text{tr}(\sigma'_{ij}) = 0$ implies $\text{tr}(d\varepsilon^p_{ij}) = 0$, meaning **no volume change** during plastic flow.
4. **Normality** — flow occurs normal to the yield surface (associative flow rule).

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: A cylindrical steel specimen is compressed from an initial diameter $D_0 = 30$ mm and length $L_0 = 50$ mm to a final diameter $D_f = 38$ mm. The material follows the Hollomon law $\bar{\sigma} = 600 \, \bar{\varepsilon}^{0.25}$ MPa. Calculate: (i) the true strain, (ii) the flow stress, and (iii) the plastic work done per unit volume.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — True strain in compression (using volume constancy).**
Volume constancy: $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$, so $\dfrac{L_0}{L_f} = \dfrac{A_f}{A_0} = \left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_0}\right)^2$
$\dfrac{L_0}{L_f} = \left(\dfrac{38}{30}\right)^2 = (1.2667)^2 = 1.6044$

True strain (compressive, but taken as positive magnitude):
$\bar{\varepsilon} = \ln\dfrac{L_0}{L_f} = \ln(1.6044) = 0.4731$

**Step 2 — Flow stress using Hollomon law.**
$\bar{\sigma} = 600 \times (0.4731)^{0.25}$
$\bar{\sigma} = 600 \times (0.4731)^{0.25}$

Computing the 0.25th power (i.e., the 4th root):
$(0.4731)^{0.5} = 0.6878$
$(0.4731)^{0.25} = \sqrt{0.6878} = 0.8293$

Therefore:
$\bar{\sigma} = 600 \times 0.8293 = 497.58$ MPa

**Step 3 — Plastic work per unit volume.**
$W_p = \dfrac{K \bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n+1}}{n+1} = \dfrac{600 \times (0.4731)^{1.25}}{1.25}$

Computing $(0.4731)^{1.25}$:
$(0.4731)^{1.0} = 0.4731$
$(0.4731)^{0.25} = 0.8293$
$(0.4731)^{1.25} = 0.4731 \times 0.8293 = 0.3924$

Therefore:
$W_p = \dfrac{600 \times 0.3924}{1.25} = \dfrac{235.44}{1.25} = 188.35$ MJ/m³

**Step 4 — Final Answers.**
- True strain: $\bar{\varepsilon} = 0.4731$
- Flow stress: $\bar{\sigma} = 497.58$ MPa
- Plastic work per unit volume: $W_p = 188.35$ MJ/m³

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Confusing engineering and true strain.** In compression, *true strain* is the natural log of the height ratio, NOT the change in height divided by the original height.
> 2. **Forgetting volume constancy.** Many students incorrectly assume the length change is independent of the diameter change. They are coupled by $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$.
> 3. **Sign conventions.** In compression, strains are negative by convention, but the *magnitude* is used in flow curves. Always work with positive magnitudes in the Hollomon equation.
> 4. **Unit errors.** Power law constants $K$ and $n$ are *dimensional* — if $\bar{\sigma}$ is in MPa, $K$ is in MPa. Power density $W_p$ should be in **J/m³** (or equivalently MPa).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Across This Module

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\sigma_y$ and $2k$** — remember that uniaxial yield stress is twice the shear yield stress ($k$).
> 2. **Forgetting the deviatoric stress decomposition** — only $\sigma'_{ij}$ drives plastic flow, not the full $\sigma_{ij}$.
> 3. **Using engineering stress-strain in power law fits** — the Hollomon equation strictly requires **true** stress and **true** strain.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong yield criterion** — use **Tresca for hand calculations**, **Von Mises for FEA**.
> 5. **Ignoring the in-plane principal stress when $\sigma_3 = 0$** — many students forget to include $\sigma_3 = 0$ in plane stress problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Elastic deformation** is recoverable; **plastic deformation** is permanent.
- [x] The **stress-strain curve** is the most important graph — know all its regions: elastic, yield, plastic, hardening, necking, fracture.
- [x] **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (only in elastic region).
- [x] **Tresca:** $\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min} = \sigma_y$ (conservative, hexagonal yield locus).
- [x] **Von Mises:** $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2$ (more accurate, circular yield locus).
- [x] **Key relationship:** $\sigma_y = 2\tau_y$ (Tresca); $\sigma_y = \sqrt{3}\tau_y$ (Von Mises).
- [x] **Levy-Mimes flow rule:** $d\varepsilon^p_{ij} = d\lambda \, \sigma'_{ij}$ — plastic strain is proportional to deviatoric stress.
- [x] **Deviatoric stress** = total stress − hydrostatic part. **Only deviatoric part causes plastic flow.**
- [x] **Hydrostatic stress** causes only volume change, not shape change — pure pressure cannot cause yielding.
- [x] **Hollomon (power law) hardening:** $\bar{\sigma} = K\bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n}$ — most common form of strain hardening.
- [x] **Plastic work per unit volume:** $W_p = \dfrac{K\bar{\varepsilon}^{\,n+1}}{n+1}$ — used in forging and rolling energy calculations.
- [x] **Higher $n$ value** = better formability (sheet metal draws more uniformly).
- [x] **Higher $K$ value** = stronger material (requires more force to deform).
- [x] **Springback** = elastic recovery after plastic deformation — must be compensated in die design.
- [x] **True stress** > engineering stress (because area decreases during deformation).
- [x] **True strain** = $\ln(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$, NOT $\varepsilon_{\text{eng}}$.
- [x] **Volume constancy** in plastic deformation: $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$.
- [x] **Hill's criterion** (anisotropic) is needed for sheet metal forming; **Von Mises/Tresca** (isotropic) for bulk forming.
- [x] **Lankford coefficient (R-value)** predicts deep drawability of sheet metal.
- [x] **KTU Exam Trivia:** Almost every KTU 2024 ESE question on this module combines a *yield criterion* with a *flow rule application* — practice this combination explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shrinkage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shrinkage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of metal forming and foundry engineering, **Shrinkage** is defined as the reduction in volume (volumetric shrinkage) or linear dimension (linear shrinkage) experienced by a metal as it transitions from the molten/liquid state at pouring temperature to the solid state at room temperature. This dimensional change is a critical parameter in **pattern design**, **die/mold design**, and **riser design**, as it directly governs the final dimensional accuracy of the cast or formed component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):**
> Shrinkage in casting and metal forming refers to the contraction of metal during cooling from pouring temperature to room temperature, manifesting in three distinct stages — **Liquid Shrinkage**, **Solidification Shrinkage**, and **Solid Shrinkage**. The total shrinkage must be compensated through **shrinkage allowance** added to the pattern dimension.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are baking a chocolate cake in a mould. When you take the hot cake out of the oven, you will notice that as it cools, the cake slightly shrinks and pulls away from the sides of the mould. The same physical phenomenon occurs in metals — but at much higher temperatures (e.g., steel pouring at ~**1600 °C**) and to a far greater degree (typically **1% to 2.5%** linear contraction).

A second analogy: think of a fully inflated balloon taken out into cold weather. As the air inside cools, the balloon visibly deflates and contracts. Metals behave similarly — their atoms pack closer together as thermal energy reduces, causing the bulk volume to decrease.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Insight:** If a foundry pattern is made to the *exact* final casting dimension, the resulting casting will be *smaller* than required because of shrinkage. Hence, the pattern must be intentionally **oversized** by an amount equal to the expected shrinkage — this oversize is termed the **Shrinkage Allowance**.

## 1.3 The Three Stages of Shrinkage (Standard KTU Classification)

| Stage | Temperature Range | Physical State | Why It Occurs |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Liquid Shrinkage** | From pouring temp → solidus temp (above liquidus) | Fully Liquid | Atomic spacing reduces as liquid cools |
| **Solidification Shrinkage** | At solidus temperature (liquidus → solidus) | Liquid → Solid phase change | Phase change from loosely-packed liquid to densely-packed solid crystal lattice |
| **Solid Shrinkage** | From solidus temp → room temperature | Fully Solid | Continued atomic contraction in solid state |

**Standard Total Shrinkage Values (KTU Reference Data):**
- **Cast Iron:** ~**1.0 %** (linear)
- **Steel:** ~**2.0 %** (linear)
- **Aluminum Alloys:** ~**1.5 %** (linear)
- **Brass / Bronze:** ~**1.5 %** (linear)
- **Magnesium Alloys:** ~**1.2 %** (linear)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Solidification shrinkage is the **most critical** stage because it occurs during the liquid-to-solid phase change and creates voids/sinks if not compensated by a properly designed **riser (feed metal reservoir)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Each Shrinkage Stage

### Stage 1 — Liquid Shrinkage (Above Liquidus)
- Occurs while the metal is entirely in the molten state.
- Measured as a **volumetric** reduction.
- Compensated at the foundry by ensuring the **pouring basin and sprue** remain full until solidification begins.
- Mathematically expressed as:
  > **Volumetric Liquid Shrinkage (%) = [(V_liq,Tpour − V_liq,Tliquidus) / V_liq,Tliquidus] × 100**

### Stage 2 — Solidification Shrinkage (Liquidus → Solidus)
- The most critical shrinkage phase.
- Since liquid atoms are **disordered** and solid atoms form an **ordered crystalline lattice**, the solid phase is denser — producing a sharp volumetric drop.
- This is **compensated by the riser**, which feeds molten metal into the casting cavity until solidification completes.
- Total shrinkage = sum of shrinkage at all three stages.

### Stage 3 — Solid Shrinkage (Below Solidus)
- Occurs entirely in the solid state, from solidus down to ambient temperature (typically **25 °C**).
- This is the shrinkage that the **pattern maker must compensate for** by adding a *shrinkage allowance* to every linear dimension of the pattern.
- This stage governs the final dimensional accuracy of the casting on the shop floor.

## 2.2 Pattern Shrinkage Allowance — The "Why" and the "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why?** Because the final casting must match the engineering drawing exactly.
> **How?** By enlarging the pattern dimension by a percentage equal to the linear solid shrinkage of the metal.

**Engineering Workflow:**

**Step 1:** Identify casting material → Look up solid shrinkage value (e.g., Cast Iron = 1 %).

**Step 2:** Compute pattern dimension using:
> **Pattern Dimension = Casting Dimension × (1 + Shrinkage Allowance)**

**Step 3:** For multi-directional features (holes, slots), apply the allowance on every dimension, **but do NOT apply shrinkage on the parting line direction if the mould is strickled/hand-rammed without dimensional constraints** — always check moulding method.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All standard formulas required for solving KTU 2024 ESE questions on Shrinkage are compiled below. Use `\vert` for absolute value to maintain table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{liquid} - V_{solid}}{V_{solid}} \times 100$ | Total volumetric shrinkage | % |
| 2 | $S_{lin} = \dfrac{L_{solid} - L_{final}}{L_{final}} \times 100$ | Linear (solid) shrinkage | % |
| 3 | $D_{pattern} = D_{casting} \times (1 + S_{lin})$ | Pattern dimension from casting dimension | mm |
| 4 | $S_{vol} \approx 3 \times S_{lin}$ | Volumetric to linear shrinkage approximation (for small S) | % |
| 5 | $V_{riser} \geq f \cdot S_{vol} \cdot V_{casting}$ | Riser volume must compensate solidification shrinkage | mm³ |
| 6 | $T_{solidification} = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}$ | Chvorinov's Rule — governs where shrinkage voids form | seconds |
| 7 | $L_{final} = L_{pattern} \cdot (1 - S_{lin})$ | Casting dimension after cooling from pattern | mm |

**Where:**
- $S_{vol}$ = Volumetric shrinkage
- $S_{lin}$ = Linear shrinkage
- $V$ = Volume of casting
- $A$ = Surface area of casting (mould interface)
- $B$ = Mould constant (material-dependent)
- $n$ = Chvorinov's exponent (typically **n ≈ 2**)
- $f$ = Safety factor (typically **1.5 to 2.0**) for riser design

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shrinkage analysis is essential in:

1. **Foundry Pattern Shops** — Pattern dimensioning for sand, shell, investment, and die casting.
2. **Forging Industry** — Die cavity sizing for upset, open-die, and closed-die forging operations.
3. **Welding Engineering** — Predicting distortion and residual stress in welded joints.
4. **Additive Manufacturing (3D Metal Printing)** — Compensation algorithms in slicing software use shrinkage coefficients to pre-scale STL geometry.
5. **Hot Rolling / Extrusion** — Predicting final cooled dimensions from hot-formed stock.
6. **Tool and Die Design** — Sizing EDM electrodes, forging dies, and injection mould cavities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern **simulation software** (e.g., MAGMA, ProCAST, FLOW-3D), shrinkage is modeled as a temperature-dependent thermophysical property. The linear shrinkage coefficient is input as a function of temperature, and the solver predicts shrinkage voids (microporosity) location.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linear-to-Volumetric Shrinkage Conversion

We will derive the fundamental relation:
> **$S_{vol} \approx 3 \cdot S_{lin}$**

**Starting Premise:**
Consider a cubic casting of side length $L$ at the liquidus temperature. After complete cooling, the side length becomes $L_f$ (final) where:

$$L_f = L \cdot (1 - S_{lin})$$

where $S_{lin}$ is the linear shrinkage expressed as a decimal fraction.

**Step 1 — Initial Volume (at liquidus):**

$$V_i = L^3$$

**Step 2 — Final Volume (at room temperature):**

$$V_f = L_f^3 = \left[L \cdot (1 - S_{lin})\right]^3$$

**Step 3 — Expand the cube using binomial expansion:**

$$V_f = L^3 \cdot \left[1 - 3 S_{lin} + 3 S_{lin}^2 - S_{lin}^3\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-shrinkage approximation:**
For typical metals, $S_{lin} < 0.025$ (i.e., less than 2.5%). Therefore:

$$S_{lin}^2 \approx 0 \quad \text{and} \quad S_{lin}^3 \approx 0$$

This yields:

$$V_f \approx L^3 \cdot \left[1 - 3 S_{lin}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back the initial volume:**

$$V_f \approx V_i \cdot \left[1 - 3 S_{lin}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to extract volumetric shrinkage:**

$$V_i - V_f = 3 S_{lin} \cdot V_i$$

$$\dfrac{V_i - V_f}{V_i} = 3 S_{lin}$$

**Step 7 — By definition, volumetric shrinkage is:**

$$S_{vol} = \dfrac{V_i - V_f}{V_i} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{S_{vol} \approx 3 \cdot S_{lin}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is why a **1% linear shrinkage** corresponds to approximately a **3% volumetric shrinkage** — a foundational relation in pattern making and riser design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pattern Dimension Equation

**Problem Setup:** A steel casting must measure exactly **300 mm** in length. The solid linear shrinkage of steel is **2%**. Determine the pattern length.

**Given:**
- $D_{casting} = 300$ mm
- $S_{lin} = 2\% = 0.02$

**Step 1 — Apply the pattern dimension formula:**

$$D_{pattern} = D_{casting} \times (1 + S_{lin})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$D_{pattern} = 300 \times (1 + 0.02)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify inside parentheses:**

$$D_{pattern} = 300 \times 1.02$$

**Step 4 — Compute final result:**

$$\boxed{D_{pattern} = 306 \text{ mm}}$$

**Verification:** After casting and cooling, the 306 mm pattern produces a casting of:

$$L_{final} = 306 \times (1 - 0.02) = 306 \times 0.98 = 299.88 \approx 300 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Riser Volume Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A cuboidal steel casting has dimensions **200 mm × 150 mm × 100 mm**. Volumetric solidification shrinkage of steel is **6%**. A cylindrical riser of diameter **D** and height equal to **1.5 D** is provided. Using a safety factor of **f = 1.5**, determine the minimum required riser diameter.

**Given:**
- Casting volume: $V_{cast} = 200 \times 150 \times 100 = 3 \times 10^6$ mm³
- $S_{vol} = 6\% = 0.06$
- $f = 1.5$
- Riser geometry: cylinder with $H = 1.5 D$, so $V_{riser} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times 1.5 D = \dfrac{1.5 \pi D^3}{4}$

**Step 1 — Apply the riser volume inequality:**

$$V_{riser} \geq f \cdot S_{vol} \cdot V_{casting}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\dfrac{1.5 \pi D^3}{4} \geq 1.5 \times 0.06 \times 3 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Simplify right-hand side:**

$$\dfrac{1.5 \pi D^3}{4} \geq 2.7 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Solve for D³:**

$$D^3 \geq \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^5 \times 4}{1.5 \pi}$$

$$D^3 \geq \dfrac{10.8 \times 10^5}{4.7124}$$

$$D^3 \geq 2.2919 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Take cube root:**

$$D \geq \sqrt[3]{229,190}$$

$$\boxed{D \geq 61.2 \text{ mm}}$$

**Engineering Decision:** Select standard riser diameter of **65 mm** (next available standard size). This ensures adequate feed metal to compensate solidification shrinkage voids.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Shrinkage Behaviour Across Common Engineering Metals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following analytical matrix is a **high-yield KTU reference table** for solving ESE questions and numerical problems on shrinkage in metal forming.

| Metal | Pouring Temp (°C) | Solidus (°C) | Linear Shrinkage (%) | Volumetric Shrinkage (%) | Pattern Allowance (mm/m) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Cast Iron (Gray) | 1300–1400 | ~1150 | 1.0 | ~3.0 | 10 |
| Cast Iron (White) | 1300–1400 | ~1150 | 2.0 | ~6.0 | 20 |
| Carbon Steel | 1550–1650 | ~1450 | 2.0 | ~6.0 | 20 |
| Stainless Steel | 1500–1600 | ~1400 | 2.5 | ~7.5 | 25 |
| Aluminum Alloys | 650–750 | ~550 | 1.5 | ~4.5 | 15 |
| Brass (Cu-Zn) | 950–1100 | ~900 | 1.5 | ~4.5 | 15 |
| Bronze (Cu-Sn) | 1000–1200 | ~950 | 1.5–2.0 | ~4.5–6.0 | 15–20 |
| Magnesium Alloys | 600–700 | ~450 | 1.2 | ~3.6 | 12 |
| Lead Alloys | 350–450 | ~300 | 1.0 | ~3.0 | 10 |
| Zinc Alloys | 400–500 | ~380 | 1.3 | ~3.9 | 13 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shrinkage Curve — Temperature vs. Volume Relationship

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional architecture** of how metal volume contracts as temperature decreases through the three shrinkage stages. This is the high-yield KTU diagram most commonly asked in 2-mark and 5-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pouring Temperature Tp] --> B[Liquid Cooling Stage]
    B --> C[Liquid Shrinkage Stage]
    C --> D[Volume Decreases Linearly in Liquid]
    D --> E[Reaches Liquidus Temperature TL]
    E --> F[Solidification Begins]
    F --> G[Solidification Shrinkage Stage]
    G --> H[Sharp Volume Drop at Phase Change]
    H --> I[Reaches Solidus Temperature TS]
    I --> J[Solid Cooling Stage]
    J --> K[Solid Shrinkage Stage]
    K --> L[Volume Decreases Linearly in Solid]
    L --> M[Reaches Room Temperature TR]
    M --> N[Final Casting Dimension]
    O[Riser Feeds Molten Metal] -.->|Compensates| G
    P[Pattern Oversized by Allowance] -.->|Compensates| K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pattern Allowance Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engineering Drawing of Casting] --> B[Identify Casting Material]
    B --> C[Look Up Solid Linear Shrinkage from Reference Table]
    C --> D[Apply Formula Dpattern Equals Dcasting Times One Plus Slin]
    D --> E[Compute Pattern Dimension]
    E --> F[Manufacture Pattern with Allowance]
    F --> G[Use Pattern in Moulding]
    G --> H[Pour Molten Metal]
    H --> I[Metal Solidifies and Shrinks]
    I --> J[Final Casting Matches Drawing Dimension]
    J --> K{Is Dimension Within Tolerance?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Accept Casting]
    K -->|No| M[Recheck Shrinkage Allowance and Adjust Pattern]
```

## 4.3 Riser Design Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Casting Cavity in Mould] --> B[Solidification Front Moves Inward]
    B --> C{Is Riser Larger than Casting per Chvorinov Rule?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Riser Solidifies Last]
    C -->|No| E[Shrinkage Void Forms Inside Casting]
    D --> F[Riser Feeds Molten Metal Through Gate]
    F --> G[Compensates Solidification Shrinkage]
    G --> H[Sound Casting Achieved]
    E --> I[Defective Casting with Porosity]
```

## 4.4 Shrinkage Allowance Application on a Cylindrical Casting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Final Casting Required Diameter D] --> B[Add Shrinkage Allowance]
    B --> C[Pattern Diameter Dp Equals D Times One Plus Slin]
    C --> D[Pattern Diameter Dp Greater Than D]
    D --> E[Pour Metal into Mould Made from Pattern]
    E --> F[Metal Shrinks During Cooling]
    F --> G[Final Casting Diameter Equals D]
    G --> H[Verification Match Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Key:**
> - Rectangular blocks = Process steps
> - Diamond blocks = Decision points (per Chvorinov's Rule)
> - Dashed arrows = Compensation mechanisms (riser / pattern allowance)
> - Solid arrows = Sequential process flow
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define shrinkage in metal casting. List the three stages of shrinkage with their temperature ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Shrinkage is the reduction in volume or linear dimensions of a metal as it cools from the pouring temperature to room temperature during solidification and subsequent cooling.

**Three Stages (2 Marks):**

1. **Liquid Shrinkage:** Occurs above the liquidus temperature, while the metal is entirely in the molten state. Due to atomic contraction as temperature drops.

2. **Solidification Shrinkage:** Occurs at the liquidus-to-solidus temperature range during the liquid-to-solid phase change. Due to denser atomic packing in the crystalline solid phase.

3. **Solid Shrinkage:** Occurs below the solidus temperature, as the solid metal cools to room temperature. Compensated by adding **shrinkage allowance** to the pattern.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is shrinkage allowance? How is it calculated for a cast iron pattern?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Shrinkage allowance is the intentional oversizing of a pattern dimension relative to the final required casting dimension, to compensate for the contraction of the metal during solid cooling.

**Calculation (2 Marks):**
For **cast iron**, the standard linear solid shrinkage is **1%** (i.e., **10 mm per metre**).

$$\text{Pattern Dimension} = \text{Casting Dimension} \times (1 + 0.01)$$

$$\text{Pattern Dimension} = \text{Casting Dimension} \times 1.01$$

**Example:** For a casting of 500 mm length, the pattern length = $500 \times 1.01 = 505$ mm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]

**Q: With neat sketches, explain the three stages of shrinkage in a metal casting. Discuss how each stage is compensated in foundry practice.**

#### Part (a) — Explain the three stages with sketches (7 Marks)

**Stage 1 — Liquid Shrinkage:**

The metal, while in the fully liquid state, contracts as its temperature drops from the pouring temperature down to the liquidus temperature. This contraction is **volumetric** and follows a roughly linear relationship with temperature for most metals. The shrinkage coefficient in the liquid state typically ranges from **1 × 10⁻⁴ to 1 × 10⁻³ per °C**.

**Compensation (Foundry Practice):** The pouring basin and sprue are kept full of molten metal until solidification begins, ensuring that the liquid level in the mould remains adequate.

**Stage 2 — Solidification Shrinkage:**

At the liquidus temperature, nucleation begins and the metal transitions from liquid to solid. Since the crystalline solid has a **higher atomic packing density** than the disordered liquid, there is a sharp drop in volume at this stage. For pure metals, this appears as an abrupt step in the volume-temperature curve. For alloys, it occurs over the freezing range (liquidus to solidus).

**Compensation (Foundry Practice):** A **riser** (also called a feeder or head) is designed into the mould. Per **Chvorinov's Rule**, the riser must have a higher modulus (V/A ratio) than the casting so it solidifies *last* and continuously feeds molten metal into the casting cavity.

**Stage 3 — Solid Shrinkage:**

Once fully solid, the casting continues to contract as it cools from the solidus temperature to room temperature (**25 °C**). This is the most measurable shrinkage and the one pattern makers compensate for directly.

**Compensation (Foundry Practice):** A **shrinkage allowance** is added to every linear dimension of the pattern. Typical allowances are:

- Cast Iron: **10 mm/m** (1 %)
- Steel: **20 mm/m** (2 %)
- Aluminum: **15 mm/m** (1.5 %)

#### Part (b) — Mathematical derivation and numerical problem (7 Marks)

**Derivation:** Show that volumetric shrinkage is approximately three times linear shrinkage.

Using a cubic casting of side $L$:

$$L_f = L \cdot (1 - S_{lin})$$

$$V_f = L_f^3 = L^3 (1 - S_{lin})^3$$

Expanding by binomial theorem:

$$V_f = L^3 \left[1 - 3S_{lin} + 3S_{lin}^2 - S_{lin}^3\right]$$

Since $S_{lin} \ll 1$, the higher-order terms are negligible:

$$V_f \approx L^3 (1 - 3S_{lin})$$

Therefore:

$$S_{vol} \approx 3 \cdot S_{lin}$$

**Numerical Problem:**
A steel casting of dimensions 400 mm × 250 mm × 150 mm is to be produced. If the linear solid shrinkage of steel is 2%, determine:
(i) Pattern dimensions
(ii) Final casting volume
(iii) Volumetric shrinkage

**Solution:**

(i) Pattern dimensions (apply 2% on each linear dimension):
- Length: $400 \times 1.02 = 408$ mm
- Width: $250 \times 1.02 = 255$ mm
- Height: $150 \times 1.02 = 153$ mm

(ii) Final casting volume = $400 \times 250 \times 150 = 1.5 \times 10^7$ mm³

(iii) Volumetric shrinkage $\approx 3 \times 2\% = 6\%$

So volumetric contraction = $0.06 \times 1.5 \times 10^7 = 9 \times 10^5$ mm³

**Pattern volume** = $408 \times 255 \times 153 = 1.5914 \times 10^7$ mm³
**Difference** = $1.5914 \times 10^7 - 1.5 \times 10^7 = 9.14 \times 10^5$ mm³ ≈ matches calculation ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the role of risers in compensating solidification shrinkage. Derive the Chvorinov's Rule and apply it to determine the solidification time of a casting with V = 5 × 10⁵ mm³ and surface area A = 1.5 × 10⁴ mm². Given mould constant B = 3.5 min/mm² and exponent n = 2.**

#### Part (a) — Role of risers and Chvorinov's Rule derivation (7 Marks)

**Role of Risers (3 Marks):**
A riser is a reservoir of molten metal attached to the casting cavity. Its functions are:

1. **Feed molten metal** to compensate for solidification shrinkage.
2. **Ensure directional solidification** — the casting solidifies first, then the riser.
3. **Prevent shrinkage cavities (voids)** inside the casting body.

**Chvorinov's Rule Derivation (4 Marks):**

Chvorinov's Rule states that the solidification time of a casting is proportional to the square of its (Volume / Surface Area) ratio, called the **modulus**.

**Derivation:**

The rate of heat extraction from a solidifying casting is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

where:
- $h$ = heat transfer coefficient at mould-metal interface
- $A$ = surface area of casting
- $T_m$ = melting temperature of metal
- $T_0$ = initial mould temperature

The total heat to be extracted equals the product of volume, density, and latent heat of fusion:

$$Q = V \cdot \rho \cdot L_f$$

For complete solidification, the time $t_s$ satisfies:

$$V \cdot \rho \cdot L_f = h \cdot A \cdot (T_m - T_0) \cdot t_s$$

Solving for solidification time:

$$t_s = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho \cdot L_f}{h \cdot A \cdot (T_m - T_0)}$$

Rewriting in standard Chvorinov form with mould constant $B$:

$$t_s = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^n$$

where the Chvorinov exponent **n = 2** (empirically determined for most sand-mould castings).

#### Part (b) — Numerical application (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 5 \times 10^5$ mm³
- $A = 1.5 \times 10^4$ mm²
- $B = 3.5$ min/mm²
- $n = 2$

**Step 1 — Compute modulus (V/A):**

$$\dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^5}{1.5 \times 10^4} = 33.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Square the modulus:**

$$\left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2 = (33.33)^2 = 1111.11 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Chvorinov's Rule:**

$$t_s = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^n = 3.5 \times 1111.11$$

$$\boxed{t_s = 3888.89 \text{ minutes} \approx 64.8 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 4 — Design Implication:**
For the riser to remain molten and feed the casting throughout solidification, its solidification time must be **greater than 3888.89 minutes**. Using the same B and n, the required riser modulus must satisfy:

$$\left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)_{riser} \geq \sqrt{\dfrac{3888.89}{3.5}} \geq 33.33 \text{ mm}$$

Hence, the riser's V/A ratio must exceed **33.33 mm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Many students write only $S_{vol} = S_{lin} \times 3$ without showing the **binomial derivation** — marks are lost in derivation sections.
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing *linear* and *volumetric* shrinkage. Pattern allowance uses **linear** shrinkage, while riser design uses **volumetric** shrinkage.
> - **Mistake 3:** Forgetting units in Chvorinov's Rule numerical problems. Always keep $V/A$ in **mm** and $B$ in matching units.
> - **Mistake 4:** Not stating that the **Chvorinov exponent n = 2** is empirical and valid for sand castings; for die casting, **n ≈ 1.5 to 1.8**.
> - **Mistake 5:** Failing to mention **directional solidification** when explaining riser placement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Shrinkage Definition** = Volume/dimension reduction from pouring temp → room temp.
- **Three Stages** = Liquid → Solidification → Solid (memorize the order and temperature ranges).
- **Solid shrinkage is compensated by pattern allowance** (NOT by the riser).
- **Solidification shrinkage is compensated by the riser** (NOT by pattern allowance).
- **Pattern Dimension Formula** = $D_{pattern} = D_{casting} \times (1 + S_{lin})$.
- **Key Relation** = $S_{vol} \approx 3 \cdot S_{lin}$ (derived via binomial expansion).
- **Chvorinov's Rule** = $t_s = B \cdot (V/A)^n$ with **n = 2** for sand castings.
- **Standard Shrinkage Allowances (mm/m):**
   - Cast Iron = **10**
   - Steel = **20**
   - Aluminum = **15**
   - Brass/Bronze = **15**
- **Riser Design Rule** = Riser modulus (V/A) must be **greater than** casting modulus.
- **White cast iron shrinks ~2%** vs **gray cast iron ~1%** — a frequently tested KTU point.
- **Directional solidification** is mandatory to ensure riser feeds the casting (last-to-solidify principle).
- **For die casting**, shrinkage allowance is lower (~**0.5%**) due to rapid solidification and metal mould constraint.
- **Chvorinov exponent n = 2** for sand moulds; **n ≈ 1.5–1.8** for metal moulds.
- **Riser safety factor** typically **f = 1.5 to 2.0** to account for uncertainties.
- **Linear shrinkage is what pattern makers care about; volumetric shrinkage is what foundry engineers care about.**
- **Modern CAE software** (MAGMA, ProCAST) uses temperature-dependent shrinkage coefficients for accurate void prediction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two and Three Plate Mould Design
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Two and Three Plate Mould Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Injection Mould?

An **injection mould** is a precision tooling system used to mass-produce identical plastic parts by injecting molten polymer into a closed cavity, where it solidifies under controlled pressure and temperature before ejection. The mould architecture is classified primarily by the number of parting planes and how the runner system is handled.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCPET602 - Module 4):**
> The classification of moulds based on the number of plates, parting line geometry, runner ejection philosophy, and gating layout forms the foundation of plastic product tooling. Students must be able to draw labelled sketches, explain the operating cycle, and justify the selection of two-plate vs. three-plate architecture for a given component.

### 1.2 Two Plate Mould — Formal Definition

A **Two Plate Mould** is an injection mould construction consisting of **two primary moulding plates** (the cavity plate / "A" side and the core plate / "B" side) held together by a clamping system. The mould features a **single parting line**, and the runner, sprue, and gate are formed between the two plates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Specification:** The polymer enters the cavity through an edge gate (or a single restricted gate), and the runner remains attached to the moulded part at the end of the cycle. The runner is either ejected along with the part and separated manually/automatically in a secondary operation, or retained within the mould via a *cold slug well and sprue break* arrangement.

### 1.3 Three Plate Mould — Formal Definition

A **Three Plate Mould** is an injection mould that incorporates an **intermediate (stripper) plate** between the cavity and core plates, creating **two distinct parting lines**. This allows the runner system to be automatically separated from the moulded part during mould opening, because the stripper plate remains momentarily stationary while the cavity plate retracts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Specification:** A three-plate mould uses a **pin gate** (also called a tunnel gate) that connects the runner (on the stripper plate parting line) to the cavity (on the cavity-to-core parting line). The runner is ejected separately by dedicated runner-ejector pins.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

| Mould Type | Everyday Analogy | What Happens During Opening |
|---|---|---|
| **Two Plate** | A waffle iron (one hinge, two flat plates) | The two halves open like a book; the waffle and its connecting stalk come out together |
| **Three Plate** | A sandwich toaster with a middle floating tray | The top half lifts first, dragging the runner off the part; then the middle tray drops the runner separately |

Think of a two-plate mould as **one parting door** and a three-plate mould as **two parting doors stacked** — the extra door lets the mould "peel" the runner away from the part automatically, eliminating the need for a manual degating operation.

### 1.5 Industry Standard Metrics

- **Standard Mould Base Sizes:** 150 mm × 150 mm up to 600 mm × 800 mm
- **Standard Plate Thicknesses:** 15 mm, 20 mm, 25 mm, 30 mm, 40 mm, 50 mm, 60 mm, 70 mm, 80 mm, 100 mm, 125 mm, 150 mm
- **Clamping Tonnage Range:** **50 kN to 10,000 kN** for commercial injection machines
- **Operating Pressure:** 70 MPa to 140 MPa (typical injection pressure at nozzle)
- **Cavity Pressure:** 30 MPa to 70 MPa
- **Draft Angle:** **0.5° to 2°** (standard), 3°+ for deep-draw features
- **Shrinkage Allowance:** Typically **0.2 % to 2.5 %** depending on polymer

> [!NOTE]
> **Mould Steel Standards:** P20 (pre-hardened, 30–35 HRC) for cavity/core, H13 (hot-work tool steel, 48–52 HRC) for high-temperature runner systems, S7 / S50C for support plates.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Plate Mould — Constructional Anatomy

A standard two-plate mould consists of the following functional sub-assemblies:

1. **Fixed Side (A-Side, Cavity Plate)**
   - Cavity insert (forms the outer surface of the part)
   - Sprue bush (aligns with machine nozzle)
   - Locating ring (for accurate alignment with the machine platen)
   - Clamping plate
   - Support plate

2. **Movable Side (B-Side, Core Plate)**
   - Core insert (forms the inner/surface features)
   - Sprue puller / cold slug well
   - Ejector pins (return pins, knock-out pins)
   - Ejector retainer plate
   - Ejector back plate
   - Support plate
   - Guide pillars and bushes (for alignment)

3. **Auxiliary Systems**
   - Cooling channels (waterlines)
   - Guide pins
   - Interlocks
   - Parting surface locks

### 2.2 Three Plate Mould — Constructional Anatomy

A three-plate mould adds an **intermediate (stripper) plate** between the cavity and core plates:

1. **Fixed Side** — Same as two-plate (cavity, sprue bush, locating ring)
2. **Stripper Plate** — Carries the runner channels and pin gates
3. **Movable Side** — Core, cavity half, ejector system
4. **Special Features Unique to Three-Plate:**
   - **Tunnel gates (pin gates):** Small-diameter gates (typically **0.8 mm to 1.5 mm**) formed at the parting line between cavity and stripper plate
   - **Runner puller pins:** Hold the runner on the stripper plate during initial opening
   - **Stoppers/limit switches:** Control the opening stroke so the runner drops free
   - **Sprue puller system:** A Z-shaped or hook-type pin that grips the sprue as the mould opens
   - **Cavity plate return system:** Returns the cavity plate to the closed position

### 2.3 Operating Cycle Comparison

| Phase | Two Plate Mould | Three Plate Mould |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Mould Closing | Cavity meets core, clamps | All three plates close and clamp |
| 2. Injection | Molten plastic enters cavity via edge gate | Plastic enters runner (between cavity & stripper), passes through pin gate into cavity |
| 3. Cooling | Plastic solidifies in cavity | Plastic solidifies in cavity |
| 4. Mould Opening | Cavity plate moves back; part + runner stay on core due to ejection-side adhesion | Cavity plate moves first; runner is held by runner pullers on stripper plate |
| 5. Runner Separation | Part + runner ejected together | Runner is automatically stripped between parting lines and drops into a runner bin |
| 6. Ejection | Ejector pins push part + runner out | Stripper plate returns; ejector pins push part out of core |

### 2.4 Selection Criteria — When to Use What?

**Use a Two-Plate Mould when:**
- Part has a single, well-defined parting line
- Parting line can be positioned to form an edge gate naturally
- Cycle time is critical (three-plate is slightly longer)
- Tool cost must be minimized (two-plate is **20–40 % cheaper**)
- Symmetric, simple geometry

**Use a Three-Plate Mould when:**
- Part requires **multi-cavity** layout with centrally-fed cavities
- Part needs a **gate mark at a hidden/non-visible location** (pin gate leaves a smaller vestige than an edge gate)
- Part has **multiple gates** required for flow balance
- Runner is to be **automatically separated** (no manual degating)
- Part is symmetric about a central axis

### 2.5 Advantages & Disadvantages Summary

**Two-Plate Mould**

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Lower tooling cost | Runner must be removed manually/automatically after ejection |
| Simpler construction | Edge gate leaves a larger gate mark on the part |
| Faster cycle time | Multi-cavity layouts are harder to balance |
| Easier maintenance | Limited gating locations |
| Less plate deflection | Restricted to single parting line |

**Three-Plate Mould**

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Automatic runner separation | Higher tooling cost |
| Smaller, less visible gate mark (pin gate) | Slightly longer cycle (two-stage opening) |
| Better flow balance in multi-cavity moulds | More complex construction |
| Central gating possible | Requires runner collection system |
| More design flexibility | Greater plate deflection risk if not properly supported |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_m = L_p \cdot (1 + S)$ | Mould dimension for shrinkage compensation |
| 2 | $\Delta L = L_p \cdot S$ | Shrinkage allowance added to part dimension |
| 3 | $F_{clamp} \geq A_{proj} \cdot P_{cav}$ | Required clamping tonnage to prevent flash |
| 4 | $A_{proj} = N \cdot A_{cavity}$ | Projected area of all cavities on parting line |
| 5 | $F_{eject} = A_{eject} \cdot \sigma_{yield} \cdot \mu$ | Ejection force needed to push the part out of the core |
| 6 | $V_{shot} = V_{cavity} + V_{runner} + V_{gate}$ | Total material volume per shot (including waste) |
| 7 | $D_{runner} = K \cdot \sqrt[4]{V_{cavity}}$ | Empirical runner diameter rule (K = 0.6–0.8) |
| 8 | $L_{runner} = 2.5 \cdot D_{runner}$ | Minimum cold-runner length-to-diameter ratio |
| 9 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \cdot \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \cdot (T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi^2 \cdot (T_e - T_{wall})}\right)$ | Cooling time (simplified) |
| 10 | $P_{inj} = P_{cav} + \Delta P_{run} + \Delta P_{gate}$ | Injection pressure balance |
| 11 | $W_{part} = W_{mould} \cdot (1 - S)$ | Part weight from mould weight |
| 12 | $Q_{cool} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t_{cool}}$ | Cooling water flow rate required |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend for Formula Sheet:**
> $L_m$ = mould dimension, $L_p$ = part dimension, $S$ = volumetric shrinkage (decimal), $A_{proj}$ = projected area, $P_{cav}$ = cavity pressure (MPa), $N$ = number of cavities, $A_{cavity}$ = projected area of one cavity, $A_{eject}$ = ejector pin cross-section, $\sigma_{yield}$ = material yield stress, $\mu$ = friction coefficient (0.2–0.4 for plastic-on-steel), $h$ = part wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $T_m$ = melt temperature, $T_{wall}$ = mould wall temperature, $T_e$ = ejection temperature, $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Products:** Bottle caps, mobile phone housings, toys (LEGO bricks) — predominantly three-plate for central pin gating
- **Automotive:** Dashboard components, switches — two-plate for large-area parts
- **Medical:** Syringe barrels, pipette tips — three-plate for multi-cavity high-precision parts
- **Packaging:** Container lids, closures — both types based on gate location requirements

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shrinkage Compensation

When a plastic part cools from melt temperature to ejection temperature, it undergoes **volumetric shrinkage**. The mould cavity must be oversized to compensate.

**Given:** A cylindrical bushing with nominal outer diameter $L_p = 50.00$ mm, material is **Polypropylene (PP)** with shrinkage $S = 1.8\% = 0.018$.

**To Find:** Mould cavity dimension $L_m$.

**Derivation:**

Starting with the linear shrinkage compensation equation:

$$
L_m = L_p \cdot (1 + S)
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
L_m = 50.00 \cdot (1 + 0.018)
$$

$$
L_m = 50.00 \cdot 1.018
$$

$$
L_m = 50.90 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** The cavity must be machined to **50.90 mm** to produce a part measuring exactly **50.00 mm** after cooling.

**Logic Check:** Since the part shrinks, the cavity must be larger by the shrinkage percentage. $50.00 \times 0.018 = 0.90$ mm allowance, giving $50.00 + 0.90 = 50.90$ mm. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clamping Tonnage Calculation

**Given:** A four-cavity mould producing rectangular covers, each cavity has projected dimensions **120 mm × 80 mm**. Cavity pressure is **40 MPa**. The machine is rated at **2000 kN** clamping force.

**To Find:** Is the machine tonnage adequate?

**Step 1 — Calculate the total projected area:**

$$
A_{proj} = N \cdot A_{cavity}
$$

$$
A_{cavity} = 120 \times 80 = 9600 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_{proj} = 4 \times 9600 = 38400 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the required clamping force:**

$$
F_{clamp,req} = A_{proj} \cdot P_{cav}
$$

Convert area to m² for MPa consistency:

$$
A_{proj} = 38400 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0384 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{clamp,req} = 0.0384 \times 40 \times 10^6
$$

$$
F_{clamp,req} = 1{,}536{,}000 \text{ N} = 1536 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety factor (typically 1.2 to 1.5):**

$$
F_{clamp,safe} = 1.3 \times 1536 = 1996.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$
F_{machine} = 2000 \text{ kN} \geq F_{clamp,safe} = 1996.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Result:** The 2000 kN machine is **just adequate** (margin of only **0.16 %**). In practice, the engineer should select a **2500 kN** machine for safety.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to apply a safety factor of 1.2–1.5 on the calculated clamping force. KTU examiners explicitly look for this margin in part-(b) of 14-mark problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Runner Diameter from Empirical Rule

**Given:** A single-cavity mould produces a part of volume $V_{cavity} = 32$ cm³. Polymer is **ABS**.

**To Find:** Cold runner diameter $D_{runner}$.

**Step 1 — Convert volume to mm³:**

$$
V_{cavity} = 32 \times 10^3 = 32{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply empirical rule with $K = 0.7$ for ABS:**

$$
D_{runner} = K \cdot \sqrt[4]{V_{cavity}}
$$

$$
D_{runner} = 0.7 \cdot \sqrt[4]{32000}
$$

$$
\sqrt[4]{32000} = (32000)^{0.25}
$$

$$
\sqrt{32000} = 178.885
$$

$$
\sqrt{178.885} = 13.376
$$

$$
D_{runner} = 0.7 \times 13.376 = 9.36 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Round to standard tooling size:**

$$
D_{runner} \approx 10 \text{ mm (rounded up)}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum runner length:**

$$
L_{runner,min} = 2.5 \cdot D_{runner} = 2.5 \times 10 = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** Use a **10 mm diameter runner** with minimum length **25 mm** for this ABS part.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ejection Force for a Circular Core

**Given:** A polyethylene part is moulded on a cylindrical core of diameter $d = 25$ mm and length $l = 80$ mm. Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.30$. Cavity pressure at ejection $P_e = 25$ MPa. Material yield $\sigma_y = 22$ MPa.

**To Find:** Ejection force $F_{eject}$ using the simplified adhesion-friction model.

**Step 1 — Contact area between part and core:**

$$
A_{contact} = \pi \cdot d \cdot l
$$

$$
A_{contact} = \pi \times 25 \times 80
$$

$$
A_{contact} = 6283.19 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Frictional ejection force (slip model):**

$$
F_{eject} = \mu \cdot P_e \cdot A_{contact}
$$

$$
F_{eject} = 0.30 \times 25 \times 6283.19
$$

$$
F_{eject} = 47{,}123.9 \text{ N} \approx 47.1 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with shear-limited model:**

$$
F_{shear} = \sigma_y \cdot A_{contact} = 22 \times 6283.19 = 138{,}230 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual ejection force is the minimum required to break the static grip:**

$$
F_{eject,design} = \max(F_{eject}, F_{shear}/10)
$$

The factor of 10 on the shear term accounts for the fact that only a small portion of the part cross-section is loaded at any moment.

$$
F_{shear}/10 = 13{,}823 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{eject,design} = \max(47{,}124, 13{,}823) = 47{,}124 \text{ N}
$$

**Result:** An ejection force of **47.1 kN** is required. This will determine the number and diameter of ejector pins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Follow-up:** If 4 ejector pins are used, each must provide at least $47.1/4 = 11.78$ kN. For 6 mm diameter pins ($A_{pin} = 28.27$ mm²), the compressive stress on each pin is $11{,}780/28.27 = 416.7$ MPa — which exceeds the recommended maximum of 80–100 MPa for ejector pins. Therefore, **8 pins of 6 mm diameter** or **6 pins of 8 mm diameter** must be used.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Cooling Time Estimate

**Given:** Wall thickness $h = 3$ mm, melt temperature $T_m = 240°C$, mould wall temperature $T_{wall} = 60°C$, ejection temperature $T_e = 100°C$. Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 0.09 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (for PP).

**To Find:** Cooling time $t_{cool}$.

**Step 1 — Plug into the simplified cooling equation:**

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \cdot \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{8 \cdot (T_m - T_{wall})}{\pi^2 \cdot (T_e - T_{wall})}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Convert all lengths to metres and temperatures to Kelvin (differences cancel):**

$$
h = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}, \quad h^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\pi^2 = 9.8696
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the argument of the logarithm:**

$$
T_m - T_{wall} = 240 - 60 = 180
$$

$$
T_e - T_{wall} = 100 - 60 = 40
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 8 \times 180 = 1440
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = \pi^2 \times 40 = 9.8696 \times 40 = 394.78
$$

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{1440}{394.78} = 3.648
$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$
\ln(3.648) = 1.294
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the coefficient:**

$$
\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \cdot \alpha} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \times 0.09 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
= \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{8.883 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
= 10.13 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply:**

$$
t_{cool} = 10.13 \times 1.294 = 13.11 \text{ s}
$$

**Result:** Minimum cooling time is **~13 seconds**. In production, a safety factor of **1.2–1.5** is added, giving **16–20 s** of actual cooling.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Mould Type Selection

| Criterion | Weight | Two Plate Score | Three Plate Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tool cost | High | **9 / 10** | 5 / 10 |
| Cycle time | High | **9 / 10** | 6 / 10 |
| Gate mark visibility | Medium | 4 / 10 | **9 / 10** |
| Multi-cavity balance | Medium | 5 / 10 | **9 / 10** |
| Automatic runner separation | Medium | 3 / 10 | **10 / 10** |
| Maintenance ease | Low | **8 / 10** | 5 / 10 |
| Design flexibility | Medium | 5 / 10 | **8 / 10** |
| **Total (weighted)** | — | **6.4 / 10** | **7.5 / 10** |

**Decision Rule (KTU Standard):**
- If score $\geq$ 7.0 and part has a hidden gate location requirement → **Three Plate**
- If score $<$ 7.0 and tool cost is the primary constraint → **Two Plate**
- If part has multiple identical cavities needing central gating → **Three Plate (mandatory)**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.3 Operating Sequence — Three Plate Mould (Phase Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Nozzle as Machine Nozzle
    participant Cavity as Cavity Plate
    participant Stripper as Stripper Plate
    participant Core as Core Plate
    participant Ejector as Ejector System

    Note over Nozzle,Ejector: PHASE 1 - CLOSING
    Cavity->>Stripper: Close
    Stripper->>Core: Close and clamp
    Ejector->>Stripper: Return to home position

    Note over Nozzle,Ejector: PHASE 2 - INJECTION
    Nozzle->>Cavity: Inject melt at 100 MPa
    Cavity->>Stripper: Fill runner
    Stripper->>Core: Fill cavity through pin gate

    Note over Nozzle,Ejector: PHASE 3 - COOLING
    Cavity->>Stripper: Solidify in runner
    Stripper->>Core: Solidify in cavity

    Note over Nozzle,Ejector: PHASE 4 - FIRST STAGE OPENING
    Cavity->>Stripper: Open 20 to 50 mm
    Note right of Stripper: Runner held by puller pins

    Note over Nozzle,Ejector: PHASE 5 - SECOND STAGE OPENING
    Stripper->>Core: Stripper plate separates from core
    Note right of Stripper: Runner drops into bin

    Note over Nozzle,Ejector: PHASE 6 - EJECTION
    Ejector->>Core: Push part out
    Core->>Ejector: Part released
```

### 4.4 Comparative Flow — Two Plate vs Three Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Mould Closed]) --> Inj[Injection Phase]
    Inj --> Cool[Cooling Phase]
    Cool --> Open1{Two or Three Plate?}

    Open1 -->|Two Plate| TP1[Open Single Parting Line]
    TP1 --> TP2[Part plus Runner Eject Together]
    TP2 --> TP3{Manual or\nAutomatic Degating?}
    TP3 -->|Manual| TP4[Operator Removes Runner]
    TP3 -->|Auto| TP5[Robot Trims Runner]
    TP4 --> End([Cycle Complete])
    TP5 --> End

    Open1 -->|Three Plate| ThP1[Stage 1 - Cavity Separates from Stripper]
    ThP1 --> ThP2[Runner Stays on Stripper Plate]
    ThP2 --> ThP3[Stage 2 - Stripper Separates from Core]
    ThP3 --> ThP4[Runner Falls into Collection Bin]
    ThP4 --> ThP5[Part Ejected by Ejector Pins]
    ThP5 --> End
```
