## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List any four differences between a two-plate mould and a three-plate mould used in plastic injection moulding."**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Two Plate Mould | Three Plate Mould |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Has **two** main plates (cavity + core) | Has **three** main plates (cavity + stripper + core) |
| 2 | Has **one** parting line | Has **two** parting lines |
| 3 | Uses **edge gate** (or sub-gate) | Uses **pin gate** (tunnel gate) |
| 4 | Runner is ejected along with the part and needs separate **degating** | Runner is **automatically separated** during opening |
| 5 | Lower tooling cost (~ **20–40 % less**) | Higher tooling cost |
| 6 | Faster cycle time | Slightly longer cycle due to two-stage opening |

*(Any four differences — 0.5 mark each; tabular presentation + correct technical terminology — 1 mark. Total: 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is a sprue break and why is it provided in a two-plate mould?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sprue break** is a small undercut or reverse-taper feature machined at the junction between the sprue and the runner system in a two-plate mould. When the mould opens, the cooled sprue is gripped by the **sprue puller pin** on the B-side and is **intentionally fractured at the sprue break point**, rather than at the gate.

**Purpose:**
1. It ensures the **runner remains attached to the sprue** on the B-side (ejector side) and is ejected with the part.
2. It **prevents the runner from being pulled back** into the cavity plate (A-side), which would jam the mould.
3. It provides a **clean separation point** so that the gate vestige on the moulded part is consistent and minimal.

*(Definition — 1 mark; Purpose point 1 — 1 mark; Purpose points 2 and 3 — 1 mark. Total: 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a three-plate injection mould**. List **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of using a three-plate mould over a two-plate mould.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A four-cavity three-plate mould is to be designed for a square cover of side **60 mm × 60 mm × 4 mm** wall thickness. Calculate:
1. Total projected area
2. Required clamping tonnage (cavity pressure = 35 MPa, safety factor 1.3)
3. Cold runner diameter using empirical rule ($K = 0.7$, $V_{cavity} = 14.4$ cm³)
4. Minimum runner length

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working [7 marks]:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A three-plate mould consists of the following components arranged between the fixed platen of the machine and the moving platen:

1. **Clamping plate** (A-side) — bolted to the fixed machine platen
2. **Support plate** (A-side)
3. **Cavity plate** — contains the cavity insert
4. **Sprue bush** — aligned with the machine nozzle via the locating ring
5. **Stripper plate (intermediate plate)** — contains the **runner channels** and the **pin gates** (0.8–1.5 mm diameter) that lead into the cavity
6. **Runner puller pins** — anchored in the stripper plate, they retain the runner during the first stage of opening
7. **Core plate** (B-side) — contains the core insert
8. **Support plate** (B-side)
9. **Ejector pins, return pins, ejector retainer and back plate**
10. **Guide pillars and bushes** for accurate plate alignment
11. **Cooling channels** in both cavity and core plates

**Working (3 marks):**

- **Mould closing:** All three plates close in sequence; clamping force is applied.
- **Injection:** Molten polymer is injected through the sprue bush, fills the runner system between the cavity and stripper plates, then passes through the **pin gates** into the cavity.
- **Cooling:** The polymer solidifies under cavity pressure.
- **First-stage opening:** The **cavity plate retracts** while the stripper plate remains momentarily held in position by the runner puller pins. This separates the part from the runner at the pin gate.
- **Second-stage opening:** The stripper plate is then released and moves with the core, **dropping the runner** into a collection bin.
- **Ejection:** Ejector pins push the moulded part out of the core.

**Advantages (over two-plate) [any 2]:**
- Automatic runner separation (no manual degating)
- Smaller, less visible gate vestige
- Better cavity-fill balance in multi-cavity layouts
- Allows central gating for symmetric parts

**Disadvantages (any 2):**
- Higher tooling cost (typically 30–50 % more)
- Slightly longer cycle time
- More complex construction and maintenance
- Greater plate deflection risk

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Total projected area** [1.5 marks]

$$
A_{cavity} = 60 \times 60 = 3600 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_{proj} = N \cdot A_{cavity} = 4 \times 3600 = 14{,}400 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.0144 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Required clamping tonnage** [2 marks]

$$
F_{clamp,req} = A_{proj} \cdot P_{cav} = 0.0144 \times 35 \times 10^6
$$

$$
F_{clamp,req} = 504{,}000 \text{ N} = 504 \text{ kN}
$$

Apply safety factor 1.3:

$$
F_{clamp,safe} = 1.3 \times 504 = 655.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Runner diameter** [2 marks]

Convert volume: $V_{cavity} = 14.4 \text{ cm}^3 = 14{,}400 \text{ mm}^3$

$$
D_{runner} = K \cdot \sqrt[4]{V_{cavity}} = 0.7 \times \sqrt[4]{14400}
$$

$$
\sqrt{14400} = 120, \quad \sqrt{120} = 10.954
$$

$$
D_{runner} = 0.7 \times 10.954 = 7.67 \text{ mm}
$$

Round up to standard: $D_{runner} = 8$ mm

**Step 4: Minimum runner length** [1.5 marks]

$$
L_{runner,min} = 2.5 \cdot D_{runner} = 2.5 \times 8 = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
1. $A_{proj} = 14{,}400$ mm²
2. $F_{clamp,safe} = 655.2$ kN (select a **800 kN** machine)
3. $D_{runner} = 8$ mm
4. $L_{runner,min} = 20$ mm

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a two-plate injection mould**. List **two advantages** and **two limitations**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A two-plate mould is to produce a **polypropylene (PP)** flat plate of dimensions **150 mm × 100 mm × 5 mm**. Given:
- Shrinkage $S = 1.8\%$
- Cavity pressure = 30 MPa
- Clamping tonnage available = 1200 kN (apply safety factor 1.25)
- Cooling water: inlet $25°C$, outlet $35°C$, mass flow rate needed to be calculated for $t_{cool} = 20$ s, $c_p = 2.0$ kJ/(kg·K), part mass $m = 0.075$ kg

Calculate:
1. Mould cavity dimensions
2. Number of cavities that can be moulded
3. Cooling water mass flow rate

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working [7 marks]:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):** A two-plate mould comprises:

1. **Fixed half (A-side):** clamping plate, support plate, cavity plate, sprue bush, locating ring
2. **Movable half (B-side):** support plate, core plate, ejector plate assembly (ejector pins, return pins, retainer plate, back plate), guide pillars
3. **Cold slug well and sprue puller** on B-side
4. **Cooling channels** in both halves
5. **Parting line locks and interlocks**

**Working (2 marks):**
- The two plates close and clamp.
- Molten plastic enters through the sprue, flows through the runner, and fills the cavity through the **edge gate** at the parting line.
- After cooling, the mould opens, the part (with attached runner) is retained on the B-side by adhesion, and is pushed out by the ejector pins.
- The runner and part are degated manually or by a robot.

**Advantages (any 2 — 0.75 each):**
- Lower tooling cost
- Faster cycle time
- Simpler construction and easier maintenance
- Higher cavity-fill efficiency for large single parts

**Limitations (any 2 — 0.75 each):**
- Runner must be removed manually/automatically
- Edge gate leaves a visible gate mark
- Difficult to achieve flow balance in multi-cavity moulds
- Restricted to a single parting line geometry

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Mould cavity dimensions** [1.5 marks]

$$
L_m = L_p \cdot (1 + S) = 150 \times 1.018 = 152.70 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
W_m = 100 \times 1.018 = 101.80 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T_m = 5 \times 1.018 = 5.09 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Number of cavities** [2.5 marks]

Area of one cavity:

$$
A_{cavity} = 150 \times 100 = 15{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.015 \text{ m}^2
$$

Available clamping force with safety:

$$
F_{clamp,use} = \frac{1200}{1.25} = 960 \text{ kN} = 960{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

Maximum permissible projected area:

$$
A_{max} = \frac{F_{clamp,use}}{P_{cav}} = \frac{960{,}000}{30 \times 10^6} = 0.032 \text{ m}^2 = 32{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Maximum number of cavities:

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{A_{max}}{A_{cavity}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{32{,}000}{15{,}000} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 2.13 \right\rfloor = 2
$$

**Result:** A maximum of **2 cavities** can be moulded on this machine.

**Step 3: Cooling water mass flow rate** [3 marks]

Heat to be removed per shot:

$$
Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

The part must be cooled from melt temperature to ejection temperature. For PP, $T_m \approx 220°C$ and $T_e \approx 80°C$, so $\Delta T = 140°C$.

$$
Q = 0.075 \times 2.0 \times 140 = 21 \text{ kJ per part}
$$

For 2 cavities, $Q_{total} = 42$ kJ per shot.

The cooling water absorbs this heat:

$$
Q = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_w
$$

Where $c_{p,w} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\Delta T_w = 35 - 25 = 10$ K.

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{Q}{\Delta t \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_w}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{42}{20 \times 4.18 \times 10}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{42}{836} = 0.0502 \text{ kg/s} = 3.01 \text{ kg/min}
$$

**Result:** Cooling water flow rate of approximately **3 kg/min** is required.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for this Topic:**
> 1. **Failure to mention the safety factor (1.2 to 1.5)** on the calculated clamping force — costs **1 mark** in every numerical on clamping tonnage.
> 2. **Confusing the cold slug well with the sprue break** — the cold slug well is a recess that traps the first cold polymer; the sprue break is a deliberate weak point. Examiners look for this distinction.
> 3. **Drawing the runner in the wrong plate** — in a three-plate mould, the **runner is in the stripper plate**, not the cavity plate. Mixing this up loses **1–2 marks** in sketches.
> 4. **Forgetting draft angles** in a labelled sketch — at least one draft angle must be shown on the core and one on the cavity; missing this loses **0.5 mark**.
> 5. **Unit conversion errors** — when using $A \cdot P$ in MPa, area must be in **m²** (not mm²). Forgetting this $10^6$ factor gives an answer that is off by **six orders of magnitude**. This is a **repeat KTU error** flagged by the board.
> 6. **Not stating gate type** in the description — a two-plate mould implies an **edge gate**, and a three-plate mould implies a **pin gate**. Omitting this loses **0.5 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Two and Three Plate Mould Design:**

- [ ] A **two-plate mould** has **one parting line**; a **three-plate mould** has **two parting lines** (cavity–stripper and stripper–core).
- [ ] **Edge gate** → two-plate mould; **Pin gate** → three-plate mould.
- [ ] **Two-plate** advantages: lower cost, faster cycle, simpler construction.
- [ ] **Three-plate** advantages: automatic runner separation, smaller gate mark, central gating for multi-cavity.
- [ ] **Sprue break** is a deliberate weak point at the sprue–runner junction in a two-plate mould.
- [ ] **Sprue puller** (Z-pin or hook type) retains the sprue on the B-side in a two-plate mould.
- [ ] **Runner puller pins** in a three-plate mould retain the runner on the stripper plate during the first stage of opening.
- [ ] **Shrinkage formula:** $L_m = L_p (1 + S)$ — used to oversize the cavity.
- [ ] **Clamping force:** $F_{clamp} = A_{proj} \cdot P_{cav} \times$ **safety factor (1.2–1.5)**.
- [ ] **Runner diameter (empirical):** $D = K \cdot \sqrt[4]{V_{cavity}}$, with $K = 0.6$ to $0.8$.
- [ ] **Minimum runner L/D** ratio: **2.5** for cold runners.
- [ ] **Pin gate diameter:** **0.8 mm to 1.5 mm** standard; **land length** = 0.8 to 1.2 mm.
- [ ] **Edge gate dimensions:** width **1 to 3 mm**, thickness **0.8 to 1.5 mm**, land length **1 to 1.5 mm**.
- [ ] **Draft angle:** **0.5° to 2°** standard; **3°+** for deep-draw features.
- [ ] **Three-plate moulds cost 30–50 % more** than equivalent two-plate moulds.
- [ ] **Three-plate cycle** is **~10–15 % longer** due to two-stage opening.
- [ ] **Selection rule:** Use three-plate when central/hidden gating is required or for multi-cavity balance; use two-plate for simple, cost-sensitive single-cavity parts.
- [ ] **Always apply safety factor** in clamping tonnage, runner sizing, and ejection force calculations.
- [ ] **Cooling time** is typically the largest portion of the cycle — design efficient waterlines.
- [ ] **Ejection force** must overcome both friction and adhesion; use multiple ejector pins to keep pin stress below **80–100 MPa**.
- [ ] **Unit discipline:** Pressure in **MPa** requires area in **m²** when calculating force in Newtons.
- [ ] **Standard mould steels:** P20 (cavity/core, 30–35 HRC); H13 (runner/hot-work, 48–52 HRC).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Mould Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Standard Mould Plates # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Standard Mould Plates** are pre-engineered, dimensionally standardized steel plates that form the structural skeleton (mould base) of an injection mould. They provide the mounting surfaces for cavities, cores, ejector systems, cooling channels, and guide elements, while also serving as the load-bearing interface between the mould and the clamping platens of the injection moulding machine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPET602 - Module 4):** > *Standard mould plates are precision-machined steel components manufactured to internationally recognized dimensional standards (HASCO, DME, MISUMI, LKM, SPI) which serve as the foundational framework of an injection mould, providing standardized mounting locations for cavity inserts, core inserts, ejector pins, cooling lines, and guiding systems.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Imagine you are building a **giant sandwich press** for shaping molten plastic into a toy car. The two halves of the press must: - Hold the **shape (cavity and core)** in exact position, - Resist **enormous clamping forces** (often 50–200 tonnes), - Allow **easy removal** of the finished part, - Be **mass-producible** so the same press can be rebuilt quickly for a different toy. To achieve this, engineers do not start from raw steel blocks. Instead, they use **pre-made "bread slices"** of exact thickness, width, and with pre-drilled holes for guide pillars, ejector pins, screws, and water lines. These standardized slices are the **Standard Mould Plates**. Just like a child uses standardized LEGO bricks to build many toys, a tool designer uses standardized plates to assemble many different moulds — quickly, economically, and reliably. ## 1.3 Role of Standardization in Tool Design - **Interchangeability** – Components from different suppliers (HASCO, DME) can be fitted together. - **Reduced Lead Time** – Plate delivery is 1–3 weeks vs. 6–10 weeks for custom-built bases. - **Cost Reduction** – Bulk manufacturing of plates lowers per-mould cost. - **Predictable Strength** – Plate thickness is matched to mould size, ensuring rigidity. - **Dimensional Accuracy** – Pre-machined datum surfaces guarantee alignment within **0.02 mm** typical tolerance. ## 1.4 Classification of Standard Mould Plates Standard mould plates are classified based on **function** and **position** in the mould: | Plate Category | Position in Mould | Primary Function | |----------------|-------------------|------------------| | Cavity Plate (A-Plate) | Stationary half, mould face | Holds cavity inserts; forms the outer shape | | Core Plate (B-Plate) | Moving half, mould face | Holds core inserts; forms the inner shape | | Support Plate | Behind Core Plate | Backs the core plate against injection pressure | | Clamping Plate (Top) | Stationary side, outermost | Bolts the mould to the stationary platen | | Clamping Plate (Bottom) | Moving side, outermost | Bolts the mould to the moving platen | | Spacer Block | Between Bottom Clamp & Ejector Plate | Creates clearance for ejector mechanism | | Ejector Retainer Plate | Moving half, mid-level | Holds ejector pins and sleeves | | Ejector Back Plate (Stripper) | Moving half, behind ejector retainer | Transfers knock-out force from machine to pins | | Stripper Plate | Three-plate mould only | Strips the runner system from cavity side | > [!NOTE] > **Common Industry Designation:** > The convention **A-plate = Cavity Plate** and **B-plate = Core Plate** is universally followed in HASCO and DME standards. The letters refer to *fixed side* and *moving side* respectively. ## 1.5 Standardization Bodies - **HASCO** (Germany) – Most widely adopted global standard; alphanumeric coding (e.g., Z 10 = clamping plate). - **DME** (USA) – Numeric coding; popular in North American mould shops. - **MISUMI** (Japan) – Modular mould base with catalogue-based selection. - **LKM** (China) – Cost-effective alternative widely used in Asian markets. - **SPI** (Society of Plastics Industry, USA) – Standards for mould identification and types (I, II, III, IV). ## 1.6 Plate Materials (Standard Steels) | Material | Hardness | Application | |----------|----------|-------------| | **P20 (DIN 1.2311)** | 30–32 HRC (pre-hardened) | Cavity & Core plates for general plastics | | **S50C / S55C** | Annealed (≤ 220 HB) | Clamping & Spacer plates (machinable) | | **1.2738 (P20+Ni)** | 30–34 HRC | Large cavity plates (improved polishability) | | **H13 (DIN 1.2344)** | 48–52 HRC (hardened) | Inserts for abrasive/filled polymers | | **S7 / S136 (Stainless)** | 28–32 HRC | Corrosion-resistant applications (PVC, medical) | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** For cavity and core plates, always mention **P20** unless the application specifically demands stainless (PVC, medical) or hardened (abrasive-filled) grades. P20 is the "default" in 90% of academic problems. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Logic of Plate Arrangement (Step-by-Step) A typical **two-plate injection mould** is assembled in this top-to-bottom order when the mould opens: **Stationary Half (Fixed Side):** 1. **Top Clamping Plate** → bolted to stationary platen of IMM. 2. **Cavity Plate (A-Plate)** → bolted to Top Clamping Plate; houses cavity inserts. 3. *(Sprue bushing, locating ring, guide pillar bushings mounted here)* **Moving Half (Moving Side):** 4. **Core Plate (B-Plate)** → houses core inserts; mates with Cavity Plate to form cavity. 5. **Support Plate** → bolted behind Core Plate; prevents deflection of core. 6. **Spacer Block** → bolted to Support Plate; creates cavity for ejector travel. 7. **Ejector Retainer Plate** → houses ejector pins, sleeves, date inserts. 8. **Ejector Back Plate** → pushes ejector pins forward during ejection. 9. **Bottom Clamping Plate** → bolted to moving platen of IMM. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Logical Flow of Force Transfer:** > *Injection Pressure → Cavity Plate → Core Plate → Support Plate → Spacer Block → Bottom Clamping Plate → Moving Platen of IMM* ## 2.2 Design Considerations for Plate Selection ### 2.2.1 Plate Width and Length (Mould Footprint) Standard mould base sizes (Width × Length) follow modular dimensions: $$ W \times L \in \{150 \times 150,\ 200 \times 200,\ 250 \times 250,\ 300 \times 300,\ 350 \times 350,\ 400 \times 400,\ 450 \times 450,\ 500 \times 500,\ 600 \times 600,\ 700 \times 700\} \text{ mm} $$ **Selection Rule:** Footprint ≥ Projected cavity area + runner area + 50–100 mm clearance on all four sides for guide pillars, bolts, and waterlines. ### 2.2.2 Plate Thickness Standards Plate thicknesses are standardized to a **modular series** (HASCO): $$ t_{\text{plate}} \in \{15,\ 20,\ 25,\ 30,\ 35,\ 40,\ 50,\ 60,\ 70,\ 80,\ 100,\ 120,\ 150\} \text{ mm} $$ **Rule-of-Thumb Thickness Selection:** | Plate Type | Thickness Guideline | |------------|--------------------| | Cavity Plate | $\geq 0.6 \times \sqrt{\text{Part Width (mm)}}$ mm (min 20 mm) | | Core Plate | $\geq 0.6 \times \sqrt{\text{Part Depth (mm)}}$ mm (min 20 mm) | | Support Plate | $\geq 0.5 \times$ Core Plate thickness (min 15 mm) | | Clamping Plates | $\geq 0.3 \times$ Mould length (min 15 mm) | | Spacer Block | = Ejector stroke + 10 mm clearance | ### 2.2.3 Plate Rigidity Check (Deflection Criterion) The maximum allowable deflection of cavity/support plate under injection pressure: $$ \delta_{\max} \leq 0.05 \text{ mm} $$ The deflection of a rectangular plate under uniform load (Timoshenko plate theory simplified): $$ \delta = \frac{k \cdot p \cdot a^{4}}{E \cdot t^{3}} $$ Where: - $k$ = geometry factor (≈ 0.111 for simply supported square plate with UDL), - $p$ = injection pressure (typically 70 MPa), - $a$ = unsupported span (mm), - $E$ = Young's modulus of plate steel = **2.1 × 10⁵ MPa**, - $t$ = plate thickness (mm). **Design Implication:** If $t$ doubles, deflection reduces by **8 times** → thickness is the most powerful rigidity parameter. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application | |---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Projected cavity area | $A_p = L_p \times W_p$ | mm² | Cavity plate sizing | | 2 | Clamping force | $F_c = p_{\text{inj}} \times A_p \times n_c$ | N | Total tonnage check | | 3 | Plate deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{k \cdot p \cdot a^{4}}{E \cdot t^{3}}$ | mm | Rigidity check | | 4 | Cavity plate thickness (rule) | $t_c \geq 0.6 \sqrt{W_p}$ | mm | Quick selection | | 5 | Support plate thickness (rule) | $t_s \geq 0.5 \, t_c$ | mm | Backing check | | 6 | Spacer block height | $h_{sb} = s_e + 10$ | mm | Ejector travel + clearance | | 7 | Number of bolts per plate | $n_b = 4 + 2 \cdot \left\lceil \dfrac{L-200}{200} \right\rceil$ | — | Bolting pattern | | 8 | Bolt diameter (rule) | $d_b \geq 0.04 \times L$ | mm | Bolt sizing | | 9 | Plate hardness range | $28$–$32$ HRC (P20) | HRC | Material selection | | 10 | Standard size modulus | 50 mm series | mm | Plate dimensional increments | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern Alert:** In Part B 14-mark questions on mould design, the examiner typically tests **(i) plate identification by function** and **(ii) thickness calculation using rule-of-thumb** — be ready with both the drawing and the calculation. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Mould Base Quotations:** Every mould maker specifies a mould base code (e.g., HASCO **CI 2730 200×250**). Knowing plate standards allows rapid quotation reading. - **Modification & Repair:** Standard plates can be replaced individually, reducing downtime. - **Strength Analysis:** FEA packages (Moldflow, Moldex3D) require accurate plate geometry, materials, and thicknesses — standards ensure data consistency. - **Global Supply Chain:** A mould designed in India using HASCO standards can be assembled in Germany or Japan with locally sourced plates — true international interoperability. ## 2.5 Why Standard Plates Are Not "Cheap Shortcuts" - **Machining Savings** – Plate datum surfaces are pre-ground to ±0.02 mm. Custom grinding this precision would cost more than the plate itself. - **Engineering Time** – Guide pillar bores, sprue bushing holes, ejector pin holes are pre-machined. The mould designer adds only the **cavity-specific** features. - **Quality Assurance** – Plate manufacturers (HASCO, DME) perform stress-relief and hardness certification; tool rooms inherit this QA. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for KTU Answer Writing:** > Always close a "list the plates" answer with a sentence on **why** they are standard — the examiner rewards the *engineering rationale*, not just enumeration. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivations Related to Standard Mould Plates ### 3.1.1 Plate Deflection Under Injection Pressure — Full Derivation **Problem Statement:** A cavity plate of unsupported span $a = 200$ mm carries an injection pressure of $p = 70$ MPa uniformly distributed over a square central region. Determine the required plate thickness to keep deflection under 0.05 mm, assuming simply supported boundary conditions and P20 steel. **Step 1: Identify Material Property** For P20 steel: $$E = 2.1 \times 10^{5} \text{ MPa} = 2.1 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 2: Apply the Plate Deflection Formula** For a simply supported square plate under uniform pressure: $$\delta = \frac{k \cdot p \cdot a^{4}}{E \cdot t^{3}}$$ For UDL on a simply supported square plate, the plate constant: $$k = 0.0444$$ **Step 3: Substitute the Deflection Limit** Set $\delta = 0.05$ mm and solve for $t$: $$0.05 = \frac{0.0444 \times 70 \times (200)^{4}}{2.1 \times 10^{5} \times t^{3}}$$ **Step 4: Compute the Numerator** $$(200)^{4} = 1.6 \times 10^{9} \text{ mm}^{4}$$ $$0.0444 \times 70 \times 1.6 \times 10^{9} = 4.9728 \times 10^{9}$$ **Step 5: Rearrange for $t^3$** $$t^{3} = \frac{4.9728 \times 10^{9}}{0.05 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{4.9728 \times 10^{9}}{1.05 \times 10^{4}}$$ $$t^{3} = 4.74 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}$$ **Step 6: Take the Cube Root** $$t = (4.74 \times 10^{5})^{1/3} \approx 78.0 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 7: Select Standard Plate Thickness** From the standard thickness series, choose the next higher value: $$t_{\text{standard}} = 80 \text{ mm}$$ **Conclusion:** The cavity plate must be at least **80 mm thick** to limit deflection to 0.05 mm. --- ### 3.1.2 Clamping Force Estimation (Total Mould Holding Force) **Problem Statement:** A mould has a projected cavity area of 200 cm² with single cavity ($n_c = 1$). Calculate the required clamping force if the injection pressure is 70 MPa. **Step 1: Write the Clamping Force Equation** $$F_c = p_{\text{inj}} \times A_p \times n_c$$ **Step 2: Convert Area to Consistent Units** $$A_p = 200 \text{ cm}^{2} = 200 \times 10^{2} \text{ mm}^{2} = 20{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$ **Step 3: Substitute Values** $$F_c = 70 \times 20{,}000 \times 1 = 1{,}400{,}000 \text{ N} = 1400 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4: Convert to Tonnes (KTU unit preference)** $$F_c = \frac{1400}{9.81} \approx 142.7 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Conclusion:** The mould needs at least a **150-tonne** injection moulding machine. --- ### 3.1.3 Support Plate Thickness Derivation **Step 1: Rule-of-Thumb Relationship** For a standard mould: $$t_{\text{support}} = 0.5 \times t_{\text{cavity}}$$ **Step 2: Apply to the Previous Example** $$t_{\text{support}} = 0.5 \times 80 = 40 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3: Select Standard Thickness** $$t_{\text{support, standard}} = 40 \text{ mm}$$ ✓ (exactly matches standard) --- ### 3.1.4 Spacer Block Height Calculation **Given:** Ejector stroke $s_e = 25$ mm (to clear the part from the cavity). **Step 1: Apply the Spacer Block Formula** $$h_{sb} = s_e + 10 \text{ mm (clearance)}$$ $$h_{sb} = 25 + 10 = 35 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Select Standard** Standard thickness series: 35 mm ✓ (exact match available) --- ### 3.1.5 Bolting Pattern for Clamping Plate **Given:** Mould footprint 400 × 400 mm. **Step 1: Bolt Diameter Rule** $$d_b = 0.04 \times L = 0.04 \times 400 = 16 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Number of Bolts** $$n_b = 4 + 2 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{L-200}{200} \right\rceil = 4 + 2 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{400-200}{200} \right\rceil = 4 + 2 \cdot 1 = 6 \text{ bolts}$$ **Step 3: Arrangement** 4 corner bolts + 2 mid-side bolts (one on each of two opposite sides). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Plate Goes Where? | Decision Criterion | Cavity Plate | Core Plate | Support Plate | Clamping Plate | Spacer Block | Ejector Plates | |-------------------|--------------|------------|---------------|----------------|--------------|----------------| | Mould face? | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | Holds inserts? | ✓ (Cavity) | ✓ (Core) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (Ejector pins) | | High hardness required? | ✓ (P20 / 1.2738) | ✓ (P20) | Moderate (S50C) | Low (S50C) | Low (S50C) | Moderate (S50C + H/W pins) | | In contact with plastic? | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | Bolted to platen? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (Top & Bottom) | ✗ | ✗ | | Requires ejector travel clearance? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ## 3.3 Standard Mould Base Nomenclature (HASCO System) HASCO uses a **letter + number** coding system for plates: | HASCO Code | Component | Function | |------------|-----------|----------| | Z 10 | Top Clamping Plate | Bolts to stationary platen | | Z 20 | Cavity Plate (A-Plate) | Holds cavity inserts | | Z 30 | Support Plate | Backs core plate | | Z 40 | Core Plate (B-Plate) | Holds core inserts | | Z 50 | Spacer Block | Ejector clearance | | Z 60 | Ejector Retainer Plate | Holds ejector pins | | Z 70 | Ejector Back Plate | Knock-out force transfer | | Z 80 | Bottom Clamping Plate | Bolts to moving platen | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks the student to *"label a mould assembly drawing"* — memorizing the HASCO Z-codes speeds up the answer. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Plate Mould The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **logical position, function, and force-transfer** between every standard mould plate in a typical two-plate injection mould. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FIXED["STATIONARY HALF (Fixed to Machine Stationary Platen)"] A1["PLATE_Z10_TOP_CLAMP

Both plates must transfer this force to their respective clamping plates. The ejector system sits between the support plate and the bottom clamping plate, held in place by the spacer block. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define Standard Mould Plates. List any four types of standard mould plates used in injection moulding.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Standard mould plates are pre-engineered, dimensionally standardized steel plates manufactured to specifications set by bodies like HASCO, DME, or MISUMI. They form the structural framework of an injection mould and provide mounting positions for cavity inserts, core inserts, ejector pins, and cooling circuits. **Four Types:** 1. **Cavity Plate (A-Plate / Z20)** – Holds cavity inserts on the stationary side. 2. **Core Plate (B-Plate / Z40)** – Holds core inserts on the moving side. 3. **Support Plate (Z30)** – Backs the core plate against injection pressure. 4. **Clamping Plate (Z10 / Z80)** – Bolts the mould halves to the machine platens. *(Additional types acceptable: Spacer Block, Ejector Retainer Plate, Ejector Back Plate, Stripper Plate.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why is P20 steel commonly used for cavity and core plates in standard mould bases?** **Model Answer:** P20 (DIN 1.2311) is a **pre-hardened tool steel (30–32 HRC)** widely used for cavity and core plates because: 1. **Supplied pre-hardened** – Eliminates post-machining heat treatment, reducing distortion and lead time. 2. **Good machinability** in the pre-hardened condition – Allows cavity machining without difficulty. 3. **Adequate wear resistance** for general-purpose polymers (PP, PE, ABS). 4. **Excellent polishability** – Can be mirror-finished for transparent parts. 5. **Good dimensional stability** – Maintains ±0.02 mm tolerance under cyclic thermal loading. 6. **Cost-effective** – Cheaper than stainless (S136) and easier to machine than fully hardened (H13). --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. You may attempt **either** Choice A **or** Choice B. Both choices are independent and from the same module. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **A. (a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **standard mould plates used in a two-plate injection mould**. State the function of each plate and identify the material commonly used for it. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Sketch (Schematic, to be drawn by student):** *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.4 for the structural layout. In a written exam, the student should draw a vertical cross-section showing plates in the following top-to-bottom order:)* | Position | Plate | Function | Material | |----------|-------|----------|----------| | 1 | Top Clamping Plate (Z10) | Bolts the stationary half to the fixed platen | S50C (Annealed) | | 2 | Cavity Plate (Z20) | Houses cavity inserts; forms outer part geometry | P20 (Pre-hardened) | | 3 | Core Plate (Z40) | Houses core inserts; forms inner part geometry | P20 (Pre-hardened) | | 4 | Support Plate (Z30) | Backs the core plate against injection pressure | S50C | | 5 | Spacer Block (Z50) | Creates clearance for ejector travel | S50C | | 6 | Ejector Retainer Plate (Z60) | Holds ejector pins and return pins | S50C | | 7 | Ejector Back Plate (Z70) | Transfers knock-out force from machine | S50C | | 8 | Bottom Clamping Plate (Z80) | Bolts the moving half to the moving platen | S50C | **Key Explanation Points (for full marks):** - The cavity and core plates mate to form the **mould cavity** (parting line lies between them). - Injection pressure (typically 70 MPa) pushes the plates apart; clamping force from the IMM resists this. - Guide pillars (in Z20) and bushings (in Z40) ensure plate alignment within ±0.02 mm. - The ejector system pushes the solidified part out of the core after mould opening. **A. (b)** A 250 × 250 mm cavity plate carries an injection pressure of 70 MPa on a 150 × 150 mm unsupported square area. The plate is made of P20 steel ($E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa) with simply supported edges. Calculate the minimum plate thickness to keep deflection under 0.05 mm. Use the plate constant $k = 0.0444$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Unsupported span: $a = 150$ mm - Injection pressure: $p = 70$ MPa - Young's modulus: $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa - Max allowable deflection: $\delta = 0.05$ mm - Plate constant: $k = 0.0444$ **Step 1: Apply the Plate Deflection Formula** $$\delta = \frac{k \cdot p \cdot a^{4}}{E \cdot t^{3}}$$ **Step 2: Rearrange to Solve for $t$** $$t^{3} = \frac{k \cdot p \cdot a^{4}}{E \cdot \delta}$$ **Step 3: Compute the Numerator** $$a^{4} = (150)^{4} = 5.0625 \times 10^{8} \text{ mm}^{4}$$ $$k \cdot p \cdot a^{4} = 0.0444 \times 70 \times 5.0625 \times 10^{8} = 1.5734 \times 10^{9}$$ **Step 4: Compute the Denominator** $$E \cdot \delta = 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 0.05 = 1.05 \times 10^{4}$$ **Step 5: Divide to Get $t^3$** $$t^{3} = \frac{1.5734 \times 10^{9}}{1.05 \times 10^{4}} = 1.4985 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}$$ **Step 6: Take the Cube Root** $$t = (1.4985 \times 10^{5})^{1/3} \approx 53.07 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 7: Select Standard Plate Thickness** From the HASCO standard series, the next higher standard thickness is: $$\boxed{t_{\text{standard}} = 60 \text{ mm}}$$ **Verification:** $$\delta_{\text{actual}} = \frac{0.0444 \times 70 \times (150)^{4}}{2.1 \times 10^{5} \times (60)^{3}} = \frac{1.5734 \times 10^{9}}{4.536 \times 10^{10}} \approx 0.0347 \text{ mm}$$ This is well below the 0.05 mm limit ✓. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **B. (a)** List the **standardization bodies** for injection mould plates. Explain the **HASCO coding system** for the major plates and justify why P20 steel is preferred over H13 for general-purpose moulds. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Standardization Bodies:** 1. **HASCO** (Germany) – Most widely adopted. 2. **DME** (USA) – North American standard. 3. **MISUMI** (Japan) – Modular catalogue-based. 4. **LKM** (China) – Cost-effective. 5. **SPI** (USA) – Mould classification. **HASCO Coding System (Key Plates):** | Code | Plate | |------|-------| | Z 10 | Top Clamping Plate | | Z 20 | Cavity Plate (A-Plate) | | Z 30 | Support Plate | | Z 40 | Core Plate (B-Plate) | | Z 50 | Spacer Block | | Z 60 | Ejector Retainer Plate | | Z 70 | Ejector Back Plate | | Z 80 | Bottom Clamping Plate | The 'Z' prefix denotes a plate component; the two-digit number denotes its position in the assembly from top (stationary) to bottom (moving side). **Justification – P20 vs. H13:** | Property | P20 | H13 | |----------|-----|-----| | Supplied hardness | 30–32 HRC (pre-hardened) | Annealed (~200 HB), requires hardening | | Machinability | Excellent in pre-hardened state | Difficult until hardened | | Wear resistance | Adequate for unfilled polymers | Superior for abrasive/glass-filled polymers | | Distortion after heat treatment | None (pre-hardened) | High risk if not stress-relieved properly | | Cost | Moderate | Higher | | Lead time | Shorter | Longer (heat treatment cycle) | | Polishability | Very good | Good | | Application | 90% of general moulds | Hot-work, die-casting, abrasive-filled moulds | **Conclusion:** For general-purpose moulds producing non-abrasive parts, **P20 is preferred** because it eliminates the heat-treatment step (saving cost and time), provides adequate hardness, and offers excellent machinability and polishability. **B. (b)** For a mould with projected cavity area 250 cm², single cavity, and injection pressure 80 MPa, calculate: **(i)** total clamping force required, and **(ii)** standard support plate thickness if the cavity plate is 70 mm. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Clamping Force Calculation:** **Given:** - Projected area: $A_p = 250$ cm² = 25,000 mm² - Number of cavities: $n_c = 1$ - Injection pressure: $p_{\text{inj}} = 80$ MPa **Step 1: Apply Clamping Force Formula** $$F_c = p_{\text{inj}} \times A_p \times n_c$$ $$F_c = 80 \times 25{,}000 \times 1$$ $$F_c = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ N} = 2000 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Convert to Tonnes** $$F_c = \frac{2000}{9.81} \approx 203.9 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 3: Select Standard IMM Tonnage (next higher standard)** $$\boxed{F_{c,\text{standard}} = 220 \text{ tonnes}}$$ **(ii) Support Plate Thickness:** **Given:** Cavity plate thickness $t_c = 70$ mm. **Step 1: Apply Rule-of-Thumb** $$t_s = 0.5 \times t_c = 0.5 \times 70 = 35 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Select Standard** From the HASCO standard series: $$\boxed{t_{s,\text{standard}} = 35 \text{ mm (exact match)}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing Cavity Plate with Core Plate** – A frequent error is to label the stationary mould face as the "core plate." Always remember: **A-Plate (Stationary) = Cavity**; **B-Plate (Moving) = Core**. > > 2. **Forgetting the Spacer Block** – In drawings, students often show the ejector plate touching the support plate, leaving no room for ejector travel. The spacer block is *essential* — its height must equal the ejector stroke + 10 mm clearance. > > 3. **Using P20 for All Plates** – P20 is expensive and unnecessary for clamping/spacer plates. Use **S50C (annealed, machinable)** for non-critical plates — examiners specifically look for this distinction. > > 4. **Ignoring Unit Conversion in Plate Deflection Problems** – Mixing cm and mm in the formula gives a wrong answer by a factor of 10⁴. Always keep units in **mm and N/mm² (MPa)**. > > 5. **Not Selecting the *Next Higher Standard* Thickness** – Calculated thicknesses like 53.07 mm or 78 mm are not standard; the answer must be the next standard value (60 mm, 80 mm respectively). > > 6. **Skipping the Mould Footprint in Cavity Plate Sizing** – Plate width/length must be ≥ projected area + 100 mm clearance. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this is omitted. > > 7. **Not Mentioning HASCO / DME Standards** – A drawing without reference to any standard is considered incomplete in KTU evaluation. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Final 5-Minute Revision Bullets — Print This Section Before the Exam:** - ☐ **Standard mould plates** = pre-engineered, modular plates forming the mould base. - ☐ **A-Plate (Cavity) is Stationary; B-Plate (Core) is Moving** — memorize this firmly. - ☐ **HASCO Codes:** Z10 = Top Clamp, Z20 = Cavity, Z30 = Support, Z40 = Core, Z50 = Spacer, Z60 = Ejector Retainer, Z70 = Ejector Back, Z80 = Bottom Clamp. - ☐ **Material for Cavity/Core = P20 (pre-hardened, 30–32 HRC).** Other plates = S50C. - ☐ **Plate deflection formula:** $\delta = \dfrac{k \cdot p \cdot a^{4}}{E \cdot t^{3}}$ — keep all units in mm and MPa. - ☐ **Cavity plate thickness rule:** $t_c \geq 0.6 \sqrt{W_p}$ mm (min 20 mm). - ☐ **Support plate thickness:** $t_s = 0.5 \times t_c$. - ☐ **Spacer block height:** $h_{sb} = s_e + 10$ mm. - ☐ **Clamping force:** $F_c = p_{\text{inj}} \times A_p \times n_c$ (always in N, then convert to tonnes by dividing by 9.81). - ☐ **Standard plate thickness series:** 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100, 120, 150 mm. - ☐ **Standard plate footprints:** 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 600, 700 mm (square). - ☐ **Young's modulus for mould steel:** $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa. - ☐ **Allowable deflection:** $\delta \leq 0.05$ mm. - ☐ **Always use next-higher standard thickness** — never quote a calculated non-standard value. - ☐ **Spacer block is mandatory** — do not forget to provide ejector travel clearance. - ☐ **Standardization bodies to remember:** HASCO, DME, MISUMI, LKM, SPI. - ☐ **Plate deflection scales with $t^3$** — doubling thickness reduces deflection by 8×. - ☐ **Injection pressure assumption (default):** $p_{\text{inj}} = 70$ MPa unless specified otherwise. --- **END OF NOTES — PCPET602 / MODULE 4 / STANDARD MOULD PLATES** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Outcome-Based Education Framework.*

topics.title: Parting Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parting Line

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of forging die design, the **Parting Line** (also referred to as the **Die Line** or **Parting Plane**) is defined as the locus of intersection between the mating surfaces of the two die halves (the upper die/shoe and the lower die/gag) that physically separate to release the forged component.

Mathematically, it is a planar or curvilinear trace (typically defined in the $xy$-plane of the die layout) given by:

$$z = f(x, y) \quad \text{where} \quad z = 0 \text{ at the die interface}$$

The parting line is the line along which the **forging flash** predominantly forms, and it is also the reference trace used for designing the **flash gutter** and **flash land** geometries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCPET602 - Module 4):** The parting line is a foundational concept in forging die design, directly influencing die life, flash generation, material flow, and the subsequent trimming operation. Its selection is one of the most critical decisions in forging design.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Cookie Cutter Model

Imagine you are using a **two-piece cookie mold** to cut a shaped biscuit from dough:
- The top half of the mold and the bottom half meet at a specific line around the silhouette of the cookie.
- That meeting line is the **parting line** of the mold.
- The excess dough that squeezes out between the two halves is the **flash** in a forging operation.

If you placed the meeting line at the wrong height (e.g., through the middle of a star instead of along its base), the star's points would be ambiguous, and the cutter would struggle. **The same problem exists in forging**: a poorly chosen parting line causes uneven die filling, excessive flash, and poor grain flow.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics (as per industrial forging practice) govern parting line design:

- **Maximum Flash Thickness ($t_f$):** Typically between **1.0 mm and 5.0 mm**, depending on forging size.
- **Flash-to-Billet Volume Ratio:** Should generally remain between **20% and 50%** of the total billet volume for proper die filling.
- **Standard Draft Angle ($\alpha$):** **3° to 7°** for external surfaces (mandatory for part removal).
- **Die Closure Tolerance:** Typical mismatch allowance of **0.2 mm to 0.8 mm** per side.

> [!NOTE]
> These numerical values are **empirical industry standards** widely cited in forging handbooks and are treated as design constraints in KTU problem-solving.

---

## 1.4 Categorical Classification of Parting Lines

Parting lines are broadly classified into four geometric categories:

| Classification Type | Geometric Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight (Horizontal)** | Lies in a single horizontal plane $z = 0$ | Symmetric disk-like forgings (gears, flanges) |
| **Straight (Vertical)** | Vertical plane, $x = c$ | Asymmetric flat components |
| **Irregular (Curved)** | Follows the contour of the forging profile | Complex non-symmetric forgings (crankshafts) |
| **Multiple/Lock-Type** | Two or more planes with interlocking geometry | Multi-cavity progressive dies |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How the Parting Line Works

The parting line is not merely a geometric trace; it is a **functional boundary** that governs the entire forging process. The following structured logic describes its operational role:

### Step-by-Step Logic of Parting Line Function:

- **Step 1 — Die Closure Initialization:** The upper die descends toward the lower die along the vertical axis. Material (billet) is placed in the lower die cavity.
- **Step 2 — Flash Genesis Zone:** As the dies close, the squeeze-out region forms at the **land** (a narrow bridge region adjacent to the parting line). The flash begins to extrude outward radially.
- **Step 3 — Material Flow Regulation:** The geometry of the parting line dictates which direction the excess material flows. An improper line causes **folds, laps, or underfilled** regions.
- **Step 4 — Flash Solidification Front:** The thin flash cools rapidly (due to high surface-area-to-volume ratio), creating a resistance that **forces plastic flow into unfilled die cavities** — this is the primary "pressure intensifier" effect.
- **Step 5 — Final Forcing & Die Lock:** Final closure forms the **flash gutter** around the parting line, which retains the excess material permanently.
- **Step 6 — Ejection & Trim Reference:** The forged part is ejected along the parting line plane, and the parting line trace becomes the **trimming reference axis** for the trim die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Flash Necessity:** Without flash at the parting line, the pressure inside the die cavity could not rise sufficiently to fill the intricate cavities. Thus, the parting line is intrinsically tied to the **pressure-rise mechanism** in closed-die forging.

## 2.2 Factors Influencing Parting Line Selection

The following structured list enumerates the **KTU-high-yield factors** that dictate parting line placement:

- **Symmetry of the Forging:** Prefer the parting line through the **largest cross-section** and the axis of symmetry.
- **Draft Direction:** Both halves of the forging must have adequate draft to be ejected without damage.
- **Grain Flow Continuity:** The parting line should be positioned so that the **grain flow lines** follow the contour of the part (important for fatigue strength).
- **Flash and Trimming:** Parting line location directly affects flash volume, trimming direction, and trim die complexity.
- **Die Cost & Machining:** A flat horizontal parting line is the **cheapest to machine**; irregular lines require EDM and increase die cost by 15%–40%.
- **Forging Tolerances:** Dimensions crossing the parting line are subject to **die-wear mismatch**; hence critical tolerances should not straddle the parting line.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **mathematically verifiable formulas** associated with parting line geometry and flash design:

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{flash} = W \cdot t \cdot L$ | Flash volume: $W$ = width, $t$ = thickness, $L$ = perimeter length | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| 2 | $V_{flash} = 0.25 \text{ to } 0.50 \cdot V_{billet}$ | Flash-to-billet volumetric ratio (empirical) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{cavity} \propto \dfrac{1}{t_{flash}}$ | Cavity pressure inversely proportional to flash thickness | MPa |
| 4 | $D_{mismatch} = \Delta_z \cdot \cot(\alpha)$ | Die-wear mismatch offset due to draft $\alpha$ | mm |
| 5 | $L_{flash} = \pi \cdot (D_{outer} + D_{inner})$ | Flash perimeter for an annular forging (C-shape) | mm |
| 6 | $F_{clamp} = P_{avg} \cdot A_{projected}$ | Required clamping force at die closure | kN |
| 7 | $t_{flash,opt} = k \cdot \sqrt{A_{plan}}$ | Optimal flash thickness; $k$ = empirical constant (0.015–0.025) | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, vertical bars and absolute-value notations have been intentionally avoided. For any conditional expression like $x \vert y$ in derivations, the **dividing line** is rendered using `\mid` or `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The parting line concept is applied in:

- **Automotive Forging:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, steering knuckles.
- **Aerospace Forgings:** Landing gear links, turbine disk hubs.
- **Hand Tool Manufacturing:** Wrenches, hammer heads, plier jaws.
- **Valve & Fitting Industry:** Gate valves, pipe elbows, flanges.

In all of these, the parting line determines whether the forging meets **fatigue life requirements, dimensional tolerances, and metallurgical soundness**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Flash Pressure Intensification Model

We derive the relationship between flash thickness and the resulting die-cavity pressure, treating the flash gutter as a thin extrusion channel.

### Given:
- Flash thickness: $t_f$
- Flash width (land): $w_f$
- Flash-land shear yield stress: $\tau_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}$ (von Mises criterion)
- Average die-cavity pressure: $P_c$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step (a): Force balance on the flash land} \quad F_{flash} &= P_c \cdot A_{projected} \\
\text{Step (b): Shear resistance on flash surfaces} \quad F_{resist} &= 2 \cdot \tau_y \cdot w_f \cdot L_f \\
\text{Step (c): Equilibrium at final closure} \quad P_c \cdot A_{projected} &= 2 \cdot \tau_y \cdot w_f \cdot L_f \\
\text{Step (d): Solve for } P_c \quad P_c &= \dfrac{2 \cdot \tau_y \cdot w_f \cdot L_f}{A_{projected}} \\
\text{Step (e): Use } \tau_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \quad P_c &= \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot w_f \cdot L_f}{\sqrt{3} \cdot A_{projected}} \\
\text{Step (f): Relate } w_f \text{ to } t_f \text{ (geometric constraint)} \quad w_f &= k_1 \cdot t_f \\
\text{Step (g): Substitute} \quad P_c &= \dfrac{2 \cdot k_1 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t_f \cdot L_f}{\sqrt{3} \cdot A_{projected}} \\
\text{Step (h): Invert to show inverse proportionality} \quad t_f &= \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot A_{projected} \cdot P_c}{2 \cdot k_1 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot L_f} \\
\text{Step (i): Inverse form: } P_c \propto \dfrac{1}{t_f} &\implies \boxed{P_c = \dfrac{C}{t_f}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation of Step (i):

This result confirms the empirical observation that **a thinner flash produces higher cavity pressure**, which is precisely why forging design intentionally uses a thin flash land to drive material into deep cavities. This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** frequently tested in numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Flash Volume for an Annular Disk Forging

Consider a disk-shaped forging with outer diameter $D_o$ and inner diameter $D_i$. The flash is extruded around the outer perimeter of the disk.

### Given:
- Flash thickness: $t_f$
- Flash width: $w_f$
- Outer diameter: $D_o$
- Inner diameter: $D_i$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step (a): Flash path length (perimeter)} \quad L_f &= \pi \cdot D_o \\
\text{Step (b): Flash cross-sectional area} \quad A_{f,cs} &= w_f \cdot t_f \\
\text{Step (c): Total flash volume} \quad V_f &= L_f \cdot A_{f,cs} = \pi \cdot D_o \cdot w_f \cdot t_f \\
\text{Step (d): Billet volume (cylindrical preform)} \quad V_b &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot h_b \\
\text{Step (e): Flash ratio constraint} \quad \dfrac{V_f}{V_b} &= 0.30 \text{ (assumed optimum)} \\
\text{Step (f): Equate} \quad \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_o \cdot w_f \cdot t_f}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot h_b} &= 0.30 \\
\text{Step (g): Solve for } t_f \quad t_f &= \dfrac{0.30 \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot h_b}{4 \cdot D_o \cdot w_f} \\
\text{Step (h): Substitute numerical values} \quad D_o &= 120\,\text{mm},\ D_i = 60\,\text{mm},\ h_b = 30\,\text{mm},\ w_f = 8\,\text{mm} \\
t_f &= \dfrac{0.30 \cdot (120^2 - 60^2) \cdot 30}{4 \cdot 120 \cdot 8} \\
t_f &= \dfrac{0.30 \cdot (14400 - 3600) \cdot 30}{3840} \\
t_f &= \dfrac{0.30 \cdot 10800 \cdot 30}{3840} \\
t_f &= \dfrac{97200}{3840} \\
t_f &= 25.31\,\text{mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of $t_f = 25.31$ mm is unrealistic; this indicates that the assumed parameters need re-scaling for actual industrial use. The derivation methodology, however, remains valid for **KTU numerical problems**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Die-Wear Mismatch Across Parting Line

The die-wear causes the parting line to drift over production runs. The mismatch $D_m$ depends on draft angle and die-life (number of forgings $N$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step (a): Wear rate per stroke} \quad \delta &= k_w \cdot P_c \\
\text{Step (b): Cumulative wear after N strokes} \quad \Delta z &= N \cdot \delta = N \cdot k_w \cdot P_c \\
\text{Step (c): Mismatch offset (geometric)} \quad D_m &= \Delta z \cdot \cot(\alpha) \\
\text{Step (d): Substitute} \quad D_m &= N \cdot k_w \cdot P_c \cdot \cot(\alpha) \\
\text{Step (e): With } \alpha = 5^\circ,\ P_c = 800\,\text{MPa},\ N = 5000,\ k_w = 10^{-6}\,\text{mm/cycle} \\
D_m &= 5000 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 800 \cdot \cot(5^\circ) \\
D_m &= 4 \cdot \cot(5^\circ) \\
\cot(5^\circ) &\approx 11.43 \\
D_m &\approx 45.72\,\text{mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Engineering Implication:

A mismatch of 45.72 mm across the parting line is **catastrophic** in practice; the assumed values are intentionally exaggerated. In real production, $k_w$ is much smaller ($\sim 10^{-8}$) and $N$ per die is capped at $\sim 5000$–$10000$ strokes. The derivation **structure** is what KTU evaluates.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Parting Line Selection

| Forging Geometry Feature | Recommended Parting Line Position | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Symmetric disk / gear | **Through center, horizontal** | Symmetric draft, single parting plane, low cost |
| Y-shaped / T-shaped | **Through junction of arms** | Avoids undercuts, balanced flow |
| Crankshaft | **Along main axis (irregular)** | Preserves grain flow continuity |
| Stepped shaft (multi-diameter) | **Through center of largest step** | Minimizes flash in thinner sections |
| Forging with deep blind cavity | **Closer to deep-cavity side** | Ensures cavity fills under pressure |
| Component with machined mating face | **Opposite the machined face** | Machining absorbs any mismatch |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Parting Line in Forging Cycle

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional flow** of the parting line through the entire forging operation. It maps the cause-and-effect relationships and process dependencies:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Billett Input] --> B[Heat to Forging Temperature]
    B --> C[Placement in Lower Die Cavity]
    C --> D[Upper Die Descent Begins]
    D --> E[Material Contacts Die Walls]
    E --> F[Plastic Flow Initiates]
    F --> G[Flash Extrudes at Parting Line]
    G --> H[Flash Land Resistance Builds]
    H --> I[Die Cavity Pressure Intensifies]
    I --> J[Deep Cavities Fill Completely]
    J --> K[Die Closure Complete]
    K --> L[Ejection of Forged Part]
    L --> M[Trimming Along Parting Line]
    M --> N[Final Forged Component]

    O[Parting Line Geometry] --> G
    O --> H
    O --> I
    O --> M

    P[Die Wear] --> Q[Mismatch Across Parting Line]
    Q --> M

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style N fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style O fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9900
    style P fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#cc0000
```

### Diagram Interpretation:

- **Node A → N** represents the sequential forging process.
- **Node O (Parting Line Geometry)** is the central design input that influences **flash extrusion (G), pressure intensification (I), and trimming (M)**.
- **Node P (Die Wear) → Q (Mismatch)** introduces the production-life degradation effect at the parting line.
- The **green node (N)** represents the desired output (defect-free forged component).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Parting Line Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Parting Line Classification] --> S[Straight Horizontal]
    R --> T[Straight Vertical]
    R --> U[Irregular Curved]
    R --> V[Multiple Lock Type]

    S --> S1[Symmetric Disk Forgings]
    S --> S2[Gears and Flanges]
    T --> T1[Flat Asymmetric Parts]
    T --> T2[L Shaped Components]
    U --> U1[Crankshafts]
    U --> U2[Connecting Rods]
    V --> V1[Multi Cavity Progressive Dies]
    V --> V2[Complex Three D Forgings]

    style R fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style S fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style T fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style U fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style V fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section (Mermaid Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Upper Die Shoe] --> A2[Upper Cavity Insert]
    B1[Lower Die Shoe] --> B2[Lower Cavity Insert]
    A2 -. Parting Line Trace .-> B2
    B2 --> C1[Flash Gutter]
    C1 --> C2[Flash Land]
    C2 --> C3[Flash Outer Edge]
    A1 --- M[Matched Die Closure]
    B1 --- M
    M --> D[Forged Component Outline]

    style A1 fill:#b8daff,stroke:#004085
    style B1 fill:#b8daff,stroke:#004085
    style A2 fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404
    style B2 fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404
    style M fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24
    style C1 fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724
    style C2 fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724
```

### Reading the Schematic:

- The **upper and lower die shoes (A1, B1)** carry the **cavity inserts (A2, B2)**.
- The **dashed line (A2 → B2)** represents the **parting line trace** where the two cavities meet.
- The **flash gutter, land, and outer edge (C1, C2, C3)** are features that lie **along the parting line** in real forgings.
- **Node M (Matched Die Closure)** is the moment when the upper and lower dies make contact, sealing the forging.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Parting Line. Mention any two factors that govern its selection. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A parting line is the locus of intersection between the mating surfaces of the upper and lower die halves in a closed-die forging operation. It is the plane along which the two die halves separate to eject the forged component and along which the forging flash predominantly forms.

**Two Governing Factors (1 Mark):**
- **Symmetry of the forging:** The parting line is preferred through the axis of symmetry of the component.
- **Draft requirement:** The parting line should be placed so that both halves of the forging have adequate draft (typically 3°–7°) for clean ejection.

---

### Question 2: State the function of flash gutter in forging. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A flash gutter is a recess machined around the parting line in the die. Its functions are:
- **To contain the excess material** that is squeezed out during die closure (the flash).
- **To provide a controlled resistance** at the parting line, which intensifies the die-cavity pressure and ensures complete filling of intricate cavities.
- **To act as a relief region** so that flash does not interfere with proper die closure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) regulations for Module 4 of PCPET602, the student must answer **one full 14-mark question** out of a choice of two. Each question carries sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the different types of parting lines used in forging dies. Discuss the criteria for selecting a parting line for a typical Y-shaped forging.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A disk-shaped forging of outer diameter 100 mm and inner diameter 50 mm is to be produced. The flash land width is 6 mm and the flash thickness is to be 2 mm. Calculate (i) the flash volume, and (ii) the optimum billet height if the flash-to-billet volume ratio is to be maintained at 0.30.

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Solution to (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Types of Parting Lines (4 Marks):**
1. **Straight Horizontal Parting Line:** Lies in a single horizontal plane. Used for symmetric disk-type forgings (gears, flanges). Most economical to machine.
2. **Straight Vertical Parting Line:** Vertical plane. Used for flat asymmetric components.
3. **Irregular Curved Parting Line:** Follows the contour of the forging. Used for complex non-symmetric parts like crankshafts; preserves grain flow.
4. **Multiple / Lock-Type Parting Line:** Two or more parting planes with interlocking geometry. Used in progressive multi-cavity dies.

**Criteria for Y-Shaped Forging (3 Marks):**
- The parting line should pass **through the junction of the three arms** (the Y-junction) to avoid undercuts.
- Each arm should have **adequate draft (3°–7°)** on both halves.
- The parting line should be positioned so that **grain flow follows the arm contours** for fatigue strength.
- The junction cavity should be placed in the **deeper die half** to ensure complete filling under pressure.

**Solution to (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- $D_o = 100$ mm, $D_i = 50$ mm
- $w_f = 6$ mm, $t_f = 2$ mm
- $V_f / V_b = 0.30$

**Step (i) — Flash Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_f &= \pi \cdot D_o = \pi \cdot 100 = 314.16 \text{ mm} \\
V_f &= L_f \cdot w_f \cdot t_f \\
V_f &= 314.16 \cdot 6 \cdot 2 \\
V_f &= 3769.91 \text{ mm}^3 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Optimum Billet Height:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_b &= \dfrac{V_f}{0.30} = \dfrac{3769.91}{0.30} = 12566.37 \text{ mm}^3 \\
V_b &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot h_b \\
12566.37 &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (100^2 - 50^2) \cdot h_b \\
12566.37 &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 7500 \cdot h_b \\
12566.37 &= 5890.49 \cdot h_b \\
h_b &= \dfrac{12566.37}{5890.49} \\
h_b &\approx 2.13 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A billet height of 2.13 mm is impractically thin; this implies the flash volume is unrealistically high for the given disk dimensions. In practice, the flash thickness is a few millimeters only, and the disk is forged from a stock whose diameter is close to $D_o$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a parting line? Discuss in detail the factors that influence the selection of a parting line in closed-die forging.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the die-cavity pressure $P_c$ in terms of flash thickness $t_f$, flash-land width $w_f$, and the yield stress $\sigma_y$ of the workpiece material at forging temperature. Use the von Mises yield criterion.

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Solution to (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A parting line is the locus where the upper and lower forging die halves meet, defining the plane of separation and the location of flash formation.

**Factors Influencing Parting Line Selection (6 Marks):**
1. **Symmetry:** Through the axis of symmetry when feasible.
2. **Draft:** Both halves must have at least 3°–7° draft for clean ejection.
3. **Flash and Trimming:** Affects flash volume and the direction of trim die movement.
4. **Grain Flow:** The parting line should preserve metallurgical grain continuity.
5. **Die Cost:** Flat horizontal lines are cheapest; curved lines increase EDM machining cost.
6. **Critical Tolerances:** Tight tolerances should lie on one die half, not across the parting line, to avoid mismatch error.
7. **Material Flow:** Should be balanced; uneven flow causes folds and laps.

**Solution to (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** Flash thickness $t_f$, land width $w_f$, perimeter $L_f$, projected area $A_p$, yield stress $\sigma_y$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Force balance on flash at die closure:} \quad P_c \cdot A_p &= 2 \cdot \tau_y \cdot w_f \cdot L_f \\
\text{Apply von Mises criterion:} \quad \tau_y &= \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\text{Substitute:} \quad P_c \cdot A_p &= 2 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot w_f \cdot L_f \\
\text{Use geometric relation:} \quad w_f &= k_1 \cdot t_f \\
\text{Substitute again:} \quad P_c \cdot A_p &= \dfrac{2 \cdot k_1 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t_f \cdot L_f}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\therefore \quad P_c &= \dfrac{2 \cdot k_1 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t_f \cdot L_f}{\sqrt{3} \cdot A_p} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication (1 Mark):** The result $P_c \propto t_f$ (with all other factors constant) shows that the **cavity pressure is directly proportional to flash thickness**. Designers therefore control $t_f$ via the flash-land geometry to regulate cavity pressure — a thinner land yields a thinner flash and a higher pressure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> - **Skipping the equilibrium step:** Many students write $P_c \propto 1/t_f$ without showing the force balance derivation — **2–3 marks lost** in 7-mark derivations.
> - **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** from the von Mises criterion: KTU examiners specifically test whether students apply the correct yield criterion.
> - **Wrong units in flash volume:** $V_f = \pi D w t$ must be in $\text{mm}^3$, not $\text{cm}^3$. A unit mismatch loses **1 full mark**.
> - **Ignoring draft angle in mismatch problems:** When the problem gives a draft angle, it must be used; ignoring it loses 1 mark.
> - **Conflating parting line with parting plane:** Parting line is a *trace*; parting plane is a *surface*. Examiner's vocabulary matters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parting Line Definition:** The locus of intersection between the two die halves where the flash forms and the forging is ejected.
- **Four Main Types:** Straight Horizontal, Straight Vertical, Irregular Curved, Multiple/Lock Type.
- **Selection Goal:** Through the axis of symmetry, at the largest cross-section, preserving grain flow and balanced material flow.
- **Flash Function:** Acts as a **pressure intensifier** — thinner flash → higher cavity pressure → better die filling.
- **Flash-to-Billet Ratio:** Typically **0.20 to 0.50** (i.e., 20%–50% of billet volume escapes as flash).
- **Draft Angle Standard:** **3°–7°** for external surfaces.
- **Critical Tolerances Rule:** Never straddle a tight tolerance across the parting line.
- **Die Cost Implication:** Flat horizontal parting lines are **cheapest**; curved/irregular lines are **15%–40% more expensive** due to EDM.
- **Trim Die Reference:** The parting line trace serves as the **trimming reference** in the subsequent trimming operation.
- **Mismatch Equation:** $D_m = N \cdot k_w \cdot P_c \cdot \cot(\alpha)$ — used to predict dimensional drift over die life.
- **Industrial Applications:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, flanges, valve bodies, hand tools, and aerospace forgings.
- **Standard Industrial Flash Thickness Range:** **1.0 mm to 5.0 mm** depending on forging size.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Recall Trick — "P-D-F-T-S":** Parting line = **P**lane of **D**ie **F**lash **T**race. **S**ymmetry-first selection rule.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Core and Cavity Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Core and Cavity Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Cavity (in Tool/Die Context):**
> A **cavity** is the negative impression machined or formed into a die block, mould, or tooling member that defines the external shape of the finished forged/moulded part. The cavity is the "hollow" into which the hot (or cold) work-piece is deformed to acquire its final geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Core (in Tool/Die Context):**
> A **core** is the positive, protruding insert (or integral projection) of a die/mould that generates the internal feature of a forged/moulded component — for example, internal holes, recesses, slots, and undercuts that cannot be formed by opposing die halves alone.

In forging terminology, since the part is **net-shape or near-net-shape** produced by plastic deformation (not by material addition), the *core* is a **core print** (an additional volume of metal deliberately left in the forging) which is later removed by a secondary machining operation to expose the internal feature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:**
> Module 4 of *PCPET602 — Metal Forming and Tool Design* treats core and cavity generation as the **bridge between die design (drawings) and die manufacturing (shop-floor fabrication)**. Without a sound cavity-generation strategy, the die cannot reproduce the forging geometry, draft, parting line, and shrinkage compensation defined in Module 3.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a baker making a **chocolate Easter egg with a hollow inside**.

- The **outer shell of the egg** is shaped by the **mould's cavity** — the curved hollow on the inside of the mould that pushes the chocolate outward into shape.
- The **inner hollow of the egg** is shaped by a **core** — a small egg-shaped solid piece that occupies the centre and prevents chocolate from filling the inside.

When you pour hot chocolate into the mould, it fills the space *between* the cavity wall and the core. When the chocolate sets, you remove the core, and you are left with a chocolate egg whose **outside = cavity** and **inside = core**.

**In a forging die the same logic applies — only the work-piece is hot metal and the deformation happens by *squeezing*, not by *pouring*:**

| Bakery Analogy | Forging Die Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Outer mould (hollow) | **Cavity** of the die block (impression) |
| Inner solid insert | **Core / Core print** (material left for machining) |
| Pouring molten chocolate | **Plastic flow of hot billet** under hammer/press |
| Demoulding | **Knockout & flash trimming** |
| Scrap chocolate flash | **Flash gutter** in die |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric to Remember:**
> - **Coefficient of thermal expansion of die steel (H11/H13):** $\alpha_d \approx 11 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\text{C}$ — used to compute die-block growth at forging temperature.
> - **Forging shrinkage allowance (carbon steel):** $\approx 1.5\% \text{ to } 2.5\%$ of the linear dimension.
> - **Standard draft angle:** $3^{\circ}\text{ to } 7^{\circ}$ on external surfaces; $5^{\circ}\text{ to } 10^{\circ}$ on internal surfaces (cores).

---

### 1.3 Why Core and Cavity Generation is a Critical Step

1. **Geometric Accuracy** — Tolerances of $\pm 0.1\,\text{mm}$ to $\pm 0.5\,\text{mm}$ must be transferred from engineering drawing to the die impression.
2. **Material Flow Control** — The cavity profile and the **flash gutter geometry** govern the metal-flow pattern and prevent die filling defects (misfits, cold shuts, laps).
3. **Tool Life** — Surface finish and die-steel integrity (free of re-hardened or HAZ-affected layers) directly influence die life measured in **number of forgings before wear-out** (typical: **5,000 – 20,000 forgings** for hammer-forging dies, **20,000 – 100,000+** for press-forging dies).
4. **Cost & Lead Time** — Cavity generation is often the **longest single operation** in die manufacture (often **40–60%** of total die-making time).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Cavity & Core Generation

Cavity and core generation is a **multi-stage decision-and-fabrication process**. The KTU 2024 module is structured around the following logical steps:

#### Step A — Forging Drawing Approval (Pre-requisite)
- Final forging drawing with **shrinkage, draft, fillet/radius, parting line, flash gutter, and machining allowance** is frozen.
- Inputs from Module 3: **Draft angles, parting-line strategy, flash-land ratio**.

#### Step B — Die Block Selection & Preparation
- **Die block material:** Standard die steels per Indian / AISI classification —
  - **AISI H11** (5% Cr hot-work steel) — for hammer forging.
  - **AISI H13** (5% Cr, +1% Mo, +1% V) — most common for press forging.
  - **AISI H21** (tungsten hot-work steel) — for severe service.
- **Hardness range:** $40\text{–}50$ HRC (working cavity), backed by **tough die-holder steel** at $30\text{–}35$ HRC.
- Die block is oversized by a **"finishing allowance"** of typically $3\text{–}6\,\text{mm}$ per face.

#### Step C — Generation Method Selection (the heart of Module 4)

There are **six principal methods** to generate a forging die cavity. The KTU syllabus expects students to know each method's **principle, application, advantages, and limitations**.

| Sl. | Method | Principle | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sinking (Die-Sinking / Sinker EDM)** | Electro-erosion using a pre-shaped graphite/copper electrode | Deep, complex, hardened-die cavities |
| 2 | **Hobbing (Die Hobbing)** | Cold / warm plastic indentation of die steel using a hardened master punch | Symmetric / axisymmetric cavities, high production |
| 3 | **CNC Milling (3-axis / 5-axis)** | Subtractive milling with ball-end / bull-nose cutters | Open-die cavities, prototypes, low-volume |
| 4 | **Broaching** | Linear / rotary pull of a multi-tooth broach through the cavity | Internal keyways, non-circular holes |
| 5 | **Casting of Die (Cast Cavity)** | Casting die steel (or cast-iron master) with cavity directly in the casting | Large dies, rough cavities, single-piece master |
| 6 | **Copy / Duplicating (Knee-type milling + tracer)** | Stylus follows a 1:1 master while cutter removes material | Replicas of an existing forging die |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> For **complex 3D hot-forging cavities** in hardened H11/H13 dies, **Die-Sinking EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining)** is the *de facto industry standard*. **Hobbing** dominates for **symmetric cold-forging** punches and dies.

#### Step D — Draft, Parting Line, and Shrinkage Compensation

Three geometric compensations are *built into* the cavity and core geometry, **not added later**:

1. **Shrinkage Compensation:**
   $$D_{cavity} = D_{forging} \times (1 + S)$$
   where $S$ is the linear shrinkage fraction (typically $0.015 \text{ to } 0.025$ for carbon steel forgings).
2. **Draft Compensation:**
   The cavity wall is tilted by the **draft angle** $\theta$ to allow part removal.
3. **Parting-Line Compensation:**
   The cavity is split at the **die-parting plane**; each half contains half the cavity depth.

#### Step E — Core (Core-Print) Generation

Cores are required when internal features (holes, recesses) cannot be produced by flash-and-die action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Strategies for Internal Features in Forging:**
> - **Impression in the die:** The internal feature is a *cavity projection* in the upper or lower die (e.g., a rib).
> - **Core print (extra stock):** For deep or small-radius internal features, **extra material is left in the forging** and removed later by drilling / milling — this extra material is the "core print" and **is NOT generated as a die core**.

This is a critical KTU pitfall: **"Core" in forging ≠ "Core" in casting / plastic moulding**. The terminology is reused, but the physical meaning differs.

#### Step F — Finishing, Inspection & Die Try-Out

- **Hand-filing, die-grinding, polishing** with diamond paste to reach $R_a \le 0.8\,\mu\text{m}$.
- **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)** verification of cavity profile.
- **Die try-out** on a forge press to validate part quality, flash thickness, and fill.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Shrinkage Compensation** | $L_c = L_f \cdot (1 + S)$ | mm | $S$ = shrinkage fraction; $L_f$ = forging dimension |
| 2 | **Cavity Depth from Draft** | $h_c = h_f + d \cdot \tan\theta$ | mm | $d$ = cavity width, $\theta$ = draft angle |
| 3 | **Projected Area (for press tonnage)** | $A_p = \sum A_{i}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Sum of projected forging + flash areas |
| 4 | **Forging Pressure (avg.)** | $p = K \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | $K$ = strength coefficient; true-strain compression |
| 5 | **Press Tonnage Required** | $F = p \cdot A_p$ | N or kN | Die must withstand $F$ without deflection |
| 6 | **Flash-to-Billet Area Ratio** | $\dfrac{A_{flash}}{A_{billet}} \approx 30\% \text{ to } 70\%$ | — | Controls material flow into cavity |
| 7 | **Die-Block Thermal Expansion (cavity dim.)** | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha_d \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | Compensate for hot-die growth at forging temp. |
| 8 | **EDM Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = K_{edm} \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ | $K_{edm}$ depends on electrode, dielectric |
| 9 | **Hobbing Force (approx.)** | $F_{hb} \approx 3 \cdot \sigma_{f,d} \cdot A_{proj}$ | N | $3\times$ flow stress for cold indent |
| 10 | **Wear Allowance on Cavity** | $\delta_w \approx (0.005 \text{ to } 0.01) \cdot L$ | mm | Adds service life before re-grinding |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Core & Cavity Generation |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Crankshafts** | Cavity generation by sinking EDM on H13 die blocks; draft $\approx 5^{\circ}$ |
| **Connecting Rods** | Hobbed cavities for high-volume runs (millions of pieces) |
| **Aerospace Turbine Blades** | 5-axis CNC milling of isothermal-forging dies; ceramic-cored cavities |
| **Cold-Heading of Bolts** | Hobbed tungsten-carbide dies; core = hex-impression punch |
| **Railway Wheels** | Cast-bronze cavity inserts in forged-steel die holders |

> [!NOTE]
> The same generation principles scale from **gram-level jewellery dies** to **multi-tonne ship-propeller forging dies** — making this module one of the highest-weightage topics in PCPET602.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix
