### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shrinkage-Compensated Cavity Dimension

**Problem:** A forging drawing specifies a flange diameter of $\phi\,200\,\text{mm}$. The forging material is medium-carbon steel (AISI 1045) with linear shrinkage $S = 2\%$. Determine the cavity diameter to be machined into the die block.

**Step 1 — Identify the known quantities.**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{forging} &= 200\,\text{mm} \\
S &= 0.02 \quad (\text{linear shrinkage fraction})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the shrinkage-compensation formula.**

The cavity must be made **larger** than the forging by the shrinkage factor, because after cooling the forging shrinks. So:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{cavity} &= D_{forging} \times (1 + S) \\
&= 200 \times (1 + 0.02) \\
&= 200 \times 1.02 \\
&= 204\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation.**

When the hot forging solidifies inside the die, the steel contracts as it cools from forging temperature (≈ $1200\,^\circ\text{C}$) to room temperature. The die cavity is intentionally over-sized by $4\,\text{mm}$ on diameter so that the **shrunken forging** lands at exactly $\phi\,200\,\text{mm}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cavity Depth with Draft-Angle Correction

**Problem:** A forging pocket is $40\,\text{mm}$ deep at the centre and $80\,\text{mm}$ wide. A draft angle of $5^{\circ}$ is applied to the side walls. What is the **actual depth** the cavity must be machined to (measured at the centre line)?

**Step 1 — Known values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{forging} &= 40\,\text{mm} \quad \text{(depth at centre, the deepest point)} \\
d &= 80\,\text{mm} \quad \text{(cavity width)} \\
\theta &= 5^{\circ} \quad \text{(draft angle)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Wall-rise due to draft.**

The two side walls, when tilted by $\theta$, **rise** over half the cavity width:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta h_{wall} &= \frac{d}{2} \times \tan(\theta) \\
&= \frac{80}{2} \times \tan(5^{\circ}) \\
&= 40 \times 0.0875 \\
&\approx 3.50\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** $\tan(5^{\circ}) \approx 0.08749$.

**Step 3 — Total cavity depth at the centre line.**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{cavity} &= h_{forging} + 2 \cdot \Delta h_{wall} \\
&= 40 + 2 \times 3.50 \\
&= 40 + 7.00 \\
&= 47.00\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

The die-cavity floor is **curved / tapered** (not flat). It slopes upward by $3.5\,\text{mm}$ from the centre to each side wall. This guarantees the forging can be **knocked out of the die** without scoring the walls.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Press Tonnage Required for Cavity Closure

**Problem:** A connecting-rod forging has a projected area of $A_{proj}=12{,}000\,\text{mm}^2$ including flash. The billet height is $h_0 = 60\,\text{mm}$ and the final height in the die is $h_f = 25\,\text{mm}$. The strength coefficient $K = 700\,\text{N/mm}^2$. Find the **press tonnage** required.

**Step 1 — True strain in compression.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) \\
&= \ln\left(\frac{60}{25}\right) \\
&= \ln(2.4) \\
&\approx 0.8755
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Average forging pressure (using slab-method approximation).**

$$\begin{aligned}
p &= K \cdot \varepsilon \\
&= 700 \times 0.8755 \\
&\approx 612.85\,\text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total forging force.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= p \times A_{proj} \\
&= 612.85 \times 12{,}000 \\
&= 7{,}354{,}200\,\text{N} \\
&\approx 7{,}354\,\text{kN} \\
&\approx 750\,\text{tonnes (metric)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation for die design.**

The die block, shrink ring, and press frame must be designed to **withstand 750 tonnes** without deflection. The cavity itself must be deep enough that the flash gutter does not bottom out, and the die set must be aligned to within $\pm 0.05\,\text{mm}$ to avoid **flash asymmetry** and **misfit**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cavity-Generation Methods

| Parameter | Sinker EDM | Hobbing | CNC Milling | Broaching | Cast Cavity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Suitable Die Hardness** | Hardened (HRC 50+) | Soft (≤ HRC 22) | Hardened OK | Hardened OK | As-cast |
| **Cavity Complexity** | Very high (3D free-form) | Axisymmetric / symmetric | High (3-axis) | Linear / non-circular | Moderate |
| **Lead Time** | High (electrode + EDM) | Low | Medium | High (broach cost) | High (mould + pour) |
| **Cost per Cavity** | High | Low (at volume) | Medium | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | $0.4 \text{ to } 1.6\,\mu\text{m}$ | $0.8 \text{ to } 3.2\,\mu\text{m}$ | $0.8 \text{ to } 3.2\,\mu\text{m}$ | $0.4 \text{ to } 1.6\,\mu\textm$ | $3.2 \text{ to } 12.5\,\mu\text{m}$ |
| **Best Material** | H11, H13, H21, S7 | O1, A2 (pre-hardened) | H13, P20 | D2, M2 | Cast iron, cast steel |
| **Min. Internal Radius** | $0.3\,\text{mm}$ (electrode) | $1.0\,\text{mm}$ (punch) | $1.0\,\text{mm}$ (tool) | $0.5\,\text{mm}$ (tooth) | $3.0\,\text{mm}$ (draft) |
| **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Yes (recast layer ≈ 0.02 mm) | No | No | No | No |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

---

### 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example — Combined Shrinkage + Draft + Wear

**Question:** A forging of length $L_f = 150\,\text{mm}$ and width $W_f = 80\,\text{mm}$ is to be produced. Given $S = 0.018$, $\theta = 5^{\circ}$, wear allowance $\delta_w = 1.2\,\text{mm}$, find the **final cavity dimensions** (length, width, and depth) to be machined by EDM.

**Step 1 — Shrinkage-compensated length.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_c &= L_f \cdot (1 + S) + \delta_w \\
&= 150 \times 1.018 + 1.2 \\
&= 152.7 + 1.2 \\
&= 153.9\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Shrinkage-compensated width.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_c &= W_f \cdot (1 + S) + 2 \cdot \delta_{w,side} \\
&= 80 \times 1.018 + 2 \times 0.6 \\
&= 81.44 + 1.2 \\
&= 82.64\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** Wear is added to **both sides** of the width dimension, so it is doubled.

**Step 3 — Cavity depth (including draft, no forging depth given — assume forging depth $h_f = 30\,\text{mm}$).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta h_{wall} &= \frac{W_c}{2} \cdot \tan(5^{\circ}) \\
&= \frac{82.64}{2} \times 0.0875 \\
&= 41.32 \times 0.0875 \\
&\approx 3.62\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{cavity} &= h_f + 2 \cdot \Delta h_{wall} + \delta_{w,depth} \\
&= 30 + 2 \times 3.62 + 0.6 \\
&= 30 + 7.24 + 0.6 \\
&\approx 37.84\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final die-cavity block dimensions (with finishing stock).**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{block} &= L_c + 2 \times 3\,\text{mm (finishing stock)} = 159.9\,\text{mm} \\
W_{block} &= W_c + 2 \times 3\,\text{mm} = 88.64\,\text{mm} \\
H_{block} &= h_{cavity} + 2 \times 3\,\text{mm} = 43.84\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Many KTU students forget to add the **flash gutter depth** ($≈ 3\text{ to } 5\,\text{mm}$) to the die block. The cavity depth is *inside* the block, and the block must be tall enough to host both the cavity AND the gutter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cavity & Core Generation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forging Drawing] --> B[Apply Shrinkage Compensation]
    B --> C[Apply Draft Angles]
    C --> D[Apply Fillet and Radius]
    D --> E[Select Die Steel H11 H13 H21]
    E --> F{Generation Method?}
    F -->|Hardened Die Complex Shape| G1[Die Sinking EDM]
    F -->|Axisymmetric High Volume| G2[Hobbing]
    F -->|Prototype Open Die| G3[CNC Milling 3 axis 5 axis]
    F -->|Internal Keyway| G4[Broaching]
    F -->|Large Rough Cavity| G5[Cast Cavity Insert]
    G1 --> H[Hand Polish Diamond Paste]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I[CMM Inspection]
    I --> J[Die Try Out on Press]
    J --> K{Quality OK?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Production Forging]
    K -->|No| M[Rework and Re Try]
    M --> H
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cavity Geometry with Draft & Shrinkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PartA[Cavity Cross Section]
        direction TB
        P1[Forging Outer Surface] --- P2[Shrinkage Gap 2 percent]
        P2 --- P3[Cavity Wall with Draft 5 deg]
        P3 --- P4[Cavity Floor Curved]
        P4 --- P3
    end
    subgraph PartB[Core Core Print]
        direction TB
        C1[Internal Hole in Forging] --- C2[Extra Stock Core Print]
        C2 --- C3[Drilled or Milled in Secondary Op]
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cavity Components Inside a Forging Die

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph UpperDie[Upper Die Half]
        U1[Upper Cavity Half] --> U2[Upper Flash Gutter]
        U2 --> U3[Knockout Pin]
    end
    subgraph LowerDie[Lower Die Half]
        L1[Lower Cavity Half] --> L2[Lower Flash Gutter]
        L2 --> L3[Parting Line Land]
        L3 --> L4[Lower Core or Core Print Seat]
    end
    UpperDie -->|Closes on| LowerDie
    L4 --> M[Hot Billet Deforms into Cavity]
    M --> F[Flash escapes into Gutter]
    F --> T[Trimming Operation]
```

---

### 4.4 Process-Flow Diagram — Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Forging Cavity Required] --> Q1{Die Hardness Required?}
    Q1 -->|Hardened HRC 45 plus| Q2{Geometry Complexity?}
    Q1 -->|Soft Annealed| Q3{Production Volume?}
    Q2 -->|High 3D free form| R1[Choose Die Sinking EDM]
    Q2 -->|Moderate| R2[Choose CNC Milling 5 axis]
    Q3 -->|Over 10000 pieces| R3[Choose Hobbing]
    Q3 -->|Below 500 pieces| R4[Choose Cast Cavity or Copy Mill]
    R1 --> End[Final Cavity Ready]
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The above flow-chart is an acceptable substitute in answer-sheets when a free-hand sketch is requested. KTU examiners award full marks to **clean labelled flow diagrams** with **arrow direction clarity**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Dec 2022 Model]**
**Differentiate between "core" and "cavity" in forging die design with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 80–100 words):**

| Aspect | Cavity | Core (Core Print) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Negative impression in the die block | Extra material left in forging |
| **Function** | Forms the **external** surface of the forging | Provides stock for **internal** feature machining |
| **Geometric Type** | Concave hollow | Convex protrusion / material volume |
| **Example** | Flange impression of a connecting rod | Solid metal cylinder left inside the big-end bore |
| **Removed in** | Knockout operation | Drilling / boring in a separate machine |

> **Keyword markers for full marks:** *negative impression, external shape, internal feature, extra stock, knockout, secondary machining*.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model as per 2024 Scheme]**
**List any three methods of generating a forging die cavity and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 3 × 1 mark each):**

1. **Die-Sinking EDM** — *Advantage:* Can cut hardened die steel (HRC 50+) to complex 3-D shapes without mechanical force.
2. **Hobbing** — *Advantage:* Lowest per-piece cost at high production volumes (no electrodes, no EDM time).
3. **CNC Milling (5-axis)** — *Advantage:* High geometric flexibility, ideal for prototypes and small batches with short lead time.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Avoid vague terms like *"good method"*. Always pair the method with a *quantified* or *technical* advantage (e.g., *"can machine HRC 50+ steel"*).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **die-sinking EDM process** for generating a forging die cavity. List **four advantages** and **two limitations** of the process. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A steel forging of dimensions $L = 200\,\text{mm}$, $W = 100\,\text{mm}$ and depth $h = 35\,\text{mm}$ is to be produced in a closed-die forging. The shrinkage allowance is $1.8\%$, draft angle is $5^{\circ}$, and finishing stock is $3\,\text{mm}$ per face. Calculate the **die-cavity dimensions** to be machined. **[7 marks]**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Die-sinking EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining) is a **non-contact, thermo-electric material-removal process** in which a pre-shaped electrode (graphite or copper) is fed into a hardened die block. Sparks generated in a dielectric fluid (kerosene or de-ionized water) erode the steel to form the desired cavity.

**2. Process Steps (3 marks — 1 mark each for 3 distinct steps):**
- **Step 1:** A **master electrode** is machined (often by CNC milling) to the **inverse of the forging shape**, scaled **down by the shrinkage factor and up by the wear/finishing stock**.
- **Step 2:** The electrode and the die block are immersed in a **dielectric bath**, and a pulsed DC voltage (typically $80\text{ to } 200\,\text{V}$) is applied.
- **Step 3:** Controlled **sparks (≈ 8000–12,000 °C local temperature)** melt and vaporize a tiny volume of die steel on every pulse, gradually eroding the cavity.
- **Step 4:** The electrode is advanced along the Z-axis to maintain a constant gap ($0.01\text{ to } 0.05\,\text{mm}$).
- **Step 5:** After rough cutting, a **finish-cut electrode (slightly smaller)** is used to improve surface finish to $R_a \le 1.6\,\mu\text{m}$.

**3. Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
- Can machine **fully hardened die steel** (HRC 50+) — no need to anneal.
- Cuts **complex 3-D free-form geometry** with no mechanical stress.
- Achieves **tolerances of ± 0.02 mm** and fine surface finish.
- **No burrs** and **no mechanical distortion** of the die.

**4. Two Limitations (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
- **Recast/HAZ layer (≈ 0.01–0.03 mm)** must be removed by subsequent polishing — otherwise fatigue cracks initiate.
- **High electrode cost** and **long cycle time** for deep cavities.

> **Sketch requirement (separate 1 mark integrated into the 7):** A simple cross-section showing the **electrode**, **dielectric tank**, **die block**, **flushing nozzle**, and **spark gap** is expected.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
$$\begin{aligned}
L_f &= 200\,\text{mm} \\
W_f &= 100\,\text{mm} \\
h_f &= 35\,\text{mm} \\
S &= 0.018 \\
\theta &= 5^{\circ} \\
\delta_{fin} &= 3\,\text{mm per face}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Shrinkage-compensated forging dimensions (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{cav,\,raw} &= 200 \times (1 + 0.018) = 200 \times 1.018 = 203.6\,\text{mm} \\
W_{cav,\,raw} &= 100 \times 1.018 = 101.8\,\text{mm} \\
h_{cav,\,raw} &= 35 \times 1.018 = 35.63\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Draft compensation in depth (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta h_{wall} &= \frac{W_{cav,\,raw}}{2} \cdot \tan(5^{\circ}) \\
&= \frac{101.8}{2} \times 0.0875 \\
&= 50.9 \times 0.0875 \\
&\approx 4.45\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{cav,\,with\,draft} &= 35.63 + 2 \times 4.45 \\
&= 35.63 + 8.90 \\
&\approx 44.53\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Finishing-stock addition (1 mark):**

Finishing stock is added to **both faces** in each direction:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{die\,cavity} &= 203.6 + 2 \times 3 = 209.6\,\text{mm} \\
W_{die\,cavity} &= 101.8 + 2 \times 3 = 107.8\,\text{mm} \\
h_{die\,cavity} &= 44.53 + 2 \times 3 = 50.53\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final die-block overall dimensions (1 mark):**

The die block must be larger than the cavity by the **shrinking-fit allowance** (≈ 1.5–2 % per side) and the **flash-gutter space** (3–5 mm on each side). Adopting a $1.5\%$ margin:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{block} &= 209.6 \times 1.015 + 2 \times 4 \approx 221\,\text{mm} \\
W_{block} &= 107.8 \times 1.015 + 2 \times 4 \approx 117\,\text{mm} \\
H_{block} &= 50.53 \times 1.015 + 2 \times 4 \approx 59\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Standard rounding (1 mark):** $\boxed{L_{block} \approx 220\,\text{mm}, \quad W_{block} \approx 120\,\text{mm}, \quad H_{block} \approx 60\,\text{mm}}$.

**Step 5 — Statement of choice of die steel (1 mark):**

For a carbon-steel forging of $L = 200\,\text{mm}$ in a press-forging die, the recommended die-block material is **AISI H13 hot-work tool steel** at working hardness **HRC 42–48**.

**Step 6 — Method recommendation (1 mark):** Given the **complex 3-D shape** of a typical forging and the **hardened die block**, **Die-Sinking EDM** is the appropriate generation method.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the **die-hobbing process** for cavity generation. Explain with a sketch how an axisymmetric forging-die cavity (e.g., a gear-blank die) is produced by hobbing. State **three advantages** and **two limitations** of hobbing. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A forging die cavity is to be generated for a hexagonal bolt-head cavity. The across-flats dimension is $A_f = 24\,\text{mm}$ and the depth is $h = 18\,\text{mm}$. The forging is medium-carbon steel, $S = 2\%$, draft $\theta = 7°$. If the cavity is produced by **die hobbing with a finishing stock of 1.5 mm**, calculate the **punch dimensions** (across-flats and depth) required to hob the cavity. **[7 marks]**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Die-hobbing is a **cold (or warm) plastic-deformation process** in which a hardened master punch (the "hob") is **pressed into a softer die blank** to form the cavity by **material displacement**, not by material removal.

**2. Process Description (3 marks):**
- The die block is pre-machined to the **backing dimensions** and annealed to a hardness of **≤ HRC 22 (≈ 220 HB)**.
- The hob (hardness ≈ HRC 60–62) is **precision-machined** with a **mirror finish** since its surface is replicated on the die cavity.
- The hob is **lubricated** (graphite/MoS₂) and pressed into the die blank using a **hydraulic hobbing press** at a force $F_{hb} \approx (2.5\text{ to } 3) \cdot \sigma_{f,d} \cdot A_{proj}$.
- After full insertion, the hob is **held under load for 5–10 seconds** to allow elastic recovery of the die.
- The hob is **retracted**, and the cavity is **stress-relieved** at $550\text{–}600\,^\circ\text{C}$ and then **hardened** to the working hardness.

**3. Sketch description (1 mark):** A neat sketch showing the **die block**, **hob / punch**, **press ram**, and **direction of indentation** with arrows must accompany the answer.

**4. Three Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
- **No material is wasted** — chips / swarf are eliminated.
- **Excellent surface finish** is achieved directly from the hob ($R_a \le 0.8\,\mu\text{m}$).
- **High repeatability and low per-piece cost** for high volumes (>10,000 pieces).

**5. Two Limitations (0.5 mark each, total 1 mark):**
- Restricted to **axisymmetric / symmetric** cavities with sufficient draft.
- **High initial cost** of the hob and the hobbing press.

> **Word count marker:** Aim for **250–300 words** plus the sketch to comfortably secure 7 marks.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
$$\begin{aligned}
A_{f, forging} &= 24\,\text{mm} \quad \text{(hex across-flats)} \\
h_{forging} &= 18\,\text{mm} \\
S &= 0.02 \\
\theta &= 7^{\circ} \\
\delta_{fin} &= 1.5\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — Hobbing Geometry:**
> In hobbing, the **punch IS the cavity shape**. The punch is made **smaller** than the desired cavity by the **shrinkage factor** (since the *die cavity* will be made larger, and the forging will shrink inside it). Hence the punch dimension is *forging dimension × (1 − S)* for an external cavity.

**Step 1 — Shrinkage-compensated cavity across-flats (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{f, cavity} &= A_{f, forging} \times (1 + S) \\
&= 24 \times 1.02 \\
&= 24.48\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Punch across-flats (must be slightly *smaller* to leave finishing stock) (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{f, punch} &= A_{f, cavity} - 2 \cdot \delta_{fin} \\
&= 24.48 - 2 \times 1.5 \\
&= 24.48 - 3.0 \\
&= 21.48\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Cavity depth including draft (1.5 marks):**

For a hexagonal bolt-head cavity, the draft is applied to all six flat faces. The effective "width" that draft lifts over is the **across-corners dimension**, related to across-flats by:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_c &= \frac{2 \cdot A_f}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2 \times 24}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{48}{1.732} \approx 27.71\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta h_{wall} &= \frac{A_c}{2} \times \tan(7^{\circ}) \\
&= \frac{27.71}{2} \times 0.1228 \\
&\approx 13.855 \times 0.1228 \\
&\approx 1.70\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{cavity} &= h_{forging} \times (1 + S) + 2 \cdot \Delta h_{wall} \\
&= 18 \times 1.02 + 2 \times 1.70 \\
&= 18.36 + 3.40 \\
&\approx 21.76\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Punch depth (must account for finishing stock) (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{punch} &= h_{cavity} - 2 \cdot \delta_{fin} \\
&= 21.76 - 2 \times 1.5 \\
&= 21.76 - 3.0 \\
&= 18.76\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final punch dimensions (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{A_{f, punch} = 21.48\,\text{mm} \quad \text{and} \quad h_{punch} = 18.76\,\text{mm}}$$

> **Examiner's note:** Students often forget to apply **shrinkage compensation to the punch** (treating punch = forging). Always remember: the **die cavity** carries the shrinkage; the **punch** is smaller than the cavity to leave a finishing / wear allowance.

**Step 6 — Method comment (1 mark):**
For a **hexagonal bolt-head cavity**, hobbing is the **most economical method** at production volumes exceeding 10,000 pieces; for lower volumes, sinker EDM is preferred.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Core & Cavity Generation Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing cavity & punch shrinkage directions** — students apply shrinkage to the punch instead of the cavity. *Marks lost: 2–3 per question.*
> 2. **Forgetting draft-angle compensation in depth** — the cavity depth is *not* equal to the forging depth; the side walls slope upward. *Marks lost: 1–2.*
> 3. **Applying finishing stock only on one side** — finishing is added to *both faces*. *Marks lost: 1.*
> 4. **Ignoring flash-gutter depth** — die-block height = cavity depth + flash gutter + back relief. *Marks lost: 1.*
> 5. **Choosing the wrong generation method** — e.g., suggesting hobbing for a non-axisymmetric forging, or EDM for a 1,000-piece run when milling is cheaper. *Marks lost: 2.*
> 6. **Not stating the die steel** — KTU board answers without an explicit die-material choice are flagged for "incomplete process planning". *Marks lost: 1.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Cavity** = negative impression; **Core (core print)** = extra material for internal feature.
- [ ] **Six generation methods**: Sinker EDM, Hobbing, CNC Milling, Broaching, Cast Cavity, Copy Milling.
- [ ] **Hardened die (HRC > 40) → Sinker EDM** is the industry-standard answer.
- [ ] **Axisymmetric + high volume → Hobbing** is the most economical.
- [ ] **Shrinkage formula:** $D_{cavity} = D_{forging} \times (1 + S)$.
- [ ] **Draft formula:** $\Delta h_{wall} = \dfrac{d}{2} \tan(\theta)$, total depth adds $2 \Delta h_{wall}$.
- [ ] **Finishing stock** is added to **both faces** in every direction.
- [ ] **Die-block height** must accommodate cavity + flash gutter + back relief.
- [ ] **Standard draft:** $3°\text{–}7°$ external, $5°\text{–}10°$ internal.
- [ ] **Standard shrinkage:** $1.5\% \text{–} 2.5\%$ for carbon-steel forgings.
- [ ] **EDM leaves a recast layer** (≈ 0.02 mm) that **must be polished off** to prevent fatigue cracks.
- [ ] **Hobbing force** $F_{hb} \approx (2.5\text{ to } 3) \cdot \sigma_{f,d} \cdot A_{proj}$.
- [ ] **Common die steels:** AISI **H11, H13, H21, S7, O1, A2, D2, P20**.
- [ ] **Hardness for working cavity:** $40\text{–}50$ HRC; **for hobbing blank:** $\le 22$ HRC.
- [ ] **Always state the die steel AND the generation method** in long answers.
- [ ] **Examiners reward flowcharts and labelled sketches** — invest 30 seconds in a clean diagram.
- [ ] **Pitfall to avoid:** Mixing up casting-mould "core" with forging "core print" terminology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Strategy:** For any *Core & Cavity Generation* question in PCPET602, structure the answer as **(i) Definition, (ii) Sketch/Flowchart, (iii) Method selection justification, (iv) Numerical calculation (if any), (v) Die-material & finishing recommendation**. This 5-point structure consistently scores 12–14/14 in KTU board valuations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runner and Gate Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Runner and Gate Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Runner and Gate System?

In **Injection Moulding** and **Die Casting**, the molten material (polymer melt or molten metal) cannot simply be poured directly into the mould cavity. It must travel through a precisely engineered network of channels that deliver the material from the **machine nozzle** (or shot chamber) into the cavity at the correct rate, pressure, temperature, and shear condition.

This delivery network is collectively called the **Runner and Gate System**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Runner** is the channel (cross-sectional passage) within a mould that conveys the molten material from the sprue (or machine nozzle) to the **Gate**. A **Gate** is the small, narrow restriction (final entry point) that controls the flow of molten material into the mould cavity. Together, the **Runner–Gate System** determines the **fill pattern**, **packing efficiency**, **part quality**, **cycle time**, and **wastage rate** of every injection-moulded or die-cast part.

## 1.2 The Three Functional Zones of a Mould Feed System

Every mould feed system consists of three sequential zones:

| Zone | Function | Engineering Control |
|------|----------|--------------------|
| **Sprue** | Receives melt from machine nozzle; converts vertical flow to horizontal flow | Sprue puller, sprue bushing taper (typically **1° to 1.5°** per side) |
| **Runner** | Distributes melt to multiple cavities (or single cavity) | Diameter, length, layout (cold/hot, balanced/unbalanced) |
| **Gate** | Final restriction that controls flow rate, shear, and freeze-off | Gate type, gate land length, gate cross-section |

> [!NOTE]
> The Gate is the **smallest cross-section** in the entire feed system. It acts like a **faucet** — the moment the cavity is filled, the gate freezes off (solidifies) due to heat loss to the cold mould walls, sealing the cavity and preventing backflow during packing and cooling.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **municipal water distribution system** supplying water to several houses simultaneously:

- The **Sprue** is the **main vertical inlet pipe** from the reservoir.
- The **Runner** is the **horizontal underground pipeline network** branching through the neighbourhood.
- The **Gate** is the **small valve at the entrance of each house** — it controls exactly how much water flows in, and once the house's tank is full, the valve closes (freezes off).
- A **Balanced Runner** is like a system where every house, no matter how far from the reservoir, receives water at the **same pressure and same time**.
- An **Unbalanced Runner** is like a system where the house closest to the reservoir gets water first and at higher pressure, while the farthest house gets less and later.

In injection moulding, this "water network" is scaled down to millimetres, and instead of water we are delivering **molten plastic at 200–300 °C** under pressures of **70–140 MPa (10,000–20,000 psi)**.

## 1.4 Why Runner and Gate Design is a KTU-Favourite Topic

KTU examiners frequently test this topic because:

1. It is **formula-driven** (perfect for 14-mark analytical problems).
2. It directly applies **fluid mechanics** (Bernoulli, Hagen–Poiseuille, shear rate).
3. It has **design trade-offs** (wastage vs quality, balance vs simplicity).
4. It is **industry-critical** — poor runner/gate design is the root cause of **70%** of injection moulding defects (weld lines, air traps, sink marks, short shots, flash, warpage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCPET602 – Module 4):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus lists "Runner and gate design" as a core topic under mould/die design for both plastic injection moulding and pressure die casting. Questions typically test: (i) types of runners and gates, (ii) runner diameter/length calculation, (iii) gate sizing, (iv) balancing equations, and (v) defect correlation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Runner Systems

### A. Based on Temperature State

#### (i) Cold Runner System
- The runner solidifies along with the part and is **ejected as scrap** (or recycled).
- **Advantages:** Simple mould design, low tooling cost, works for all thermoplastics.
- **Disadvantages:** Material wastage (**20–50%** of shot), longer cycle time (runner must cool), higher part cost.
- **Typical Use:** Short production runs, prototyping, multicavity moulds.

#### (ii) Hot Runner System
- The runner is kept molten by externally heated manifolds; only the part is ejected.
- **Advantages:** Zero runner scrap, shorter cycle, consistent melt temperature, lower injection pressure.
- **Disadvantages:** High tooling cost, complex temperature control, not suitable for all resins (e.g., PVC degrades), difficult for colour changes.
- **Typical Use:** High-volume production (>100,000 parts), thin-wall parts, medical parts.

### B. Based on Flow Distribution (Layout)

#### (i) Balanced Runner
- Every cavity receives melt at the **same pressure, same temperature, and same time**.
- Achieved by making the **runner path length from sprue to each gate identical**.
- All cavities fill simultaneously → uniform part quality, lower reject rate.
- **Disadvantage:** Mould footprint is larger; runner material volume is higher.

#### (ii) Unbalanced Runner (Natural)
- Cavities at different distances from the sprue fill at different times and pressures.
- Cavity nearest to sprue fills first and at higher pressure → tends to flash.
- Cavity farthest fills last and at lower pressure → tends to short-shot.
- **Use:** Family moulds (where part sizes differ), or when geometry forces asymmetry.

## 2.2 Classification of Gates (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Gate Type | Geometry | Typical Application | Land Length (L) | Land Thickness (t) |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Edge Gate (Side Gate)** | Rectangular cross-section on part edge | General purpose, flat parts | 0.5–1.5 mm | 50–80% of part wall thickness |
| **Fan Gate** | Wide tapered entry (fan-shaped) | Large flat parts, discs, lids | 1.0–2.0 mm | Decreases gradually to wall thickness |
| **Tab Gate** | Edge gate with extended tab (sub-gate) | Parts requiring scar-free appearance, cosmetic parts | 1.0–1.5 mm | Matches tab thickness |
| **Sprue Gate (Direct Gate)** | Direct connection from sprue to part | Single-cavity moulds, thick parts, large parts | — | Matches sprue diameter (4–10 mm) |
| **Pin Gate (Tunnel Gate)** | Small circular gate (0.5–1.5 mm dia.) cut by ejector pin | Multi-cavity moulds, small parts, automatic degating | 0.5–1.5 mm | Diameter = 0.5–1.5 mm |
| **Submarine Gate (Tunnel Gate)** | Pin gate with angled entry below parting line | Parts where gate vestige must be hidden | 0.5–1.2 mm | Small circular cross-section |
| **Diaphragm Gate** | Cylindrical disc of melt entering concentrically | Parts requiring uniform radial flow (lens, gear blanks) | — | Disc thickness = 0.5–1.5 mm |
| **Ring Gate** | Continuous ring-shaped entry | Tubular parts, pipes, bushings | — | Ring cross-section ~ wall thickness |
| **Film Gate (Flash Gate)** | Thin wide strip | Large flat thin parts | 0.2–0.5 mm | Very thin, wide |

## 2.3 Key Design Equations (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorise units and boundary conditions.

### Table 2.1: Core Runner and Gate Design Formulas

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Runner diameter (D) — empirical | $D = 0.264 \cdot W^{0.5}$ | D in mm, W = shot weight (g) including runners | Valid for **1 mm wall thickness**. Correction factor applied otherwise. |
| 2 | Runner diameter — wall-thickness corrected | $D = 0.264 \cdot t^{0.5} \cdot W^{0.5}$ | t = nominal wall thickness (mm) | For thin-wall parts, t < 1.5 mm |
| 3 | Pressure drop in runner (Poiseuille) | $\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$ | ΔP in Pa, μ = viscosity (Pa·s), L = length (m), v = velocity (m/s), D = diameter (m) | Laminar flow assumption; melt is **non-Newtonian** so use **apparent viscosity** |
| 4 | Pressure drop in runner (general) | $\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | Q = volumetric flow rate (m³/s) | Hagen–Poiseuille equation for circular runners |
| 5 | Shear rate in runner (circular) | $\dot{\gamma} = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3} = \frac{8 v}{D}$ | s⁻¹ | Target: **1,000–10,000 s⁻¹** for most thermoplastics |
| 6 | Shear rate in rectangular gate | $\dot{\gamma} = \frac{6 Q}{W h^2}$ | W = gate width, h = gate thickness (m) | For aspect ratio W/h > 10 |
| 7 | Gate land length | $L_{gate} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2) \times h_{gate}$ | h = gate thickness | Prevents premature freeze-off |
| 8 | Gate thickness (edge gate) | $h_{gate} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.8) \times t_{wall}$ | t = part wall thickness | Common rule: **h = 2/3 × wall** |
| 9 | Gate width (edge gate) | $W_{gate} = (3 \text{ to } 6) \times h_{gate}$ | mm | Wider gate = lower shear, better fill |
| 10 | Runner length-to-diameter ratio (L/D) | Recommended ≤ **8** | dimensionless | If L/D > 8, pressure drop excessive |
| 11 | Runner volume | $V_{runner} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L_{runner}$ | mm³ | Used for wastage % calculation |
| 12 | Material wastage % | $\% W = \frac{W_{runner}}{W_{shot}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Hot runner: ~2–5%; Cold runner: 20–50% |
| 13 | Number of cavities (balancing) | $n = \frac{\text{Total runner length available}}{\text{Common runner length + branch runner length}}$ | dimensionless | Geometry-based balancing |
| 14 | Cavity fill time (Stefan equation) | $t_{fill} = \frac{2 L^2 \eta}{P h}$ | L = flow length, η = viscosity, P = pressure, h = wall thickness | Used in mould-filling simulation |
| 15 | Reynolds number in runner | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | In moulding Re is very low (1–100) → laminar flow always |

## 2.4 Engineering Logic Behind Each Equation

### Why Gate Land Length = 1.5–2 × Gate Thickness?

If the land is too **short**, the gate freezes off before the cavity is fully packed → **short shots, sink marks, voids**.

If the land is too **long**, excessive frictional heating occurs → **drool, flash, material degradation**.

The **1.5–2 ratio** ensures the gate remains open during the entire **packing and holding phase** (typically 2–5 seconds for medium parts), then freezes cleanly during cooling.

### Why Gate Thickness = 0.5–0.8 × Wall Thickness?

This rule balances two competing demands:

- **If gate is too thick** (≥ wall): the gate freezes later than the part → cavity cannot be sealed during packing → **sink marks and voids** form as the part shrinks but melt can still flow back through the gate.
- **If gate is too thin** (≤ 0.3 × wall): excessive shear heating → **flash, material degradation, gate vestige visible** on the part.

The **2/3 rule** (0.67 × wall) is the industry standard compromise.

### Why Runner L/D ≤ 8?

Beyond L/D = 8, the **pressure drop** in the runner rises sharply (ΔP ∝ L, and D must shrink to maintain shear rate, but D² in denominator drives ΔP even higher for a given flow rate). Excessive pressure drop leads to:

- Short shots in far cavities
- Burn marks at the runner walls (shear-induced degradation)
- Higher clamp tonnage requirement

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Why Runner/Gate Design is Critical |
|----------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Automotive (dashboards, bumpers)** | Large flat parts → **fan gate** + balanced runner critical to prevent weld lines and warpage |
| **Medical (syringes, vials)** | Zero-defect requirement → **hot runner with pin gate** for clean, flash-free parts |
| **Electronics (connectors, housings)** | Thin walls (0.5–1.0 mm) → **submarine/pin gates** with precise shear rate control |
| **Packaging (caps, closures)** | High volume → **hot runner, multi-cavity**, runner balance directly affects OEE |
| **Die Casting (Al, Zn alloy parts)** | Same principles apply with **higher temperatures (400–700 °C)** and shorter cycle times |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Runner Diameter from Shot Weight

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU 2024 examinations.

### Given
A thermoplastic part has the following specifications:
- **Shot weight** (total weight per shot, including runners): W = 200 g
- **Nominal wall thickness** of the part: t = 2.5 mm
- **Number of cavities** in the mould: 4
- **Runner system:** Cold runner, balanced

### To Find
The diameter of the main (primary) runner.

### Step 1: Recall the Empirical Equation

The empirical formula relating runner diameter to shot weight and wall thickness (developed from moulding experience and fluid mechanics) is:

$$D = 0.264 \cdot t^{0.5} \cdot W^{0.5}$$

where:
- D = runner diameter in mm
- t = nominal wall thickness in mm
- W = shot weight (total, including runners) in grams

### Step 2: Substitute the Wall Thickness Exponent

The wall thickness factor is:

$$t^{0.5} = (2.5)^{0.5} = \sqrt{2.5} = 1.5811$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Shot Weight Exponent

The shot weight factor is:

$$W^{0.5} = (200)^{0.5} = \sqrt{200} = 14.1421$$

### Step 4: Multiply All Factors

$$D = 0.264 \times 1.5811 \times 14.1421$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$0.264 \times 1.5811 = 0.41741$$

$$0.41741 \times 14.1421 = 5.9034 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Apply Engineering Standard Rounding

In practice, runner diameters are standardised to the nearest **0.5 mm** or use standard drill sizes. So:

$$D \approx 6.0 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6: Sanity Check via Runner-to-Wall Ratio

A common sanity check is the **runner-to-wall thickness ratio**:

$$\frac{D}{t} = \frac{6.0}{2.5} = 2.4$$

This is within the recommended range of **2 to 3** for general-purpose moulding. ✓

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{D \approx 6.0 \text{ mm}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Drop Across a Runner (Hagen–Poiseuille)

### Background

The melt in a runner behaves as a **highly viscous, non-Newtonian fluid in laminar flow**. For laminar flow through a circular pipe, the Hagen–Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$$

Rearranging for pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$$

### Numerical Problem

A polypropylene (PP) melt is flowing through a runner of:
- Diameter: D = 6 mm = 0.006 m
- Length: L = 80 mm = 0.080 m
- Apparent viscosity: μ = 200 Pa·s
- Volumetric flow rate: Q = 4 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s

### Step 1: Substitute the Values

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \times 200 \times 0.080 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (0.006)^4}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Numerator

$$128 \times 200 = 25{,}600$$

$$25{,}600 \times 0.080 = 2{,}048$$

$$2{,}048 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 8.192 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Denominator

$$\pi \times (0.006)^4 = \pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-9} = 4.0715 \times 10^{-9}$$

### Step 4: Divide

$$\Delta P = \frac{8.192 \times 10^{-3}}{4.0715 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.012 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 5: Convert to MPa and Bar

$$\Delta P = 2.012 \text{ MPa} = 20.12 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 6: Comment on Acceptability

A pressure drop of **2.0 MPa** in the runner is acceptable (typically, total runner + gate pressure drop should be **< 30% of injection pressure**). If injection pressure is 80 MPa, then 2 MPa represents only **2.5%** → runner is well-sized. ✓

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{\Delta P_{runner} \approx 2.01 \text{ MPa}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shear Rate in a Rectangular Edge Gate

### Why This Matters
Excessive shear rate in the gate causes **molecular orientation, residual stress, and warpage** in the moulded part. Most thermoplastics have a **safe upper limit of shear rate ≈ 50,000 s⁻¹** (above which degradation begins).

### Formula for Shear Rate in a Rectangular Channel

For a rectangular channel of width W and height (thickness) h, assuming W >> h (wide-slit approximation):

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{6 Q}{W h^2}$$

### Numerical Problem

An edge gate has:
- Width: W = 6 mm = 0.006 m
- Thickness (height): h = 1.5 mm = 0.0015 m
- Volumetric flow rate: Q = 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s

### Step 1: Substitute

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.006 \times (0.0015)^2}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Denominator

$$(0.0015)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$0.006 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.35 \times 10^{-8}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Numerator

$$6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6} = 7.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

### Step 4: Divide

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-6}}{1.35 \times 10^{-8}} = 533.33 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

### Step 5: Compare with Recommended Range

The recommended shear rate in a gate is typically **10,000 to 50,000 s⁻¹** for most thermoplastics. Our value of **533 s⁻¹** is **too low** — the gate is oversized; the melt will not generate enough frictional heating to flow smoothly, and there is a risk of **cold slugs and hesitation**.

### Step 6: Recommend a Redesign

To achieve a target shear rate of **30,000 s⁻¹**, the required gate thickness is:

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{6 Q}{W \dot{\gamma}_{target}}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.006 \times 30{,}000}}$$

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{7.2 \times 10^{-6}}{180}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{-8}} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.2 \text{ mm}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{gate} = 533 \text{ s}^{-1} \text{ (undersized shear)} \implies h_{new} = 0.2 \text{ mm}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cold Runner Material Wastage %

### Problem
A 4-cavity mould produces a part weighing 45 g (each). The runner system has:
- Main runner: 1 piece, length 80 mm, diameter 6 mm
- Branch runners: 4 pieces, length 40 mm each, diameter 5 mm
- Cold sprue: 1 piece, length 50 mm, taper 4° to 2°

Polypropylene density: ρ = 0.905 g/cm³

### Step 1: Volume of Main Runner

$$V_{main} = \frac{\pi (6)^2}{4} \times 80 = 2{,}261.95 \text{ mm}^3 = 2.262 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 2: Volume of 4 Branch Runners

$$V_{branch,total} = 4 \times \frac{\pi (5)^2}{4} \times 40 = 4 \times 196.35 \times 40$$

Wait — let me correct the arithmetic:

$$V_{branch,1} = \frac{\pi (5)^2}{4} \times 40 = 19.635 \times 40 = 785.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$V_{branch,total} = 4 \times 785.4 = 3{,}141.6 \text{ mm}^3 = 3.142 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 3: Volume of Sprue (Average Diameter)

For a tapered sprue with entry diameter 6 mm and exit diameter 4 mm, average diameter = 5 mm:

$$V_{sprue} = \frac{\pi (5)^2}{4} \times 50 = 19.635 \times 50 = 981.75 \text{ mm}^3 = 0.982 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 4: Total Runner Volume

$$V_{runner,total} = 2.262 + 3.142 + 0.982 = 6.386 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 5: Runner Mass

$$m_{runner} = \rho \times V = 0.905 \times 6.386 = 5.78 \text{ g}$$

### Step 6: Total Shot Mass

$$m_{shot} = m_{part,total} + m_{runner} = (4 \times 45) + 5.78 = 185.78 \text{ g}$$

### Step 7: Wastage Percentage

$$\% W = \frac{m_{runner}}{m_{shot}} \times 100 = \frac{5.78}{185.78} \times 100 = 3.11\%$$

### Step 8: Interpretation

A wastage of **3.11%** is **excellent** for a cold runner system (typical range 20–50% for cold runners; <5% is industry-leading). This indicates a very efficient runner design.

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{\% W_{wastage} = 3.11\% \text{ (excellent cold runner efficiency)}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Design Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Effect on Fill Quality | Effect on Cycle Time | Effect on Cost | KTU Keyword |
|---------------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------|-------------|
| Increase runner diameter | Lower ΔP, easier fill | Longer cooling | More wastage, more material | "Pressure drop reduction" |
| Decrease gate thickness | Higher shear, better surface | Faster freeze-off | Lower part weight | "Shear rate optimisation" |
| Switch to hot runner | No runner cooling needed | Shorter cycle | Higher tool cost | "Wastage elimination" |
| Increase number of cavities | Higher productivity | Same cycle time | Mould becomes more complex | "Family mould balancing" |
| Use balanced runner | Uniform part quality | Same cycle time | Higher runner volume | "Cavity fill synchronisation" |

> [!NOTE]

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Balanced vs Unbalanced Runner Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sprue Inlet] --> B{Configuration}

    B -->|Balanced Runner| C[Main Runner Common Trunk]
    C --> D1[Branch Length L1 Equal]
    C --> D2[Branch Length L2 Equal L1]
    C --> D3[Branch Length L3 Equal L1]
    D1 --> E1[Cavity 1]
    D2 --> E2[Cavity 2]
    D3 --> E3[Cavity 3]

    B -->|Unbalanced Runner| F[Main Runner Common Trunk]
    F --> G1[Short Branch L1 Short]
    F --> G2[Medium Branch L2 Longer]
    F --> G3[Long Branch L3 Longest]
    G1 --> H1[Cavity 1 Fills First]
    G2 --> H2[Cavity 2 Fills Second]
    G3 --> H3[Cavity 3 Fills Last Risk of Short Shot]

    classDef input fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    classDef balanced fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef unbalanced fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    classDef cavity fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000

    class A,B input
    class C,D1,D2,D3,E1,E2,E3 balanced
    class F,G1,G2,G3,H1,H2,H3 unbalanced
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In the **balanced layout**, all three branches have identical length from the main trunk to their respective gates, ensuring simultaneous cavity filling. In the **unbalanced layout**, the cavity nearest the sprue fills first, the farthest fills last — a classic cause of **weld lines** and **inconsistent part quality** in multi-cavity moulds.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a runner and a gate in an injection mould. What is the primary functional difference between them?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

A **runner** is the channel in the mould that conveys the molten plastic from the sprue to the gate. Its primary function is **distribution** of the melt to one or more cavities. It is typically a circular cross-section of diameter **4–10 mm**.

A **gate** is the small, narrow restriction that forms the final entry point of melt into the mould cavity. Its primary function is **flow control** — regulating shear rate, fill time, and freeze-off behaviour. It is the smallest cross-section in the feed system, with thickness typically **0.5–0.8 × part wall thickness**.

**Key difference:** The runner *distributes*; the gate *controls*. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any three design rules for gate land length in injection moulding.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

1. **Gate land length = (1.5 to 2.0) × gate thickness.** This prevents premature freeze-off and ensures the gate remains open during the packing phase. **(1 mark)**

2. **Minimum land length = 0.5 mm** for most thermoplastics, to avoid gate wash and hesitation. **(1 mark)**

3. **Maximum land length should not exceed 2 × gate thickness** because longer lands cause excessive frictional heating, drool, and material degradation. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the different types of gates used in injection moulding. State the applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 4-cavity injection mould, the part is a flat rectangular plate of dimensions 80 mm × 60 mm × 2.5 mm thick. The runner system is cold and balanced. The moulding material is polypropylene (density 0.905 g/cm³). Design:
- (i) The main runner diameter using the empirical formula.
- (ii) The edge gate thickness and width.
- (iii) The percentage material wastage in the runner system, assuming main runner length 100 mm, four branch runners each 50 mm long, and one sprue of length 60 mm with average diameter 5 mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

The following gate types are commonly used:

| Gate Type | Sketch / Description | Application | Marks |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Edge Gate** | Rectangular gate cut on the edge of the part, land perpendicular to flow | Most common general-purpose gate; used for flat parts, boxes, housings | 1 |
| **Fan Gate** | Wide, fan-shaped entry; thickness decreases gradually from runner to cavity | Large flat parts (lids, plates, discs) to avoid jetting and weld lines | 1 |
| **Tab Gate** | Edge gate with a small tab extending from the part; gate cut is on the tab | Cosmetic parts (appliance housings) where gate vestige must be hidden from view | 1 |
| **Sprue Gate (Direct)** | Sprue feeds directly into the part; no separate runner | Single-cavity moulds, thick-walled parts, large parts | 1 |
| **Pin Gate (Tunnel)** | Small circular gate (0.5–1.5 mm) drilled at an angle; gate vestige is a pinpoint | Multi-cavity moulds, small parts, automatic degating | 1 |
| **Submarine Gate** | Pin gate whose channel lies entirely below the parting line | Parts where gate mark must be invisible on the visible surface | 1 |
| **Diaphragm Gate** | Cylindrical disc of melt enters the part concentrically | Lens covers, gear blanks, parts requiring radial flow symmetry | 1 |
| **Ring Gate** | Continuous ring-shaped channel around the part | Tubular parts, pipes, bushings | 1 |

> Any 4–5 types with sketches and applications are sufficient for 7 marks.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Part dimensions: 80 mm × 60 mm × 2.5 mm
- Number of cavities: n = 4
- Material: Polypropylene, ρ = 0.905 g/cm³
- Runner lengths: main = 100 mm, branch = 4 × 50 mm, sprue = 60 mm
- Average sprue diameter = 5 mm

**Step (i): Main runner diameter**

Mass of one part:

$$m_{part} = 8 \times 6 \times 0.25 \text{ cm} \times 0.905 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 10.86 \text{ g}$$

Wait — let me convert carefully. 80 mm × 60 mm × 2.5 mm = 80 × 60 × 2.5 = 12,000 mm³ = 12 cm³.

$$m_{part,1} = 12 \times 0.905 = 10.86 \text{ g}$$

Mass of 4 parts:

$$m_{parts,total} = 4 \times 10.86 = 43.44 \text{ g}$$

Estimated runner mass (assume ~20% of part mass for cold runner):

$$m_{runner,est} = 0.20 \times 43.44 = 8.69 \text{ g}$$

Total shot weight:

$$W = 43.44 + 8.69 = 52.13 \text{ g}$$

Using empirical formula:

$$D = 0.264 \cdot t^{0.5} \cdot W^{0.5}$$

$$D = 0.264 \times (2.5)^{0.5} \times (52.13)^{0.5}$$

$$D = 0.264 \times 1.5811 \times 7.2194 = 3.013 \text{ mm}$$

Round to standard drill size: **D ≈ 3.0 mm** (or use 3.2 mm) **(2 marks)**

**Step (ii): Gate thickness and width**

Wall thickness t = 2.5 mm.

$$h_{gate} = 0.7 \times t = 0.7 \times 2.5 = 1.75 \text{ mm} \approx 1.8 \text{ mm}$$

$$W_{gate} = 4 \times h_{gate} = 4 \times 1.75 = 7.0 \text{ mm}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step (iii): Material wastage %**

Main runner volume:

$$V_{main} = \frac{\pi (3.0)^2}{4} \times 100 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^3 = 0.707 \text{ cm}^3$$

Branch runners total volume (assume branch diameter = main = 3.0 mm for balanced):

$$V_{branch,total} = 4 \times \frac{\pi (3.0)^2}{4} \times 50 = 4 \times 353.43 = 1{,}413.7 \text{ mm}^3 = 1.414 \text{ cm}^3$$

Sprue volume:

$$V_{sprue} = \frac{\pi (5)^2}{4} \times 60 = 1{,}178.1 \text{ mm}^3 = 1.178 \text{ cm}^3$$

Total runner volume:

$$V_{runner,total} = 0.707 + 1.414 + 1.178 = 3.299 \text{ cm}^3$$

Runner mass:

$$m_{runner} = 3.299 \times 0.905 = 2.985 \text{ g}$$

Total shot mass:

$$m_{shot} = 43.44 + 2.985 = 46.43 \text{ g}$$

Wastage percentage:

$$\% W = \frac{2.985}{46.43} \times 100 = 6.43\%$$ **(3 marks)**

**Final Answer for (b):**
- Main runner diameter: **3.0 mm**
- Gate thickness: **1.75 mm**, Gate width: **7.0 mm**
- Material wastage: **6.43%** (Excellent for cold runner)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a balanced runner system in a multi-cavity injection mould. How is the balance achieved? What are its advantages and limitations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hot runner injection mould produces a part with the following data:
- Volumetric flow rate: Q = 8 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s
- Runner diameter: D = 5 mm
- Runner length: L = 60 mm
- Melt apparent viscosity: μ = 150 Pa·s

Calculate:
- (i) The pressure drop across the runner.
- (ii) The shear rate in the runner.
- (iii) Comment on whether the pressure drop is acceptable if the injection pressure is 70 MPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **balanced runner system** is one in which all cavities in a multi-cavity mould receive molten material at the **same pressure, same temperature, and at the same time**. This is achieved by making the **runner path length from the sprue to each gate identical**.

**How Balance is Achieved (2 marks):**
- The main runner from the sprue is a common trunk.
- Branch runners of equal length branch off symmetrically (e.g., in an "H", "X", or radial pattern).
- Each branch terminates in a gate of identical size (since flow is identical).
- The sprue is positioned such that all branches have the same length.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Uniform part quality** — all cavities produce identical parts, no short shots or flash.
2. **Lower reject rate** → higher yield, lower cost per part.
3. **Easier process setup** — once a balanced mould is tuned, it runs consistently.
4. **Lower injection pressure** requirement because no cavity is over-fed.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. **Larger mould footprint** — symmetry requires more space.
2. **Higher runner volume** — more material is held in runners.
3. **Constraint on cavity layout** — asymmetric parts cannot be perfectly balanced.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** Q = 8 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s, D = 5 mm = 0.005 m, L = 60 mm = 0.060 m, μ = 150 Pa·s, P_inj = 70 MPa.

**Step (i): Pressure Drop (Hagen–Poiseuille)**

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \times 150 \times 0.060 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (0.005)^4}$$

Numerator: $128 \times 150 = 19{,}200$; $\times 0.060 = 1{,}152$; $\times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 9.216 \times 10^{-3}$.

Denominator: $(0.005)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{-10}$; $\times \pi = 1.9635 \times 10^{-9}$.

$$\Delta P = \frac{9.216 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.694 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 4.69 \text{ MPa}$$ **(3 marks)**

**Step (ii): Shear Rate in Runner**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$$

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{32 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (0.005)^3}$$

Numerator: $32 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 2.56 \times 10^{-4}$.

Denominator: $(0.005)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-7}$; $\times \pi = 3.927 \times 10^{-7}$.

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{-4}}{3.927 \times 10^{-7}} = 652.0 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step (iii): Comment on Acceptability**

Pressure drop as percentage of injection pressure:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_{inj}} \times 100 = \frac{4.69}{70} \times 100 = 6.7\%$$

A runner pressure drop of **6.7%** of injection pressure is **excellent** (industry guideline: total runner + gate pressure drop should be **< 30%** of injection pressure). The runner is well-sized.

However, the shear rate of **652 s⁻¹** is **low** for a hot runner (typical range 1,000–10,000 s⁻¹). The runner could be downsized to D ≈ 3.0 mm to increase shear rate and reduce material residence time, which is especially important in hot runners (long residence time causes thermal degradation). **(2 marks)**

**Final Answer for (b):**
- Pressure drop: **4.69 MPa**
- Shear rate: **652 s⁻¹**
- Pressure drop is acceptable; runner diameter could be reduced to improve shear rate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Exams on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Hagen–Poiseuille:** Students often mix mm and m in the denominator. Always convert D and L to **metres** when using SI units for μ (Pa·s). The D⁴ term amplifies errors 4-fold.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Wall-Thickness Correction:** The empirical runner formula $D = 0.264 \cdot t^{0.5} \cdot W^{0.5}$ REQUIRES the t⁰·⁵ factor. Using only $D = 0.264 \cdot W^{0.5}$ for thin-wall parts (t < 1.5 mm) gives an oversized runner and costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Gate Thickness vs Wall Thickness:** Writing $h_{gate} = t_{wall}$ instead of $h_{gate} = 0.5 \text{ to } 0.8 \times t_{wall}$ is a common error. Examiners explicitly deduct for this.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the 1.5–2 × Rule for Gate Land:** Drawing a gate without specifying the land length ratio loses 1 mark in sketches.
>
> 5. **Wastage % Calculation:** Forgetting to include the **sprue volume** in $V_{runner,total}$ is a frequent mistake. Sprue typically contributes 20–30% of total runner volume.
>
> 6. **Confusing Balanced and Hot Runners:** Hot runner ≠ balanced runner. They are orthogonal concepts. Hot/cold refers to temperature state; balanced/unbalanced refers to layout.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Bulletins — KTU 2024 Module 4 — Runner and Gate Design**

- [ ] **Runner** = distribution channel; **Gate** = flow-control restriction. Gate is the **smallest cross-section**.
- [ ] Empirical runner diameter: $D = 0.264 \cdot t^{0.5} \cdot W^{0.5}$ (D in mm, t in mm, W in g).
- [ ] Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop: $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ — always SI units (Pa, m, m³/s).
- [ ] Shear rate in circular runner: $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^3} = \dfrac{8 v}{D}$.
- [ ] Shear rate in rectangular gate: $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{6 Q}{W h^2}$.
- [ ] Recommended **shear rate range:** 1,000–10,000 s⁻¹ in runners; 10,000–50,000 s⁻¹ in gates.
- [ ] **Gate land length = 1.5 to 2.0 × gate thickness.**
- [ ] **Gate thickness = 0.5 to 0.8 × part wall thickness** (commonly 2/3 rule).
- [ ] **Gate width = 3 to 6 × gate thickness** for edge gates.
- [ ] **Maximum runner L/D ratio ≤ 8** to control pressure drop.
- [ ] **Cold runner wastage:** 20–50%; **Hot runner wastage:** 2–5%.
- [ ] **Balanced runner:** all branches equal length → simultaneous cavity fill.
- [ ] **Unbalanced runner:** natural layout → near-cavity over-fed, far-cavity short-shot risk.
- [ ] **Sprue taper:** 1° to 1.5° per side for easy ejection; cold slug well at sprue base.
- [ ] **Pin gate diameter:** 0.5–1.5 mm; used for automatic degating.
- [ ] **Fan gate:** for large flat parts; thickness gradually tapers to wall thickness.
- [ ] **Diaphragm/ring gate:** for parts requiring concentric, uniform radial flow.
- [ ] **Acceptable runner pressure drop:** < 30% of total injection pressure.
- [ ] **Melt flow is laminar** in mould runners (Re ≈ 1–100).
- [ ] For non-Newtonian melts, use **apparent viscosity** μ_a in Hagen–Poiseuille equation.
- [ ] **Mould filling time** (Stefan): $t_{fill} = \dfrac{2 L^2 \eta}{P h}$.
- [ ] **Hot runners** eliminate runner scrap but require precise temperature control (heater bands, thermocouples, manifold).
- [ ] **Defect → design cause:** Weld lines → unbalanced runner or low gate temp; Short shot → low pressure or undersized runner; Flash → over-packed cavity or unbalanced runner; Sink marks → gate too thick or packing time insufficient.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mould Cooling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mould Cooling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mould Cooling** is the controlled removal of heat from a mould (or die) using a circulating coolant fluid through an engineered network of channels, in order to solidify/cool the moulded/cast part uniformly, regulate cycle time, maintain dimensional accuracy, and ensure consistent mechanical properties of the finished product. In the context of **PCPET602 (Metal Forming and Tool Design)**, mould cooling is a critical subsystem of tooling used in processes such as **injection moulding, die casting, hot forging, and die pressing**. It governs: - **Cycle time optimization** (productivity) - **Part warpage control** (dimensional stability) - **Residual stress minimization** (mechanical integrity) - **Tool life extension** (thermal fatigue resistance) - **Surface finish consistency** (aesthetic quality) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body's Cooling System Imagine your body is a mould that constantly generates heat when working hard (running). Your body uses **blood circulation** — a fluid pumped through a network of vessels (channels) near the skin — to carry heat away and release it to the environment. Similarly, a mould: - **Generates heat** when molten/soft metal fills the cavity. - **Uses a coolant** (water, oil, or a glycol mixture) as the "blood." - **Circulates it** through drilled channels (the "vessels") inside the mould. - **Rejects heat** to an external chiller or cooling tower (the "environment"). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** If cooling is uneven (one side fast, one side slow), the part cools unequally — just like a fever localized to one part of your body leads to discomfort. Uniform, well-designed cooling = uniform, defect-free parts. ### 1.3 Core Engineering Metrics - **Coolant inlet temperature (T_in):** typically **20 °C to 30 °C** for water. - **Coolant outlet temperature (T_out):** generally **5 °C to 10 °C** above T_in. - **Coolant flow regime:** **Turbulent (Re > 10,000)** is preferred for high heat transfer. - **Cooling time (t_c):** typically **30% to 70%** of total cycle time. - **Heat transfer coefficient (h):** ranges from **200 to 50,000 W/m²·K** depending on flow regime and coolant. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome for this topic emphasizes the student's ability to **design cooling layouts, compute cooling time, and select appropriate coolants** for forming dies and moulds. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Objectives of an Efficient Mould Cooling System 1. **Rapid heat extraction** to minimize solidification/cooling time. 2. **Uniform temperature distribution** across the mould surface to prevent warpage. 3. **Maintain steady-state thermal conditions** at the mould-working surface. 4. **Prevent localized hot spots** that cause part sticking, sink marks, or die failure. 5. **Allow easy integration** with mould manufacturing (drilling, EDM, or additive processes for conformal cooling). ### 2.2 Fundamental Modes of Heat Transfer in Mould Cooling Heat from the hot moulded part travels to the coolant via three sequential steps: **Step 1 — Conduction (Solid Mould Body)** Heat flows from the cavity surface, through the bulk mould steel, to the inner wall of the cooling channel. $$q_{cond} = \frac{k_m \cdot A \cdot (T_{cavity} - T_{channel\ wall})}{L}$$ Where: - $k_m$ = thermal conductivity of mould steel (~**29 W/m·K** for P20, **25 W/m·K** for H13). - $A$ = heat transfer area (m²). - $L$ = distance from cavity to channel centerline (m). - $T_{cavity}$, $T_{channel\ wall}$ = temperatures in Kelvin (K). **Step 2 — Convection (Channel Wall to Coolant)** The dominant resistance — heat is carried away by the moving coolant. $$q_{conv} = h \cdot A_c \cdot (T_{wall} - T_{bulk\ coolant})$$ Where: - $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K). - $A_c$ = internal channel surface area (m²). **Step 3 — Bulk Transport (Coolant Flow)** Sensible heat is carried by the moving fluid to the chiller. $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ Where: - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of coolant (kg/s). - $c_p$ = specific heat of coolant (~**4186 J/kg·K** for water). ### 2.3 Reynolds Number and Flow Regime The heat transfer coefficient $h$ is highly sensitive to whether the flow is **laminar** or **turbulent**. $$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{v \cdot D}{\nu}$$ Where: - $\rho$ = coolant density (kg/m³). - $v$ = mean coolant velocity (m/s). - $D$ = channel hydraulic diameter (m). - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s). - $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s). **Flow Regime Boundaries:** | Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Heat Transfer Efficiency | |---|---|---| | **Laminar** | $Re < 2300$ | Low ($h \approx 100\text{–}500$ W/m²·K) | | **Transitional** | $2300 \le Re \le 10{,}000$ | Unstable, avoid in design | | **Turbulent** | $Re > 10{,}000$ | High ($h \approx 5000\text{–}50{,}000$ W/m²·K) | ### 2.4 Nusselt Number Correlations The Nusselt number links $h$ to coolant properties: $$Nu = \frac{h \cdot D}{k_f}$$ Where $k_f$ = thermal conductivity of the fluid (W/m·K). **For Turbulent Flow in Smooth Circular Tubes (Dittus-Boelter Equation):** $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{n}$$ - $n = 0.4$ when the fluid is being **heated**. - $n = 0.3$ when the fluid is being **cooled**. - $Pr$ = Prandtl number (dimensionless). **For Laminar Flow in Circular Tubes with Constant Wall Temperature:** $$Nu = 3.66 \quad \text{(fully developed, infinite length)}$$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat removal by coolant | W | | 2 | $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Overall heat transfer | W | | 3 | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 4 | $Nu = h D / k_f$ | Nusselt number | dimensionless | | 5 | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter (turbulent) | dimensionless | | 6 | $t_c = \frac{s^2}{2 \alpha} \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}}\right)$ | 1D cooling time (Carslaw-Jaeger) | s | | 7 | $t_c = B \cdot (V/A)^n$ | Chvorinov's rule | s | | 8 | $h = Nu \cdot k_f / D$ | Heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K | | 9 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach) | Pa | | 10 | $\dot{V} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s | **Critical Note on Pumping Power:** $$P_{pump} = \dot{V} \cdot \Delta P$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert $\Delta T$ to Kelvin. A temperature difference in °C is numerically equal to one in K, but the values **must be in absolute units (K)** when used in Stefan-Boltzmann or ideal gas relations. ### 2.6 Engineering Utility in Metal Forming - **Injection Moulding:** Cooling constitutes 50–70% of the cycle time. - **Die Casting:** Cooling channels prolong die life by reducing thermal shock. - **Hot Forging:** Internal cooling reduces die softening and heat-checking. - **Powder Compaction:** Cooled punches prevent part sticking and improve density. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Cooling Time for a Slab (1-D Transient Conduction) We consider a semi-infinite plate of thickness $2s$ (or thickness $s$ cooled from one side), initially at uniform temperature $T_m$, suddenly placed in contact with a mould wall at constant temperature $T_{wall}$, and we want the time $t_c$ for the centreline to reach ejection temperature $T_e$. **Step 1 — Governing Equation (Heat Equation in 1D):** $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$ Where: - $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity** of the moulded material (m²/s). - $t$ = time (s). - $x$ = spatial coordinate (m). **Step 2 — Boundary and Initial Conditions:** $$T(x, 0) = T_m \quad \text{(initially uniform)}$$ $$T(0, t) = T_{wall} \quad \text{(surface held at mould wall temperature)}$$ $$T(s, t) \text{ symmetrical / insulated, depending on geometry}$$ **Step 3 — Non-Dimensionalization:** Introduce dimensionless temperature: $$\theta = \frac{T - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}}$$ So the equation becomes: $$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 4 — Separation of Variables:** Assume a solution of the form: $$\theta(x, t) = X(x) \cdot \Gamma(t)$$ Substituting: $$X \cdot \Gamma' = \alpha \cdot X'' \cdot \Gamma$$ Dividing by $\alpha X \Gamma$: $$\frac{\Gamma'}{\alpha \Gamma} = \frac{X''}{X} = -\lambda^2$$ Where $-\lambda^2$ is the separation constant (negative because the solution is exponential decay). **Step 5 — Solve the Spatial Equation:** $$X'' + \lambda^2 X = 0$$ With $X(0) = 0$ and $X'(s) = 0$ (symmetry at the centre). $$X(x) = \sin(\lambda x)$$ Applying the second condition: $$\lambda_n = \frac{(2n+1)\pi}{2s}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ **Step 6 — Solve the Temporal Equation:** $$\Gamma_n(t) = C_n \exp\left(-\alpha \lambda_n^2 t\right)$$ **Step 7 — Superpose All Modes:** $$\theta(x, t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} C_n \sin(\lambda_n x) \exp(-\alpha \lambda_n^2 t)$$ Applying the initial condition $\theta(x, 0) = 1$: $$1 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} C_n \sin(\lambda_n x)$$ By Fourier series orthogonality: $$C_n = \frac{4}{(2n+1)\pi}$$ **Step 8 — Solution at the Centreline ($x = s$):** $$T(s, t) - T_{wall} = (T_m - T_{wall}) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} (-1)^n \exp\left(-\alpha \lambda_n^2 t\right)$$ **Step 9 — Approximate by First Term (for $t$ not too small):** $$T(s, t) - T_{wall} \approx (T_m - T_{wall}) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\left(-\alpha \frac{\pi^2}{4 s^2} t\right)$$ **Step 10 — Solve for Cooling Time:** Set $T(s, t_c) = T_e$ and solve for $t_c$: $$\frac{T_e - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}} = \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha}{4 s^2} t_c\right)$$ Rearranging: $$\exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha}{4 s^2} t_c\right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{T_e - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}}$$ Taking natural logarithm: $$-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha}{4 s^2} t_c = \ln\left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{T_e - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{t_c = \frac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}}\right)}$$ **Verification with Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Consider a plastic moulded slab of thickness $2s = 6$ mm ($s = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m), initially at $T_m = 220$ °C, ejected at $T_e = 90$ °C, mould wall at $T_{wall} = 40$ °C. The material thermal diffusivity is $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s. $$t_c = \frac{4 \cdot (0.003)^2}{\pi^2 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-7}} \ln\left(\frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{220 - 40}{90 - 40}\right)$$ Compute the argument of the logarithm: $$\frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{180}{50} = 1.273 \cdot 3.6 = 4.583$$ $$\ln(4.583) = 1.522$$ Compute the prefactor: $$\frac{4 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{9.8696 \times 10^{-7}} = 36.47 \text{ s}$$ Therefore: $$t_c = 36.47 \cdot 1.522 = 55.5 \text{ s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The cooling time scales with the **square of the wall thickness**. Doubling $s$ quadruples $t_c$. This is why thick sections of a moulded part dominate the cycle time. ### 3.2 Worked Problem — Coolant Flow and Heat Transfer > **Problem (KTU-style 14 mark):** Water at 25 °C flows through a circular cooling channel of diameter $D = 10$ mm at a velocity of $v = 2$ m/s. The mould wall temperature is 60 °C. Compute: > (a) Reynolds number and flow regime. > (b) Nusselt number using Dittus-Boelter. > (c) Convective heat transfer coefficient. > (d) Heat removed per metre of channel length. **Given Properties of Water at ~40 °C (mean temperature):** - $\rho = 992$ kg/m³ - $\mu = 6.53 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s - $k_f = 0.628$ W/m·K - $c_p = 4178$ J/kg·K - $Pr = 4.34$ **Part (a): Reynolds Number** $$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{992 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.010}{6.53 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{19.84}{6.53 \times 10^{-4}} = 30{,}383$$ Since $Re > 10{,}000$, the flow is **turbulent**. **Part (b): Nusselt Number (Dittus-Boelter, fluid being heated, $n = 0.4$):** $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$$ $$Re^{0.8} = 30{,}383^{0.8}$$ Compute step by step: $$\ln(30{,}383) = 10.322$$ $$0.8 \cdot 10.322 = 8.257$$ $$e^{8.257} = 3845$$ $$Pr^{0.4} = 4.34^{0.4}$$ $$\ln(4.34) = 1.468$$ $$0.4 \cdot 1.468 = 0.587$$ $$e^{0.587} = 1.799$$ $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot 3845 \cdot 1.799 = 159.1$$ **Part (c): Heat Transfer Coefficient** $$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_f}{D} = \frac{159.1 \cdot 0.628}{0.010} = 9992 \text{ W/m²·K}$$ **Part (d): Heat Removed per Metre of Channel** Channel surface area per metre: $$A_c = \pi D L = \pi \cdot 0.010 \cdot 1 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$ Using $\Delta T$ between wall and bulk coolant (mean coolant temp ≈ 25 °C, wall = 60 °C): $$q' = h \cdot A_c \cdot (T_{wall} - T_{coolant}) = 9992 \cdot 0.0314 \cdot (60 - 25)$$ $$q' = 9992 \cdot 0.0314 \cdot 35 = 10{,}982 \text{ W/m}$$ > **Answer:** Approximately **11 kW per metre** of channel length. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mould Cooling System This Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** of the mould cooling loop — the components, energy flow, and control hierarchy. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hot Mould Cavity
Molten or Soft Metal] -->|Heat Conduction| B[Mould Steel Body
k_m approx 29 W/mK] B -->|Convection Resistance| C[Cooling Channel Inner Wall
T_wall] C -->|Forced Convection h| D[Coolant Flow
Turbulent Re greater than 10000] D -->|Mass Transport| E[Outlet Manifold
T_out 30 to 35 C] E -->|Pipe Network| F[Chiller or Cooling Tower
Heat Rejection] F -->|Pump P_pump| G[Inlet Manifold
T_in 20 to 25 C] G -->|Distribution| D D -->|Pressure Drop DP| H[Pressure Regulator
and Flow Meter] H -->|Feedback Signal| I[PLC Controller
Cycle Optimization] I -->|Valve Actuation| G style A fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style B fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style C fill:#ffff99,stroke:#cccc00,color:#000000 style D fill:#99ccff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 style E fill:#99ccff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 style F fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 style G fill:#99ccff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 style H fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333,color:#000000 style I fill:#ff99ff,stroke:#660066,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 Cooling Channel Layout Strategies ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Cooling Channel
Design Types] --> B[Straight Drilled Holes
Simple Low Cost] A --> C[Baffles and Baffles with Inserts
Redirect Flow Near Cavity] A --> D[Bubbler Tubes
Spray Cooling Deep Cores] A --> E[Conformal Cooling
3D Printed Curved Channels] A --> F[Heat Pipes
Passive High Conductivity] B --> B1[Limitation: Hot Spots
Between Drilled Lines] C --> C1[Advantage: Uniform
Near Flat Cavity] D --> D1[Advantage: Localized
High Heat Flux] E --> E1[Advantage: Complex
Geometry Uniformity] F --> F1[Advantage: No External
Pumping Needed] style A fill:#ffe699,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Coolant Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: Choose Coolant] --> Q1{Temperature Range?} Q1 -->|Below 100 C| Q2{Pressure Available?} Q1 -->|100 to 250 C| Oil[Use Thermal Oil] Q1 -->|Above 250 C| Salt[Use Molten Salt
or Air Cooling] Q2 -->|Yes High Pressure| Water[Use Water
Best h, Low Cost] Q2 -->|No Low Pressure| Glycol[Use Water Glycol Mix
Lower Freezing Point] Water --> Check1[Verify Corrosion
and Scaling] Glycol --> Check2[Verify Viscosity
and Prandtl] Oil --> Check3[Verify Flash Point
and Pump Compatibility] Salt --> Check4[Verify Material
Compatibility with Mould] style Start fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style Oil fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style Salt fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style Water fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style Glycol fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "convective heat transfer coefficient" in the context of mould cooling. State the typical range of values for water flowing in turbulent regime.** **Model Answer:** > The convective heat transfer coefficient ($h$) is the proportionality constant between the heat flux at a solid-fluid interface and the temperature difference between the wall and the bulk fluid. > > Mathematically: $q'' = h \cdot (T_{wall} - T_{bulk})$. > > For water in **turbulent flow** through a cooling channel, $h$ typically ranges from **5,000 to 50,000 W/m²·K**, depending on velocity, channel diameter, and fluid properties. Higher velocities give higher $h$ values because of enhanced mixing in the boundary layer. **(3 marks)** **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is Chvorinov's rule? Write the equation and explain the significance of the mould constant $B$.** **Model Answer:** > Chvorinov's rule states that the **solidification (or cooling) time** of a casting or moulded part is proportional to the **square of the volume-to-surface-area ratio** $(V/A)$: > > $$t_c = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$ > > Where $n \approx 2$ and $B$ is the **mould constant**, which depends on the material properties (thermal diffusivity, latent heat), superheat, and mould material. $B$ is **experimentally determined** for a given mould-part combination. > > **Significance:** A higher $V/A$ (thicker part) → longer cooling time. Designers can predict cycle times and identify the regions that will dominate cycle time. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Long Answer** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **mould cooling system** of an injection mould. Explain the **functions of baffles, bubblers, and conformal cooling** with their specific applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **polypropylene slab** of thickness $12$ mm is moulded at $T_m = 230$ °C and must be ejected at $T_e = 100$ °C. The mould surface temperature is $T_{wall} = 35$ °C. The thermal diffusivity of polypropylene is $\alpha = 8.5 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/s.
