Compute the **cooling time** using the 1D transient conduction formula. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** **Part (a) — Mould Cooling System Description (7 marks)** A typical injection mould cooling system consists of: 1. **Coolant inlet manifold** — receives chilled water from the chiller. 2. **Drilled channels** — typically 8–12 mm diameter, located 1–1.5 × channel diameter from the cavity surface. 3. **Baffles** — flat plates inserted into the channel to redirect flow closer to the cavity side, eliminating stagnant zones and improving local heat transfer. 4. **Bubblers** — hollow tubes with a small discharge hole that deliver a jet of coolant into deep core regions (e.g., long cores, internal features) that are inaccessible to drilled channels. 5. **Conformal cooling channels** — produced by **3D printing (DMLS/SLM)** of the mould insert, allowing channels to follow the contour of the cavity. This provides **uniform cooling** even on complex geometries. 6. **Outlet manifold** — collects heated coolant and returns it to the chiller. 7. **Temperature and pressure sensors** — for closed-loop control. **Functions:** - **Baffles:** redirect flow, increase velocity near cavity, prevent stagnation. *Application: large flat cavities.* - **Bubblers:** provide spot cooling in deep core pins. *Application: internal cores in pipe fittings.* - **Conformal cooling:** uniform temperature, reduced warpage, 20–40% cycle time reduction. *Application: medical devices, optics.* **(7 marks — 2 for sketch, 3 for description, 2 for functions)** **Part (b) — Cooling Time Calculation (7 marks)** Given: - Thickness $2s = 12$ mm $\Rightarrow s = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m - $T_m = 230$ °C, $T_e = 100$ °C, $T_{wall} = 35$ °C - $\alpha = 8.5 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/s Formula (derived in Section 3.1): $$t_c = \frac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}}\right)$$ **Step 1 — Compute the temperature ratio:** $$\frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}} = \frac{230 - 35}{100 - 35} = \frac{195}{65} = 3.0$$ **Step 2 — Compute the logarithmic argument:** $$\frac{4}{\pi} \cdot 3.0 = 1.2732 \cdot 3.0 = 3.8197$$ $$\ln(3.8197) = 1.3403$$ **Step 3 — Compute the prefactor:** $$\frac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \frac{4 \cdot (0.006)^2}{\pi^2 \cdot 8.5 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{4 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{9.8696 \cdot 8.5 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$= \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{8.389 \times 10^{-7}} = 171.65 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute cooling time:** $$t_c = 171.65 \cdot 1.3403 = 230.1 \text{ s}$$ > **Answer:** The cooling time is approximately **230 seconds (≈ 3.83 minutes)**. **(7 marks — 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for unit/final answer)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Long Answer** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the **heat transfer mechanisms** in mould cooling. Derive the **Reynolds number** equation and discuss the importance of maintaining **turbulent flow**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Water at $T_{in} = 25$ °C enters a circular cooling channel of diameter $D = 8$ mm at velocity $v = 1.5$ m/s. The mould wall temperature is 55 °C. The bulk coolant outlet temperature is 32 °C. Given: $\rho = 996$ kg/m³, $\mu = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k_f = 0.615$ W/m·K, $c_p = 4178$ J/kg·K, $Pr = 5.4$. Compute: (i) Reynolds number and confirm flow regime. (ii) Nusselt number using Dittus-Boelter (fluid being cooled, $n = 0.3$). (iii) Convective heat transfer coefficient. (iv) Total heat removed per metre of channel using $\Delta T = (T_{out} - T_{in})$. Compare with the convective estimate. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **Part (a) — Heat Transfer Mechanisms & Reynolds Number (7 marks)** In mould cooling, three sequential heat transfer mechanisms occur: 1. **Conduction** through the mould steel from the cavity surface to the channel wall. Governed by Fourier's law: $q = -k A \, dT/dx$. 2. **Convection** from the channel inner wall to the moving coolant. Governed by Newton's law of cooling: $q = h A \Delta T$. 3. **Advection (bulk transport)** by the coolant itself: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$. **Reynolds Number Derivation:** Consider a fluid flowing through a circular pipe of diameter $D$. Define: - $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³) - $v$ = mean velocity (m/s) - $D$ = pipe diameter (m) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) The ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** is dimensionless: $$Re = \frac{F_{inertial}}{F_{viscous}} = \frac{\rho v^2 D^2}{\mu v D} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$ Where $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is the kinematic viscosity. **Importance of Turbulent Flow ($Re > 10{,}000$):** - Increases the convective heat transfer coefficient by **5× to 50×** compared to laminar flow. - Eliminates the thick thermal boundary layer, bringing cooler fluid closer to the wall. - Provides **uniform wall temperature** (smaller $\Delta T$ between inlet and outlet). - Prevents localized overheating and part warpage. However, turbulence increases **pumping power** ($P \propto v^3$ roughly), so an optimum exists (typically $Re = 10{,}000$ to $50{,}000$). **(7 marks — 2 for mechanisms, 3 for derivation, 2 for importance)** **Part (b) — Coolant Heat Transfer Calculation (7 marks)** **(i) Reynolds Number:** $$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{996 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 0.008}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{11.952}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 14{,}940$$ Since $Re > 10{,}000$, flow is **turbulent**. ✓ **(ii) Nusselt Number (Dittus-Boelter, fluid being cooled, $n = 0.3$):** $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.3}$$ Step by step: $$Re^{0.8} = 14{,}940^{0.8}$$ $$\ln(14{,}940) = 9.612, \quad 0.8 \cdot 9.612 = 7.690, \quad e^{7.690} = 2184$$ $$Pr^{0.3} = 5.4^{0.3}$$ $$\ln(5.4) = 1.686, \quad 0.3 \cdot 1.686 = 0.506, \quad e^{0.506} = 1.659$$ $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot 2184 \cdot 1.659 = 83.3$$ **(iii) Heat Transfer Coefficient:** $$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_f}{D} = \frac{83.3 \cdot 0.615}{0.008} = 6404 \text{ W/m²·K}$$ **(iv) Heat Removed per Metre:** Convective estimate (using $\Delta T = T_{wall} - T_{mean\ bulk} = 55 - 28.5 = 26.5$ °C): $$A_c = \pi D L = \pi \cdot 0.008 \cdot 1 = 0.02513 \text{ m}^2$$ $$q' = h \cdot A_c \cdot \Delta T = 6404 \cdot 0.02513 \cdot 26.5 = 4266 \text{ W/m}$$ Mass-flow estimate (using $\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} = 32 - 25 = 7$ °C): $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot v \cdot A_c = 996 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 0.02513 = 37.55 \text{ kg/s per metre... (corrected for unit length)}$$ For 1 m length: $\dot{V} = v \cdot A_c = 1.5 \cdot 0.02513 = 0.0377$ m³/s $$\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V} = 996 \cdot 0.0377 = 37.55 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 37.55 \cdot 4178 \cdot 7 = 1{,}098{,}350 \text{ W/m}$$ > **Comparison Note:** The convective estimate (4.3 kW/m) considers **local wall-to-fluid** heat transfer, while the bulk mass-flow estimate (1.1 MW/m) corresponds to the **total enthalpy carried by the fluid**. The two methods measure different physical quantities. The mass-flow method gives the total capacity, and the convective method gives the local heat flux at the wall. In practice, they must be balanced through proper channel length design. > **Final Answer:** > (i) $Re = 14{,}940$ (Turbulent) > (ii) $Nu = 83.3$ > (iii) $h = 6404$ W/m²·K > (iv) Convective: 4.3 kW/m; Mass-flow: 1.10 MW/m **(7 marks — 1 for (i), 2 for (ii), 2 for (iii), 2 for (iv))** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Mould Cooling Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in the cooling time formula — a 1000× error is the most common mistake. Always convert **all length units to metres** before substituting. > 2. **Using the wrong Dittus-Boelter exponent** ($n = 0.3$ for cooling, $n = 0.4$ for heating). Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this. > 3. **Confusing Reynolds number threshold** — some students write "turbulent" for $Re > 4000$. The accepted KTU answer is $Re > 10{,}000$. > 4. **Skipping units in the final answer** — always write units explicitly (W, W/m·K, s, etc.). > 5. **Not stating the assumptions** of the 1D cooling model (semi-infinite slab, constant wall temperature). Examiners award 1 mark just for stating these. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mould Cooling (Module 4)** - **Definition:** Controlled heat removal from mould using circulating coolant through engineered channels. - **Primary Goals:** Reduce cycle time, ensure uniform part quality, extend tool life, prevent warpage. - **Heat Transfer Path:** Conduction (steel) → Convection (channel) → Advection (coolant flow). - **Three Core Equations to Memorize:** - Cooling time: $t_c = \dfrac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{4}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_e - T_{wall}}\right)$ - Reynolds: $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ - Dittus-Boelter: $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ (heating) or $0.3$ (cooling) - **Turbulent Flow Required:** $Re > 10{,}000$ → $h$ increases dramatically. - **Cooling Time Scales:** $t_c \propto s^2$ — **doubling thickness quadruples time**. - **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_c = B (V/A)^2$ — volume-to-area ratio governs solidification. - **Channel Diameter Range:** Typically **8 to 12 mm** for injection moulds. - **Channel Placement:** **1 to 1.5 × diameter** from cavity surface. - **Coolant Temperature Range:** Inlet **20–30 °C**, Outlet **30–40 °C** (ΔT ≈ 5–10 °C). - **Coolant Selection Tree:** Water (best $h$, < 100 °C) → Water-glycol (low temp) → Thermal oil (100–250 °C) → Molten salt (> 250 °C). - **Cooling Strategies:** Drilled channels, baffles, bubblers, conformal cooling, heat pipes. - **Conformal Cooling:** 3D-printed channels following cavity geometry — reduces cycle time by **20–40%**. - **Common Coolant:** Water (high $c_p$, low viscosity, cheap, available). - **Bubbler Use Case:** Spot cooling deep core pins inaccessible to drilled channels. - **Baffle Use Case:** Redirect flow in large flat cavities to eliminate stagnation. - **Pump Power:** $P_{pump} = \dot{V} \cdot \Delta P$ — must be balanced against $h$ improvement. - **KTU Favourite Numerical:** Cooling time + Reynolds + Nusselt combo problem. - **Unit Vigilance:** Convert mm → m, °C → K (only if required by equation), kg/s must be consistent.

topics.title: Ejection Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ejection Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ejection** is the controlled mechanical separation of a forged, stamped, or formed workpiece from the die cavity immediately after the forming stroke is completed and the dies are opened. **Ejection methods** refer to the standardized set of mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or gravity-assisted devices built into the die assembly that deliver a precisely directed force to the workpiece, overcoming die adhesion, friction, and shrinkage grip, without causing part distortion or die damage.

In the context of **Impression Die Forging** and **Press Tool Design** (the focus of Module 4), ejection is a **critical sub-system** that bridges the forming operation and part-handling. The ejected component is called the **Knockout (KO) system**, and the device that lifts the forging off the lower die is the **Knockout**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Releasing a Cake from a Mould"

Imagine you have baked a dense, sticky chocolate cake in a deep, narrow bundt pan. Even after greasing, the cake stubbornly clings to the pan's grooves. You solve this by:
1. Tapping the pan upside down (→ **Gravity / Drop-Through Ejection**).
2. Pushing small rods up through holes in the pan bottom (→ **Knockout Pin Ejection**).
3. Using a flat plate that slides down the pan walls and pushes the cake out evenly (→ **Stripper Plate Ejection**).
4. Using a syringe of compressed air to pop it out (→ **Air / Pneumatic Ejection**).

A forging die faces the **same problem**, but with forces on the order of **hundreds to thousands of kilonewtons**, **red-hot workpieces (1100 °C – 1250 °C)**, and **zero tolerance for part distortion**. Therefore, the "tap" must be replaced with engineered, repeatable, force-controlled ejection systems.

## 1.3 Why is Ejection Required? (The Governing Physics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A finished forging remains inside the lower die after ram retraction due to **three principal retention forces**. The ejector must overcome the algebraic sum of these:

| Retention Phenomenon | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Die Shrinkage Grip** | Hot forging contracts upon cooling and clamps onto die wall | **Largest component** |
| **Frictional Adhesion** | Asperity-level micro-welding at die–workpiece interface | Moderate |
| **Undercut / Side-Spall Features** | Ribs, bosses, draft-less pockets create geometric locking | Geometry dependent |

## 1.4 Primary Functions of an Ejection System

1. **Positive Part Release** — guarantees the forging exits the die on every stroke.
2. **Part Protection** — delivers the load over a sufficient area so the forging does **not bend, scratch, or distort**.
3. **Die Protection** — prevents the operator from prying the part out with tools (which damages the die).
4. **Cycle Time Reduction** — automation-ready ejection enables high production rates (typically **60 – 120 strokes/min** for header work, **20 – 40 strokes/min** for forging presses).
5. **Operator Safety** — removes the need for manual handling near hot dies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Ejection Methods

Ejection methods are classified based on the **mechanism that generates the ejection force**. The KTU syllabus (Module 4) recognizes the following master taxonomy:

```
EJECTION METHODS
├── 2.1.1 Die-Released (Gravity / Drop-Through) Ejection
├── 2.1.2 Mechanical Knockout (KO) Systems
│       ├── a) Bottom Knockout (Pin / Bar type)
│       ├── b) Cam-Actuated Knockout
│       └── c) Lever / Walking-Beam Knockout
├── 2.1.3 Stripper Plate Ejection
│       ├── a) Fixed Stripper
│       └── b) Movable / Spring-Loaded Stripper
├── 2.1.4 Spring-Loaded Ejectors
├── 2.1.5 Hydraulic / Pneumatic (Air) Ejection
└── 2.1.6 Combination / Hybrid Systems
```

### 2.1.1 Die-Released (Gravity) Ejection

The forging **falls out of the die under its own weight** as the press opens. Applicable only when:
- The die is oriented **upside-down opening**.
- The forging has **no undercut features**.
- Cycle allows a chute or chute-and-conveyor beneath the press.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** Cannot be used for **positive-part-on-die** operations (the part must always remain in the upper die for the next operation, e.g., in multi-impression dies with coining, trimming, bending stations).

### 2.1.2 Mechanical Knockout (KO) System — The Industry Workhorse

This is the **most widely used ejection method** in drop-hammer, press, and upsetter forging. The complete KO system consists of five components arranged in series:

1. **Knockout Pins (KO Pins)** — contact the forging.
2. **Knockout Bar / Knockout Plate** — joins all KO pins into one rigid plane.
3. **Knockout Rod (or Shank)** — transmits force from below.
4. **Knockout Cushion (Spring / Rubber / Air / Hydraulic)** — provides the stored energy.
5. **Knockout Stopper** — limits the upward travel and adjusts pin height.

#### Mode of Operation (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 (Die Closed):** The knockout pins are flush with, or slightly recessed below, the die cavity floor.
- **Step 2 (Forging Complete):** The upper ram retracts; the forging is gripped inside the lower die.
- **Step 3 (Ram Fully Open):** The press's knockout bar is engaged and drives the KO rod **upward**.
- **Step 4 (Ejection):** The KO bar lifts the KO plate; the pins push the forging out by a stroke equal to the part's depth.
- **Step 5 (Reset):** Springs or gravity push the KO assembly back down, ready for the next cycle.

### 2.1.3 Stripper Plate Ejection

A **flat plate** that surrounds the punch (or die opening) and pushes the forging off vertically downward onto the plate as the punch retracts. Strippers are of two types:

| Type | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Stripper** | Bolted to die set; non-moving | Thin blanks, fine blanking |
| **Movable (Spring-Loaded) Stripper** | Floats on springs; moves with punch initially, holds part while punch retracts | Progressive dies, deep draws |

### 2.1.4 Spring-Loaded Ejectors

Springs (helical compression, die springs, or urethane pads) store energy during the press downstroke and release it during upstroke. Used for **low-to-medium ejection forces** in blanking and piercing dies.

### 2.1.5 Hydraulic / Pneumatic Air Ejection

- **Air Ejection:** Compressed air (typically **0.4 – 0.7 MPa**) is blown through ports in the die to push the forging out. Used for **non-critical, simple shapes**.
- **Hydraulic Ejection:** Uses a hydraulic cylinder (built into the press or the die) for high, controllable force. Found in **precision forging presses**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **direct board-exam relevant** for Module 4 — Ejection Methods. Memorize the symbols and the physical meaning of each term.

| # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{strip} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_{strip}$ | $F_{strip}$ = stripping force; $L$ = perimeter of cut/web; $t$ = thickness; $\tau_{strip}$ = shear stress at strip | N, mm, MPa |
| 2 | $\tau_{strip} = (0.70 \text{ to } 0.80)\,\tau_{uts}$ | $\tau_{uts}$ = ultimate shear strength of material at operating temp | MPa |
| 3 | $F_{eject} = k \cdot F_{strip}$ | $k$ = safety factor / die-condition factor ($\mathbf{1.25 \le k \le 2.0}$) | — |
| 4 | $F_{spring} = k_{s}\,\Delta x$ | $k_s$ = spring rate; $\Delta x$ = spring deflection (compression) | N, N/mm, mm |
| 5 | $n_{springs} = \dfrac{F_{eject}}{F_{one\,spring}}$ | $n_{springs}$ = number of springs required | — |
| 6 | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F_{eject}}{A_{piston}}$ | $P_{hyd}$ = hydraulic pressure; $A_{piston}$ = piston area | MPa, mm² |
| 7 | $\sigma_{b,pin} = \dfrac{4\,F_{pin}}{\pi\,d_{pin}^{2}}$ | $\sigma_{b,pin}$ = bending stress on KO pin; $F_{pin}$ = load per pin | MPa, N, mm |
| 8 | $\tau_{pin} = \dfrac{2\,F_{pin}}{\pi\,d_{pin}^{2}}$ | $\tau_{pin}$ = shear stress in KO pin | MPa |
| 9 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{F_{out}}{F_{in}}$ | $\eta_{mech}$ = mechanical efficiency of cam/lever ejector ($\mathbf{0.7 - 0.9}$) | — |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

Ejection systems are not academic curiosities — they dictate the **economics of mass production**. A poorly designed knockout causes:
- **Die downtime** (every minute of press downtime ≈ ₹ 500 – ₹ 2,000 loss in industry).
- **Operator injury** from hot ejected parts.
- **Scrap rates** climbing from < 1% to > 5% if ejection is unreliable.

The **automotive connecting rod**, **crank-shaft**, **gear blanks**, and **fasteners (bolts, screws)** are all produced on dies whose cycle time is fundamentally limited by **how fast and reliably the part can be ejected**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stripping Force for a Ring-Type Forging

> This is the **single most important derivation** for Module 4. Examiners almost always ask a 7-mark variation of this.

**Given:** A hot-steel forging in the shape of a flat ring (washer-like) sits in the lower die. The ring has:
- Outer diameter $D_o = 120 \text{ mm}$
- Inner diameter $D_i = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Flash-web thickness $t = 8 \text{ mm}$
- Material at ejection temperature: medium-carbon steel, ultimate shear strength $\tau_{uts} = 280 \text{ MPa}$
- Die-condition factor $k = 1.5$

**Required:** Compute the **stripping force** $F_{strip}$ and the **design ejection force** $F_{eject}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Perimeter of the Stripping Surface

The forging will be stripped from the die surface on which it grips. For a ring forging resting on the **lower die's flat cavity floor**, the gripping perimeter is the **outer circumference** of the ring (the inner hole is supported on a core pin and does not contribute to stripping).

$$
L = \pi \cdot D_o
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
L = \pi \times 120 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L = 376.99 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Determine the Effective Shear Stress at Stripping

A conservative industry convention (codified in **ASM Handbook Vol. 14A**) sets the shear stress at stripping to **75% of the ultimate shear strength** to account for work-hardening during forging:

$$
\tau_{strip} = 0.75 \times \tau_{uts}
$$

$$
\tau_{strip} = 0.75 \times 280 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_{strip} = 210 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Stripping Force

Using the primary formula:

$$
F_{strip} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_{strip}
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_{strip} = 376.99 \text{ mm} \times 8 \text{ mm} \times 210 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
F_{strip} = 633,344 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{strip} \approx 633.34 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Die-Condition Factor for Design

The actual ejector must overcome more than the stripping force — it must also account for die wear, oxide scale adhesion, and misalignment. The industry-standard amplification factor is:

$$
F_{eject} = k \cdot F_{strip}
$$

$$
F_{eject} = 1.5 \times 633.34 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_{eject} = 950.0 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 5 — Sanity Check & Reporting

$$
\boxed{F_{eject} \approx 950 \text{ kN} \;\; (\approx 96.85 \text{ tonnes-force})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Logic:** A 950 kN ejection force on an 8 mm flash web is **plausible for a hot-steel forging** in a 1500 – 2000 ton press. If the calculated force had been, say, 50 kN, it would indicate a unit error (likely using $\sigma_{uts}$ instead of $\tau_{uts}$, or omitting the **0.75 stripping factor**).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Knockout Pin Diameter (Bending + Shear Check)

**Given:** The same forging is ejected using **3 knockout pins** distributed around the ring. Each pin must carry one-third of $F_{eject}$. Allowable bending stress in the pin material (H13 hot-work tool steel at 200 °C) is $\sigma_{allow} = 850 \text{ MPa}$. The pins are cantilevered with an effective length $l = 40 \text{ mm}$.

### Step 1 — Load per Pin

$$
F_{pin} = \dfrac{F_{eject}}{n_{pins}}
$$

$$
F_{pin} = \dfrac{950{,}000 \text{ N}}{3}
$$

$$
F_{pin} = 316{,}667 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Bending Diameter

For a cantilever pin of circular cross-section, the bending stress is:

$$
\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{32 \cdot F_{pin} \cdot l}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}
$$

Setting $\sigma_b = \sigma_{allow}$ and solving for $d$:

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{32 \cdot F_{pin} \cdot l}{\pi \cdot \sigma_{allow}}
$$

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{32 \times 316{,}667 \times 40}{\pi \times 850}
$$

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{405{,}333{,}333}{2670.4}
$$

$$
d^{3} = 151{,}789 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt[3]{151{,}789}
$$

$$
d = 53.26 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Shear Diameter Check

$$
\tau_{pin} = \dfrac{4 \cdot F_{pin}}{\pi \cdot d^{2}}
$$

$$
\tau_{pin} = \dfrac{4 \times 316{,}667}{\pi \times (53.26)^{2}}
$$

$$
\tau_{pin} = \dfrac{1{,}266{,}668}{8911.6}
$$

$$
\tau_{pin} = 142.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $\tau_{pin} = 142.1 \text{ MPa} \ll \tau_{allow} \approx 600 \text{ MPa}$ for H13, the design is **governed by bending**, and the **standard 54 mm pin** is selected.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydraulic Ejector Pressure

If a hydraulic cylinder with piston diameter $D_p = 80 \text{ mm}$ is used to deliver $F_{eject}$:

$$
P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F_{eject}}{A_{piston}}
$$

$$
A_{piston} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_p^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 80^{2}}{4} = 5026.5 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = \dfrac{950{,}000}{5026.5}
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 189.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> 189 MPa exceeds the **standard hydraulic supply pressure of 14 – 21 MPa**. This tells the designer to either **increase the piston area to ~ 250 mm diameter** or use **mechanical knockout**. This kind of **design-feasibility check** is a high-scoring point in KTU valuations.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Selection Criterion | Gravity | Knockout Pin | Stripper | Air | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Force capability** | Lowest | High | Medium-High | Low | Highest |
| **Cycle time impact** | Negligible | Adds 0.2 – 0.5 s | Adds 0.1 – 0.3 s | Negligible | Adds 0.3 – 0.6 s |
| **Capital cost** | ₹ 0 | ₹ Low | ₹ Medium | ₹ Low | ₹ High |
| **Maintenance** | Nil | Periodic pin replacement | Plate alignment | Filter cleaning | Seal replacement |
| **Best for** | Drop-through dies | Most forging | Blanking/drawing | Small parts | Precision forging |
| **Disadvantage** | Limited geometry | Pin impression marks | Wear on edges | Noisy, oil-laden air | Leakage risk |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Knockout (KO) System

This block-flow renders the **force-flow and component hierarchy** of a typical forging-press KO system. Each block is a discrete, identifiable component; arrows show mechanical force transmission.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Press Ram Retracts] --> B[Knockout Cushion Energized]
    B --> C[Knockout Bar Moves Upward]
    C --> D[Knockout Rod Transmits Force]
    D --> E[Knockout Plate Lifts Rigidly]
    E --> F[Knockout Pins Push Forging]
    F --> G[Forging Released from Die Cavity]
    G --> H{Spring Reset?}
    H -- Yes --> I[KO Assembly Returns to Flush Position]
    H -- No --> J[Cycle Aborted - Maintenance Required]
    I --> K[Ready for Next Stroke]
    K --> A

    subgraph EnergySource["Energy Source Layer"]
        B1[Mechanical Spring] --> B
        B2[Rubber Pad] --> B
        B3[Air Cushion] --> B
        B4[Hydraulic Accumulator] --> B
    end

    subgraph MechanicalAdvantage["Force Amplification Layer"]
        C1[Direct Push - Efficiency 1.0] --> C
        C2[Cam Actuation - Efficiency 0.7 to 0.9] --> C
        C3[Lever Actuation - Efficiency 0.6 to 0.85] --> C
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **left main flow** is the operational sequence (start → cycle).
- The **`EnergySource` subgraph** is colour-coded in Mermaid as a logical grouping of the four standard cushion technologies.
- The **`MechanicalAdvantage` subgraph** shows that the force available at the forging can be **less than, equal to, or greater than** the raw cushion force depending on the actuation method.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Ejection-Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Forging Design] --> Q1{Part Undercuts?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Max Undercut Depth?}
    Q2 -- Less Than 3 mm --> Q3[Use Knockout Pins with Side Clearance]
    Q2 -- Greater Than 3 mm --> Q4[Use Side-Action Cams or Split Die]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Part Stays in Upper Die?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Upper-Die Knockout with Lifter]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7{Daily Production Volume?}
    Q7 -- Less Than 500 --> Q8[Manual Ejection with Tongs]
    Q7 -- 500 to 5000 --> Q9[Mechanical Knockout System]
    Q7 -- More Than 5000 --> Q10{Precision Required?}
    Q10 -- Yes --> Q11[Hydraulic Ejection]
    Q10 -- No --> Q12[Spring-Loaded Knockout with Stripper]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Force Flow in a Cam-Actuated Ejector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CamActuator["CAM-ACTUATED EJECTOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        CamInput[Cam Profile on Press Ram] -->|Vertical Force F_cam| CamFollower[Cam Follower Roller]
        CamFollower -->|Torque Converted to Lift| LifterArm[Lifter / Walking Beam]
        LifterArm -->|F_eject = eta x F_cam| EjectorPin[Ejector Pin]
        EjectorPin -->|Contact| Forging[Hot Forging]
    end

    Forging -->|Reaction| LowerDie[Lower Die Cavity]
    LowerDie -->|Reaction to Press Bed| PressFrame[Press Frame]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The cam actuator does **mechanical work** during the down-stroke (lifting the ejector pins out of the way to clear the die cavity) and **releases that stored energy** during the up-stroke to drive the pins upward. This is why cam ejectors are **self-contained** and **don't need external springs or hydraulics** — a frequent KTU short-answer question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Q:** Define **ejection** in the context of forging die design. List **any four** factors that necessitate a positive ejection system.

**Model Answer:**
Ejection is the controlled mechanical separation of a completed forging from the die cavity after the upper ram retracts, accomplished by a dedicated sub-system built into the die.

The four principal factors necessitating positive ejection are:
1. **Shrinkage grip** of the cooling forging on the die walls (largest component).
2. **Frictional adhesion** at the die–workpiece interface due to high contact pressure.
3. **Geometric undercuts** in features such as ribs, bosses, and draftless pockets.
4. **Operator safety and cycle-time automation**, eliminating manual part removal.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]

**Q:** Differentiate between a **fixed stripper** and a **movable (spring-loaded) stripper**. State one application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fixed Stripper | Movable (Spring-Loaded) Stripper |
|---|---|---|
| Mounting | Bolted rigidly to die set; no relative motion | Floats on helical/urethane springs |
| Function | Holds sheet stock down while punch retracts | Presses part off punch as punch retracts |
| Application | Blanking, fine blanking of thin sheets | Progressive dies, deep-drawing operations |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Each Part-B question offers either of two alternatives. You attempt **one**.

---

### Question 3A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q:** A forging die is to be designed for a flat ring-shaped hot-steel component. The dimensions and process data are:
- Outer diameter $D_o = 150 \text{ mm}$
- Inner diameter $D_i = 75 \text{ mm}$
- Flash/web thickness $t = 10 \text{ mm}$
- Ultimate shear strength of steel at ejection temperature: $\tau_{uts} = 320 \text{ MPa}$
- Die-condition factor: $k = 1.6$
- Number of knockout pins: $n = 4$
- Pin material: H13 tool steel, allowable bending stress $\sigma_{allow} = 900 \text{ MPa}$
- Cantilever pin length: $l = 45 \text{ mm}$

**(a)** Calculate the **stripping force** and the **design ejection force**.
**(b)** Determine the **minimum diameter of the knockout pins** based on bending stress, and verify the design against shear.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Gripping Perimeter**
The forging is stripped from the lower die's flat floor. The gripping perimeter is the outer circumference:
$$
L = \pi \cdot D_o = \pi \times 150 = 471.24 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Effective Stripping Shear Stress**
$$
\tau_{strip} = 0.75 \cdot \tau_{uts} = 0.75 \times 320 = 240 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Stripping Force**
$$
F_{strip} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_{strip} = 471.24 \times 10 \times 240 = 1{,}130{,}976 \text{ N}
$$
$$
F_{strip} \approx 1131 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Design Ejection Force**
$$
F_{eject} = k \cdot F_{strip} = 1.6 \times 1131 = 1809.6 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
\boxed{F_{eject} \approx 1810 \text{ kN}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Load per Pin**
$$
F_{pin} = \dfrac{F_{eject}}{n} = \dfrac{1{,}810{,}000}{4} = 452{,}500 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Bending Diameter**
For a cantilever pin:
$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 \cdot F_{pin} \cdot l}{\pi \cdot d^{3}} \le \sigma_{allow}
$$
$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{32 \cdot F_{pin} \cdot l}{\pi \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{32 \times 452{,}500 \times 45}{\pi \times 900}
$$
$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{651{,}600{,}000}{2827.4} = 230{,}456 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$
$$
d = \sqrt[3]{230{,}456} \approx 61.4 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\boxed{d_{pin} \approx 62 \text{ mm (standard size)}}
$$

**Step 3: Shear Verification**
$$
\tau_{pin} = \dfrac{4 \cdot F_{pin}}{\pi \cdot d^{2}} = \dfrac{4 \times 452{,}500}{\pi \times (62)^{2}} = \dfrac{1{,}810{,}000}{12{,}076} = 149.9 \text{ MPa}
$$
Since $\tau_{pin} = 149.9 \text{ MPa} \ll \tau_{allow,shear} \approx 700 \text{ MPa}$ for H13, the **bending criterion governs** and the design is **safe**.

---

### Question 3B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

**Q:** Write brief notes on the following ejection methods used in forging die design:
**(a)** **Die-released (gravity) ejection** with its merits, demerits, and typical applications.
**(b)** **Hydraulic ejection system** with a neat sketch of its layout and the calculation of the hydraulic pressure required for a forging needing an ejection force of $1200 \text{ kN}$, given piston diameter $D_p = 100 \text{ mm}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Die-Released (Gravity) Ejection** relies on the self-weight of the hot forging to drop out of the lower die as the press opens. It is the **simplest and cheapest** ejection method.

**Merits:**
1. Zero mechanical components — virtually **no maintenance cost**.
2. No cycle-time penalty — ejection occurs **simultaneously** with die opening.
3. Highest **reliability** — no springs or seals to fail.
4. Suitable for **high-temperature, high-sticking** materials where pin marks would be unacceptable.

**Demerits:**
1. Applicable **only for simple, symmetrical shapes** with no undercuts.
2. The forging **must always fall in the lower die** — restricts multi-impression die sequences.
3. Requires a **chute / conveyor** underneath — space-consuming.
4. Risk of **part damage** on impact with the chute (scale loss, deformation of thin sections).

**Typical Applications:** Drop-hammer forging of simple axle blanks, large gear blanks, ring-rolling preforms, and open-die forging of cylindrical billets.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Hydraulic Ejection System Layout:**
A self-contained hydraulic cylinder is mounted beneath the press bed, coaxial with the die centerline. The piston pushes the knockout rod and pin assembly upward during the ejection phase. The cylinder is connected to a high-pressure hydraulic power pack (typically **14 – 21 MPa**) via a directional control valve synchronised with the press cycle.

**Sketch Description (verbal, for record):**
- Cylinder body bolted to press bed.
- Piston rod extends upward through the bolster.
- Connects to knockout bar → knockout plate → KO pins.
- Hydraulic lines enter from the side; return spring retracts piston after cycle.

**Hydraulic Pressure Calculation:**
$$
A_{piston} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_p^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (100)^{2}}{4} = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F_{eject}}{A_{piston}} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000 \text{ N}}{7853.98 \text{ mm}^{2}}
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 152.79 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{hyd} \approx 152.8 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** The required pressure of ~153 MPa is **much higher than the standard hydraulic supply of 14 – 21 MPa**. A designer would either increase the piston diameter or use a **mechanical knockout**. This **interpretation** carries extra marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Top 5 Pitfalls in Ejection-Method Questions:**
> 1. **Using $\sigma_{uts}$ (tensile) instead of $\tau_{uts}$ (shear)** — almost 60% of the class makes this error. The gripping force is shear, not tensile.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.75 stripping factor** — examiners explicitly test whether you know the difference between $\tau_{uts}$ and $\tau_{strip}$.
> 3. **Omitting the die-condition factor $k$** — the answer then appears theoretically clean but is **industrially non-viable**.
> 4. **Confusing fixed and movable strippers** — remember: **fixed** is bolted to die set (holds stock); **movable** floats on springs (strips part).
> 5. **Not validating feasibility** of calculated hydraulic pressures — always check whether $P_{hyd}$ lies within **standard supply range (14 – 21 MPa)**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Ejection Methods**

- [ ] **Ejection** = controlled separation of forging from die cavity after forming.
- [ ] Three retention forces: **shrinkage grip, friction, geometric undercut**.
- [ ] Ejection methods taxonomy: **gravity, KO pins, stripper, spring, hydraulic, pneumatic**.
- [ ] The **mechanical knockout (KO) system** is the industry workhorse — five components: **pin, bar, rod, cushion, stopper**.
- [ ] Master formula: $\;F_{strip} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau_{strip}\;$ where $\;\tau_{strip} = 0.75\,\tau_{uts}$.
- [ ] Design force must be amplified: $\;F_{eject} = k \cdot F_{strip}\;$ with $\;k = 1.25 - 2.0$.
- [ ] **Knockout pin diameter is governed by bending**, not shear: $\;d^{3} = \dfrac{32\,F_{pin}\,l}{\pi\,\sigma_{allow}}$.
- [ ] Hydraulic pressure check: standard supply is **14 – 21 MPa**; if $P_{hyd} > 21$ MPa, redesign with larger piston.
- [ ] **Fixed stripper** = bolted, holds stock; **Movable stripper** = spring-loaded, strips part.
- [ ] **Gravity ejection** is cheapest but **only for simple shapes** with no undercuts and where the part stays in the **lower die**.
- [ ] Always validate numerical answers against **physical plausibility** — examiners reward sanity checks.
- [ ] **Ejection method selection depends on**: part geometry, daily volume, precision required, and capital cost budget.
- [ ] Typical **ejection stroke** = depth of die cavity + 5 mm clearance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tool Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tool Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Tool Materials

**Tool materials** are specially engineered metallic, ceramic, or composite materials used to fabricate dies, punches, mandrels, rollers, and other forming tools that shape, cut, or deform workpieces through plastic deformation processes (forging, extrusion, drawing, rolling, stamping, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):**
> *Tool materials are those engineering materials that possess the requisite combination of **hardness, toughness, wear resistance, hot hardness (red hardness), thermal conductivity, and dimensional stability** to withstand the severe mechanical, thermal, and tribological loads imposed during metal forming operations without failure.*

The selection of an appropriate tool material governs:
- **Tool life** (number of components produced before failure)
- **Surface finish** of the formed part
- **Dimensional accuracy** of the final product
- **Production economics** (cost per part)
- **Downtime** and maintenance requirements

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Workshop Knife" 🍳

Imagine you walk into a professional kitchen. A sushi chef, a butcher, and a bread baker do NOT use the same knife.

- A **bread knife** (serrated, flexible steel) — survives slicing crusty loaves without chipping (high *toughness*).
- A **chef's knife** (hardened stainless steel) — holds an edge through daily chopping (high *wear resistance*).
- A **sashimi knife** (very hard high-carbon steel) — slices paper-thin fish (extreme *hardness*, low toughness).
- A **ceramic knife** — stays sharp for months but shatters if dropped (extreme *hardness*, brittle).

**Each tool is matched to its specific operating envelope.** Tool materials follow the same logic:

| Kitchen Knife | Equivalent Tool Material | Key Property |
|---------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| Bread knife | Hot-work die steel (H11, H13) | High toughness, thermal fatigue resistance |
| Chef's knife | High Speed Steel (M2, M35) | Balanced hardness + wear resistance |
| Sashimi knife | Carbide (WC-Co) | Extreme hardness + wear resistance |
| Ceramic knife | Alumina, Sialon, CBN | Supreme hardness, brittle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Golden Rule" of Tool Materials:**
> There is NO universally "best" tool material. The optimum is always a *trade-off* between **hardness** (wear resistance) and **toughness** (impact absorption), modulated by the **operating temperature** and **production volume**.

---

## 1.3 Why Tool Materials Matter in Metal Forming

In a forging press or a sheet-metal stamping line, the tool experiences during each stroke:

1. **High contact stresses** (often > 2000 MPa).
2. **Elevated temperatures** (up to 500–900 °C in hot forging).
3. **Adhesive and abrasive wear** from sliding contact with the workpiece.
4. **Thermal cycling** (die heats up during stroke, cools between strokes).
5. **Cyclic impact / fatigue loading**.

A tool material that fails in any one of these regimes causes **catastrophic production loss** — broken tools, scrapped parts, and machine downtime.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Whenever a question describes a metal forming scenario, ALWAYS begin your answer by listing the **operating conditions** (temperature, stress, production volume) and THEN justify the tool material choice. This structured approach earns full marks.

---

## 1.4 Essential Property Definitions (Must Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Seven Critical Properties of Tool Materials — KTU Module 4 Syllabus Core**

| Property | Formal Definition | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Hardness** | Resistance to localized plastic deformation (measured in HRC, HV, HB) | Determines wear resistance and ability to penetrate workpiece |
| **Toughness** | Ability to absorb energy and deform plastically before fracture (J or J/cm²) | Resistance to chipping and catastrophic fracture under impact |
| **Wear Resistance** | Resistance to material loss by adhesion, abrasion, or diffusion | Determines tool life and dimensional stability of the die cavity |
| **Hot Hardness (Red Hardness)** | Ability to retain hardness at elevated service temperatures | Essential for hot-work tools (forging, extrusion) |
| **Thermal Fatigue Resistance** | Resistance to repeated heating/cooling cycles without cracking | Critical for die-casting and hot-forging dies |
| **Temper Resistance** | Ability to retain hardness after prolonged exposure to elevated temperatures | Determines dimensional stability during heat generation in service |
| **Machinability & Grindability** | Ease of shaping the tool material to required geometry | Affects tool manufacturing cost and lead time |

---

## 1.5 Overview of the Tool Material Family

Tool materials can be broadly classified along a **hardness–toughness spectrum**:

```
LOW  ───────────────────────────────────────────────►  HIGH
Hardness / High Toughness                High Hardness / Low Toughness

Hot-Work Steels  ─►  HSS  ─►  Cold-Work Steels  ─►  Carbides  ─►  Ceramics  ─►  CBN/Diamond
(H11, H13)           (M2)        (D2, D3)              (WC-Co)     (Al2O3)        (Cubic BN)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Problems:**
> - **Cold forming (room temperature) and high production runs** → Carbides or HSS.
> - **Hot forming (forging, hot extrusion)** → Hot-work die steels (H11, H13, H21).
> - **High-speed machining of formed parts** → Carbides, ceramics, or CBN.
> - **Ultra-precision / non-ferrous cutting** → Diamond (PCD).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Tool Materials (Detailed)

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes tool materials into the following hierarchical structure:

### A. Metallic Tool Materials

#### 1. Carbon Tool Steels (W-group, plain carbon)
- Examples: **W1, W2** (water-hardening)
- Composition: 0.6–1.4 % C, small Mn, Si; **no alloying elements**.
- Hardness achievable: **62–65 HRC** (after water quenching).
- **Limitations:** Low hot hardness (soften above ~150 °C), high distortion during quenching, poor wear resistance.
- **KTU Use Case:** Short-run blanking and piercing dies, hand tools (files, chisels).

#### 2. Alloy Tool Steels
- **Cold-work die steels (AISI D, A, O series):**
  - **D2** (1.5 % C, 12 % Cr, 1 % Mo, 1 % V) — High-C, high-Cr; air-hardening.
  - **D3** (2.1 % C, 12 % Cr) — Highest wear resistance among cold-work steels.
  - **A2** (1.0 % C, 5 % Cr, 1 % Mo) — Air-hardening, good toughness.
  - **O1** (0.9 % C, 1 % Mn, 0.5 % Cr, 0.5 % W) — Oil-hardening; good dimensional stability.
- **Hot-work die steels (AISI H series):**
  - **H11** (0.35 % C, 5 % Cr, 1.5 % Mo, 0.4 % V) — Chromium hot-work steel.
  - **H13** (0.4 % C, 5 % Cr, 1.5 % Mo, 1 % V) — Industry workhorse for die casting and forging.
  - **H21** (0.3 % C, 9 % W, 3.5 % Cr) — Tungsten hot-work steel.
- **Shock-resistant steels (S series):** S1, S5 — for punches and chisels.
- **High Speed Steels (HSS):** The crown jewel of tool steels.
  - **Tungsten HSS (T1):** 18 % W, 4 % Cr, 1 % V, 0.75 % C.
  - **Molybdenum HSS (M2):** 6 % W, 5 % Mo, 4 % Cr, 2 % V, 0.85 % C — *most widely used HSS in the world*.
  - **M35:** M2 + 5 % Co → improved hot hardness.
  - **M42:** 8 % Co, 1.5 % W, 9.5 % Mo → premium HSS for difficult-to-machine materials.
  - **T15:** 12 % Co, high V → best hot hardness among HSS.

#### 3. Cast Tool Alloys (Stellite, Ferro-TiC)
- Non-ferrous, very hard; used for cutting tools and wear-resistant inserts.
- Cannot be forged — only cast and ground.

### B. Non-Metallic Tool Materials

#### 4. Cemented Carbides (Cermets based on WC)
- Tungsten Carbide (WC) particles bonded with Cobalt (Co).
- Composition: **94 % WC + 6 % Co** (C-grade, cutting cast iron).
- **ISO Classification:** P (steel), M (stainless), K (cast iron), N (non-ferrous).
- Hardness: **82–94 HRA** (equivalent to ~70–80 HRC and above).
- Hot hardness: Retain hardness up to **800–1000 °C**.

#### 5. Ceramics
- **Alumina (Al₂O₃):** Cold-pressed and sintered; very hard, brittle.
- **Sialon (Si-Al-O-N):** Silicon-aluminum oxynitride; tougher than pure alumina.
- **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN):** Second hardest material after diamond; stable up to ~1400 °C.
- **Diamond / PCD (Polycrystalline Diamond):** Hardest known material; reacts with iron at high temperatures.

#### 6. Superhard Materials
- Diamond and CBN — for ultra-precision and hard-material machining.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-exam-relevant. Memorize the symbols, units, and application context.**

### Master Formula Table

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Application |
|---|------------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $HRC \approx 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_{eff}}{0.001}\right)$ | Approx. relation between HRC and modulus-related wear constant | Quick hardness estimation |
| 2 | $V \cdot T^n = C$  (**Taylor's Tool Life Equation**) | Cutting speed (V) × tool life (T)^n = constant C | Tool life prediction in machining |
| 3 | $\log V = \log V_0 - n \log T$ | Linearized form of Taylor's equation (for log-log plot) | Plotting tool-life curves |
| 4 | $\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$ | Speed-life equivalence for two cutting conditions | Solving tool-life problems |
| 5 | $H_{hot} = H_{RT} \cdot e^{-k(T-T_{RT})}$ | Exponential decay of hardness with temperature | Hot hardness estimation (k = material constant) |
| 6 | $R_w = k \cdot \frac{F_N \cdot L}{H}$ | Archard's wear equation: $R_w$ = wear rate, $F_N$ = normal load, $L$ = sliding distance, $H$ = hardness | Relative wear comparison |
| 7 | $\sigma_f = \frac{K_{Ic}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}$ | Fracture stress for a crack of length $a$ (fracture mechanics) | Predicting brittle tool failure |
| 8 | $K_{Ic} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity factor; $K_{Ic}$ = fracture toughness | Material selection for toughness |
| 9 | $T_{service} \le 0.5 \cdot T_{melt}$ (K) | Rule of thumb: max service temperature ≈ ½ melting point (K) | Quick material screening |
| 10 | $HV \approx 0.95 \cdot HRC \cdot 10 \quad (\text{for } HRC > 40)$ | Approx. Vickers–Rockwell C correlation | Hardness conversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Validity:**
> - Taylor's equation is valid for **single-point cutting tools** in **continuous turning** under steady conditions.
> - Hot hardness decay equation is empirical; the constant $k$ is material-specific (for HSS, $k \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ per °C).
> - Archard's equation assumes *mild wear* (no gross plastic deformation or fracture).
> - Hardness conversion formulas are approximations; refer to ASTM E140 tables for exact values.

---

## 2.3 Why and How — The Logic Behind Tool Material Selection

### The Selection Logic (KTU Decision Flow)

**Step 1 — Identify the forming process:**
- Cold forming? → Need high surface hardness, dimensional accuracy, low temp.
- Hot forming? → Need hot hardness, thermal fatigue resistance, temper resistance.
- High-speed cutting of formed part? → Need hot hardness and chemical stability.

**Step 2 — Identify production volume:**
- Prototype / < 1000 parts → Carbon or low-alloy tool steel (low cost).
- Medium (1000–100 000 parts) → HSS or hot-work die steel.
- High (> 100 000 parts) → Carbide or ceramic (high initial cost, low per-part cost).

**Step 3 — Identify workpiece material:**
- Workpiece harder than tool → Will wear tool rapidly; choose harder tool.
- Workpiece chemically reactive with tool (e.g., Fe + diamond) → Avoid that combination.

**Step 4 — Apply the 3T Rule — Temperature, Toughness, Tool life:**
- **T₁ (Temperature):** If service T > 600 °C → hot-work steel or carbide.
- **T₂ (Toughness):** If impact loading is high (forging hammer) → HSS or hot-work steel (avoid ceramics).
- **T₃ (Tool life expectation):** If > 10⁵ parts → carbide insert (indexable) is the standard choice.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Mark-Scheme Insight:** Examiners allocate 2 marks for the *final justified choice*, 3 marks for the *systematic elimination of alternatives*, and 2 marks for the *process-condition mapping*. Structure your answer using the 3T Rule to capture full marks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Forming Process | Preferred Tool Material | Why |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|-----|
| Automotive body panels | Sheet metal stamping | D2, DC53 cold-work die steel | High wear resistance, dimensional accuracy |
| Connecting rod forging | Hot forging | H13 hot-work die steel | Hot hardness, thermal fatigue resistance |
| Aluminum extrusion | Hot extrusion | H13, sometimes H11 | Heat checking resistance |
| Sheet metal piercing (high volume) | Blanking / piercing | Carbide (WC-Co) | 10× tool life over D2 |
| Wire drawing | Cold drawing | Carbide nibs, diamond dies for fine wire | Extreme wear resistance |
| Injection molding of plastics | Mold cavity | P20, H13, S136 (stainless) | Polishability, corrosion resistance |
| Cutting Inconel / Titanium | Turning | CBN, coated carbide | Hot hardness, chemical stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Course Connection (KTU NEP 2020):** Tool material selection directly influences **Production Engineering (PCPCT604)**, **Manufacturing Processes (PCMET206)**, and **Tribology (elective)** outcomes. Always connect tool material science to the broader manufacturing value chain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Formulas (Full Algebraic Walkthrough)

### Derivation 3.1.1 — Taylor's Tool Life Equation (Foundational)

Taylor's tool life equation is the **most important empirical relationship** in tool material science. Its derivation proceeds from experimental observations.

**Step 1 — Empirical Observation:**
In 1907, Frederick W. Taylor observed that, for a given tool material and workpiece combination, the relationship between cutting speed $V$ and tool life $T$ follows a power law:

$$V \cdot T^n = C$$

where:
- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = tool life (min)
- $n$ = Taylor's exponent (tool life exponent), dimensionless
- $C$ = Taylor's constant (cutting speed for $T = 1$ min)

**Step 2 — Taking Logarithms (Log-linearization):**
Apply $\log_{10}$ to both sides:

$$\log_{10}(V) + n \cdot \log_{10}(T) = \log_{10}(C)$$

Rearranging into the standard slope-intercept form $y = mx + c$:

$$\log_{10}(V) = \log_{10}(C) - n \cdot \log_{10}(T)$$

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation:**
- The y-intercept (at $\log T = 0$, i.e., $T = 1$ min) gives $\log C$.
- The slope of the $\log V$ vs. $\log T$ line is $-n$.

**Step 4 — Two-Condition Speed-Life Equivalence:**
For two different operating points $(V_1, T_1)$ and $(V_2, T_2)$ on the same tool-life curve:

$$V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = V_2 \cdot T_2^{n} = C$$

Dividing the two:

$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 5 — Typical Values of $n$:**
| Tool Material | Typical $n$ value |
|---------------|-------------------|
| Carbon steel (W1) | 0.10 – 0.15 |
| HSS (M2) | 0.20 – 0.25 |
| Coated carbide | 0.25 – 0.30 |
| Ceramics (Al₂O₃) | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| CBN / Diamond | 0.40 – 0.50 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher $n$ means the tool is more sensitive to speed — small speed increases drastically reduce tool life.** Carbides and ceramics have higher $n$, demanding precise speed control.

---

### Derivation 3.1.2 — Hot Hardness Decay Model

The hardness of any tool material decreases with temperature following an approximately exponential decay.

**Step 1 — Assumption:**
The fractional loss in hardness is proportional to the temperature increase above room temperature.

**Step 2 — Differential Form:**

$$\frac{dH}{dT} = -k \cdot H$$

where $k$ is a material-specific decay constant (per °C).

**Step 3 — Separation and Integration:**

$$\int_{H_{RT}}^{H_{hot}} \frac{dH}{H} = -k \int_{T_{RT}}^{T} dT$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{H_{hot}}{H_{RT}}\right) = -k(T - T_{RT})$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiation:**

$$\frac{H_{hot}}{H_{RT}} = e^{-k(T - T_{RT})}$$

**Step 5 — Final Form:**

$$H_{hot} = H_{RT} \cdot e^{-k(T - T_{RT})}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
A HSS (M2) tool has a room-temperature hardness of 64 HRC. If the cutting temperature rises to 600 °C, and $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ per °C, find the hot hardness.

Substitute $H_{RT} = 64$, $T = 600$, $T_{RT} = 25$, $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$\Delta T = 600 - 25 = 575 \text{ °C}$$

$$H_{hot} = 64 \cdot e^{-(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(575)}$$

$$H_{hot} = 64 \cdot e^{-0.8625}$$

$$H_{hot} = 64 \cdot 0.4222$$

$$H_{hot} \approx 27.0 \text{ HRC}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The HSS has lost more than 50 % of its room-temperature hardness at 600 °C!** This is why HSS is unsuitable for sustained high-temperature work, and why hot-work die steels and carbides were developed.

---

### Derivation 3.1.3 — Archard's Wear Equation (Brief)

The volumetric wear rate $W_v$ of a sliding contact is:

$$W_v = k_w \cdot \frac{F_N \cdot L}{H}$$

where:
- $k_w$ = wear coefficient (dimensionless, typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-8}$)
- $F_N$ = normal load (N)
- $L$ = sliding distance (m)
- $H$ = hardness of the softer material (Pa)

**Implication for tool materials:** Doubling the hardness halves the wear rate (for the same load and sliding distance). This is the fundamental justification for selecting **harder tool materials** in abrasive applications.

---

## 3.2 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix — Tool Material Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Comparison Table (KTU Module 4 High-Yield Content):**

| Property | Carbon Tool Steel (W1) | HSS (M2) | Coated Carbide (P20) | Ceramics (Al₂O₃) | CBN |
|----------|------------------------|----------|----------------------|--------------------|------|
| **Composition (key)** | 1.0 % C | 0.85 % C, 6 % W, 5 % Mo, 4 % Cr, 2 % V | WC + TiC + Co, TiN coating | Al₂O₃ (95 %+) | Cubic BN (polycrystalline) |
| **Hardness (HRC equivalent)** | 62 – 65 | 63 – 66 | 78 – 85 | 80 – 85 | 85 – 92 |
| **Hot Hardness (at 600 °C)** | Very poor (drops to ~30 HRC) | Poor (~50 HRC) | Good (~75 HRC) | Excellent (~80 HRC) | Excellent (~85 HRC) |
| **Transverse Rupture Strength (MPa)** | 2500 – 3000 | 3500 – 4500 | 1500 – 2500 | 400 – 700 | 700 – 1000 |
| **Fracture Toughness $K_{Ic}$ (MPa·m^½)** | 25 – 30 | 18 – 25 | 12 – 18 | 3 – 5 | 6 – 8 |
| **Max Service Temperature (°C)** | ~200 | ~600 | ~900 | ~1200 | ~1400 |
| **Wear Resistance** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Extreme |
| **Cost (relative index)** | 1× | 3× | 6× | 10× | 30× |
| **Typical Forming Application** | Hand tools, simple blanking dies | Drills, taps, broaches, form tools | High-volume sheet metal dies | High-speed turning of cast iron | Hardened steel machining (HRC > 50) |
| **Major Limitation** | Low hot hardness; distortion in quenching | Softens at red heat | Brittle; not for impact | Very brittle; chipping under vibration | High cost; reacts with low-Mg alloys |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For "compare" type questions, use a **minimum of 5 property columns** (hardness, hot hardness, toughness, wear resistance, cost) and provide a **1-line justification** for each numerical entry. Examiners reward structured tabular answers.

---

## 3.3 Heat Treatment Aspects (Conceptual Walkthrough)

Tool steels derive their properties from **carefully controlled heat treatment sequences**. The standard steps for HSS (M2) are:

**Step 1 — Preheating (550 °C, then 850 °C):**
Reduces thermal shock and ensures uniform temperature before the high-temperature soak.

**Step 2 — Austenitizing (1200 – 1220 °C):**
The steel transforms to a high-C, high-V austenite, dissolving alloy carbides.

**Step 3 — Quenching (oil, salt bath, or air):**
Rapid cooling transforms austenite to martensite — a hard, supersaturated BCT structure.

**Step 4 — Tempering (540 – 570 °C, double or triple temper):**
- Precipitates fine secondary carbides (V₄C₃, Mo₂C).
- Reduces internal stresses.
- Promotes **secondary hardening** — the hardness *increases* during tempering!
- This phenomenon (called *secondary hardening*) is unique to HSS and is the metallurgical basis for their superior hot hardness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid — The "4-Step HSS Heat Treatment":** **P**reheat → **A**ustenitize → **Q**uench → **T**emper (with double tempering for hot-work steels). Remember **PAQT**.

---

## 3.4 Failure Analysis of Tools (Diagnostic Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Tool Failure Modes in Metal Forming:**

| Failure Mode | Visual Signature | Root Cause | Material Solution |
|--------------|------------------|------------|-------------------|
| **Abrasive wear** | Uniform material loss on cutting edges | Hard particles in workpiece, low tool hardness | Switch to carbide, coated tools |
| **Adhesive wear (built-up edge)** | Material from workpiece welds onto tool | Low cutting speed, low tool–workpiece hardness ratio | Increase speed; use coated tools |
| **Diffusion wear** | Crater wear on rake face (chip flow zone) | High temperature + chemical affinity | Use ceramics, CBN, coated carbides |
| **Thermal cracking (heat checking)** | Network of fine cracks on die surface | Repeated thermal cycling | Use hot-work steels (H13) with thermal fatigue resistance |
| **Chipping** | Localized fracture of cutting edge | Impact loading, low toughness, large included angle | Increase tool toughness (HSS, hot-work steel) |
| **Gross fracture** | Catastrophic tool breakage | Severe overload, material defects, wrong grade | Redesign; use tougher grade |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse **abrasive wear** and **adhesive wear**. Abrasive wear is *mechanical scratching*; adhesive wear involves *atomic bonding and material transfer* between tool and workpiece. The remedies are different!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
