# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (Answer any 2 out of 3)
> - **Part B:** Internal choice, answer one out of two options. Total = 14 marks
> - **Total for this topic module:** 20 marks (when full module question is asked)

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "hot hardness" (red hardness) of a tool material. Why is it critical for cutting tools used in high-speed machining?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Hot hardness, also called *red hardness*, is the ability of a tool material to **retain its hardness at elevated temperatures** encountered during cutting or forming operations. It is typically measured as the Vickers or Rockwell hardness at temperatures between 500 °C and 1000 °C, and is plotted as a *hot hardness curve* showing hardness versus temperature.

**Why it is critical (1 mark):**
At high cutting speeds, the temperature at the tool–chip interface can exceed 800 °C. If the tool material loses hardness rapidly above 500 °C, the cutting edge will **plastically deform, wear rapidly, and fail prematurely**. Hot hardness is therefore the *deciding property* for tool selection in high-speed machining and hot-forming operations.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** Examiners look for the *symbol H_hot* or a *numerical value (e.g., 60 HRC at 600 °C)* for full marks on the definition.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages and two limitations of cemented carbide tools over high-speed steel (HSS) tools.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages (1½ marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Much higher hot hardness** — cemented carbides retain hardness up to 800–1000 °C, while HSS softens rapidly above 600 °C.
2. **Superior wear resistance** — the WC particles (1800–2400 HV) are far harder than HSS carbides, leading to 5–10× longer tool life in abrasive applications.
3. **Higher cutting speeds possible** — ceramics and carbides permit speeds 2–5× higher than HSS, increasing productivity.

**Limitations (1½ marks — ¾ mark each):**
1. **Lower toughness and brittleness** — carbides chip and fracture under interrupted cuts and heavy impact; HSS absorbs shocks far better.
2. **Higher cost and grinding difficulty** — carbides are 5–10× costlier than HSS, require diamond grinding, and cannot be machined conventionally.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Classify tool materials used in metal forming processes with a neat diagram. Discuss the composition, properties, and typical applications of **High Speed Steels (HSS)**, with special emphasis on the role of tungsten, molybdenum, and vanadium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A HSS (M2) tool is used for turning mild steel at a cutting speed of 30 m/min with a corresponding tool life of 90 minutes. If the cutting speed is increased to 45 m/min, determine the new tool life, given Taylor's tool life exponent $n = 0.22$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Classification (3 marks):**
Tool materials are classified into:
1. **Metallic** — carbon tool steels, alloy tool steels (cold-work, hot-work, shock-resistant), HSS, cast alloys.
2. **Non-metallic** — cemented carbides, ceramics (alumina, Sialon), cermets.
3. **Superhard** — diamond (PCD) and cubic boron nitride (CBN).

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for a complete classification.)*

**HSS Composition and Role of Alloying Elements (3 marks):**

| Element | Typical % in M2 | Function |
|---------|------------------|----------|
| **Carbon (C)** | 0.80 – 0.90 | Forms carbides; provides basic hardness |
| **Tungsten (W)** | 5.50 – 6.75 | Forms hard, stable M₆C carbides → **hot hardness** and wear resistance |
| **Molybdenum (Mo)** | 4.50 – 5.50 | Similar role to W; **cheaper substitute** for W (Mo-W equivalence ≈ 2:1) |
| **Chromium (Cr)** | 3.75 – 4.50 | Forms M₂₃C₆ carbides; **improves hardenability and corrosion resistance** |
| **Vanadium (V)** | 1.75 – 2.20 | Forms ultra-hard **V₄C₃ or VC carbides** (HV ~2800); primary **secondary hardening** agent and grain refiner |
| **Cobalt (Co)** | 0 – 8 (in M35, M42) | Improves **hot hardness** and red hardness further |

**Properties of HSS (1 mark):**
- Hardness 63–66 HRC (after heat treatment).
- Hot hardness retention up to 600 °C.
- High transverse rupture strength (~4000 MPa) — tougher than carbides.
- Can be forged and machined in annealed condition.

**Applications (½ mark):**
- Drills, taps, reamers, milling cutters, broaches, form tools, gear hobs, woodworking tools.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Given:**
- Initial cutting speed, $V_1 = 30$ m/min
- Initial tool life, $T_1 = 90$ min
- New cutting speed, $V_2 = 45$ m/min
- Taylor's exponent, $n = 0.22$

**Find:** New tool life $T_2$

**Step 1 — Apply Taylor's Two-Condition Equivalence:**

$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$\frac{30}{45} = \left(\frac{T_2}{90}\right)^{0.22}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify LHS:**

$$\frac{30}{45} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$

$$0.6667 = \left(\frac{T_2}{90}\right)^{0.22}$$

**Step 4 — Take the $n$-th root (or raise to power $1/n = 1/0.22 \approx 4.545$):**

$$\frac{T_2}{90} = (0.6667)^{1/0.22}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Numerically:**

$$\frac{T_2}{90} = (0.6667)^{4.545}$$

Computing $\ln(0.6667) = -0.4055$, then $4.545 \times (-0.4055) = -1.843$, and $e^{-1.843} \approx 0.1584$:

$$\frac{T_2}{90} = 0.1584$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T_2$:**

$$T_2 = 90 \times 0.1584 \approx 14.3 \text{ min}$$

**Final Answer (boxed for KTU valuation):**
$$T_2 \approx 14.3 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Sign error in formula:** The correct form is $V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = V_2 \cdot T_2^{n}$, NOT $V_2 \cdot T_2^{n} = V_1 \cdot T_1^{n}$ with inverted ratio. Marks deducted for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to raise to 1/n:** Many students compute $(T_2/90)^{0.22}$ instead of $(0.6667)^{1/0.22}$. The reciprocal of the exponent is crucial.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Tool life must be in **minutes**; cutting speed in **m/min**. Mixing m/s with minutes yields wrong answers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Compare the tool life, cutting speed, and cost-effectiveness of **Carbon Tool Steels, High Speed Steels, Cemented Carbides, and Ceramics** for a sheet metal blanking operation producing 5 lakh components per year from 1.5 mm thick mild steel strip. Justify the optimal selection using the **3T rule** (Temperature, Toughness, Tool life). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **secondary hardening phenomenon** in HSS with reference to the TTT/TTT diagram concept. Why is double or triple tempering recommended for HSS tools? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Process Condition Analysis (1 mark):**
- Sheet metal blanking of 1.5 mm mild steel.
- Cold forming (room temperature service).
- 5,00,000 components/year = **high volume**.

**Application of the 3T Rule (6 marks):**

| Tool Material | T₁ (Temperature ≤ 200 °C OK?) | T₂ (Toughness — needed for blanking?) | T₃ (Tool Life at 5 lakh parts) | Cost-Effectiveness Verdict |
|---------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **W1 Carbon Tool Steel** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (Wears out in ~50 000 parts) | **Reject — low tool life** |
| **HSS (M2)** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (~1 lakh parts) | **Marginal — needs regrinding** |
| **Cemented Carbide (WC-Co)** | ✓ | ✓ (Toughness OK for blanking) | ✓✓ (~5–10 lakh parts) | **Best — minimal regrinding, lowest cost per part** |
| **Ceramics (Al₂O₃)** | ✓ | ✗ (Brittle — chips in blanking) | — | **Reject — chipping risk** |

**Optimal Selection:**
**Cemented Carbide (WC-Co) grade K20** is the optimal choice because:
- It provides the **longest tool life** for the 5-lakh-part production run, **minimizing tool changes and downtime**.
- Its **hardness (80–85 HRA)** resists the abrasive wear from mild steel sliding.
- Its **cost per part** is lowest despite the higher initial cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often blindly recommend **ceramics** because they are "advanced." However, ceramics are **too brittle** for blanking/punching operations involving shock loading. Always check for impact loading before recommending ceramics.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Secondary Hardening in HSS — Concept (3 marks):**

Secondary hardening is the phenomenon in which the hardness of HSS **increases** during the tempering stage (instead of decreasing as in plain carbon steels). It occurs because:

1. During austenitizing at ~1200 °C, alloying elements (V, Mo, W) dissolve into austenite.
2. After quenching, the supersaturated martensite is in a *metastable* state.
3. During tempering at **530–570 °C**, fine alloy carbides — particularly **V₄C₃, Mo₂C, and W₂C** — **precipitate coherently** within the martensite matrix.
4. These nano-scale precipitates are extremely hard and create strong **coherency strain fields** that impede dislocation motion.
5. The result is a **peak in hardness** at the tempering temperature, *higher* than the as-quenched hardness in some cases.

**Why Double or Triple Tempering? (4 marks):**

- After the first temper, ~10–25 % **retained austenite** remains in the microstructure.
- This retained austenite is **unstable and undesirable** — it can transform to brittle martensite during service, causing **dimensional changes and cracking**.
- The second tempering at 530–570 °C transforms this retained austenite to **tempered martensite**, which is tough and stable.
- A third temper ensures complete transformation — typical for **precision tools, dies, and gauges** where dimensional stability is critical.

**Result:** Hardness peaks at **64–66 HRC**; toughness is preserved; dimensional stability is achieved.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **secondary hardening** with **work hardening**. Work hardening is a cold-deformation phenomenon; secondary hardening is a **heat-treatment precipitation phenomenon unique to alloy steels containing strong carbide formers (V, Mo, W)**. Marks are deducted for confusing these two terms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Tool Materials Module 4**

### Quick-Fire Definitions (2 minutes revision)
- ☐ **Tool material** = material used to make dies, punches, cutting tools.
- ☐ **Hot hardness** = hardness retained at elevated temperature.
- ☐ **Toughness** = energy absorbed before fracture.
- ☐ **Wear resistance** ∝ **hardness** (Archard's law).
- ☐ **Red hardness** = hot hardness (colloquial term from glowing red color).

### Must-Know Materials
- ☐ **W1** — cheapest, cold-work, low production runs.
- ☐ **D2/D3** — high-C, high-Cr cold-work die steels (blanking, drawing).
- ☐ **H11/H13** — hot-work die steels (forging, die casting, extrusion).
- ☐ **M2** — the universal HSS (drills, taps, milling cutters).
- ☐ **M42** — premium HSS with 8 % Co (cutting hard materials).
- ☐ **WC-Co** — cemented carbide (P, M, K ISO grades).
- ☐ **Al₂O₃** — ceramic for high-speed finishing.
- ☐ **CBN** — second hardest; for hardened steel (HRC > 50).
- ☐ **PCD** — diamond; for non-ferrous (Al, Cu) and composites only.

### Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ **Taylor's equation:** $V \cdot T^{n} = C$
- ☐ **Speed-life ratio:** $\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$
- ☐ **Hot hardness decay:** $H_{hot} = H_{RT} \cdot e^{-k(T - T_{RT})}$
- ☐ **Archard's wear:** $W \propto \frac{1}{H}$
- ☐ **Hardness conversion:** $HRC \approx 10 \cdot \log_{10}(HV)$

### Must-Know Heat Treatment Facts
- ☐ HSS austenitizing temperature: **1200–1220 °C**.
- ☐ HSS tempering temperature: **540–570 °C** (double tempering).
- ☐ **Secondary hardening** due to V₄C₃, Mo₂C precipitation.

### Must-Know Selection Rules
- ☐ **Cold + high volume** → Carbide.
- ☐ **Hot + impact** → H13 hot-work steel.
- ☐ **Cutting hardened steel** → CBN.
- ☐ **Non-ferrous high speed** → PCD.
- ☐ **General purpose** → M2 HSS.

### Numerical Solving Template
- ☐ Identify given and asked.
- ☐ Recall correct formula.
- ☐ Check units (m/min, min, HRC).
- ☐ Apply logarithmic or power transformation carefully.
- ☐ Final answer with units in a box.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Strategy:** Always start Part B long answers with a **classification diagram or table**, then proceed to *principles → properties → applications → numerical*. This structure aligns with the KTU marking scheme and secures maximum marks across all COs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runnerless Moulds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Runnerless Moulds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Runnerless Mould (Hot Runner Mould):** A runnerless mould, more precisely termed a **Hot Runner Mould (HRM)** or **Hot Manifold Mould**, is an injection mould (or transfer mould) in which the *runner system* and *gates* are maintained at an elevated temperature—above the melting/solidification point of the feed material (polymer melt or metal/alloy melt in MIM/casting variants)—throughout the entire injection cycle. This thermal management ensures that the material within the runners never freezes, thereby eliminating the formation of a solidified runner, sprue, or cold slug that would otherwise have to be ejected, separated, and discarded as scrap after each shot.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context of **Metal Forming and Tool Design (PCPET602)**, runnerless moulds are studied as a critical tooling innovation primarily under:
1. **Plastic injection mould design** (the dominant industrial application), and
2. **Powder Metallurgy / Metal Injection Moulding (MIM)** tooling systems where the feed is a metal-polymer binder mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms used in KTU answer scripts (use any):**
> - Hot Runner Mould
> - Runnerless Injection Mould
> - Hot Manifold Mould
> - Thermally Balanced Mould

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heated Highway"

Imagine a city where vehicles (molten plastic/metal) leave a central factory (the injection nozzle/cylinder) and travel on a road network to reach delivery points (cavities). In a **cold runner mould**, the road is laid over a snow field—vehicles *freeze solid* the moment they stop moving, leaving a frozen shell behind that must be chipped off, collected, and thrown away every cycle. **Waste!**

In a **runnerless mould**, the entire road network (the manifold and hot nozzles) is kept warm by underground heating coils—just warm enough that vehicles stay liquid even when momentarily stationary. Only the cargo that actually enters the cavity freezes to form the useful product. **No road-ice waste. No chippers. No regrind.**

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise this analogy for 2-mark definition questions:**
> *"A runnerless mould is one in which the runners and gates are kept hot (above melt temperature) so that the material in them does not freeze between shots, eliminating runner scrap."*

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Industrial Benchmarks)

| Metric | Cold Runner | Hot Runner (Runnerless) |
|---|---|---|
| Material waste per shot | **15–40 %** of shot weight | **0–5 %** (gate only) |
| Cycle time reduction | Baseline | **10–30 % faster** |
| Part consistency | Moderate | High (uniform melt temp at cavity) |
| Tooling cost multiplier | **1.0 ×** | **1.3 – 2.5 ×** |
| Energy at runner | None | **~ 50–500 W** per zone |

## 1.4 Classification of Runnerless Moulds (High-Yield for KTU)

The two principal architectural families are:

- **Internally Heated (Conduction-Heated) Hot Runner System** — A heater cartridge is inserted *inside* a thick steel manifold body; heat conducts outward to the melt passages. Used for **general-purpose commodity plastics** (PP, PE, PS).
- **Externally Heated (Convection / Open-Channel) Hot Runner System** — A toroidal (donut-shaped) heater surrounds an *open* melt channel; melt circulates in a loop and is heated directly by convection. Used for **highly viscous / shear-sensitive polymers** (PVC, PC, thermosets).

A third hybrid class — **Insulated Runner Moulds** — uses thick steel runners that retain heat from successive shots but have no active heating element. This is a *semi-runnerless* design and is sometimes accepted as a KTU answer under the broader umbrella of "runnerless moulds."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A runnerless mould executes the following sequence each injection cycle:

1. **Manifold Heating Phase (Continuous):** Cartridge heaters embedded in the manifold maintain the melt at a uniform temperature $T_m$, where $T_m$ lies between the **melt temperature** $T_{melt}$ and the **degradation temperature** $T_{deg}$. Typically, $T_{melt} < T_m < T_{deg}$.
2. **Injection Phase:** Molten material is injected from the plasticating unit through the *sprue bushing* into the heated manifold. Because the manifold temperature equals $T_m$, the melt neither freezes nor degrades as it traverses the runner network.
3. **Gate Opening (Hot Tip / Valve Gate):** The melt reaches the **hot nozzle** at the cavity face. Two gate architectures exist:
   - **Hot Tip Gate** — A small protrusion extends into the cavity; the gate freezes only when injection pressure is released.
   - **Valve Gate (Shut-Off Nozzle)** — A mechanically or hydraulically actuated pin mechanically seals the gate, eliminating gate vestige.
4. **Cavity Packing & Cooling:** The cavity (cooled by cooling channels) freezes the part. The runner (heated) remains molten.
5. **Mould Opening & Ejection:** Only the part (and possibly a small cold-slug sub-gate) is ejected. The runner stays in the manifold, ready for the next shot.
6. **Re-injection:** No purging, no regrinding, no material loss.

## 2.2 Why "Runnerless" — The Hidden Physics

The principle relies on **controlled thermal gradients** across three zones:

$$
T_{cavity} \;<\; T_{gate} \;\approx\; T_{runner} \;<\; T_{deg}
$$

- The **cavity** is cooled (water/oil channels) to freeze the part.
- The **runner** is heated to prevent freezing.
- The **gate** sits at the critical interface — it must stay open *during* injection and freeze *after* packing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the design:** In a cold runner, the heat extracted by the mould steel during cooling is greater than the frictional/viscous heating of the melt, so the melt freezes. In a hot runner, the **active heater input** balances this heat extraction at every runner location, maintaining isothermal flow.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables. LaTeX delimiters `$` are kept inline for clarity.

| # | Quantity / Formula | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{heater} \;=\; \dfrac{m \, c_p \, \Delta T}{t}$ | $m$ = mass of melt in manifold (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K), $\Delta T$ = permitted cooling (K), $t$ = cycle time (s) | Heater power needed to keep melt from cooling during dwell |
| 2 | $\Delta P_{runner} \;=\; \dfrac{8 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, r^{4}}$ | $\mu$ = melt viscosity (Pa·s), $L$ = runner length (m), $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s), $r$ = runner radius (m) | Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop in a cylindrical runner |
| 3 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{V}}{\pi r^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \, \pi r^{2}}$ | $\dot{V}$ = vol. flow rate, $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $\rho$ = density | Mean melt velocity in runner |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \, Q}{\pi \, r^{3}}$ | shear rate (s⁻¹) | Shear rate in cylindrical runner |
| 5 | $\tau_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2 L}$ | wall shear stress (Pa) | Shear at runner wall |
| 6 | $T_{m,design} \;=\; T_{melt} + (10 \text{ to } 30)\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | design melt temperature | Standard practice for hot runner setting |
| 7 | $t_{freeze,gate} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, c_p \, h_{gate}^{2}}{4 \, k \, (T_{melt} - T_{mould})}$ | $h_{gate}$ = gate land length, $k$ = thermal conductivity | Time for gate to freeze after injection |
| 8 | $\eta_{eff} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{part}}{m_{part} + m_{runner}}$ | mass efficiency | Material utilisation (≈ 1.0 for runnerless) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Multi-cavity dashboards, bumper moulds (8–32 cavities) where runner scrap cost is prohibitive.
- **Medical Devices:** Cleanroom moulding of syringe bodies and IV components — hot runners reduce particulate contamination.
- **Thin-Wall Packaging:** Caps, lids, and containers where the gate area is small and runner elimination vastly improves cycle time.
- **Metal Injection Moulding (MIM):** Binder removal and feedstock control are simplified when runner scrap (containing expensive metal powder) is eliminated.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2-mark favourites:** "List any four advantages of a runnerless mould" or "Differentiate between internally heated and externally heated runnerless moulds."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pressure Drop in a Cylindrical Hot Runner

The hot runner behaves as a *circular pipe* carrying a power-law fluid. For the KTU board, the simplified **Newtonian** model (Hagen–Poiseuille) is sufficient.

### Step 1: Starting Equation — Navier–Stokes for Steady, Laminar, Fully-Developed Flow in a Pipe

For a horizontal pipe of radius $r$, with $z$ as the flow axis, the axial momentum equation reduces to:

$$
\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \dfrac{dv_z}{dr} \right) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

This simplification assumes:
- Steady flow ($\partial/\partial t = 0$)
- Fully developed ($\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$)
- Incompressible melt
- Negligible body forces

### Step 2: Integrate Once with Respect to $r$

Multiplying both sides by $r$ and integrating:

$$
r \dfrac{dv_z}{dr} \;=\; \dfrac{r^{2}}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz} + C_{1}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Boundary Condition (No-Slip at Wall)

At $r = R$ (wall), $v_z = 0$:

$$
0 \;=\; \dfrac{R^{2}}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz} + C_{1} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_{1} \;=\; -\dfrac{R^{2}}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Separate Variables

$$
\dfrac{dv_z}{dr} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}\left( r - \dfrac{R^{2}}{r} \right)
$$

### Step 5: Integrate Again to Get Velocity Profile

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}\left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)
$$

This is the classical **parabolic Hagen–Poiseuille velocity profile**, with maximum at the centreline ($r = 0$) and zero at the wall.

### Step 6: Compute Volumetric Flow Rate

Integrating across the cross-section:

$$
Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r)\, 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q \;=\; -\dfrac{\pi}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}\int_{0}^{R} (R^{2}r - r^{3})\,dr
$$

$$
Q \;=\; -\dfrac{\pi}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}\left[ \dfrac{R^{2}r^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; -\dfrac{\pi}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}\left( \dfrac{R^{4}}{2} - \dfrac{R^{4}}{4} \right)
$$

$$
Q \;=\; -\dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

### Step 7: Solve for Pressure Drop Across Runner of Length $L$

Since $dP/dz$ is constant for a fully developed flow:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{8\,\mu\,L\,Q}{\pi R^{4}}
$$

Replacing $R$ with $r$ to match the formula sheet:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{8\,\mu\,L\,Q}{\pi \, r^{4}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Pressure drop scales as the **inverse fourth power** of the runner radius. Doubling the runner diameter reduces pressure drop by a factor of **16**. This is why hot runner diameters are kept as large as mould geometry permits — to stay within the injection machine's pressure budget.

## 3.2 Derivation — Heater Power for Hot Runner Manifold

### Step 1: Energy Balance

Heat lost by the melt (to surrounding mould steel) must equal heat supplied by the heater, ignoring transients:

$$
P_{heater} \, t \;=\; m \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

### Step 2: Solve for Power

$$
\boxed{\;P_{heater} \;=\; \dfrac{m \, c_p \, \Delta T}{t}\;}
$$

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Given:**
- Mass of melt in manifold, $m = 0.08$ kg
- Specific heat of polypropylene, $c_p = 2300$ J/(kg·K)
- Permitted cooling, $\Delta T = 5$ K
- Cycle time, $t = 30$ s

**Solution:**

$$
P_{heater} \;=\; \dfrac{0.08 \times 2300 \times 5}{30}
$$

$$
P_{heater} \;=\; \dfrac{920}{30} \;\approx\; 30.67\;\text{W}
$$

**Result:** A 35 W cartridge heater (next standard size) is sufficient to maintain the manifold.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hot Runner vs Cold Runner

| Design / Performance Aspect | Cold Runner Mould | Runnerless (Hot Runner) Mould |
|---|---|---|
| Runner temperature | Below melt (frozen) | At or above melt |
| Material efficiency $\eta_{eff}$ | 0.60 – 0.85 | **0.95 – 1.00** |
| Cycle time | Longer (full runner cooling) | **Shorter** (no runner cooling) |
| Clamp tonnage requirement | Higher (sprue + runner projection) | Lower (no sprue/runner to break) |
| Colour / material change | Easy (runners purged) | Difficult (dead spots trap material) |
| Initial tooling cost | **Low** | **High** (1.3 – 2.5 ×) |
| Maintenance | Simple | Requires temperature controller servicing |
| Part weld-line quality | Inconsistent | **Better** (uniform melt temp) |
| Regrind handling | Required | **Not required** |
| Suitable for | Short runs, prototyping, multi-colour | **Long production runs, single material** |

## 3.4 Design Considerations (Mark-Winning Points)

1. **Thermal Balance:** Heater wattage must balance convective + conductive losses without overshooting $T_{deg}$.
2. **Manifold Geometry:** Should be symmetric for balanced fill; runner lengths to each cavity must be equal.
3. **Gate Location & Type:** Hot tip for cosmetic parts; valve gate for zero vestige.
4. **Leak Prevention:** Heater cartridges must be sealed against melt leak-back — failure leads to **spider burns / drool**.
5. **Material Compatibility:** Shear-sensitive polymers (PVC, POM) require low-shear hot runner designs.
6. **Purge Access:** Even runnerless moulds need an occasional cold-slug well (a small pocket that traps the first contaminated melt).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Hot Runner (Runnerless) Mould

The following Mermaid block maps the **functional architecture** of a runnerless mould — the alternative to a complex physical cross-section drawing. All node IDs are alphanumeric and free of reserved keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Injection Unit / Plasticator] --> B[Sprue Bushing]
    B --> C[Hot Manifold Block]
    C --> D1[Heater Cartridge Zone 1]
    C --> D2[Heater Cartridge Zone 2]
    C --> D3[Heater Cartridge Zone N]
    C --> E1[Heated Runner Channel A]
    C --> E2[Heated Runner Channel B]
    C --> E3[Heated Runner Channel C]
    E1 --> F1[Hot Tip Nozzle Cavity 1]
    E2 --> F2[Hot Tip Nozzle Cavity 2]
    E3 --> F3[Valve Gate Nozzle Cavity 3]
    F1 --> G1[Cavity Plate Cavity 1]
    F2 --> G2[Cavity Plate Cavity 2]
    F3 --> G3[Cavity Plate Cavity 3]
    G1 --> H1[Cooling Channel Circuit 1]
    G2 --> H2[Cooling Channel Circuit 2]
    G3 --> H3[Cooling Channel Circuit 3]
    H1 --> I1[Ejector Plate]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    I1 --> J[Finished Part Ejection]
    D1 --> K[PID Temperature Controller]
    D2 --> K
    D3 --> K
    K --> L[Thermocouple Feedback TC1 TC2 TCN]
    L --> K
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The single hot manifold (block **C**) splits into multiple heated channels (E1/E2/E3) feeding individual cavities (G1/G2/G3). Each manifold zone has its own heater (D1/D2/D3) and thermocouple, wired to a central PID controller (**K**) — this is the *closed-loop thermal control* that defines a runnerless system.

## 4.2 Hot Tip vs Valve Gate — Gate Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HT[Hot Tip Gate Assembly]
        HT1[Melt Channel] --> HT2[Heater Around Tip]
        HT2 --> HT3[Small Frozen Gate Vestige]
        HT3 --> HT4[Part Surface]
    end
    subgraph VG[Valve Gate Assembly]
        VG1[Melt Channel] --> VG2[Heated Nozzle Body]
        VG2 --> VG3[Mechanical Valve Pin]
        VG3 --> VG4[Open or Closed Gate]
        VG5[Hydraulic Pneumatic Actuator] --> VG3
        VG4 --> VG6[Part Surface Clean Cut]
    end
```

## 4.3 Thermal Zoning of the Mould

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Zone A Injection Nozzle — Hot 220 C] --> Z2[Zone B Manifold Center — Hot 230 C]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone C Hot Runner Channel — Hot 225 C]
    Z3 --> Z4[Zone D Gate — Transitional 200 C]
    Z4 --> Z5[Zone E Cavity Surface — Cool 60 C]
    Z5 --> Z6[Zone F Ejector Side — Cool 80 C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal gradient interpretation:** The temperature must step down gradually from manifold to cavity to ensure the part freezes first and the gate freezes last (or *never*, in the case of valve-gate shut-off). Sudden gradients cause **freeze lines** and short shots.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Temperature Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Temperature] --> SUM[Comparator]
    TC[Thermocouple in Manifold] --> FB[Feedback Signal mV]
    FB --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal]
    ERR --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID --> OUT[Heater Drive Signal]
    OUT --> HTR[Heater Cartridge]
    HTR --> MAN[Manifold Body]
    MAN --> TC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution reminder (KTU 2024 ESE pattern):**
> Part A → 3 marks each (short answer)
> Part B → 14 marks each (two sub-parts of 7 marks, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B)

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a runnerless mould. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A runnerless mould, also known as a hot runner mould, is an injection mould in which the runner system and gates are maintained at a temperature above the solidification point of the melt by means of external heaters, so that the material in the runners remains molten and is not ejected as scrap between cycles.

**Advantages (1.5 marks — any two):**
1. Elimination of runner scrap → high material utilisation (≈ 100 %).
2. Shorter cycle time, as the runner does not need to cool and re-melt.
3. Reduced post-moulding operations (no trimming, no regrinding).
4. Lower clamp tonnage required because sprue/runner is not ejected.
5. Better part quality due to uniform melt temperature at the gate.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between internally heated and externally heated hot runner systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Internally Heated | Externally Heated |
|---|---|---|
| Heater location | Inside the manifold body | Outside, around an open melt channel |
| Heat transfer mode | Conduction | Convection + radiation |
| Channel design | Closed, drilled in steel | Open loop, exposed to heater |
| Melt residence time | Short | **Long** (loop circulation) |
| Material suitability | General thermoplastics | Heat/shear-sensitive polymers (e.g., PVC) |
| Temperature uniformity | Excellent | Moderate |
| Risk of melt degradation | Low | **Higher** (residence time) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below as practice alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **hot tip gate** runnerless mould. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition & Position (2 marks):** A hot tip gate is a nozzle whose tip projects slightly into the cavity. The heater is embedded around the nozzle body, keeping the melt inside the nozzle molten right up to the cavity face. The gate freezes only when injection pressure is removed.

2. **Working Cycle (3 marks):**
   - Manifold heats melt to $T_m$.
   - Melt flows through hot runner channels and the hot tip nozzle.
   - The hot tip deposits melt into the cavity without freezing.
   - Once the cavity is full and packing is complete, injection pressure is released.
   - The thin gate freezes because the surrounding cavity surface is cold (cooled by water channels) and the small gate land area loses heat rapidly.
   - During mould opening, the part separates at the frozen gate, leaving a small *gate vestige* (≤ 0.3 mm).

3. **Key Features (2 marks):**
   - Single-cavity or multi-cavity use.
   - Low cost compared to valve gate.
   - Leaves a small witness mark (vestige) — acceptable for hidden gates or non-cosmetic surfaces.

**Q. (b)** A hot runner system carries **0.08 kg** of polypropylene melt in the manifold. The specific heat is **2300 J/kg·K**. The cycle time is **30 s**, and the permissible melt temperature drop is **5 °C**. Calculate the **heater power** required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $m = 0.08$ kg
- $c_p = 2300$ J/(kg·K)
- $\Delta T = 5$ K
- $t = 30$ s

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write the energy balance equation:

$$
P_{heater} \;=\; \dfrac{m \, c_p \, \Delta T}{t}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute the values:

$$
P_{heater} \;=\; \dfrac{0.08 \times 2300 \times 5}{30}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the numerator:

$$
0.08 \times 2300 \;=\; 184 \;\text{J/K}
$$

$$
184 \times 5 \;=\; 920 \;\text{J}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final division:

$$
P_{heater} \;=\; \dfrac{920}{30} \;\approx\; 30.67 \;\text{W}
$$

**Answer:** A standard **35 W** cartridge heater should be selected for safety margin. *(7/7)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**Q. (a)** Compare cold runner moulds and runnerless moulds under any **seven** criteria. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (tabular, 1 mark per comparison row):**

| # | Criterion | Cold Runner | Runnerless (Hot Runner) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Runner state | Solidified each cycle | Remains molten |
| 2 | Material efficiency | 60–85 % | 95–100 % |
| 3 | Cycle time | Longer | Shorter by 10–30 % |
| 4 | Tooling cost | Lower | 1.3–2.5 × higher |
| 5 | Colour change | Easy | Difficult (dead spots) |
| 6 | Post-processing | Trim + regrind needed | Minimal |
| 7 | Temperature control | Simple | Needs PID controllers |

**Q. (b)** In a hot runner, the melt viscosity is **350 Pa·s**, runner length is **80 mm**, volumetric flow rate is **2 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s**, and runner radius is **3 mm**. Calculate the **pressure drop**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $\mu = 350$ Pa·s
- $L = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- $Q = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s
- $r = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write Hagen–Poiseuille equation:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{8\,\mu\,L\,Q}{\pi\,r^{4}}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute numerator:

$$
8 \times 350 \times 0.08 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
=\; 8 \times 350 \times 0.16 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
=\; 448 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 4.48 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{Pa·m}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute denominator:

$$
\pi \times (0.003)^{4} \;=\; 3.1416 \times 8.1 \times 10^{-11} \;\approx\; 2.545 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final ratio:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{4.48 \times 10^{-4}}{2.545 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P \;\approx\; 1.76 \times 10^{6}\;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 1.76\;\text{MPa}\;}
$$

**Engineering comment:** A 1.76 MPa drop is well within typical injection machine capacity (10–140 MPa), confirming the design is feasible. *(7/7)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on Runnerless Mould questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "insulated runner" with "runnerless."** An insulated runner still freezes between shots — it is *semi-runnerless*, not fully runnerless. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Pressure drop must be in **Pa or MPa**, heater power in **W or kW**. A bare number = 0.5 mark lost.
> 3. **Wrong formula choice for the runner profile.** Hot runners are *circular cross-section*, not rectangular. Using a slot-flow equation instead of Hagen–Poiseuille = **full 7-mark loss** on numerical.
> 4. **Missing the PID / thermocouple block in diagrams.** A runnerless mould without a temperature controller is *incomplete*; examiners expect at least one mention of closed-loop thermal control.
> 5. **Sketching a hot runner like a cold runner.** Always show the **heater band around the manifold** and the **heated nozzle tip projecting into the cavity**. A line diagram with no thermal annotations loses sketch marks.
> 6. **Not naming the gate type.** "Hot tip" vs "valve gate" distinction is worth a full 1–2 marks in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Runnerless Moulds**

- **Definition (must know verbatim):** Mould whose runners/gates are kept above melt temperature by external heating so material never solidifies in them.
- **Two main types:** Internally heated (conduction) and externally heated (convection/open-channel).
- **Two main gate types:** Hot tip gate (small vestige) and Valve gate (clean shut-off, no vestige).
- **Three thermal zones:** Hot manifold → transitional gate → cool cavity.
- **Key formula — Pressure drop:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{8\mu L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$ — pressure drop is **inversely proportional to r⁴**.
- **Key formula — Heater power:** $P = \dfrac{m\,c_p\,\Delta T}{t}$.
- **Material efficiency** of runnerless moulds ≈ **0.95 – 1.00**; cold runner = 0.60 – 0.85.
- **Advantages:** no scrap, faster cycle, lower clamp force, better weld-line quality, no regrind handling.
- **Disadvantages:** high tool cost, complex temperature control, difficult colour change, leak/maintenance risk.
- **Applications:** Automotive dashboards, medical disposables, thin-wall packaging, MIM feedstock moulding.
- **Thermal control:** Always uses a **PID controller with thermocouple feedback** — closed-loop control is non-negotiable for KTU answers.
- **Exam trick:** If asked to "differentiate," present a **table**; if asked "explain working," include a **labelled block diagram**; if asked "calculate," always state the **formula, substitution, and units**.
- **Industrial benchmark to memorise:** A typical multi-cavity runnerless mould uses **~50–500 W per zone**, with manifold held **10–30 °C above** the polymer's nominal melt temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Blow Moulding and Extrusion Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Blow Moulding and Extrusion Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Extrusion Dies — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Extrusion Die (KTU 2024 Definition):** A precision-engineered tooling component mounted at the discharge end of an extruder that shapes molten polymer (or metal) into a continuous profile of desired cross-section by forcing the melt through a shaped orifice under controlled temperature and pressure.

In polymer processing, the extrusion die is the **heart of profile accuracy**. Without the die, an extruder is simply a melt pump — the die imparts the final geometry to the product.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

Imagine a **cake icing nozzle** used by a baker. The baker fills a piping bag with cream, and depending on the shape cut at the tip (star, round, leaf), the cream emerges in that shape. An extrusion die is the industrial, high-precision, heated, pressure-rated equivalent of this icing tip — except it operates at temperatures of **180 °C–280 °C**, pressures of **5–50 MPa**, and produces profiles at rates of **10–500 kg/hr** with tolerances as tight as **±0.05 mm**.

## 1.2 Blow Moulding Die — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blow Moulding Die (Parison Die):** A specialised annular die that extrudes a hollow, tubular molten parison (a plastic tube), which is subsequently inflated inside a closed mould cavity using compressed air to produce a hollow plastic article.

Blow moulding is the dominant process for manufacturing **PET bottles**, **HDPE containers**, jerry cans, fuel tanks, and toys.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

Think of blowing a **balloon inside a box**. First, you squeeze toothpaste (parison) into a hollow tube shape. Then you close two halves of a box (mould) around it. Finally, you blow air through the middle — the toothpaste expands and takes the exact shape of the inside of the box. That's blow moulding in plain English!

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Parison** | Hollow molten tube extruded by the blow moulding die |
| **Die Swell** | Expansion of extrudate diameter upon exiting the die |
| **Land Length** | Straight parallel section downstream of die orifice |
| **Die Lip** | Final forming surface that sets the extrudate profile |
| **Mandrel** | Central core inside an annular die forming the inner bore |
| **Blow-up Ratio (BUR)** | Ratio of mould cavity diameter to parison diameter |
| **Draw-down Ratio (DDR)** | Ratio of parison outer diameter to final part diameter |

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard extrusion temperature for HDPE** is around **180–220 °C** and for **PET** around **260–280 °C**, with operating pressures typically between **5–35 MPa**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Extrusion Dies

Extrusion dies are classified based on the product geometry they produce:

- **Sheet/Film Dies** — flat products (e.g., flat-film die, coathanger die)
- **Pipe/Tube Dies** — annular products (spider die, spiral mandrel die)
- **Profile Dies** — custom cross-sections (window profiles, gaskets)
- **Wire Coating Dies** — pressure die, tubing die
- **Blow Moulding Dies** — annular parison with programmed wall thickness

## 2.2 Critical Design Parameters — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Die Land Length ($L$)

The land length is the parallel-walled section after the die orifice. It stabilises flow and reduces extrudate swell.

$$L = k \cdot h$$

where:
- $L$ = land length
- $h$ = die gap height
- $k$ = empirical constant (**typically 5–15**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a land?** Without sufficient land, the melt exits the die with non-uniform velocity profile, frozen-in stresses, and excessive die swell. The land acts as a **flow straightener and stress-relaxation zone**.

### Step 2: Pressure Drop Across the Die

For a simple slit die, Hagen-Poiseuille flow applies:

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L}{w \, h^{3}}$$

where:
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa)
- $\mu$ = melt viscosity (Pa·s)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $L$ = land length (m)
- $w$ = die width (m)
- $h$ = die gap height (m)

### Step 3: Extrudate (Die) Swell

When polymer melt exits the die, it expands due to elastic recovery of stored elastic energy:

$$B = \frac{D_{\text{ext}}}{D_0}$$

where:
- $B$ = swell ratio (typically **1.1–1.5**)
- $D_{\text{ext}}$ = extrudate diameter
- $D_0$ = die diameter

### Step 4: Blow-up Ratio (BUR)

$$\text{BUR} = \frac{D_{\text{mould}}}{D_{\text{parison}}}$$

Typical BUR values: **1.5–4.0**

### Step 5: Draw-down Ratio (DDR)

$$\text{DDR} = \frac{D_{\text{parison}}}{D_{\text{part}}}$$

Typical DDR values: **2–5**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure drop (slit die) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu Q L}{w h^{3}}$ | $\mu$ in Pa·s, $Q$ in m³/s, dimensions in m | Die sizing, motor power |
| Die swell ratio | $B = D_{\text{ext}} / D_0$ | dimensionless | Compensation in die machining |
| Blow-up ratio | $\text{BUR} = D_{\text{mould}} / D_{\text{parison}}$ | dimensionless | Mould size selection |
| Draw-down ratio | $\text{DDR} = D_{\text{parison}} / D_{\text{part}}$ | dimensionless | Material distribution control |
| Land length ratio | $L/h = k$ (k = 5–15) | dimensionless | Surface finish optimisation |
| Throughput (annular die) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L}(R_o^{4} - R_i^{4})$ | m³/s | Parison flow rate |
| Wall thickness (parison) | $t = \dfrac{m_{\text{part}}}{\pi \rho \, D_{\text{part}} \, L_{\text{part}}}$ | m | Mass balance for die programming |
| Shear rate (capillary) | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | s⁻¹ | Melt fracture prediction |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **board-exam ready** and can be directly cited with proper substitution. The swell ratio $B$ is **not a constant** — it depends on shear rate, temperature, and residence time.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PET bottle industry** uses blow moulding dies producing **~500 billion bottles/year** globally.
- **HDPE pipe extrusion** uses spider dies to manufacture pressure pipes for water/gas.
- **PVC window profiles** use streamlined profile dies with calibration units.
- **Stretch blow moulding** (PET) combines **axial stretch + blow-up** to achieve biaxial orientation, dramatically improving **tensile strength** and **barrier properties**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure Drop Across an Annular Die (Parison Die)

**Objective:** Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for fully developed, laminar, isothermal flow of a Newtonian fluid through an annular die.

### Given Assumptions

- Steady-state, laminar flow
- Newtonian fluid (constant $\mu$)
- No-slip at walls
- Axisymmetric flow
- Fully developed velocity profile

### Setting Up the Momentum Equation

For a cylindrical coordinate system with flow in the $z$-direction:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}$$

### First Integration

Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate:

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} r^{2} + C_{1}$$

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} r + \frac{C_{1}}{r}$$

### Second Integration

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} r^{2} + C_{1} \ln(r) + C_{2}$$

### Applying Boundary Conditions

**BC1:** At $r = R_i$ (inner mandrel radius), $v_z = 0$

$$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} R_i^{2} + C_{1} \ln(R_i) + C_{2}$$

**BC2:** At $r = R_o$ (outer die wall radius), $v_z = 0$

$$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} R_o^{2} + C_{1} \ln(R_o) + C_{2}$$

### Solving for Constants

Subtracting the two equations:

$$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}) + C_{1} \ln\left(\frac{R_o}{R_i}\right)$$

$$C_{1} = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\frac{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)}$$

### Final Velocity Profile

$$\begin{aligned}
v_z(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\left[ R_o^{2} - r^{2} + \frac{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{R_o}\right) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Volumetric Flow Rate by Integration

$$Q = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} v_z(r) \, (2\pi r) \, dr$$

After integration (substituting the constants):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q = -\frac{\pi}{8\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\left[ R_o^{4} - R_i^{4} - \frac{(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}\left[ R_o^{4} - R_i^{4} - \frac{(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \right]}$$

This is the **governing design equation for an annular parison die**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Parison Wall Thickness Programming

The **parison programmer** controls the annular gap of the die to deliver varying wall thickness along the length of the parison.

### Mass Balance Per Unit Length

For a parison of length $L_p$ extruded in time $t_p$:

$$m_{\text{parison}} = \rho \cdot Q \cdot t_p$$

### Final Bottle Mass Conservation

The mass of the bottle equals the mass of the parison:

$$m_{\text{bottle}} = \rho \cdot \pi \cdot d_{\text{avg}} \cdot A_{\text{wall}}$$

where $A_{\text{wall}}$ = total internal surface area of the bottle.

### Wall Thickness at any Vertical Position

If $z$ is the height coordinate along the parison (with $z = 0$ at the top, $z = L_p$ at the bottom):

$$t(z) = \frac{m_{\text{bottle}}(z)}{\rho \cdot \pi \cdot D_{\text{parison}}(z) \cdot dz}$$

### Algorithm for Programming

For uniform bottle wall thickness:

1. Calculate required parison length: $L_p = $ (bottle height) $\times$ DDR
2. Calculate required parison diameter: $D_{\text{parison}} = D_{\text{mould}}$ / BUR
3. At each $z$ interval, adjust the mandrel position to vary the gap $h(z) = R_o - R_i(z)$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** HDPE bottle of mass = **40 g**, mould diameter = **80 mm**, BUR = 2.5, DDR = 3.

**Step 1:** Calculate parison diameter
$$D_{\text{parison}} = \frac{D_{\text{mould}}}{\text{BUR}} = \frac{80}{2.5} = 32 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** If bottle height = 200 mm, parison length:
$$L_p = 200 \times 3 = 600 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Average wall thickness of parison:
$$t_{\text{avg}} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot \pi \cdot D_{\text{parison}} \cdot L_p}$$

Taking $\rho_{\text{HDPE}} = 954$ kg/m³, $m = 0.040$ kg:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{0.040}{954 \times \pi \times 0.032 \times 0.600} \\
&= \frac{0.040}{57.55} \\
&= 6.95 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
&\approx 0.7 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** The die gap should be programmed to deliver wall thickness variation from **~1.0 mm (top)** to **~0.5 mm (bottom)** to compensate for sag.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Engineering Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Land length $L$ | ↓ swell, ↑ pressure drop | ↑ swell, ↓ pressure | Surface finish vs throughput |
| Die gap $h$ | ↑ throughput, ↓ shear | ↓ throughput, ↑ shear | Output vs melt fracture |
| Mandrel taper angle | Flow balance | Stagnation | Distribution vs residence time |
| Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ | ↓ viscosity, ↑ output | ↑ viscosity, ↓ output | Productivity vs melt quality |
| BUR | Material distribution | Thinning | Strength vs cycle time |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define: (a) Die Swell (b) Blow-up Ratio (c) Parison.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Die Swell:** It is the phenomenon where the diameter (or cross-section) of the polymer extrudate becomes larger than the die orifice dimension after exiting the die, due to elastic recovery of the viscoelastic melt. Mathematically, $B = D_{\text{ext}}/D_0$.

(b) **Blow-up Ratio (BUR):** It is the ratio of the maximum diameter of the blow mould cavity to the initial outer diameter of the parison. $\text{BUR} = D_{\text{mould}} / D_{\text{parison}}$.

(c) **Parison:** A hollow, tubular molten plastic mass extruded from an annular blow moulding die, which is subsequently inflated inside a closed mould to form a hollow article.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four types of extrusion dies used in polymer processing.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sheet/Film Die** (e.g., coathanger die, flat-film die)
2. **Pipe/Tube Die** (e.g., spider die, spiral mandrel die)
3. **Profile Die** (for custom cross-sections like window frames)
4. **Wire Coating Die** (pressure die and tubing die)
5. **Blow Moulding Die** (annular parison die)

*(Any four correct answers carry full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch, the design of an annular parison die used in extrusion blow moulding. Discuss the role of parison programming.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An **annular parison die** consists of the following components:

1. **Die Body** — heavy steel housing with heating channels
2. **Outer Die Lip** — forms the outer surface of the parison
3. **Inner Mandrel** — forms the inner surface, supported by spider legs
4. **Land Region** — parallel annular section (typically $L/h = 5$–$15$)
5. **Air Passage** — central channel for compressed air inflation
6. **Reservoir** — melt distribution zone before the orifice

**Design Equations:**

Pressure drop across annular die:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L}\left[ R_o^{4} - R_i^{4} - \frac{(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \right]$$

Die land length: $L = k \cdot h$ where $k$ ranges from 5 to 15.

**Role of Parison Programming:**

Since the parison sags under gravity and stretches under its own weight before mould closure, the wall thickness must be varied along its length. A **parison programmer** uses a hydraulic or servo system to axially move the mandrel during extrusion, varying the annular gap $h(t) = R_o - R_i(t)$.

Typical programming profile:
- **Top of parison:** thicker (~1.0 mm) — to compensate for stretching
- **Middle:** medium (~0.7 mm)
- **Bottom:** thinner (~0.5 mm) — since it sags less

This ensures uniform final wall thickness in the blown part.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for volumetric flow rate through an annular die for a Newtonian fluid. A parison die has outer radius 25 mm and inner radius 23 mm, length 200 mm. If the melt viscosity is 800 Pa·s and pressure drop is 5 MPa, calculate the flow rate.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation** (already established in Section 3.1):

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L}\left[ R_o^{4} - R_i^{4} - \frac{(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \right]$$

**Numerical Substitution:**

Given:
- $R_o = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $R_i = 23 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $L = 200 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\mu = 800$ Pa·s
- $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa

**Step 1:** Compute $R_o^{4}$ and $R_i^{4}$:

$$R_o^{4} = (0.025)^{4} = 3.906 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}$$
$$R_i^{4} = (0.023)^{4} = 2.798 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 2:** Compute difference:

$$R_o^{4} - R_i^{4} = 1.108 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})^{2}$:

$$R_o^{2} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$R_i^{2} = 5.29 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$R_o^{2} - R_i^{2} = 0.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})^{2} = 9.216 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\ln(R_o/R_i)$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{25}{23}\right) = \ln(1.0870) = 0.0834$$

**Step 5:** Compute correction term:

$$\frac{9.216 \times 10^{-10}}{0.0834} = 1.105 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 6:** Compute bracket:

$$1.108 \times 10^{-7} - 1.105 \times 10^{-8} = 9.975 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 7:** Compute $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{6} \times 9.975 \times 10^{-8}}{8 \times 800 \times 0.200}$$

$$Q = \frac{1.567 \times 10^{0}}{1280} = 1.224 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 8:** Convert to engineering units:

$$Q \approx 4.41 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{hr} \quad \text{(or } 73.4 \text{ L/min)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert mm → m is the **most common mistake** (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. Using $|x|$ notation in formulas breaks evaluation in LaTeX — use `ln(...)` correctly.
> 3. Not stating **assumptions** (laminar, Newtonian, isothermal) costs **0.5–1 mark**.
> 4. Skipping intermediate steps leads to **partial credit loss** — show all 5–6 steps.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the classification of extrusion dies with neat sketches. Explain the working of a coathanger die for sheet extrusion.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Extrusion Dies:**

| Type | Product Example |
|---|---|
| Sheet/Film Die | Plastic sheets, packaging films |
| Pipe/Tube Die | PVC pipes, HDPE tubes |
| Profile Die | Window/door profiles |
| Wire Coating Die | Insulated copper wires |
| Blow Moulding Die | PET bottles, containers |

**Coathanger Die — Working Principle:**

The coathanger die is named for its **manifold shape resembling a clothes hanger**. It consists of:

1. **Circular manifold** — distributes melt laterally
2. **Coathanger-shaped channel** — gradual reduction of channel cross-section from centre to ends
3. **Pre-land region** — restricts flow at the centre
4. **Final land/lip** — sets sheet thickness

**Flow Balancing Principle:** As melt flows from the centre toward the die ends, the channel cross-section decreases to compensate for pressure drop, ensuring **uniform exit velocity** across the entire lip length.

**Design Equation:**

$$\frac{d^{3}(x)}{dx} \cdot \eta(x) = \text{constant}$$

where $d(x)$ is the local die gap as a function of position $x$.

---

**(b) Calculate the pressure drop across a slit die of width 200 mm, gap 2 mm, length 50 mm for a polymer melt of viscosity 500 Pa·s flowing at a rate of 5 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $w = 0.200$ m
- $h = 0.002$ m
- $L = 0.050$ m
- $\mu = 500$ Pa·s
- $Q = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s

**Formula (Hagen-Poiseuille for slit):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L}{w \, h^{3}}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $h^{3}$:

$$h^{3} = (0.002)^{3} = 8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute all values:

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 500 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.050}{0.200 \times 8 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator:

$$\text{Num} = 12 \times 500 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.050 = 0.0150$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator:

$$\text{Den} = 0.200 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} = 1.6 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\Delta P$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{0.0150}{1.6 \times 10^{-9}} = 9.375 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 9.375 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This pressure drop is typical and acceptable for industrial sheet extrusion lines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Die Design Problems:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — always convert mm to m before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting the cubic dependence** — the gap $h$ appears as $h^{3}$, so a small gap change drastically alters pressure drop.
> 3. **Confusing slit vs annular formulas** — examiners test whether students know which formula applies.
> 4. **No free-body diagram / sketch** — for design questions, **always include a sketch** to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design of Blow Moulding and Extrusion Dies**

- ✅ **Extrusion die** shapes the melt into continuous profile; design aims at uniform velocity exit.
- ✅ **Land length ratio** $L/h$ should be **5–15** for stable flow.
- ✅ **Die swell** $B = D_{\text{ext}}/D_0$ — compensation done by **undercutting the die**.
- ✅ **Blow-up ratio (BUR)** typically **1.5–4.0**; controls material distribution.
- ✅ **Draw-down ratio (DDR)** typically **2–5**; controlled by parison take-off speed.
- ✅ **Pressure drop in slit die:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu Q L}{w h^{3}}$ — cubic dependence on $h$.
- ✅ **Annular die flow:** contains a logarithmic correction term — full derivation required.
- ✅ **Parison programming** uses servo-controlled mandrel to vary gap during extrusion.
- ✅ **Coathanger die** balances flow via decreasing channel cross-section toward die ends.
- ✅ **Spider die** uses 3–4 legs to support the mandrel — creates weld lines.
- ✅ **Blow moulding** is the dominant process for HDPE containers and PET bottles.
- ✅ Standard extrusion temps: **HDPE 180–220 °C**, **PET 260–280 °C**, **PVC 160–190 °C**.
- ✅ Operating pressures: **5–50 MPa** depending on viscosity and die geometry.
- ✅ For numerical problems, always show **assumptions, formula, substitution, computation**.
- ✅ Mermaid diagrams must avoid **reserved keywords** as node IDs and avoid `|` inside tables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU typically asks derivations for 7 marks and numerical problems for 7 marks. Memorise the **annular flow derivation** and the **slit die equation** — these appear in nearly every exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Forging and Casting Dies: Design Considerations for Forging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Forging and Casting Dies: Design Considerations for Forging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forging Die Design** is the systematic engineering process of determining the geometry, material, clearances, and operating parameters of dies used in forging operations, such that the die will produce forgings of the required shape, dimensional accuracy, and metallurgical quality at the lowest possible cost per part.

A **Forging Die** is a hardened tool (usually consisting of two halves) that contains a precisely machined cavity (the *impression*) into which heated or cold metal is plastically deformed under high compressive loads to obtain the desired final shape.

**Design Considerations for Forging** constitute the set of governing rules, empirical relationships, and metallurgical constraints that must be simultaneously satisfied by the die designer — including parting line location, draft, fillets, flash gutter, shrinkage allowance, preform design, die material, lubrication, and grain flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 – Must-Know Definition:**
> *Forging is a hot/cold working process in which compressive forces are applied to a heated/room-temperature billet, causing plastic deformation to take the shape of the die cavity. The die is therefore a "negative" of the forging — the die designer literally sculpts a cavity that the metal must fill completely without defects.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a forging die as a **two-part waffle iron** that has been customized for an industrial part. When you pour waffle batter and close the iron, the batter fills the engraved pattern. Similarly, when a hot steel billet is placed between forging dies and the press closes, the metal flows into every nook and cranny of the die cavity. However, unlike a waffle iron:

- The metal does not flow like a liquid — it flows as a **visco-plastic solid**, governed by flow stress, temperature, and friction.
- Excess metal must escape somewhere → that is why every forging die has a **flash gutter** (the "overflow channel").
- The two die halves must meet along a carefully chosen **parting line**, just like the seam on a moulded plastic toy.

A more mechanical analogy: imagine pressing a lump of clay between your two palms. To get a clean imprint:
- You must orient the seam (parting line) along the largest cross-section.
- You must slightly angle the cavity walls (draft) so the clay releases without tearing.
- You must round the corners (fillets/radii) so the clay flows smoothly.

Every one of these "obvious" design rules in clay-pressing translates directly into a formal forging die design rule.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Forging Die Design

- **Forging Temperature Range:** 800 °C – 1250 °C (hot forging) ; room temperature (cold forging).
- **Die Block Material Standard:** AISI H11 / H13 hot-work tool steel (typical).
- **Standard Draft Angle:** 3° – 7° (for hot forging on hammers/presses); 0.5° – 1.5° (precision forging).
- **Standard Flash Thickness:** typically **2% – 5%** of the maximum forging thickness.
- **Shrinkage Allowance:** **0.5% – 1.5%** linear (depending on material and temperature).
- **Fillet Radius:** typically **0.4 × web thickness** (minimum) up to **0.8 × web thickness** (preferred).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** A die is not designed for "the forging" — it is designed for the *production of thousands of identical forgings*. Hence **die life, ease of machining, repair, and flash handling** are as important as the geometry itself.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Logic (The "Why" Behind the Rules)

#### A. Parting Line Location
- **Definition:** The parting line is the locus where the upper (top) and lower (bottom) die halves meet.
- **Rule of Thumb:** Locate the parting line on the **largest projected area** of the forging when viewed in the direction of forging force. This minimises lateral (sideways) die thrust, reduces die wear, and makes the forging balanced during ejection.
- **Why:** If the parting line is offset, one die half carries more load, the forging may not fill symmetrically, and the part may "swing" or get stuck on ejection.

#### B. Draft (Taper) on Cavity Walls
- **Definition:** A slight angle (taper) provided on all vertical walls of the forging cavity, so the finished forging can be ejected from the die without dragging or scoring.
- **Why:** During cooling, the forging contracts onto the die walls. Without draft, the forging grips the walls (mechanical interlock) and cannot be ejected. The taper converts this mechanical lock into a small clearance.

#### C. Fillets and Radii
- **Definition:** Internal corners of the forging are given **rounded radii (fillets)**; external corners are given **chamfers (outside radii)**.
- **Why:** Sharp corners in the forging require sharp corners in the die. Sharp die corners:
  1. Concentrate stress → premature die failure (cracking).
  2. Restrict metal flow → unfilled corners (under-fill defects).
  3. Create notches in the forging → fatigue failure in service.

#### D. Flash Gutter (Flash Land + Flash Throat)
- **Definition:** A shallow channel surrounding the forging impression that traps and throttles the excess metal that escapes from the cavity.
- **Components:**
  1. **Flash land** (or *flash thickness* $t_f$) — the narrowest gap between the die faces, which meters the metal flow.
  2. **flash throat width** $w$ — the wider region that collects the escaped metal.
  3. **flash gutter depth** $g$ — total depth of the gutter.
- **Why it exists:** As the press closes, the cavity is completely filled, and any additional downward travel of the upper die forces surplus metal out through the thinnest available path → the flash land. The flash land acts as a "controlled leak", which builds up the die-cavity pressure and **forces the metal into the deepest corners of the impression**.

#### E. Shrinkage Allowance
- **Definition:** The forging cavity is made larger than the finished (cold) forging dimension by a factor equal to the **linear shrinkage** of the forging alloy on cooling.
- **Why:** All metals contract as they cool from forging temperature to room temperature. If the cavity is sized at the cold dimensions, the cooled forging will be undersize.

#### F. Preform / Blanking Volume
- **Definition:** A *preform* is an intermediate shape (in a multi-impression die) that distributes the metal such that the final cavity fills uniformly.
- **Why:** Without preforming, a simple cylindrical billet placed in a complex final cavity will leave some regions unfilled and force excess material into the flash.

#### G. Die Block Dimensions
- **Definition:** The overall length, width, and thickness of the die block must be sufficient to:
  1. Contain the impression (with adequate *die margin* or *land* around the impression).
  2. Resist the forging load without bending or cracking.
  3. Provide enough mass to absorb heat without softening.

#### H. Die Material Selection
- **Standard Choice:** **AISI H13** (or H11) — a hot-work tool steel containing Cr, Mo, V.
- **Why:** Must retain hardness at elevated temperature (≈ 600 °C at the die surface), resist thermal fatigue (heat checking), and have good toughness.

#### I. Lubrication
- **Definition:** A film (graphite/water-based, or proprietary) sprayed on the die cavity before each forging stroke.
- **Why:** Reduces die-workpiece friction, prevents sticking, dissipates heat, and prolongs die life. Excessive lubrication, however, traps gas and may cause under-fill.

#### J. Grain Flow
- **Definition:** During forging, the grain structure of the metal is elongated in the direction of metal flow. The die must be designed so that the **grain flow follows the contour of the part** and never terminates at a free (machined) surface.
- **Why:** Grain flow terminating at a free surface creates a weak, fatigue-prone region.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula / Empirical Rule | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flash thickness $t_f$ | $t_f \approx (0.015 \text{ to } 0.05) \cdot h_{max}$ | 2 – 5 % of max height | mm |
| 2 | Flash land width $L$ | $L \approx (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot t_f$ | 3 – 6 | mm |
| 3 | Flash gutter width $w$ | $w \approx (8 \text{ to } 10) \cdot t_f$ | 8 – 25 | mm |
| 4 | Draft angle $\alpha$ (hot forging) | Empirical: 3° – 7° (hammers/presses) | 5 (typical) | degrees |
| 5 | Draft angle $\alpha$ (precision) | 0.5° – 1.5° | 1 | degrees |
| 6 | Fillet radius $R$ (internal) | $R \approx 0.4 h \text{ to } 0.8 h$ | $R_{min} = 0.4 h$ | mm |
| 7 | Outside radius $r$ (external) | $r \approx (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) h$ | small | mm |
| 8 | Linear shrinkage $S$ | $S = L_{cold} \cdot k_s$ | $k_s$ = 0.005 – 0.015 | mm |
| 9 | Die block width $W$ | $W = 2.5 \cdot h_{max} + w_{cavity}$ | — | mm |
| 10 | Die block thickness $H$ | $H = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot W$ | — | mm |
| 11 | Forging load $F$ | $F = K_f \cdot A_{proj}$ (lower bound) | depends on material | N |
| 12 | Die life $N$ (cycles) | depends on material, temp, lubrication | $5000$ – $50000$ | cycles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table symbols:** All absolute values and the mid-bar in formulas are written as `\vert` or `\mid`/parentheses to keep the markdown table valid. **Never write `|x|` inside a table cell** — it breaks the column separator.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

These design rules govern the production of every forged component in service today — from **automotive connecting rods and crankshafts**, to **aerospace turbine disks**, to **railway axles** and **forged hand tools**. A well-designed die determines:
- Whether the forging meets metallurgical specifications.
- Whether the production line runs at acceptable cycle time.
- Whether the die survives 10 000 strokes or fails after 1 000.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Flash-Thickness Rule (Why $t_f = 0.02$–$0.05$ of the maximum thickness)

Let the maximum local forging thickness be $h_{max}$. The forging press supplies a fixed downward stroke $S$ (a small, controlled overshoot of the die). The volume of metal displaced by this overshoot must be accommodated by:

1. Compression of the forging vertically (slight).
2. Lateral outflow into the flash land and gutter.

Assume the forging behaves as a **rigid-plastic material** under plane-strain flow. The pressure $p$ inside the cavity just before the flash forms can be approximated by the simple forging equation:

$$p = K_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 \mu \, L}{3 \, t_f}\right)$$

where:
- $K_f$ = flow stress of the material at forging temperature (a material constant, in MPa).
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at the die–workpiece interface (typically 0.05 – 0.3).
- $L$ = length of the flash land (mm).
- $t_f$ = flash thickness (mm).

The term $\frac{2 \mu L}{3 t_f}$ is the **friction hill**, representing the additional pressure needed to push metal through the thin flash land against die friction.

Now apply the **conservation of volume** during the final moments of die closure. If the upper die advances an additional infinitesimal distance $dS$ after the cavity is nominally filled, an equal volume of metal $dV$ must be expelled through the flash:

$$dV = A_{cavity} \cdot dS = A_{flash} \cdot \frac{d t_f}{2}$$

where $A_{flash}$ is the cross-sectional area of the flash (≈ land length $L$ times the perimeter of the impression). Combining and integrating for a small but finite overshoot $S_o$:

$$\int_0^{S_o} A_{cavity} \, dS = \int_0^{t_f} A_{flash} \cdot \frac{d t_f}{2}$$

$$A_{cavity} \cdot S_o = \frac{A_{flash} \cdot t_f}{2}$$

For a circular forging of radius $R$ and height $h_{max}$, $A_{cavity} \approx \pi R^2$ and $A_{flash} \approx 2 \pi R \cdot L$ (the perimeter of the impression times the land length). Substituting:

$$\pi R^2 \cdot S_o = \frac{2 \pi R L \cdot t_f}{2}$$

$$t_f = \frac{R \cdot S_o}{L}$$

The **overshoot $S_o$** is usually a small fraction of $h_{max}$, typically $S_o \approx 0.05 \, h_{max}$. The land length $L$ is chosen to be at least $1.5 \, t_f$. Substituting these empirical proportions and simplifying the geometric scaling gives the **empirical rule**:

$$t_f \approx (0.02 \text{ to } 0.05) \cdot h_{max}$$

which matches the textbook value used universally in forging practice.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation shows that the flash thickness is **not arbitrary** — it emerges from a balance of (a) the volume of excess metal, (b) the friction in the flash land, and (c) the geometry of the impression.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Forging Die Design Parameter

| Design Parameter | Influencing Factors | Designer’s Action | Penalty if Ignored |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parting line | Symmetry, draft, ejector position | Place on largest projected area | Die shift, unbalanced fill |
| Draft | Depth of cavity, finish, ejector type | 3°–7° hot, 0.5°–1.5° cold | Stuck forging, scoring |
| Internal fillet $R$ | Web thickness, flow length | $R \ge 0.4 h$ | Die crack, unfilled corner |
| External radius $r$ | Machining allowance, fatigue | $r \ge 0.05 h$ | Notch fatigue in service |
| Flash thickness | $h_{max}$, press stiffness | 2 – 5 % of $h_{max}$ | Under-fill (too thick) or die failure (too thin) |
| Flash land $L$ | $t_f$, metal viscosity | $L = 1.5$–$2.0 \, t_f$ | Friction hill too low / too high |
| Shrinkage | Alloy, forging temp, die temp | $k_s = 0.005$ – $0.015$ | Out-of-tolerance forging |
| Preform shape | Volume distribution | CAE / trial-and-error | Fold defects, lap defects |
| Die material | Workpiece material, temp, lot size | H11 / H13, 45–50 HRC | Heat-checking, low life |
| Lubricant | Die temp, workpiece alloy | Graphite-water for hot forging | Scoring, die wear |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Forging Die Set

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Upper Die Block]
    C --> D[Upper Impression Cavity]
    C --> E[Upper Flash Land]
    C --> F[Upper Flash Gutter]
    G[Lower Die Half Holder] --> H[Lower Die Block]
    H --> I[Lower Impression Cavity]
    H --> J[Lower Flash Land]
    H --> K[Lower Flash Gutter]
    L[Heated Billet] --> M[Placement on Lower Impression]
    M --> I
    D --> N[Plastic Deformation of Metal]
    I --> N
    N --> O[Forced Cavity Fill]
    O --> P[Excess Metal Escapes as Flash]
    P --> E
    P --> J
    P --> F
    P --> K
    O --> Q[Finished Forging + Flash Ribbon]
    Q --> R[Trimming Operation Removes Flash]
    R --> S[Final Forged Part]

    T --> I

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upper Die Face] --> B[Impression Cavity]

```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Selecting Die Design Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Forging Drawing Received] --> B{Is forging symmetric?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Place parting line on largest projected area]
    B -- No --> D[Place parting line to balance fill]
    C --> E[Choose draft angle 3 to 7 deg]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply fillet radius R >= 0.4 times h]
    F --> G[Apply external radius r >= 0.05 times h]
    G --> H[Compute shrinkage allowance k_s = 0.005 to 0.015]
    H --> I[Compute flash thickness t_f = 0.02 to 0.05 times h_max]
    I --> J[Set flash land L = 1.5 to 2.0 times t_f]
    J --> K[Set flash gutter w = 8 to 10 times t_f]
    K --> L[Select die material H13, 45 to 50 HRC]
    L --> M[Plan lubrication graphite water]
    M --> N[Check grain flow continuity]
    N --> O[Issue die drawing for machining]
```
