---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define *parting line* in forging die design. State two guidelines for locating it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** A parting line is the line along which the upper and lower die halves meet and separate. It is the "seam line" of the forging.
- **Guideline 1 (1 Mark):** The parting line should lie on the **largest projected cross-section** of the forging (in the direction of ram travel), so that the forging has a balanced fill and the dies are loaded symmetrically.
- **Guideline 2 (1 Mark):** The parting line should be chosen so that **one half of the forging is deeper or more complex** and the other half is simpler — to facilitate machining and ejection of the forging.

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is a *flash gutter* provided in a forging die? List its three parts.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Purpose (1 Mark):** A flash gutter is provided to **accommodate the excess metal** that is squeezed out of the cavity during the final moments of die closure, and to provide **back-pressure (friction hill)** that forces the metal to completely fill the deepest corners of the impression.
- **Part 1 (1 Mark):** *Flash land* (or flash thickness) — the narrow, throttled gap between the die faces that meters the flow of excess metal.
- **Part 2 (1 Mark):** *Flash throat* (or flash well) — the wider region adjoining the flash land that collects the expelled metal.
- *(For full credit, students may also name:* **flash gutter width** *as the third feature.)*

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** For 14-mark forging die design questions, students routinely lose marks by:
> 1. Skipping the **parting-line justification** (always required for 1–2 marks).
> 2. Forgetting to **add shrinkage allowance** to the cavity dimensions.
> 3. Computing **flash land length** as equal to flash thickness (it should be **1.5× to 2×** the flash thickness).
> 4. Drawing a die cross-section **without a flash gutter** — the examiner's key ALWAYS includes the flash.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define *flash* in forging and explain with a neat sketch how the **flash gutter** controls the cavity pressure during the final stage of die closure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hot-forged steel connecting-rod component has a maximum thickness $h_{max} = 40$ mm. Determine **(i)** flash thickness $t_f$, **(ii)** flash land length $L$, and **(iii)** flash gutter width $w$ using standard empirical rules. Assume a 5° draft angle is used on vertical walls of a 60 mm deep cavity. Compute the **draft allowance** (extra width at the bottom of the cavity due to draft). **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Definition and Role of Flash Gutter (7 Marks)**

- **Definition (2 Marks):** Flash is the thin ribbon of metal that is deliberately squeezed out between the upper and lower die faces when the cavity is full, and is later trimmed off.
- **Sketch (3 Marks):** Draw a die cross-section showing:
  - The forging impression.
  - The flash land (narrow gap of thickness $t_f$).
  - The flash throat (wider gutter).
  - Arrows showing metal flow from cavity → flash land → flash throat.
- **Explanation (2 Marks):** During the final 1–3 % of ram travel, the cavity is full and any further movement forces surplus metal out through the flash land. The narrow land acts as a throttle: the metal must flow against die friction, which generates a **friction hill** in pressure, raising the cavity pressure by a factor $(1 + \frac{2 \mu L}{3 t_f})$. This elevated pressure **forces the metal to fill the deepest, sharpest corners** of the impression. Without flash, the cavity would simply *under-fill*.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $h_{max} = 40$ mm; cavity depth = 60 mm; draft angle $\alpha = 5°$.

**Step 1 — Flash thickness $t_f$ (1 Mark):**

$$t_f = 0.03 \times h_{max} = 0.03 \times 40 = 1.2 \text{ mm}$$

(Taking 3 % of $h_{max}$, the midpoint of the 2 %–5 % range.)

**Step 2 — Flash land length $L$ (2 Marks):**

$$L = 1.5 \times t_f = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.8 \text{ mm}$$

(Using the lower limit of 1.5×; alternative answer $L = 2 \, t_f = 2.4$ mm is also acceptable.)

**Step 3 — Flash gutter width $w$ (2 Marks):**

$$w = 10 \times t_f = 10 \times 1.2 = 12 \text{ mm}$$

(Using the upper empirical value $w = (8$–$10) t_f$.)

**Step 4 — Draft allowance (2 Marks):**

Draft allowance is the increase in lateral cavity dimension from top to bottom of the cavity, due to the draft angle. For a cavity depth of $h_d = 60$ mm and draft $\alpha = 5°$:

$$\text{Draft} = h_d \cdot \tan(\alpha) = 60 \times \tan(5°)$$

$$\tan(5°) = 0.0875$$

$$\text{Draft allowance} = 60 \times 0.0875 = 5.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Final answers:**
- $t_f = 1.2$ mm, $L = 1.8$ mm, $w = 12$ mm, Draft allowance = 5.25 mm per side.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the importance of **(i) draft, (ii) fillet radius, and (iii) shrinkage allowance** in forging die design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A forging made of medium-carbon steel is to be produced with a finished (cold) length of 200 mm. The die is heated to 200 °C, and the workpiece is forged at 1200 °C. The linear shrinkage coefficient for steel in this range is $k_s = 0.012$. Compute **(i)** the die-cavity length at forging temperature, and **(ii)** the **die block thickness** if the die block width is 200 mm and $H = 1.75 \, W$. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Importance of Draft, Fillet, Shrinkage (7 Marks)**

- **(i) Draft (2 Marks):** Draft is a small taper (3°–7° for hot forging) given to all vertical cavity walls. It is essential so the forging can be **ejected from the die** after each stroke. Without draft, the forging contracts onto the die walls during cooling and cannot be removed without scoring or distortion. Draft is also a key factor for forging **dimensional accuracy** and **die life**.
- **(ii) Fillet radius (2 Marks):** Internal corners of the forging are given a generous fillet (typically $R \ge 0.4 h$ where $h$ is the adjacent web thickness). This serves three purposes:
  1. Allows **smooth metal flow** into the corner (eliminates *under-fill*).
  2. Reduces **stress concentration** in the die (extends die life).
  3. Avoids **notch effect** in the finished forging (improves fatigue strength).
- **(iii) Shrinkage allowance (3 Marks):** All metals contract as they cool from forging temperature. If the die cavity is made to the cold dimensions, the finished forging will be undersize. The cavity is therefore **enlarged by the linear shrinkage factor** (e.g., 0.5 %–1.5 % for steels, 1.0 %–1.5 % for aluminium, etc.) so that after cooling the forging reaches the required size. For complex shapes, **different parts of the forging may need different shrinkages** if they cool at different rates.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:** Cold forging length $L_{cold} = 200$ mm; shrinkage coefficient $k_s = 0.012$.

**Step 1 — Die cavity length at forging temperature (4 Marks):**

The die cavity must be larger than the cold forging by the linear shrinkage factor:

$$L_{hot} = L_{cold} \cdot (1 + k_s)$$

$$L_{hot} = 200 \times (1 + 0.012)$$

$$L_{hot} = 200 \times 1.012 = 202.4 \text{ mm}$$

So the die-cavity length at forging temperature is **202.4 mm**.

**Step 2 — Die block thickness $H$ (3 Marks):**

Given die block width $W = 200$ mm and $H = 1.75 \, W$:

$$H = 1.75 \times 200 = 350 \text{ mm}$$

**Final answers:**
- $L_{hot} = 202.4$ mm.
- Die block thickness $H = 350$ mm.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parting line** = largest projected cross-section; balanced fill, easier ejection.
- **Draft** = 3°–7° (hot), 0.5°–1.5° (precision/cold); needed for ejection.
- **Internal fillet** $R \ge 0.4 h$; prevents die crack + under-fill.
- **External radius** $r \ge 0.05 h$; prevents forging notch fatigue.
- **Flash thickness** $t_f = 0.02 h_{max}$ to $0.05 h_{max}$.
- **Flash land** $L = 1.5 \, t_f$ to $2.0 \, t_f$.
- **Flash gutter** $w = 8 \, t_f$ to $10 \, t_f$.
- **Shrinkage** $k_s$ = 0.005 – 0.015 (steel); cavity sized *hot*.
- **Die block** $H \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ times $W$ (resists bending).
- **Die material** = H11 / H13 hot-work tool steel, 45 – 50 HRC.
- **Lubricant** = graphite-water for hot forging (reduces friction, cools die).
- **Grain flow** must follow forging contour; no flow ending at a free surface.
- **Multi-impression dies** need a *preform* / *blocking* cavity to distribute metal volume.
- **Trimming** of the flash is a separate post-forging operation; the die designer must allow easy access for the trim die.
- **CAE (DEFORM, FORGE, QForm)** is used in industry to optimise preform shapes and predict die fill before machining the die.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Die Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Die Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Die Casting (PDC)** is a permanent mould casting process in which molten metal is forced into a reusable metal die cavity (tool steel) under **high pressure (typically 7–200 MPa)** and at **high velocity**, where it solidifies rapidly to produce a near-net-shape component with excellent dimensional accuracy and surface finish.

The process is governed by the principle of *rapid cavity fill followed by intensified pressure during solidification* to compensate for solidification shrinkage. The high injection pressure distinguishes PDC from **gravity die casting** (which relies solely on metallostatic head).

**Classification of Pressure Die Casting (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**

| Type | Working Principle | Typical Alloys |
|---|---|---|
| **Hot Chamber (Gooseneck)** | Plunger is immersed in molten metal bath; injection is direct. | Zn, Sn, Pb, Mg alloys (low melting point, < 420 °C) |
| **Cold Chamber** | Molten metal is ladled into shot sleeve; a separate plunger injects it. | Al, Cu, Mg, brass (high melting point) |
| **Vacuum Die Casting** | Evacuation of die cavity before injection to reduce porosity. | High-integrity Al automotive parts |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Pressure Die Casting machine as a giant industrial "syringe" injecting molten metal into a steel mould.**
>
> - The **barrel = shot sleeve**, the **plunger = piston**, and the **die cavity = the cavity of a mould**.
> - Just as a syringe pushes medicine at high speed into your arm, the PDC machine forces molten metal at high speed into a closed steel die.
> - The difference is that the die is **clamped under enormous force** (hundreds of tonnes) so it does not fly open like the lid of a pressure cooker.
> - Once the metal freezes, the die opens like a **clam-shell**, the part is ejected, and the cycle repeats in **seconds**.

This is why PDC is a **mass-production** process — the cycle time per component is often **10–60 seconds**.

## 1.3 Salient Features (Key Distinguishing Parameters)

- **Injection Pressure:** **7–70 MPa (Hot chamber)**, **30–200 MPa (Cold chamber)**
- **Die Temperature:** **150–300 °C** (kept warm to avoid cold shuts)
- **Cycle Time:** **10–60 s** depending on wall thickness
- **Tolerance:** **±0.05 mm** for small components
- **Surface Finish:** **Ra 0.8–3.2 µm** (as-cast, no machining required)
- **Production Rate:** Up to **200 shots/hour** in automated cells
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Pressure Die Casting cycle follows a strict, repeatable sequence:

1. **Die Closure (Clamping):** The two die halves (cover & ejector) are clamped together by a toggle or hydraulic locking mechanism. The **locking force (tonnage)** must exceed the separating force generated by injection pressure.
2. **Metal Injection:** Molten metal is forced through a **gate**, **runner**, and into the **die cavity** at high velocity via the plunger.
3. **Intensification (Pressure Holding):** After cavity fill, pressure is *maintained* (not released) until solidification completes — this feeds liquid metal to compensate for shrinkage.
4. **Cooling & Solidification:** Heat is extracted through water-cooled die channels; solidification time scales with the **Chvorinov's rule** ($t_s = B(V/A)^2$).
5. **Die Opening & Ejection:** Die opens, ejector pins push the casting (with sprue/runner attached) out.
6. **Trimming:** Excess flash at die parting line and gates is removed in a trim die or by a hydraulic press.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Critical Design Rules

- **Why intensify pressure after fill?** Because liquid metal shrinks **3–6 %** during solidification. Without continued pressure, **porosity and shrinkage cavities** form, ruining mechanical properties.
- **Why vacuum die casting?** Trapped air in the cavity causes **blowholes and gas porosity**. Evacuating the cavity to **50–100 mbar** gives denser, heat-treatable castings.
- **Why a draft angle of 1°–3°?** Friction between the solidified casting and the die wall would otherwise cause **ejection scratches and die wear**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam relevant and must be memorised.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}$ | **Clamping Force** = Injection pressure $\times$ Projected area | N |
| 2 | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{F_c}{A_{proj}}$ | Rearranged to compute required tonnage | Pa (MPa) |
| 3 | $A_{proj} = A_{cast} + A_{sprue} + A_{runner}$ | Total projected area on parting plane | mm² or m² |
| 4 | $t_f = \dfrac{V_c}{Q}$ | **Cavity Fill Time** = Cavity volume / Volumetric flow rate | s |
| 5 | $Q = A_g \cdot v_g$ | Volumetric flow rate = Gate area $\times$ Gate velocity | m³/s |
| 6 | $v_g = \dfrac{L_f}{t_f}$ | **Gate velocity** = Flow length / Fill time | m/s |
| 7 | $W_s = V_c \cdot \rho$ | **Shot Weight** = Cavity volume $\times$ Density of alloy | kg |
| 8 | $F_s = P_{int} \cdot A_{plunger}$ | **Intensification Force** = Intensification pressure $\times$ Plunger area | N |
| 9 | $B = \dfrac{V_{shot}}{t_{cycle}}$ | Production rate = Shot volume / Cycle time | parts/h |
| 10 | $t_s = k \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$ | **Chvorinov's Rule** for solidification time | s |

> **Standard Reference Values (commit to memory):**
> - Density of Al: $\rho_{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Density of Zn: $\rho_{Zn} = 7135 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Density of Cu: $\rho_{Cu} = 8960 \text{ kg/m}^3$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Engine blocks, transmission housings, cylinder heads (cold-chamber Al die casting).
- **Consumer Electronics:** Laptop chassis, mobile phone frames (Mg/Zn alloys).
- **Hardware:** Door locks, hinges, gears (Zn die casting — *Mazak* alloys).
- **Toy Industry:** Die-cast model cars, collectible miniatures (Zn alloy — *Zamak 3/5*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Clamping Force

The **separating force** acting on the die halves during injection must be resisted by the machine's locking force. Consider a casting with a total projected area $A_{proj}$ (in m²) on the parting plane. The molten metal pushes against the die halves with the injection pressure $P_{inj}$ (in Pa).

$$
F_{separating} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj}
$$

For die integrity, the **clamping force** $F_c$ must exceed the separating force by a **safety factor** $n$ (typically $n = 1.25$ to $1.5$):

$$
F_c = n \cdot P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}
$$

In engineering practice, the clamping tonnage is reported in **tonnes-force (1 tf ≈ 9.81 kN)**:

$$
T = \dfrac{F_c \cdot n_{sf}}{g} = \dfrac{P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \quad \text{(tonnes, with } A_{proj} \text{ in mm², } P_{inj} \text{ in MPa)}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** An aluminium die casting has a projected area of $A_{proj} = 25{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$. The injection pressure is $P_{inj} = 50 \text{ MPa}$. Find the required machine clamping tonnage (use $n = 1.3$).

**Solution:**

$$
F_{separating} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj} = 50 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \times 25{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{separating} = 1{,}250{,}000 \text{ N} = 1.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

Applying safety factor:

$$
F_c = 1.3 \times 1.25 \times 10^{6} = 1.625 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

Converting to tonnes:

$$
T = \dfrac{1.625 \times 10^{6}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \approx 165.6 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**∴ Machine tonnage required ≈ 166 tonnes** (next standard size 180 t or 200 t).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cavity Fill Time and Gate Velocity

The die designer must ensure the metal front travels at a **critical gate velocity** $v_g$ (typically 25–60 m/s for Al) so that:
- Surface oxide films do not freeze prematurely.
- Air is pushed ahead and vented properly.

Given a cavity volume $V_c$ and gate area $A_g$, by **continuity of incompressible flow**:

$$
Q = A_g \cdot v_g = \dfrac{V_c}{t_f}
$$

Solving for the fill time:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{V_c}{A_g \cdot v_g}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A die cavity has a volume $V_c = 250 \text{ cm}^3 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$. The gate area is $A_g = 50 \text{ mm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$. Critical gate velocity for the Al alloy is $v_g = 40 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate (a) volumetric flow rate, (b) cavity fill time.

**Solution (a):**

$$
Q = A_g \cdot v_g = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 \times 40 \text{ m/s} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
t_f = \dfrac{V_c}{Q} = \dfrac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.125 \text{ s}
$$

**∴ Fill time = 0.125 s (125 ms)** — typical for thin-wall Al die castings.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shot Weight Computation

The shot weight is the **total molten metal** injected per cycle — it fills the cavity, runner, gate, and overflow wells.

$$
W_s = \rho_{alloy} \times V_{shot}
$$

where

$$
V_{shot} = V_{cavity} + V_{runner} + V_{gate} + V_{overflow}
$$

### Quick Example

For Al casting: $V_{shot} = 300 \text{ cm}^3$, $\rho_{Al} = 2.7 \text{ g/cm}^3$:

$$
W_s = 2.7 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 300 \text{ cm}^3 = 810 \text{ g} = 0.81 \text{ kg}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pressure Die Casting — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Melting Furnace] --> B[Ladle Transfer]
    B --> C{Chamber Type}
    C -->|Hot Chamber| D1[Gooseneck Plunger]
    C -->|Cold Chamber| D2[Shot Sleeve Fill]
    D1 --> E[High Pressure Injection]
    D2 --> E[High Pressure Injection]
    E --> F[Die Cavity Fill via Gate and Runner]
    F --> G[Intensification Pressure Holding]
    G --> H[Solidification with Water Cooling]
    H --> I[Die Opening Toggle Mechanism]
    I --> J[Ejector Pin Push Out]
    J --> K[Trimming of Flash and Runner]
    K --> L[Finished Casting Quality Check]
    L --> M[Recycle Revert]
    M --> A
    K --> N[Reject]
    L --> O[Packaging and Dispatch]
```

## 4.2 Hot Chamber vs Cold Chamber — Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HotChamber[Hot Chamber PDC]
        H1[Melting Pot with Molten Metal] --> H2[Gooseneck Nozzle]
        H2 --> H3[Plunger Pushes Metal]
        H3 --> H4[Die Cavity]
    end
    subgraph ColdChamber[Cold Chamber PDC]
        C1[External Furnace] --> C2[Ladle Pour into Shot Sleeve]
        C2 --> C3[Plunger Pushes Metal Horizontally]
        C3 --> C4[Die Cavity]
    end
```

## 4.3 Die Force Balance Schematic (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Injection Pressure P_inj acting on Projected Area] -->|Separating Force| F1[Pushes Die Halves Apart]
    F1 -->|Resisted by| F2[Clamping Force from Toggle Lock]
    F2 -->|Net Result| F3[Safe Clamp if F2 greater than 1.3 times F1]
    F3 --> R[Die Remains Closed Casting Forms]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently pose a *"Why is clamping tonnage so critical?"* question. Always anchor your answer to the **separating force = injection pressure × projected area** relationship.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between Hot Chamber and Cold Chamber Pressure Die Casting. Mention two alloys processed by each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Hot Chamber PDC | Cold Chamber PDC |
|---|---|---|
| **Metal-melt contact** | Plunger remains immersed in molten bath | Molten metal is ladled into cold shot sleeve |
| **Cycle time** | Faster (no ladling) | Slower (ladling adds time) |
| **Pressure capability** | 7–35 MPa (lower) | 30–200 MPa (higher) |
| **Alloys** | Zn, Sn, Pb, Mg (low m.p.) | Al, Cu, brass (high m.p.) |
| **Corrosion/attack on plunger** | Yes — limits alloy choice | No — plunger is cold |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for process difference, 1 mark for alloy identification)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define "Intensification Pressure" in pressure die casting. Why is it applied after cavity fill?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Intensification pressure is the **additional pressure (typically 50–150 MPa)** applied by the plunger *after* the cavity is completely filled, and held until solidification ends.
- **Purpose (2 marks):** It compensates for **solidification shrinkage** (3–6 % volumetric contraction), feeds liquid metal into the mushy zone, and reduces internal **porosity / micro-shrinkage**. Without it, the casting would have poor mechanical strength and pressure-tightness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the clamping force derivation is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always write `N → tonnes` explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing "injection pressure" with "intensification pressure."** Injection = *during fill*; Intensification = *after fill, during solidification*.
> 3. **Skipping safety factor $n$** in tonnage problems. Examiners will *not* give full marks without it.
> 4. **Not mentioning the runner/gate projected area** when computing $A_{proj}$.

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**
*[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]*

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Cold Chamber Pressure Die Casting** machine with a neat sketch. Discuss its advantages over Hot Chamber process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cold chamber die casting machine is to be selected for producing an aluminium alloy component. The projected area on the parting plane is $A_p = 18{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$, the injection pressure required is $P_{inj} = 60 \text{ MPa}$, and a safety factor of $1.3$ is to be used. Calculate: (i) the required clamping tonnage, and (ii) the intensification force if the plunger diameter is $90 \text{ mm}$ and intensification pressure is $90 \text{ MPa}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
1. Molten Al is poured manually (or by robot) into the **shot sleeve** of the cold-chamber machine.
2. A **hydraulic plunger** then advances, sealing the shot sleeve and forcing the metal through the **nozzle, sprue, runner, and gate** into the closed die cavity at high velocity.
3. Once the cavity is filled, the plunger continues to apply **intensification pressure** during solidification.
4. After solidification, the die opens; **ejector pins** push the casting out; the cycle repeats.

**Sketch (2 marks):** *Labelled block diagram showing: Furnace → Ladle → Shot Sleeve → Plunger → Die Cavity (with clamping unit) → Ejector → Casting.*

**Advantages over Hot Chamber (1 mark):**
- Can process **high-melting-point alloys** (Al, Cu) without attacking the plunger.
- Allows **higher injection pressures** and hence thinner walls.
- Reduces metal contamination and slag entrainment.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Clamping Tonnage (4 marks):**

$$
F_{sep} = P_{inj} \times A_p = 60 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \times 18{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{sep} = 1{,}080{,}000 \text{ N} = 1.08 \text{ MN}
$$

With safety factor:

$$
F_c = 1.3 \times 1.08 \times 10^{6} = 1.404 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{1.404 \times 10^{6}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \approx 143.1 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**∴ Required clamping tonnage ≈ 144 tonnes** (use standard **160 t** machine).

**(ii) Intensification Force (3 marks):**

Plunger area:

$$
A_{pl} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.090)^2 = 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Intensification force:

$$
F_{int} = P_{int} \times A_{pl} = 90 \times 10^{6} \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3} = 5.726 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{int} \approx 572.6 \text{ kN} \;(\approx 58.4 \text{ tonnes-force})
$$

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks)**
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Alternate]*

**(a)** What are the common **defects in pressure die castings**? Discuss any four in detail, with their causes and remedies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A die cavity of volume $V = 180 \text{ cm}^3$ is to be filled in $t_f = 0.09 \text{ s}$ through a gate of area $A_g = 40 \text{ mm}^2$. Calculate: (i) the gate velocity, (ii) the volumetric flow rate, and (iii) the mass of molten aluminium injected per shot. Take $\rho_{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Common defects (any four to be detailed, 1.75 marks each):**

1. **Porosity (Gas + Shrinkage):**
   - *Cause:* Air entrapment during high-velocity fill; insufficient intensification pressure; volatile lubricants.
   - *Remedy:* Vacuum die casting; proper venting; increased intensification pressure; pre-heating die.

2. **Cold Shuts & Flow Lines:**
   - *Cause:* Metal front freezes prematurely due to low die temperature or low gate velocity.
   - *Remedy:* Pre-heat die to 150–300 °C; raise injection velocity; improve gate design.

3. **Flash (Burrs):**
   - *Cause:* Insufficient clamping force; worn die parting surfaces; excessive injection pressure.
   - *Remedy:* Increase tonnage; re-machine die faces; deburr; trim in trim die.

4. **Soldering (Die Sticking):**
   - *Cause:* Alloy chemically welds to die steel (especially Al with H13 steel at high temperatures).
   - *Remedy:* Use proper die coatings (e.g., nitriding + graphite); correct die temp; release agents.

5. **Cracks & Hot Tears:**
   - *Cause:* Restricted contraction in die; uneven cooling; improper ejection.
   - *Remedy:* Adequate draft angles; uniform cooling channel design; proper ejection timing.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

Given: $V_c = 180 \text{ cm}^3 = 180 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$, $t_f = 0.09 \text{ s}$, $A_g = 40 \text{ mm}^2 = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $\rho_{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Gate Velocity (2.5 marks):**

$$
v_g = \dfrac{V_c}{A_g \cdot t_f} = \dfrac{180 \times 10^{-6}}{40 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.09}
$$

$$
v_g = \dfrac{180 \times 10^{-6}}{3.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 50 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate (2.5 marks):**

$$
Q = \dfrac{V_c}{t_f} = \dfrac{180 \times 10^{-6}}{0.09} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Mass per Shot (2 marks):**

$$
m = \rho_{Al} \times V_c = 2700 \times 180 \times 10^{-6} = 0.486 \text{ kg}
$$

**∴ Mass per shot = 486 g.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorise Before Exam):**

- **Pressure Die Casting = High-pressure injection of molten metal into a permanent steel die.** ⭐
- Two main types: **Hot Chamber** (Zn, Mg) and **Cold Chamber** (Al, Cu). ⭐
- Key pressures: **Injection 30–200 MPa**, **Intensification 50–150 MPa.** ⭐
- Master formula: $F_c = n \cdot P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}$. ⭐
- **1 tonne-force ≈ 9.81 kN** — convert carefully in derivations.
- **Intensification** = pressure *after* fill, *during* solidification → fights shrinkage.
- **Cavity fill time** is governed by $t_f = V_c / (A_g v_g)$.
- **Critical gate velocity** for Al ≈ 25–60 m/s.
- **Top 4 defects:** Porosity, cold shuts, flash, soldering — know at least 2 deeply.
- **Chvorinov's rule** $t_s = k (V/A)^2$ governs local solidification time.
- **Standard alloys:** Zamak 3/5 (Zn), A380 / A383 (Al), AZ91 (Mg).
- **Die material:** Hot-work tool steel **H13** (pre-hardened, nitrided).
- **Process advantage:** Near-net shape, ±0.05 mm tolerance, Ra 0.8–3.2 µm finish.
- **Process limitation:** High initial tooling cost → only economical for **large batches** (>5000 parts).
- **Vacuum die casting** is used when castings must be **weldable / heat-treatable** (low porosity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powder Metallurgy and Rubber Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET602 - METAL FORMING AND TOOL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Powder Metallurgy and Rubber Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Powder Metallurgy (P/M) — The Big Picture

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Powder Metallurgy (P/M)** is a metal-forming manufacturing process in which fine metal powders (or a mixture of metal and non-metal powders) are compacted in a die under high pressure to form a "green" part, and then heated (sintered) in a controlled-atmosphere furnace below the melting point of the main constituent, so that the particles metallurgically bond together to produce a finished or near-finished component.

### The Three Magic Steps of P/M (High-Level)

| Step | What Happens | Simple Analogy |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| **1. Powder Preparation** | Pure metal or alloy is converted into fine powder via atomization, reduction, or chemical methods. | Imagine grinding sugar into different grades (fine/medium/coarse). |
| **2. Compaction (Pressing)** | Powder is pressed in a rigid die at 200–1500 MPa to form a "green compact" with sufficient strength to be handled. | Making a snowball — you pack loose snow tightly so it holds its shape. |
| **3. Sintering** | Green compact is heated to 0.7–0.9 Tm (in Kelvin) in a protective atmosphere. Particles diffuse and bond. | Baking a clay pot — the particles fuse without melting entirely. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Green Compact:** The as-pressed (but un-sintered) part. It is fragile, like a chalk stick — handle with care.
> **Tm** = Melting temperature of the major constituent in **Kelvin (K)**.

---

## 1.2 Rubber Moulding — The Big Picture

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rubber Moulding** is a polymer-processing technique in which uncured elastomeric compounds (typically pre-heated blanks or strips of rubber) are shaped inside a closed, heated mould cavity and simultaneously **vulcanized (cured)** — cross-linking the long-chain polymer molecules with sulfur — to produce a durable, elastic finished product.

### Three Principal Rubber Moulding Processes

| Process | Core Idea | Analogy |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Compression Moulding** | A pre-weighed rubber blank is placed directly in an open heated mould; the mould is closed and pressure forces material into the cavity. | Making a sandwich in a panini press — you put the dough, close the lid, and squeeze. |
| **Transfer Moulding** | Rubber is pre-heated in a transfer pot above the mould cavity; a plunger forces it through a runner system into the closed cavity. | Cake decorating with a piping bag — you push icing from the bag into the shaped nozzle. |
| **Injection Moulding** | Rubber is plasticized in a screw/barrel and injected at high speed through a small gate into a closed mould. | Syringe injecting medicine — fast, precise, repeatable. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vulcanization (Curing):** A chemical process discovered by Charles Goodyear (1839) in which sulfur bridges (–S–S–) form between polymer chains, transforming sticky raw rubber into a strong, elastic, dimensionally stable material.

---

## 1.3 Why Are These Topics Combined in KTU Module 4?

Both processes are **near-net-shape / net-shape** forming techniques that minimize material waste and machining. KTU examiners frequently test the comparison between them under the same CO because both rely on **closed-die cavity filling** but differ fundamentally in material state (rigid metal particles vs. viscous elastomer).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Powder Metallurgy Pipeline

### Step 1 — Powder Manufacturing Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the **four commercial powder-making routes**:

**(a) Atomization (most common — ~80% of metal powders)**
- Molten metal is forced through a small orifice; a high-velocity gas (air) or water jet disintegrates the stream into fine droplets, which solidify as spherical powder.
- **Particle shape:** spherical (excellent flow).
- **Used for:** steels, copper, brass, aluminum, superalloys.

**(b) Reduction of Oxides**
- Metal oxides (e.g., Fe₂O₃, MoO₂, WO₂) are reduced with hydrogen or carbon monoxide at high temperature to produce sponge-like, porous powder.
- **Particle shape:** irregular, porous (good green strength due to mechanical interlocking).
- **Used for:** iron, molybdenum, tungsten.

**(c) Electrolytic Deposition**
- A metal is deposited electrolytically from an aqueous salt solution onto a cathode, scraped off, washed, and ground. Produces very pure, dendritic powder.
- **Used for:** high-purity iron, copper, beryllium.

**(d) Mechanical Crushing / Milling**
- Brittle materials (e.g., cemented carbides, oxides, ferro-alloys) are crushed in jaw crushers, hammer mills, or ball mills.
- **Particle shape:** angular (poor flow, high green strength due to interlocking).

### Step 2 — Powder Characteristics (Test-Worthy)

| Property | Definition | Why It Matters |
|----------|------------|----------------|
| **Particle Size** | Typically 1–200 µm; measured by sieve analysis or laser diffraction. | Affects surface area, sintering rate, and final density. |
| **Particle Shape** | Spherical, irregular, dendritic, angular, flake. | Spherical flows well; angular locks together (good green strength). |
| **Apparent Density (ρ_a)** | Mass of powder that freely fills a unit volume container (includes inter-particle voids). | Determines die fill depth. |
| **Tap Density (ρ_t)** | Density after mechanical tapping until volume stabilizes. | Upper bound of loose packing. |
| **Flow Rate** | Time (seconds) for 50 g powder to flow through a Hall funnel of 2.54 mm orifice. | Critical for automatic die filling. |
| **Compressibility** | Ability to densify under pressure. | Determines final green strength. |
| **Green Strength** | Mechanical strength of the pressed-but-un-sintered compact. | Determines handling robustness. |

### Step 3 — Compaction (Pressing)

- **Die Compaction (uniaxial, single-action or double-action):** Powder is filled into a die cavity; an upper punch (and/or lower punch) presses at 200–1500 MPa.
- **Cold Isostatic Pressing (CIP):** Powder sealed in a flexible mould, placed in a high-pressure fluid chamber (100–400 MPa). Pressure is uniform from all sides → uniform density.
- **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP):** Same as CIP but at elevated temperature, applied simultaneously — produces near-theoretical density.
- **Powder Rolling:** Powder fed between two counter-rotating rolls → continuous green strip (used for sheet stock).

### Step 4 — Sintering (Heart of P/M)

> [!NOTE]
> Sintering temperature ≈ **0.7 to 0.9 × T_m (in Kelvin)**. At this temperature, atomic diffusion becomes significant but no liquid phase forms (in solid-state sintering).

**Three classical sintering stages:**

1. **Initial Stage** — Neck growth between adjacent particles at contact points. Strong inter-particle bonding develops; porosity remains largely continuous (open).
2. **Intermediate Stage** — Pore channels close off; pores become isolated (closed). Grain growth begins. Major densification occurs.
3. **Final Stage** — Isolated spherical pores shrink and slowly disappear. Density approaches theoretical. Slow kinetics.

**Atmosphere Control:**
- Vacuum, hydrogen, dissociated ammonia, or endothermic gas — prevents oxidation, decarburization, and removes lubricant residues.

### Step 5 — Secondary Operations

- **Sizing / Coining:** Re-press sintered part in a die to improve dimensional accuracy and density.
- **Infiltration:** Molten metal (e.g., Cu) infiltrates porous sintered skeleton to fill residual pores.
- **Impregnation:** Oil (e.g., 10–30% by volume) impregnates porous bearings — creates self-lubricating bushings.
- **Heat Treatment, Machining, Plating.**

---

## 2.2 Detailed Rubber Moulding Processes

### 2.2.1 Compression Moulding

- A pre-weighed **blank** (slab of uncured rubber) is placed in the lower half of an open, heated mould.
- The mould is closed in a hydraulic press; the rubber flows under pressure into the cavity.
- Curing (vulcanization) occurs simultaneously by virtue of mould heat.
- **Cure time** typically 2–30 min at 150–200 °C depending on part thickness and compound.
- Best for: large, low-quantity parts; thick sections; materials that do not flow easily (e.g., hard rubber).

### 2.2.2 Transfer Moulding

- A measured charge of pre-heated rubber is placed in a **transfer pot** above the cavity.
- A plunger pushes the rubber through a **sprue and runner system** into the closed, heated mould cavity.
- Two sub-types: **Pot Transfer** (plunger above the mould) and **Plunger Transfer** (separate ram chamber).
- Best for: parts with inserts (metal pins, electrodes), intricate shapes, tighter tolerances.

### 2.2.3 Injection Moulding of Rubber

- Rubber strip is fed from a hopper into a heated **screw/barrel**, where it is plasticized (shear-heated) into a viscous, flowable state.
- The screw (acting as a plunger) injects the rubber through a small **gate** into a closed, heated mould cavity.
- Cures rapidly (often 30 s – 3 min); automated, high volume.
- Best for: small, high-volume precision parts (seals, grommets, O-rings).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **examinable** in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks). Memorize the units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Compressibility Ratio** | $C_R = \dfrac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{apparent}}$ | dimensionless | Lower CR ⇒ better flowability (ideal ≤ 1.25) |
| 2 | **% Compressibility** | $\%C = \dfrac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{apparent}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100$ | % | Measure of densification under tapping |
| 3 | **Porosity (P)** | $P = \dfrac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}} \times 100 = \dfrac{\rho_{s} - \rho_{apparent}}{\rho_{s}} \times 100$ | % | $\rho_s$ = theoretical solid density |
| 4 | **Sintering Shrinkage** | $\%S = \dfrac{L_{green} - L_{final}}{L_{green}} \times 100$ | % | Negative ⇒ part swells (rare) |
| 5 | **Compaction Pressure** | $P_c = \dfrac{F_{press}}{A_{projected}}$ | MPa | $F_{press}$ in N, $A$ in mm² |
| 6 | **Green Density** | $\rho_{green} = \dfrac{m_{compact}}{V_{compact}}$ | g/cm³ | Measured before sintering |
| 7 | **Densification Parameter** | $D = \dfrac{\rho_{sintered} - \rho_{green}}{\rho_{theoretical} - \rho_{green}}$ | dimensionless | 0 = no sintering; 1 = fully dense |
| 8 | **Cure Time Estimate (Rubber)** | $t_{cure} = t_{0} + k \cdot h$ | minutes | $t_0$ = induction time, $h$ = thickness (mm), $k$ = empirical factor (≈ 0.5–1 min/mm) |
| 9 | **Mould Shrinkage (Rubber)** | $\%MS = \dfrac{L_{mould} - L_{part}}{L_{mould}} \times 100$ | % | Typically 1.5–3% for rubber |

> [!NOTE]
> Avoid using the vertical bar symbol in formula tables (e.g., write $\vert x - \bar{x} \vert$ instead of $|x - \bar{x}|$ when inside a markdown table row).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Powder Metallurgy Application | Rubber Moulding Application |
|--------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Automotive** | Self-lubricating bearing bushes, synchronizer hubs, oil-impregnated gears | Engine mounts, gaskets, O-rings, tires |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine discs (HIP), high-temperature superalloy parts (after HIP) | Vibration dampers, seals |
| **Tooling** | Cemented carbide (WC-Co) cutting tool inserts (made by P/M) | Rubber moulds for casting, soft jaws for clamping |
| **Electrical** | Tungsten contacts, soft magnetic cores (Fe-Si-P) | Cable insulation, grommets |
| **Consumer Goods** | Watch cases, lock parts, zipper teeth | Shoe soles, mobile phone covers, headphones |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains fully worked-out derivations. No step is skipped — KTU valuation rewards complete logical flow.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Porosity of a Powder Compact from Density Data

### Problem Setup
A powder compact has a measured mass of **85.0 g** and a measured volume of **12.0 cm³**. The solid (theoretical) density of the constituent metal (brass) is **8.50 g/cm³**. Find the porosity in %.

### Step 1: Write the porosity definition
Porosity is the volume fraction of pores inside the compact.

$$
P = \frac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}} \times 100 \%
$$

### Step 2: Express pore volume in terms of mass and densities
The compact's actual mass is the same as the mass of the solid material (the pores contain air, which is massless for this calculation). Thus:

$$
V_{solid} = \frac{m}{\rho_s}
$$

Where:
- $m$ = measured mass = **85.0 g**
- $\rho_s$ = theoretical solid density = **8.50 g/cm³**

Substitute:

$$
V_{solid} = \frac{85.0 \text{ g}}{8.50 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 10.00 \text{ cm}^3
$$

### Step 3: Compute the pore volume
The total volume of the compact is the sum of solid volume and pore volume.

$$
V_{total} = V_{solid} + V_{pores}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
V_{pores} = V_{total} - V_{solid} = 12.0 \text{ cm}^3 - 10.0 \text{ cm}^3 = 2.0 \text{ cm}^3
$$

### Step 4: Compute the apparent (bulk) density of the compact
Apparent density is mass divided by total volume (including pores).

$$
\rho_{apparent} = \frac{m}{V_{total}} = \frac{85.0 \text{ g}}{12.0 \text{ cm}^3 = 7.083 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute into the porosity formula

$$
P = \frac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.0 \text{ cm}^3}{12.0 \text{ cm}^3} \times 100\% = 16.67\%
$$

### Step 6: Cross-check using the density-based porosity formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\rho_s - \rho_{apparent}}{\rho_s} \times 100\% \\
  &= \frac{8.50 - 7.083}{8.50} \times 100\% \\
  &= \frac{1.417}{8.50} \times 100\% \\
  &= 16.67\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer
**Porosity of the compact = 16.67 %** (≈ 16.7 %).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sintering Shrinkage from Dimensional Measurements

### Problem Setup
A cylindrical green compact has a diameter of **25.40 mm** and a length of **50.80 mm**. After sintering, the diameter becomes **24.89 mm** and the length becomes **49.78 mm**. Determine the diametral and longitudinal shrinkage. Also determine the **% volume change**.

### Step 1: Apply the shrinkage formula in each direction

$$
\%S_d = \frac{D_{green} - D_{final}}{D_{green}} \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%S_d &= \frac{25.40 - 24.89}{25.40} \times 100 \\
      &= \frac{0.51}{25.40} \times 100 \\
      &= 2.008\% \quad \approx 2.01\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%S_L &= \frac{L_{green} - L_{final}}{L_{green}} \times 100 \\
      &= \frac{50.80 - 49.78}{50.80} \times 100 \\
      &= \frac{1.02}{50.80} \times 100 \\
      &= 2.008\% \quad \approx 2.01\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Note the physical meaning
Because sintering shrinkage is **isotropic** (nearly equal in all directions in solid-state sintering), both directions should give almost the same value. The fact that both are 2.01 % confirms proper experimental conditions.

### Step 3: Compute the volume shrinkage

$$
V_{green} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_{green}^2 \cdot L_{green}
$$

$$
V_{final} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_{final}^2 \cdot L_{final}
$$

The ratio:

$$
\frac{V_{final}}{V_{green}} = \left(\frac{D_{final}}{D_{green}}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{L_{final}}{L_{green}}\right)
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{final}}{V_{green}} &= \left(\frac{24.89}{25.40}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{49.78}{50.80}\right) \\
&= (0.9799)^2 \cdot (0.9800) \\
&= 0.9602 \cdot 0.9800 \\
&= 0.9410
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute % volume shrinkage

$$
\%V_{shrink} = (1 - 0.9410) \times 100\% = 5.90\%
$$

### Final Answer
- **Diametral shrinkage:** 2.01 %
- **Longitudinal shrinkage:** 2.01 %
- **Volume shrinkage:** 5.90 %

> [!NOTE]
> Volume shrinkage ≈ 3 × linear shrinkage (for small values) ⇒ 3 × 2.01 % = 6.03 %. The small difference (5.90 vs 6.03) is due to rounding.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Compaction Pressure from Press Force and Piston Area

### Problem Setup
A hydraulic press applies a force of **350 kN** on a punch of cross-sectional area **35.0 cm²**. A powder compact is being formed with a projected area equal to the punch area. Find the compaction pressure in MPa.

### Step 1: Convert units consistently
Use SI base units:
- $F = 350 \text{ kN} = 350{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $A = 35.0 \text{ cm}^2 = 35.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 3.50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

### Step 2: Apply pressure formula

$$
P_c = \frac{F}{A}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{350{,}000 \text{ N}}{3.50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2} \\
    &= 1.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \\
    &= 100 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Practical interpretation
This is a typical compaction pressure for ferrous powder compaction (range 400–1000 MPa is common; 100 MPa is light). Higher pressure ⇒ higher green density, but risk of die seizure and tool damage.

### Final Answer
**Compaction pressure = 100 MPa** (≈ 1000 bar).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cure Time Estimation for a Rubber Moulding Part

### Problem Setup
A rubber component of maximum wall thickness **h = 8 mm** is to be compression moulded. The rubber compound has an empirical induction time $t_0 = 2.0$ min and a thickness factor $k = 0.75$ min/mm. Estimate the cure time.

### Step 1: Apply the empirical cure-time model

$$
t_{cure} = t_0 + k \cdot h
$$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cure} &= 2.0 \text{ min} + (0.75 \text{ min/mm}) \times (8 \text{ mm}) \\
         &= 2.0 \text{ min} + 6.0 \text{ min} \\
         &= 8.0 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Engineering remark
Heat transfer through rubber is slow (low thermal conductivity ≈ 0.15 W/m·K). Cure time grows linearly with thickness because curing front propagates from the mould wall inward by conduction.

### Final Answer
**Estimated cure time ≈ 8.0 minutes.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (P/M vs. Rubber Moulding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most test-worthy comparison** in this module. KTU examiners routinely ask: "Compare and contrast P/M and rubber moulding" (often as a 14-mark question).

| Parameter | Powder Metallurgy | Rubber Moulding |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Material state** | Solid metal powder | Viscous elastomer (uncured) |
| **Forming temperature** | 0.7–0.9 T_m (sintering), no melting | 150–200 °C (curing) |
| **Pressure** | 200–1500 MPa (compaction) | 5–100 MPa (moulding) |
| **Cycle time** | Minutes to hours (sintering dominates) | Seconds (injection) to tens of min (compression) |
| **Material utilization** | Excellent (≤ 5% waste) | Excellent (flash is reusable) |
| **Geometry limit** | Undercuts difficult; thin walls possible | Undercuts require split moulds |
| **Density** | 80–95% theoretical (often porous) | Near 100% (after vulcanization) |
| **Mechanical behaviour** | Rigid metallic; porosity affects ductility | Elastic, recoverable |
| **Typical parts** | Bushes, gears, filters, cutting tools | Seals, gaskets, tyres, dampers |

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, CO4, Remembering]`

**Q: List any four methods of manufacturing metal powders used in powder metallurgy. Mention the typical particle shape produced by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Method | Typical Particle Shape |
|---|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | Atomization | Spherical |
| 2 | Reduction of oxide | Irregular, sponge-like |
| 3 | Electrolytic deposition | Dendritic |
| 4 | Mechanical crushing / ball milling | Angular, irregular |

Each method carries 0.5 mark for name + 0.25 mark for shape → **3 marks**.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, CO4, Understanding]`

**Q: What is vulcanization? Why is it essential in rubber moulding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Vulcanization is a chemical curing process in which sulfur atoms form cross-link bridges (–S–S– or –S–Sx–S–) between adjacent long-chain rubber polymer molecules, typically under heat (140–180 °C) and pressure, in the presence of accelerators and activators.

**Importance (1.5 marks):**
1. Converts sticky, weak raw rubber into a strong, elastic, dimensionally stable solid.
2. Improves tensile strength, hardness, abrasion resistance, and thermal stability.
3. Prevents permanent deformation under load — gives rubber its "elastic memory."

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 4 typically permits an internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, CO4, Applying + Analysing]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the steps involved in the powder metallurgy manufacturing process with a neat flow diagram. Mention at least **two applications** of P/M parts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of powder metallurgy compared to conventional manufacturing processes like casting and machining? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Steps in P/M Manufacturing (7 marks):**

1. **Powder Manufacture (1 mark):** Atomization, reduction, electrolytic, mechanical crushing.
2. **Powder Blending (0.5 mark):** Mixing of elemental/alloy powders + lubricant (e.g., zinc stearate 0.5–1.5 wt%).
3. **Compaction (2 marks):** Uniaxial die compaction at 200–1500 MPa produces "green compact." Also covers CIP and HIP.
4. **Sintering (2 marks):** Heating to 0.7–0.9 T_m in controlled atmosphere; diffusion bonding; open → closed porosity.
5. **Secondary Operations (1 mark):** Sizing, coining, infiltration, impregnation, heat treatment.
6. **Applications (0.5 mark):** Self-lubricating bushes, cemented carbide cutting inserts, oil pump gears, porous filters.

**Flow Diagram (in text, 0 marks for ASCII but examiner expects labelled block):** `Powder → Blending → Compaction → Green Compact → Sintering → Sintered Part → Secondary Operations → Final Part`.

**(b) Advantages and Limitations (7 marks):**

| Advantages (3.5 marks) | Limitations (3.5 marks) |
|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Near-net-shape → minimal machining | High initial die/tool cost |
| Excellent material utilization (≤ 5% scrap) | Limited to small-to-medium parts |
| Controlled porosity for self-lubrication | Porosity reduces ductility and impact strength |
| Refractory metals (W, Mo, WC) easily produced | Difficult to make complex shapes / undercuts |
| Good dimensional accuracy (± 0.025 mm after sizing) | Health hazard from fine powders (fire/explosion risk) |
| Suitable for mass production | High energy cost for sintering furnace |

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, CO4, Applying + Evaluating]`

**Q: (a)** Compare **compression moulding**, **transfer moulding**, and **injection moulding** of rubber. Tabulate your answer covering **process steps, pressure range, cure time, and suitable applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A powder metallurgy part has the following data:
- Green length = 40.00 mm; Sintered length = 39.20 mm.
- Mass of compact = 78.5 g; Volume of green compact = 11.0 cm³.
- Theoretical density of solid metal = 8.93 g/cm³ (copper).

Calculate: **(i)** Linear sintering shrinkage, **(ii)** Apparent (green) density, **(iii)** Porosity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Compression Moulding | Transfer Moulding | Injection Moulding |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Process** | Blank placed in open mould, mould closed | Charge in pot, plunger pushes through runners | Screw plasticizes rubber, injects through gate |
| **Pressure** | 5–30 MPa | 30–70 MPa | 70–140 MPa |
| **Cure time** | 5–30 min | 2–10 min | 30 s – 3 min |
| **Best for** | Large, thick, low-volume parts | Parts with inserts, intricate geometry | Small, high-volume precision parts |
| **Waste/Flash** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Cost** | Lowest tooling | Medium | Highest |

Each correctly populated row: ~1 mark. Add 1 mark for a short introductory sentence.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Linear sintering shrinkage (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%S &= \frac{L_{green} - L_{final}}{L_{green}} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{40.00 - 39.20}{40.00} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{0.80}{40.00} \times 100 \\
    &= 2.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Apparent (green) density (2 marks):**

$$
\rho_{apparent} = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{78.5 \text{ g}}{11.0 \text{ cm}^3} = 7.136 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**(iii) Porosity (3 marks):**

First compute solid volume:

$$
V_{solid} = \frac{m}{\rho_s} = \frac{78.5 \text{ g}}{8.93 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 8.789 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Pore volume:

$$
V_{pores} = V_{total} - V_{solid} = 11.0 - 8.789 = 2.211 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Porosity:

$$
P = \frac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{2.211}{11.0} \times 100 = 20.10\%
$$

Cross-check using density formula:

$$
P = \frac{\rho_s - \rho_{apparent}}{\rho_s} \times 100 = \frac{8.93 - 7.136}{8.93} \times 100 = 20.10\% \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes students make in this module:**
> 1. **Confusing "sintering temperature" with "melting temperature."** Sintering is **solid-state** (no melting). Always quote the value as a fraction of T_m (in **Kelvin**), not °C.
> 2. **Using mass instead of volume in porosity calculation.** Many students write $P = m_{pore}/m_{total}$, which is dimensionally wrong. Always use volumes (or densities).
> 3. **Forgetting the "0.5–1.5 wt% lubricant" detail** in compaction. Examiners award a full mark if mentioned.
> 4. **Writing "injection moulding" instead of "transfer moulding"** when describing rubber moulding for parts with **metal inserts**. The decision rule is: insert-heavy part → transfer; high-volume small part → injection; large thick part → compression.
> 5. **Sintering shrinkage is positive when part shrinks** — students often sign-error. Memorize: **%S = (Green − Final)/Green × 100** (always positive if Green > Final).
> 6. **Rubber curing time is NOT a constant** — it scales with the **maximum wall thickness**, not volume. Examiners deduct marks if students use volume.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Powder Metallurgy** = powder prep → blending → compaction → sintering → secondary ops.
- [ ] **Atomization** → spherical powder; **Reduction** → spongy powder; **Electrolytic** → dendritic; **Mechanical** → angular.
- [ ] **Sintering temperature = 0.7 to 0.9 × T_m (Kelvin)** — solid-state diffusion bonding.
- [ ] **Three sintering stages:** Neck formation (initial) → pore closure (intermediate) → pore elimination (final).
- [ ] **Porosity** $P = (\rho_s - \rho_{apparent})/\rho_s \times 100$.
- [ ] **Sintering shrinkage** $\%S = (L_{green} - L_{final})/L_{green} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Densification parameter** $D$ goes from 0 (unsintered) to 1 (fully dense).
- [ ] **CIP** = uniform pressure, complex shapes; **HIP** = CIP + heat → near-theoretical density.
- [ ] **P/M advantages:** minimal waste, controlled porosity, refractory metals, net-shape.
- [ ] **P/M limitations:** die cost, residual porosity, ductility loss, fine powder hazards.
- [ ] **Rubber Moulding = Compression + Transfer + Injection.** All three require **vulcanization**.
- [ ] **Compression** = low pressure, large parts; **Transfer** = medium pressure, inserts; **Injection** = high pressure, small parts.
- [ ] **Vulcanization** = sulfur cross-linking → elasticity, strength, dimensional stability.
- [ ] **Cure time ∝ wall thickness:** $t_{cure} = t_0 + k \cdot h$.
- [ ] **Mould shrinkage for rubber = 1.5–3 %**; designers must oversize the cavity.
- [ ] **P/M is for rigid metals with tolerable porosity**; **Rubber moulding is for elastomers requiring elasticity** — never confuse the two in numerical problems.
- [ ] **KTU keyword triggers:**
  - "Spherical particles" → atomization.
  - "Self-lubricating" → oil impregnation of sintered porous part.
  - "Insert-heavy rubber part" → transfer moulding.
  - "Refractory metal part" → P/M (only feasible route).
- [ ] Always show **unit consistency** (g/cm³ ↔ kg/m³ ↔ g/cc) in numerical answers — examiners reward this habit with full marks.
